A comprehensive Infrastructure as Code documentation designed for Python/Django/FastAPI, DevOps & AWS developers. Master Terraform from fundamentals to advanced techniques, with real-world projects and certification preparation.

Professional Ready

CHAPTER 31

Advanced Configuration Patterns

Dynamic Blocks Type Manipulation Null Resources External Data Templates Conditionals Iteration Cross-Module Targeting
9 Sections
33+ Subsections
25+ Code Examples
9 Patterns
PART 9 ADVANCED ★★★★★

Master advanced HCL patterns including dynamic blocks, complex type manipulation, null resources, external data sources, template files, conditional creation, iteration patterns, cross-module references, and resource targeting for production-grade Terraform configurations.

Learning Objectives

  • Build complex nested dynamic blocks with conditional logic
  • Master deep merge, object transformation, and flattening patterns
  • Use null_resource and terraform_data for orchestration
  • Integrate external scripts via the external data source
  • Generate configuration files with templatefile() and complex templates
  • Implement conditional resource creation with count and for_each
  • Choose the right iteration pattern for each use case
  • Wire cross-module references via remote state and SSM
  • Understand resource targeting risks and best practices

Chapter Contents

31.1 Dynamic Blocks - Advanced 31.2 Complex Type Manipulation 31.3 Null Resources 31.4 External Data Sources 31.5 Template Files 31.6 Conditional Resource Creation 31.7 Resource Iteration Patterns 31.8 Cross-Module References 31.9 Resource Targeting

31.1 Dynamic Blocks - Advanced

PATTERN 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Dynamic Blocks | For Expressions

Dynamic blocks eliminate repetitive nested block configurations by generating them programmatically from collections, enabling DRY infrastructure code that scales with complex requirements.

💻 Practical Examples

31.1.1 Complex Dynamic Structures

Dynamic blocks generate repeated nested blocks from maps or lists. They replace copy-paste patterns for security group rules, IAM policies, and similar repetitive configurations.

Example: Security Group with Dynamic Ingress Rules

Generate multiple ingress rules from a variable map, with each rule having its own CIDR, port, and description.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "ingress_rules" {
  description = "Map of ingress rules"
  type = map(object({
    from_port   = number
    to_port     = number
    protocol    = string
    cidr_blocks = list(string)
    description = string
  }))
  default = {
    http = {
      from_port   = 80
      to_port     = 80
      protocol    = "tcp"
      cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
      description = "HTTP from anywhere"
    }
    https = {
      from_port   = 443
      to_port     = 443
      protocol    = "tcp"
      cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
      description = "HTTPS from anywhere"
    }
    ssh = {
      from_port   = 22
      to_port     = 22
      protocol    = "tcp"
      cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/8"]
      description = "SSH from internal network"
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_security_group" "dynamic_sg" {
  name_prefix = "dynamic-"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = var.ingress_rules
    content {
      from_port   = ingress.value.from_port
      to_port     = ingress.value.to_port
      protocol    = ingress.value.protocol
      cidr_blocks = ingress.value.cidr_blocks
      description = "${ingress.key}: ${ingress.value.description}"
    }
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "dynamic-security-group"
  }
}
31.1.2 Nested Dynamic Blocks

Dynamic blocks can be nested inside each other for multi-level structures like WAF rules with statements, or load balancer listeners with actions and conditions.

Example: ALB Listener with Nested Dynamic Rules

Generate listener rules with nested conditions and actions from a structured variable.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "listener_rules" {
  type = map(object({
    priority = number
    conditions = list(object({
      field  = string
      values = list(string)
    }))
    target_group_arn = string
  }))
}

resource "aws_lb_listener_rule" "host_based" {
  for_each     = var.listener_rules
  listener_arn = aws_lb_listener.front_end.arn
  priority     = each.value.priority

  action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = each.value.target_group_arn
  }

  dynamic "condition" {
    for_each = each.value.conditions
    content {
      dynamic "host_header" {
        for_each = condition.value.field == "host-header" ? [1] : []
        content {
          values = condition.value.values
        }
      }
      dynamic "path_pattern" {
        for_each = condition.value.field == "path-pattern" ? [1] : []
        content {
          values = condition.value.values
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
31.1.3 Conditionally Include Blocks

Use empty lists to conditionally include or exclude dynamic blocks. When for_each receives an empty collection, the block is omitted entirely.

Example: Optional Encryption Configuration

Conditionally add encryption block only when a KMS key is provided.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "kms_key_id" {
  description = "Optional KMS key for encryption"
  type        = string
  default     = null
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "this" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.main.id

  dynamic "rule" {
    for_each = var.kms_key_id != null ? [1] : []
    content {
      apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
        sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
        kms_master_key_id = var.kms_key_id
      }
      bucket_key_enabled = true
    }
  }

  # Default AES256 when no KMS key
  dynamic "rule" {
    for_each = var.kms_key_id == null ? [1] : []
    content {
      apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
        sse_algorithm = "AES256"
      }
    }
  }
}
31.1.4 Dynamic Block Performance

Best practices and performance considerations when using dynamic blocks at scale.

Consideration Recommendation Impact
Collection Size Keep under 100 items per dynamic block Plan time & readability
Nesting Depth Max 2 levels of nesting Debugging complexity
Readability Add comments explaining the iteration logic Team maintenance
Iterator Naming Use explicit iterator argument for clarity Code clarity
Alternatives Consider for_each on resources instead State granularity
⚠️ Exam Tip: Dynamic blocks only work within resource, data, provider, and provisioner blocks. They cannot be used at the top level of a module.

31.2 Complex Type Manipulation

PATTERN ADVANCED
📚 Official Documentation: Type Constraints | merge()

Advanced data structure transformations using for expressions, merge, lookup, flatten, and type conversion functions for production-grade variable processing.

💻 Practical Examples

31.2.1 Deep Merge Patterns

Combine default settings with environment-specific overrides using nested merge operations for multi-layer configuration.

Example: Multi-Layer Configuration Merge

Merge default, environment, and application-specific settings with override priority.

Terraform Configuration:

locals {
  # Default settings (lowest priority)
  defaults = {
    instance_type = "t3.micro"
    volume_size   = 20
    monitoring    = false
    tags = {
      ManagedBy = "terraform"
    }
  }

  # Environment overrides (medium priority)
  env_overrides = {
    dev = {
      instance_type = "t3.small"
      monitoring    = false
    }
    staging = {
      instance_type = "t3.medium"
      volume_size   = 50
      monitoring    = true
    }
    prod = {
      instance_type = "t3.large"
      volume_size   = 100
      monitoring    = true
    }
  }

  # Deep merge: defaults <- env overrides
  config = merge(
    local.defaults,
    lookup(local.env_overrides, var.environment, {})
  )

  # Merge tags separately (nested maps need special handling)
  merged_tags = merge(
    local.defaults.tags,
    {
      Environment = var.environment
      Project     = var.project_name
    }
  )
}
31.2.2 Object Transformation

Transform objects from one shape to another using for expressions, useful when API input formats differ from your variable structure.

Example: Transform User List to IAM Policy Map

Convert a flat user list into a structured map suitable for IAM user creation.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "team_members" {
  type = list(object({
    name       = string
    email      = string
    role       = string
    department = string
  }))
  default = [
    { name = "alice", email = "alice@co.com", role = "admin", department = "platform" },
    { name = "bob",   email = "bob@co.com",   role = "dev",   department = "backend" },
    { name = "carol", email = "carol@co.com", role = "admin", department = "security" },
  ]
}

locals {
  # Transform list to map keyed by name
  users_map = { for user in var.team_members : user.name => user }

  # Group by role
  users_by_role = {
    for role in distinct([for u in var.team_members : u.role]) :
    role => [for u in var.team_members : u.name if u.role == role]
  }

  # Group by department
  users_by_dept = {
    for dept in distinct([for u in var.team_members : u.department]) :
    dept => [for u in var.team_members : u.name if u.department == dept]
  }
}

# Output: users_by_role = { admin = ["alice", "carol"], dev = ["bob"] }
31.2.3 List to Map Conversion

Convert lists to maps for for_each compatibility — a common pattern since for_each requires a map or set, not a list.

Example: Converting Subnet List to Map

Transform a list of subnet definitions into a map keyed by availability zone for for_each usage.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "subnets" {
  type = list(object({
    az         = string
    cidr_block = string
    public     = bool
  }))
  default = [
    { az = "us-east-1a", cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24", public = true },
    { az = "us-east-1b", cidr_block = "10.0.2.0/24", public = true },
    { az = "us-east-1a", cidr_block = "10.0.10.0/24", public = false },
    { az = "us-east-1b", cidr_block = "10.0.11.0/24", public = false },
  ]
}

locals {
  # Convert to map keyed by CIDR (guaranteed unique)
  subnet_map = { for s in var.subnets : s.cidr_block => s }

  # Filter: only public subnets
  public_subnets = {
    for cidr, s in local.subnet_map : cidr => s if s.public
  }

  # Filter: only private subnets
  private_subnets = {
    for cidr, s in local.subnet_map : cidr => s if !s.public
  }
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  for_each          = local.public_subnets
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = each.key
  availability_zone = each.value.az
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true

  tags = { Name = "public-${each.value.az}" }
}
31.2.4 Flattening Nested Structures

The flatten() function combined with for expressions handles multi-dimensional data structures, turning nested lists into flat collections for resource creation.

Example: Flatten VPC + Subnet Configuration

Create subnets across multiple VPCs using flatten to generate a flat list of VPC-subnet pairs.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "vpcs" {
  type = map(object({
    cidr_block = string
    subnets = map(object({
      cidr_block = string
      az         = string
    }))
  }))
  default = {
    app = {
      cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
      subnets = {
        web = { cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24", az = "us-east-1a" }
        api = { cidr_block = "10.0.2.0/24", az = "us-east-1b" }
      }
    }
    data = {
      cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"
      subnets = {
        db  = { cidr_block = "10.1.1.0/24", az = "us-east-1a" }
        cache = { cidr_block = "10.1.2.0/24", az = "us-east-1b" }
      }
    }
  }
}

locals {
  # Flatten nested VPC->subnet into flat list
  flat_subnets = flatten([
    for vpc_key, vpc in var.vpcs : [
      for subnet_key, subnet in vpc.subnets : {
        vpc_key    = vpc_key
        subnet_key = subnet_key
        vpc_cidr   = vpc.cidr_block
        cidr_block = subnet.cidr_block
        az         = subnet.az
      }
    ]
  ])

  # Convert to map for for_each
  subnet_map = {
    for s in local.flat_subnets :
    "${s.vpc_key}-${s.subnet_key}" => s
  }
}

resource "aws_subnet" "all" {
  for_each          = local.subnet_map
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.vpcs[each.value.vpc_key].id
  cidr_block        = each.value.cidr_block
  availability_zone = each.value.az

  tags = {
    Name = "${each.value.vpc_key}-${each.value.subnet_key}"
    VPC  = each.value.vpc_key
  }
}

31.3 Null Resources

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: null_resource | terraform_data

Null resources and their modern replacement (terraform_data) provide escape hatches for running provisioners, triggering actions, and orchestrating dependencies that don't map to real infrastructure resources.

💻 Practical Examples

31.3.1 null_resource Usage

The null_resource implements standard resource lifecycle but creates no real infrastructure. It serves as a container for provisioners and triggers.

Example: Post-Deployment Script Execution

Run a deployment script after infrastructure is created, with dependency on EC2 instance.

Terraform Configuration:

resource "null_resource" "post_deploy" {
  # Re-run when instance changes
  triggers = {
    instance_id = aws_instance.app.id
  }

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "bash scripts/configure-app.sh ${aws_instance.app.private_ip}"
  }

  provisioner "remote-exec" {
    inline = [
      "sudo systemctl restart nginx",
      "sudo systemctl status nginx",
    ]

    connection {
      type        = "ssh"
      user        = "ec2-user"
      private_key = file("~/.ssh/deploy-key.pem")
      host        = aws_instance.app.private_ip
    }
  }

  depends_on = [aws_instance.app]
}
31.3.2 Triggers

Triggers are a map of values that, when changed, cause the null_resource to be replaced (destroyed and recreated), re-running all provisioners.

Example: Trigger on Configuration Changes

Re-run configuration script when application version, config hash, or deployment timestamp changes.

Terraform Configuration:

resource "null_resource" "app_config" {
  triggers = {
    # Re-run when app version changes
    app_version = var.app_version

    # Re-run when config file changes
    config_hash = filemd5("configs/app.conf")

    # Force re-run every apply (use sparingly!)
    # always_run = timestamp()
  }

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command     = "ansible-playbook -i inventory deploy.yml"
    working_dir = "${path.module}/ansible"
    environment = {
      APP_VERSION = var.app_version
      ENVIRONMENT = var.environment
    }
  }
}
31.3.3 Use Cases (Orchestration)

Common orchestration patterns with null_resource:

Use Case Pattern When to Use
Database Migration local-exec after RDS creation Schema setup on first deploy
DNS Verification Wait for ACM validation Certificate provisioning
Ansible Integration Run playbook post-provision Complex app configuration
API Calls curl/wget in local-exec Webhooks, notifications
Dependency Ordering depends_on chain Multi-step provisioning
31.3.4 terraform_data (Replacement)

Since Terraform 1.4+, terraform_data is the built-in replacement for null_resource — no external provider needed. It supports input and output attributes plus triggers_replace.

Example: Modern terraform_data Resource

Replace null_resource with the built-in terraform_data managed resource.

Terraform Configuration:

# Modern approach (Terraform 1.4+)
resource "terraform_data" "bootstrap" {
  input = {
    instance_ip = aws_instance.app.private_ip
    app_version = var.app_version
  }

  # Replaces triggers — recreate when these change
  triggers_replace = [
    aws_instance.app.id,
    var.app_version,
  ]

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = "bash scripts/bootstrap.sh"
    environment = {
      TARGET_IP   = self.input.instance_ip
      APP_VERSION = self.input.app_version
    }
  }
}

# Access stored values
output "bootstrap_data" {
  value = terraform_data.bootstrap.output
}
💡 Migration: Replace null_resource + triggers with terraform_data + triggers_replace. The input/output attributes allow storing and passing data between resources without workarounds.

31.4 External Data Sources

DATA SOURCE ADVANCED
📚 Official Documentation: external Data Source

The external data source invokes an external program and reads its JSON output, enabling integration with custom scripts, APIs, and tools that Terraform cannot natively manage.

💻 Practical Examples

31.4.1 external Provider

The external provider executes a program and expects JSON output to stdout. The program must accept JSON input on stdin and return a flat map of string values.

Example: Fetch Latest AMI via Script

Use a Python script to query the AWS API for the latest custom AMI matching specific criteria.

Terraform Configuration:

data "external" "latest_ami" {
  program = ["python3", "${path.module}/scripts/get_ami.py"]

  query = {
    region    = var.aws_region
    name_filter = "my-app-*"
    owner     = "self"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.external.latest_ami.result["ami_id"]
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  tags = {
    Name      = "app-server"
    AMI_Name  = data.external.latest_ami.result["ami_name"]
    AMI_Date  = data.external.latest_ami.result["creation_date"]
  }
}
31.4.2 Script Requirements

External scripts must follow strict rules for Terraform compatibility:

Requirement Details
Input Read JSON from stdin
Output Write JSON to stdout
Output Format Flat map — all values must be strings
Errors Write to stderr and exit non-zero
Idempotency Must produce same output for same input
Side Effects Should be read-only (no modifications)
31.4.3 JSON Input/Output

The script receives query parameters as JSON on stdin and must return a flat JSON object on stdout.

Example: Python Script for External Data

A compliant Python script that reads JSON input, queries an API, and returns JSON output.

scripts/get_ami.py:

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""External data source script for Terraform."""
import json
import sys
import subprocess

def main():
    # Read input from stdin
    input_data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    region = input_data.get("region", "us-east-1")
    name_filter = input_data.get("name_filter", "*")

    try:
        # Query AWS CLI (example)
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["aws", "ec2", "describe-images",
             "--region", region,
             "--owners", "self",
             "--filters", f"Name=name,Values={name_filter}",
             "--query", "sort_by(Images, &CreationDate)[-1]",
             "--output", "json"],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
        )
        ami = json.loads(result.stdout)

        # Output must be flat map of strings
        output = {
            "ami_id": ami["ImageId"],
            "ami_name": ami["Name"],
            "creation_date": ami["CreationDate"]
        }
        json.dump(output, sys.stdout)

    except Exception as e:
        print(str(e), file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
31.4.4 Security Considerations

External data sources introduce security risks since they execute arbitrary code during plan/apply:

🔒 Security Checklist:
  • Never pass secrets through query — they appear in state
  • Validate all input in the script (never trust Terraform variables blindly)
  • Pin script dependencies to specific versions
  • Use absolute paths for executables to prevent PATH injection
  • Review scripts in code review — they run with Terraform's permissions
  • Prefer native data sources over external when available

31.5 Template Files

FUNCTION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: templatefile() | String Templates

The templatefile() function reads a file and renders it as a template, replacing interpolation sequences with provided variable values — ideal for generating config files, user data scripts, and policy documents.

💻 Practical Examples

31.5.1 templatefile() Function

Basic syntax: templatefile(path, vars) where path is relative to the module root and vars is a map of template variables.

Example: EC2 User Data from Template

Generate a cloud-init user data script with environment-specific values.

templates/user_data.sh.tpl:

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Configure application
echo "Environment: ${environment}" > /etc/app/config
echo "DB_HOST=${db_host}" >> /etc/app/config
echo "DB_PORT=${db_port}" >> /etc/app/config
echo "APP_VERSION=${app_version}" >> /etc/app/config

# Install packages
yum update -y
yum install -y docker htop

# Start services
systemctl enable docker
systemctl start docker

# Pull and run application
docker pull myregistry/${app_name}:${app_version}
docker run -d --name app \
  -p 80:8080 \
  -e DB_HOST=${db_host} \
  myregistry/${app_name}:${app_version}

Terraform Configuration:

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  user_data = templatefile("${path.module}/templates/user_data.sh.tpl", {
    environment = var.environment
    db_host     = aws_db_instance.main.address
    db_port     = aws_db_instance.main.port
    app_name    = var.app_name
    app_version = var.app_version
  })

  tags = { Name = "${var.environment}-app" }
}
31.5.2 Template Syntax

Templates support interpolation (${...}), directives (%{...}), loops, and conditionals:

Syntax Purpose Example
${var} Variable interpolation ${hostname}
%{if cond}...%{endif} Conditional %{if enable_ssl}ssl on%{endif}
%{for x in list}...%{endfor} Loop %{for s in servers}${s}%{endfor}
~ (tilde) Strip whitespace %{~ if cond ~}
31.5.3 Complex Templates

Templates with loops and conditionals enable dynamic configuration file generation.

Example: Nginx Configuration Template

Generate nginx.conf with dynamic upstream servers and optional SSL.

templates/nginx.conf.tpl:

upstream backend {
%{~ for server in backend_servers }
    server ${server.host}:${server.port} weight=${server.weight};
%{~ endfor }
}

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name ${domain};

%{~ if enable_ssl }
    listen 443 ssl;
    ssl_certificate     /etc/ssl/${domain}.crt;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/${domain}.key;

    # Redirect HTTP to HTTPS
    if ($scheme != "https") {
        return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
    }
%{~ endif }

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://backend;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
    }

%{~ for path, upstream in custom_routes }
    location ${path} {
        proxy_pass http://${upstream};
    }
%{~ endfor }
}
31.5.4 YAML/JSON Generation

Use yamlencode() and jsonencode() within templates for structured data output, or generate YAML/JSON files from Terraform variables.

Example: Kubernetes ConfigMap from Template

Generate a Kubernetes YAML manifest using templatefile.

Terraform Configuration:

resource "local_file" "k8s_config" {
  filename = "${path.module}/manifests/configmap.yaml"
  content = templatefile("${path.module}/templates/configmap.yaml.tpl", {
    namespace   = var.namespace
    app_name    = var.app_name
    config_data = var.config_map_data
  })
}

# Alternative: Use yamlencode directly
resource "local_file" "k8s_config_v2" {
  filename = "${path.module}/manifests/configmap-v2.yaml"
  content = yamlencode({
    apiVersion = "v1"
    kind       = "ConfigMap"
    metadata = {
      name      = "${var.app_name}-config"
      namespace = var.namespace
    }
    data = var.config_map_data
  })
}

31.6 Conditional Resource Creation

PATTERN 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: count | for_each

Conditionally create or skip resources based on variables, enabling feature flags and environment-specific infrastructure without separate configurations.

💻 Practical Examples

31.6.1 count = condition ? 1 : 0

The classic conditional creation pattern: set count to 1 (create) or 0 (skip) based on a boolean expression.

Example: Optional CloudWatch Alarms

Create monitoring alarms only in production environment.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "environment" {
  type = string
}

variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type    = bool
  default = true
}

# Only create alarm in production OR when explicitly enabled
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "cpu_high" {
  count = var.environment == "prod" || var.enable_monitoring ? 1 : 0

  alarm_name          = "${var.environment}-cpu-high"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  namespace           = "AWS/EC2"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Average"
  threshold           = 80

  dimensions = {
    InstanceId = aws_instance.app.id
  }

  alarm_actions = [aws_sns_topic.alerts[0].arn]
}

# Reference conditional resource safely
output "alarm_arn" {
  value = length(aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm.cpu_high) > 0 ? aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm.cpu_high[0].arn : null
}
31.6.2 for_each with Conditions

Use for expressions with if clauses inside for_each to selectively create resources from a filtered collection.

Example: Create Resources for Active Services Only

Only create load balancer target groups for services marked as active.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "services" {
  type = map(object({
    port     = number
    protocol = string
    active   = bool
    health_check_path = string
  }))
  default = {
    api     = { port = 8080, protocol = "HTTP",  active = true,  health_check_path = "/health" }
    admin   = { port = 8081, protocol = "HTTP",  active = true,  health_check_path = "/status" }
    legacy  = { port = 9090, protocol = "HTTP",  active = false, health_check_path = "/ping" }
    grpc    = { port = 50051, protocol = "HTTP", active = true,  health_check_path = "/grpc.health" }
  }
}

resource "aws_lb_target_group" "service" {
  # Only create for active services
  for_each = { for k, v in var.services : k => v if v.active }

  name     = "${var.environment}-${each.key}"
  port     = each.value.port
  protocol = each.value.protocol
  vpc_id   = aws_vpc.main.id

  health_check {
    path = each.value.health_check_path
    port = each.value.port
  }
}
31.6.3 Conditional Module Calls

The same count pattern works on module blocks to conditionally include entire modules.

Example: Optional WAF Module

Include WAF protection only for production and staging environments.

Terraform Configuration:

module "waf" {
  source = "./modules/waf"
  count  = contains(["prod", "staging"], var.environment) ? 1 : 0

  name_prefix     = var.environment
  alb_arn         = module.alb.alb_arn
  rate_limit      = var.environment == "prod" ? 2000 : 500
  blocked_countries = var.blocked_countries
}

# Safe reference to conditional module
resource "aws_wafv2_web_acl_association" "alb" {
  count = contains(["prod", "staging"], var.environment) ? 1 : 0

  resource_arn = module.alb.alb_arn
  web_acl_arn  = module.waf[0].web_acl_arn
}
⚠️ Important: When referencing conditional resources/modules (count = 0 or 1), always use index [0] and guard with length() > 0 or a ternary to avoid index-out-of-range errors.

31.7 Resource Iteration Patterns

PATTERN 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: count | for_each

Choosing the right iteration strategy is critical for state stability. This section provides decision matrices and patterns for count, for_each with lists, maps, and nested structures.

💻 Practical Examples

31.7.1 count vs for_each Decision Matrix

Choose the right meta-argument based on your data structure and state requirements:

Criteria count for_each
State Key Numeric index [0], [1] Map key ["name"]
Reordering ⚠️ Causes destroy/recreate ✅ Safe — keyed by name
Input Type Number Map or set of strings
Conditional ✅ count = 0 or 1 ✅ Empty map = skip
Best For Identical resources, toggles Named resources, varied config
Recommendation Only for on/off flags ✅ Default choice
31.7.2 List Iteration

Convert lists to sets or maps before using with for_each, since for_each requires a map or set — not a list.

Example: Create IAM Users from List

Convert a list of usernames to a set for safe for_each iteration.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "usernames" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["alice", "bob", "carol", "dave"]
}

# ✅ Convert list to set
resource "aws_iam_user" "team" {
  for_each = toset(var.usernames)
  name     = each.value

  tags = {
    ManagedBy = "terraform"
  }
}

# Reference: aws_iam_user.team["alice"].arn
output "user_arns" {
  value = { for name, user in aws_iam_user.team : name => user.arn }
}
31.7.3 Map Iteration

Maps are the natural input type for for_each, providing both a key (for state addressing) and value (for configuration).

Example: S3 Buckets with Different Configurations

Create multiple S3 buckets with varied settings from a map variable.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "buckets" {
  type = map(object({
    versioning   = bool
    lifecycle_days = number
    public       = bool
  }))
  default = {
    logs = {
      versioning     = false
      lifecycle_days = 30
      public         = false
    }
    assets = {
      versioning     = true
      lifecycle_days = 365
      public         = true
    }
    backups = {
      versioning     = true
      lifecycle_days = 90
      public         = false
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "managed" {
  for_each = var.buckets
  bucket   = "${var.project}-${each.key}-${var.environment}"

  tags = {
    Name    = each.key
    Public  = each.value.public
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "managed" {
  for_each = { for k, v in var.buckets : k => v if v.versioning }
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.managed[each.key].id

  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}
31.7.4 Nested Iteration

Use flatten() + for expressions to iterate over nested structures, creating a flat map of composite keys.

Example: Security Group Rules Across Multiple Groups

Create security group rules for multiple groups, each with their own rule sets.

Terraform Configuration:

variable "security_groups" {
  type = map(object({
    description = string
    rules = map(object({
      type        = string
      from_port   = number
      to_port     = number
      protocol    = string
      cidr_blocks = list(string)
    }))
  }))
}

locals {
  # Flatten: sg_name + rule_name => rule config
  sg_rules = flatten([
    for sg_name, sg in var.security_groups : [
      for rule_name, rule in sg.rules : {
        key         = "${sg_name}-${rule_name}"
        sg_name     = sg_name
        rule_name   = rule_name
        type        = rule.type
        from_port   = rule.from_port
        to_port     = rule.to_port
        protocol    = rule.protocol
        cidr_blocks = rule.cidr_blocks
      }
    ]
  ])
}

resource "aws_security_group_rule" "all" {
  for_each = { for r in local.sg_rules : r.key => r }

  security_group_id = aws_security_group.groups[each.value.sg_name].id
  type              = each.value.type
  from_port         = each.value.from_port
  to_port           = each.value.to_port
  protocol          = each.value.protocol
  cidr_blocks       = each.value.cidr_blocks
}

31.8 Cross-Module References

PATTERN ADVANCED
📚 Official Documentation: terraform_remote_state | Data Sources

Share data between independently managed Terraform configurations using remote state references, data source queries, and parameter store pass-through patterns.

💻 Practical Examples

31.8.1 terraform_remote_state

Read outputs from another Terraform configuration's state file. The producing configuration must define outputs; the consuming configuration reads them via the terraform_remote_state data source.

Example: Reference Network Stack Outputs

An application stack reads VPC and subnet IDs from the network stack's remote state.

Network Stack (outputs):

# network/outputs.tf
output "vpc_id" {
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  value = aws_subnet.public[*].id
}

Application Stack (consumer):

# app/data.tf
data "terraform_remote_state" "network" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "mycompany-terraform-state"
    key    = "network/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Use remote state outputs
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.latest.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  subnet_id     = data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.private_subnet_ids[0]

  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.app.id]
}

resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
  vpc_id = data.terraform_remote_state.network.outputs.vpc_id
  # ...rules...
}
31.8.2 Data Source Queries

Instead of remote state, use native AWS data sources to look up resources by tags or attributes — more loosely coupled.

Example: Look Up VPC by Tags

Query existing VPC and subnets by tags instead of hardcoding remote state paths.

Terraform Configuration:

# Look up VPC by tag (no remote state needed)
data "aws_vpc" "main" {
  filter {
    name   = "tag:Environment"
    values = [var.environment]
  }
  filter {
    name   = "tag:Project"
    values = [var.project_name]
  }
}

data "aws_subnets" "private" {
  filter {
    name   = "vpc-id"
    values = [data.aws_vpc.main.id]
  }
  tags = {
    Tier = "private"
  }
}

# Use discovered resources
resource "aws_lb" "app" {
  name               = "${var.project_name}-alb"
  internal           = false
  load_balancer_type = "application"
  subnets            = data.aws_subnets.private.ids
}
31.8.3 SSM Parameter Pass-Through

Store outputs in AWS SSM Parameter Store for cross-stack sharing — more durable and doesn't require state access permissions.

Example: SSM-Based Cross-Stack Communication

The network stack writes VPC ID to SSM; the app stack reads it.

Producer Stack:

# network/ssm.tf — Write outputs to SSM
resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "vpc_id" {
  name  = "/${var.environment}/network/vpc_id"
  type  = "String"
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
  tags  = { ManagedBy = "terraform-network" }
}

resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "private_subnets" {
  name  = "/${var.environment}/network/private_subnet_ids"
  type  = "StringList"
  value = join(",", aws_subnet.private[*].id)
}

Consumer Stack:

# app/data.tf — Read from SSM
data "aws_ssm_parameter" "vpc_id" {
  name = "/${var.environment}/network/vpc_id"
}

data "aws_ssm_parameter" "private_subnets" {
  name = "/${var.environment}/network/private_subnet_ids"
}

locals {
  vpc_id             = data.aws_ssm_parameter.vpc_id.value
  private_subnet_ids = split(",", data.aws_ssm_parameter.private_subnets.value)
}
💡 Comparison: Remote state gives direct access but creates tight coupling. Data sources are loosely coupled but may return ambiguous results. SSM is the most decoupled approach — works across accounts and requires no state access.

31.9 Resource Targeting

CLI 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Resource Targeting

The -target flag limits plan/apply operations to specific resources and their dependencies. While powerful for debugging, it can create state drift when misused.

💻 Practical Examples

31.9.1 -target Use Cases

Legitimate use cases for resource targeting:

Example: Targeted Plan and Apply

Target specific resources during recovery or debugging scenarios.

CLI Commands:

# Target a single resource
terraform plan -target=aws_instance.app

# Target a module
terraform plan -target=module.vpc

# Target multiple resources
terraform apply \
  -target=aws_security_group.app \
  -target=aws_instance.app

# Target a specific instance in for_each
terraform plan -target='aws_s3_bucket.managed["logs"]'

# Target a specific count index
terraform plan -target='aws_subnet.public[0]'
31.9.2 Targeted Applies in CI/CD

Targeted applies in CI/CD should be rare and require explicit approval — they bypass the full dependency graph check.

Example: Emergency Targeted Deploy Pipeline

A pipeline that allows targeted applies only with manual approval and audit logging.

GitHub Actions:

# .github/workflows/emergency-deploy.yml
name: Emergency Targeted Deploy
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      target_resource:
        description: 'Resource address to target'
        required: true
      reason:
        description: 'Reason for targeted deploy'
        required: true

jobs:
  targeted-apply:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production  # Requires approval
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Audit Log
        run: |
          echo "TARGETED APPLY by ${{ github.actor }}"
          echo "Target: ${{ inputs.target_resource }}"
          echo "Reason: ${{ inputs.reason }}"

      - name: Terraform Plan (Targeted)
        run: |
          terraform init
          terraform plan \
            -target='${{ inputs.target_resource }}' \
            -out=targeted.tfplan

      - name: Terraform Apply (Targeted)
        run: terraform apply targeted.tfplan
31.9.3 Dangers and Best Practices

Resource targeting risks and mitigations:

⚠️ Dangers of -target:
  • State drift: Other resources may depend on the targeted resource but won't be updated
  • Partial state: Outputs and data sources may contain stale values
  • Hidden breakage: Subsequent non-targeted applies may show unexpected changes
  • Team confusion: Others won't know that a partial apply occurred
Best Practice Why
Always run a full plan after targeted apply Detect and resolve any drift created
Document why you used -target Team awareness and audit trail
Use for debugging only, not routine operations Prevents hidden state inconsistencies
Prefer state decomposition over targeting Permanent solution vs. one-off workaround
Never use in automated pipelines without approval Prevent accidental partial applies
🌍 🔀 📂 🚀
CHAPTER 32

Managing Multiple Environments

Environment Strategies Directory-Based Workspaces Variable Files Terragrunt Backend Config Promotion
7 Sections
21 Subsections
20+ Code Examples
5 Strategies
PART 9 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

Explore strategies for managing dev, staging, and production environments with Terraform — from directory-based isolation to workspaces, tfvars, Terragrunt, and GitOps promotion workflows.

Learning Objectives

  • Compare directory-based, workspace, tfvars, and Terragrunt strategies
  • Implement directory-based environment isolation
  • Use workspaces for lightweight environment switching
  • Configure per-environment variable files and auto-loading
  • Set up Terragrunt for DRY multi-environment management
  • Manage backend configuration per environment
  • Design GitOps promotion workflows with approval gates

Chapter Contents

32.1 Environment Strategies Comparison 32.2 Directory-Based Environments 32.3 Workspace-Based Environments 32.4 Variable File-Based Environments 32.5 Terragrunt for Environments 32.6 Environment-Specific Backend Configuration 32.7 Promoting Changes Between Environments

32.1 Environment Strategies Comparison

STRATEGY 📝 EXAM

Choose the right environment management strategy based on team size, infrastructure complexity, and operational requirements.

💻 Strategy Analysis

32.1.1 Strategy Matrix

Overview of the four primary environment management approaches:

Strategy Isolation DRY Code Complexity Best For
Directory-Based 🟢 Full 🔴 Low (duplication) 🟢 Low Small teams, simple infra
Workspaces 🟡 Shared code 🟢 High 🟢 Low Identical environments
Variable Files 🟡 Shared code 🟢 High 🟡 Medium Medium teams, varied config
Terragrunt 🟢 Full 🟢 High 🔴 High Large teams, complex infra
32.1.2 Pros and Cons
Strategy ✅ Pros ❌ Cons
Directories Complete isolation, easy to understand, independent applies Code duplication, hard to keep in sync
Workspaces No duplication, built-in to Terraform, easy switching Same code for all envs, limited env-specific config, shared state backend
Tfvars Single codebase, env-specific tuning, CI/CD friendly Need careful variable design, state management complexity
Terragrunt DRY + isolation, dependency management, auto backend config Extra tooling, learning curve, not HashiCorp native
32.1.3 Scale Considerations

Decision framework based on organizational scale:

📊 Recommendations by Scale:
  • Solo / Small team (1-3): Workspaces or tfvars — simplicity wins
  • Medium team (4-10): Directory-based or tfvars with CI/CD
  • Large team (10+): Terragrunt or Terraform Cloud workspaces
  • Enterprise (50+): Terragrunt + module registry + policy-as-code

32.2 Directory-Based Environments

STRATEGY

Each environment gets its own directory with independent state, providers, and configurations. The simplest approach with the strongest isolation guarantees.

💻 Practical Examples

32.2.1 Structure Pattern

Typical directory layout for environment-per-folder approach:

Example: Directory Structure

Each environment is a complete, independent Terraform root module.

infrastructure/
├── modules/                    # Shared modules
│   ├── vpc/
│   ├── ecs-service/
│   └── rds/
├── environments/
│   ├── dev/
│   │   ├── main.tf            # Module calls
│   │   ├── variables.tf       # Variable declarations
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars   # Dev-specific values
│   │   ├── outputs.tf
│   │   └── backend.tf         # Dev state config
│   ├── staging/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars   # Staging-specific values
│   │   ├── outputs.tf
│   │   └── backend.tf
│   └── prod/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       ├── terraform.tfvars   # Prod-specific values
│       ├── outputs.tf
│       └── backend.tf
└── scripts/
    └── deploy.sh
32.2.2 Shared Modules

Reduce duplication by extracting common infrastructure into shared modules that each environment calls with different parameters.

Example: Environment Calling Shared Modules

Dev environment using shared VPC and ECS modules with dev-specific sizing.

environments/dev/main.tf:

module "vpc" {
  source = "../../modules/vpc"

  environment    = "dev"
  vpc_cidr       = "10.0.0.0/16"
  azs            = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]
  public_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.10.0/24", "10.0.11.0/24"]
  
  enable_nat_gateway = false  # Cost saving for dev
}

module "ecs_service" {
  source = "../../modules/ecs-service"

  environment    = "dev"
  vpc_id         = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids     = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids
  instance_type  = "t3.small"      # Smaller for dev
  desired_count  = 1               # Single instance
  min_capacity   = 1
  max_capacity   = 2
}
32.2.3 CI/CD Configuration

CI/CD pipelines target each environment directory independently.

Example: GitHub Actions per Environment

Deploy to specific environment based on branch or manual trigger.

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy Infrastructure
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['environments/**']
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        type: choice
        options: [dev, staging, prod]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        environment: ${{ github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' 
          && fromJSON(format('["{0}"]', inputs.environment))
          || fromJSON('["dev"]') }}
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: environments/${{ matrix.environment }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: terraform init
      - run: terraform plan -out=tfplan
      - run: terraform apply tfplan
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'

32.3 Workspace-Based Environments

STRATEGY 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Workspaces

Terraform workspaces provide named state instances within a single configuration, enabling environment switching with terraform workspace select.

💻 Practical Examples

32.3.1 When to Use Workspaces

Workspaces work best when environments share identical infrastructure structure with only sizing/count differences.

✅ Good Fit: Same resources across all envs, differing only in size/count
❌ Bad Fit: Environments with different resource types (e.g., prod has WAF, dev doesn't)
32.3.2 Workspace Naming

Use terraform.workspace to drive environment-specific configuration.

Example: Workspace-Driven Configuration

Use the workspace name to select instance sizes, counts, and feature flags.

# Create and switch workspaces
terraform workspace new dev
terraform workspace new staging
terraform workspace new prod
terraform workspace select dev
terraform workspace list

Terraform Configuration:

locals {
  env_config = {
    dev = {
      instance_type  = "t3.small"
      desired_count  = 1
      enable_waf     = false
      db_instance    = "db.t3.micro"
    }
    staging = {
      instance_type  = "t3.medium"
      desired_count  = 2
      enable_waf     = true
      db_instance    = "db.t3.small"
    }
    prod = {
      instance_type  = "t3.large"
      desired_count  = 3
      enable_waf     = true
      db_instance    = "db.r6g.large"
    }
  }

  config = local.env_config[terraform.workspace]
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = local.config.instance_type

  tags = {
    Name        = "${terraform.workspace}-app"
    Environment = terraform.workspace
  }
}
32.3.3 Environment Variables

Set TF_WORKSPACE in CI/CD to automatically select the workspace without manual commands.

Example: Workspace with S3 Backend

S3 backend automatically creates workspace-prefixed state keys.

# Each workspace gets: env:/<workspace>/app/terraform.tfstate
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "mycompany-terraform-state"
    key            = "app/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}

# State paths:
# default  → app/terraform.tfstate
# dev      → env:/dev/app/terraform.tfstate
# staging  → env:/staging/app/terraform.tfstate
# prod     → env:/prod/app/terraform.tfstate

32.4 Variable File-Based Environments

STRATEGY

Use per-environment .tfvars files with a single codebase, selecting the right file at plan/apply time via -var-file.

💻 Practical Examples

32.4.1 Environment tfvars

Maintain separate .tfvars files for each environment containing only the values that differ.

Example: Per-Environment Variable Files

Single codebase with environment-specific variable files.

Project Structure:

infrastructure/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── backend.tf
└── envs/
    ├── dev.tfvars
    ├── staging.tfvars
    └── prod.tfvars

envs/dev.tfvars:

environment    = "dev"
instance_type  = "t3.small"
desired_count  = 1
enable_waf     = false
db_instance    = "db.t3.micro"
enable_backup  = false
domain_name    = "dev.example.com"

envs/prod.tfvars:

environment    = "prod"
instance_type  = "t3.large"
desired_count  = 3
enable_waf     = true
db_instance    = "db.r6g.large"
enable_backup  = true
domain_name    = "example.com"

Usage:

# Plan for dev
terraform plan -var-file=envs/dev.tfvars

# Apply for production
terraform apply -var-file=envs/prod.tfvars
32.4.2 Auto-loading Patterns

Terraform automatically loads terraform.tfvars and *.auto.tfvars files. Use symlinks or CI/CD to select the right one.

Example: Makefile Wrapper

Use a Makefile to simplify environment-specific commands.

# Makefile
ENV ?= dev

init:
	terraform init -backend-config=backends/$(ENV).hcl

plan:
	terraform plan -var-file=envs/$(ENV).tfvars -out=$(ENV).tfplan

apply:
	terraform apply $(ENV).tfplan

destroy:
	terraform destroy -var-file=envs/$(ENV).tfvars

# Usage:
# make plan ENV=staging
# make apply ENV=staging
32.4.3 CI/CD Integration

Pass the environment name to CI/CD pipelines to select the correct tfvars and backend config.

Example: GitLab CI with tfvars

Deploy to different environments using the same pipeline with different variable files.

# .gitlab-ci.yml
stages: [validate, plan, apply]

.terraform_base:
  image: hashicorp/terraform:1.7
  before_script:
    - terraform init -backend-config=backends/${CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME}.hcl

plan:
  extends: .terraform_base
  stage: plan
  environment:
    name: $ENVIRONMENT
  script:
    - terraform plan -var-file=envs/${CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME}.tfvars -out=plan.tfplan
  artifacts:
    paths: [plan.tfplan]

apply:
  extends: .terraform_base
  stage: apply
  environment:
    name: $ENVIRONMENT
  script:
    - terraform apply plan.tfplan
  when: manual  # Require manual approval
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == "main"

32.5 Terragrunt for Environments

TOOL
📚 Official Documentation: Terragrunt Docs

Terragrunt wraps Terraform to provide DRY configurations, automatic backend setup, and dependency orchestration across environments — giving you both full isolation and zero duplication.

💻 Practical Examples

32.5.1 terragrunt.hcl Hierarchy

Terragrunt uses a hierarchy of terragrunt.hcl files. Child configs inherit from parents using include.

Example: Terragrunt Project Layout

One set of Terraform modules, multiple environment configs referencing them.

infrastructure/
├── terragrunt.hcl          # Root config (backend, provider)
├── modules/                # Terraform modules (source of truth)
│   ├── vpc/
│   ├── ecs/
│   └── rds/
├── dev/
│   ├── env.hcl             # Dev-specific variables
│   ├── vpc/
│   │   └── terragrunt.hcl  # Points to modules/vpc
│   ├── ecs/
│   │   └── terragrunt.hcl  # Points to modules/ecs
│   └── rds/
│       └── terragrunt.hcl  # Points to modules/rds
├── staging/
│   ├── env.hcl
│   ├── vpc/terragrunt.hcl
│   ├── ecs/terragrunt.hcl
│   └── rds/terragrunt.hcl
└── prod/
    ├── env.hcl
    ├── vpc/terragrunt.hcl
    ├── ecs/terragrunt.hcl
    └── rds/terragrunt.hcl

Root terragrunt.hcl:

# infrastructure/terragrunt.hcl
remote_state {
  backend = "s3"
  generate = {
    path      = "backend.tf"
    if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  }
  config = {
    bucket         = "mycompany-terraform-state"
    key            = "${path_relative_to_include()}/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}

generate "provider" {
  path      = "provider.tf"
  if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  contents  = <<EOF
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      ManagedBy = "terraform"
    }
  }
}
EOF
}
32.5.2 Environment Inheritance

Child configs include the root and merge environment-specific variables.

Example: Child terragrunt.hcl with Dependencies

ECS service in dev inheriting root config and referencing VPC output.

dev/env.hcl:

locals {
  environment   = "dev"
  instance_type = "t3.small"
  desired_count = 1
}

dev/ecs/terragrunt.hcl:

include "root" {
  path = find_in_parent_folders()
}

locals {
  env_vars = read_terragrunt_config(find_in_parent_folders("env.hcl"))
}

terraform {
  source = "../../modules/ecs"
}

dependency "vpc" {
  config_path = "../vpc"
  mock_outputs = {
    vpc_id             = "vpc-mock"
    private_subnet_ids = ["subnet-mock1", "subnet-mock2"]
  }
}

inputs = {
  environment   = local.env_vars.locals.environment
  vpc_id        = dependency.vpc.outputs.vpc_id
  subnet_ids    = dependency.vpc.outputs.private_subnet_ids
  instance_type = local.env_vars.locals.instance_type
  desired_count = local.env_vars.locals.desired_count
}
32.5.3 Input Variables

Terragrunt inputs block passes variables to the underlying Terraform module.

Example: Terragrunt Commands

Apply all modules in an environment or target specific ones.

# Apply all modules in dev (respects dependency order)
cd infrastructure/dev
terragrunt run-all apply

# Apply only the ECS module
cd infrastructure/dev/ecs
terragrunt apply

# Plan all modules in staging
cd infrastructure/staging
terragrunt run-all plan

# Destroy in reverse dependency order
cd infrastructure/dev
terragrunt run-all destroy

32.6 Environment-Specific Backend Configuration

BACKEND

Isolate environment state using separate backend configurations — different state file keys, separate S3 buckets, or distinct accounts — to prevent accidental cross-environment modifications.

💻 Practical Examples

32.6.1 Backend per Environment

Use partial backend configuration with per-environment .hcl files.

Example: Partial Backend Config

Separate backend files for each environment passed during terraform init.

backend.tf (shared):

terraform {
  backend "s3" {}
}

backends/dev.hcl:

bucket         = "mycompany-tf-state-dev"
key            = "app/terraform.tfstate"
region         = "us-east-1"
dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks-dev"
encrypt        = true

backends/prod.hcl:

bucket         = "mycompany-tf-state-prod"
key            = "app/terraform.tfstate"
region         = "us-east-1"
dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks-prod"
encrypt        = true
role_arn       = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformProdRole"

Usage:

# Initialize with the right backend
terraform init -backend-config=backends/dev.hcl

# Switch environments (requires re-init)
terraform init -backend-config=backends/prod.hcl -reconfigure
32.6.2 Partial Configuration

Combine static and dynamic partial configurations to keep secrets out of code.

Example: Multi-Account Backend

Production uses a separate AWS account for state storage.

Environment Account State Bucket Lock Table
dev 111111111111 dev-tf-state dev-tf-locks
staging 222222222222 stg-tf-state stg-tf-locks
prod 333333333333 prod-tf-state prod-tf-locks
32.6.3 CI/CD Secrets

Pass backend credentials via environment variables in CI/CD — never commit secrets to version control.

Example: CI/CD Backend Init with Secrets

Using environment variables and OIDC for secure backend access.

# GitHub Actions - backend init with OIDC
jobs:
  deploy:
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::${{ vars.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}:role/GitHubTerraform
          aws-region: us-east-1
      
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: |
          terraform init \
            -backend-config="bucket=${{ vars.TF_STATE_BUCKET }}" \
            -backend-config="key=${{ vars.TF_STATE_KEY }}" \
            -backend-config="region=us-east-1" \
            -backend-config="dynamodb_table=${{ vars.TF_LOCK_TABLE }}"

32.7 Promoting Changes Between Environments

WORKFLOW 📝 EXAM

Safely propagate infrastructure changes from dev → staging → production using GitOps workflows, plan reviews, and automated approval gates.

💻 Practical Examples

32.7.1 GitOps Workflow

Changes flow through git branches and pull requests — dev branch auto-deploys, staging and prod require PR approval.

Example: Branch-Based Promotion

Git branching strategy for environment promotion.

# 1. Developer creates feature branch
git checkout -b feature/add-redis
# Make changes...
git push origin feature/add-redis

# 2. PR to dev branch → auto-deploys to dev environment
# PR reviewed and merged → dev deployed automatically

# 3. After testing, promote to staging
git checkout staging
git merge dev
git push origin staging
# CI/CD runs → plan posted to PR → manual approval → apply

# 4. After staging validation, promote to prod
git checkout main
git merge staging
git push origin main
# CI/CD runs → plan posted → team lead approves → apply
🔄 Promotion Flow:
feature/* → dev (auto) → staging (approval) → main/prod (team lead approval)
32.7.2 Plan Review Process

Always post Terraform plan output to the pull request for review. Never apply without a reviewed plan.

Example: PR Comment with Plan Output

GitHub Actions posts the plan as a PR comment for review.

# .github/workflows/plan.yml
name: Terraform Plan
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['infrastructure/**']

jobs:
  plan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      
      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -no-color -var-file=envs/${{ env.TARGET_ENV }}.tfvars
        continue-on-error: true
      
      - name: Post Plan to PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const plan = `${{ steps.plan.outputs.stdout }}`;
            const truncated = plan.length > 60000
              ? plan.substring(0, 60000) + '\n... (truncated)'
              : plan;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `### Terraform Plan 📋\n\`\`\`\n${truncated}\n\`\`\``
            });
32.7.3 Approval Gates

Use GitHub environment protection rules or equivalent CI/CD features to enforce approvals before production deployments.

Example: GitHub Environment Protection

Configure environments with required reviewers and wait timers.

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
jobs:
  deploy-staging:
    environment:
      name: staging
    # Requires 1 reviewer (configured in GitHub Settings)
    steps:
      - run: terraform apply plan.tfplan

  deploy-prod:
    needs: deploy-staging
    environment:
      name: production
    # Requires 2 reviewers + 30 minute wait timer
    steps:
      - run: terraform apply plan.tfplan
Environment Reviewers Wait Timer Branch Policy
dev None (auto) 0 min Any branch
staging 1 reviewer 0 min staging, main
production 2 reviewers 30 min main only
🏗️ 📊 ⚡ 🔧
CHAPTER 33

Terraform at Scale

Module Registry Monorepo vs Polyrepo State Management Performance Automation Governance Observability
7 Sections
24 Subsections
25+ Code Examples
1000+ Resources
PART 9 EXPERT ★★★★★

Master the patterns and practices for running Terraform across hundreds of services, thousands of resources, and dozens of teams — from module registries and state splitting to performance tuning and organizational governance.

Learning Objectives

  • Build and operate a private module registry
  • Choose between monorepo and polyrepo strategies
  • Split and manage state across large organizations
  • Optimize Terraform performance for large configurations
  • Automate Terraform operations with CI/CD at scale
  • Implement governance through policy-as-code
  • Build observability into infrastructure pipelines

Chapter Contents

33.1 Module Registry 33.2 Monorepo vs Polyrepo 33.3 State Management at Scale 33.4 Performance Optimization 33.5 CI/CD Automation at Scale 33.6 Organizational Governance 33.7 Observability and Reporting

33.1 Module Registry

REGISTRY 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Publishing Modules

A private module registry serves as the single source of truth for reusable infrastructure components, enabling teams to discover, share, and version modules across the organization.

💻 Practical Examples

33.1.1 Private Registry Setup

Terraform Cloud and Enterprise include a built-in private registry. Alternatively, host modules via Git tags with semantic versioning.

Example: Module Registry Sources

Consuming modules from different registry types.

# From Terraform Cloud Private Registry
module "vpc" {
  source  = "app.terraform.io/myorg/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 3.0"
}

# From GitHub (Git source with tag)
module "vpc" {
  source = "git::https://github.com/myorg/terraform-aws-vpc.git?ref=v3.1.0"
}

# From S3 bucket (artifact registry)
module "vpc" {
  source = "s3::https://my-terraform-modules.s3.amazonaws.com/vpc/v3.1.0.zip"
}

# From local path (during development)
module "vpc" {
  source = "../modules/vpc"
}
33.1.2 Module Versioning

Use semantic versioning (SemVer) for modules. Pin consumers to compatible ranges.

Constraint Meaning Example Match
= 3.1.0 Exact version 3.1.0 only
~> 3.1 Pessimistic (patch only) 3.1.x (not 3.2.0)
~> 3.0 Pessimistic (minor + patch) 3.x.x (not 4.0.0)
>= 3.0, < 4.0 Range constraint Any 3.x version
33.1.3 Publishing Workflow

Automate module publishing with CI/CD — validate, test, tag, and publish on merge to main.

Example: Module CI/CD Pipeline

Automated validation, testing, and tagging of Terraform modules.

# .github/workflows/module-release.yml
name: Module Release
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    
jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
      - run: terraform init -backend=false
      - run: terraform validate
  
  test:
    needs: validate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: gruntwork-io/terratest-action@v2
        with:
          test_dir: test/
  
  release:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Semantic Release
        uses: cycjimmy/semantic-release-action@v4
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
33.1.4 Module Documentation

Generate documentation automatically using terraform-docs to keep README files in sync with actual inputs, outputs, and resources.

Example: Auto-Generated README

Using terraform-docs to auto-update module documentation.

# .terraform-docs.yml
formatter: markdown table
header-from: docs/header.md
sections:
  show:
    - header
    - requirements
    - providers
    - inputs
    - outputs
    - resources
output:
  file: README.md
  mode: inject
  
# Pre-commit hook:
# repos:
#   - repo: https://github.com/terraform-docs/terraform-docs
#     hooks:
#       - id: terraform-docs-go
#         args: ["markdown", "table", "--output-file", "README.md"]

33.2 Monorepo vs Polyrepo

ARCHITECTURE

Choose between a single repository for all infrastructure code (monorepo) or separate repositories per service/team (polyrepo). Each has trade-offs in visibility, ownership, and tooling.

💻 Strategy Analysis

33.2.1 Monorepo Layout

All infrastructure code in one repository. Teams own specific directories.

Example: Monorepo Structure

Single repository with CODEOWNERS for team ownership boundaries.

terraform-infra/                    # Single repo
├── CODEOWNERS                      # Ownership per directory
├── modules/                        # Shared modules
│   ├── networking/                  # Platform team owns
│   ├── compute/
│   └── database/
├── platform/                       # Platform team
│   ├── networking/
│   ├── dns/
│   └── shared-services/
├── team-alpha/                     # Team Alpha services
│   ├── api-service/
│   └── worker-service/
├── team-beta/                      # Team Beta services
│   ├── web-app/
│   └── data-pipeline/
└── policies/                       # Security team
    └── sentinel/

CODEOWNERS:

# CODEOWNERS
/modules/       @platform-team
/platform/      @platform-team
/team-alpha/    @team-alpha
/team-beta/     @team-beta
/policies/      @security-team
33.2.2 Polyrepo Layout

Separate repositories per team or service, with shared modules published to a registry.

Example: Polyrepo Organization

Separate repos consuming shared modules from a private registry.

# Repository: terraform-modules (platform team)
# Published to registry as versioned modules

# Repository: team-alpha-infra
team-alpha-infra/
├── api-service/
│   ├── main.tf        # Consumes registry modules
│   └── envs/
└── worker-service/
    ├── main.tf
    └── envs/

# team-alpha-infra/api-service/main.tf
module "ecs_service" {
  source  = "app.terraform.io/myorg/ecs-service/aws"
  version = "~> 2.0"
  
  service_name = "api"
  # ...
}
33.2.3 Decision Framework
Factor Monorepo Polyrepo
Visibility 🟢 Full visibility across all infra 🔴 Fragmented view
Refactoring 🟢 Atomic cross-team changes 🔴 Multi-repo coordination
Autonomy 🔴 Shared CI/CD, merge conflicts 🟢 Independent releases
Access Control 🟡 CODEOWNERS (limited enforcement) 🟢 Repo-level permissions
CI/CD Speed 🔴 Needs path-based filtering 🟢 Fast, scoped pipelines

33.3 State Management at Scale

STATE 📝 EXAM

At scale, monolithic state files become bottlenecks. Split state files by service, environment, and lifecycle to reduce blast radius and improve performance.

💻 Practical Examples

33.3.1 State Splitting Strategies

Split monolithic state into smaller, focused state files along natural boundaries.

📐 State Splitting Guidelines:
  • By service: Each microservice has its own state
  • By layer: Networking, compute, data, DNS — separate states
  • By lifecycle: Long-lived (VPC) vs short-lived (Lambda) separate
  • By team: Each team owns their state files
33.3.2 Cross-State References

Use terraform_remote_state or data sources to read outputs from other state files.

Example: Layered State Architecture

Networking layer publishes outputs consumed by the compute layer.

Layer 1 — Networking (outputs.tf):

output "vpc_id" {
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
  description = "VPC ID for dependent layers"
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  description = "Private subnet IDs"
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
  description = "Public subnet IDs"
}

Layer 2 — Compute (main.tf):

data "terraform_remote_state" "networking" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "mycompany-terraform-state"
    key    = "networking/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

resource "aws_ecs_cluster" "main" {
  name = "app-cluster"
}

resource "aws_ecs_service" "app" {
  cluster = aws_ecs_cluster.main.id

  network_configuration {
    subnets = data.terraform_remote_state.networking.outputs.private_subnet_ids
  }
}
33.3.3 State Locking

At scale, concurrent operations are common. Ensure proper locking with DynamoDB (AWS) or equivalent.

Example: DynamoDB Lock Table Setup

Bootstrap the lock table for all state files in the organization.

# Bootstrap configuration (run once)
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "terraform_locks" {
  name         = "terraform-state-locks"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "LockID"

  attribute {
    name = "LockID"
    type = "S"
  }

  tags = {
    Purpose = "Terraform state locking"
  }

  # Enable point-in-time recovery
  point_in_time_recovery {
    enabled = true
  }
}
33.3.4 State Migration

Moving resources between state files using terraform state mv and terraform import.

Example: Splitting a Monolith State

Move networking resources from a monolith into a dedicated networking state.

# Step 1: List resources in monolith
terraform state list | grep -E "aws_vpc|aws_subnet|aws_route"

# Step 2: Move to new state file
terraform state mv -state=terraform.tfstate \
  -state-out=networking/terraform.tfstate \
  'aws_vpc.main' 'aws_vpc.main'

terraform state mv -state=terraform.tfstate \
  -state-out=networking/terraform.tfstate \
  'aws_subnet.private' 'aws_subnet.private'

# Step 3: Verify both states
cd networking && terraform plan  # Should show no changes
cd ../app && terraform plan      # Should show no changes

# Alternative: Use import in new config
# cd networking
# terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-12345678

33.4 Performance Optimization

PERFORMANCE

Large Terraform configurations can be slow. Optimize refresh times, plan duration, and apply parallelism to keep pipelines fast.

💻 Practical Examples

33.4.1 Parallelism Tuning

Terraform defaults to 10 concurrent operations. Increase for large configs, decrease for rate-limited APIs.

Example: Parallelism Configuration

Tune parallelism based on provider API limits and resource count.

# Increase parallelism for large applies (default: 10)
terraform apply -parallelism=30

# Decrease for rate-limited providers
terraform apply -parallelism=5

# In CI/CD, set via environment variable
export TF_CLI_ARGS_apply="-parallelism=20"
export TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-parallelism=20"
Scenario Recommended Parallelism Reason
AWS (100+ resources) 20-30 AWS API handles high concurrency
Azure (100+ resources) 10-15 Some Azure APIs rate-limit lower
Rate-limited API 3-5 Avoid 429 errors
Local providers 50+ No API limits
33.4.2 Reduce Refresh Time

State refresh is often the slowest part. Use -refresh=false when safe, or target specific resources.

Example: Targeted Refresh Strategies

Skip refresh for faster iteration during development.

# Skip refresh entirely (use when state is known-current)
terraform plan -refresh=false

# Refresh only specific resources
terraform apply -refresh-only -target=aws_instance.web

# Use -refresh=false with a saved plan
terraform plan -refresh=false -out=fast.tfplan
terraform apply fast.tfplan
33.4.3 Provider Caching

Cache provider plugins across runs to speed up terraform init.

Example: Plugin Cache Directory

Configure a shared plugin cache to avoid re-downloading providers.

# Set plugin cache directory (in ~/.terraformrc or via env var)
export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
mkdir -p "$TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR"

# Or in ~/.terraformrc
cat <<EOF > ~/.terraformrc
plugin_cache_dir = "$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
EOF

# In CI/CD, use a persistent cache volume
# GitHub Actions:
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.terraform.d/plugin-cache
    key: terraform-plugins-${{ hashFiles('**/.terraform.lock.hcl') }}
33.4.4 Output Contracts

Define explicit output contracts between state layers to reduce unnecessary data source lookups and improve plan speed.

Example: Typed Output Contracts

Publish well-defined outputs that downstream consumers can depend on without additional API calls.

# Networking layer — explicit output contract
output "network_config" {
  description = "Network configuration for consuming layers"
  value = {
    vpc_id             = aws_vpc.main.id
    vpc_cidr           = aws_vpc.main.cidr_block
    private_subnet_ids = aws_subnet.private[*].id
    public_subnet_ids  = aws_subnet.public[*].id
    nat_gateway_ips    = aws_eip.nat[*].public_ip
    availability_zones = var.availability_zones
  }
}

# Compute layer — consume without extra data sources
locals {
  network = data.terraform_remote_state.networking.outputs.network_config
}

resource "aws_ecs_service" "app" {
  # Direct reference — no additional AWS API call required
  network_configuration {
    subnets = local.network.private_subnet_ids
  }
}
⚡ Performance Tips Summary:
  • Increase -parallelism for large, API-tolerant providers
  • Use -refresh=false during development iteration
  • Cache provider plugins in CI/CD
  • Define output contracts to reduce cross-state API calls
  • Split large states to reduce refresh surface area

33.5 CI/CD Automation at Scale

AUTOMATION

Automate Terraform operations for dozens of services with path-based triggers, parallel runs, scheduled drifts, and centralized orchestration.

💻 Practical Examples

33.5.1 Path-Based Triggers

Only run Terraform for directories that actually changed — critical in monorepos with 50+ services.

Example: Dynamic Matrix from Changed Paths

Detect changed directories and run Terraform only for affected services.

# .github/workflows/terraform.yml
name: Terraform
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['services/**', 'platform/**']

jobs:
  detect-changes:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      directories: ${{ steps.changes.outputs.dirs }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - id: changes
        run: |
          DIRS=$(git diff --name-only ${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }} \
            | grep -E '^(services|platform)/' \
            | cut -d/ -f1,2 | sort -u \
            | jq -R -s 'split("\n") | map(select(length > 0))')
          echo "dirs=$DIRS" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
  
  plan:
    needs: detect-changes
    if: needs.detect-changes.outputs.directories != '[]'
    strategy:
      matrix:
        directory: ${{ fromJson(needs.detect-changes.outputs.directories) }}
      fail-fast: false
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.directory }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: terraform init
      - run: terraform plan -no-color
33.5.2 Atlantis / Terraform Cloud

Use dedicated orchestration tools for pull-request-driven Terraform workflows at scale.

Example: Atlantis Configuration

Atlantis auto-plans on PR and applies after approval comment.

# atlantis.yaml (repo-level config)
version: 3
automerge: false
parallel_plan: true
parallel_apply: true

projects:
  - name: networking
    dir: platform/networking
    workspace: default
    autoplan:
      when_modified: ["*.tf", "*.tfvars", "../../modules/networking/**"]
      enabled: true
    apply_requirements: [approved, mergeable]
    
  - name: api-service
    dir: services/api-service
    workspace: default
    autoplan:
      when_modified: ["*.tf", "*.tfvars", "../../modules/ecs-service/**"]
      enabled: true
    apply_requirements: [approved, mergeable]

# Team comments on PR:
# atlantis plan -p networking     → plans networking project
# atlantis apply -p networking    → applies after approval
33.5.3 Scheduled Drift Detection

Run terraform plan on a schedule to detect configuration drift — resources changed outside Terraform.

Example: Nightly Drift Detection

Scheduled workflow that alerts on drift via Slack or PagerDuty.

# .github/workflows/drift-detection.yml
name: Drift Detection
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'   # Daily at 6 AM UTC

jobs:
  detect-drift:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        service: [networking, api-service, database, monitoring]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: services/${{ matrix.service }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: terraform init
      
      - name: Check for drift
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -no-color
        continue-on-error: true
      
      - name: Alert on drift
        if: steps.plan.outcome == 'failure'
        run: |
          curl -X POST "${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}" \
            -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
            -d "{\"text\": \"⚠️ Drift detected in ${{ matrix.service }}\"}"

33.6 Organizational Governance

GOVERNANCE 📝 EXAM

Enforce organizational standards, compliance, and cost controls through policy-as-code, required tags, and resource restrictions.

💻 Practical Examples

33.6.1 Sentinel Policies

HashiCorp Sentinel is the policy-as-code framework for Terraform Cloud/Enterprise that enforces rules before apply.

Example: Sentinel Policy — Require Tags

Enforce that all AWS resources include mandatory tags.

# policies/require-tags.sentinel
import "tfplan/v2" as tfplan

mandatory_tags = ["Environment", "Team", "CostCenter"]

allResources = filter tfplan.resource_changes as _, rc {
  rc.mode is "managed" and
  (rc.change.actions contains "create" or rc.change.actions contains "update")
}

taggedResources = filter allResources as _, rc {
  rc.change.after.tags is not null
}

violations = []
for taggedResources as _, rc {
  for mandatory_tags as tag {
    if rc.change.after.tags[tag] is undefined {
      append(violations, "${rc.address} missing tag: ${tag}")
    }
  }
}

main = rule { length(violations) == 0 }
33.6.2 OPA / Conftest

Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Conftest provides vendor-neutral policy enforcement using Rego language.

Example: Conftest Policy — No Public S3

Deny publicly accessible S3 buckets in Terraform plans.

# Generate plan JSON
terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan
terraform show -json plan.tfplan > plan.json

# Run Conftest
conftest test plan.json --policy policies/

policies/s3.rego:

# policies/s3.rego
package main

deny[msg] {
  resource := input.resource_changes[_]
  resource.type == "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block"
  change := resource.change.after
  change.block_public_acls != true
  msg := sprintf("S3 bucket %s must block public ACLs", [resource.address])
}

deny[msg] {
  resource := input.resource_changes[_]
  resource.type == "aws_s3_bucket"
  not has_encryption(resource)
  msg := sprintf("S3 bucket %s must have encryption", [resource.address])
}
33.6.3 Cost Estimation

Integrate cost estimation into PR workflows to catch expensive changes before they're applied.

Example: Infracost in CI/CD

Post cost diff as a PR comment for every infrastructure change.

# .github/workflows/infracost.yml
name: Infracost
on: pull_request

jobs:
  cost:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: infracost/actions/setup@v3
        with:
          api-key: ${{ secrets.INFRACOST_API_KEY }}
      
      - name: Generate cost diff
        run: |
          infracost diff \
            --path=. \
            --format=json \
            --compare-to=infracost-base.json \
            --out-file=/tmp/infracost.json
      
      - name: Post PR comment
        run: |
          infracost comment github \
            --path=/tmp/infracost.json \
            --repo=${{ github.repository }} \
            --pull-request=${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
            --github-token=${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} \
            --behavior=update
33.6.4 Resource Restrictions

Limit which resource types and instance sizes teams can provision using allowed lists.

Team Allowed Instance Types Max Resources Restricted Services
Development t3.small, t3.medium 50 Direct Connect, Transit GW
Platform Any 500 None
Data Team r6g.*, i3.* 200 VPC peering

33.7 Observability and Reporting

MONITORING

Track Terraform runs, resource counts, drift, and costs across the organization with dashboards and alerts.

💻 Practical Examples

33.7.1 Run Metrics

Track plan/apply duration, success rates, and resource counts to identify bottlenecks.

Example: Structured Logging for Terraform Runs

Emit JSON logs from CI/CD for ingestion into your observability platform.

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/terraform-run.sh
START_TIME=$(date +%s)
SERVICE_NAME=$(basename "$PWD")

terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=plan.tfplan 2>&1
PLAN_EXIT=$?

END_TIME=$(date +%s)
DURATION=$((END_TIME - START_TIME))

# Emit structured log
jq -n \
  --arg service "$SERVICE_NAME" \
  --arg env "$ENVIRONMENT" \
  --arg duration "$DURATION" \
  --arg exit_code "$PLAN_EXIT" \
  --arg timestamp "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" \
  '{
    event: "terraform_plan",
    service: $service,
    environment: $env,
    duration_seconds: ($duration | tonumber),
    exit_code: ($exit_code | tonumber),
    has_changes: (($exit_code | tonumber) == 2),
    timestamp: $timestamp
  }'
33.7.2 Dashboard Design

Build dashboards showing infrastructure health across the organization.

Metric Source Alert Threshold
Plan Duration CI/CD logs > 10 min
Apply Success Rate CI/CD metrics < 95%
Drift Occurrences Scheduled plans Any drift
Resource Count State file analysis > 500 per state
Monthly Cost Delta Infracost / AWS CUR > 10% increase
33.7.3 Audit Logging

Log who ran what, when, and what changed for compliance and troubleshooting.

Example: Apply Audit Trail

Record every Terraform apply with the operator, changes, and approval status.

# Store audit records in DynamoDB
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "terraform_audit" {
  name         = "terraform-audit-log"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "RunID"
  range_key    = "Timestamp"

  attribute {
    name = "RunID"
    type = "S"
  }

  attribute {
    name = "Timestamp"
    type = "S"
  }

  attribute {
    name = "Operator"
    type = "S"
  }

  global_secondary_index {
    name            = "OperatorIndex"
    hash_key        = "Operator"
    range_key       = "Timestamp"
    projection_type = "ALL"
  }

  tags = {
    Purpose = "Terraform audit logging"
  }
}
🔒 🛡️ 🔑 ⚠️
CHAPTER 34

Security Best Practices

Secrets Management State Security IAM Policy-as-Code Encryption Supply Chain Compliance Audit
8 Sections
30 Subsections
30+ Code Examples
CIS Benchmark Ready
PART 9 EXPERT ★★★★★

Harden every layer of your Terraform workflow — from secrets management and state encryption to least-privilege IAM, provider supply chain security, and continuous compliance scanning.

Learning Objectives
