  • Manage secrets securely without leaking into state or logs
  • Encrypt and lock down Terraform state files
  • Apply least-privilege IAM for Terraform pipelines
  • Enforce security policies with Sentinel and OPA
  • Encrypt data at rest and in transit across resources
  • Secure the Terraform provider supply chain
  • Implement continuous compliance frameworks
  • Build comprehensive audit trails

Chapter Contents

34.1 Secrets Management 34.2 State File Security 34.3 Least Privilege IAM 34.4 Security Policy Enforcement 34.5 Encryption Patterns 34.6 Provider Supply Chain Security 34.7 Compliance Frameworks 34.8 Security Audit and Logging

34.1 Secrets Management

SECURITY 📝 EXAM

Never hardcode secrets in Terraform code. Use external secret stores and mark sensitive values to prevent exposure in plans and logs.

💻 Practical Examples

34.1.1 Vault Integration

HashiCorp Vault is the gold standard for dynamic secret management with Terraform.

Example: Reading Secrets from Vault

Fetch database credentials dynamically from Vault at plan/apply time.

provider "vault" {
  address = "https://vault.example.com:8200"
  # Auth via VAULT_TOKEN env var or AppRole
}

# Read static secret
data "vault_kv_secret_v2" "db_creds" {
  mount = "secret"
  name  = "production/database"
}

# Read dynamic database credentials
data "vault_database_credentials" "app_db" {
  backend = "database"
  role    = "app-readonly"
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine         = "postgres"
  instance_class = "db.r6g.large"
  username       = data.vault_database_credentials.app_db.username
  password       = data.vault_database_credentials.app_db.password

  # Credentials rotate automatically via Vault
}
34.1.2 AWS Secrets Manager

Use AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store with the AWS provider.

Example: AWS Secrets Manager with Terraform

Store and retrieve secrets using AWS Secrets Manager.

# Create a secret (value set outside Terraform)
resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret" "db_password" {
  name                    = "prod/db/master-password"
  recovery_window_in_days = 30
  
  tags = {
    Environment = "production"
  }
}

# Read an existing secret
data "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_password" {
  secret_id = "prod/db/master-password"
}

# Use in resource
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine         = "postgres"
  instance_class = "db.r6g.large"
  username       = "admin"
  password       = data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db_password.secret_string

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [password]  # Don't drift on rotation
  }
}

# SSM Parameter Store (alternative)
data "aws_ssm_parameter" "api_key" {
  name            = "/prod/api/external-api-key"
  with_decryption = true
}
34.1.3 Sensitive Variables

Mark variables and outputs as sensitive to prevent values from appearing in plan output.

Example: Sensitive Marking

Variables marked sensitive are redacted in CLI output and logs.

variable "db_password" {
  type      = string
  sensitive = true  # Hidden in plan output
}

variable "api_key" {
  type      = string
  sensitive = true
}

output "connection_string" {
  value     = "postgres://admin:${var.db_password}@${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}/app"
  sensitive = true  # Required since it contains sensitive data
}

# Pass sensitive values via environment variables:
# export TF_VAR_db_password="super-secret"
# export TF_VAR_api_key="sk-123456789"
⚠️ Important: Even with sensitive = true, values are still stored in plain text in the state file. Always encrypt your state backend and restrict access tightly.
34.1.4 Environment Variable Patterns

Use TF_VAR_ prefixed environment variables to inject secrets without any file-based configuration.

Method Security Level Rotation Audit Trail
Hardcoded in .tf 🔴 None Manual Git history
terraform.tfvars 🔴 None (if committed) Manual Git history
TF_VAR_ env vars 🟡 Medium Manual None
AWS Secrets Manager 🟢 High Automatic CloudTrail
Vault Dynamic Secrets 🟢 Highest Automatic + TTL Vault audit log
34.1.5 AWS SSM Parameter Store

AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store provides a centralized, hierarchical store for configuration data and secrets with optional KMS encryption — a simpler, cost-effective alternative to Secrets Manager for non-rotating secrets.

💡 SSM vs Secrets Manager:
SSM Parameter Store — Free for standard parameters, $0.05/10K advanced API calls. Best for config values, feature flags, and secrets that don't need auto-rotation.
Secrets Manager — $0.40/secret/month + $0.05/10K API calls. Best for database credentials and secrets requiring auto-rotation.
Example: Reading SSM Parameters in Terraform

Retrieve secrets and configuration from SSM Parameter Store using both plaintext and SecureString parameters.

# Read a SecureString parameter (auto-decrypted)
data "aws_ssm_parameter" "db_password" {
  name            = "/production/database/password"
  with_decryption = true
}

# Read multiple parameters by path
data "aws_ssm_parameters_by_path" "app_config" {
  path            = "/production/app/"
  with_decryption = true
  recursive       = true
}

# Use in resource configuration
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine               = "postgres"
  instance_class       = "db.r6g.large"
  username             = "admin"
  password             = data.aws_ssm_parameter.db_password.value
  skip_final_snapshot  = false
}

# Create SSM parameters for outputs
resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "api_endpoint" {
  name        = "/production/api/endpoint"
  description = "API Gateway endpoint URL"
  type        = "String"
  value       = aws_apigatewayv2_api.main.api_endpoint
  tags        = var.common_tags
}

resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "db_connection" {
  name        = "/production/database/connection_string"
  description = "Database connection string"
  type        = "SecureString"
  key_id      = aws_kms_key.app.arn
  value       = "postgresql://admin:${data.aws_ssm_parameter.db_password.value}@${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}/mydb"
  tags        = var.common_tags
}
Parameter Type Encryption Max Size Cost Use Case
String None 4 KB / 8 KB Free / Advanced Config values, endpoints
StringList None 4 KB / 8 KB Free / Advanced Comma-separated lists
SecureString KMS 4 KB / 8 KB Free / Advanced Passwords, API keys, tokens

34.2 State File Security

STATE 📝 EXAM

The Terraform state file contains every attribute of every managed resource — including passwords, keys, and tokens in plain text. Securing it is critical.

💻 Practical Examples

34.2.1 Encrypted Backend

Always enable encryption for remote state storage.

Example: S3 Backend with KMS Encryption

State encrypted at rest with a customer-managed KMS key.

# KMS key for state encryption
resource "aws_kms_key" "terraform_state" {
  description             = "Encrypt Terraform state files"
  deletion_window_in_days = 30
  enable_key_rotation     = true

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "AllowTerraformRole"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          AWS = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole"
        }
        Action   = ["kms:Encrypt", "kms:Decrypt", "kms:GenerateDataKey"]
        Resource = "*"
      }
    ]
  })
}

# S3 bucket with encryption
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "terraform_state" {
  bucket = "mycompany-terraform-state"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.terraform_state.arn
    }
    bucket_key_enabled = true
  }
}

# Backend configuration
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "mycompany-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    kms_key_id     = "alias/terraform-state-key"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}
34.2.2 Access Controls

Restrict who can read state files — they contain all resource attributes including secrets.

Example: State Bucket IAM Policy

Only the Terraform CI/CD role and break-glass admin can access state.

resource "aws_s3_bucket_policy" "terraform_state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "DenyUnauthorizedAccess"
        Effect = "Deny"
        Principal = "*"
        Action = "s3:*"
        Resource = [
          aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.arn,
          "${aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.arn}/*"
        ]
        Condition = {
          StringNotEquals = {
            "aws:PrincipalArn" = [
              "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole",
              "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/BreakGlassAdmin"
            ]
          }
        }
      },
      {
        Sid    = "DenyInsecureTransport"
        Effect = "Deny"
        Principal = "*"
        Action = "s3:*"
        Resource = [
          aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.arn,
          "${aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.arn}/*"
        ]
        Condition = {
          Bool = { "aws:SecureTransport" = "false" }
        }
      }
    ]
  })
}
34.2.3 State Versioning

Enable S3 versioning to recover from accidental state corruption or deletion.

Example: Versioned State Bucket

Versioning + lifecycle rules for state file history retention.

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration" "state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id

  rule {
    id     = "retain-state-versions"
    status = "Enabled"

    noncurrent_version_expiration {
      noncurrent_days = 90  # Keep 90 days of history
    }

    noncurrent_version_transition {
      noncurrent_days = 30
      storage_class   = "STANDARD_IA"
    }
  }
}
34.2.4 State Locking Security

Ensure the DynamoDB lock table is restricted to prevent malicious lock manipulation.

🔒 State Security Checklist:
  • ☐ Backend encryption enabled (KMS)
  • ☐ S3 bucket policy restricts access
  • ☐ Versioning enabled for rollback
  • ☐ Server access logging enabled
  • ☐ Public access blocked on bucket
  • ☐ DynamoDB lock table restricted
  • ☐ .terraform in .gitignore
  • ☐ No local state files in repo

34.3 Least Privilege IAM

IAM 📝 EXAM

Terraform should never run with admin privileges. Scope IAM permissions to exactly what each pipeline or workspace needs — nothing more.

💻 Practical Examples

34.3.1 Scoped Terraform Roles

Create separate IAM roles per service/team with only the permissions they need.

Example: Scoped CI/CD Role

IAM role for the networking pipeline — only VPC, subnet, and route table actions.

resource "aws_iam_role" "terraform_networking" {
  name = "terraform-networking-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Federated = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:oidc-provider/token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
      }
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub" = "repo:myorg/infra:ref:refs/heads/main"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "networking" {
  name = "terraform-networking-policy"
  role = aws_iam_role.terraform_networking.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "NetworkingResources"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "ec2:CreateVpc", "ec2:DeleteVpc", "ec2:DescribeVpcs",
          "ec2:CreateSubnet", "ec2:DeleteSubnet", "ec2:DescribeSubnets",
          "ec2:CreateRouteTable", "ec2:DeleteRouteTable",
          "ec2:CreateRoute", "ec2:DeleteRoute",
          "ec2:CreateInternetGateway", "ec2:DeleteInternetGateway",
          "ec2:AttachInternetGateway", "ec2:DetachInternetGateway",
          "ec2:DescribeRouteTables", "ec2:DescribeInternetGateways",
          "ec2:CreateTags", "ec2:DeleteTags"
        ]
        Resource = "*"
      },
      {
        Sid    = "StateBucketAccess"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
        Resource = [
          "arn:aws:s3:::mycompany-tf-state",
          "arn:aws:s3:::mycompany-tf-state/networking/*"
        ]
      },
      {
        Sid    = "LockTableAccess"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = ["dynamodb:GetItem", "dynamodb:PutItem", "dynamodb:DeleteItem"]
        Resource = "arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:123456789012:table/terraform-locks"
      }
    ]
  })
}
34.3.2 OIDC Federation

Use OpenID Connect (OIDC) to authenticate CI/CD pipelines without long-lived credentials.

Example: GitHub Actions OIDC

Short-lived credentials via OIDC — no secret keys stored anywhere.

# OIDC Provider for GitHub Actions
resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github" {
  url             = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
  client_id_list  = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
  thumbprint_list = ["6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1"]
}

# Role constrained to specific repo + branch
resource "aws_iam_role" "github_terraform" {
  name = "github-terraform-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Federated = aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github.arn
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
      Condition = {
        StringLike = {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub" = "repo:myorg/infra:*"
        }
        StringEquals = {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud" = "sts.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}
34.3.3 Session Policies

Use AWS session policies to further restrict assumed roles per workspace or pipeline.

Pipeline IAM Role Permissions
Networking terraform-networking VPC, Subnet, Route, NAT, IGW
Compute terraform-compute EC2, ECS, ALB, ASG, ACM
Database terraform-database RDS, ElastiCache, DynamoDB
Monitoring terraform-monitoring CloudWatch, SNS, Lambda
34.3.4 Permission Boundaries

Use AWS permission boundaries to set a ceiling on what Terraform-created roles can do, preventing privilege escalation.

Example: Permission Boundary

Prevent Terraform from creating overly permissive roles.

resource "aws_iam_policy" "boundary" {
  name = "terraform-permission-boundary"
  
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "AllowedServices"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "ec2:*", "ecs:*", "rds:*", "s3:*",
          "elasticloadbalancing:*", "cloudwatch:*",
          "logs:*", "sns:*", "sqs:*"
        ]
        Resource = "*"
      },
      {
        Sid    = "DenyDangerousActions"
        Effect = "Deny"
        Action = [
          "iam:CreateUser",
          "iam:CreateAccessKey",
          "organizations:*",
          "account:*"
        ]
        Resource = "*"
      }
    ]
  })
}

# Apply boundary to all roles created by Terraform
resource "aws_iam_role" "app_role" {
  name                 = "app-service-role"
  permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.boundary.arn
  # ...
}

34.4 Security Policy Enforcement

POLICY

Automate security guardrails that prevent insecure configurations from being applied — catch misconfigurations before they reach production.

💻 Practical Examples

34.4.1 Pre-commit Scanning

Run security scans locally before code is committed using tools like tfsec, checkov, and trivy.

Example: Pre-commit Hook Configuration

Scan Terraform code for security issues on every commit.

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.88.0
    hooks:
      - id: terraform_fmt
      - id: terraform_validate
      - id: terraform_tfsec
        args: ['--args=--minimum-severity HIGH']
      - id: terraform_checkov
        args:
          - --args=--framework terraform
          - --args=--check CKV_AWS_18      # S3 access logging
          - --args=--check CKV_AWS_19      # S3 encryption
          - --args=--check CKV_AWS_145     # KMS encryption
      - id: terraform_trivy
        args: ['--args=--severity HIGH,CRITICAL']
34.4.2 CI Pipeline Scanning

Block PRs that introduce security violations using CI/CD gate checks.

Example: Checkov in CI/CD

Fail the pipeline on HIGH or CRITICAL security findings.

# .github/workflows/security-scan.yml
name: Security Scan
on: pull_request

jobs:
  checkov:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@v12
        with:
          directory: .
          framework: terraform
          soft_fail: false          # Blocks PR on failure
          output_format: sarif
          output_file_path: results.sarif
          check: |
            CKV_AWS_18,CKV_AWS_19,CKV_AWS_145,
            CKV_AWS_20,CKV_AWS_21,CKV_AWS_23
      
      - uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v3
        if: always()
        with:
          sarif_file: results.sarif
34.4.3 Custom Policy Rules

Write custom policies for organization-specific security requirements.

Example: Custom Checkov Policy (Python)

Enforce that all EC2 instances use encrypted EBS volumes.

# custom_checks/ec2_encrypted_ebs.py
from checkov.terraform.checks.resource.base_resource_check import BaseResourceCheck
from checkov.common.models.enums import CheckResult, CheckCategories

class EC2EncryptedEBS(BaseResourceCheck):
    def __init__(self):
        name = "Ensure all EC2 EBS volumes are encrypted"
        id = "CUSTOM_AWS_001"
        supported_resources = ["aws_instance"]
        categories = [CheckCategories.ENCRYPTION]
        super().__init__(name=name, id=id,
              categories=categories,
              supported_resources=supported_resources)

    def scan_resource_conf(self, conf):
        ebs_blocks = conf.get("ebs_block_device", [])
        for ebs in ebs_blocks:
            encrypted = ebs.get("encrypted", [False])
            if encrypted == [False] or encrypted is False:
                return CheckResult.FAILED
        return CheckResult.PASSED

check = EC2EncryptedEBS()
34.4.4 Drift Prevention Policies

Detect and alert when manual changes create security-relevant drift from the Terraform-defined configuration.

🛡️ Security Scanning Pipeline:
Pre-commit (local) → PR scan (CI) → Plan review (Sentinel/OPA) → Post-apply (drift detection)

Each layer catches different types of security issues. Defense in depth is essential.

34.5 Encryption Patterns

ENCRYPTION

Enforce encryption at rest and in transit across all resources using reusable patterns for KMS, TLS certificates, and default encryption configurations.

💻 Practical Examples

34.5.1 KMS Key Management

Centralize encryption key management with per-service KMS keys and automatic rotation.

Example: KMS Key Module

Reusable KMS key with automatic rotation and scoped access.

# modules/kms-key/main.tf
resource "aws_kms_key" "this" {
  description             = var.description
  deletion_window_in_days = 30
  enable_key_rotation     = true
  multi_region            = var.multi_region

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "RootAccess"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = { AWS = "arn:aws:iam::${var.account_id}:root" }
        Action   = "kms:*"
        Resource = "*"
      },
      {
        Sid    = "ServiceAccess"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = { AWS = var.allowed_role_arns }
        Action = [
          "kms:Encrypt", "kms:Decrypt",
          "kms:ReEncrypt*", "kms:GenerateDataKey*",
          "kms:DescribeKey"
        ]
        Resource = "*"
      }
    ]
  })
}

resource "aws_kms_alias" "this" {
  name          = "alias/${var.alias}"
  target_key_id = aws_kms_key.this.key_id
}

# Usage
module "rds_key" {
  source           = "./modules/kms-key"
  description      = "RDS encryption key"
  alias            = "rds-encryption"
  account_id       = data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id
  allowed_role_arns = [aws_iam_role.rds_service.arn]
}
34.5.2 Encryption at Rest

Enable default encryption for every storage service — S3, EBS, RDS, EFS, DynamoDB.

Example: Default Encryption for Storage

Ensure all data at rest is encrypted with customer-managed keys.

# EBS default encryption (account-level)
resource "aws_ebs_encryption_by_default" "enabled" {
  enabled = true
}

resource "aws_ebs_default_kms_key" "custom" {
  key_arn = module.ebs_key.key_arn
}

# RDS encryption
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine         = "postgres"
  instance_class = "db.r6g.large"
  storage_encrypted = true
  kms_key_id       = module.rds_key.key_arn
  # ...
}

# DynamoDB encryption
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "main" {
  name     = "app-data"
  hash_key = "PK"

  server_side_encryption {
    enabled     = true
    kms_key_arn = module.dynamodb_key.key_arn
  }
  # ...
}
34.5.3 TLS/SSL Certificates

Automate certificate provisioning and renewal with ACM.

Example: ACM Certificate with DNS Validation

Automated certificate creation with Route53 validation.

resource "aws_acm_certificate" "main" {
  domain_name       = var.domain_name
  validation_method = "DNS"

  subject_alternative_names = [
    "*.${var.domain_name}"
  ]

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

resource "aws_route53_record" "cert_validation" {
  for_each = {
    for dvo in aws_acm_certificate.main.domain_validation_options : dvo.domain_name => {
      name   = dvo.resource_record_name
      record = dvo.resource_record_value
      type   = dvo.resource_record_type
    }
  }

  zone_id = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = each.value.name
  type    = each.value.type
  records = [each.value.record]
  ttl     = 60
}

resource "aws_acm_certificate_validation" "main" {
  certificate_arn         = aws_acm_certificate.main.arn
  validation_record_fqdns = [for r in aws_route53_record.cert_validation : r.fqdn]
}
34.5.4 Encryption in Transit

Enforce HTTPS, TLS, and encrypted connections for all network communication.

Service At Rest In Transit Terraform Resource
S3 SSE-KMS Bucket policy (aws:SecureTransport) aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration
RDS KMS encryption rds.force_ssl parameter aws_db_instance
ElastiCache at_rest_encryption_enabled transit_encryption_enabled aws_elasticache_replication_group
EFS KMS encryption Mount target SG + TLS aws_efs_file_system
ALB N/A HTTPS listener + TLS 1.2 aws_lb_listener

34.6 Provider Supply Chain Security

SUPPLY CHAIN

Verify the integrity of Terraform providers and modules to prevent supply chain attacks. Lock versions, verify checksums, and use private mirrors.

💻 Practical Examples

34.6.1 Lock Files

The .terraform.lock.hcl file records checksums for every provider. Commit it to version control.

Example: Provider Lock File

Lock file ensuring provider integrity across all platforms.

# .terraform.lock.hcl (auto-generated, commit to VCS)
provider "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws" {
  version     = "5.31.0"
  constraints = "~> 5.0"
  hashes = [
    "h1:XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX=",  # zh: hash
    "zp:XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX=",  # zip hash
  ]
}

# Update lock file for all platforms
# terraform providers lock \
#   -platform=linux_amd64 \
#   -platform=darwin_amd64 \
#   -platform=darwin_arm64 \
#   -platform=windows_amd64
34.6.2 Provider Mirrors

Run a private provider mirror to avoid downloading directly from the public registry.

Example: Network Mirror Configuration

Configure Terraform to use an internal mirror for providers.

# ~/.terraformrc or terraform.rc
provider_installation {
  network_mirror {
    url = "https://terraform-mirror.internal.example.com/"
  }
  
  # Fallback to direct for providers not in mirror
  direct {
    exclude = ["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/*"]
  }
}

# S3-based filesystem mirror
provider_installation {
  filesystem_mirror {
    path    = "/opt/terraform/providers"
    include = ["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/*"]
  }
  direct {
    exclude = ["registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/*"]
  }
}
34.6.3 Version Pinning

Pin provider versions strictly in production to prevent unexpected changes from upstream updates.

Example: Strict Version Constraints

Pin providers and modules to exact or tightly constrained versions.

terraform {
  required_version = "~> 1.7.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "= 5.31.0"     # Exact pin for prod
    }
    kubernetes = {
      source  = "hashicorp/kubernetes"
      version = "~> 2.25"     # Allow patch updates
    }
    helm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/helm"
      version = ">= 2.12, < 3.0"
    }
  }
}
🔐 Supply Chain Checklist:
  • ☐ .terraform.lock.hcl committed to VCS
  • ☐ Providers pinned to specific versions
  • ☐ Lock file updated for all platforms
  • ☐ Private mirror for air-gapped environments
  • ☐ Module sources point to tagged versions (not branches)
  • ☐ Dependabot / Renovate configured for updates
34.6.4 Dependency Scanning

Automate scanning for known vulnerabilities in providers and modules using Renovate or Dependabot.

// renovate.json
{
  "$schema": "https://docs.renovatebot.com/renovate-schema.json",
  "extends": ["config:base"],
  "terraform": {
    "enabled": true
  },
  "packageRules": [
    {
      "matchManagers": ["terraform"],
      "matchPackagePatterns": ["hashicorp/*"],
      "groupName": "HashiCorp providers",
      "automerge": false,
      "labels": ["terraform", "dependencies"]
    }
  ]
}

34.7 Compliance Frameworks

COMPLIANCE 📝 EXAM

Map Terraform configurations to compliance frameworks like CIS, SOC 2, HIPAA, and PCI-DSS. Use automated scanning to verify compliance continuously.

💻 Practical Examples

34.7.1 CIS Benchmark Mapping

The CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark provides specific controls that map to Terraform resources.

CIS Control Description Terraform Resource
2.1.1 S3 bucket encryption aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration
2.1.2 S3 bucket deny HTTP aws_s3_bucket_policy
2.3.1 RDS encryption at rest aws_db_instance (storage_encrypted)
3.1 CloudTrail enabled aws_cloudtrail
4.1 No SG with 0.0.0.0/0 SSH aws_security_group_rule
34.7.2 SOC 2 Controls

SOC 2 Type II requires evidence of controls over time — Terraform provides infrastructure change audit trails.

Example: SOC 2 Evidence Collection

Terraform state + git history = audit evidence for SOC 2.

# CloudTrail for API audit (SOC 2 CC6.1, CC7.2)
resource "aws_cloudtrail" "audit" {
  name                          = "org-audit-trail"
  s3_bucket_name                = aws_s3_bucket.audit_logs.id
  include_global_service_events = true
  is_multi_region_trail         = true
  enable_log_file_validation    = true
  kms_key_id                    = aws_kms_key.audit.arn

  event_selector {
    read_write_type           = "All"
    include_management_events = true
  }
}

# Config Rules for continuous compliance (SOC 2 CC7.1)
resource "aws_config_config_rule" "encrypted_volumes" {
  name = "encrypted-volumes"
  source {
    owner             = "AWS"
    source_identifier = "ENCRYPTED_VOLUMES"
  }
}

resource "aws_config_config_rule" "s3_encryption" {
  name = "s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled"
  source {
    owner             = "AWS"
    source_identifier = "S3_BUCKET_SERVER_SIDE_ENCRYPTION_ENABLED"
  }
}
34.7.3 HIPAA / PCI-DSS

Healthcare and payment card compliance require additional encryption, logging, and access control configurations.

🏥 HIPAA Key Requirements in Terraform:
  • All PHI data encrypted at rest (KMS)
  • All network traffic encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+)
  • VPC flow logs enabled for network monitoring
  • CloudTrail with log file validation
  • No public S3 buckets or security groups
  • MFA delete on S3 buckets containing backups
34.7.4 Automated Compliance Reporting

Generate compliance reports automatically from Terraform state and scanning tools.

Example: Compliance Report Pipeline

Weekly compliance scan with HTML report uploaded to S3.

# .github/workflows/compliance-report.yml
name: Compliance Report
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 8 * * 1'    # Every Monday 8 AM
  workflow_dispatch:

jobs:
  compliance:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Run Checkov with CIS framework
        run: |
          pip install checkov
          checkov -d . \
            --framework terraform \
            --check CKV_AWS_18,CKV_AWS_19,CKV_AWS_20,CKV_AWS_21 \
            --output json > compliance-report.json
      
      - name: Upload report
        run: |
          aws s3 cp compliance-report.json \
            s3://compliance-reports/$(date +%Y-%m-%d)/report.json

34.8 Security Audit and Logging

AUDIT

Build comprehensive audit trails for infrastructure changes — who changed what, when, why, and whether it was approved.

💻 Practical Examples

34.8.1 CloudTrail Configuration

CloudTrail records all AWS API calls — it's the foundational audit log for Terraform-managed infrastructure.

Example: Organization-Wide CloudTrail

Multi-region trail with S3 data events and log validation.

resource "aws_cloudtrail" "org_trail" {
  name                       = "organization-trail"
  s3_bucket_name             = aws_s3_bucket.cloudtrail.id
  is_organization_trail      = true
  is_multi_region_trail      = true
  enable_log_file_validation = true
  kms_key_id                 = aws_kms_key.cloudtrail.arn

  # Log management events
  event_selector {
    read_write_type           = "All"
    include_management_events = true
  }

  # Log S3 data events for sensitive buckets
  event_selector {
    read_write_type           = "WriteOnly"
    include_management_events = false

    data_resource {
      type   = "AWS::S3::Object"
      values = [
        "${aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.arn}/",
        "${aws_s3_bucket.secrets.arn}/"
      ]
    }
  }

  # Send to CloudWatch for real-time alerting
  cloud_watch_logs_group_arn = "${aws_cloudwatch_log_group.cloudtrail.arn}:*"
  cloud_watch_logs_role_arn  = aws_iam_role.cloudtrail_cw.arn
}
34.8.2 VPC Flow Logs

Monitor network traffic patterns for security analysis and troubleshooting.

Example: VPC Flow Logs to CloudWatch

Capture all traffic for security investigation capabilities.

resource "aws_flow_log" "vpc" {
  vpc_id                   = aws_vpc.main.id
  traffic_type             = "ALL"
  log_destination_type     = "cloud-watch-logs"
  log_destination          = aws_cloudwatch_log_group.flow_logs.arn
  iam_role_arn             = aws_iam_role.flow_logs.arn
  max_aggregation_interval = 60

  tags = {
    Name = "vpc-flow-logs"
  }
}

resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "flow_logs" {
  name              = "/aws/vpc/flow-logs"
  retention_in_days = 365
  kms_key_id        = aws_kms_key.logs.arn
}

# Alert on rejected traffic to sensitive subnets
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_filter" "rejected_traffic" {
  name           = "rejected-traffic"
  log_group_name = aws_cloudwatch_log_group.flow_logs.name
  pattern        = "[version, account_id, interface_id, srcaddr, dstaddr, srcport, dstport, protocol, packets, bytes, start, end, action=\"REJECT\", log_status]"

  metric_transformation {
    name      = "RejectedTraffic"
    namespace = "VPCFlowLogs"
    value     = "1"
  }
}
34.8.3 Security Hub Integration

Enable AWS Security Hub for centralized security findings from multiple services and standards.

Example: Security Hub with Standards

Enable CIS and PCI-DSS standards in Security Hub via Terraform.

resource "aws_securityhub_account" "main" {}

resource "aws_securityhub_standards_subscription" "cis" {
  depends_on    = [aws_securityhub_account.main]
  standards_arn = "arn:aws:securityhub:::ruleset/cis-aws-foundations-benchmark/v/1.4.0"
}

resource "aws_securityhub_standards_subscription" "pci" {
  depends_on    = [aws_securityhub_account.main]
  standards_arn = "arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::standards/pci-dss/v/3.2.1"
}

resource "aws_securityhub_standards_subscription" "aws_best_practices" {
  depends_on    = [aws_securityhub_account.main]
  standards_arn = "arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::standards/aws-foundational-security-best-practices/v/1.0.0"
}
34.8.4 Alerting on Security Events

Create real-time alerts for security-critical infrastructure changes.

Example: Alert on Security Group Changes

CloudWatch alarm triggers when security groups are modified.

# Metric filter for SG changes
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_filter" "sg_changes" {
  name           = "security-group-changes"
  log_group_name = aws_cloudwatch_log_group.cloudtrail.name
  pattern        = "{ ($.eventName = AuthorizeSecurityGroupIngress) || ($.eventName = AuthorizeSecurityGroupEgress) || ($.eventName = RevokeSecurityGroupIngress) || ($.eventName = RevokeSecurityGroupEgress) || ($.eventName = CreateSecurityGroup) || ($.eventName = DeleteSecurityGroup) }"

  metric_transformation {
    name      = "SecurityGroupChanges"
    namespace = "SecurityAlarms"
    value     = "1"
  }
}

resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "sg_changes" {
  alarm_name          = "security-group-changes"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "SecurityGroupChanges"
  namespace           = "SecurityAlarms"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 1
  alarm_description   = "Alert: Security Group modified"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.security_alerts.arn]
}

resource "aws_sns_topic" "security_alerts" {
  name              = "security-alerts"
  kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.sns.id
}
Event Type CloudTrail Event Severity
SG rule added/removed AuthorizeSecurityGroup* 🟡 Medium
IAM policy changed PutRolePolicy, AttachRolePolicy 🔴 High
Root login ConsoleLogin (root) 🔴 Critical
KMS key deletion ScheduleKeyDeletion 🔴 Critical
S3 bucket policy change PutBucketPolicy 🟡 Medium
🧪 🔍 🛡️ ✅
CHAPTER 35

Terraform Testing Strategies

Static Analysis terraform validate terraform fmt tflint tfsec checkov terrascan trivy Pre-Commit
9 Sections
29 Subsections
30+ Code Examples
6 Tools Covered
PART 10 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

Build a comprehensive testing strategy for Terraform configurations using static analysis tools, linters, security scanners, and policy engines to catch bugs, misconfigurations, and security vulnerabilities before they reach production.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand why testing infrastructure code is critical and the cost of IaC bugs
  • Apply the testing pyramid concept to Terraform configurations
  • Run static analysis with pre-commit hooks and CI pipelines
  • Use terraform validate and terraform fmt for baseline checks
  • Configure tflint with AWS/Azure/GCP plugins and custom rules
  • Scan for security vulnerabilities with tfsec and checkov
  • Write custom policies in Python (checkov) and Rego (terrascan)
  • Detect secrets and misconfigurations with trivy
  • Build an integrated testing pipeline combining all tools

Chapter Contents

35.1 Why Test Infrastructure Code? 35.2 Static Analysis 35.3 terraform validate 35.4 terraform fmt -check 35.5 tflint - Terraform Linter 35.6 tfsec - Security Scanner 35.7 checkov - Policy Scanner 35.8 terrascan 35.9 trivy for IaC

35.1 Why Test Infrastructure Code?

CONCEPT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Tests | Custom Conditions

Infrastructure code manages production systems where a single misconfiguration can cause outages, security breaches, or massive cloud bills. Testing catches these issues before they reach real environments.

💻 Practical Examples

35.1.1 Cost of Infrastructure Bugs

Unlike application bugs that can often be hot-fixed, infrastructure bugs can destroy data, expose systems to attacks, or generate runaway costs that appear on the next billing cycle.

Bug Category Example Impact Detection Stage
Security S3 bucket set to public 🔴 Data breach Static analysis
Cost Wrong instance type (x2xlarge vs t3.micro) 🟠 $10K+/month waste Plan review
Availability Single-AZ database deployment 🟠 Downtime risk Policy check
Compliance Unencrypted EBS volumes 🔴 Audit failure Security scanner
Operational Missing tags for cost allocation 🟡 Tracking issues Linter
Drift Manual console change overwritten 🟡 Config mismatch Integration test
💰 Real Cost Impact:
According to industry data, the average cost of a cloud misconfiguration incident is $3.86 million. A $50/month testing pipeline can prevent millions in damage. The earlier you catch a bug, the cheaper the fix — static analysis is 100x cheaper than a production rollback.
35.1.2 Testing Pyramid for IaC

The traditional testing pyramid applies to infrastructure code. Each layer catches different types of issues at different speeds and costs.

Diagram: IaC Testing Pyramid

The testing pyramid for Infrastructure as Code — fast, cheap tests at the bottom; slow, expensive tests at the top.

                    ┌─────────────┐
                    │  End-to-End │  ← Full stack deploy + verify
                    │   Tests     │     (Slowest: 30-60 min)
                    ├─────────────┤
                    │ Integration │  ← Deploy real resources + test
                    │   Tests     │     (Slow: 10-30 min)
                    ├─────────────┤
                    │   Unit      │  ← terraform test, mock providers
                    │   Tests     │     (Fast: 1-5 min)
                    ├─────────────┤
                    │  Contract   │  ← Module interface validation
                    │   Tests     │     (Fast: <1 min)
                    ├─────────────┤
                    │  Security   │  ← tfsec, checkov, trivy
                    │   Scans     │     (Fast: <1 min)
                    ├─────────────┤
                    │  Linting    │  ← tflint, terraform validate
                    │  & Format   │     (Fastest: seconds)
                    └─────────────┘

            ◀════ More tests here (base) ════▶
            ◀═══ Fewer tests here (top) ═══▶
Test Layer Speed Cost Coverage Tools
Linting & Format ⚡ Seconds Free Syntax, style validate, fmt, tflint
Security Scans ⚡ <1 min Free Security misconfig tfsec, checkov, trivy
Contract Tests ⚡ <1 min Free Module interfaces terraform test
Unit Tests 🕐 1-5 min Free Logic, conditions terraform test + mocks
Integration Tests 🕐 10-30 min $$ Real resources Terratest, terraform test
E2E Tests 🕐 30-60 min $$$ Full stack Terratest, custom scripts
35.1.3 Testing Philosophy

Effective IaC testing follows the principle of "shift left" — catch issues as early as possible in the development cycle.

Example: Shift-Left Testing Pipeline

Complete CI/CD pipeline showing where each test type runs in the development workflow.

# .github/workflows/terraform-test.yml
name: Terraform Testing Pipeline
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['**.tf', '**.tfvars']
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  # Layer 1: Fastest checks (seconds)
  lint-and-format:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      
      - name: Terraform Format Check
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff
      
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init -backend=false
      
      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform validate
      
      - name: TFLint
        uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@v4
        with:
          tflint_version: latest
      - run: |
          tflint --init
          tflint --recursive --format compact

  # Layer 2: Security scans (<1 min)
  security-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: lint-and-format
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: tfsec
        uses: aquasecurity/tfsec-action@v1.0.3
        with:
          soft_fail: false
      
      - name: Checkov
        uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@v12
        with:
          directory: .
          framework: terraform
          quiet: true
          soft_fail: false

  # Layer 3: Unit tests (1-5 min)
  unit-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: security-scan
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: terraform test -filter=tests/unit/
🎯 Testing Strategy Rule of Thumb:
70% static analysis & linting (every commit) · 20% unit & contract tests (every PR) · 10% integration tests (nightly or pre-release). This ratio maximizes bug detection while keeping pipeline times under 15 minutes for PR checks.

35.2 Static Analysis

TOOLING 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: pre-commit Framework | pre-commit-terraform

Static analysis examines Terraform configurations without executing them, catching syntax errors, style violations, security misconfigurations, and best practice violations instantly at the developer's workstation.

💻 Practical Examples

35.2.1 Pre-Commit Hooks

Pre-commit hooks run automatically before every git commit, enforcing code quality at the earliest possible stage. The pre-commit-terraform framework bundles all major Terraform tools.

Example: Comprehensive Pre-Commit Configuration

A complete .pre-commit-config.yaml combining formatting, validation, linting, and security scanning.

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.88.0
    hooks:
      # Format check - auto-fix style issues
      - id: terraform_fmt
        args:
          - --args=-recursive
      
      # Validate configuration syntax
      - id: terraform_validate
        args:
          - --hook-config=--retry-once-with-cleanup=true
          - --tf-init-args=-backend=false
      
      # Lint with tflint
      - id: terraform_tflint
        args:
          - --args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.tflint.hcl
          - --args=--recursive
      
      # Security scan with tfsec
      - id: terraform_tfsec
        args:
          - --args=--minimum-severity=MEDIUM
      
      # Documentation generation
      - id: terraform_docs
        args:
          - --hook-config=--path-to-file=README.md
          - --hook-config=--add-to-existing-file=true
          - --hook-config=--create-file-if-not-exist=true
      
      # Checkov security scan
      - id: terraform_checkov
        args:
          - --args=--quiet
          - --args=--compact

  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-private-key
      - id: check-merge-conflict
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: trailing-whitespace
35.2.2 CI Pipeline Integration

CI pipelines enforce the same checks server-side, preventing any code from reaching the main branch without passing all static analysis gates.

Example: GitHub Actions Static Analysis Job

Dedicated CI job that runs all static analysis tools in parallel for maximum speed.

# .github/workflows/static-analysis.yml
name: Static Analysis
on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['**.tf', '**.tfvars', '.tflint.hcl']

jobs:
  static-analysis:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        tool: [fmt, validate, tflint, tfsec, checkov]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Run ${{ matrix.tool }}
        run: |
          case "${{ matrix.tool }}" in
            fmt)
              terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff
              ;;
            validate)
              terraform init -backend=false
              terraform validate
              ;;
            tflint)
              curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/terraform-linters/tflint/master/install_linux.sh | bash
              tflint --init
              tflint --recursive --format compact
              ;;
            tfsec)
              curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/tfsec/master/scripts/install_linux.sh | bash
              tfsec . --minimum-severity MEDIUM
              ;;
            checkov)
              pip install checkov
              checkov -d . --framework terraform --compact --quiet
              ;;
          esac
35.2.3 Automated Code Review

Tools like reviewdog post static analysis findings directly as PR review comments, making issues immediately visible to the developer.

Example: Reviewdog Integration with tfsec

Post tfsec findings as inline PR comments on the exact lines with issues.

# .github/workflows/reviewdog.yml
name: Reviewdog
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  tfsec-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: tfsec with reviewdog
        uses: reviewdog/action-tfsec@v1
        with:
          reporter: github-pr-review
          filter_mode: nofilter
          fail_on_error: true
          tfsec_flags: --minimum-severity MEDIUM
          
  tflint-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: reviewdog/action-tflint@v1
        with:
          reporter: github-pr-review
          filter_mode: nofilter
          tflint_init: true
💡 Pro Tip — Review Comments:
Reviewdog posts findings as GitHub review comments directly on the affected lines, making it impossible for developers to miss issues. Combined with required status checks, this creates a zero-tolerance gate for security violations.

35.3 terraform validate

COMMAND 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: terraform validate

The terraform validate command verifies that a configuration is syntactically valid and internally consistent — checking attribute names, argument types, and required values without accessing any remote services or state.

💻 Practical Examples

35.3.1 Configuration Syntax Validation

Validate checks for references to undeclared variables, resources, or modules, and verifies that all required arguments are provided with correct types.

Example: Running terraform validate

Initialize without a backend and validate configuration syntax and internal consistency.

# Initialize without remote backend (for validation only)
$ terraform init -backend=false

Initializing provider plugins...
- Finding hashicorp/aws versions matching "~> 5.0"...
- Installing hashicorp/aws v5.31.0...

# Run validation
$ terraform validate

Success! The configuration is valid.

# JSON output for CI parsing
$ terraform validate -json | jq .
{
  "valid": true,
  "error_count": 0,
  "warning_count": 0,
  "diagnostics": []
}

# Example with errors
$ terraform validate -json | jq .
{
  "valid": false,
  "error_count": 2,
  "diagnostics": [
    {
      "severity": "error",
      "summary": "Unsupported argument",
      "detail": "An argument named \"instence_type\" is not expected here. Did you mean \"instance_type\"?",
      "range": {
        "filename": "main.tf",
        "start": { "line": 12, "column": 3 }
      }
    }
  ]
}
35.3.2 Type Checking

Validate catches type mismatches between variable declarations and their usage, ensuring that a number isn't passed where a string is required.

Example: Type Mismatch Detection

Validate catches when a variable of one type is used in a context requiring a different type.

# variables.tf — port declared as string (BUG!)
variable "app_port" {
  type    = string
  default = "8080"
}

# main.tf — used in numeric context
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "app" {
  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = var.app_port    # ⚠️ string used as number
  to_port           = var.app_port
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["10.0.0.0/8"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.app.id
}

# terraform validate catches this:
# Error: Incorrect attribute value type
#   from_port requires number, got string
What validate Catches What It Doesn't Catch
✅ Syntax errors in HCL ❌ Invalid resource configurations (e.g., wrong AMI)
✅ Type mismatches ❌ Permission errors
✅ Missing required arguments ❌ Resource naming violations
✅ Invalid attribute names ❌ Security misconfigurations
✅ Unsupported block types ❌ Cost implications
✅ Circular references ❌ Best practice violations
35.3.3 CI/CD Integration

In CI pipelines, use -json output with jq to parse validate results and generate structured reports.

Example: Validate in Multi-Module Project

Script to validate all Terraform directories in a monorepo, with JSON output for CI processing.

#!/bin/bash
# validate-all.sh — Validate all Terraform directories
set -euo pipefail

ERRORS=0
VALIDATED=0

for dir in $(find . -name "*.tf" -exec dirname {} \; | sort -u); do
  echo "📁 Validating: $dir"
  
  # Init without backend
  terraform -chdir="$dir" init -backend=false -input=false > /dev/null 2>&1
  
  # Validate and capture JSON
  RESULT=$(terraform -chdir="$dir" validate -json)
  VALID=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.valid')
  ERROR_COUNT=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.error_count')
  
  if [ "$VALID" = "true" ]; then
    echo "  ✅ Valid"
    VALIDATED=$((VALIDATED + 1))
  else
    echo "  ❌ Invalid ($ERROR_COUNT errors)"
    echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.diagnostics[] | "    \(.severity): \(.summary) (\(.range.filename):\(.range.start.line))"'
    ERRORS=$((ERRORS + ERROR_COUNT))
  fi
done

echo ""
echo "═══════════════════════════"
echo "Validated: $VALIDATED directories"
echo "Errors: $ERRORS"
echo "═══════════════════════════"

exit $ERRORS

35.4 terraform fmt -check

COMMAND
📚 Official Documentation: terraform fmt

The terraform fmt command rewrites Terraform configuration files to a canonical format and style. With -check, it acts as a linter — reporting files that don't match the standard style without modifying them.

💻 Practical Examples

35.4.1 Format Verification

The -check flag returns a non-zero exit code if any files need formatting, making it ideal for CI gates. The -diff flag shows exactly what would change.

Example: Format Check with Diff Output

Check formatting across all directories and show differences for files that don't match the canonical style.

# Check format — returns exit code 0 if all files pass
$ terraform fmt -check -recursive
main.tf
modules/vpc/variables.tf

# Show what would change (diff mode)
$ terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff
--- main.tf
+++ main.tf.formatted
@@ -1,6 +1,6 @@
 resource "aws_instance" "web" {
-  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
-  instance_type  = "t3.micro"
+  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
+  instance_type = "t3.micro"

   tags = {
-    Name    =      "web-server"
+    Name = "web-server"
   }
 }

# Auto-fix all formatting issues
$ terraform fmt -recursive -write=true
main.tf
modules/vpc/variables.tf

# List files that need formatting (for scripting)
$ terraform fmt -check -recursive -list=true | wc -l
2
Flag Purpose Exit Code
-check Check without modifying files 0 = pass, 3 = diff found
-diff Show formatting changes Same as -check
-recursive Process subdirectories —
-list=false Suppress filename output —
-write=true Auto-fix files in place Always 0
35.4.2 CI Enforcement

In CI pipelines, run fmt -check as a required status check. Developers should use editor plugins or pre-commit hooks to auto-format before pushing.

Example: Format Gate in GitLab CI

GitLab CI job that blocks merge requests with unformatted Terraform files.

# .gitlab-ci.yml
terraform-fmt:
  stage: validate
  image: hashicorp/terraform:1.7
  script:
    - terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"
      changes:
        - "**/*.tf"
        - "**/*.tfvars"
  allow_failure: false
⚡ Editor Integration:
Configure your editor to auto-format on save:
VS Code: Add "[terraform]": {"editor.formatOnSave": true} to settings.json
Vim: autocmd BufWritePre *.tf silent! execute '!terraform fmt %'
JetBrains: Enable "Reformat code" in File Watchers for .tf files

35.5 tflint - Terraform Linter

TOOL 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: TFLint GitHub | AWS Ruleset

TFLint is a pluggable Terraform linter that catches provider-specific errors that terraform validate misses — like invalid instance types, deprecated AMI usage, and naming convention violations.

💻 Practical Examples

35.5.1 Installation

TFLint supports multiple installation methods and runs on all major platforms.

Example: Installing TFLint

Installation methods for different platforms and CI environments.

# macOS via Homebrew
$ brew install tflint

# Linux — official install script
$ curl -s https://raw.githubusercontent.com/terraform-linters/tflint/master/install_linux.sh | bash

# Docker
$ docker run --rm -v $(pwd):/data -t ghcr.io/terraform-linters/tflint

# GitHub Actions
- uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@v4
  with:
    tflint_version: v0.50.3

# Verify installation
$ tflint --version
TFLint version 0.50.3
35.5.2 Rule Configuration

TFLint rules can be individually enabled, disabled, or configured with custom severity levels in the .tflint.hcl configuration file.

Example: Rule Configuration

Enable, disable, and customize rules for your project's specific needs.

# .tflint.hcl
config {
  # Module inspection (requires terraform init)
  module = true
  
  # Force mode - return error even for warnings
  force = false
}

# Enable the AWS ruleset plugin
plugin "aws" {
  enabled = true
  version = "0.30.0"
  source  = "github.com/terraform-linters/tflint-ruleset-aws"
}

# Customize individual rules
rule "terraform_naming_convention" {
  enabled = true
  format  = "snake_case"
}

rule "terraform_documented_variables" {
  enabled = true
}

rule "terraform_documented_outputs" {
  enabled = true
}

# Disable a specific rule
rule "terraform_unused_declarations" {
  enabled = false
}

# Set custom severity
rule "aws_instance_invalid_type" {
  enabled = true
}
35.5.3 AWS Plugin

The AWS plugin adds 200+ rules specific to AWS resources, catching invalid instance types, deprecated features, and service-specific misconfigurations.

Example: AWS Plugin Catching Invalid Instance Type

TFLint detects instance types that don't exist in AWS, which terraform validate would miss.

# main.tf — contains a typo in instance type
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t3.xmicro"    # ❌ Invalid type!

  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"
  }
}
# terraform validate says it's fine:
$ terraform validate
Success! The configuration is valid.

# tflint catches the real error:
$ tflint
1 issue(s) found:

Error: "t3.xmicro" is an invalid value as instance_type (aws_instance_invalid_type)

  on main.tf line 3:
   3:   instance_type = "t3.xmicro"
AWS Rule Category Examples Count
Invalid Types instance_type, db_instance_class 50+
Deprecated Features Previous generation instances 20+
Naming Conventions Resource naming patterns 30+
Best Practices Default VPC usage, missing tags 100+
35.5.4 Custom Rules

Write custom TFLint rules to enforce organization-specific policies like mandatory tags, naming conventions, or resource restrictions.

Example: Custom Rule — Enforce Required Tags

A custom tflint rule written in Go that ensures all AWS resources have the required tagging policy.

// rules/aws_required_tags.go
package rules

import (
    "fmt"
    "github.com/terraform-linters/tflint-plugin-sdk/tflint"
)

// AWSRequiredTagsRule checks for required tags
type AWSRequiredTagsRule struct {
    tflint.DefaultRule
}

func (r *AWSRequiredTagsRule) Name() string {
    return "aws_required_tags"
}

func (r *AWSRequiredTagsRule) Severity() tflint.Severity {
    return tflint.ERROR
}

func (r *AWSRequiredTagsRule) Check(runner tflint.Runner) error {
    requiredTags := []string{"Environment", "Team", "CostCenter"}
    
    // Check each AWS resource for tags
    resources, _ := runner.GetResourceContent("aws_instance",
        &hclext.BodySchema{
            Attributes: []hclext.AttributeSchema{
                {Name: "tags"},
            },
        }, nil)
    
    for _, resource := range resources.Blocks {
        tags := resource.Body.Attributes["tags"]
        for _, required := range requiredTags {
            if !hasTag(tags, required) {
                runner.EmitIssue(r,
                    fmt.Sprintf("Missing required tag: %s", required),
                    resource.DefRange)
            }
        }
    }
    return nil
}
35.5.5 .tflint.hcl Configuration

A production-ready TFLint configuration that balances thoroughness with developer experience, organized by rule category.

Example: Production TFLint Configuration

Complete .tflint.hcl for an AWS project with all recommended rules enabled.

# .tflint.hcl — Production Configuration
config {
  module     = true
  force      = false
}

# ── AWS Plugin ──────────────────────────────────
plugin "aws" {
  enabled = true
  version = "0.30.0"
  source  = "github.com/terraform-linters/tflint-ruleset-aws"
  
  # Deep checking mode (requires AWS credentials)
  deep_check = false
}

# ── Terraform Rules ─────────────────────────────
# Naming convention enforcement
rule "terraform_naming_convention" {
  enabled = true
  format  = "snake_case"
  
  custom_formats = {
    # Allow hyphens in resource names
    "aws_s3_bucket" = {
      format = "^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$"
    }
  }
}

# Require descriptions on all variables
rule "terraform_documented_variables" {
  enabled = true
}

# Require descriptions on all outputs
rule "terraform_documented_outputs" {
  enabled = true
}

# Require type declarations on variables
rule "terraform_typed_variables" {
  enabled = true
}

# Standard module structure
rule "terraform_standard_module_structure" {
  enabled = true
}

# Warn on deprecated interpolation syntax
rule "terraform_deprecated_interpolation" {
  enabled = true
}
🔧 TFLint vs terraform validate:
terraform validate checks HCL syntax and provider schema compliance. tflint goes deeper — it validates actual cloud resource values (like instance types), enforces naming conventions, and runs provider-specific rules. Always use both in your pipeline.

35.6 tfsec - Security Scanner

SECURITY 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: tfsec Documentation | Check Reference

tfsec (now part of Trivy) is a static security scanner for Terraform that detects potential security issues like open security groups, unencrypted storage, overly permissive IAM policies, and missing logging configurations.

💻 Practical Examples

35.6.1 Security Rule Categories

tfsec organizes its 400+ rules by cloud provider and service, each mapped to security frameworks like CIS Benchmarks and AWS Well-Architected.

Example: Running tfsec and Understanding Output

Scan a project and interpret the categorized security findings.

# Basic scan
$ tfsec .

Result #1 CRITICAL Security group rule allows ingress from public internet
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  main.tf:15-22
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   15 │   resource "aws_security_group_rule" "ssh" {
   16 │     type              = "ingress"
   17 │     from_port         = 22
   18 │     to_port           = 22
   19 │     protocol          = "tcp"
   20 │     cidr_blocks       = ["0.0.0.0/0"]   # ← FLAGGED
   21 │     security_group_id = aws_security_group.main.id
   22 │   }
───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ID:         aws-ec2-no-public-ingress-sgr
  Impact:     Your port exposed to the internet
  Resolution: Set a more restrictive cidr range
  See:        https://avd.aquasec.com/misconfig/avd-aws-0107

  passed: 47, ignored: 0, warnings: 3, failures: 5
Category Rule Count Examples
AWS EC2 30+ Public SG, unencrypted EBS, metadata v1
AWS S3 15+ Public access, missing encryption, no versioning
AWS IAM 20+ Wildcard actions, no MFA, overly permissive
AWS RDS 15+ Public access, no encryption, no backups
Azure / GCP 150+ Provider-specific security checks
General 50+ Sensitive values, hardcoded secrets
35.6.2 Severity Levels

Filter findings by severity to focus on critical issues first. Use --minimum-severity to set a threshold for CI gates.

Example: Severity-Based Scanning

Run tfsec with severity filtering and multiple output formats.

# Only show CRITICAL and HIGH findings
$ tfsec . --minimum-severity HIGH

# Generate SARIF report for GitHub Security tab
$ tfsec . --format sarif --out tfsec-results.sarif

# Generate JUnit XML for CI integration
$ tfsec . --format junit --out tfsec-results.xml

# JSON output for custom processing
$ tfsec . --format json | jq '.results | group_by(.severity) | 
  map({severity: .[0].severity, count: length})'
[
  {"severity": "CRITICAL", "count": 2},
  {"severity": "HIGH", "count": 5},
  {"severity": "MEDIUM", "count": 8},
  {"severity": "LOW", "count": 3}
]

# Exclude specific directories
$ tfsec . --exclude-path examples/ --exclude-path test/fixtures/

# Soft fail mode (report but don't block)
$ tfsec . --soft-fail
35.6.3 Ignoring False Positives

Some findings are intentional or not applicable to your context. Use inline comments or a configuration file to suppress specific findings with documented reasons.

Example: Suppressing False Positives

Multiple methods for ignoring specific tfsec findings with audit trails.

# Method 1: Inline comment ignore (with reason)
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "alb_http" {
  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = 80
  to_port           = 80
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["0.0.0.0/0"]  #tfsec:ignore:aws-ec2-no-public-ingress-sgr ALB must accept public HTTP
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.alb.id
}

# Method 2: Block-level ignore
#tfsec:ignore:aws-s3-enable-versioning This is a temp build artifacts bucket
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "build_artifacts" {
  bucket = "company-build-artifacts-temp"
}

# Method 3: Expiring ignore (auto-re-enables after date)
#tfsec:ignore:aws-ec2-no-public-ingress-sgr:exp:2025-06-01
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "temp_debug" {
  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = 8080
  to_port           = 8080
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.debug.id
}
35.6.4 Custom Checks

Write custom tfsec checks in YAML to enforce organization-specific security policies beyond the built-in rules.

Example: Custom tfsec Check

Define a custom check that ensures all S3 buckets have a specific tag pattern.

# .tfsec/custom_checks.yaml
checks:
  - code: CUSTOM-001
    description: S3 buckets must have a DataClassification tag
    impact: Data classification is required for compliance
    resolution: Add a DataClassification tag with value public|internal|confidential|restricted
    requiredTypes:
      - resource
    requiredLabels:
      - aws_s3_bucket
    severity: HIGH
    matchSpec:
      name: tags
      action: contains
      value: DataClassification

  - code: CUSTOM-002
    description: RDS instances must not use default parameter groups
    impact: Default parameter groups share settings across all instances
    resolution: Create a custom parameter group for each RDS instance
    requiredTypes:
      - resource
    requiredLabels:
      - aws_db_instance
    severity: MEDIUM
    matchSpec:
      name: parameter_group_name
      action: notEquals
      value: "default"
🔄 tfsec → Trivy Migration:
tfsec has been integrated into Trivy as of 2023. While tfsec still works standalone, new features only go into Trivy. Migrate with: trivy config . (replaces tfsec .). All tfsec checks are included in Trivy's misconfiguration scanner.

35.7 checkov - Policy Scanner

SECURITY 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Checkov Documentation | Policy Index

Checkov by Bridgecrew (Palo Alto Networks) is a policy-as-code framework with 1,000+ built-in policies for Terraform, covering CIS Benchmarks, SOC2, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and NIST frameworks with Python-based custom policy extensibility.

💻 Practical Examples

35.7.1 Built-in Policies

Checkov ships with over 1,000 policies organized by compliance framework, making it trivial to check configurations against industry standards.

Example: Running Checkov with Framework Filtering

Scan a project against specific compliance frameworks and generate structured output.

# Install
$ pip install checkov

# Basic scan
$ checkov -d . --framework terraform

# Scan against specific CIS checks
$ checkov -d . --check CKV_AWS_18,CKV_AWS_19,CKV_AWS_20,CKV_AWS_21

# Target specific compliance framework
$ checkov -d . --framework terraform \
  --bc-api-key $BC_API_KEY \
  --repo-id company/infra

# Output formats
$ checkov -d . --output json --output-file checkov-report.json
$ checkov -d . --output junitxml --output-file checkov-results.xml
$ checkov -d . --output sarif --output-file checkov-results.sarif

# Compact output for CI
$ checkov -d . --compact --quiet

# Example output
Passed checks: 142, Failed checks: 8, Skipped checks: 2

Check: CKV_AWS_18: "Ensure the S3 bucket has access logging enabled"
  FAILED for resource: aws_s3_bucket.data
  File: /storage.tf:1-10
  Guide: https://docs.prismacloud.io/en/enterprise-edition/policy-reference/aws/s3/s3-13

Check: CKV_AWS_145: "Ensure S3 bucket is encrypted with KMS"
  FAILED for resource: aws_s3_bucket.data
  File: /storage.tf:1-10
Framework Policy Count Example Checks
CIS AWS Benchmark 200+ S3 encryption, CloudTrail logging, VPC flow logs
SOC2 150+ Access controls, encryption, monitoring
HIPAA 100+ PHI encryption, audit trails, access logs
PCI-DSS 80+ Network segmentation, encryption at rest
NIST 800-53 250+ Full federal security controls
35.7.2 Custom Policies (Python)

Write custom Checkov policies in Python for organization-specific requirements that aren't covered by built-in checks.

Example: Custom Python Policy

A custom Checkov policy that ensures all EC2 instances use encrypted EBS volumes with a specific KMS key.

# custom_policies/ec2_encrypted_ebs.py
from checkov.terraform.checks.resource.base_resource_check import BaseResourceCheck
from checkov.common.models.enums import CheckResult, CheckCategories

class EC2EncryptedEBS(BaseResourceCheck):
    def __init__(self):
        name = "Ensure EC2 root volumes use customer-managed KMS keys"
        id = "CUSTOM_AWS_001"
        supported_resources = ["aws_instance"]
        categories = [CheckCategories.ENCRYPTION]
        super().__init__(
            name=name,
            id=id,
            categories=categories,
            supported_resources=supported_resources
        )

    def scan_resource_conf(self, conf):
        # Check root_block_device configuration
        root_block = conf.get("root_block_device", [{}])
        if isinstance(root_block, list):
            root_block = root_block[0] if root_block else {}
        
        encrypted = root_block.get("encrypted", [False])
        kms_key = root_block.get("kms_key_id", [""])
        
        if encrypted == [True] and kms_key != [""]:
            return CheckResult.PASSED
        return CheckResult.FAILED

check = EC2EncryptedEBS()
# Run with custom policies
$ checkov -d . --external-checks-dir ./custom_policies
$ checkov -d . --check CUSTOM_AWS_001
35.7.3 Baseline Generation

For existing projects with many findings, use baselines to track only new violations — perfect for gradually improving security posture without blocking all development.

Example: Baseline Workflow

Generate a baseline of existing issues and only fail CI on new violations.

# Step 1: Generate baseline from current state
$ checkov -d . --framework terraform --output json > .checkov.baseline

# Step 2: Run future scans against the baseline
$ checkov -d . --baseline .checkov.baseline

# Only NEW failures will be reported
# Existing (baselined) failures are suppressed

# Step 3: Inline skip for known exceptions
# In your .tf file:
# checkov:skip=CKV_AWS_18: Access logging not needed for public static site
📊 checkov vs tfsec Comparison:
checkov: 1,000+ policies, Python custom checks, multi-framework compliance maps, Prisma Cloud integration, baseline support.
tfsec: 400+ rules, YAML custom checks, faster execution, simpler output. Now merged into Trivy.
Recommendation: Use checkov for compliance-heavy environments; use tfsec/trivy for speed-focused pipelines. Or run both!

35.8 terrascan

SECURITY
📚 Official Documentation: Terrascan Documentation | GitHub Repository

Terrascan by Tenable detects compliance and security violations using Open Policy Agent (OPA) Rego policies, supporting Terraform, Kubernetes, Helm, and Dockerfiles with 500+ built-in policies.

💻 Practical Examples

35.8.1 Policy Library

Terrascan's built-in policy library covers AWS, Azure, GCP, and Kubernetes with policies mapped to CIS Benchmarks and compliance frameworks.

Example: Running Terrascan

Scan Terraform configurations with various output formats and policy filters.

# Install
$ brew install terrascan
# or
$ curl -L "$(curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/tenable/terrascan/releases/latest \
  | grep -o -E 'https://.+?_Linux_x86_64.tar.gz')" | tar -xz -C /usr/local/bin

# Basic scan
$ terrascan scan -t aws

# Scan with specific policy type
$ terrascan scan -t aws -p /path/to/custom-policies

# JSON output for CI
$ terrascan scan -t aws -o json

# Only show violations (not passed)
$ terrascan scan -t aws --verbose

# Scan specific directory
$ terrascan scan -t aws -d ./modules/networking

# Example output
Violation Details -
  Description    : S3 bucket has public access enabled
  File           : storage.tf
  Module Name    : root
  Plan Root      : ./
  Line           : 1
  Severity       : HIGH
  Rule Name      : s3BucketPublicAccess
  Rule ID        : AC_AWS_0208
  Resource Name  : aws_s3_bucket.public_data
  Resource Type  : aws_s3_bucket
  Category       : Data Protection

Scan Summary -
  File/Folder    : ./
  IaC Type       : terraform
  Scanned At     : 2025-01-15 10:30:00
  Policies Validated : 287
  Violated Policies  : 5
  Low: 1, Medium: 2, High: 2, Critical: 0
35.8.2 Custom Policies (Rego)

Write custom policies in OPA Rego language for fine-grained control over what Terrascan checks, with full access to the resource attributes.

Example: Custom Rego Policy

A custom Terrascan policy written in Rego that enforces encryption on all EBS volumes.

# policies/aws/ebs_encryption.rego
package accurics

# Policy metadata
{{.prefix}}{{.name}}{{.suffix}}[retVal] {
    resource := input.aws_ebs_volume[_]
    resource.config.encrypted != true

    retVal := {
        "Id": resource.id,
        "ReplaceType": "edit",
        "CodeType": "attribute",
        "Traverse": "encrypted",
        "Attribute": "encrypted",
        "AttributeDataType": "bool",
        "Expected": true,
        "Actual": resource.config.encrypted
    }
}
// policies/aws/ebs_encryption.json (policy metadata)
{
    "name": "ebsVolumeEncryption",
    "file": "ebs_encryption.rego",
    "policy_type": "aws",
    "resource_type": "aws_ebs_volume",
    "template_args": {
        "prefix": "",
        "name": "ebsVolumeEncryption",
        "suffix": ""
    },
    "severity": "HIGH",
    "description": "Ensure all EBS volumes are encrypted at rest",
    "category": "Data Protection",
    "version": 1,
    "id": "AC_CUSTOM_001"
}
Scanner Custom Policy Language Built-in Policies Best For
tfsec YAML 400+ Quick security checks, simple custom rules
checkov Python 1,000+ Compliance frameworks, complex logic
terrascan Rego (OPA) 500+ OPA ecosystem, multi-IaC scanning
trivy Rego (OPA) All tfsec + more Unified scanning (IaC + containers + SCA)

35.9 trivy for IaC

SECURITY
📚 Official Documentation: Trivy Documentation | Misconfiguration Scanning

Trivy is a comprehensive security scanner that combines IaC misconfiguration detection (absorbing tfsec), container vulnerability scanning, secret detection, and SCA in a single tool — making it the Swiss Army knife of DevSecOps.

💻 Practical Examples

35.9.1 Misconfiguration Scanning

Trivy's config scanner detects security misconfigurations in Terraform, CloudFormation, Kubernetes, Dockerfile, and more — all through one command.

Example: Trivy IaC Misconfiguration Scan

Comprehensive Terraform security scan with Trivy including all tfsec rules plus additional checks.

# Install Trivy
$ brew install trivy
# or
$ curl -sfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/contrib/install.sh | sh

# Scan Terraform directory for misconfigurations
$ trivy config .

# Severity filtering
$ trivy config --severity CRITICAL,HIGH .

# Specific file scan
$ trivy config --file-patterns "terraform:*.tf" .

# Output formats
$ trivy config --format json --output trivy-results.json .
$ trivy config --format sarif --output trivy-results.sarif .
$ trivy config --format table .

# Example output
main.tf (terraform)
═══════════════════════════════════════════
Tests: 142 (SUCCESSES: 134, FAILURES: 8, EXCEPTIONS: 0)
Failures: 8 (CRITICAL: 1, HIGH: 3, MEDIUM: 3, LOW: 1)

CRITICAL: Security group allows open ingress to port 22
═══════════════════════════════════════════
S3 bucket does not have access logging enabled.
See https://avd.aquasec.com/misconfig/avd-aws-0089

───────────────────────────────────────────
 main.tf:15-22
───────────────────────────────────────────
  15 │   resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
  16 │     bucket = "company-data-bucket"
  17 │   }
35.9.2 Secret Detection

Trivy scans Terraform files for accidentally committed secrets — API keys, passwords, tokens, and private keys that shouldn't be in source control.

Example: Secret Detection in Terraform Files

Detect hardcoded secrets and credentials in Terraform configuration files.

# Scan for secrets in the filesystem
$ trivy fs --scanners secret .

# Example findings
SECRET: AWS Access Key ID found
Category: AWS
Severity: CRITICAL
File:    providers.tf
Line:    5
Match:   access_key = "AKIA*************"

SECRET: Generic High Entropy String
Category: General  
Severity: HIGH
File:    variables.tf
Line:    22
Match:   default = "sk-proj-abc123..."

# Combined scan: misconfigurations + secrets + vulnerabilities
$ trivy fs --scanners config,secret,vuln .
🏆 Recommended Tool Strategy:
For most teams, we recommend this combination:
Pre-commit: terraform fmt + validate + tflint (fast local checks)
CI Pipeline: trivy config + trivy fs --scanners secret (unified security)
Compliance: checkov with framework targeting (SOC2, HIPAA, etc.)
This gives layered coverage without excessive pipeline time.
🔗 🧪 🏗️ 🧹
CHAPTER 36

Integration Testing

Terratest Kitchen-Terraform LocalStack Test Fixtures Cleanup Contract Testing
6 Sections
22 Subsections
25+ Code Examples
3 Frameworks
PART 10 EXPERT ★★★★★

Deploy real infrastructure in isolated test environments, validate behavior with Go-based Terratest and Ruby-based Kitchen-Terraform, use LocalStack for AWS testing without cloud costs, and implement robust cleanup strategies to prevent orphaned resources.

Learning Objectives

  • Write Go-based integration tests with Terratest
  • Structure test fixtures with terraform.Options and retry logic
  • Validate HTTP endpoints, SSH connectivity, and AWS SDK assertions
  • Run parallel integration tests safely
  • Use LocalStack for offline AWS testing
  • Implement contract tests for module interfaces

Chapter Contents

36.1 Terratest (Go-based Testing) 36.2 Kitchen-Terraform (Ruby) 36.3 LocalStack for AWS Testing 36.4 Test Fixtures and Helpers 36.5 Cleanup Strategies 36.6 Contract Testing

36.1 Terratest (Go-based Testing)

FRAMEWORK 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terratest Documentation | GitHub Repository

Terratest is a Go library by Gruntwork that provides patterns and helper functions for testing infrastructure code. It deploys real resources, validates them with assertions, and tears everything down — the gold standard for Terraform integration testing.

💻 Practical Examples

36.1.1 Setup and Installation

Terratest is a Go module — you'll need Go 1.21+ and a standard Go project structure for your tests.

Example: Terratest Project Setup

Initialize a Go module for Terratest and install dependencies.

# Project structure
modules/vpc/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
└── test/
    ├── go.mod
    ├── go.sum
    └── vpc_test.go

# Initialize Go module
$ cd modules/vpc/test
$ go mod init github.com/company/infra/modules/vpc/test

# Install Terratest
$ go get github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/terraform
$ go get github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/aws
$ go get github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/http-helper
$ go get github.com/stretchr/testify/assert

# Run tests
$ go test -v -timeout 30m
36.1.2 Test Structure

Every Terratest test follows a consistent pattern: configure options, deploy with InitAndApply, validate with assertions, and destroy with defer.

Example: Basic Terratest VPC Test

Deploy a VPC module, verify it created the expected resources, and tear it down.

// test/vpc_test.go
package test

import (
    "testing"
    "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/terraform"
    "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/aws"
    "github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
)

func TestVpcModule(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    // Configure Terraform options
    terraformOptions := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        // Path to the Terraform code to test
        TerraformDir: "../",

        // Variables to pass
        Vars: map[string]interface{}{
            "vpc_cidr":     "10.99.0.0/16",
            "environment":  "test",
            "project_name": "terratest-vpc",
        },

        // Environment variables
        EnvVars: map[string]string{
            "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION": "us-east-1",
        },
    })

    // Clean up resources when test completes
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, terraformOptions)

    // Deploy the infrastructure
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, terraformOptions)

    // Get outputs
    vpcId := terraform.Output(t, terraformOptions, "vpc_id")
    publicSubnets := terraform.OutputList(t, terraformOptions, "public_subnet_ids")
    privateSubnets := terraform.OutputList(t, terraformOptions, "private_subnet_ids")

    // Assertions
    assert.NotEmpty(t, vpcId)
    assert.Equal(t, 3, len(publicSubnets))
    assert.Equal(t, 3, len(privateSubnets))

    // Verify VPC exists in AWS
    vpc := aws.GetVpcById(t, vpcId, "us-east-1")
    assert.Equal(t, "10.99.0.0/16", vpc.CidrBlock)

    // Verify tags
    assert.Equal(t, "test", vpc.Tags["Environment"])
}
36.1.3 terraform.Options

The terraform.Options struct controls all aspects of how Terratest interacts with Terraform — directory, variables, backend config, and more.

Example: Advanced terraform.Options Configuration

Comprehensive options including backend override, plan-only mode, and lock configuration.

terraformOptions := &terraform.Options{
    // Directory containing Terraform code
    TerraformDir: "../examples/complete",

    // Input variables
    Vars: map[string]interface{}{
        "region":       "us-west-2",
        "environment":  "test",
        "instance_type": "t3.micro",
        "enable_monitoring": true,
        "tags": map[string]string{
            "ManagedBy": "terratest",
            "TestRun":   t.Name(),
        },
    },

    // Variable files
    VarFiles: []string{
        "fixtures/test.tfvars",
    },

    // Backend configuration override
    BackendConfig: map[string]interface{}{
        "bucket": "test-terraform-state",
        "key":    fmt.Sprintf("test/%s/terraform.tfstate", t.Name()),
        "region": "us-east-1",
    },

    // Environment variables
    EnvVars: map[string]string{
        "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION": "us-west-2",
        "TF_LOG":            "WARN",
    },

    // Terraform binary (useful for testing specific versions)
    TerraformBinary: "terraform",

    // Disable colors for CI
    NoColor: true,

    // Parallelism
    Parallelism: 10,

    // Lock timeout
    Lock:        true,
    LockTimeout: "10m",

    // Plan file (for plan-then-apply workflows)
    PlanFilePath: "/tmp/tfplan",

    // Retry settings for transient errors
    RetryableTerraformErrors: map[string]string{
        ".*TooManyRequestsException.*":     "AWS rate limit",
        ".*connection reset by peer.*":      "Network hiccup",
