        ".*timeout while waiting.*":         "Resource creation timeout",
    },
    MaxRetries:         3,
    TimeBetweenRetries: 5 * time.Second,
}
Option Type Purpose
TerraformDir string Path to Terraform configuration directory
Vars map[string]interface{} Input variables (like -var)
VarFiles []string Variable files (like -var-file)
BackendConfig map[string]interface{} Backend configuration override
EnvVars map[string]string Environment variables for terraform commands
Targets []string Resource targeting (like -target)
36.1.4 Resource Assertions

Terratest provides cloud-specific helper modules to query real resources and assert their properties match expectations.

Example: AWS Resource Assertions

Verify that deployed resources have the expected properties using AWS SDK helper functions.

func TestEC2Instance(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../examples/ec2",
        Vars: map[string]interface{}{
            "instance_type": "t3.micro",
            "environment":  "test",
        },
    })

    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    // Get instance ID from output
    instanceId := terraform.Output(t, opts, "instance_id")
    region := "us-east-1"

    // Assert instance properties
    instance := aws.GetInstanceById(t, instanceId, region)
    assert.Equal(t, "t3.micro", instance.InstanceType)
    assert.Equal(t, "running", instance.State)

    // Assert Security Group
    sgId := terraform.Output(t, opts, "security_group_id")
    sg := aws.GetSecurityGroupById(t, sgId, region)
    assert.Contains(t, sg.Name, "test")

    // Assert S3 bucket properties
    bucketName := terraform.Output(t, opts, "bucket_name")
    aws.AssertS3BucketExists(t, region, bucketName)
    aws.AssertS3BucketVersioningExists(t, region, bucketName)

    // Assert RDS
    dbEndpoint := terraform.Output(t, opts, "db_endpoint")
    assert.NotEmpty(t, dbEndpoint)
    assert.Contains(t, dbEndpoint, ".rds.amazonaws.com")
}
36.1.5 HTTP Testing

Validate that deployed web services respond correctly on expected endpoints with expected status codes and body content.

Example: HTTP Endpoint Validation

Test that an ALB health check endpoint returns 200 OK after deployment.

import (
    http_helper "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/http-helper"
)

func TestAlbEndpoint(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../examples/alb",
    })

    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    // Get the ALB DNS name
    albDns := terraform.Output(t, opts, "alb_dns_name")
    url := fmt.Sprintf("http://%s/health", albDns)

    // Retry HTTP check — ALB takes time to become healthy
    maxRetries := 30
    sleepBetweenRetries := 10 * time.Second
    expectedStatus := 200
    expectedBody := "OK"

    http_helper.HttpGetWithRetry(
        t,
        url,
        nil,   // TLS config
        expectedStatus,
        expectedBody,
        maxRetries,
        sleepBetweenRetries,
    )

    // Custom validation function
    http_helper.HttpGetWithRetryWithCustomValidation(
        t, url, nil, maxRetries, sleepBetweenRetries,
        func(status int, body string) bool {
            return status == 200 && strings.Contains(body, "healthy")
        },
    )
}
36.1.6 SSH Testing

Verify that deployed EC2 instances are accessible via SSH and running expected services.

Example: SSH into EC2 and Run Commands

SSH into a deployed instance and verify that nginx is running.

import (
    "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/ssh"
)

func TestSshAccess(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    // Generate a temporary SSH key pair
    keyPair := ssh.GenerateRSAKeyPair(t, 2048)

    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../examples/ec2",
        Vars: map[string]interface{}{
            "public_key": keyPair.PublicKey,
        },
    })

    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    publicIp := terraform.Output(t, opts, "public_ip")

    // Configure SSH host
    host := ssh.Host{
        Hostname:    publicIp,
        SshKeyPair:  keyPair,
        SshUserName: "ec2-user",
    }

    // Wait for SSH to become available
    maxRetries := 30
    sleepBetweenRetries := 10 * time.Second
    ssh.CheckSshConnectionWithRetry(t, host, maxRetries, sleepBetweenRetries)

    // Run commands and check output
    output := ssh.CheckSshCommand(t, host, "nginx -v 2>&1")
    assert.Contains(t, output, "nginx")

    // Check service status
    output = ssh.CheckSshCommand(t, host, "systemctl is-active nginx")
    assert.Equal(t, "active", strings.TrimSpace(output))
}
36.1.7 AWS SDK Assertions

Use Terratest's AWS helper functions to query real resources and make assertions about their configuration.

Example: Comprehensive AWS Assertions

Verify multiple AWS resource properties including tags, encryption, and networking.

func TestAwsResources(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../",
    })
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    region := "us-east-1"

    // ── VPC Assertions ────────────────────────
    vpcId := terraform.Output(t, opts, "vpc_id")
    subnets := aws.GetSubnetsForVpc(t, vpcId, region)
    assert.Equal(t, 6, len(subnets))  // 3 public + 3 private

    // ── S3 Assertions ─────────────────────────
    bucket := terraform.Output(t, opts, "bucket_name")
    aws.AssertS3BucketExists(t, region, bucket)
    
    policy := aws.GetS3BucketPolicy(t, region, bucket)
    assert.Contains(t, policy, "ssl-requests-only")

    // ── IAM Assertions ────────────────────────
    roleName := terraform.Output(t, opts, "role_name")
    role := aws.GetIamRole(t, region, roleName)
    assert.Contains(t, role.AssumeRolePolicyDocument, "ecs-tasks.amazonaws.com")

    // ── Tags Assertions ───────────────────────
    tags := aws.GetTagsForVpc(t, vpcId, region)
    assert.Equal(t, "production", tags["Environment"])
    assert.Equal(t, "platform-team", tags["Team"])
}
36.1.8 Retry Logic

Cloud resources take time to become available. Terratest provides retry helpers to handle eventual consistency gracefully.

Example: Custom Retry with Backoff

Retry assertions with exponential backoff for resources that take time to stabilize.

import (
    "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/retry"
)

func TestWithRetry(t *testing.T) {
    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../",
    })
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    endpoint := terraform.Output(t, opts, "api_endpoint")

    // Retry with custom logic
    retry.DoWithRetry(t, "Wait for API to be ready", 30, 10*time.Second,
        func() (string, error) {
            resp, err := http.Get(endpoint + "/health")
            if err != nil {
                return "", err
            }
            defer resp.Body.Close()
            
            if resp.StatusCode != 200 {
                return "", fmt.Errorf("expected 200, got %d", resp.StatusCode)
            }
            return "API is healthy", nil
        },
    )
}
36.1.9 Parallel Testing

Run multiple integration tests in parallel using unique naming to avoid resource conflicts. Each test gets its own isolated namespace.

Example: Parallel-Safe Test Naming

Use unique IDs to prevent resource name collisions across parallel test runs.

import (
    "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/random"
)

func TestParallelVpc(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()  // Enable parallel execution

    // Generate unique ID for this test run
    uniqueId := random.UniqueId()
    name := fmt.Sprintf("terratest-vpc-%s", uniqueId)

    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../",
        Vars: map[string]interface{}{
            "project_name": name,
            "vpc_cidr":     fmt.Sprintf("10.%d.0.0/16", random.Random(1, 254)),
        },
    })

    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    // Assertions...
    vpcId := terraform.Output(t, opts, "vpc_id")
    assert.NotEmpty(t, vpcId)
}
⚠️ Parallel Testing Caution:
When running tests in parallel, each test creates real AWS resources. Ensure:
• Unique resource names (use random.UniqueId())
• Non-overlapping CIDR ranges for VPCs
• Adequate AWS service quotas
• Separate state files per test
A typical integration test suite costs $2-10 per run in AWS charges.

36.2 Kitchen-Terraform (Ruby)

FRAMEWORK
📚 Official Documentation: Kitchen-Terraform GitHub | InSpec

Kitchen-Terraform integrates Test Kitchen with Terraform and InSpec, providing a Ruby-based alternative to Terratest. It uses InSpec controls to verify infrastructure compliance against profiles.

💻 Practical Examples

36.2.1 InSpec Controls

InSpec is a compliance testing framework that describes infrastructure expectations in human-readable Ruby DSL.

Example: InSpec Controls for AWS Resources

Write compliance controls that verify the deployed infrastructure matches security requirements.

# test/integration/default/controls/vpc_test.rb
control 'vpc-01' do
  impact 1.0
  title 'VPC Configuration'
  desc 'Verify VPC is configured correctly'

  # Get VPC ID from Terraform output
  vpc_id = attribute('vpc_id')

  describe aws_vpc(vpc_id) do
    it { should exist }
    its('cidr_block') { should eq '10.0.0.0/16' }
    its('state') { should eq 'available' }
    it { should_not be_default }
  end

  # Verify DNS settings
  describe aws_vpc(vpc_id) do
    it { should have_dns_support }
    it { should have_dns_hostnames }
  end
end

control 'sg-01' do
  impact 0.9
  title 'Security Group Restrictions'
  desc 'Verify no security groups allow unrestricted SSH'

  aws_security_groups.group_ids.each do |sg_id|
    describe aws_security_group(sg_id) do
      it { should_not allow_in(port: 22, ipv4_range: '0.0.0.0/0') }
    end
  end
end

control 's3-01' do
  impact 1.0
  title 'S3 Bucket Security'
  
  bucket_name = attribute('bucket_name')

  describe aws_s3_bucket(bucket_name) do
    it { should exist }
    it { should_not be_public }
    it { should have_versioning_enabled }
    it { should have_default_encryption_enabled }
  end
end
36.2.2 Test Kitchen Workflow

Test Kitchen orchestrates the create → converge → verify → destroy lifecycle for testing Terraform modules.

Example: Kitchen-Terraform Configuration

Complete .kitchen.yml and workflow for testing a Terraform module with InSpec.

# .kitchen.yml
driver:
  name: terraform
  root_module_directory: test/fixtures/default
  command_timeout: 1800      # 30-minute timeout
  verify_version: true

provisioner:
  name: terraform

verifier:
  name: terraform
  systems:
    - name: default
      backend: aws
      controls:
        - vpc-01
        - sg-01
        - s3-01
      profile_locations:
        - test/integration/default

platforms:
  - name: aws

suites:
  - name: default
    driver:
      variables:
        environment: test
        region: us-east-1
    verifier:
      systems:
        - name: default
          attrs_outputs:
            vpc_id: vpc_id
            bucket_name: bucket_name
# Kitchen-Terraform Workflow
$ kitchen create     # terraform init
$ kitchen converge   # terraform apply
$ kitchen verify     # run InSpec controls
$ kitchen destroy    # terraform destroy

# Or all-in-one:
$ kitchen test       # create → converge → verify → destroy
Framework Language Strengths Best For
Terratest Go Speed, AWS SDK integration, flexible Teams comfortable with Go
Kitchen-Terraform Ruby InSpec compliance, readable DSL Compliance-focused testing
terraform test HCL Native, no external deps Unit tests, module validation

36.3 LocalStack for AWS Testing

TOOL 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: LocalStack Docs | Terraform Integration

LocalStack emulates AWS cloud APIs on your local machine, enabling integration tests without incurring cloud costs or requiring network access. It supports 50+ AWS services and can be run in Docker for CI/CD pipelines.

💻 Practical Examples

36.3.1 LocalStack Pro vs Community

LocalStack offers a free Community edition with basic AWS services and a Pro edition with advanced services like RDS, ECS, and EKS.

Example: LocalStack Docker Setup

Start LocalStack with Docker Compose for local testing.

# docker-compose.yml
version: "3.8"
services:
  localstack:
    image: localstack/localstack:latest
    container_name: localstack
    ports:
      - "4566:4566"       # Gateway port
      - "4510-4559:4510-4559"  # Service ports
    environment:
      - SERVICES=s3,sqs,dynamodb,lambda,iam,ec2
      - DEBUG=0
      - PERSISTENCE=1
      - DOCKER_HOST=unix:///var/run/docker.sock
    volumes:
      - "./localstack-data:/var/lib/localstack"
      - "/var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock"
Feature Community (Free) Pro ($35/mo)
S3, SQS, SNS, DynamoDB ✅ ✅
Lambda, API Gateway ✅ ✅
IAM, CloudFormation ✅ ✅
RDS, ElastiCache ❌ ✅
ECS, EKS ❌ ✅
State persistence ❌ ✅
CI/CD integration Basic Full
36.3.2 Provider Endpoint Configuration

Point the AWS provider to LocalStack's endpoints to run Terraform commands against the local emulator.

Example: Terraform Provider for LocalStack

Configure the AWS provider to use LocalStack endpoints for local testing.

# providers_localstack.tf
provider "aws" {
  region                      = "us-east-1"
  access_key                  = "test"
  secret_key                  = "test"
  skip_credentials_validation = true
  skip_metadata_api_check     = true
  skip_requesting_account_id  = true

  endpoints {
    s3       = "http://localhost:4566"
    sqs      = "http://localhost:4566"
    dynamodb = "http://localhost:4566"
    lambda   = "http://localhost:4566"
    iam      = "http://localhost:4566"
    ec2      = "http://localhost:4566"
    sts      = "http://localhost:4566"
  }

  # Force path-style for S3 (required for LocalStack)
  s3_use_path_style = true
}

# ── Resources work exactly the same ──
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "test" {
  bucket = "my-test-bucket"
}

resource "aws_sqs_queue" "test" {
  name = "my-test-queue"
}
Example: Terratest with LocalStack

Use Terratest to test against LocalStack instead of real AWS.

func TestWithLocalStack(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    opts := &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../examples/localstack",
        EnvVars: map[string]string{
            "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION":    "us-east-1",
            "AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID":     "test",
            "AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY": "test",
        },
    }

    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    // Verify S3 bucket was created in LocalStack
    bucketName := terraform.Output(t, opts, "bucket_name")
    
    // Use custom AWS SDK client pointing to LocalStack
    sess := session.Must(session.NewSession(&aws_sdk.Config{
        Region:           aws_sdk.String("us-east-1"),
        Endpoint:         aws_sdk.String("http://localhost:4566"),
        S3ForcePathStyle: aws_sdk.Bool(true),
        Credentials:      credentials.NewStaticCredentials("test", "test", ""),
    }))
    
    s3Client := s3.New(sess)
    _, err := s3Client.HeadBucket(&s3.HeadBucketInput{
        Bucket: aws_sdk.String(bucketName),
    })
    assert.NoError(t, err)
}
36.3.3 Test Isolation

Use ephemeral LocalStack containers per test to ensure complete isolation between test runs.

Example: Per-Test LocalStack Container

Spin up a fresh LocalStack container for each test using Testcontainers.

import (
    "github.com/testcontainers/testcontainers-go"
    "github.com/testcontainers/testcontainers-go/wait"
)

func startLocalStack(t *testing.T) (string, func()) {
    ctx := context.Background()

    req := testcontainers.ContainerRequest{
        Image:        "localstack/localstack:latest",
        ExposedPorts: []string{"4566/tcp"},
        Env: map[string]string{
            "SERVICES": "s3,sqs,dynamodb",
        },
        WaitingFor: wait.ForLog("Ready.").WithStartupTimeout(60 * time.Second),
    }

    container, err := testcontainers.GenericContainer(ctx,
        testcontainers.GenericContainerRequest{
            ContainerRequest: req,
            Started:          true,
        },
    )
    require.NoError(t, err)

    host, _ := container.Host(ctx)
    port, _ := container.MappedPort(ctx, "4566")
    endpoint := fmt.Sprintf("http://%s:%s", host, port.Port())

    cleanup := func() { container.Terminate(ctx) }
    return endpoint, cleanup
}

func TestIsolatedS3(t *testing.T) {
    endpoint, cleanup := startLocalStack(t)
    defer cleanup()

    opts := &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../",
        Vars: map[string]interface{}{
            "localstack_endpoint": endpoint,
        },
    }
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)
}
💡 LocalStack Best Practice:
LocalStack is ideal for unit-level integration tests — verifying that your HCL creates the right resources with the right properties. However, it cannot validate cloud-specific behavior like IAM permission boundaries, cross-account access, or network latency. Always complement LocalStack tests with real-cloud integration tests in a sandbox account.

36.4 Test Fixtures and Helpers

PATTERN

Organize reusable test infrastructure with fixture directories and shared Go helper functions to keep tests DRY and maintainable.

💻 Practical Examples

36.4.1 Example Directories

Use examples/ directories as test fixtures — small, complete Terraform configurations that exercise specific module features.

Example: Test Fixture Directory Structure

Organize test fixtures as reusable example configurations.

modules/vpc/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── examples/
│   ├── simple/               # Minimal configuration
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── outputs.tf
│   │   └── terraform.tfvars
│   ├── complete/             # Full-featured configuration
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── outputs.tf
│   │   └── terraform.tfvars
│   └── multi-az/             # Specific scenario
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── variables.tf
│       └── outputs.tf
└── test/
    ├── go.mod
    ├── go.sum
    ├── simple_test.go        # Tests examples/simple
    ├── complete_test.go      # Tests examples/complete
    └── multi_az_test.go      # Tests examples/multi-az
Example: Minimal Test Fixture

A simple fixture that wires the module with test-specific values.

# examples/simple/main.tf
module "vpc" {
  source = "../../"

  vpc_cidr     = var.vpc_cidr
  environment  = var.environment
  project_name = var.project_name
}

output "vpc_id" {
  value = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  value = module.vpc.public_subnet_ids
}

# examples/simple/variables.tf
variable "vpc_cidr" {
  default = "10.99.0.0/16"
}

variable "environment" {
  default = "test"
}

variable "project_name" {
  default = "fixture-simple"
}
36.4.2 Shared Test Utilities

Extract common test setup, assertion patterns, and cleanup logic into shared Go packages.

Example: Shared Test Helper Package

Reusable helper functions for common test patterns.

// test/helpers/terraform_helpers.go
package helpers

import (
    "testing"
    "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/terraform"
    "github.com/gruntwork-io/terratest/modules/random"
)

// DefaultTerraformOptions creates options with standard settings
func DefaultTerraformOptions(t *testing.T, dir string) *terraform.Options {
    uniqueId := random.UniqueId()

    return terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: dir,
        Vars: map[string]interface{}{
            "environment":  "test",
            "project_name": fmt.Sprintf("test-%s", uniqueId),
        },
        EnvVars: map[string]string{
            "AWS_DEFAULT_REGION": "us-east-1",
        },
        NoColor: true,
    })
}

// DeployAndValidate runs init+apply and returns outputs
func DeployAndValidate(t *testing.T, opts *terraform.Options) map[string]string {
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    outputs := make(map[string]string)
    for _, key := range []string{"vpc_id", "public_subnet_ids"} {
        outputs[key] = terraform.Output(t, opts, key)
    }
    return outputs
}

// ── Usage in test files ──────────────────
// test/simple_test.go
func TestSimpleVpc(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()
    
    opts := helpers.DefaultTerraformOptions(t, "../examples/simple")
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    
    outputs := helpers.DeployAndValidate(t, opts)
    assert.NotEmpty(t, outputs["vpc_id"])
}

36.5 Cleanup Strategies

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM

Integration tests create real cloud resources. Reliable cleanup is critical — orphaned resources waste money and can cause quota issues. Use multiple layers of defense to guarantee cleanup.

💻 Practical Examples

36.5.1 defer Cleanup

Go's defer keyword ensures terraform destroy runs even if the test panics. Always place defer before InitAndApply.

Example: Proper defer Patterns

Multiple strategies for ensuring cleanup runs reliably.

// ✅ Correct: defer BEFORE apply
func TestCorrectCleanup(t *testing.T) {
    opts := &terraform.Options{TerraformDir: "../"}
    
    // Register cleanup FIRST
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    
    // Then deploy
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)
    
    // Assertions...
}

// ❌ Wrong: defer AFTER apply — if apply panics, no cleanup
func TestBrokenCleanup(t *testing.T) {
    opts := &terraform.Options{TerraformDir: "../"}
    
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)  // If this panics...
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts) // ...this never registers!
}

// ✅ Enhanced: Retry destroy on transient errors
func TestRobustCleanup(t *testing.T) {
    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../",
    })
    
    defer func() {
        // Retry destroy up to 3 times
        for i := 0; i < 3; i++ {
            _, err := terraform.DestroyE(t, opts)
            if err == nil {
                return
            }
            t.Logf("Destroy attempt %d failed: %v", i+1, err)
            time.Sleep(30 * time.Second)
        }
        t.Error("CRITICAL: Failed to destroy resources after 3 attempts!")
    }()
    
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)
}
36.5.2 Orphan Resource Detection

Even with defer, tests can leave orphaned resources (CI runner killed, network timeout). Implement scheduled scans to find and remove them.

Example: Orphan Detection Script

Find and flag resources older than 4 hours with the "terratest" tag.

#!/bin/bash
# find_orphans.sh — Detect stale test resources

MAX_AGE_HOURS=4
TAG_KEY="ManagedBy"
TAG_VALUE="terratest"

echo "🔍 Scanning for orphaned test resources..."

# Find VPCs
aws ec2 describe-vpcs \
  --filters "Name=tag:${TAG_KEY},Values=${TAG_VALUE}" \
  --query "Vpcs[?Tags[?Key=='CreatedAt']].{
    VpcId: VpcId,
    CIDR: CidrBlock,
    Created: Tags[?Key=='CreatedAt'].Value | [0]
  }" \
  --output table

# Find EC2 instances
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --filters \
    "Name=tag:${TAG_KEY},Values=${TAG_VALUE}" \
    "Name=instance-state-name,Values=running" \
  --query "Reservations[].Instances[].{
    InstanceId: InstanceId,
    Type: InstanceType,
    LaunchTime: LaunchTime
  }" \
  --output table

# Find S3 buckets with "test" prefix older than MAX_AGE
CUTOFF=$(date -d "-${MAX_AGE_HOURS} hours" +%s)
for bucket in $(aws s3api list-buckets \
  --query "Buckets[?starts_with(Name,'terratest-')].Name" \
  --output text); do
    CREATED=$(aws s3api get-bucket-tagging \
      --bucket "$bucket" \
      --query "TagSet[?Key=='CreatedAt'].Value | [0]" \
      --output text 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ "$CREATED" != "None" ] && [ "$CREATED" -lt "$CUTOFF" ]; then
        echo "⚠️  ORPHAN: s3://$bucket (created: $CREATED)"
    fi
done
36.5.3 AWS Nuke

aws-nuke is a nuclear option — it deletes ALL resources in an AWS account. Use it on dedicated test accounts as a scheduled safety net.

Example: AWS Nuke Configuration

Configure aws-nuke to clean a dedicated test account on schedule.

# aws-nuke-config.yml
regions:
  - us-east-1
  - us-west-2
  - global

account-blocklist:
  - "111111111111"   # Production — NEVER nuke
  - "222222222222"   # Staging

accounts:
  "999999999999":    # Test account — safe to nuke
    filters:
      # Preserve essential resources
      IAMRole:
        - "OrganizationAccountAccessRole"
        - "AWSServiceRoleForOrganizations"
      IAMRolePolicyAttachment:
        - "OrganizationAccountAccessRole -> AdministratorAccess"
      IAMPolicy:
        - type: glob
          value: "arn:aws:iam::policy/aws-service-role/*"
      CloudFormationStack:
        - type: glob
          value: "StackSet-*"
      S3Bucket:
        - "company-audit-logs"  # Keep audit logs
# Run aws-nuke in dry-run mode first!
$ aws-nuke -c aws-nuke-config.yml --profile test-account

# Once verified, add --no-dry-run
$ aws-nuke -c aws-nuke-config.yml --profile test-account --no-dry-run

# Schedule in CI (e.g., daily at 2 AM)
# .github/workflows/nuke-test-account.yml
# cron: '0 2 * * *'
⚠️ AWS Nuke Safety Checklist:
1. Dedicated test account only — never run on shared/production accounts
2. Account blocklist — explicitly blocklist all non-test accounts
3. Dry run first — always verify what will be deleted
4. Filter essential resources — preserve IAM roles, audit logs, CloudTrail
5. Require MFA — add MFA requirement for manual runs

36.6 Contract Testing

PATTERN 📝 EXAM

Contract tests verify that a module's interface (inputs and outputs) behaves as documented. They catch breaking changes early — before downstream consumers are affected.

💻 Practical Examples

36.6.1 Module Interface Tests

Verify that a module accepts the documented variables and rejects invalid values — testing the contract between module author and consumer.

Example: Module Interface Contract Tests

Test that a module's input validation works correctly.

func TestModuleContract_ValidInputs(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    // Test Case 1: Minimum required inputs
    opts := &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../examples/simple",
        Vars: map[string]interface{}{
            "vpc_cidr":    "10.0.0.0/16",
            "environment": "dev",
        },
    }
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    // Verify all documented outputs exist
    requiredOutputs := []string{
        "vpc_id", "public_subnet_ids", "private_subnet_ids",
        "nat_gateway_ips", "vpc_cidr_block",
    }
    for _, output := range requiredOutputs {
        value := terraform.Output(t, opts, output)
        assert.NotEmpty(t, value,
            "Required output '%s' must not be empty", output)
    }
}

func TestModuleContract_InvalidInputs(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    testCases := []struct {
        name        string
        vars        map[string]interface{}
        expectError string
    }{
        {
            name: "invalid CIDR",
            vars: map[string]interface{}{
                "vpc_cidr":    "not-a-cidr",
                "environment": "dev",
            },
            expectError: "invalid CIDR",
        },
        {
            name: "invalid environment",
            vars: map[string]interface{}{
                "vpc_cidr":    "10.0.0.0/16",
                "environment": "invalid",
            },
            expectError: "must be one of",
        },
    }

    for _, tc := range testCases {
        tc := tc
        t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            t.Parallel()
            opts := &terraform.Options{
                TerraformDir: "../examples/simple",
                Vars:         tc.vars,
            }
            _, err := terraform.InitAndPlanE(t, opts)
            assert.Error(t, err)
            assert.Contains(t, err.Error(), tc.expectError)
        })
    }
}
36.6.2 Output Validation

Test that module outputs conform to expected formats, types, and value ranges — ensuring downstream consumers get reliable data.

Example: Output Format Validation

Validate that outputs match expected patterns and constraints.

func TestOutputContracts(t *testing.T) {
    t.Parallel()

    opts := terraform.WithDefaultRetryableErrors(t, &terraform.Options{
        TerraformDir: "../examples/complete",
    })
    defer terraform.Destroy(t, opts)
    terraform.InitAndApply(t, opts)

    // ── VPC ID format ────────────────────
    vpcId := terraform.Output(t, opts, "vpc_id")
    assert.Regexp(t, `^vpc-[a-f0-9]+$`, vpcId,
        "VPC ID must match AWS format")

    // ── Subnet count ─────────────────────
    publicSubnets := terraform.OutputList(t, opts, "public_subnet_ids")
    privateSubnets := terraform.OutputList(t, opts, "private_subnet_ids")
    assert.GreaterOrEqual(t, len(publicSubnets), 2,
        "Must have at least 2 public subnets for HA")
    assert.Equal(t, len(publicSubnets), len(privateSubnets),
        "Public and private subnet counts must match")

    // ── Subnet ID format ─────────────────
    for _, subnetId := range publicSubnets {
        assert.Regexp(t, `^subnet-[a-f0-9]+$`, subnetId)
    }

    // ── CIDR block output ────────────────
    cidr := terraform.Output(t, opts, "vpc_cidr_block")
    assert.Regexp(t, `^\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+/\d+$`, cidr)

    // ── NAT Gateway IPs (Elastic IPs) ────
    natIps := terraform.OutputList(t, opts, "nat_gateway_ips")
    for _, ip := range natIps {
        assert.Regexp(t, `^\d+\.\d+\.\d+\.\d+$`, ip,
            "NAT IP must be a valid IPv4 address")
    }

    // ── Map output ───────────────────────
    outputMap := terraform.OutputMap(t, opts, "availability_zones")
    assert.NotEmpty(t, outputMap)
}
💡 Contract Testing Strategy:
Test Type What It Validates Speed
Input validation Required vars, type constraints, allowed values Fast (plan only)
Output existence All documented outputs are present Slow (apply needed)
Output format IDs, ARNs, IPs match expected patterns Slow (apply needed)
Semantic correctness Subnet counts, CIDR ranges, HA compliance Slow (apply needed)
🧪 📋 🔬 🎯
CHAPTER 37

terraform test - Native Testing

terraform test .tftest.hcl run Blocks Assertions Mock Providers Module Testing
7 Sections
18 Subsections
20+ Code Examples
1.6+ Min TF Version
PART 10 INTERMEDIATE ★★★★☆

Terraform 1.6+ introduced the native terraform test command — write tests in HCL without any external tooling. Test files use .tftest.hcl extension, run blocks define test steps, and assertions validate outputs and resource attributes with zero Go or Ruby required.

Learning Objectives

  • Write .tftest.hcl test files with run blocks
  • Use plan-only and apply-mode assertions
  • Pass variables and chain run blocks sequentially
  • Mock providers for isolated unit tests
  • Test modules with overrides and custom conditions
  • Execute tests in CI/CD pipelines

Chapter Contents

37.1 Test File Structure 37.2 run Blocks 37.3 Assertions 37.4 Variables in Tests 37.5 Mock Providers 37.6 Module Testing 37.7 Test Execution

37.1 Test File Structure

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Tests | terraform test CLI

Test files use the .tftest.hcl extension and live in a tests/ directory (or alongside your configuration). Each file contains one or more run blocks that define sequential test steps.

💻 Practical Examples

37.1.1 File Organization

Terraform discovers test files automatically in the tests/ directory and any .tftest.hcl files in the root module directory.

Example: Test File Directory Layout

Standard project structure with native Terraform tests.

modules/vpc/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── versions.tf
├── tests/
│   ├── basic.tftest.hcl         # Basic functionality tests
│   ├── validation.tftest.hcl    # Input validation tests
│   ├── complete.tftest.hcl      # Full-featured tests
│   └── setup/                   # Helper modules for tests
│       └── main.tf
└── examples/
    ├── simple/
    │   └── main.tf
    └── complete/
        └── main.tf
37.1.2 Basic Test File Anatomy

A test file combines variables, provider, and run blocks. Run blocks execute sequentially and share state.

Example: Complete Test File

A full .tftest.hcl file demonstrating all structural elements.

# tests/basic.tftest.hcl

# ── Global variables for all run blocks ──
variables {
  vpc_cidr     = "10.99.0.0/16"
  environment  = "test"
  project_name = "tftest-basic"
}

# ── Provider configuration (optional) ──
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

# ── Test: Plan-only validation ──
run "validate_plan" {
  command = plan

  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.cidr_block == "10.99.0.0/16"
    error_message = "VPC CIDR block mismatch"
  }
}

# ── Test: Full apply and verify ──
run "create_vpc" {
  command = apply

  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.cidr_block == "10.99.0.0/16"
    error_message = "VPC CIDR block mismatch"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = output.vpc_id != ""
    error_message = "VPC ID must not be empty"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(output.public_subnet_ids) == 3
    error_message = "Expected 3 public subnets"
  }
}
📌 Key Rules:
• Test files must end with .tftest.hcl (or .tftest.json)
• Run blocks execute sequentially — order matters
• After all runs complete, Terraform automatically destroys resources
• command = plan (default) runs plan only — no resources created
• command = apply creates real resources (destroyed after test)

37.2 run Blocks

CORE 📝 EXAM

Each run block defines a single test step. Blocks execute top-to-bottom, and later blocks can reference outputs and state from earlier blocks.

💻 Practical Examples

37.2.1 Plan-Only Runs

Plan-only runs validate configuration logic without creating resources — fast and free.

Example: Plan-Only Assertions

Validate resource attributes from the plan without deploying anything.

# tests/plan_checks.tftest.hcl

variables {
  environment  = "staging"
  instance_type = "t3.medium"
}

run "verify_instance_plan" {
  command = plan

  # Check planned instance type
  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.web.instance_type == "t3.medium"
    error_message = "Instance type should be t3.medium"
  }

  # Check tag is set correctly
  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.web.tags["Environment"] == "staging"
    error_message = "Environment tag should be staging"
  }

  # Check EBS optimization
  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.web.ebs_optimized == true
    error_message = "Instance should be EBS optimized"
  }
}

run "verify_security_group_plan" {
  command = plan

  # Verify no SSH from 0.0.0.0/0
  assert {
    condition = !contains(
      [for rule in aws_security_group.web.ingress : rule.cidr_blocks],
      ["0.0.0.0/0"]
    )
    error_message = "Security group must not allow unrestricted access"
  }
}
37.2.2 Apply Runs

Apply runs create real infrastructure, validate it, then automatically destroy it when the test completes.

Example: Apply and Verify Infrastructure

Deploy resources, verify real-world state, then auto-cleanup.

# tests/apply_checks.tftest.hcl

variables {
  bucket_name = "tftest-bucket-abc123"
  environment = "test"
}

# Step 1: Create the S3 bucket
run "create_bucket" {
  command = apply

  assert {
    condition     = aws_s3_bucket.main.bucket == var.bucket_name
    error_message = "Bucket name mismatch"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_s3_bucket.main.tags["Environment"] == "test"
    error_message = "Environment tag mismatch"
  }
}

# Step 2: Verify bucket properties (uses state from step 1)
run "verify_encryption" {
  command = apply

  assert {
    condition = aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.main.rule[0].apply_server_side_encryption_by_default[0].sse_algorithm == "aws:kms"
    error_message = "Bucket must use KMS encryption"
  }
}

# Step 3: Verify public access is blocked
run "verify_public_access" {
  command = apply

  assert {
    condition     = aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.main.block_public_acls == true
    error_message = "Public ACLs must be blocked"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.main.block_public_policy == true
    error_message = "Public policies must be blocked"
  }
}

# Terraform automatically runs destroy after all runs complete
37.2.3 Chained Run Blocks

Run blocks execute sequentially — use earlier block outputs in later blocks to test multi-step workflows.

Example: Multi-Step Test Workflow

Create a VPC, then deploy an EC2 instance into it, verifying at each step.

# tests/workflow.tftest.hcl

# Step 1: Deploy VPC using a helper module
run "setup_vpc" {
  command = apply

  module {
    source = "./tests/setup"
  }
}

# Step 2: Deploy the main module using VPC from step 1
run "deploy_application" {
  command = apply

  variables {
    vpc_id    = run.setup_vpc.vpc_id
    subnet_id = run.setup_vpc.public_subnet_ids[0]
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.app.subnet_id == run.setup_vpc.public_subnet_ids[0]
    error_message = "Instance must be in the correct subnet"
  }
}

# Step 3: Verify the deployed application
run "verify_application" {
  command = plan

  assert {
    condition     = output.app_url != ""
    error_message = "Application URL must be available"
  }
}
Run Block Feature Syntax Purpose
Plan mode command = plan Validate without deploying (default)
Apply mode command = apply Deploy and validate real resources
Module override module { source = "..." } Test a different module (setup)
Variables override variables { ... } Override variables for this run
Cross-run reference run.<name>.<output> Use output from a prior run block
Expect failure expect_failures = [...] Verify that specific errors occur

37.3 Assertions

CORE 📝 EXAM

Assertions are the heart of terraform test. Each assert block requires a boolean condition and an error_message that displays on failure.

💻 Practical Examples

37.3.1 Condition Expressions

Use any Terraform expression that evaluates to true or false — comparisons, regex, functions, and complex logic.

Example: Assertion Patterns

Common assertion patterns for different validation scenarios.

# tests/assertions.tftest.hcl

variables {
  environment = "production"
  vpc_cidr    = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

run "assertion_patterns" {
  command = plan

  # ── Equality ────────────────────────────
  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.cidr_block == var.vpc_cidr
    error_message = "VPC CIDR must be ${var.vpc_cidr}"
  }

  # ── String contains ─────────────────────
  assert {
    condition     = can(regex("^10\\.", aws_vpc.main.cidr_block))
    error_message = "CIDR must start with 10."
  }

  # ── List length ─────────────────────────
  assert {
    condition     = length(aws_subnet.public) >= 2
    error_message = "Need at least 2 public subnets for HA"
  }

  # ── Contains check ─────────────────────
  assert {
    condition     = contains(["production", "staging"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be production or staging"
  }

  # ── All items match ─────────────────────
  assert {
    condition = alltrue([
      for subnet in aws_subnet.private : subnet.map_public_ip_on_launch == false
    ])
    error_message = "Private subnets must not auto-assign public IPs"
  }

  # ── Null check ──────────────────────────
  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.id != null
    error_message = "VPC ID must not be null"
  }

  # ── Output validation ──────────────────
  assert {
    condition     = can(regex("^vpc-", output.vpc_id))
    error_message = "Output vpc_id must start with 'vpc-'"
  }
}
37.3.2 Expected Failures

Use expect_failures to test that invalid input is correctly rejected — negative testing.

Example: Testing Validation Rules

Verify that custom validation rules correctly reject bad input.

# tests/validation.tftest.hcl

# Test: Invalid CIDR should fail
run "reject_invalid_cidr" {
  command = plan

  variables {
    vpc_cidr    = "not-a-cidr"
    environment = "test"
  }

  # Tell Terraform we EXPECT this to fail on vpc_cidr validation
  expect_failures = [
    var.vpc_cidr,
  ]
}

# Test: Invalid environment should fail
run "reject_invalid_environment" {
  command = plan

  variables {
    vpc_cidr    = "10.0.0.0/16"
    environment = "banana"
  }

  expect_failures = [
    var.environment,
  ]
}

# Test: Too small CIDR should fail precondition
run "reject_small_cidr" {
  command = plan

  variables {
    vpc_cidr    = "10.0.0.0/28"
    environment = "test"
  }

  expect_failures = [
    # Reference the resource with the precondition
    aws_vpc.main,
  ]
}
37.3.3 Complex Assertions

Combine multiple functions and expressions for comprehensive validation of complex infrastructure.

Example: Advanced Multi-Condition Assertions

Validate networking, security, and tagging standards in a single test.

# tests/comprehensive.tftest.hcl

variables {
  environment  = "production"
  vpc_cidr     = "10.0.0.0/16"
  project_name = "myapp"
}

run "comprehensive_validation" {
  command = plan

  # Verify subnet CIDR ranges are within VPC CIDR
  assert {
    condition = alltrue([
      for subnet in aws_subnet.private :
        cidrhost(subnet.cidr_block, 0) == cidrhost(
          cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, index(aws_subnet.private[*], subnet)),
          0
        )
    ])
    error_message = "All private subnets must use CIDRs within the VPC range"
  }

  # Verify required tags on all taggable resources
  assert {
    condition = alltrue([
      for key in ["Environment", "Project", "ManagedBy"] :
        lookup(aws_vpc.main.tags, key, null) != null
    ])
    error_message = "VPC must have Environment, Project, and ManagedBy tags"
  }

  # Verify encryption is enabled where required
  assert {
    condition = anytrue([
      for rule in aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.main.rule :
        rule.apply_server_side_encryption_by_default[0].sse_algorithm == "aws:kms"
    ])
    error_message = "S3 bucket must use KMS encryption"
  }
}

37.4 Variables in Tests

CORE

Variables can be set at the file level (global), per-run block (override), or loaded from .tfvars files — giving flexible control over test inputs.

💻 Practical Examples

37.4.1 Global vs Run-Level Variables

File-level variables apply to all run blocks. Run-level variables override globals for that specific step.

Example: Variable Scoping in Tests

Demonstrate global, per-run, and .tfvars variable sources.

# tests/variables.tftest.hcl

# ── Global variables (apply to ALL run blocks) ──
variables {
  environment  = "test"
  project_name = "tftest-vars"
  region       = "us-east-1"
}

# Test 1: Uses global variables as-is
run "test_defaults" {
  command = plan

  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.tags["Environment"] == "test"
    error_message = "Should use global environment variable"
  }
}

# Test 2: Override specific variables for this run
run "test_production_config" {
  command = plan

  variables {
    environment   = "production"     # Overrides global
    instance_type = "m5.xlarge"      # Additional variable
    # project_name and region still come from global block
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.tags["Environment"] == "production"
    error_message = "Should use overridden environment variable"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.app.instance_type == "m5.xlarge"
    error_message = "Should use production instance type"
  }
}

# Test 3: Multiple environments in sequence
run "test_staging" {
  command = plan

  variables {
    environment = "staging"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.tags["Environment"] == "staging"
    error_message = "Should use staging environment"
  }
}
37.4.2 Using .tfvars Files

Load variable values from .tfvars files for complex test scenarios.

Example: Test with .tfvars File

Separate test data into reusable variable files.

# tests/fixtures/production.tfvars
environment   = "production"
instance_type = "m5.xlarge"
min_instances = 3
max_instances = 10
enable_waf    = true
multi_az      = true

# tests/fixtures/development.tfvars
environment   = "development"
instance_type = "t3.micro"
min_instances = 1
max_instances = 2
enable_waf    = false
multi_az      = false
# Run tests with specific variable files
$ terraform test -var-file=tests/fixtures/production.tfvars
$ terraform test -var-file=tests/fixtures/development.tfvars

# Override a single variable on CLI
$ terraform test -var="environment=staging"

37.5 Mock Providers

ADVANCED 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Mock Providers

Mock providers (Terraform 1.7+) replace real cloud providers with simulated stubs — no API calls, no credentials needed. Perfect for testing module logic in isolation without any cloud dependency.

💻 Practical Examples

37.5.1 mock_provider Block

Replace a provider entirely with a mock that returns predefined values for all resources and data sources.

Example: Basic Mock Provider

Test module logic without connecting to AWS.

# tests/unit.tftest.hcl

# Mock the AWS provider — no real API calls
mock_provider "aws" {}

variables {
  environment  = "test"
  vpc_cidr     = "10.0.0.0/16"
  project_name = "unit-test"
}

# This runs WITHOUT credentials — provider is mocked!
run "test_resource_count" {
  command = plan

  assert {
    condition     = length(aws_subnet.public) == 3
    error_message = "Should create 3 public subnets"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(aws_subnet.private) == 3
    error_message = "Should create 3 private subnets"
  }
}

run "test_naming_convention" {
  command = plan

  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.tags["Name"] == "unit-test-vpc"
    error_message = "VPC name should follow convention"
  }
}
37.5.2 Override Resources and Data

Customize mock responses for specific resources and data sources to control test scenarios precisely.

Example: Mock with Custom Overrides

Return specific values from mocked resources and data sources.

# tests/mock_overrides.tftest.hcl

mock_provider "aws" {
  # Override a specific data source
  mock_data "aws_caller_identity" {
    defaults = {
      account_id = "123456789012"
      arn        = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/test"
      user_id    = "AIDATEST123"
    }
  }

  # Override a specific resource
  mock_resource "aws_vpc" {
    defaults = {
      id         = "vpc-mock12345"
      cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
      arn        = "arn:aws:ec2:us-east-1:123456789012:vpc/vpc-mock12345"
    }
  }

  mock_data "aws_availability_zones" {
    defaults = {
      names = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
      zone_ids = ["use1-az1", "use1-az2", "use1-az3"]
    }
  }
}

variables {
  environment = "test"
}

run "test_with_mocked_data" {
  command = plan

  assert {
    condition     = data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id == "123456789012"
    error_message = "Account ID should match mock"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(data.aws_availability_zones.available.names) == 3
    error_message = "Should have 3 mocked AZs"
  }
}
37.5.3 Partial Mocking

Mix real and mock providers — use mocks for costly services while testing real interactions where needed.

Example: Selective Provider Mocking

Mock AWS but use a real local provider for testing.

# tests/partial_mock.tftest.hcl

# Mock expensive cloud provider
mock_provider "aws" {
  mock_resource "aws_instance" {
    defaults = {
      id               = "i-mock123"
      private_ip       = "10.0.1.100"
      public_ip        = "203.0.113.50"
      availability_zone = "us-east-1a"
    }
  }
}

# Real provider for local operations (no mock)
# The 'local' and 'null' providers work as normal

variables {
  environment = "test"
  app_name    = "myapp"
}

run "test_local_file_generation" {
  command = apply

  # local_file resources are real — actually create files
  assert {
    condition     = local_file.config.filename == "/tmp/myapp-config.json"
    error_message = "Config file should be generated"
  }

  # AWS resources are mocked — no real instances
  assert {
    condition     = aws_instance.app.id == "i-mock123"
    error_message = "Instance should use mock ID"
  }
}
💡 When to Use Mock Providers:
Scenario Use Mocks? Reasoning
Variable validation ✅ Yes No cloud interaction needed
Resource count/naming ✅ Yes Logic testing only
Tag verification ✅ Yes Computed from variables
IAM policy correctness ⚠️ Maybe Mock for structure, real for behavior
Network connectivity ❌ No Must test real infrastructure
End-to-end workflow ❌ No Need real cloud interaction

37.6 Module Testing

PATTERN 📝 EXAM

Test reusable modules by pointing run blocks at different module configurations. Use the module block to test example directories or helper modules that set up prerequisites.

💻 Practical Examples

37.6.1 Testing with Alternate Modules

Use the module block inside a run to test the module against different example configurations.

Example: Testing Module Examples

Validate each example directory as a separate test scenario.

# tests/examples.tftest.hcl

mock_provider "aws" {}

# Test the minimal example
run "test_simple_example" {
  command = plan

  module {
    source = "./examples/simple"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(module.vpc.public_subnet_ids) >= 1
    error_message = "Simple example must create at least 1 public subnet"
  }
}

# Test the full-featured example
run "test_complete_example" {
  command = plan

  module {
    source = "./examples/complete"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(module.vpc.public_subnet_ids) == 3
    error_message = "Complete example must create 3 public subnets"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(module.vpc.nat_gateway_ids) >= 1
    error_message = "Complete example must create NAT gateways"
  }
}

# Test a specific scenario
run "test_single_az_example" {
  command = plan

  module {
    source = "./examples/single-az"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(module.vpc.public_subnet_ids) == 1
    error_message = "Single-AZ must create exactly 1 subnet"
  }
}
37.6.2 Setup Helper Modules

Create lightweight Terraform modules in tests/setup/ that provision prerequisites required by your main module's tests.

Example: Setup Module for Prerequisites

Create a VPC prerequisite before testing an ECS module.

# tests/setup/main.tf — Helper module
# Creates prerequisites needed by the main module

resource "aws_vpc" "test" {
  cidr_block = "10.99.0.0/16"
  tags = { Name = "test-prereq" }
}

resource "aws_subnet" "test" {
  count             = 2
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.test.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(aws_vpc.test.cidr_block, 8, count.index)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.az.names[count.index]
}

data "aws_availability_zones" "az" {
  state = "available"
}

output "vpc_id" {
  value = aws_vpc.test.id
}

output "subnet_ids" {
  value = aws_subnet.test[*].id
}
# tests/ecs.tftest.hcl — Uses the setup module

run "create_prerequisites" {
  command = apply
  module {
    source = "./tests/setup"
  }
}

run "deploy_ecs_service" {
  command = apply

  variables {
    vpc_id     = run.create_prerequisites.vpc_id
    subnet_ids = run.create_prerequisites.subnet_ids
    service_name = "test-api"
    container_image = "nginx:latest"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_ecs_service.main.desired_count >= 1
    error_message = "ECS service must have at least 1 task"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = output.service_url != ""
    error_message = "Service URL must be available"
  }
}

37.7 Test Execution

CLI 📝 EXAM

Run tests from the CLI with filtering, verbose output, and CI/CD-friendly options. Understand the test lifecycle: init → plan/apply → assert → destroy.

💻 Practical Examples

37.7.1 CLI Commands and Flags

The terraform test command discovers and runs all .tftest.hcl files with various filtering and output options.

Example: Running Tests from CLI

Common commands for running Terraform tests.

# Run all tests in the module
$ terraform test

# Verbose output — show each assertion
$ terraform test -verbose

# Run a specific test file
$ terraform test -filter=tests/basic.tftest.hcl

# Run a specific run block within a file
$ terraform test -filter=tests/basic.tftest.hcl/create_vpc

# Pass variables
$ terraform test -var="environment=staging"
$ terraform test -var-file="tests/fixtures/production.tfvars"

# JSON output for CI/CD parsing
$ terraform test -json

# No color (for logs)
$ terraform test -no-color
Flag Purpose Example
-filter Run specific test file or run block -filter=tests/basic.tftest.hcl
-verbose Show detailed output per assertion -verbose
-json Machine-readable JSON output -json
-var Set input variable -var="env=prod"
-var-file Load variables from file -var-file="test.tfvars"
-no-color Disable color output -no-color
37.7.2 CI/CD Integration

Integrate terraform test into CI/CD pipelines for automated module validation on every commit.

Example: GitHub Actions Testing Pipeline

Run native Terraform tests automatically on pull requests.

# .github/workflows/terraform-test.yml
name: Terraform Module Tests

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'modules/**'
      - '*.tf'
      - 'tests/**'

jobs:
  test:
    name: Run Terraform Tests
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    strategy:
      matrix:
        module:
          - modules/vpc
          - modules/ecs
          - modules/rds

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.7.0"

      - name: Terraform Init
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.module }}
        run: terraform init -backend=false

      - name: Terraform Test (Unit - Mock Providers)
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.module }}
        run: terraform test -filter=tests/unit.tftest.hcl -verbose

      - name: Terraform Test (Integration)
        if: github.event_name == 'push'
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.module }}
        env:
          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
        run: terraform test -filter=tests/integration.tftest.hcl -verbose
37.7.3 Test Output Interpretation

Understand the test output format to quickly diagnose failures and track test coverage.

Example: Test Output Analysis

Sample test output with pass, fail, and skip states.

# Successful test run
$ terraform test -verbose

tests/basic.tftest.hcl... in progress
  run "validate_plan"... pass
  run "create_vpc"... pass (12s)
  run "verify_subnets"... pass
tests/basic.tftest.hcl... tearing down
tests/basic.tftest.hcl... pass

tests/validation.tftest.hcl... in progress
  run "reject_invalid_cidr"... pass
  run "reject_invalid_environment"... pass
tests/validation.tftest.hcl... tearing down
tests/validation.tftest.hcl... pass

Success! 5 passed, 0 failed.

# ─── Failed test output ───
tests/security.tftest.hcl... in progress
  run "verify_encryption"... fail
╷
│ Error: Test assertion failed
│
│   on tests/security.tftest.hcl line 15, in run "verify_encryption":
│   15:     condition     = aws_s3_bucket.main.tags["Encrypted"] == "true"
│       ├────────────────
│       │ aws_s3_bucket.main.tags["Encrypted"] is null
│
│ S3 bucket must have Encrypted tag
╵
tests/security.tftest.hcl... tearing down
tests/security.tftest.hcl... fail

Failure! 3 passed, 1 failed.
💡 terraform test vs Other Frameworks:
Feature terraform test Terratest
Language HCL Go
External deps None Go toolchain
Mock providers ✅ Native ❌ Use LocalStack
HTTP validation ❌ Not built-in ✅ http-helper
SSH testing ❌ Not built-in ✅ ssh module
AWS SDK queries ❌ Not built-in ✅ Full SDK
Best for Unit tests, validation Integration, E2E

Recommendation: Use terraform test with mocks for fast unit tests, and Terratest for integration/E2E tests that need cloud SDK assertions.
✅ 🛡️ 🔒 ⚙️
CHAPTER 38

Custom Validation

Variable Validation Preconditions Postconditions Check Blocks Custom Functions
4 Sections
14 Subsections
18+ Code Examples
1.2+ Min TF Version
PART 10 INTERMEDIATE ★★★★☆

Build guardrails directly into your Terraform configurations. Variable validation rules catch bad input at plan time, preconditions and postconditions enforce assumptions on resources, check blocks provide continuous assertions, and custom validation functions centralize reusable logic.

Learning Objectives

  • Write variable validation blocks with custom rules
  • Add precondition and postcondition blocks to resources and data sources
  • Use check blocks for continuous infrastructure assertions
  • Create reusable validation functions for common patterns

Chapter Contents

38.1 Variable Validation Rules 38.2 Preconditions and Postconditions 38.3 Check Blocks 38.4 Custom Validation Functions

38.1 Variable Validation Rules

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Custom Validation Rules

Variable validation blocks let you define custom rules that check input values during terraform plan. Invalid values are rejected with clear error messages before any resources are created.

💻 Practical Examples

38.1.1 Basic Validation Rules

Add one or more validation blocks inside a variable to enforce constraints on input values.

Example: Common Variable Validations

Validate strings, numbers, and enum-style selections.

# ── String: Allowed values (enum) ──
variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Deployment environment"

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "production"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or production."
  }
}

# ── String: Pattern matching ──
variable "project_name" {
  type        = string
  description = "Project name (lowercase, hyphens only)"

  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,28}[a-z0-9]$", var.project_name))
    error_message = "Project name must be 4-30 chars, lowercase alphanumeric and hyphens, start with letter."
  }
}

# ── Number: Range check ──
variable "instance_count" {
  type        = number
  description = "Number of instances"

  validation {
    condition     = var.instance_count >= 1 && var.instance_count <= 20
    error_message = "Instance count must be between 1 and 20."
  }
}

# ── String: CIDR block format ──
variable "vpc_cidr" {
  type        = string
  description = "VPC CIDR block"

  validation {
    condition     = can(cidrhost(var.vpc_cidr, 0))
    error_message = "Must be a valid CIDR block (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16)."
  }

  validation {
    condition     = tonumber(split("/", var.vpc_cidr)[1]) <= 24
    error_message = "VPC CIDR prefix must be /24 or larger (smaller number)."
  }
}
38.1.2 Complex Validation Patterns

Validate complex types like maps, lists, and objects with nested validation logic.

Example: Complex Type Validations

Validate maps, lists, and object variables with multi-rule constraints.

# ── List: Non-empty with valid entries ──
variable "availability_zones" {
  type        = list(string)
  description = "List of AZs to use"

  validation {
    condition     = length(var.availability_zones) >= 2
    error_message = "At least 2 availability zones required for HA."
  }

  validation {
    condition = alltrue([
      for az in var.availability_zones :
      can(regex("^[a-z]{2}-[a-z]+-[0-9][a-z]$", az))
    ])
    error_message = "Each AZ must match format: us-east-1a."
  }
}

# ── Map: Required keys ──
variable "tags" {
  type        = map(string)
  description = "Required resource tags"

  validation {
    condition = alltrue([
      for key in ["Environment", "Team", "CostCenter"] :
      contains(keys(var.tags), key)
    ])
    error_message = "Tags must include Environment, Team, and CostCenter."
  }
}

# ── Object: Cross-field validation ──
variable "scaling_config" {
  type = object({
    min_capacity     = number
    max_capacity     = number
    target_cpu       = number
    cooldown_seconds = number
  })

  validation {
    condition     = var.scaling_config.min_capacity <= var.scaling_config.max_capacity
    error_message = "min_capacity must not exceed max_capacity."
  }

  validation {
    condition     = var.scaling_config.target_cpu >= 10 && var.scaling_config.target_cpu <= 90
    error_message = "Target CPU must be between 10% and 90%."
  }

  validation {
    condition     = var.scaling_config.cooldown_seconds >= 60
    error_message = "Cooldown must be at least 60 seconds."
  }
}
38.1.3 Multiple Validation Blocks

Stack multiple validation blocks on a single variable — each adds an independent constraint with its own error message.

Example: Layered Validation Rules

Progressive validation with specific error messages at each level.

variable "bucket_name" {
  type        = string
  description = "S3 bucket name"

  # Rule 1: Length check
  validation {
    condition     = length(var.bucket_name) >= 3 && length(var.bucket_name) <= 63
    error_message = "Bucket name must be 3-63 characters."
  }

  # Rule 2: Character set
  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9.-]*[a-z0-9]$", var.bucket_name))
    error_message = "Bucket name must start/end with alphanumeric, contain only lowercase, numbers, dots, hyphens."
  }

  # Rule 3: No consecutive dots
  validation {
    condition     = !can(regex("\\.\\.", var.bucket_name))
    error_message = "Bucket name must not contain consecutive dots."
  }

  # Rule 4: Not an IP address
  validation {
    condition     = !can(regex("^\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+\\.\\d+$", var.bucket_name))
    error_message = "Bucket name must not be formatted as an IP address."
  }
}
💡 Validation Best Practices:
• Use can() to wrap expressions that might error (like regex, cidrhost)
• Write specific error messages — tell users what's wrong AND what's expected
• Variable validation only has access to var.<name> — cannot reference other variables
• Multiple validation blocks run independently — all must pass
• Validation runs at plan time, before any resources are created

38.2 Preconditions and Postconditions

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Preconditions & Postconditions

Preconditions validate assumptions before a resource is created. Postconditions verify guarantees after a resource is created. Both use lifecycle blocks and are available on resources, data sources, and outputs (Terraform 1.2+).

💻 Practical Examples

38.2.1 Resource Preconditions

Preconditions check that required conditions are met before Terraform attempts to create or modify a resource.

Example: Preconditions on AWS Resources

Guard resources with assumptions about the environment.

# Verify AMI is in the correct region
data "aws_ami" "app" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["self"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["myapp-*"]
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.app.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  subnet_id     = var.subnet_id

  lifecycle {
    # Precondition: Verify AMI exists and is recent
    precondition {
      condition     = data.aws_ami.app.id != null
      error_message = "No AMI found matching 'myapp-*'. Build the AMI first."
    }

    # Precondition: Verify instance type is valid for the region
    precondition {
      condition     = contains(["t3.micro", "t3.small", "t3.medium", "m5.large"], var.instance_type)
      error_message = "Instance type '${var.instance_type}' is not in the approved list."
    }
  }

  tags = var.tags
}

# Precondition on a data source
data "aws_vpc" "selected" {
  id = var.vpc_id

  lifecycle {
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.state == "available"
      error_message = "VPC ${var.vpc_id} is not in 'available' state."
    }
  }
}
38.2.2 Resource Postconditions

Postconditions verify resource state after creation — use self to reference the resource's own attributes.

Example: Postconditions — Verify After Creation

Ensure resources were created with expected properties.

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  identifier           = "${var.project_name}-db"
  engine               = "postgres"
  engine_version       = "15.4"
  instance_class       = var.db_instance_class
  allocated_storage    = var.db_storage_gb
  storage_encrypted    = true
  multi_az             = var.environment == "production"
  skip_final_snapshot  = var.environment != "production"

  lifecycle {
    # Verify encryption was actually enabled
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.storage_encrypted == true
      error_message = "Database storage encryption must be enabled."
    }

    # Verify multi-AZ for production
    postcondition {
      condition     = var.environment != "production" || self.multi_az == true
      error_message = "Production databases must be multi-AZ."
    }

    # Verify endpoint is available
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.endpoint != ""
      error_message = "Database endpoint must be available after creation."
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "main" {
  bucket = var.bucket_name

  lifecycle {
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.arn != ""
      error_message = "Bucket ARN must be available."
    }
  }
}
38.2.3 Output Preconditions

Add preconditions to outputs to guarantee that exported values meet quality standards before they're consumed by other modules.

Example: Output Validation

Guard module outputs with preconditions to ensure data quality.

output "vpc_id" {
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
  description = "ID of the created VPC"

  precondition {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.state == "available"
    error_message = "VPC is not in available state — cannot export ID."
  }
}

output "database_endpoint" {
  value       = aws_db_instance.main.endpoint
  description = "RDS database endpoint"
  sensitive   = false

  precondition {
    condition     = can(regex(".*\\.rds\\.amazonaws\\.com", aws_db_instance.main.endpoint))
    error_message = "Database endpoint doesn't look like a valid RDS endpoint."
  }
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  value = aws_subnet.public[*].id

  precondition {
    condition     = length(aws_subnet.public) >= 2
    error_message = "Module must output at least 2 public subnets for HA."
  }
}
Feature Precondition Postcondition
When Before create/update After create/update
Can use self ❌ No ✅ Yes
Accesses Variables, data sources, other resources The resource's own attributes via self
Purpose Validate assumptions / prerequisites Verify guarantees / invariants
On failure Blocks resource creation Marks apply as failed
38.2.4 Cross-Resource Validation

Use preconditions to enforce relationships between resources — ensuring database sizes match workload or subnet counts match AZ count.

Example: Cross-Resource Preconditions

Validate relationships between dependent resources.

# Ensure ECS tasks have enough resources
resource "aws_ecs_service" "app" {
  name            = "${var.project_name}-service"
  cluster         = aws_ecs_cluster.main.id
  task_definition = aws_ecs_task_definition.app.arn
  desired_count   = var.desired_count

  network_configuration {
    subnets = var.private_subnet_ids
  }

  lifecycle {
    precondition {
      condition     = length(var.private_subnet_ids) >= var.desired_count
      error_message = "Need at least ${var.desired_count} subnets for ${var.desired_count} tasks (one per AZ)."
    }

    precondition {
      condition     = var.desired_count >= 2 || var.environment != "production"
      error_message = "Production must run at least 2 tasks for high availability."
    }
  }
}

# Ensure ALB is in same VPC as targets
resource "aws_lb_target_group" "app" {
  name     = "${var.project_name}-tg"
  port     = 8080
  protocol = "HTTP"
  vpc_id   = var.vpc_id

  lifecycle {
    precondition {
      condition     = var.vpc_id == data.aws_vpc.selected.id
      error_message = "Target group VPC must match the selected VPC."
    }
  }
}

38.3 Check Blocks

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Check Blocks

Check blocks (Terraform 1.5+) define continuous assertions that run every plan and apply but produce warnings instead of errors. They're ideal for monitoring infrastructure health and compliance drift without blocking deployments.

💻 Practical Examples

38.3.1 Basic Check Blocks

A check block contains a name, optional scoped data source, and one or more assert blocks.

Example: Infrastructure Health Checks

Monitor certificate expiry and endpoint health as continuous checks.

# ── Check: SSL certificate not expiring soon ──
check "certificate_validity" {
  data "aws_acm_certificate" "main" {
    domain   = "app.example.com"
    statuses = ["ISSUED"]
  }

  assert {
    condition = timecmp(
      plantimestamp(),
      timeadd(data.aws_acm_certificate.main.not_after, "-720h")
    ) < 0
    error_message = "⚠️ SSL certificate expires within 30 days! Renew immediately."
  }
}

# ── Check: Website is responding ──
check "website_health" {
  data "http" "app_health" {
    url = "https://app.example.com/health"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = data.http.app_health.status_code == 200
    error_message = "⚠️ Application health endpoint is not returning 200."
  }
}

# ── Check: Budget not exceeded ──
check "cost_budget" {
  assert {
    condition     = var.monthly_budget > 0
    error_message = "⚠️ Monthly budget must be configured."
  }
}
📌 Check vs Precondition — Key Difference:
Check blocks produce warnings — they report issues but do NOT block plan/apply.
Preconditions produce errors — they block the operation entirely.

Use checks for advisory monitoring; use preconditions for hard enforcement.
38.3.2 Scoped Data Sources

Check blocks can include scoped data sources that are only queried within the check context — they don't pollute the main configuration namespace.

Example: Check with Scoped Data Source

Query external data within a check without affecting main resources.

# Check: Verify DNS is properly configured
check "dns_resolution" {
  # Scoped data source — only available within this check
  data "dns_a_record_set" "app" {
    host = "app.example.com"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(data.dns_a_record_set.app.addrs) > 0
    error_message = "⚠️ DNS record for app.example.com has no A records."
  }

  assert {
    condition = alltrue([
      for addr in data.dns_a_record_set.app.addrs :
      can(regex("^10\\.", addr))
    ])
    error_message = "⚠️ DNS records should resolve to internal 10.x addresses."
  }
}

# Check: Verify S3 bucket is not public
check "s3_public_access" {
  data "aws_s3_bucket" "public_check" {
    bucket = aws_s3_bucket.main.id
  }

  assert {
    condition     = !data.aws_s3_bucket.public_check.website_endpoint
    error_message = "⚠️ S3 bucket should not have website hosting enabled."
  }
}
38.3.3 Compliance Checks

Use check blocks to continuously verify that infrastructure stays compliant with organizational policies.

Example: Continuous Compliance Monitoring

Check tagging standards, encryption, and access controls on every plan.

# Check: All required tags present
check "tagging_compliance" {
  assert {
    condition = alltrue([
      for key in ["Environment", "Team", "CostCenter", "ManagedBy"] :
      lookup(aws_vpc.main.tags, key, null) != null
    ])
    error_message = "⚠️ VPC is missing required tags. Expected: Environment, Team, CostCenter, ManagedBy."
  }
}

# Check: Encryption standards
check "encryption_compliance" {
  assert {
    condition     = aws_db_instance.main.storage_encrypted == true
    error_message = "⚠️ RDS instance storage is not encrypted. This violates policy SEC-001."
  }

  assert {
    condition     = aws_db_instance.main.kms_key_id != ""
    error_message = "⚠️ RDS should use customer-managed KMS key, not default."
  }
}

# Check: No public ingress on port 22
check "ssh_access_compliance" {
  assert {
    condition = !anytrue([
      for rule in aws_security_group.app.ingress :
      rule.from_port <= 22 && rule.to_port >= 22 &&
      contains(rule.cidr_blocks, "0.0.0.0/0")
    ])
    error_message = "⚠️ Security group allows SSH from 0.0.0.0/0. This violates policy NET-003."
  }
}
38.3.4 External Endpoint Checks

Use the http data source inside checks to verify external service availability and response correctness.

Example: External API and Service Checks

Monitor dependencies and service integrations every Terraform run.

# Check: API Gateway responds correctly
check "api_gateway_health" {
  data "http" "api_check" {
    url = "${aws_apigatewayv2_api.main.api_endpoint}/health"
    
    request_headers = {
      Accept = "application/json"
    }
  }

  assert {
    condition     = data.http.api_check.status_code == 200
    error_message = "⚠️ API Gateway health endpoint returned ${data.http.api_check.status_code}."
  }

  assert {
    condition     = jsondecode(data.http.api_check.response_body).status == "healthy"
    error_message = "⚠️ API reports unhealthy status."
  }
}

# Check: Terraform version constraint
check "terraform_version" {
  assert {
    condition     = tonumber(split(".", terraform.version)[1]) >= 5
    error_message = "⚠️ Terraform 1.5+ recommended for check block support."
  }
}

38.4 Custom Validation Functions

ADVANCED
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Functions

While Terraform doesn't support user-defined functions in the traditional sense, you can create reusable validation patterns using locals, modules, and Terraform 1.8+ provider-defined functions to centralize complex validation logic.

💻 Practical Examples

38.4.1 Validation with Locals

Use locals to compute reusable validation predicates that can be referenced in preconditions and checks.

Example: Centralized Validation Locals

Compute validation results once and reference them across multiple resources.

# ── Centralized validation predicates ──
locals {
  # Naming convention check
  valid_name = can(regex("^[a-z][a-z0-9-]{2,28}[a-z0-9]$", var.project_name))

  # Environment checks
  is_production  = var.environment == "production"
  is_high_avail  = contains(["production", "staging"], var.environment)

  # Tag validation
  required_tags = ["Environment", "Team", "CostCenter", "ManagedBy"]
  has_all_required_tags = alltrue([
    for tag in local.required_tags :
    contains(keys(var.tags), tag)
  ])

  # CIDR validation
  is_valid_cidr   = can(cidrhost(var.vpc_cidr, 0))
  cidr_prefix     = local.is_valid_cidr ? tonumber(split("/", var.vpc_cidr)[1]) : 32
  cidr_large_enough = local.cidr_prefix <= 20
}

# ── Use in resource preconditions ──
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_cidr
  enable_dns_support   = true
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  tags                 = var.tags

  lifecycle {
    precondition {
      condition     = local.is_valid_cidr
      error_message = "Invalid CIDR block format."
    }

    precondition {
      condition     = local.cidr_large_enough
      error_message = "VPC CIDR must be /20 or larger for sufficient IP space."
    }

    precondition {
      condition     = local.has_all_required_tags
      error_message = "Missing required tags: ${join(", ", local.required_tags)}"
    }
  }
}

# ── Use in check blocks ──
check "naming_convention" {
  assert {
    condition     = local.valid_name
    error_message = "⚠️ Project name '${var.project_name}' violates naming convention."
  }
}
38.4.2 Validation Module Pattern

Create a dedicated validation module that only contains variable validation — consumers call it to validate their inputs before using the real infrastructure module.

Example: Reusable Validation Module

A module that validates inputs and outputs a "validation passed" flag.

# modules/validators/aws-naming/main.tf
# Reusable validation module for AWS naming conventions

variable "resource_name" {
  type = string

  validation {
    condition     = length(var.resource_name) >= 3 && length(var.resource_name) <= 63
    error_message = "Resource name must be 3-63 characters."
  }

  validation {
    condition     = can(regex("^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9]$", var.resource_name))
    error_message = "Name must be lowercase, alphanumeric, hyphens only."
  }
}

variable "resource_type" {
  type = string

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["vpc", "subnet", "sg", "ec2", "rds", "s3", "lambda"], var.resource_type)
    error_message = "Unknown resource type. Must be one of: vpc, subnet, sg, ec2, rds, s3, lambda."
  }
}

output "validated_name" {
  value = var.resource_name
}

output "full_name" {
  value = "${var.resource_type}-${var.resource_name}"
}
# Usage in root module
module "vpc_name" {
  source        = "./modules/validators/aws-naming"
  resource_name = var.project_name
  resource_type = "vpc"
}

resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr
  tags = {
    Name = module.vpc_name.full_name
  }
}
38.4.3 Comprehensive Validation Strategy

Combine all validation techniques into a layered defense-in-depth approach for infrastructure safety.

Example: Full Validation Pipeline

A complete module showing all validation layers working together.

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# LAYER 1: Variable Validation (Plan Time)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
variable "environment" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "production"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Invalid environment."
  }
}

variable "vpc_cidr" {
  type = string
  validation {
    condition     = can(cidrhost(var.vpc_cidr, 0))
    error_message = "Invalid CIDR."
  }
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# LAYER 2: Locals (Computed Validations)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
locals {
  is_production = var.environment == "production"
  min_subnets   = local.is_production ? 3 : 2
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# LAYER 3: Resource Preconditions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr

  lifecycle {
    precondition {
      condition     = tonumber(split("/", var.vpc_cidr)[1]) <= 20
      error_message = "VPC CIDR too small for production workloads."
    }
  }
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# LAYER 4: Resource Postconditions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count      = local.min_subnets
  vpc_id     = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index)

  lifecycle {
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.available_ip_address_count > 200
      error_message = "Subnet has fewer than 200 available IPs."
    }
  }
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# LAYER 5: Output Preconditions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
output "vpc_id" {
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
  precondition {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.state == "available"
    error_message = "VPC not ready."
  }
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# LAYER 6: Check Blocks (Warnings)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
check "subnet_capacity" {
  assert {
    condition = alltrue([
      for s in aws_subnet.private :
      s.available_ip_address_count > 100
    ])
    error_message = "⚠️ Some subnets running low on IP addresses."
  }
}
🏗️ Validation Defense-in-Depth:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Layer 1: Variable Validation               │ ← Catches bad input
│  Layer 2: Locals (Computed Checks)          │ ← Derives constraints
│  Layer 3: Preconditions                     │ ← Guards before create
│  Layer 4: Postconditions                    │ ← Verifies after create
│  Layer 5: Output Preconditions              │ ← Guards exported data
│  Layer 6: Check Blocks (Warnings)           │ ← Continuous monitoring
│  Layer 7: terraform test                    │ ← Automated testing
│  Layer 8: External Scanners (tflint/tfsec)  │ ← Policy enforcement
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       ↑ Plan Time          Apply Time ↓
🔄 🚀 ⚙️ 🛡️
CHAPTER 39

Terraform in CI/CD Pipelines
