│     │                              │  • Audience claim         │         │
│     │                              └──────────┬─────────────────┘         │
│     ▼                                         │                           │
│  ⑤ Receive temporary creds ◀─────────────────┘                           │
│     • AccessKeyId (temp)                                                  │
│     • SecretAccessKey (temp)                                              │
│     • SessionToken                                                        │
│     • Expires: ~1 hour                                                    │
│     │                                                                     │
│     ▼                                                                     │
│  ⑥ terraform init / plan / apply                                         │
│     (uses temp credentials)                                               │
│     │                                                                     │
│     ▼                                                                     │
│  ⑦ Credentials expire automatically                                      │
│     (no cleanup needed)                                                   │
│                                                                           │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Criteria 🔴 Static Access Keys 🟡 Vault Dynamic Secrets 🟢 OIDC (Recommended)
Setup Complexity Low — paste keys High — deploy Vault, configure engines Medium — one-time IAM config
Credential Lifetime ∞ (until manually rotated) Hours (TTL-based) Minutes (per workflow run)
Rotation Manual (often forgotten) Automatic (Vault lease) Not needed (no stored creds)
Blast Radius if Leaked 🔴 Full access until revoked 🟡 Access until TTL expires 🟢 ~1 hour max, scoped to run
Audit Trail Shared key — hard to trace Good (Vault audit log) Excellent (CloudTrail + GitHub log)
Cross-Environment Separate keys per env Separate roles per env Separate roles, scoped by claims
Cost Free Vault license / infra Free
💡 OIDC Pro Tips:
• Use environment claim in trust policy to restrict production access: "repo:org/infra:environment:production"
• Each AWS account should have its own OIDC provider + IAM role
• Set role-duration-seconds to the minimum needed (default: 3600)
• Monitor AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity calls in CloudTrail for anomalies

💻 Practical Examples

40.2.1 OIDC Provider Setup

Create an OIDC identity provider in AWS that trusts GitHub's token issuer. This is a one-time setup per AWS account.

Example: OIDC Provider in Terraform

Create the GitHub OIDC provider in AWS using Terraform.

# ── GitHub OIDC Provider (one per AWS account) ──
resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github_actions" {
  url             = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
  client_id_list  = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
  
  # GitHub's OIDC thumbprint
  thumbprint_list = ["6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1"]

  tags = {
    Name      = "GitHub Actions OIDC"
    ManagedBy = "Terraform"
  }
}
40.2.2 IAM Role Configuration

Create an IAM role that GitHub Actions can assume via OIDC. The trust policy restricts which repositories, branches, and environments can assume the role.

Example: IAM Role with Scoped Trust Policy

Restrict role assumption to specific repos, branches, and environments.

# ── Trust Policy: Who can assume this role ──
data "aws_iam_policy_document" "github_actions_trust" {
  statement {
    effect  = "Allow"
    actions = ["sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"]

    principals {
      type        = "Federated"
      identifiers = [aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github_actions.arn]
    }

    # Audience check
    condition {
      test     = "StringEquals"
      variable = "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud"
      values   = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
    }

    # Restrict to specific repo + branch/environment
    condition {
      test     = "StringLike"
      variable = "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub"
      values   = [
        # Format: repo:{owner}/{repo}:ref:refs/heads/{branch}
        "repo:my-org/infrastructure:ref:refs/heads/main",
        # Or scope to environment
        "repo:my-org/infrastructure:environment:production",
        "repo:my-org/infrastructure:environment:staging"
      ]
    }
  }
}

# ── IAM Role ──
resource "aws_iam_role" "github_actions_terraform" {
  name               = "github-actions-terraform"
  assume_role_policy = data.aws_iam_policy_document.github_actions_trust.json
  max_session_duration = 3600  # 1 hour max

  tags = {
    Purpose   = "GitHub Actions Terraform Pipeline"
    ManagedBy = "Terraform"
  }
}

# ── Attach permissions (customize for your needs) ──
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "terraform_permissions" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.github_actions_terraform.name
  policy_arn = aws_iam_policy.terraform_pipeline.arn
}

# ── Output the role ARN (store in GitHub environment secrets) ──
output "github_actions_role_arn" {
  value       = aws_iam_role.github_actions_terraform.arn
  description = "ARN to configure in GitHub Actions secrets"
}
40.2.3 aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials

The official aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials action handles the OIDC token exchange and sets the AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, and AWS_SESSION_TOKEN environment variables automatically.

Example: OIDC Authentication Step

Complete OIDC auth configuration in a workflow.

permissions:
  id-token: write    # REQUIRED for OIDC
  contents: read

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # ── OIDC Authentication (no static keys!) ──
      - name: Configure AWS Credentials
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
          # Optional: session name for CloudTrail auditing
          role-session-name: GitHubActions-${{ github.run_id }}
          # Optional: custom duration (default: 1 hour)
          role-duration-seconds: 1800  # 30 minutes

      # ── Verify authentication ──
      - name: Verify AWS Identity
        run: aws sts get-caller-identity

      # ── Now Terraform uses these credentials automatically ──
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init
      - name: Terraform Plan
        run: terraform plan -out=tfplan
Parameter Required Description
role-to-assume Yes ARN of the IAM role to assume
aws-region Yes AWS region for API calls
role-session-name No Appears in CloudTrail for auditing
role-duration-seconds No Session duration (default: 3600)
audience No OIDC audience (default: sts.amazonaws.com)

40.3 Terraform Workflow

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: hashicorp/setup-terraform | Terraform GitHub Actions Tutorial

The Terraform workflow in GitHub Actions follows a standard sequence: setup, init, format check, validate, plan, and apply. Each step serves a specific purpose and can fail independently to provide clear feedback.

📋 Terraform Step Sequence & Dependencies

Each step has prerequisites. Understanding the dependency chain helps you diagnose failures fast.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                TERRAFORM WORKFLOW STEP CHAIN                                 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│  ┌──────────────┐                                                           │
│  │ setup-       │  Downloads Terraform binary                               │
│  │ terraform    │  No cloud access needed                                   │
│  └──────┬───────┘                                                           │
│         │                                                                   │
│  ┌──────▼───────┐  ┌──────────────┐                                        │
│  │ terraform    │  │ terraform    │  These two can run in parallel:         │
│  │ fmt -check   │  │ init         │  • fmt needs no backend                 │
│  │              │  │              │  • init needs backend access             │
│  │ No backend   │  │ Backend +    │                                         │
│  │ needed       │  │ provider     │                                         │
│  └──────┬───────┘  │ download     │                                         │
│         │          └──────┬───────┘                                          │
│         │                 │                                                  │
│         └────────┬────────┘                                                  │
│                  │ Both must pass                                            │
│         ┌────────▼────────┐                                                  │
│         │ terraform       │  Checks HCL syntax + provider schemas           │
│         │ validate        │  Needs: init completed (providers downloaded)    │
│         └────────┬────────┘                                                  │
│                  │                                                           │
│         ┌────────▼────────┐                                                  │
│         │ terraform       │  Compares desired vs actual state                │
│         │ plan            │  Needs: init + validate + cloud credentials      │
│         │ -out=tfplan     │  Output: binary plan file + human-readable text  │
│         └────────┬────────┘                                                  │
│                  │                                                           │
│         ┌────────▼────────┐                                                  │
│         │ PR Comment      │  Posts plan output to pull request               │
│         │ (github-script) │  Needs: plan completed + pull-requests: write    │
│         └────────┬────────┘                                                  │
│                  │  ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ MERGE BOUNDARY ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─     │
│                  │                                                           │
│         ┌────────▼────────┐                                                  │
│         │ terraform       │  Executes the reviewed plan                      │
│         │ apply           │  Needs: saved plan file + write credentials      │
│         │ -auto-approve   │  ONLY runs on merge to main (never on PR)       │
│         └────────┬────────┘                                                  │
│                  │                                                           │
│         ┌────────▼────────┐                                                  │
│         │ Post-Apply      │  Health checks, Slack notify, metrics            │
│         │ Verification    │  Needs: apply completed successfully             │
│         └─────────────────┘                                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Error Step Cause Fix
Command not found: terraform Any setup-terraform not run Add hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3 step before terraform commands
Backend initialization required validate/plan init not run first Run terraform init before validate/plan
Error acquiring the state lock plan/apply Another pipeline running Wait for other run to finish, or force-unlock
No valid credential sources init/plan AWS credentials not configured Add configure-aws-credentials step with OIDC
Error: Saved plan is stale apply State changed since plan Re-plan and apply the new plan
id-token permission not set OIDC auth Missing permissions: block Add permissions: id-token: write to job
Resource already exists apply Resource created outside Terraform terraform import the existing resource

💻 Practical Examples

40.3.1 hashicorp/setup-terraform Action

The official hashicorp/setup-terraform action installs a specific Terraform version and optionally configures Terraform Cloud credentials. It also provides a wrapper that captures plan output for use in subsequent steps.

Example: Setup Terraform with Options

Install Terraform with version pinning and optional TFC configuration.

steps:
  # ── Basic setup with version pinning ──
  - name: Setup Terraform
    uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
    with:
      terraform_version: "1.7.0"    # Pin exact version
      # terraform_wrapper: true      # Default: wraps terraform CLI
      # cli_config_credentials_token: ${{ secrets.TF_API_TOKEN }}
      #   ↑ For Terraform Cloud/Enterprise

  # ── Verify installation ──
  - name: Terraform Version
    run: terraform version
    # Output: Terraform v1.7.0 on linux_amd64
40.3.2 terraform init

Initialize the working directory, download providers, and configure the remote backend. Use -input=false to prevent hanging on interactive prompts in CI.

Example: Init with Backend Configuration

Dynamic backend configuration using environment variables.

steps:
  - name: Terraform Init
    run: |
      terraform init \
        -input=false \
        -backend-config="bucket=${{ vars.TF_STATE_BUCKET }}" \
        -backend-config="key=${{ vars.TF_STATE_KEY }}" \
        -backend-config="region=${{ vars.AWS_REGION }}" \
        -backend-config="dynamodb_table=${{ vars.TF_LOCK_TABLE }}" \
        -backend-config="encrypt=true"
    # vars.* = GitHub Actions configuration variables (non-secret)
    # Use for non-sensitive backend config
40.3.3 terraform fmt -check

Check formatting as a fast first step. Fails if any files aren't properly formatted, giving developers immediate feedback before longer operations run.

Example: Format Check Step

Fail fast on formatting issues with helpful output.

steps:
  - name: Terraform Format Check
    id: fmt
    run: terraform fmt -check -recursive -diff
    continue-on-error: true
    # -check: exits 1 if unformatted files found
    # -recursive: checks all subdirectories
    # -diff: shows what needs to change

  - name: Comment on Format Failure
    if: steps.fmt.outcome == 'failure'
    uses: actions/github-script@v7
    with:
      script: |
        github.rest.issues.createComment({
          issue_number: context.issue.number,
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          body: '❌ **Terraform Format Check Failed**\n\nRun `terraform fmt -recursive` locally and push the changes.'
        })
40.3.4 terraform validate

Validate configuration syntax and internal consistency. This catches errors like invalid references, missing required arguments, and type mismatches — without connecting to any backend.

Example: Validate Step

Run validation after init to catch configuration errors early.

steps:
  - name: Terraform Validate
    id: validate
    run: terraform validate -no-color
    # -no-color: clean output for CI logs

  # Alternative: Validate without backend (for PRs from forks)
  # - run: terraform init -backend=false
  # - run: terraform validate
40.3.5 terraform plan

Generate the execution plan showing what will change. Save the plan as a binary file for later apply and capture the text output for PR comments.

Example: Plan with Output Capture

Capture plan output for PR comments and save plan artifact.

steps:
  - name: Terraform Plan
    id: plan
    run: |
      terraform plan \
        -input=false \
        -no-color \
        -out=tfplan \
        -var-file=environments/${{ inputs.environment }}/terraform.tfvars \
        2>&1 | tee plan_output.txt
    continue-on-error: true

  # Check for plan failure
  - name: Plan Status
    if: steps.plan.outcome == 'failure'
    run: exit 1

  # Save plan as artifact
  - name: Upload Plan Artifact
    uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
    with:
      name: tfplan-${{ github.sha }}
      path: |
        tfplan
        plan_output.txt
      retention-days: 5
40.3.6 terraform apply

Apply the saved plan — only on merge to main. Always use -auto-approve or apply from a saved plan file (which implies auto-approve). Never run apply in a PR check.

Example: Apply Step with Safety Guards

Apply only on main branch with notifications.

steps:
  # Download saved plan from plan stage
  - name: Download Plan Artifact
    uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
    with:
      name: tfplan-${{ github.sha }}

  - name: Terraform Apply
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    run: terraform apply -input=false tfplan

  # Notify on success/failure
  - name: Notify Slack on Success
    if: success()
    uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
    with:
      payload: |
        {
          "text": "✅ Terraform apply succeeded for ${{ github.repository }}",
          "blocks": [
            {
              "type": "section",
              "text": {
                "type": "mrkdwn",
                "text": "✅ *Terraform Apply Succeeded*\n• Repo: ${{ github.repository }}\n• Commit: ${{ github.sha }}\n• Actor: ${{ github.actor }}"
              }
            }
          ]
        }

  - name: Notify Slack on Failure
    if: failure()
    uses: slackapi/slack-github-action@v1
    with:
      payload: |
        {
          "text": "❌ Terraform apply FAILED for ${{ github.repository }} — investigate immediately!"
        }

40.4 PR Comments with Plan Output

REVIEW 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: actions/github-script | Issues/Comments API

Posting Terraform plan output as a PR comment is the key feedback loop in GitOps. It lets reviewers see exactly what will change — adds, modifications, deletions — right in the pull request conversation, without needing to check CI logs.

💻 Practical Examples

40.4.1 actions/github-script

Use actions/github-script to interact with the GitHub API directly from a workflow step. It provides an authenticated Octokit client in the github object.

Example: Post Plan Output as PR Comment

Complete step to post the full Terraform plan as a collapsible PR comment.

steps:
  - name: Terraform Plan
    id: plan
    run: terraform plan -no-color -input=false -out=tfplan 2>&1 | tee plan.txt
    continue-on-error: true

  - name: Post Plan to PR
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    uses: actions/github-script@v7
    with:
      github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      script: |
        const fs = require('fs');
        const plan = fs.readFileSync('plan.txt', 'utf8');
        const maxLength = 65000; // GitHub comment limit ~65536
        const truncated = plan.length > maxLength 
          ? plan.substring(0, maxLength) + '\n\n... (truncated)'
          : plan;

        const body = `### Terraform Plan 📋
        
        **Status:** \`${{ steps.plan.outcome }}\`
        **Workspace:** \`${{ env.WORKING_DIR }}\`
        
        <details>
        <summary>Show Plan Output</summary>
        
        \`\`\`hcl
        ${truncated}
        \`\`\`
        
        </details>
        
        *Pushed by: @${{ github.actor }}, Commit: \`${{ github.sha }}\`*`;

        github.rest.issues.createComment({
          issue_number: context.issue.number,
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          body: body
        });
40.4.2 Plan Summary Formatting

Instead of dumping the full plan output, extract a summary showing the count of resources to add, change, and destroy. This gives reviewers a quick overview before diving into details.

Example: Formatted Plan Summary

Extract and format a concise plan summary for the PR comment.

steps:
  - name: Terraform Plan
    id: plan
    run: |
      terraform plan -no-color -input=false -out=tfplan \
        -detailed-exitcode 2>&1 | tee plan.txt
      echo "exitcode=$?" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
    continue-on-error: true

  - name: Generate Plan Summary
    id: summary
    run: |
      # Extract the summary line
      SUMMARY=$(grep -E "Plan:|No changes" plan.txt | tail -1)
      
      # Count changes from JSON plan
      terraform show -json tfplan > plan.json
      ADD=$(jq '[.resource_changes[]? | select(.change.actions | contains(["create"]))] | length' plan.json)
      CHANGE=$(jq '[.resource_changes[]? | select(.change.actions | contains(["update"]))] | length' plan.json)
      DESTROY=$(jq '[.resource_changes[]? | select(.change.actions | contains(["delete"]))] | length' plan.json)
      
      echo "summary=$SUMMARY" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      echo "add=$ADD" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      echo "change=$CHANGE" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      echo "destroy=$DESTROY" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

  - name: Post Summary to PR
    if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
    uses: actions/github-script@v7
    with:
      script: |
        const add = '${{ steps.summary.outputs.add }}';
        const change = '${{ steps.summary.outputs.change }}';
        const destroy = '${{ steps.summary.outputs.destroy }}';
        
        const body = `### 📊 Terraform Plan Summary
        
        | Action | Count |
        |--------|-------|
        | ➕ Add | ${add} |
        | 🔄 Change | ${change} |
        | ❌ Destroy | ${destroy} |
        
        ${parseInt(destroy) > 0 ? '⚠️ **WARNING: Resources will be destroyed!**' : ''}
        
        <details><summary>Full Plan</summary>
        
        \`\`\`
        ${{ steps.plan.outputs.stdout || 'See CI logs' }}
        \`\`\`
        </details>`;
        
        github.rest.issues.createComment({
          issue_number: context.issue.number,
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          body: body
        });
40.4.3 Comment Updates

Instead of posting a new comment on every push, update the existing comment. This keeps the PR conversation clean and avoids comment flood on busy PRs.

Example: Update Existing Comment

Find and update existing bot comment instead of creating new ones.

- name: Update or Create PR Comment
  if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
  uses: actions/github-script@v7
  with:
    script: |
      const fs = require('fs');
      const plan = fs.readFileSync('plan.txt', 'utf8');
      const marker = '<!-- terraform-plan-comment -->';
      
      const body = `${marker}
      ### Terraform Plan 📋
      **Updated:** ${new Date().toISOString()}
      
      <details><summary>Plan Output</summary>
      
      \`\`\`
      ${plan.substring(0, 60000)}
      \`\`\`
      </details>`;
      
      // Find existing comment
      const { data: comments } = await github.rest.issues.listComments({
        owner: context.repo.owner,
        repo: context.repo.repo,
        issue_number: context.issue.number
      });
      
      const existing = comments.find(c => c.body.includes(marker));
      
      if (existing) {
        // Update existing comment
        await github.rest.issues.updateComment({
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          comment_id: existing.id,
          body: body
        });
      } else {
        // Create new comment
        await github.rest.issues.createComment({
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          issue_number: context.issue.number,
          body: body
        });
      }
💡 PR Comment Tips:
• Use <details>/<summary> to collapse long plan output
• Truncate comments to <65,000 characters (GitHub API limit)
• Include a hidden marker (HTML comment) to find/update existing comments
• Show a summary table (add/change/destroy counts) above the full plan
• Highlight destructive changes with warnings (⚠️) to draw reviewer attention

40.5 Managing Multiple Environments

PATTERN 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Matrix Strategy | workflow_dispatch

Real-world projects deploy the same Terraform code to multiple environments (dev, staging, production) with different configurations. GitHub Actions provides several patterns for managing this: matrix strategies, reusable workflows, and manual triggers.

Strategy How It Works Pros Cons Best For
Matrix Strategy Single workflow, parallel jobs per env Simple, parallel execution, one file Same approval for all envs, limited customization Planning all envs on PR
Reusable Workflow Shared workflow called with different inputs DRY, highly configurable, sequential control More complex YAML, harder to debug Production pipelines with promotion
Separate Workflow Files One .yml per environment Maximum flexibility, independent triggers Duplication, harder to maintain consistency Very different env configs
Manual Dispatch workflow_dispatch with env input On-demand, explicit control Not automated, requires manual trigger Emergency deploys, destroy ops
Terragrunt Wrapper that orchestrates multi-env DRY config, dependency management Extra tool, learning curve Complex monorepos with many envs
📁 Common Directory Structures for Multi-Environment

Three common patterns for organizing Terraform code across environments.

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  PATTERN 1: Shared Code + Variable Files (Recommended)                   │
│                                                                          │
│  infrastructure/                                                         │
│  ├── main.tf                    # Shared HCL code                        │
│  ├── variables.tf                                                        │
│  ├── outputs.tf                                                          │
│  └── environments/                                                       │
│      ├── dev/                                                            │
│      │   ├── terraform.tfvars   # Dev-specific values                    │
│      │   └── backend.hcl        # Dev state backend                      │
│      ├── staging/                                                        │
│      │   ├── terraform.tfvars                                            │
│      │   └── backend.hcl                                                 │
│      └── production/                                                     │
│          ├── terraform.tfvars                                            │
│          └── backend.hcl                                                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PATTERN 2: Workspace-Based (Simple but limited)                         │
│                                                                          │
│  infrastructure/                                                         │
│  ├── main.tf                                                             │
│  ├── variables.tf                                                        │
│  └── terraform.tfvars.{workspace}   # Auto-loaded per workspace          │
│                                                                          │
│  # Usage: terraform workspace select dev                                 │
│  #        terraform plan                                                 │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PATTERN 3: Separate Root Modules (Maximum isolation)                    │
│                                                                          │
│  infrastructure/                                                         │
│  ├── modules/                   # Shared module library                  │
│  │   ├── vpc/                                                            │
│  │   └── ecs/                                                            │
│  ├── dev/                       # Independent root module                │
│  │   ├── main.tf               # Calls shared modules                   │
│  │   └── backend.tf                                                      │
│  ├── staging/                                                            │
│  │   ├── main.tf                                                         │
│  │   └── backend.tf                                                      │
│  └── production/                                                         │
│      ├── main.tf                                                         │
│      └── backend.tf                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💻 Practical Examples

40.5.1 Workflow Matrix

Use a matrix strategy to run the same workflow steps across multiple environments in parallel. Each matrix combination gets its own job with environment-specific variables.

Example: Matrix-Based Multi-Environment Plan

Plan across all environments simultaneously on PR.

name: "Terraform Multi-Env Plan"
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  plan:
    name: "Plan - ${{ matrix.environment }}"
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false          # Don't cancel others on one failure
      matrix:
        environment: [dev, staging, production]
        include:
          - environment: dev
            aws_role: arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/tf-dev
            region: us-east-1
          - environment: staging
            aws_role: arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/tf-staging
            region: us-east-1
          - environment: production
            aws_role: arn:aws:iam::333333333333:role/tf-prod
            region: us-east-1

    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ matrix.aws_role }}
          aws-region: ${{ matrix.region }}

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Terraform Init (${{ matrix.environment }})
        run: |
          terraform init \
            -backend-config=environments/${{ matrix.environment }}/backend.hcl

      - name: Terraform Plan (${{ matrix.environment }})
        run: |
          terraform plan \
            -var-file=environments/${{ matrix.environment }}/terraform.tfvars \
            -input=false -no-color -out=tfplan \
            2>&1 | tee plan.txt
40.5.2 Environment-Specific Workflows

For complex environments with different approval requirements, create separate workflow files or use reusable workflows called with different inputs.

Example: Reusable Workflow Pattern

Define a reusable workflow and call it with environment-specific parameters.

# ── .github/workflows/terraform-reusable.yml ──
name: "Terraform Reusable"
on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      environment:
        required: true
        type: string
      terraform_version:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: "1.7.0"
    secrets:
      AWS_ROLE_ARN:
        required: true

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: ${{ inputs.environment }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: ${{ inputs.terraform_version }}
      - run: terraform init -backend-config=env/${{ inputs.environment }}/backend.hcl
      - run: terraform plan -var-file=env/${{ inputs.environment }}/terraform.tfvars -out=tfplan -input=false
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan

---
# ── .github/workflows/deploy.yml ──
name: "Deploy All Environments"
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy-dev:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/terraform-reusable.yml
    with:
      environment: development
    secrets:
      AWS_ROLE_ARN: ${{ secrets.DEV_AWS_ROLE_ARN }}

  deploy-staging:
    needs: deploy-dev
    uses: ./.github/workflows/terraform-reusable.yml
    with:
      environment: staging
    secrets:
      AWS_ROLE_ARN: ${{ secrets.STAGING_AWS_ROLE_ARN }}

  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    uses: ./.github/workflows/terraform-reusable.yml
    with:
      environment: production
    secrets:
      AWS_ROLE_ARN: ${{ secrets.PROD_AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
40.5.3 Manual Triggers

Use workflow_dispatch for on-demand deployments — useful for emergency fixes, targeted environment deploys, or destroy operations that shouldn't run automatically.

Example: Manual Trigger with Inputs

On-demand deployment with environment selection and action choice.

name: "Terraform Manual Deploy"
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        description: 'Target environment'
        required: true
        type: choice
        options: [dev, staging, production]
      action:
        description: 'Terraform action'
        required: true
        type: choice
        options: [plan, apply, destroy]
      confirm_destroy:
        description: 'Type DESTROY to confirm (required for destroy)'
        required: false
        type: string
        default: ""

jobs:
  execute:
    name: "${{ inputs.action }} → ${{ inputs.environment }}"
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: ${{ inputs.environment }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # ── Safety check for destroy ──
      - name: Validate Destroy Confirmation
        if: inputs.action == 'destroy' && inputs.confirm_destroy != 'DESTROY'
        run: |
          echo "❌ Destroy requires typing 'DESTROY' in the confirmation field"
          exit 1

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init -backend-config=env/${{ inputs.environment }}/backend.hcl

      - name: Terraform Plan
        if: inputs.action == 'plan' || inputs.action == 'apply'
        run: |
          terraform plan \
            -var-file=env/${{ inputs.environment }}/terraform.tfvars \
            -out=tfplan -input=false

      - name: Terraform Apply
        if: inputs.action == 'apply'
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan

      - name: Terraform Destroy
        if: inputs.action == 'destroy'
        run: |
          terraform destroy \
            -var-file=env/${{ inputs.environment }}/terraform.tfvars \
            -auto-approve
💡 Multi-Environment Tips:
• Use reusable workflows (workflow_call) to avoid duplicating pipeline logic
• Matrix strategy is great for parallel plans; use sequential jobs with needs for applies
• Store environment-specific Role ARNs in GitHub environment secrets
• Add safety checks for destroy operations (confirmation inputs)
• Use separate AWS accounts per environment for blast-radius isolation

40.6 Caching

PERFORMANCE
📚 Official Documentation: actions/cache | Provider Plugin Cache

Terraform provider downloads can take 30-60 seconds, adding up across multiple runs. Caching provider plugins between workflow runs significantly speeds up terraform init and reduces bandwidth usage.

⚡ Cache Impact on Pipeline Performance

Typical time savings from caching Terraform providers and modules in CI/CD.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     PIPELINE EXECUTION TIME COMPARISON                     │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                           │
│  WITHOUT CACHE:                                                           │
│  ┌──────┐┌─────────────────────────────┐┌──────┐┌────────┐┌──────┐       │
│  │setup ││        terraform init       ││ fmt  ││validate││ plan │       │
│  │ 3s   ││         45-60s              ││  2s  ││   3s   ││ 15s  │       │
│  └──────┘└─────────────────────────────┘└──────┘└────────┘└──────┘       │
│  Total: ~75-85 seconds                                                    │
│                                                                           │
│  WITH PROVIDER CACHE:                                                     │
│  ┌──────┐┌───────┐┌─────────────┐┌──────┐┌────────┐┌──────┐             │
│  │setup ││restore││ terraform   ││ fmt  ││validate││ plan │             │
│  │ 3s   ││cache  ││ init ~5s    ││  2s  ││   3s   ││ 15s  │             │
│  │      ││  5s   ││ (cached!)   ││      ││        ││      │             │
│  └──────┘└───────┘└─────────────┘└──────┘└────────┘└──────┘             │
│  Total: ~33 seconds  (55-61% faster! 🚀)                                 │
│                                                                           │
│  WITH PROVIDER + MODULE CACHE:                                            │
│  ┌──────┐┌───────┐┌──────────┐┌──────┐┌────────┐┌──────┐               │
│  │setup ││restore││ init ~2s ││ fmt  ││validate││ plan │               │
│  │ 3s   ││cache  ││ (all     ││  2s  ││   3s   ││ 15s  │               │
│  │      ││  5s   ││ cached!) ││      ││        ││      │               │
│  └──────┘└───────┘└──────────┘└──────┘└────────┘└──────┘               │
│  Total: ~30 seconds  (60-65% faster! 🚀🚀)                               │
│                                                                           │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  📊 For a matrix of 3 environments:                                       │
│     Without cache: 3 × 80s = 240s total compute                          │
│     With cache:    3 × 30s =  90s total compute                           │
│     Savings:       150 seconds per PR  ×  ~20 PRs/week = 50 min/week     │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Cache Key Best Practices:
• Always base cache key on hashFiles('**/.terraform.lock.hcl') — invalidates when provider versions change
• Use restore-keys: for partial matches — better than no cache at all
• Include runner.os in key — providers are platform-specific binaries
• GitHub Actions cache has a 10 GB limit per repository
• Caches are automatically evicted after 7 days of no access
• Use TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR (not caching .terraform) for the most reliable approach

💻 Practical Examples

40.6.1 Provider Plugin Cache

Terraform supports a plugin cache directory that stores downloaded providers. Set the TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR environment variable to enable caching across multiple init runs.

Example: Plugin Cache with actions/cache

Cache Terraform providers between workflow runs for faster init.

env:
  TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR: ${{ github.workspace }}/.terraform.d/plugin-cache

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # ── Create cache directory ──
      - name: Create Plugin Cache Dir
        run: mkdir -p $TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR

      # ── Restore cached providers ──
      - name: Cache Terraform Providers
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ${{ env.TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR }}
          key: terraform-providers-${{ hashFiles('**/.terraform.lock.hcl') }}
          restore-keys: |
            terraform-providers-

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      # Init will use cached providers if available
      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init -input=false
        # First run: downloads providers (~30s)
        # Cached run: skips download (~2s)
40.6.2 actions/cache

The actions/cache action stores and restores files between workflow runs. The cache key should be based on the lock file hash, so the cache updates whenever provider versions change.

Example: Advanced Cache Configuration

Cache with fallback keys and the .terraform directory.

# ── Cache Strategy 1: Plugin cache only (recommended) ──
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: |
      ~/.terraform.d/plugin-cache
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-terraform-${{ hashFiles('**/.terraform.lock.hcl') }}
    restore-keys: |
      ${{ runner.os }}-terraform-

# ── Cache Strategy 2: Full .terraform directory ──
# ⚠️ Larger cache but faster init (skips provider verification)
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: |
      **/.terraform
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-terraform-full-${{ hashFiles('**/.terraform.lock.hcl') }}
    restore-keys: |
      ${{ runner.os }}-terraform-full-

# ── Cache Strategy 3: Multi-layer (modules + providers) ──
- name: Cache Terraform Modules
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: |
      .terraform/modules
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-tf-modules-${{ hashFiles('**/*.tf') }}
    restore-keys: |
      ${{ runner.os }}-tf-modules-

- name: Cache Terraform Providers
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: |
      .terraform/providers
      ~/.terraform.d/plugin-cache
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-tf-providers-${{ hashFiles('.terraform.lock.hcl') }}
    restore-keys: |
      ${{ runner.os }}-tf-providers-
Cache Strategy Cache Size Speed Gain Reliability
Plugin Cache Dir ~100-300 MB 20-40 seconds 🟢 High (official method)
Full .terraform ~200-500 MB 30-60 seconds 🟡 Medium (may have stale data)
Multi-Layer ~150-400 MB 25-50 seconds 🟢 High (granular invalidation)

40.7 Complete Workflow Examples

PRODUCTION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: GitHub Actions Tutorial

Complete, production-ready workflow examples that bring together all the concepts from this chapter: OIDC auth, caching, plan comments, multi-environment deploys, and security best practices.

💻 Practical Examples

40.7.1 Basic PR Workflow

A complete workflow for PR-based Terraform reviews: validate, plan, post comment. This is the starting point for any Terraform CI/CD setup.

Example: Complete PR Workflow

Full workflow for plan-on-PR with formatting, validation, and PR comments.

# .github/workflows/terraform-pr.yml
name: "Terraform PR Check"
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['**.tf', '**.tfvars', '.github/workflows/terraform-pr.yml']

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write

env:
  TF_VERSION: "1.7.0"
  WORKING_DIR: "./infrastructure"
  TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR: ${{ github.workspace }}/.terraform.d/plugin-cache

jobs:
  terraform:
    name: "Terraform Plan"
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: ${{ env.WORKING_DIR }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Create Plugin Cache Dir
        run: mkdir -p $TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR

      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ${{ env.TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR }}
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-tf-${{ hashFiles('**/.terraform.lock.hcl') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-tf-

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Terraform Format
        id: fmt
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Terraform Init
        id: init
        run: terraform init -input=false

      - name: Terraform Validate
        id: validate
        run: terraform validate -no-color

      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -no-color -input=false -out=tfplan 2>&1 | tee plan.txt
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Post PR Comment
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const plan = fs.readFileSync('${{ env.WORKING_DIR }}/plan.txt', 'utf8');
            const body = `### Terraform Results 🏗️
            | Step | Status |
            |------|--------|
            | Format | \`${{ steps.fmt.outcome }}\` |
            | Init | \`${{ steps.init.outcome }}\` |
            | Validate | \`${{ steps.validate.outcome }}\` |
            | Plan | \`${{ steps.plan.outcome }}\` |
            
            <details><summary>Show Plan</summary>
            
            \`\`\`hcl\n${plan.substring(0,60000)}\n\`\`\`
            </details>`;
            
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: body
            });

      - name: Fail on Plan Error
        if: steps.plan.outcome == 'failure'
        run: exit 1
40.7.2 Multi-Environment Workflow

A sequential deployment pipeline: dev → staging (with tests) → production (with approval). Uses reusable workflows for DRY pipeline definitions.

Example: Sequential Multi-Environment Deploy

Full promotion pipeline from dev through production with gates.

# .github/workflows/terraform-deploy.yml
name: "Terraform Deploy"
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['**.tf', '**.tfvars']

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

jobs:
  # ── Dev: Auto-deploy ──
  deploy-dev:
    name: "Deploy → Dev"
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: development
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: |
          cd infrastructure
          terraform init -backend-config=env/dev/backend.hcl
          terraform plan -var-file=env/dev/terraform.tfvars -out=tfplan -input=false
          terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan

  # ── Staging: Deploy after dev + run tests ──
  deploy-staging:
    name: "Deploy → Staging"
    needs: deploy-dev
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: |
          cd infrastructure
          terraform init -backend-config=env/staging/backend.hcl
          terraform plan -var-file=env/staging/terraform.tfvars -out=tfplan -input=false
          terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan

  # ── Integration Tests ──
  test-staging:
    name: "Test Staging"
    needs: deploy-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Health Checks
        run: |
          curl -sf https://staging-api.example.com/health || exit 1
          echo "✅ Staging health check passed"

  # ── Production: Manual approval required ──
  deploy-production:
    name: "Deploy → Production"
    needs: test-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: production            # Triggers approval gate
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - run: |
          cd infrastructure
          terraform init -backend-config=env/production/backend.hcl
          terraform plan -var-file=env/production/terraform.tfvars -out=tfplan -input=false
          terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
40.7.3 Monorepo Workflow

In monorepos with multiple Terraform projects, use path filters and dynamic matrix generation to only plan/apply the directories that changed.

Example: Monorepo with Changed-Path Detection

Detect which Terraform directories changed and only run pipelines for those.

# .github/workflows/terraform-monorepo.yml
name: "Terraform Monorepo"
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['infrastructure/**']

jobs:
  # ── Detect changed directories ──
  detect-changes:
    name: "Detect Changes"
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      directories: ${{ steps.changes.outputs.directories }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0  # Full history for diff

      - name: Find Changed Terraform Directories
        id: changes
        run: |
          # Get list of changed .tf files
          CHANGED=$(git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD -- 'infrastructure/**/*.tf')
          
          # Extract unique directories
          DIRS=$(echo "$CHANGED" | xargs -I{} dirname {} | sort -u | jq -R -s -c 'split("\n") | map(select(. != ""))')
          
          echo "directories=$DIRS" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          echo "Changed directories: $DIRS"

  # ── Plan each changed directory ──
  plan:
    name: "Plan - ${{ matrix.directory }}"
    needs: detect-changes
    if: needs.detect-changes.outputs.directories != '[]'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        directory: ${{ fromJson(needs.detect-changes.outputs.directories) }}
    
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Terraform Plan
        working-directory: ${{ matrix.directory }}
        run: |
          terraform init -input=false
          terraform plan -no-color -input=false 2>&1 | tee plan.txt

      - name: Comment Plan on PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const plan = fs.readFileSync('${{ matrix.directory }}/plan.txt', 'utf8');
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: `### 📁 ${{ matrix.directory }}\n<details><summary>Plan</summary>\n\n\`\`\`\n${plan.substring(0,60000)}\n\`\`\`\n</details>`
            });
💡 Monorepo Best Practices:
• Use path filters in triggers to avoid running everything on every change
• Detect changed directories dynamically with git diff
• Use matrix strategy to plan changed directories in parallel
• Keep shared modules in a separate directory with broader trigger rules
• Consider Terragrunt or Atlantis for complex monorepo orchestration
40.7.4 Production Readiness Checklist

Before shipping your Terraform GitHub Actions workflow to production, verify every item on this checklist. Each item maps to a concept from this chapter.

✅ GitHub Actions + Terraform Production Checklist

Comprehensive checklist covering security, reliability, and observability for production-grade pipelines.

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║           TERRAFORM + GITHUB ACTIONS PRODUCTION READINESS                    ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╣
║                                                                              ║
║  🔐 SECURITY                                                                ║
║  ───────────                                                                 ║
║  □ OIDC authentication (no static AWS keys)              [Section 40.2]      ║
║  □ Separate IAM roles for plan (read) vs apply (write)   [Section 39.6]      ║
║  □ OIDC trust scoped to specific repo + branch           [Section 40.2]      ║
║  □ Actions pinned to commit SHA (not tags)               [Section 39.6]      ║
║  □ .terraform.lock.hcl committed and verified            [Section 39.6]      ║
║  □ Secrets use GitHub encrypted secrets (masked)         [Section 39.6]      ║
║  □ All outputs use sensitive = true where needed         [Section 39.6]      ║
║                                                                              ║
║  📋 WORKFLOW STRUCTURE                                                       ║
║  ─────────────────────                                                       ║
║  □ terraform fmt -check runs on every PR                 [Section 40.3]      ║
║  □ terraform validate runs after init                    [Section 40.3]      ║
║  □ terraform plan runs on PR, apply on merge only        [Section 39.2]      ║
║  □ Plan output posted as PR comment (collapsible)        [Section 40.4]      ║
║  □ Plan saved as artifact (-out=tfplan)                  [Section 39.3]      ║
║  □ -input=false on all terraform commands                [Section 39.2]      ║
║  □ -no-color for plan output captured for comments       [Section 40.4]      ║
║  □ Terraform version pinned (required_version)           [Section 40.3]      ║
║                                                                              ║
║  🌍 MULTI-ENVIRONMENT                                                        ║
║  ────────────────────                                                        ║
║  □ Separate variable files per environment               [Section 40.5]      ║
║  □ Separate state backends per environment               [Section 40.5]      ║
║  □ Environment protection rules configured               [Section 39.4]      ║
║  □ Production requires manual approval                   [Section 39.2]      ║
║  □ Sequential promotion: dev → staging → production      [Section 39.2]      ║
║  □ Integration tests run in staging                      [Section 40.7]      ║
║                                                                              ║
║  ⚡ PERFORMANCE                                                              ║
║  ──────────────                                                              ║
║  □ Provider cache configured (TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR)       [Section 40.6]      ║
║  □ actions/cache with lock file hash key                 [Section 40.6]      ║
║  □ Path filters on triggers (only run on .tf changes)    [Section 40.1]      ║
║  □ fail-fast: false on matrix (don't cancel siblings)    [Section 40.5]      ║
║                                                                              ║
║  📊 OBSERVABILITY                                                            ║
║  ────────────────                                                            ║
║  □ Slack/Teams notification on apply success/failure     [Section 40.3]      ║
║  □ Plan summary table in PR comment (add/change/destroy) [Section 40.4]      ║
║  □ Destructive change warnings (⚠️ destroy count > 0)    [Section 40.4]      ║
║  □ Post-apply health checks / smoke tests                [Section 40.7]      ║
║                                                                              ║
║  🔄 RELIABILITY                                                              ║
║  ──────────────                                                              ║
║  □ State backend has versioning enabled                  [Section 39.5]      ║
║  □ State locking configured (DynamoDB)                   [Section 39.5]      ║
║  □ Pre-apply state backup script                         [Section 39.5]      ║
║  □ Rollback runbook documented                           [Section 39.5]      ║
║  □ Plan staleness check (max age before re-plan)         [Section 39.3]      ║
║                                                                              ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
Action Version Purpose Required For
actions/checkout v4 Clone repository code Every workflow
hashicorp/setup-terraform v3 Install Terraform CLI Every workflow
aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials v4 AWS OIDC authentication AWS deployments
actions/cache v4 Cache providers between runs Performance optimization
actions/upload-artifact v4 Store plan files as artifacts Saved plan workflows
actions/download-artifact v4 Retrieve plan files for apply Saved plan workflows
actions/github-script v7 Post plan as PR comment PR feedback loop
🎯 Chapter 40 Summary — Key Takeaways:
• OIDC is non-negotiable — never use static AWS keys in GitHub Actions
• Plan on PR, apply on merge — the golden rule of infrastructure CI/CD
• Cache providers — saves 30-60 seconds per run, adds up fast
• Post plans as PR comments — reviewers shouldn't have to check CI logs
• Update comments, don't flood — use hidden markers to find/update existing comments
• Reusable workflows — avoid duplicating pipeline logic across environments
• Pin everything — Terraform version, provider versions, action versions (to SHA)
🦊 🔄 🏗️ 📋
CHAPTER 41

GitLab CI/CD with Terraform

GitLab CI Terraform Pipeline Managed State MR Integration
4Sections
8Subsections
12Examples
🦊Platform
PART 11 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

Master GitLab CI/CD pipelines for Terraform — leveraging managed state, native Terraform integration images, and merge request workflows for seamless infrastructure deployment.

Learning Objectives

  • Configure GitLab CI/CD YAML pipelines for Terraform workflows
  • Use the official gitlab-terraform helper image for native integration
  • Leverage GitLab Managed Terraform State for remote state storage
  • Integrate Terraform plan output into Merge Request widgets
  • Set up OIDC-based AWS authentication from GitLab runners
  • Build complete multi-environment GitLab Terraform pipelines

Chapter Contents

41.1 GitLab CI Basics 41.2 Terraform Pipeline 41.3 AWS Authentication 41.4 Complete Pipeline Example

41.1 GitLab CI Basics

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: GitLab CI/CD

GitLab CI/CD uses a .gitlab-ci.yml file at the root of your repository to define pipelines. Pipelines consist of stages (sequential phases) and jobs (individual tasks within stages).

💡 GitLab CI/CD vs GitHub Actions: GitLab CI/CD uses stages/jobs (sequential then parallel) while GitHub Actions uses workflows/jobs/steps. GitLab has built-in Terraform support with managed state, making it particularly well-suited for infrastructure pipelines.
41.1.1 .gitlab-ci.yml Structure

The .gitlab-ci.yml file defines the entire pipeline. It supports inheritance, includes, and templates for reusable configurations.

📂 Pipeline Architecture: 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 .gitlab-ci.yml                       │
│                                                      │
│  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐          │
│  │ validate  │→│   plan    │→│  apply    │  STAGES  │
│  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘          │
│       │              │             │                 │
│  ┌────┴────┐   ┌────┴────┐  ┌────┴────┐           │
│  │  fmt    │   │plan-dev │  │apply-dev│  JOBS     │
│  │validate │   │plan-stg │  │apply-stg│           │
│  └─────────┘   └─────────┘  └─────────┘           │
│                                                      │
│  variables:          before_script:                  │
│    TF_ROOT: "infra"    - cd $TF_ROOT               │
│    TF_STATE: "default" - terraform init             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Basic .gitlab-ci.yml Structure
Minimal pipeline definition with stages, variables, and jobs 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — Basic structure
#
# Stages run sequentially; jobs within the same stage run in parallel

# Define global variables
variables:
  TF_ROOT: ${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/terraform
  TF_STATE_NAME: default

# Define pipeline stages (sequential order)
stages:
  - validate
  - plan
  - apply

# Default settings for all jobs
default:
  image: hashicorp/terraform:1.7
  before_script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - terraform init

# ─── Validate Stage ───────────────────────────
fmt:
  stage: validate
  script:
    - terraform fmt -check -recursive
  allow_failure: true

validate:
  stage: validate
  script:
    - terraform validate

# ─── Plan Stage ───────────────────────────────
plan:
  stage: plan
  script:
    - terraform plan -out=plan.cache
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    expire_in: 7 days
  only:
    - merge_requests
    - main

# ─── Apply Stage ──────────────────────────────
apply:
  stage: apply
  script:
    - terraform apply -auto-approve plan.cache
  dependencies:
    - plan
  when: manual
  only:
    - main
Keyword Purpose Example
stagesDefine sequential pipeline phasesstages: [validate, plan, apply]
variablesGlobal/job-level environment variablesTF_ROOT: "./infra"
defaultDefault settings inherited by all jobsimage: hashicorp/terraform:1.7
before_scriptCommands run before each job's script- terraform init
artifactsFiles passed between jobs/stagespaths: [plan.cache]
only/exceptBranch/event filters (legacy)only: [main, merge_requests]
rulesModern conditional job executionrules: - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"
41.1.2 Stages and Jobs

GitLab pipelines execute stages sequentially. All jobs within a stage run in parallel by default. Use needs to create a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for cross-stage dependencies.

DAG Pipeline with needs Keyword
Fine-grained job dependencies using DAG 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — DAG pipeline for multi-environment Terraform
#
# Using 'needs' allows jobs to start as soon as dependencies complete,
# rather than waiting for the entire stage to finish

stages:
  - validate
  - plan
  - apply

validate:
  stage: validate
  script:
    - terraform fmt -check
    - terraform validate

# ─── Dev Environment ──────────────────────────
plan-dev:
  stage: plan
  needs: ["validate"]
  script:
    - terraform workspace select dev
    - terraform plan -out=dev.tfplan
  artifacts:
    paths: [dev.tfplan]

apply-dev:
  stage: apply
  needs: ["plan-dev"]
  script:
    - terraform workspace select dev
    - terraform apply -auto-approve dev.tfplan
  when: manual
  environment:
    name: development
    url: https://dev.example.com

# ─── Staging Environment ─────────────────────
plan-staging:
  stage: plan
  needs: ["validate"]
  script:
    - terraform workspace select staging
    - terraform plan -out=staging.tfplan
  artifacts:
    paths: [staging.tfplan]

apply-staging:
  stage: apply
  needs: ["plan-staging", "apply-dev"]
  script:
    - terraform workspace select staging
    - terraform apply -auto-approve staging.tfplan
  when: manual
  environment:
    name: staging
    url: https://staging.example.com
⚡ DAG vs Sequential Stages:
FeatureSequential StagesDAG (needs)
Execution OrderAll stage jobs must complete before next stageJobs start when their dependencies complete
SpeedSlower — waits for entire stageFaster — parallel where possible
Use CaseSimple linear pipelinesComplex multi-environment deployments
Artifact PassingAll artifacts from previous stages availableOnly artifacts from specified needs
41.1.3 Variables

GitLab CI/CD variables can be defined at the project, group, or pipeline level. Use them to pass configuration, secrets, and feature flags to jobs.

Variable Configuration
Different scopes and types of CI/CD variables 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — Variable configuration
#
# Variables cascade: GitLab instance → Group → Project → Pipeline → Job

# Global pipeline variables
variables:
  TF_ROOT: "${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/terraform"
  TF_STATE_NAME: "default"
  # Terraform log level (set in GitLab UI for debugging)
  # TF_LOG: "DEBUG"

plan:
  stage: plan
  variables:
    # Job-level variables override global ones
    TF_STATE_NAME: "production"
    # Reference other variables
    PLAN_FILE: "${TF_ROOT}/plan-${CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA}.cache"
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - terraform init
    - terraform plan -out=${PLAN_FILE}
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${PLAN_FILE}
Protected and Masked Variables
Configuring sensitive variables in GitLab UI (Settings → CI/CD → Variables) 
# GitLab UI → Settings → CI/CD → Variables
#
# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │ Key:          AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID                             │
# │ Value:        AKIA********************                      │
# │ Type:         Variable                                      │
# │ Protected:    ✅ (only available on protected branches)     │
# │ Masked:       ✅ (hidden in job logs)                       │
# │ Expanded:     ✅ (variable references expanded)             │
# │ Environment:  production                                    │
# ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
# │ Key:          AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY                         │
# │ Value:        ****************************************      │
# │ Type:         Variable                                      │
# │ Protected:    ✅                                            │
# │ Masked:       ✅                                            │
# │ Environment:  production                                    │
# └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
#
# BEST PRACTICE: Use OIDC instead of static credentials!
# See Section 41.3 for AWS OIDC integration
Variable Scope Defined In Precedence
InstanceAdmin → CI/CD → VariablesLowest
GroupGroup → Settings → CI/CD⬆️
ProjectProject → Settings → CI/CD⬆️
Pipelinevariables: in YAML⬆️
JobJob-level variables:Highest
🔒 Predefined CI/CD Variables: GitLab provides many built-in variables: CI_PROJECT_DIR (repo root), CI_COMMIT_SHA (full commit hash), CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA (8-char short hash), CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE (trigger type), CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID (MR number), CI_ENVIRONMENT_NAME (deployment environment). See https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/ci/variables/predefined_variables.html for the full list.

41.2 Terraform Pipeline

INTEGRATION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: GitLab Terraform Integration

GitLab provides first-class Terraform integration through the gitlab-terraform helper image, managed Terraform state backend, and native merge request plan widgets.

41.2.1 gitlab-terraform Image

The gitlab-terraform image wraps Terraform commands, automatically handling state configuration, plan caching, and report generation for GitLab integration.

🐳 gitlab-terraform Helper: 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          gitlab-terraform Helper Image                   │
│                                                          │
│  Command:                     What it does:             │
│  ─────────────────────────── ──────────────────────────  │
│  gitlab-terraform init      → terraform init             │
│                               + auto-configure HTTP      │
│                               backend to GitLab state    │
│                                                          │
│  gitlab-terraform plan      → terraform plan             │
│                               + save plan to cache       │
│                               + generate plan.json       │
│                               (for MR widget)            │
│                                                          │
│  gitlab-terraform apply     → terraform apply            │
│                               + use cached plan          │
│                                                          │
│  gitlab-terraform destroy   → terraform destroy          │
│                               + cleanup state            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Using the gitlab-terraform Image
Pipeline with the official GitLab Terraform template image 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — Using gitlab-terraform helper
#
# The gitlab-terraform image auto-configures:
#   - HTTP backend pointing to GitLab Managed Terraform State
#   - Plan caching between jobs via artifacts
#   - JSON plan output for MR widget integration

image:
  name: "registry.gitlab.com/gitlab-org/terraform-images/releases/1.4:v1.1.0"

variables:
  TF_ROOT: ${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/terraform
  TF_STATE_NAME: default
  TF_ADDRESS: ${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/terraform/state/${TF_STATE_NAME}

stages:
  - init
  - validate
  - plan
  - apply

init:
  stage: init
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform init
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/.terraform

validate:
  stage: validate
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform validate

plan:
  stage: plan
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform plan
    - gitlab-terraform plan-json
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    reports:
      terraform: ${TF_ROOT}/plan.json    # ← MR widget integration!
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH

apply:
  stage: apply
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform apply
  dependencies:
    - plan
  when: manual
  rules:
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH
Command Equivalent Extra Features
gitlab-terraform initterraform initAuto-configures HTTP backend
gitlab-terraform planterraform plan -out=plan.cacheSaves binary plan artifact
gitlab-terraform plan-jsonterraform show -json plan.cacheGenerates JSON for MR widget
gitlab-terraform applyterraform apply plan.cacheUses cached plan automatically
41.2.2 Managed Terraform State

GitLab can serve as a Terraform HTTP backend, storing state files directly in the project. This eliminates the need for external state backends like S3 + DynamoDB.

GitLab Managed State Configuration
Using GitLab as the Terraform HTTP backend 
# backend.tf — GitLab HTTP backend configuration
#
# GitLab Managed Terraform State provides:
#   - Built-in state locking (no DynamoDB needed)
#   - State versioning (rollback available)
#   - Access control via GitLab permissions
#   - Encryption at rest

terraform {
  backend "http" {
    # These values are auto-configured by gitlab-terraform
    # but can be set manually for local development:
    #
    # address        = "https://gitlab.com/api/v4/projects/<ID>/terraform/state/default"
    # lock_address   = "https://gitlab.com/api/v4/projects/<ID>/terraform/state/default/lock"
    # unlock_address = "https://gitlab.com/api/v4/projects/<ID>/terraform/state/default/lock"
    # username       = "gitlab-ci-token"
    # password       = $CI_JOB_TOKEN  # Set via environment
    # lock_method    = "POST"
    # unlock_method  = "DELETE"
    # retry_wait_min = 5
  }
}
Multiple State Files per Project
Managing separate state files for different environments 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — Multiple state files
#
# Use TF_STATE_NAME to manage separate states per environment

variables:
  TF_ROOT: ${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/terraform

# ─── Development ──────────────────────────────
plan-dev:
  stage: plan
  variables:
    TF_STATE_NAME: "dev"
    TF_ADDRESS: "${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/terraform/state/dev"
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform init
    - gitlab-terraform plan
    - gitlab-terraform plan-json
  artifacts:
    name: plan-dev
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    reports:
      terraform: ${TF_ROOT}/plan.json

# ─── Production ───────────────────────────────
plan-prod:
  stage: plan
  variables:
    TF_STATE_NAME: "prod"
    TF_ADDRESS: "${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/terraform/state/prod"
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform init
    - gitlab-terraform plan
    - gitlab-terraform plan-json
  artifacts:
    name: plan-prod
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    reports:
      terraform: ${TF_ROOT}/plan.json
📊 GitLab Managed State vs S3 Backend:
FeatureGitLab Managed StateS3 + DynamoDB
Setup ComplexityZero — built into GitLabRequires S3 bucket + DynamoDB table
State LockingBuilt-inRequires DynamoDB
Access ControlGitLab project permissionsIAM policies
VersioningBuilt-in with rollbackS3 versioning
CostFree with GitLabAWS charges for S3 + DynamoDB
Multi-CloudLimited to GitLabAWS-specific
Production ScaleGood for small/mediumBattle-tested at scale
41.2.3 MR Integration

GitLab natively displays Terraform plan output in Merge Request widgets when reports: terraform is configured. This provides instant visibility into infrastructure changes during code review.

🔀 MR Terraform Widget: 
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Merge Request !42: Add RDS instance                     │
│  ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ │
│                                                          │
│  ┌── Terraform Plan ───────────────────────────────────┐│
│  │  📊 Plan: 3 to add, 1 to change, 0 to destroy      ││
│  │                                                      ││
│  │  + aws_db_instance.main                             ││
│  │  + aws_db_subnet_group.main                         ││
│  │  + aws_security_group.rds                           ││
│  │  ~ aws_security_group_rule.app_to_rds               ││
│  │                                                      ││
│  │  [View Full Plan]  [Apply] (manual)                 ││
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘│
│                                                          │
│  ✅ Pipeline passed    🔒 Approved by @devops-lead      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Enabling MR Plan Widget
Configure the Terraform report artifact for MR integration 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — MR widget integration
#
# The 'reports: terraform' artifact type triggers
# GitLab's built-in Terraform plan widget in MRs

plan:
  stage: plan
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform init
    - gitlab-terraform plan
    - gitlab-terraform plan-json
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    reports:
      # This is the key line — tells GitLab to parse the JSON
      # plan and display it in the MR widget
      terraform: ${TF_ROOT}/plan.json
  rules:
    # Only generate plan for merge requests
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"
    # Also for default branch (for apply)
    - if: $CI_COMMIT_BRANCH == $CI_DEFAULT_BRANCH
Custom MR Comments with Plan Output
Posting detailed plan output as an MR note via GitLab API 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — Custom plan comment on MR
#
# For more detail than the built-in widget provides

plan-with-comment:
  stage: plan
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - gitlab-terraform init
    - gitlab-terraform plan | tee plan_output.txt
    - gitlab-terraform plan-json
    # Post plan output as MR comment
    - |
      if [ -n "$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID" ]; then
        PLAN_OUTPUT=$(cat plan_output.txt)
        COMMENT_BODY=$(cat <<EOF
      ## 🏗️ Terraform Plan Output

      \`\`\`
      ${PLAN_OUTPUT}
      \`\`\`

      **Pipeline:** [#${CI_PIPELINE_ID}](${CI_PIPELINE_URL})
      **Commit:** \`${CI_COMMIT_SHORT_SHA}\`
      EOF
        )
        curl --silent --request POST \
          --header "PRIVATE-TOKEN: ${GITLAB_API_TOKEN}" \
          --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
          --data "$(jq -n --arg body "$COMMENT_BODY" '{body: $body}')" \
          "${CI_API_V4_URL}/projects/${CI_PROJECT_ID}/merge_requests/${CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID}/notes"
      fi
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache
    reports:
      terraform: ${TF_ROOT}/plan.json
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"

41.3 AWS Authentication

SECURITY 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: GitLab OIDC with AWS

Use OpenID Connect (OIDC) to authenticate GitLab CI runners with AWS — no static credentials needed. GitLab issues JWT tokens that AWS STS exchanges for temporary IAM credentials.

41.3.1 OIDC Integration

Configure AWS IAM to trust GitLab as an OIDC identity provider. This enables keyless authentication where GitLab-issued JWTs are exchanged for temporary AWS credentials.

🔐 OIDC Authentication Flow: 
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
│  GitLab CI   │     │  AWS STS     │     │  AWS IAM     │
│  Runner      │     │  Service     │     │  Role        │
│              │     │              │     │              │
│  1. Request  │────▶│  2. Validate │────▶│  3. Check    │
│     JWT      │     │     JWT with │     │     trust    │
│     Token    │     │     OIDC     │     │     policy   │
│              │     │     provider │     │              │
│  6. Use temp │◀────│  5. Return   │◀────│  4. Approve  │
│     creds    │     │     temp     │     │     assume   │
│     for TF   │     │     creds    │     │     role     │
└──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘
AWS IAM OIDC Provider for GitLab
Terraform configuration to set up GitLab as an OIDC provider in AWS 
# oidc.tf — GitLab OIDC provider for AWS
#
# This creates the trust relationship between GitLab and AWS

# OIDC Identity Provider
resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "gitlab" {
  url = "https://gitlab.com"

  client_id_list = [
    "https://gitlab.com"    # GitLab's OIDC audience
  ]

  thumbprint_list = [
    "b3dd7606d2b5a8b4a13771dbecc9ee1cecafa38a"
  ]

  tags = {
    Name        = "gitlab-oidc-provider"
    Environment = "shared"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

# IAM Role for GitLab CI
resource "aws_iam_role" "gitlab_ci" {
  name = "gitlab-ci-terraform"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Federated = aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.gitlab.arn
        }
        Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
        Condition = {
          StringEquals = {
            # Restrict to specific GitLab project
            "gitlab.com:sub" = "project_path:my-group/my-project:ref_type:branch:ref:main"
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  })

  tags = {
    Name      = "gitlab-ci-terraform"
    ManagedBy = "terraform"
  }
}

# Attach policies for Terraform operations
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "terraform_access" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.gitlab_ci.name
  policy_arn = aws_iam_policy.terraform_deployer.arn
}

# Output the role ARN for pipeline configuration
output "gitlab_ci_role_arn" {
  value       = aws_iam_role.gitlab_ci.arn
  description = "ARN of the IAM role for GitLab CI"
}
GitLab Pipeline with OIDC Authentication
Using id_tokens to authenticate with AWS via OIDC 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — OIDC authentication with AWS
#
# GitLab 15.7+ supports id_tokens for OIDC

variables:
  TF_ROOT: ${CI_PROJECT_DIR}/terraform
  ROLE_ARN: "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/gitlab-ci-terraform"

# Request an OIDC token from GitLab
.aws_auth:
  id_tokens:
    GITLAB_OIDC_TOKEN:
      aud: "https://gitlab.com"
  before_script:
    # Exchange GitLab JWT for AWS temporary credentials
    - >
      STS_RESPONSE=$(aws sts assume-role-with-web-identity
      --role-arn ${ROLE_ARN}
      --role-session-name "gitlab-ci-${CI_PIPELINE_ID}"
      --web-identity-token ${GITLAB_OIDC_TOKEN}
      --duration-seconds 3600)
    - export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=$(echo $STS_RESPONSE | jq -r '.Credentials.AccessKeyId')
    - export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=$(echo $STS_RESPONSE | jq -r '.Credentials.SecretAccessKey')
    - export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=$(echo $STS_RESPONSE | jq -r '.Credentials.SessionToken')

plan:
  stage: plan
  extends: .aws_auth
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - terraform init
    - terraform plan -out=plan.cache
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - ${TF_ROOT}/plan.cache

apply:
  stage: apply
  extends: .aws_auth
  script:
    - cd ${TF_ROOT}
    - terraform apply -auto-approve plan.cache
  dependencies:
    - plan
  when: manual
OIDC Claim Description Example Value
subSubject — project + refproject_path:group/project:ref_type:branch:ref:main
audAudiencehttps://gitlab.com
issIssuerhttps://gitlab.com
namespace_pathGroup/subgroup pathmy-org/infra-team
project_pathFull project pathmy-org/infra-team/aws-infra
refGit referencemain
41.3.2 CI/CD Variables

For scenarios where OIDC is not available (self-managed GitLab, older versions), CI/CD variables provide an alternative for AWS credential management.

Environment-Scoped Variables
Using GitLab environments with scoped credentials 
# .gitlab-ci.yml — Environment-scoped credentials
#
# Different AWS credentials per environment
# Configured in: Settings → CI/CD → Variables → Environment scope

stages:
  - plan
  - apply

# Dev uses dev account credentials
# (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY scoped to 'development')
plan-dev:
  stage: plan
  environment:
    name: development
  script:
    - cd terraform/environments/dev
    - terraform init
    - terraform plan -out=plan.cache
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - terraform/environments/dev/plan.cache
  rules:
    - if: $CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"

apply-dev:
  stage: apply
  environment:
    name: development
    url: https://dev.example.com
  script:
    - cd terraform/environments/dev
    - terraform apply -auto-approve plan.cache
  dependencies:
    - plan-dev
  when: manual
  rules:
