  allowed_workspace_ids = [
    tfe_workspace.prod_networking.id,
    tfe_workspace.prod_compute.id,
  ]
}

# ─── Deploy agents as ECS tasks (production) ───
resource "aws_ecs_task_definition" "tfe_agent" {
  family                   = "tfc-agent"
  requires_compatibilities = ["FARGATE"]
  network_mode             = "awsvpc"
  cpu                      = 1024
  memory                   = 2048

  container_definitions = jsonencode([{
    name  = "tfc-agent"
    image = "hashicorp/tfc-agent:latest"
    environment = [
      {
        name  = "TFC_AGENT_TOKEN"
        value = tfe_agent_token.tokens["production"].token
      },
      {
        name  = "TFC_ADDRESS"
        value = "https://tfe.internal.acme.com"
      },
      {
        name  = "TFC_AGENT_NAME"
        value = "ecs-agent"
      },
      {
        name  = "TFC_AGENT_SINGLE"
        value = "false"   # Run continuously
      }
    ]
  }])
}

resource "aws_ecs_service" "tfe_agents" {
  name            = "tfc-agents-prod"
  cluster         = aws_ecs_cluster.main.id
  task_definition = aws_ecs_task_definition.tfe_agent.arn
  desired_count   = 3    # Scale as needed
  launch_type     = "FARGATE"

  network_configuration {
    subnets         = var.private_subnet_ids
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.tfe_agent.id]
  }
}
44.5.2 Agent Scaling

Agents can be scaled horizontally to handle concurrent Terraform operations. Each agent handles one run at a time.

Team Size Concurrent Runs Recommended Agents Agent Spec
Small (1-10 devs)2-53 agents1 vCPU / 2 GB
Medium (10-50 devs)5-158 agents2 vCPU / 4 GB
Large (50-200 devs)15-5020+ agents2 vCPU / 4 GB
Enterprise (200+)50+Autoscale4 vCPU / 8 GB
 
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              AGENT SCALING PATTERNS                            │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                               │
│  FIXED POOL              AUTOSCALING (K8s HPA)                │
│  ──────────              ──────────────────────               │
│                                                               │
│  ┌───────┐               ┌───────┐  min: 2                   │
│  │Agent 1│ ← Always on   │Agent 1│  max: 20                  │
│  │Agent 2│               │Agent 2│  metric: queue depth       │
│  │Agent 3│               │Agent 3│ ← Scaled up               │
│  └───────┘               │Agent 4│   on demand                │
│                          │  ...  │                            │
│  Simple, predictable     └───────┘                            │
│  cost                                                         │
│                          Efficient resource                    │
│                          utilization                           │
│                                                               │
│  SINGLE-USE              MIXED POOLS                          │
│  ──────────              ───────────                          │
│                                                               │
│  TFC_AGENT_SINGLE=true   Pool A: Production (dedicated)       │
│  Agent runs ONE job,     Pool B: Dev/Staging (shared)         │
│  then exits.             Pool C: Air-gapped (specialized)     │
│  → Clean environment                                          │
│     every time           Each pool independently scaled        │
│                                                               │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
44.5.3 Network Requirements

Agents initiate outbound connections to TFE — they do not require inbound connectivity. This simplifies firewall rules and security configurations.

Connection Direction Port Purpose
Agent → TFEOutbound443Job polling, status updates, log streaming
Agent → ProvidersOutboundVariousCloud API access (AWS, Azure, GCP, etc.)
Agent → InternalOutboundVariousPrivate infrastructure (vSphere, databases, etc.)
Agent → RegistryOutbound443Module/provider downloads (or local mirror)
Inbound to Agent❌ None-Agents are pull-based; no inbound ports needed
💡 Key Architecture Point: Agents use a long-polling mechanism to check for new jobs. The agent connects outbound to TFE on port 443 and holds the connection open. When a workspace with execution_mode = "agent" queues a run, TFE responds on the existing connection. This means agents work behind NAT, firewalls, and proxies without any inbound port rules.
🛠️ 📦 🔄 🏗️
CHAPTER 45

Terragrunt

DRY Code Multi-Module Backend Config Dependencies Hooks run-all
9Sections
22Subsections
30+Examples
🛠️Ecosystem
PART 13 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

Terragrunt is a thin wrapper around Terraform that provides extra tools for keeping your Terraform configurations DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), managing remote state, and working with multiple Terraform modules. It solves common pain points like duplicated backend configurations, provider blocks, and multi-environment management.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand what Terragrunt is and when to use it over plain Terraform
  • Install and configure Terragrunt for multi-environment infrastructure
  • Eliminate backend and provider duplication using DRY patterns
  • Structure terragrunt.hcl files with root and child configurations
  • Manage cross-module dependencies with dependency and dependencies blocks
  • Execute commands across multiple modules with run-all
  • Use before_hook, after_hook, and error_hook for lifecycle automation

Chapter Contents

45.1 What is Terragrunt? 45.2 Installation 45.3 DRY Backend Configuration 45.4 DRY Provider Configuration 45.5 DRY Terraform Code 45.6 terragrunt.hcl Structure 45.7 Dependencies Between Modules 45.8 Executing Commands 45.9 Before and After Hooks

45.1 What is Terragrunt?

CONCEPT
📚 Official Documentation: Terragrunt Documentation

Terragrunt is a thin wrapper around Terraform developed by Gruntwork. It provides extra tools for keeping your configurations DRY, managing remote state, and working with multiple Terraform modules as a cohesive unit.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TERRAGRUNT ARCHITECTURE                             │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│   Developer runs:  terragrunt plan / apply / destroy                  │
│          │                                                            │
│          ▼                                                            │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│   │                   TERRAGRUNT LAYER                            │    │
│   │                                                              │    │
│   │  1. Read terragrunt.hcl configuration                        │    │
│   │  2. Generate backend.tf (DRY backend config)                 │    │
│   │  3. Generate provider.tf (DRY provider config)               │    │
│   │  4. Resolve module source (terraform { source = "..." })     │    │
│   │  5. Resolve dependencies (dependency blocks)                 │    │
│   │  6. Execute before_hooks                                     │    │
│   │  7. Pass inputs as TF_VAR_* environment variables            │    │
│   │  8. Delegate to terraform binary                             │    │
│   │  9. Execute after_hooks                                      │    │
│   └──────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                          │                                            │
│                          ▼                                            │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│   │                   TERRAFORM ENGINE                            │    │
│   │                                                              │    │
│   │  terraform init → plan → apply                               │    │
│   │  (uses generated backend.tf, provider.tf, and TF_VAR_*)     │    │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
45.1.1 Terragrunt vs Terraform

Terragrunt does not replace Terraform — it wraps it. Every Terragrunt command delegates to the underlying Terraform binary after performing its own preprocessing steps.

Aspect Plain Terraform Terraform + Terragrunt
Backend ConfigDuplicated in every moduleDefined once in root terragrunt.hcl
Provider ConfigDuplicated in every moduleGenerated via generate block
Module SourceCalled from root configsReferenced via terraform { source }
Cross-Module DepsManual output passing or terraform_remote_statedependency block with auto-wiring
Multi-Module ApplyRun each module individually or use scriptsterragrunt run-all apply
HooksExternal scripts or CI/CDBuilt-in before_hook / after_hook
Config LanguageHCL (.tf files)HCL (terragrunt.hcl + .tf files)
State ManagementManual backend setupAuto-configured per module
45.1.2 When to Use Terragrunt

Terragrunt shines in multi-environment, multi-account infrastructure with many Terraform modules that share common patterns.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WHEN TO USE TERRAGRUNT                                 │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  ✅ USE TERRAGRUNT WHEN:           │  ❌ SKIP TERRAGRUNT WHEN:   │
│  ─────────────────────             │  ──────────────────────      │
│  • 3+ environments (dev/stg/prod)  │  • Single environment       │
│  • Multiple AWS accounts           │  • Small project (1-3       │
│  • 5+ Terraform modules            │    modules)                 │
│  • Repeated backend config         │  • Team unfamiliar with     │
│  • Cross-module dependencies       │    HCL                      │
│  • Need run-all orchestration      │  • Using HCP Terraform      │
│  • Want lifecycle hooks            │    (handles state/runs)      │
│  • Monorepo with shared modules    │  • Simple single-module     │
│                                    │    deployments               │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Tip: If you're already using HCP Terraform or Terraform Enterprise for remote state and run orchestration, Terragrunt may add unnecessary complexity. Terragrunt is most valuable for teams managing infrastructure with the Terraform CLI directly.

45.2 Installation

SETUP
📚 Official Documentation: Install Terragrunt

Terragrunt is distributed as a single binary. Install it alongside Terraform — Terragrunt requires Terraform to be available in your PATH.

Installation Methods
Multiple ways to install Terragrunt on different platforms 
# ─── macOS (Homebrew) ──────────────────────────
brew install terragrunt

# ─── Windows (Chocolatey) ──────────────────────
choco install terragrunt

# ─── Windows (Scoop) ──────────────────────────
scoop install terragrunt

# ─── Linux (manual download) ───────────────────
TERRAGRUNT_VERSION="0.55.0"
curl -sL "https://github.com/gruntwork-io/terragrunt/releases/download/v${TERRAGRUNT_VERSION}/terragrunt_linux_amd64" \
  -o /usr/local/bin/terragrunt
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/terragrunt

# ─── Verify installation ──────────────────────
terragrunt --version
# terragrunt version v0.55.0

# ─── Prerequisites ─────────────────────────────
terraform --version   # Must be installed
# Terraform v1.7.0
Platform Method Command
macOSHomebrewbrew install terragrunt
WindowsChocolateychoco install terragrunt
WindowsScoopscoop install terragrunt
LinuxDirect binaryDownload from GitHub releases
DockerAlpine imagealpine/terragrunt:latest
CI/CDGitHub Actiongruntwork-io/terragrunt-action

45.3 DRY Backend Configuration

PATTERN
📚 Official Documentation: Keep Remote State DRY

One of Terragrunt's most impactful features is eliminating duplicated backend configuration. Instead of copying backend blocks into every module, you define it once and Terragrunt generates the appropriate backend.tf file for each module automatically.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DRY BACKEND CONFIGURATION FLOW                            │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  WITHOUT TERRAGRUNT:                WITH TERRAGRUNT:                  │
│  ───────────────────                ─────────────────                  │
│                                                                       │
│  modules/vpc/backend.tf             root terragrunt.hcl               │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐            ┌───────────────────────────┐     │
│  │ backend "s3" {      │            │ remote_state {            │     │
│  │   bucket="tfstate"  │ ← COPY     │   backend = "s3"          │     │
│  │   key="vpc/tf"      │            │   config = {              │     │
│  │   region="us-east-1"│            │     bucket = "tfstate"    │     │
│  │ }                   │            │     key = "${path}/tf"    │     │
│  └─────────────────────┘            │     region = "us-east-1"  │     │
│  modules/ec2/backend.tf             │   }                       │     │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐            │ }                         │     │
│  │ backend "s3" {      │            └───────────────────────────┘     │
│  │   bucket="tfstate"  │ ← COPY              │                       │
│  │   key="ec2/tf"      │              auto-generates                  │
│  │   region="us-east-1"│              per-module key                   │
│  │ }                   │                     │                        │
│  └─────────────────────┘            ┌────────▼────────┐               │
│  modules/rds/backend.tf             │ backend.tf      │               │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐            │ (generated for  │               │
│  │ backend "s3" {      │ ← COPY     │  each module)   │               │
│  │   bucket="tfstate"  │            └─────────────────┘               │
│  │   key="rds/tf"      │                                             │
│  └─────────────────────┘  10 modules = 10 copied files ❌           │
│                            1 root config = automatic ✅              │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
45.3.1 generate Block

The generate block instructs Terragrunt to create a file in the working directory before running Terraform. This is the mechanism that makes backend config DRY.

Generate Backend File
Root terragrunt.hcl that generates backend.tf for every module 
# root terragrunt.hcl — defines backend for ALL child modules
generate "backend" {
  path      = "backend.tf"
  if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  contents  = <<EOF
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "acme-terraform-state"
    key            = "${path_relative_to_include()}/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}
EOF
}

# Each child module inherits this via include block
# The key auto-resolves to the child's relative path:
#   dev/vpc/terraform.tfstate
#   dev/ec2/terraform.tfstate
#   prod/vpc/terraform.tfstate
Parameter Description Example
pathFile name to generate"backend.tf"
if_existsBehavior if file already exists"overwrite_terragrunt"
contentsFile content (HCL template)Backend block with interpolation
45.3.2 Remote State Configuration

The remote_state block is the alternative approach — it both generates the backend configuration and auto-creates the backend resources (S3 bucket, DynamoDB table) if they don't exist.

Remote State with Auto-Creation
Terragrunt creates backend resources automatically 
# root terragrunt.hcl — auto-creates S3 bucket & DynamoDB table
remote_state {
  backend = "s3"
  
  generate = {
    path      = "backend.tf"
    if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  }
  
  config = {
    bucket         = "acme-${get_aws_account_id()}-terraform-state"
    key            = "${path_relative_to_include()}/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    
    s3_bucket_tags = {
      Owner       = "terraform"
      Environment = "shared"
    }
  }
}

# Terragrunt will:
# 1. Create S3 bucket if it doesn't exist (with versioning & encryption)
# 2. Create DynamoDB table if it doesn't exist (with LockID key)
# 3. Generate backend.tf with the config above
# 4. Run terraform init with the generated backend
💡 Key Difference: generate "backend" only creates the backend.tf file. remote_state does the same plus auto-creates the backend infrastructure (S3 bucket, DynamoDB table, GCS bucket, etc.) — eliminating the chicken-and-egg problem of needing infrastructure to store state before you have state.

45.4 DRY Provider Configuration

PATTERN
📚 Official Documentation: Keep Code DRY

Just like backend configs, provider blocks are often duplicated across every module. Terragrunt's generate block can create provider.tf files to centralize provider configuration, version constraints, and authentication.

45.4.1 Provider Generation

Generate a provider.tf file that all child modules inherit, ensuring consistent provider versions and configuration.

Generate Provider Configuration
Centralized AWS provider config for all modules 
# root terragrunt.hcl
generate "provider" {
  path      = "provider.tf"
  if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  contents  = <<EOF
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.30"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = "${local.aws_region}"
  
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Terraform   = "true"
      Environment = "${local.environment}"
      Project     = "${local.project}"
    }
  }
}
EOF
}

# ─── Read from environment-specific vars ───────
locals {
  env_vars    = read_terragrunt_config(find_in_parent_folders("env.hcl"))
  aws_region  = local.env_vars.locals.aws_region
  environment = local.env_vars.locals.environment
  project     = local.env_vars.locals.project
}
45.4.2 Multi-Account Patterns

For organizations with multiple AWS accounts, Terragrunt can generate provider configs that assume roles into different accounts based on directory structure.

Multi-Account Provider with AssumeRole
Different AWS accounts per environment 
# account.hcl — placed in each account directory
locals {
  account_name = "production"
  account_id   = "111111111111"
  role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/TerraformDeployRole"
}

# root terragrunt.hcl — reads account.hcl from parent folders
locals {
  account = read_terragrunt_config(find_in_parent_folders("account.hcl"))
}

generate "provider" {
  path      = "provider.tf"
  if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  contents  = <<EOF
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "${local.account.locals.role_arn}"
  }
}
EOF
}

# Directory structure:
# ├── terragrunt.hcl         (root — generate blocks)
# ├── production/
# │   ├── account.hcl        (account_id, role_arn)
# │   ├── vpc/terragrunt.hcl
# │   └── ec2/terragrunt.hcl
# └── staging/
#     ├── account.hcl
#     ├── vpc/terragrunt.hcl
#     └── ec2/terragrunt.hcl
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MULTI-ACCOUNT DIRECTORY STRUCTURE                      │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  infrastructure-live/                                             │
│  ├── terragrunt.hcl         ← Root: generate blocks, remote_state│
│  ├── _envcommon/                                                  │
│  │   ├── vpc.hcl            ← Shared module configs               │
│  │   ├── ec2.hcl                                                  │
│  │   └── rds.hcl                                                  │
│  ├── production/                                                  │
│  │   ├── account.hcl        ← account_id: 111111111111           │
│  │   ├── us-east-1/                                               │
│  │   │   ├── env.hcl        ← region: us-east-1, env: prod       │
│  │   │   ├── vpc/                                                 │
│  │   │   │   └── terragrunt.hcl  → state key: prod/us-east-1/vpc │
│  │   │   └── ec2/                                                 │
│  │   │       └── terragrunt.hcl  → state key: prod/us-east-1/ec2 │
│  │   └── eu-west-1/                                               │
│  │       └── ...                                                  │
│  └── staging/                                                     │
│      ├── account.hcl        ← account_id: 222222222222           │
│      └── us-east-1/                                               │
│          └── ...                                                  │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

45.5 DRY Terraform Code

PATTERN
📚 Official Documentation: Deploy Terraform Modules

Terragrunt separates module definitions (reusable code in a modules repo) from module invocations (environment-specific terragrunt.hcl files). This eliminates the need to copy module code across environments.

45.5.1 Module Reference Patterns

The terraform block in terragrunt.hcl specifies the source module to deploy. Terragrunt downloads the module, configures it with inputs, and runs Terraform.

Module Source References
Different ways to reference Terraform modules 
# ─── Git repository (versioned) ────────────────
terraform {
  source = "git::git@github.com:acme/modules.git//vpc?ref=v1.2.0"
}

# ─── Terraform Registry ───────────────────────
terraform {
  source = "tfr:///terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws?version=5.4.0"
}

# ─── Local path (for development) ─────────────
terraform {
  source = "${path_relative_from_include()}/../modules//vpc"
}

# ─── GitHub with double-slash (subdirectory) ───
terraform {
  source = "github.com/acme/infrastructure-modules.git//networking/vpc?ref=main"
}

# ─── S3 bucket (versioned artifact) ───────────
terraform {
  source = "s3::https://s3.amazonaws.com/acme-modules/vpc/v1.2.0.zip"
}
45.5.2 Input Variables

The inputs block in terragrunt.hcl passes values to the Terraform module. Terragrunt converts these to TF_VAR_ environment variables automatically.

Passing Inputs to Modules
Child terragrunt.hcl with module source and inputs 
# production/us-east-1/vpc/terragrunt.hcl
include "root" {
  path = find_in_parent_folders()
}

terraform {
  source = "git::git@github.com:acme/modules.git//vpc?ref=v1.2.0"
}

# Read environment-specific variables
locals {
  env_vars = read_terragrunt_config(find_in_parent_folders("env.hcl"))
}

inputs = {
  # These become TF_VAR_vpc_name, TF_VAR_cidr_block, etc.
  vpc_name    = "prod-vpc-${local.env_vars.locals.aws_region}"
  cidr_block  = "10.0.0.0/16"
  environment = local.env_vars.locals.environment
  
  private_subnets = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24", "10.0.3.0/24"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.101.0/24", "10.0.102.0/24", "10.0.103.0/24"]
  
  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = false
  
  tags = {
    Terraform   = "true"
    Environment = "production"
  }
}
💡 How Inputs Work: When you define inputs = { vpc_name = "prod-vpc" }, Terragrunt sets TF_VAR_vpc_name=prod-vpc as an environment variable before running terraform plan/apply. Your Terraform module must declare variable "vpc_name" {} to receive it.

45.6 terragrunt.hcl Structure

CONFIGURATION
📚 Official Documentation: Configuration

Terragrunt uses a hierarchical configuration model: a root terragrunt.hcl defines shared settings (backend, providers), and child terragrunt.hcl files in each module directory inherit from the root via the include block.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TERRAGRUNT CONFIGURATION HIERARCHY                        │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  ROOT terragrunt.hcl                                           │  │
│  │  ─────────────────────                                         │  │
│  │  • remote_state {} — backend config for all modules            │  │
│  │  • generate "provider" {} — shared provider config             │  │
│  │  • locals {} — common variables                                │  │
│  │  • inputs {} — default inputs for all children                 │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ include                                 │
│              ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐                          │
│              ▼              ▼              ▼                           │
│  ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐ ┌────────────────┐            │
│  │ CHILD: vpc/    │ │ CHILD: ec2/    │ │ CHILD: rds/    │            │
│  │ terragrunt.hcl │ │ terragrunt.hcl │ │ terragrunt.hcl │            │
│  │                │ │                │ │                │            │
│  │ include "root" │ │ include "root" │ │ include "root" │            │
│  │ terraform {    │ │ terraform {    │ │ terraform {    │            │
│  │   source=...   │ │   source=...   │ │   source=...   │            │
│  │ }              │ │ }              │ │ }              │            │
│  │ inputs = {     │ │ inputs = {     │ │ inputs = {     │            │
│  │   ...override  │ │   ...override  │ │   ...override  │            │
│  │ }              │ │ }              │ │ }              │            │
│  └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘ └────────────────┘            │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
45.6.1 Root Configuration

The root terragrunt.hcl sits at the top of your infrastructure-live repository. It defines shared backend, provider, and common inputs.

Complete Root Configuration
Root-level terragrunt.hcl with all common settings 
# infrastructure-live/terragrunt.hcl (ROOT)

# ─── Shared Remote State ───────────────────────
remote_state {
  backend = "s3"
  generate = {
    path      = "backend.tf"
    if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  }
  config = {
    bucket         = "acme-${local.account_id}-terraform-state"
    key            = "${path_relative_to_include()}/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = local.aws_region
    encrypt        = true
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}

# ─── Shared Provider Config ────────────────────
generate "provider" {
  path      = "provider.tf"
  if_exists = "overwrite_terragrunt"
  contents  = <<EOF
provider "aws" {
  region = "${local.aws_region}"
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      ManagedBy   = "terragrunt"
      Environment = "${local.environment}"
    }
  }
}
EOF
}

# ─── Common Locals ─────────────────────────────
locals {
  account_vars = read_terragrunt_config(find_in_parent_folders("account.hcl"))
  env_vars     = read_terragrunt_config(find_in_parent_folders("env.hcl"))

  account_id   = local.account_vars.locals.account_id
  aws_region   = local.env_vars.locals.aws_region
  environment  = local.env_vars.locals.environment
}
45.6.2 Child Configuration

Each module directory contains a child terragrunt.hcl that includes the root and specifies module-specific source and inputs.

Child Module Configuration
production/us-east-1/vpc/terragrunt.hcl 
# production/us-east-1/vpc/terragrunt.hcl (CHILD)

include "root" {
  path = find_in_parent_folders()
}

# Optionally include shared module config
include "env" {
  path   = "${dirname(find_in_parent_folders())}/_envcommon/vpc.hcl"
  expose = true
}

terraform {
  source = "git::git@github.com:acme/modules.git//vpc?ref=v2.1.0"
}

inputs = {
  vpc_name   = "prod-vpc"
  cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16"
  azs        = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
}
45.6.3 include Block

The include block tells Terragrunt to merge settings from a parent or shared configuration file. You can have multiple named includes.

Parameter Description Default
pathPath to the parent terragrunt.hclRequired
exposeMakes parent locals accessible via include.<name>.localsfalse
merge_strategyHow to merge: "no_merge", "shallow", "deep""deep"
 
# Multiple named includes
include "root" {
  path = find_in_parent_folders()   # Finds root terragrunt.hcl
}

include "vpc_common" {
  path   = "${dirname(find_in_parent_folders())}/_envcommon/vpc.hcl"
  expose = true
  merge_strategy = "deep"
}

# Access exposed locals from the included config
inputs = {
  common_tags = include.vpc_common.locals.default_tags
}
45.6.4 dependency Block

The dependency block allows one module to read outputs from another module's state. This is covered in depth in the next section.

# Quick preview — full details in Section 45.7
dependency "vpc" {
  config_path = "../vpc"
}

inputs = {
  vpc_id     = dependency.vpc.outputs.vpc_id
  subnet_ids = dependency.vpc.outputs.private_subnet_ids
}

45.7 Dependencies Between Modules

ORCHESTRATION
📚 Official Documentation: Dependencies

Terragrunt provides two dependency mechanisms: the dependency block (reads outputs from another module) and the dependencies block (declares ordering without reading outputs). Together, they enable Terragrunt to build a dependency graph and apply modules in the correct order.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DEPENDENCY GRAPH EXAMPLE                                   │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│   ┌─────────┐                                                        │
│   │   VPC   │ ─────────────────────────────┐                         │
│   └────┬────┘                              │                         │
│        │ outputs:                           │ outputs:                │
│        │ vpc_id, subnet_ids                 │ vpc_id                  │
│        ▼                                    ▼                         │
│   ┌─────────┐         ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────┐                    │
│   │ Security│         │   RDS   │    │   ALB    │                    │
│   │ Groups  │         │         │    │          │                    │
│   └────┬────┘         └────┬────┘    └─────┬────┘                    │
│        │ outputs:          │               │                         │
│        │ sg_ids            │               │                         │
│        ▼                   │               │                         │
│   ┌─────────┐              │               │                         │
│   │   EC2   │ ◀────────────┘               │                         │
│   │         │ ◀─────────────────────────────┘                        │
│   └─────────┘                                                        │
│                                                                       │
│   terragrunt run-all apply → VPC → (SG, RDS, ALB) → EC2             │
│   Parallel where possible, sequential where dependencies exist        │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
45.7.1 dependency Block

The dependency block reads the Terraform outputs from another module's state file. This wires modules together without using terraform_remote_state.

Reading Outputs from Dependencies
EC2 module reading VPC and security group outputs 
# production/us-east-1/ec2/terragrunt.hcl
include "root" {
  path = find_in_parent_folders()
}

terraform {
  source = "git::git@github.com:acme/modules.git//ec2?ref=v1.5.0"
}

# ─── Declare dependencies ─────────────────────
dependency "vpc" {
  config_path = "../vpc"
}

dependency "sg" {
  config_path = "../security-groups"
}

dependency "alb" {
  config_path = "../alb"
}

# ─── Wire outputs to inputs ───────────────────
inputs = {
  vpc_id            = dependency.vpc.outputs.vpc_id
  subnet_ids        = dependency.vpc.outputs.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids = [dependency.sg.outputs.app_sg_id]
  target_group_arn  = dependency.alb.outputs.target_group_arn
  
  instance_type     = "t3.medium"
  min_size          = 2
  max_size          = 10
}
45.7.2 dependencies Block

The dependencies block declares ordering constraints without reading outputs. Use this when a module must apply after another but doesn't need its outputs.

# Ensure DNS zone exists before creating records
dependencies {
  paths = ["../dns-zone", "../certificate"]
}

# This is different from dependency:
# - dependency: reads outputs + creates ordering
# - dependencies: creates ordering only (no output access)
Block Reads Outputs? Creates Order? Use Case
dependency✅ Yes✅ YesPass VPC ID → EC2 module
dependencies❌ No✅ YesEnsure DNS zone exists first
45.7.3 Mock Outputs

When a dependency hasn't been applied yet (no state), Terragrunt can't read its outputs. Mock outputs provide placeholder values so you can run terragrunt plan without requiring all dependencies to be applied first.

Mock Outputs for Plan-time Validation
Allow plan to work before dependencies are applied 
dependency "vpc" {
  config_path = "../vpc"
  
  mock_outputs = {
    vpc_id             = "vpc-mock-00000"
    private_subnet_ids = ["subnet-mock-1", "subnet-mock-2"]
  }
  
  # Only use mocks during validate/plan, fail on apply
  mock_outputs_allowed_terraform_commands = ["validate", "plan"]
  
  # Merge strategy for mocks
  mock_outputs_merge_strategy_with_state = "shallow"
}
💡 Tip: Always set mock_outputs_allowed_terraform_commands to ["validate", "plan"]. This ensures mocks are never used during apply, preventing accidental deployment with placeholder values.

45.8 Executing Commands

OPERATIONS
📚 Official Documentation: Execute Commands

Terragrunt's run-all command lets you run Terraform commands across multiple modules simultaneously, respecting the dependency graph. This is one of its most powerful orchestration features.

45.8.1 terragrunt run-all

The run-all command discovers all terragrunt.hcl files in subdirectories, builds a dependency graph, and executes the specified Terraform command on each module in the correct order.

run-all Commands
Execute Terraform operations across all modules 
# Apply all modules in dependency order
cd infrastructure-live/production/us-east-1
terragrunt run-all apply

# Plan all modules (good for CI/CD validation)
terragrunt run-all plan

# Destroy all modules in REVERSE dependency order
terragrunt run-all destroy

# Init all modules
terragrunt run-all init

# Output from all modules
terragrunt run-all output

# Validate all modules
terragrunt run-all validate

# Apply with auto-approve (CI/CD pipelines)
terragrunt run-all apply --terragrunt-non-interactive
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              run-all EXECUTION ORDER                                │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  terragrunt run-all apply                                         │
│  ═══════════════════════                                          │
│                                                                   │
│  Phase 1 (parallel):  VPC ──────── DNS-Zone                      │
│                        │              │                           │
│  Phase 2 (parallel):  Subnets    Certificate                     │
│                        │              │                           │
│  Phase 3 (parallel):  SG    RDS    ALB                            │
│                        │              │                           │
│  Phase 4:             EC2 ◀───────────┘                           │
│                                                                   │
│  terragrunt run-all destroy  → REVERSE order:                     │
│  EC2 → (SG, RDS, ALB) → (Subnets, Cert) → (VPC, DNS-Zone)       │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
45.8.2 Parallel Execution

By default, Terragrunt runs independent modules in parallel. Configure parallelism and targeting for fine-grained control.

# Limit parallelism (default: unlimited parallel)
terragrunt run-all apply --terragrunt-parallelism 3

# Exclude specific modules
terragrunt run-all apply \
  --terragrunt-exclude-dir production/us-east-1/monitoring

# Include only specific modules
terragrunt run-all apply \
  --terragrunt-include-dir production/us-east-1/vpc \
  --terragrunt-include-dir production/us-east-1/ec2

# Ignore dependency errors (continue despite failures)
terragrunt run-all apply --terragrunt-ignore-dependency-errors

# Show the dependency graph without running anything
terragrunt graph-dependencies | dot -Tpng -o deps.png
45.8.3 Dependency Graph

Terragrunt can generate a visual dependency graph in DOT format, compatible with Graphviz.

# Generate dependency graph
cd infrastructure-live/production
terragrunt graph-dependencies

# Output (DOT format):
# digraph {
#   "vpc" ;
#   "security-groups" ;
#   "rds" ;
#   "ec2" ;
#   "vpc" -> "security-groups" ;
#   "vpc" -> "rds" ;
#   "security-groups" -> "ec2" ;
#   "rds" -> "ec2" ;
# }

# Render to PNG (requires Graphviz)
terragrunt graph-dependencies | dot -Tpng -o dependency-graph.png

# Render to SVG for web
terragrunt graph-dependencies | dot -Tsvg -o dependency-graph.svg

45.9 Before and After Hooks

AUTOMATION
📚 Official Documentation: Before and After Hooks

Hooks allow you to execute custom commands before or after Terraform operations. Use them for validation, notifications, cleanup, or integration with external tools.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              HOOK EXECUTION FLOW                                    │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│   terragrunt apply                                                │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│   ┌─────────────────┐                                             │
│   │  before_hook     │  → Run validation, lint, notify start      │
│   │  (pre-apply)     │                                             │
│   └────────┬────────┘                                             │
│            │ success                                               │
│            ▼                                                       │
│   ┌─────────────────┐                                             │
│   │  terraform apply │  → Normal Terraform execution              │
│   └────────┬────────┘                                             │
│            │                                                       │
│       ┌────┴────┐                                                 │
│       │         │                                                 │
│    success    error                                               │
│       │         │                                                 │
│       ▼         ▼                                                 │
│   ┌────────┐ ┌──────────┐                                         │
│   │ after  │ │ error    │                                         │
│   │ _hook  │ │ _hook    │  → Alert, rollback, cleanup             │
│   └────────┘ └──────────┘                                         │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
45.9.1 before_hook

Runs before Terraform commands. Common uses: validation, linting, security scanning, notification.

Before Hooks
Run checks before Terraform operations 
terraform {
  before_hook "validate_inputs" {
    commands = ["apply", "plan"]
    execute  = ["bash", "-c", "echo 'Validating inputs for ${path_relative_to_include()}...'"]
  }
  
  before_hook "tflint" {
    commands     = ["apply", "plan"]
    execute      = ["tflint", "--init"]
    working_dir  = get_terragrunt_dir()
    run_on_error = false
  }
  
  before_hook "checkov_scan" {
    commands = ["apply"]
    execute  = ["checkov", "-d", ".", "--framework", "terraform"]
  }
  
  before_hook "notify_start" {
    commands = ["apply"]
    execute  = [
      "bash", "-c",
      "curl -s -X POST $SLACK_WEBHOOK -d '{\"text\": \"🚀 Deploying ${path_relative_to_include()}...\"}'"
    ]
  }
}
45.9.2 after_hook

Runs after successful Terraform commands. Common uses: notifications, documentation updates, test triggers.

terraform {
  after_hook "notify_success" {
    commands = ["apply"]
    execute  = [
      "bash", "-c",
      "curl -s -X POST $SLACK_WEBHOOK -d '{\"text\": \"✅ ${path_relative_to_include()} deployed successfully\"}'"
    ]
  }
  
  after_hook "update_docs" {
    commands = ["apply"]
    execute  = ["terraform-docs", "markdown", ".", "--output-file", "README.md"]
  }
  
  after_hook "smoke_test" {
    commands = ["apply"]
    execute  = ["bash", "scripts/smoke-test.sh"]
  }
}
45.9.3 error_hook

Runs when a Terraform command fails. Use for alerting, cleanup, or automated rollback actions.

terraform {
  error_hook "notify_failure" {
    commands  = ["apply", "plan"]
    execute   = [
      "bash", "-c",
      "curl -s -X POST $SLACK_WEBHOOK -d '{\"text\": \"❌ FAILED: ${path_relative_to_include()}\"}'"
    ]
    on_errors = [".*"]   # Match all errors (regex)
  }
  
  error_hook "cleanup_temp" {
    commands  = ["apply"]
    execute   = ["bash", "-c", "rm -f /tmp/tg-*.tmp"]
    on_errors = [".*"]
  }
}
Hook Type When It Runs Common Uses
before_hookBefore the Terraform commandLint, validate, scan, notify start
after_hookAfter successful commandNotify success, docs, smoke tests
error_hookAfter failed commandAlert, cleanup, log failure
💡 Hook Parameters: All hooks support: commands (which TF commands trigger it), execute (command to run), working_dir (directory to run in), run_on_error (for before/after hooks — run even if previous hook failed), and on_errors (for error_hook — regex patterns to match).
🐍 📝 ⚡ 🔧
CHAPTER 46

Terraform CDK (CDKTF)

Python TypeScript Go Constructs cdktf synth HCL Convert
6Sections
17Subsections
25+Examples
🐍Python Focus
PART 13 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

The Cloud Development Kit for Terraform (CDKTF) allows you to define infrastructure using familiar programming languages — Python, TypeScript, Go, C#, and Java — instead of HCL. CDKTF synthesizes your code into standard Terraform JSON configuration, enabling the full Terraform ecosystem while writing in your preferred language.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the CDKTF architecture: constructs, stacks, and synthesis
  • Compare supported languages and their trade-offs
  • Build infrastructure with Python CDKTF for Django/FastAPI developers
  • Master the CDKTF CLI: init, synth, deploy, destroy
  • Convert existing HCL configurations to CDKTF
  • Apply CDKTF best practices for production workflows

Chapter Contents

46.1 What is CDKTF? 46.2 Supported Languages 46.3 Python with CDKTF 46.4 CDKTF CLI 46.5 Converting HCL to CDKTF 46.6 CDKTF Best Practices

46.1 What is CDKTF?

CONCEPT
📚 Official Documentation: CDKTF Documentation

CDKTF (Cloud Development Kit for Terraform) lets you write infrastructure as code using programming languages. It uses the Constructs framework (same as AWS CDK) and synthesizes your code into standard Terraform JSON — meaning the full Terraform provider ecosystem is available.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CDKTF ARCHITECTURE                                  │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  YOUR CODE (Python / TypeScript / Go / C# / Java)                     │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  from cdktf import App, TerraformStack                          │  │
│  │  from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws import ...                       │  │
│  │                                                                 │  │
│  │  class MyStack(TerraformStack):                                 │  │
│  │      def __init__(self, scope, id):                             │  │
│  │          VPC(self, "vpc", cidr_block="10.0.0.0/16")            │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ cdktf synth                             │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  TERRAFORM JSON   (cdktf.out/stacks/my-stack/cdk.tf.json)      │  │
│  │  {                                                              │  │
│  │    "resource": {                                                │  │
│  │      "aws_vpc": {                                               │  │
│  │        "vpc": { "cidr_block": "10.0.0.0/16" }                  │  │
│  │      }                                                          │  │
│  │    }                                                            │  │
│  │  }                                                              │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ terraform apply                         │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  TERRAFORM ENGINE  (providers, state, plan, apply)              │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
46.1.1 Constructs Framework

CDKTF is built on the Constructs framework, the same foundation used by AWS CDK. It provides three levels of abstraction for defining infrastructure.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CONSTRUCTS ABSTRACTION LEVELS                          │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Level 3: Patterns (custom high-level)                            │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  WebAppStack  → VPC + ALB + ECS + RDS + CloudWatch          │  │
│  │  (Your reusable patterns combining multiple L1/L2 resources │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                   │
│  Level 2: Higher-level constructs (convenience)                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  cdktf modules (e.g., VPC module with sensible defaults)    │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                   │
│  Level 1: Direct provider resources (1:1 mapping)                 │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Vpc(), Subnet(), SecurityGroup(), Instance()               │  │
│  │  ← Equivalent to aws_vpc, aws_subnet, etc. in HCL          │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
46.1.2 Benefits and Trade-offs

CDKTF brings software engineering practices to infrastructure but introduces complexity. Understand the trade-offs before adopting.

Benefits Trade-offs
✅ Use familiar languages (Python, TS, Go)⚠️ Additional abstraction layer
✅ Full IDE support (autocomplete, types)⚠️ Debugging is harder (synth → JSON → TF)
✅ Real loops, conditionals, unit tests⚠️ Team must know both language + Terraform
✅ Share code as packages (pip, npm)⚠️ Provider bindings lag behind HCL releases
✅ Full Terraform provider ecosystem⚠️ Generated JSON harder to review than HCL
✅ Build custom abstractions (L3 constructs)⚠️ Smaller community than plain HCL

46.2 Supported Languages

REFERENCE
📚 Official Documentation: Language Support

CDKTF supports five programming languages via JSII (JavaScript Interoperability Interface). Each language has full access to all Terraform providers and modules.

46.2.1 TypeScript/JavaScript

The primary and most mature CDKTF language. Best documentation, fastest provider binding updates, and largest community.

// TypeScript CDKTF example
import { App, TerraformStack } from "cdktf";
import { AwsProvider } from "@cdktf/provider-aws/lib/provider";
import { Vpc } from "@cdktf/provider-aws/lib/vpc";
import { Subnet } from "@cdktf/provider-aws/lib/subnet";

class NetworkStack extends TerraformStack {
  constructor(scope: Construct, id: string) {
    super(scope, id);

    new AwsProvider(this, "aws", { region: "us-east-1" });

    const vpc = new Vpc(this, "main", {
      cidrBlock: "10.0.0.0/16",
      enableDnsHostnames: true,
      tags: { Name: "cdktf-vpc" },
    });

    new Subnet(this, "public", {
      vpcId: vpc.id,
      cidrBlock: "10.0.1.0/24",
      availabilityZone: "us-east-1a",
    });
  }
}

const app = new App();
new NetworkStack(app, "network");
app.synth();
46.2.2 Python

Ideal for Django/FastAPI developers. Uses pip for package management and Python classes for infrastructure definitions.

# Python CDKTF example
from cdktf import App, TerraformStack
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.provider import AwsProvider
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.vpc import Vpc
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.subnet import Subnet

class NetworkStack(TerraformStack):
    def __init__(self, scope, id):
        super().__init__(scope, id)

        AwsProvider(self, "aws", region="us-east-1")

        vpc = Vpc(self, "main",
            cidr_block="10.0.0.0/16",
            enable_dns_hostnames=True,
            tags={"Name": "cdktf-vpc"}
        )

        Subnet(self, "public",
            vpc_id=vpc.id,
            cidr_block="10.0.1.0/24",
            availability_zone="us-east-1a"
        )

app = App()
NetworkStack(app, "network")
app.synth()
46.2.3 Go

Strong typing and compiled binaries. Well-suited for teams already using Go for backends or Kubernetes operators.

// Go CDKTF example
package main

import (
    "github.com/aws/constructs-go/constructs/v10"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-cdk-go/cdktf"
    aws "github.com/cdktf/cdktf-provider-aws-go/aws/v19/provider"
    "github.com/cdktf/cdktf-provider-aws-go/aws/v19/vpc"
)

func NewNetworkStack(scope constructs.Construct, id string) cdktf.TerraformStack {
    stack := cdktf.NewTerraformStack(scope, &id)
    
    aws.NewAwsProvider(stack, jsii.String("aws"), &aws.AwsProviderConfig{
        Region: jsii.String("us-east-1"),
    })
    
    vpc.NewVpc(stack, jsii.String("main"), &vpc.VpcConfig{
        CidrBlock:          jsii.String("10.0.0.0/16"),
        EnableDnsHostnames: jsii.Bool(true),
    })
    
    return stack
}

func main() {
    app := cdktf.NewApp(nil)
    NewNetworkStack(app, "network")
    app.Synth()
}
46.2.4 C#

Uses .NET and NuGet packages. Great for teams in Microsoft-centric environments managing Azure infrastructure.

// C# CDKTF example
using Constructs;
using HashiCorp.Cdktf;
using aws.Provider;
using aws.Vpc;

class NetworkStack : TerraformStack
{
    public NetworkStack(Construct scope, string id) : base(scope, id)
    {
        new AwsProvider(this, "aws", new AwsProviderConfig {
            Region = "us-east-1"
        });

        new Vpc(this, "main", new VpcConfig {
            CidrBlock = "10.0.0.0/16",
            EnableDnsHostnames = true,
            Tags = new Dictionary<string, string> {
                { "Name", "cdktf-vpc" }
            }
        });
    }
}

var app = new App();
new NetworkStack(app, "network");
app.Synth();
46.2.5 Java

Uses Maven/Gradle for dependency management. Suitable for enterprise Java teams.

// Java CDKTF example
import software.constructs.Construct;
import com.hashicorp.cdktf.*;
import imports.aws.provider.AwsProvider;
import imports.aws.vpc.Vpc;
import imports.aws.vpc.VpcConfig;

public class NetworkStack extends TerraformStack {
    public NetworkStack(Construct scope, String id) {
        super(scope, id);

        AwsProvider.Builder.create(this, "aws")
            .region("us-east-1")
            .build();

        Vpc.Builder.create(this, "main")
            .cidrBlock("10.0.0.0/16")
            .enableDnsHostnames(true)
            .build();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        App app = new App();
        new NetworkStack(app, "network");
        app.synth();
    }
}
Language Package Manager Maturity Best For
TypeScriptnpm★★★★★Primary language, best docs
Pythonpip★★★★☆Django/FastAPI/ML teams
Gogo mod★★★★☆K8s/backend teams
C#NuGet★★★☆☆.NET/Azure teams
JavaMaven★★★☆☆Enterprise Java teams

46.3 Python with CDKTF (For Your Background)

HANDS-ON
📚 Official Documentation: CDKTF Architecture

Since this documentation is tailored for Python/Django/FastAPI developers, this section provides a deep dive into using CDKTF with Python — from installation to building production stacks.

46.3.1 Installation

CDKTF requires Node.js (for the JSII runtime) and your preferred language runtime.

# ─── Prerequisites ─────────────────────────────
# Node.js 18+ (required for CDKTF CLI)
node --version    # v18.0.0+

# Python 3.8+ and pip
python3 --version  # Python 3.11+
pip --version

# Terraform CLI
terraform --version  # v1.7.0+

# ─── Install CDKTF CLI ────────────────────────
npm install -g cdktf-cli

# Verify
cdktf --version
# 0.20.0
46.3.2 Project Setup

Initialize a new CDKTF Python project with the CLI. This creates a virtual environment, installs dependencies, and scaffolds the project structure.

Create a New Python CDKTF Project
Initialize and configure a project from scratch 
# Create project directory
mkdir cdktf-aws-infra && cd cdktf-aws-infra

# Initialize with Python template
cdktf init --template=python \
  --project-name=aws-infrastructure \
  --project-description="AWS infra with CDKTF Python" \
  --providers=aws@~>5.30

# Project structure created:
# cdktf-aws-infra/
# ├── cdktf.json           ← CDKTF configuration
# ├── main.py              ← Your infrastructure code
# ├── Pipfile               ← Python dependencies
# ├── help                  ← Usage instructions
# └── .gitignore

# Install AWS provider bindings
pip install cdktf-cdktf-provider-aws

# Or add additional providers
cdktf provider add hashicorp/random
cdktf provider add hashicorp/null
46.3.3 Writing Infrastructure

Build a complete production-ready stack with VPC, subnets, security groups, and an EC2 instance using Python.

Production VPC Stack in Python
Complete networking stack with Python CDKTF 
# main.py — Production VPC with CDKTF
from constructs import Construct
from cdktf import App, TerraformStack, TerraformOutput, Fn
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.provider import AwsProvider
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.vpc import Vpc
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.subnet import Subnet
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.internet_gateway import InternetGateway
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.route_table import RouteTable, RouteTableRoute
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.route_table_association import RouteTableAssociation
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.security_group import SecurityGroup, SecurityGroupIngress

class VpcStack(TerraformStack):
    def __init__(self, scope: Construct, id: str, 
                 environment: str = "dev",
                 cidr: str = "10.0.0.0/16"):
        super().__init__(scope, id)

        # Provider
        AwsProvider(self, "aws", region="us-east-1")
        
        # VPC
        self.vpc = Vpc(self, "vpc",
            cidr_block=cidr,
            enable_dns_hostnames=True,
            enable_dns_support=True,
            tags={"Name": f"{environment}-vpc", "Env": environment}
        )
        
        # Internet Gateway
        igw = InternetGateway(self, "igw",
            vpc_id=self.vpc.id,
            tags={"Name": f"{environment}-igw"}
        )
        
        # Public Subnets (using Python loop!)
        azs = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
        self.public_subnets = []
        
        for i, az in enumerate(azs):
            subnet = Subnet(self, f"public-{i}",
                vpc_id=self.vpc.id,
                cidr_block=f"10.0.{i + 1}.0/24",
                availability_zone=az,
                map_public_ip_on_launch=True,
                tags={"Name": f"{environment}-public-{az}"}
            )
            self.public_subnets.append(subnet)
        
        # Route table for public subnets
        public_rt = RouteTable(self, "public-rt",
            vpc_id=self.vpc.id,
            route=[RouteTableRoute(
                cidr_block="0.0.0.0/0",
                gateway_id=igw.id
            )],
            tags={"Name": f"{environment}-public-rt"}
        )
        
        for i, subnet in enumerate(self.public_subnets):
            RouteTableAssociation(self, f"public-rta-{i}",
                subnet_id=subnet.id,
                route_table_id=public_rt.id
            )
        
        # Outputs
        TerraformOutput(self, "vpc_id", value=self.vpc.id)
        TerraformOutput(self, "public_subnet_ids",
            value=Fn.jsonencode([s.id for s in self.public_subnets])
        )

# ─── Multi-Stack Application ──────────────────
app = App()
VpcStack(app, "dev-network", environment="dev", cidr="10.0.0.0/16")
VpcStack(app, "prod-network", environment="prod", cidr="10.1.0.0/16")
app.synth()
46.3.4 Provider Bindings

CDKTF generates language-specific bindings from Terraform provider schemas. Each resource, data source, and provider becomes a typed class.

HCL Resource Python Class Package
aws_vpcVpc()cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.vpc
aws_instanceInstance()cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.instance
aws_s3_bucketS3Bucket()cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.s3_bucket
aws_iam_roleIamRole()cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.iam_role
data.aws_amiDataAwsAmi()cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.data_aws_ami
46.3.5 Constructs and Stacks

Build reusable L3 constructs (patterns) by composing multiple resources into a single Python class — similar to creating a Terraform module.

# constructs/web_app.py — Reusable L3 construct
from constructs import Construct
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.instance import Instance
from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.security_group import (
    SecurityGroup, SecurityGroupIngress, SecurityGroupEgress
)

class WebServer(Construct):
    """Reusable web server construct with security group."""
    
    def __init__(self, scope: Construct, id: str, *,
                 vpc_id: str,
                 subnet_id: str,
                 instance_type: str = "t3.micro",
                 ami: str = "ami-0123456789abcdef0"):
        super().__init__(scope, id)
        
        self.sg = SecurityGroup(self, "sg",
            vpc_id=vpc_id,
            ingress=[
                SecurityGroupIngress(
                    from_port=80, to_port=80,
                    protocol="tcp", cidr_blocks=["0.0.0.0/0"]
                ),
                SecurityGroupIngress(
                    from_port=443, to_port=443,
                    protocol="tcp", cidr_blocks=["0.0.0.0/0"]
                ),
            ],
            egress=[SecurityGroupEgress(
                from_port=0, to_port=0,
                protocol="-1", cidr_blocks=["0.0.0.0/0"]
            )]
        )
        
        self.instance = Instance(self, "server",
            ami=ami,
            instance_type=instance_type,
            subnet_id=subnet_id,
            vpc_security_group_ids=[self.sg.id],
            tags={"Name": f"{id}-web-server"}
        )

# Usage in a stack:
# web = WebServer(self, "api", vpc_id=vpc.id, subnet_id=subnet.id)
💡 Python Advantage: Unlike HCL modules, Python constructs support inheritance, mixins, abstract classes, and decorators. You can build a BaseStack with common tagging/naming conventions and have all stacks inherit from it.

46.4 CDKTF CLI

COMMANDS
📚 Official Documentation: CLI Reference

The CDKTF CLI provides commands for the full lifecycle: project scaffolding, synthesis, deployment, and teardown. It wraps the Terraform CLI and adds language-specific tooling.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CDKTF CLI WORKFLOW                                     │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  cdktf init     → Scaffold project (language, providers)          │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  Write Code     → Python/TypeScript/Go/C#/Java                    │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  cdktf get      → Generate provider bindings                      │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  cdktf synth    → Compile code → Terraform JSON                   │
│       │           (cdktf.out/stacks/*/cdk.tf.json)                │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  cdktf diff     → terraform plan (shows changes)                  │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  cdktf deploy   → terraform apply (creates resources)             │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  cdktf destroy  → terraform destroy (removes resources)           │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
46.4.1 cdktf init

Scaffolds a new CDKTF project with the chosen language template and configures providers.

# Interactive mode
cdktf init

# Non-interactive with options
cdktf init --template=python \
  --project-name=my-infra \
  --project-description="Production AWS infrastructure" \
  --providers="hashicorp/aws@~>5.30" \
  --cdktf-version="0.20.0" \
  --local                     # Use local state (not HCP Terraform)
46.4.2 cdktf synth

Compiles your code into Terraform-compatible JSON. This is the synthesis step — it runs your Python/TS code and produces configuration files.

# Synthesize all stacks
cdktf synth

# Output directory structure:
# cdktf.out/
# └── stacks/
#     ├── dev-network/
#     │   └── cdk.tf.json     ← Terraform JSON for dev
#     └── prod-network/
#         └── cdk.tf.json     ← Terraform JSON for prod

# View generated JSON
cat cdktf.out/stacks/dev-network/cdk.tf.json | python -m json.tool
46.4.3 cdktf deploy

Runs synth + terraform apply. Deploys one or more stacks.

# Deploy a specific stack
cdktf deploy dev-network

# Deploy all stacks
cdktf deploy '*'

# Deploy with auto-approve (CI/CD)
cdktf deploy dev-network --auto-approve

# Deploy multiple specific stacks
cdktf deploy dev-network prod-network

# Show plan without applying
cdktf diff dev-network
46.4.4 cdktf destroy

Tears down infrastructure created by CDKTF stacks.

# Destroy a specific stack
cdktf destroy dev-network

# Destroy with auto-approve
cdktf destroy dev-network --auto-approve

# Destroy all stacks
cdktf destroy '*'
Command Terraform Equivalent Description
cdktf init—Scaffold new CDKTF project
cdktf get—Generate provider/module bindings
cdktf synth—Compile code to Terraform JSON
cdktf diffterraform planPreview changes
cdktf deployterraform applyApply infrastructure
cdktf destroyterraform destroyTear down infrastructure
cdktf outputterraform outputShow stack outputs

46.5 Converting HCL to CDKTF

MIGRATION
📚 Official Documentation: cdktf convert

CDKTF provides tools to convert existing HCL configurations to your preferred programming language, making migration from HCL to CDKTF straightforward.

46.5.1 cdktf convert

The cdktf convert command reads HCL from stdin and outputs equivalent CDKTF code in your chosen language.

Convert HCL to Python
Transform an existing Terraform config to CDKTF Python 
# Convert a .tf file to Python
cat main.tf | cdktf convert --language python

# Convert to TypeScript (default)
cat main.tf | cdktf convert

# Convert to Go
cat main.tf | cdktf convert --language go

# Example input (main.tf):
# resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" {
#   bucket = "acme-data-bucket"
#   tags = {
#     Environment = "production"
#   }
# }

# Example output (Python):
# from cdktf_cdktf_provider_aws.s3_bucket import S3Bucket
# S3Bucket(self, "data",
#     bucket="acme-data-bucket",
#     tags={"Environment": "production"}
# )
46.5.2 Manual Conversion

For complex configurations, manual conversion gives you better control and the opportunity to add abstractions.

HCL Concept CDKTF Python Equivalent
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {}Vpc(self, "main", ...)
data "aws_ami" "latest" {}DataAwsAmi(self, "latest", ...)
variable "name" {}TerraformVariable(self, "name")
output "id" { value = ... }TerraformOutput(self, "id", value=...)
locals { x = "y" }Python variables: x = "y"
for_each = toset([...])Python loop: for item in [...]:
count = 3Python loop: for i in range(3):
module "vpc" {}TerraformHclModule(self, "vpc", source=...)

46.6 CDKTF Best Practices

BEST PRACTICE
📚 Official Documentation: CDKTF Best Practices

Follow these patterns to build maintainable, testable, and production-ready CDKTF infrastructure.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CDKTF BEST PRACTICES CHECKLIST                        │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Project Structure                                                │
│  ✅ Separate stacks per environment (dev/prod)                    │
│  ✅ Reusable constructs in a shared library                       │
│  ✅ One stack per Terraform state file                            │
│  ✅ Pin provider and CDKTF versions                               │
│                                                                   │
│  Code Quality                                                     │
│  ✅ Write unit tests for constructs (pytest/jest)                 │
│  ✅ Use type hints (Python) or strict TypeScript                  │
│  ✅ Review generated JSON (cdktf synth) in PRs                    │
│  ✅ Lint code with language-specific tools (ruff, eslint)         │
│                                                                   │
│  CI/CD Integration                                                │
│  ✅ Run cdktf synth in CI to catch errors early                   │
│  ✅ Use cdktf diff for plan output in PRs                         │
│  ✅ Store state in remote backend (S3, HCP Terraform)             │
│  ✅ Auto-approve only for non-production environments             │
│                                                                   │
│  Avoid                                                            │
│  ❌ Mixing HCL and CDKTF in the same state                       │
│  ❌ Over-abstracting (keep constructs simple)                     │
│  ❌ Ignoring generated JSON drift                                 │
│  ❌ Using CDKTF for simple, small projects (HCL is fine)          │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Testing CDKTF: One major advantage of CDKTF over HCL is unit testing. Use cdktf.Testing to write assertions against the synthesized JSON without deploying real infrastructure:

from cdktf import Testing
synth = Testing.synth(MyStack(Testing.app(), "test"))
assert Testing.to_have_resource(synth, "aws_vpc")
🧰 💰 📄 🔍
CHAPTER 47

Additional Tools & Ecosystem

Infracost terraform-docs pre-commit tfenv Visualization VS Code
7Sections
21Subsections
30+Examples
🧰Toolbox
PART 13 INTERMEDIATE ★★★☆☆

The Terraform ecosystem extends far beyond the CLI itself. A rich set of third-party tools covers cost estimation, documentation generation, linting, version management, infrastructure visualization, and IDE support — all essential for professional Terraform workflows.

Learning Objectives

  • Estimate infrastructure costs before deployment with Infracost
  • Auto-generate module documentation with terraform-docs
  • Enforce code quality with pre-commit hooks
  • Manage multiple Terraform versions with tfenv and tfswitch
  • Visualize infrastructure with Blast Radius, Rover, and Pluralith
  • Enhance your IDE with Terraform VS Code extensions

Chapter Contents

47.1 Infracost 47.2 terraform-docs 47.3 pre-commit Hooks 47.4 tfenv 47.5 tfswitcher / tfswitch 47.6 Visualization Tools 47.7 VS Code Extensions

47.1 Infracost

COST MANAGEMENT
📚 Official Documentation: Infracost Documentation

Infracost shows cloud cost estimates for Terraform changes directly in pull requests, terminals, and CI/CD pipelines. It supports AWS, Azure, and GCP with over 1,000 resources priced.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              INFRACOST WORKFLOW                                     │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Developer writes Terraform code                                  │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  infracost breakdown --path .                                     │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Monthly Cost Estimate                                      │  │
│  │  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │  │
│  │  aws_instance.web          $8.47/mo  (t3.micro)             │  │
│  │  aws_rds_cluster.db       $72.00/mo  (db.r5.large)          │  │
│  │  aws_s3_bucket.data        $2.30/mo  (100GB)                │  │
│  │  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │  │
│  │  TOTAL                    $82.77/mo                          │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│       │                                                           │
│       ▼                                                           │
│  PR comment with cost diff (before → after)                       │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
47.1.1 Cost Estimation

Infracost parses Terraform plan output and calculates monthly costs using cloud provider pricing APIs.

# ─── Installation ──────────────────────────────
# macOS
brew install infracost

# Linux/WSL
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/infracost/infracost/master/scripts/install.sh | sh

# Windows (Chocolatey)
choco install infracost

# API key registration (free tier)
infracost auth login

# ─── Cost Breakdown ───────────────────────────
# Show costs for current directory
infracost breakdown --path .

# Show costs for a specific tfvars file
infracost breakdown --path . --terraform-var-file=prod.tfvars

# JSON output for CI/CD
infracost breakdown --path . --format json --out-file infracost.json

# HTML report
infracost breakdown --path . --format html --out-file report.html
47.1.2 CI/CD Integration

Infracost integrates with GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps, and other CI/CD systems to post cost estimates as PR comments.

# .github/workflows/infracost.yml
name: Infracost
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  infracost:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write  # Required for PR comments
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Setup Infracost
        uses: infracost/actions/setup@v3
        with:
          api-key: ${{ secrets.INFRACOST_API_KEY }}
      
      # Generate cost for base branch
      - name: Baseline costs
        run: |
          infracost breakdown --path=. \
            --format=json --out-file=/tmp/base.json
        working-directory: terraform/
      
      # Compare with PR changes
      - name: Cost diff
        run: |
          infracost diff --path=. \
            --compare-to=/tmp/base.json \
            --format=json --out-file=/tmp/diff.json
        working-directory: terraform/
      
      # Post comment on PR
      - name: Post PR comment
        run: |
          infracost comment github \
            --path=/tmp/diff.json \
            --repo=$GITHUB_REPOSITORY \
            --pull-request=${{ github.event.pull_request.number }} \
            --github-token=${{ github.token }} \
            --behavior=update
47.1.3 Cost Policies

Define cost policies using Open Policy Agent (OPA) Rego to prevent deployments that exceed budget thresholds.

# policy.rego — Block PRs over $500/month increase
package infracost

deny[msg] {
    maxDiff = 500.0
    msg := sprintf(
        "Monthly cost increase ($%.2f) exceeds policy limit ($%.2f)",
        [to_number(input.diffTotalMonthlyCost), maxDiff]
    )
    to_number(input.diffTotalMonthlyCost) > maxDiff
}

# Block specific expensive resources
deny[msg] {
    r := input.projects[_].breakdown.resources[_]
    startswith(r.name, "aws_instance")
    to_number(r.monthlyCost) > 100
    msg := sprintf("%s costs $%.2f/mo — use a smaller instance", 
        [r.name, to_number(r.monthlyCost)])
}
47.1.4 Cloud Pricing API

Infracost provides a free GraphQL API for querying cloud pricing data across AWS, Azure, and GCP.

# Query pricing via API
curl -sS https://pricing.api.infracost.io/graphql \
  -H "X-Api-Key: $INFRACOST_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "query": "{ products(filter: {vendorName: \"aws\", service: \"AmazonEC2\", productFamily: \"Compute Instance\", attributeFilters: [{key: \"instanceType\", value: \"t3.micro\"}, {key: \"operatingSystem\", value: \"Linux\"}, {key: \"tenancy\", value: \"Shared\"}, {key: \"capacitystatus\", value: \"Used\"}, {key: \"regionCode\", value: \"us-east-1\"}]}) { prices(filter: {purchaseOption: \"on_demand\"}) { USD } } }"
  }'
💡 Tip: Use infracost breakdown --show-skipped to see resources that don't have cost estimates yet. The free tier supports up to 1,000 runs per month.

47.2 terraform-docs

DOCUMENTATION
📚 Official Documentation: terraform-docs.io

terraform-docs automatically generates documentation from Terraform modules — extracting variables, outputs, providers, resources, and descriptions into formatted Markdown, JSON, or other formats.

47.2.1 Documentation Generation

Generate clean documentation from your module code. terraform-docs reads .tf files and extracts all input variables, outputs, providers, and resource data.

# ─── Installation ──────────────────────────────
# macOS
brew install terraform-docs

# Linux
curl -sSLo terraform-docs.tar.gz \
  https://terraform-docs.io/dl/v0.17.0/terraform-docs-v0.17.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz
tar -xzf terraform-docs.tar.gz
chmod +x terraform-docs
sudo mv terraform-docs /usr/local/bin/

# ─── Generate Documentation ───────────────────
# Markdown table format (most common)
terraform-docs markdown table ./modules/vpc

# Inject into existing README between markers
terraform-docs markdown table --output-file README.md --output-mode inject ./modules/vpc

# README.md markers:
# <!-- BEGIN_TF_DOCS -->
# (auto-generated content goes here)
# <!-- END_TF_DOCS -->
47.2.2 Output Formats

terraform-docs supports multiple output formats for different use cases.

Format Command Use Case
Markdown Tablemarkdown tableGitHub/GitLab READMEs
Markdown Documentmarkdown documentDetailed docs
JSONjsonProgrammatic consumption
AsciiDocasciidoc tableEnterprise wikis
tfvars HCLtfvars hclGenerate template .tfvars
TOMLtomlTOML-based toolchains
47.2.3 Configuration File

Customize terraform-docs behavior with a .terraform-docs.yml configuration file in your module directory.

# .terraform-docs.yml
formatter: "markdown table"

version: ">= 0.17.0"

header-from: "main.tf"
footer-from: ""

recursive:
  enabled: false

sections:
  show:
    - header
    - inputs
    - outputs
    - providers
    - requirements
    - resources

content: |-
  {{ .Header }}

  ## Usage

  ```hcl
  module "vpc" {
    source = "./modules/vpc"
    
    cidr_block   = "10.0.0.0/16"
    environment  = "production"
  }
  ```

  {{ .Providers }}
  {{ .Inputs }}
  {{ .Outputs }}

output:
  file: "README.md"
  mode: inject   # inject between markers

sort:
  enabled: true
  by: required   # required inputs first

settings:
  anchor: true
  color: true
  default: true
  description: true
  escape: true
  indent: 3
  required: true
  sensitive: true
  type: true
47.2.4 Pre-commit Integration

Auto-update documentation on every commit using pre-commit hooks.

# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/terraform-docs/terraform-docs
    rev: v0.17.0
    hooks:
      - id: terraform-docs-go
        args: ["markdown", "table", "--output-file", "README.md", "--output-mode", "inject"]

47.3 pre-commit Hooks

CODE QUALITY
📚 Official Documentation: pre-commit-terraform

pre-commit hooks run automated checks before every git commit — formatting, validation, linting, and documentation. The pre-commit-terraform framework provides Terraform-specific hooks maintained by Anton Babenko.

47.3.1 pre-commit-terraform

The most popular collection of pre-commit hooks for Terraform. Supports formatting, validation, tflint, tfsec, checkov, and more.

# ─── Install pre-commit ────────────────────────
pip install pre-commit

# Install hooks in the git repo
cd your-terraform-project
pre-commit install

# Run all hooks manually
pre-commit run --all-files

# Update hooks to latest versions
pre-commit autoupdate
47.3.2 Hook Configuration

Configure hooks in .pre-commit-config.yaml at the root of your repository.

# .pre-commit-config.yaml — Production-ready setup
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.88.0
    hooks:
      # Format all .tf files
      - id: terraform_fmt
      
      # Validate Terraform configuration
      - id: terraform_validate
        args:
          - --hook-config=--retry-once-with-cleanup=true
      
      # Lint with tflint
      - id: terraform_tflint
        args:
          - --args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.tflint.hcl
      
      # Security scanning with tfsec
      - id: terraform_tfsec
        args:
          - --args=--soft-fail
      
      # Security scanning with checkov
      - id: terraform_checkov
        args:
          - --args=--quiet
          - --args=--skip-check CKV_AWS_18,CKV_AWS_21
      
      # Auto-generate docs
      - id: terraform_docs
        args:
          - --args=--config=.terraform-docs.yml
