      # Check for hardcoded credentials
      - id: terraform_credentials_detector

  # General pre-commit hooks
  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-merge-conflict
      - id: detect-private-key
47.3.3 Available Hooks

Reference list of the most useful Terraform pre-commit hooks.

Hook ID Tool Purpose
terraform_fmtterraform fmtAuto-format HCL files
terraform_validateterraform validateValidate configuration syntax
terraform_tflintTFLintLint rules & best practices
terraform_tfsectfsecStatic security analysis
terraform_checkovCheckovPolicy-as-code scanning
terraform_docsterraform-docsAuto-generate documentation
terraform_trivyTrivyVulnerability scanning
terraform_credentials_detectorbuilt-inDetect hardcoded secrets
infracost_breakdownInfracostCost estimation

47.4 tfenv

VERSION MGMT
📚 Official Documentation: tfutils/tfenv

tfenv is a Terraform version manager inspired by rbenv. It lets you install and switch between multiple Terraform versions per project — essential when managing infrastructure across different version requirements.

47.4.1 Version Management

Install, list, and switch between Terraform versions effortlessly.

# ─── Installation ──────────────────────────────
# macOS
brew install tfenv

# Linux (manual)
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/tfutils/tfenv.git ~/.tfenv
echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc

# ─── Usage ─────────────────────────────────────
# List available versions
tfenv list-remote | head -20

# Install a specific version
tfenv install 1.7.5
tfenv install 1.6.6
tfenv install latest

# List installed versions
tfenv list
#   1.7.5
#   1.6.6
# * 1.7.5 (set by /home/user/.tfenv/version)

# Switch global version
tfenv use 1.6.6

# Verify
terraform --version
47.4.2 .terraform-version

Pin a project to a specific Terraform version with a .terraform-version file. tfenv reads this file and automatically uses the correct version.

# Create version pin file
echo "1.7.5" > .terraform-version

# Supports constraints
echo "~> 1.7.0" > .terraform-version   # Latest 1.7.x
echo "latest" > .terraform-version      # Always latest

# tfenv automatically uses the pinned version
cd my-project/
terraform --version
# Terraform v1.7.5

# Team members get the right version automatically
git add .terraform-version
git commit -m "Pin Terraform to 1.7.5"
47.4.3 Auto-switching

tfenv automatically detects .terraform-version when you enter a directory. If the version isn't installed, it downloads it for you.

# Enable auto-install (installs missing versions automatically)
echo 'export TFENV_AUTO_INSTALL=true' >> ~/.bashrc

# Directory structure with different versions:
# project-a/.terraform-version  →  1.7.5
# project-b/.terraform-version  →  1.6.6

cd project-a/
terraform --version   # v1.7.5

cd ../project-b/
terraform --version   # v1.6.6 (auto-switched!)
💡 CI/CD Tip: Commit .terraform-version to your repository so CI/CD pipelines use the same Terraform version as your local development environment. Add tfenv install && tfenv use to your CI setup step.

47.5 tfswitcher / tfswitch

VERSION MGMT
📚 Official Documentation: tfswitch Documentation

tfswitch is an alternative to tfenv for Terraform version management. It provides an interactive TUI (terminal UI) for selecting versions and supports reading version constraints from versions.tf files.

# ─── Installation ──────────────────────────────
# macOS
brew install warrensbox/tap/tfswitch

# Linux
curl -L https://raw.githubusercontent.com/warrensbox/terraform-switcher/release/install.sh | bash

# ─── Interactive Usage ─────────────────────────
# Launch TUI selector
tfswitch
# Use arrow keys to select version, Enter to install

# Install specific version
tfswitch 1.7.5

# Auto-detect from versions.tf
# versions.tf:
#   terraform {
#     required_version = "~> 1.7.0"
#   }
cd your-project/
tfswitch    # Reads required_version constraint

# Also reads .tfswitchrc and .terraform-version files
Feature tfenv tfswitch
Interactive TUI❌✅
.terraform-version✅✅
Read versions.tf❌✅
Auto-install✅ (env var)✅ (default)
Shell completion✅✅
Written inBashGo (single binary)

47.6 Visualization Tools

VISUALIZATION
📌 Category: Infrastructure Diagramming & Dependency Visualization

Visualize your Terraform infrastructure as interactive diagrams to understand resource relationships, plan reviews, and team communication.

47.6.1 Blast Radius

Blast Radius generates interactive dependency graphs from Terraform state files. It shows how resources are connected and helps understand the blast radius of changes.

# Install via pip
pip install blastradius

# Run against current directory
blast-radius --serve .

# Opens interactive graph at http://localhost:5000
# - Nodes = resources
# - Edges = dependencies
# - Color = resource type
# - Click to highlight blast radius

# Generate SVG for documentation
blast-radius --svg . > infrastructure.svg
47.6.2 Rover

Rover (by HashiCorp community) generates interactive resource maps from Terraform plan files. It produces a standalone HTML file with a zoomable, filterable graph.

# Install Rover
# macOS
brew install im2nguyen/rover/rover

# Or download binary from GitHub releases
# https://github.com/im2nguyen/rover/releases

# Generate plan and visualize
terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan
rover -planPath plan.tfplan

# Opens browser with interactive map at http://localhost:9000

# Generate standalone HTML
rover -planPath plan.tfplan -standalone > rover-map.html

# With saved state
rover -planPath plan.tfplan -tfStatePath terraform.tfstate
47.6.3 Pluralith

Pluralith creates publication-quality infrastructure diagrams that automatically update when your Terraform code changes. Integrates with CI/CD for PR diagram comments.

# Install Pluralith CLI
curl -sSL https://www.pluralith.com/v1/install.sh | bash

# Initialize in your Terraform project
pluralith init --api-key=YOUR_KEY

# Generate diagram (runs terraform plan internally)
pluralith run plan

# Export as PDF/PNG
pluralith run plan --export-pdf
pluralith run plan --export-png

# CI/CD: Post diagram as PR comment
pluralith run plan --ci-cd=github \
  --title="Infrastructure Changes" \
  --show-changes=true
47.6.4 Brainboard

Brainboard is a visual cloud designer that generates Terraform code from diagrams. Drag-and-drop architecture design that outputs production-ready HCL.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              VISUALIZATION TOOLS COMPARISON                        │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Tool          │ Input            │ Output         │ Interactive  │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── │
│  Blast Radius  │ State / HCL      │ Web graph      │ ✅           │
│  Rover         │ Plan file        │ HTML / Web     │ ✅           │
│  Pluralith     │ Plan + State     │ PDF / PNG      │ ✅           │
│  Brainboard    │ Diagram (GUI)    │ HCL code       │ ✅           │
│  terraform     │ HCL              │ DOT graph      │ ❌           │
│    graph       │                  │                │              │
│                                                                   │
│  Use Cases:                                                       │
│  • Blast Radius  → Quick local exploration                        │
│  • Rover         → PR reviews, team communication                 │
│  • Pluralith     → CI/CD integration, documentation               │
│  • Brainboard    → Visual design → code generation                │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

47.7 VS Code Extensions

IDE
📌 Category: Developer Productivity & IDE Integration

VS Code extensions enhance the Terraform development experience with syntax highlighting, auto-completion, linting, formatting, and plan visualization — all within your editor.

47.7.1 HashiCorp Terraform

The official Terraform extension by HashiCorp. Provides the most essential features for working with HCL files.

Feature Description
Syntax HighlightingFull HCL2 syntax highlighting for .tf and .tfvars
Auto-CompleteIntelliSense for resources, data sources, variables, and functions
Go to DefinitionCtrl+Click to navigate to variable/module/resource definitions
Hover DocumentationHover over resources for provider documentation
Format on SaveAutomatic terraform fmt on file save
ValidationReal-time syntax validation and error detection
Module SupportModule completion from registry and local sources
 
// settings.json — Recommended configuration
{
  "[terraform]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.formatOnSaveMode": "file"
  },
  "[terraform-vars]": {
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "hashicorp.terraform",
    "editor.formatOnSave": true
  },
  "terraform.languageServer.enable": true,
  "terraform.validation.enableEnhancedValidation": true,
  "terraform.codelens.referenceCount": true
}
47.7.2 Terraform Advanced

Complementary extensions that add advanced features beyond the official extension.

Extension Publisher Key Features
Terraform Linter (tflint)4opsTFLint integration, lint on save
Terraform Completererd0sProvider-aware autocompletion
Terraform GraphadamiBsVisualize terraform graph in VS Code
InfracostInfracostInline cost estimates per resource
tfsectfsecSecurity issues highlighted inline
47.7.3 terraform-plan-parser

Tools and extensions for parsing, displaying, and reviewing Terraform plan output in a more readable format.

# ─── CLI Plan Visualization ────────────────────
# Save plan as JSON
terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan
terraform show -json plan.tfplan > plan.json

# Use landscape for colored, readable output
pip install terraform-landscape
terraform plan | landscape

# Use tf-summarize for plan summaries
# https://github.com/dineshba/tf-summarize
terraform show -json plan.tfplan | tf-summarize

# Example output:
# ┌──────────────────┬────────┬────────┬─────────┐
# │ RESOURCE         │ CREATE │ UPDATE │ DESTROY │
# ├──────────────────┼────────┼────────┼─────────┤
# │ aws_instance     │   2    │   0    │    0    │
# │ aws_vpc          │   1    │   0    │    0    │
# │ aws_subnet       │   3    │   0    │    0    │
# └──────────────────┴────────┴────────┴─────────┘
# Total: 6 to create, 0 to update, 0 to destroy
💡 Recommended VS Code Setup: Install at minimum: (1) HashiCorp Terraform (official), (2) Infracost (cost estimates), and (3) a tflint extension. Use "terraform.languageServer.enable": true in settings for the best IntelliSense experience.
☁️ 🔷 🏢 🔐
CHAPTER 48

Azure Provider

azurerm Resource Groups VNets App Services AKS Azure AD
7Sections
19Subsections
30+Examples
🔷Azure
PART 14 INTERMEDIATE ★★★★☆

The Azure Resource Manager (azurerm) provider enables Terraform to manage Microsoft Azure infrastructure. This chapter covers provider setup, authentication, core resources, App Services, AKS, databases, and a comprehensive AWS-to-Azure comparison to help you transfer your Terraform skills across cloud platforms.

Learning Objectives

  • Configure the azurerm provider with authentication and features block
  • Deploy core Azure resources: resource groups, VNets, NSGs, VMs, and storage
  • Build App Services, Web Apps, and Function Apps with Terraform
  • Provision Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) clusters
  • Manage Azure SQL and Cosmos DB databases
  • Integrate Azure Active Directory for RBAC
  • Map AWS Terraform concepts to Azure equivalents

Chapter Contents

48.1 Azure Provider Setup 48.2 Core Azure Resources 48.3 Azure App Services 48.4 Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) 48.5 Azure SQL and Cosmos DB 48.6 Azure Active Directory Integration 48.7 Azure/AWS Comparison for Terraform

48.1 Azure Provider Setup

CONFIGURATION
📚 Official Documentation: azurerm Provider

The azurerm provider is Terraform's interface to Microsoft Azure Resource Manager. It requires an Azure subscription, authentication credentials, and a mandatory features block for configuration.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    AZURE PROVIDER ARCHITECTURE                         │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  Terraform CLI                                                        │
│       │                                                               │
│       ▼                                                               │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  azurerm Provider                                               │  │
│  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │ Auth Engine   │  │ Features Block│  │ Subscription Config │ │  │
│  │  │ - CLI         │  │ - key_vault   │  │ - subscription_id   │ │  │
│  │  │ - SPN         │  │ - virtual_machine│ - tenant_id        │ │  │
│  │  │ - MSI         │  │ - resource_group│  │ - environment     │ │  │
│  │  │ - OIDC        │  │ - template_deployment │               │ │  │
│  │  └──────────────┘  └───────────────┘  └──────────────────────┘ │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ Azure REST API                          │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  AZURE RESOURCE MANAGER (ARM)                                   │  │
│  │  Resource Groups → Resources → Properties → State               │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
48.1.1 azurerm Provider Configuration

The basic provider block requires a features {} block (even if empty) and optionally sets subscription and tenant details.

Basic Provider Configuration
Minimal azurerm setup with version pinning 
# versions.tf
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"
  
  required_providers {
    azurerm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azurerm"
      version = "~> 3.90"
    }
  }
  
  # Remote backend on Azure
  backend "azurerm" {
    resource_group_name  = "tfstate-rg"
    storage_account_name = "tfstateacct2024"
    container_name       = "tfstate"
    key                  = "production.terraform.tfstate"
  }
}

# Provider configuration
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  
  # Explicit subscription (recommended for multi-sub)
  subscription_id = var.subscription_id
  tenant_id       = var.tenant_id
}
48.1.2 Authentication Methods

Azure supports four primary authentication methods, each suited to different workflows.

Method Use Case Configuration
Azure CLILocal developmentaz login → auto-detected
Service Principal (Secret)CI/CD pipelinesClient ID + Client Secret
Service Principal (Certificate)High-security CI/CDClient ID + PFX cert
Managed Identity (MSI)Azure-hosted runnersAuto-detected on Azure VMs
OIDC (Federated)GitHub ActionsWorkload Identity Federation
 
# Service Principal authentication (CI/CD)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  
  subscription_id = var.subscription_id
  tenant_id       = var.tenant_id
  client_id       = var.client_id
  client_secret   = var.client_secret
}

# OIDC / Workload Identity (GitHub Actions)
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  
  subscription_id            = var.subscription_id
  tenant_id                  = var.tenant_id
  client_id                  = var.client_id
  use_oidc                   = true
}

# Environment variables (recommended for secrets)
# ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID, ARM_TENANT_ID
# ARM_CLIENT_ID, ARM_CLIENT_SECRET
48.1.3 Subscription and Tenant

Azure organizes resources in a hierarchy: Tenant → Management Groups → Subscriptions → Resource Groups → Resources. Terraform can manage resources across multiple subscriptions with provider aliases.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              AZURE RESOURCE HIERARCHY                               │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Azure AD Tenant (contoso.onmicrosoft.com)                        │
│  └── Management Group: "Production"                               │
│      ├── Subscription: "Prod-Workloads" (sub-111...)              │
│      │   ├── RG: prod-networking                                  │
│      │   │   ├── azurerm_virtual_network.main                     │
│      │   │   └── azurerm_subnet.app                               │
│      │   └── RG: prod-app                                         │
│      │       ├── azurerm_linux_web_app.api                        │
│      │       └── azurerm_mssql_database.db                        │
│      └── Subscription: "Prod-Shared" (sub-222...)                 │
│          └── RG: shared-services                                  │
│              ├── azurerm_key_vault.main                            │
│              └── azurerm_log_analytics_workspace.central           │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Multi-subscription pattern with aliases
provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  alias           = "workloads"
  subscription_id = "sub-111-workloads"
}

provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
  alias           = "shared"
  subscription_id = "sub-222-shared"
}

# Use provider alias in resources
resource "azurerm_resource_group" "app" {
  provider = azurerm.workloads
  name     = "prod-app-rg"
  location = "East US"
}

resource "azurerm_key_vault" "main" {
  provider            = azurerm.shared
  name                = "shared-kv-2024"
  location            = "East US"
  resource_group_name = "shared-services"
  tenant_id           = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id
  sku_name            = "standard"
}
48.1.4 Features Block

The features block is mandatory and controls provider behavior for specific resource types — especially around deletion and cleanup policies.

# Production-ready features block
provider "azurerm" {
  features {
    key_vault {
      # Purge soft-deleted vaults on destroy
      purge_soft_delete_on_destroy    = true
      recover_soft_deleted_key_vaults = true
    }
    
    virtual_machine {
      # Delete OS disk when VM is destroyed
      delete_os_disk_on_deletion     = true
      graceful_shutdown              = false
      skip_shutdown_and_force_delete = false
    }
    
    resource_group {
      # Prevent destroy if resources exist inside
      prevent_deletion_if_contains_resources = true
    }
    
    template_deployment {
      delete_nested_items_during_deletion = true
    }
    
    log_analytics_workspace {
      permanently_delete_on_destroy = true
    }
  }
}
💡 Features Block Tip: Even an empty features {} is required. In production, always configure resource_group.prevent_deletion_if_contains_resources = true to avoid accidental deletion of resource groups that still contain resources.

48.2 Core Azure Resources

INFRASTRUCTURE
📚 Official Documentation: azurerm Resources

Azure's foundational resources — resource groups, virtual networks, subnets, NSGs, VMs, and storage accounts — are the building blocks for all Azure deployments.

48.2.1 Resource Groups

Resource groups are Azure's mandatory logical container for all resources. Every Azure resource must belong to exactly one resource group. They provide lifecycle management, RBAC scope, and cost tracking boundaries.

# Resource Group — the foundation of Azure
resource "azurerm_resource_group" "main" {
  name     = "${var.project}-${var.environment}-rg"
  location = var.location
  
  tags = {
    Environment = var.environment
    Project     = var.project
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    CostCenter  = "engineering"
  }
}

# Data source: reference existing resource group
data "azurerm_resource_group" "existing" {
  name = "legacy-app-rg"
}
48.2.2 Virtual Networks

Azure Virtual Networks (VNets) are the networking foundation — equivalent to AWS VPCs.

# Virtual Network with DNS settings
resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "main" {
  name                = "${var.project}-vnet"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  address_space       = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
  dns_servers         = ["10.0.0.4", "10.0.0.5"]
  
  tags = var.common_tags
}

# VNet Peering (connect two VNets)
resource "azurerm_virtual_network_peering" "hub_to_spoke" {
  name                      = "hub-to-spoke"
  resource_group_name       = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  virtual_network_name      = azurerm_virtual_network.hub.name
  remote_virtual_network_id = azurerm_virtual_network.spoke.id
  
  allow_virtual_network_access = true
  allow_forwarded_traffic      = true
  allow_gateway_transit        = true
}
48.2.3 Subnets

Subnets segment a VNet into smaller networks. Some Azure services require dedicated subnets with service delegation.

# Application subnets
resource "azurerm_subnet" "app" {
  name                 = "app-subnet"
  resource_group_name  = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.main.name
  address_prefixes     = ["10.0.1.0/24"]

  # Delegate to App Service for VNet integration
  delegation {
    name = "app-service-delegation"
    service_delegation {
      name    = "Microsoft.Web/serverFarms"
      actions = ["Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/subnets/action"]
    }
  }
}

resource "azurerm_subnet" "db" {
  name                 = "db-subnet"
  resource_group_name  = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  virtual_network_name = azurerm_virtual_network.main.name
  address_prefixes     = ["10.0.2.0/24"]
  
  service_endpoints    = ["Microsoft.Sql", "Microsoft.Storage"]
}
48.2.4 Network Security Groups

NSGs filter network traffic to and from Azure resources — equivalent to AWS Security Groups but with explicit priority-based rules.

# Network Security Group with rules
resource "azurerm_network_security_group" "web" {
  name                = "${var.project}-web-nsg"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name

  security_rule {
    name                       = "AllowHTTPS"
    priority                   = 100
    direction                  = "Inbound"
    access                     = "Allow"
    protocol                   = "Tcp"
    source_port_range          = "*"
    destination_port_range     = "443"
    source_address_prefix      = "*"
    destination_address_prefix = "*"
  }

  security_rule {
    name                       = "AllowHTTP"
    priority                   = 110
    direction                  = "Inbound"
    access                     = "Allow"
    protocol                   = "Tcp"
    source_port_range          = "*"
    destination_port_range     = "80"
    source_address_prefix      = "*"
    destination_address_prefix = "*"
  }

  security_rule {
    name                       = "DenyAllInbound"
    priority                   = 4096
    direction                  = "Inbound"
    access                     = "Deny"
    protocol                   = "*"
    source_port_range          = "*"
    destination_port_range     = "*"
    source_address_prefix      = "*"
    destination_address_prefix = "*"
  }
  
  tags = var.common_tags
}

# Associate NSG with subnet
resource "azurerm_subnet_network_security_group_association" "web" {
  subnet_id                 = azurerm_subnet.app.id
  network_security_group_id = azurerm_network_security_group.web.id
}
48.2.5 Virtual Machines

Azure Linux and Windows VMs require a NIC, OS disk, and image reference. The newer azurerm_linux_virtual_machine resource replaces the legacy azurerm_virtual_machine.

# Network Interface
resource "azurerm_network_interface" "web" {
  name                = "${var.project}-web-nic"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name

  ip_configuration {
    name                          = "internal"
    subnet_id                     = azurerm_subnet.app.id
    private_ip_address_allocation = "Dynamic"
  }
}

# Linux Virtual Machine
resource "azurerm_linux_virtual_machine" "web" {
  name                = "${var.project}-web-vm"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  size                = "Standard_B2s"
  admin_username      = "azureuser"
  
  network_interface_ids = [azurerm_network_interface.web.id]

  admin_ssh_key {
    username   = "azureuser"
    public_key = file("~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")
  }

  os_disk {
    caching              = "ReadWrite"
    storage_account_type = "Premium_LRS"
    disk_size_gb         = 30
  }

  source_image_reference {
    publisher = "Canonical"
    offer     = "0001-com-ubuntu-server-jammy"
    sku       = "22_04-lts-gen2"
    version   = "latest"
  }

  tags = var.common_tags
}
48.2.6 Storage Accounts

Azure Storage Accounts provide blob, file, queue, and table storage — the equivalent of AWS S3 + SQS + DynamoDB combined.

# Storage Account
resource "azurerm_storage_account" "main" {
  name                     = "${var.project}storage2024"
  resource_group_name      = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  location                 = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  account_tier             = "Standard"
  account_replication_type = "GRS"      # Geo-redundant
  account_kind             = "StorageV2"
  min_tls_version          = "TLS1_2"
  
  blob_properties {
    versioning_enabled  = true
    delete_retention_policy {
      days = 30
    }
    container_delete_retention_policy {
      days = 7
    }
  }
  
  network_rules {
    default_action             = "Deny"
    ip_rules                   = ["203.0.113.0/24"]
    virtual_network_subnet_ids = [azurerm_subnet.app.id]
  }
  
  tags = var.common_tags
}

# Blob Container
resource "azurerm_storage_container" "data" {
  name                  = "app-data"
  storage_account_name  = azurerm_storage_account.main.name
  container_access_type = "private"
}
💡 Naming: Azure Storage Account names must be globally unique, 3-24 chars, lowercase alphanumeric only. Use random_string or project-based naming to avoid conflicts.

48.3 Azure App Services

COMPUTE
📚 Official Documentation: App Service Resources

Azure App Service is a fully managed platform for building, deploying, and scaling web apps — ideal for Django, FastAPI, Node.js, and .NET applications.

48.3.1 App Service Plans

App Service Plans define the compute resources (CPU, memory, scaling) for your web apps. Multiple apps can share one plan.

# App Service Plan (Linux)
resource "azurerm_service_plan" "main" {
  name                = "${var.project}-asp"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  os_type             = "Linux"
  sku_name            = "P1v3"     # Premium v3

  tags = var.common_tags
}

# SKU Reference:
# F1     = Free tier        (shared, 1GB RAM, 60min/day CPU)
# B1-B3  = Basic            (dedicated, 1.75-7GB RAM)
# S1-S3  = Standard         (auto-scale, staging slots)
# P1v3   = Premium v3       (enhanced perf, VNet integration)
# I1v2   = Isolated v2      (dedicated environment, ASE)
48.3.2 Web Apps

Linux Web Apps deploy containerized or runtime-based applications. Perfect for Django/FastAPI Python applications.

# Linux Web App for Django/FastAPI
resource "azurerm_linux_web_app" "api" {
  name                = "${var.project}-api"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  service_plan_id     = azurerm_service_plan.main.id

  site_config {
    always_on = true
    
    application_stack {
      python_version = "3.11"
    }
    
    # Health check endpoint
    health_check_path = "/health"
    
    # VNet integration
    vnet_route_all_enabled = true
  }

  app_settings = {
    "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE" = "config.settings.production"
    "DATABASE_URL"           = "@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=${azurerm_key_vault_secret.db_url.id})"
    "ALLOWED_HOSTS"          = "${var.project}-api.azurewebsites.net"
    "SCM_DO_BUILD_DURING_DEPLOYMENT" = "true"
  }

  # Deploy from GitHub
  connection_string {
    name  = "Database"
    type  = "PostgreSQL"
    value = "host=${azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.main.fqdn};database=app;user=admin"
  }

  tags = var.common_tags
}
48.3.3 Function Apps

Azure Functions are serverless compute — equivalent to AWS Lambda. They can be triggered by HTTP, queues, timers, and events.

# Function App (Python)
resource "azurerm_linux_function_app" "processor" {
  name                = "${var.project}-func"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  service_plan_id     = azurerm_service_plan.functions.id

  storage_account_name       = azurerm_storage_account.main.name
  storage_account_access_key = azurerm_storage_account.main.primary_access_key

  site_config {
    application_stack {
      python_version = "3.11"
    }
    
    application_insights_connection_string = azurerm_application_insights.main.connection_string
  }

  app_settings = {
    "FUNCTIONS_WORKER_RUNTIME" = "python"
    "AzureWebJobsStorage"     = azurerm_storage_account.main.primary_connection_string
  }

  tags = var.common_tags
}

48.4 Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)

CONTAINERS
📚 Official Documentation: AKS Cluster

Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) provides a managed Kubernetes cluster — equivalent to AWS EKS. Terraform manages the cluster, node pools, networking, and RBAC integration.

# AKS Cluster
resource "azurerm_kubernetes_cluster" "main" {
  name                = "${var.project}-aks"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  dns_prefix          = "${var.project}-aks"
  kubernetes_version  = "1.29"

  default_node_pool {
    name                = "system"
    node_count          = 3
    vm_size             = "Standard_D4s_v5"
    os_disk_size_gb     = 50
    vnet_subnet_id      = azurerm_subnet.aks.id
    
    upgrade_settings {
      max_surge = "33%"
    }
  }

  identity {
    type = "SystemAssigned"
  }

  network_profile {
    network_plugin    = "azure"     # Azure CNI
    network_policy    = "calico"
    load_balancer_sku = "standard"
    service_cidr      = "172.16.0.0/16"
    dns_service_ip    = "172.16.0.10"
  }

  oms_agent {
    log_analytics_workspace_id = azurerm_log_analytics_workspace.main.id
  }

  azure_active_directory_role_based_access_control {
    managed            = true
    azure_rbac_enabled = true
  }

  tags = var.common_tags
}

# Additional user node pool (application workloads)
resource "azurerm_kubernetes_cluster_node_pool" "app" {
  name                  = "apppool"
  kubernetes_cluster_id = azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.id
  vm_size               = "Standard_D8s_v5"
  node_count            = 2
  max_count             = 10
  min_count             = 2
  enable_auto_scaling   = true
  vnet_subnet_id        = azurerm_subnet.aks.id
  
  node_labels = {
    "workload" = "application"
  }
  
  node_taints = ["workload=application:NoSchedule"]
}

48.5 Azure SQL and Cosmos DB

DATABASES
📚 Official Documentation: Azure SQL | Cosmos DB

Azure provides multiple database options: Azure SQL (relational), PostgreSQL Flexible Server (for Django), and Cosmos DB (multi-model NoSQL).

# ─── Azure SQL (Managed SQL Server) ────────────
resource "azurerm_mssql_server" "main" {
  name                         = "${var.project}-sqlserver"
  resource_group_name          = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  location                     = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  version                      = "12.0"
  administrator_login          = "sqladmin"
  administrator_login_password = var.sql_admin_password
  minimum_tls_version          = "1.2"
}

resource "azurerm_mssql_database" "app" {
  name         = "${var.project}-db"
  server_id    = azurerm_mssql_server.main.id
  collation    = "SQL_Latin1_General_CP1_CI_AS"
  max_size_gb  = 50
  sku_name     = "S1"
  
  short_term_retention_policy {
    retention_days = 7
  }
}

# ─── PostgreSQL Flexible Server (for Django) ──
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server" "main" {
  name                   = "${var.project}-pgdb"
  resource_group_name    = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  location               = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  version                = "16"
  administrator_login    = "pgadmin"
  administrator_password = var.pg_admin_password
  zone                   = "1"
  storage_mb             = 32768
  sku_name               = "GP_Standard_D2s_v3"

  delegated_subnet_id    = azurerm_subnet.db.id
  private_dns_zone_id    = azurerm_private_dns_zone.pg.id
}

# ─── Cosmos DB (NoSQL) ────────────────────────
resource "azurerm_cosmosdb_account" "main" {
  name                = "${var.project}-cosmos"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  offer_type          = "Standard"
  kind                = "GlobalDocumentDB"

  consistency_policy {
    consistency_level = "Session"
  }

  geo_location {
    location          = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
    failover_priority = 0
  }

  geo_location {
    location          = "West US"
    failover_priority = 1
  }
}

48.6 Azure Active Directory Integration

IDENTITY
📚 Official Documentation: AzureAD Provider

Azure Active Directory (now Microsoft Entra ID) manages identities and access. Terraform uses the separate azuread provider for AAD resources and can assign RBAC roles with the azurerm provider.

# AzureAD provider (separate from azurerm)
terraform {
  required_providers {
    azuread = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azuread"
      version = "~> 2.47"
    }
  }
}

# Create an App Registration (Service Principal)
resource "azuread_application" "api" {
  display_name = "${var.project}-api-app"
}

resource "azuread_service_principal" "api" {
  client_id = azuread_application.api.client_id
}

# Assign RBAC role to Service Principal
resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "api_contributor" {
  scope                = azurerm_resource_group.main.id
  role_definition_name = "Contributor"
  principal_id         = azuread_service_principal.api.object_id
}

# Managed Identity + Role Assignment
resource "azurerm_user_assigned_identity" "app" {
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  name                = "${var.project}-app-identity"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
}

resource "azurerm_role_assignment" "app_storage" {
  scope                = azurerm_storage_account.main.id
  role_definition_name = "Storage Blob Data Contributor"
  principal_id         = azurerm_user_assigned_identity.app.principal_id
}

48.7 Azure/AWS Comparison for Terraform

REFERENCE
📌 Category: Cross-Cloud Comparison Guide

This comparison maps AWS Terraform resources to their Azure equivalents — helping AWS-experienced developers quickly adopt the Azure provider.

Category AWS Resource Azure Resource
Container—azurerm_resource_group
VPC/VNetaws_vpcazurerm_virtual_network
Subnetsaws_subnetazurerm_subnet
Firewallaws_security_groupazurerm_network_security_group
VMsaws_instanceazurerm_linux_virtual_machine
Storageaws_s3_bucketazurerm_storage_account
Serverlessaws_lambda_functionazurerm_linux_function_app
Web Appsaws_elastic_beanstalkazurerm_linux_web_app
Kubernetesaws_eks_clusterazurerm_kubernetes_cluster
SQL DBaws_db_instance (RDS)azurerm_mssql_database
NoSQLaws_dynamodb_tableazurerm_cosmosdb_account
Secretsaws_secretsmanager_secretazurerm_key_vault_secret
DNSaws_route53_zoneazurerm_dns_zone
CDNaws_cloudfront_distributionazurerm_cdn_endpoint
IAMaws_iam_roleazurerm_role_assignment
 
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              KEY DIFFERENCES: AWS vs AZURE TERRAFORM               │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  RESOURCE GROUPING                                                │
│  AWS:   Resources exist independently (tagged for grouping)       │
│  Azure: ALL resources MUST belong to a Resource Group             │
│                                                                   │
│  PROVIDER FEATURES                                                │
│  AWS:   No features block needed                                  │
│  Azure: Mandatory features {} block (even if empty)               │
│                                                                   │
│  NETWORKING                                                       │
│  AWS:   VPC → Subnet → Route Table → NAT/IGW                     │
│  Azure: VNet → Subnet → NSG → Route Table                        │
│  Azure: NSGs have explicit priority numbers (100-4096)            │
│                                                                   │
│  IDENTITY                                                         │
│  AWS:   IAM Roles + Policies (same provider)                      │
│  Azure: azuread provider (separate) + azurerm role assignments    │
│                                                                   │
│  NAMING                                                           │
│  AWS:   Most resources auto-generate unique IDs                   │
│  Azure: Storage accounts, Key Vaults require globally unique names│
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 For AWS Developers: The biggest paradigm shift is Resource Groups. In AWS, resources float independently with tags for organization. In Azure, every single resource must be placed in a resource group. Destroying a resource group destroys everything inside it. Design your resource group strategy carefully.
🌐 🔴 📊 ⚙️
CHAPTER 49

Google Cloud Provider

google Compute Engine VPC GKE BigQuery Cloud Functions
6Sections
13Subsections
25+Examples
🔴GCP
PART 14 INTERMEDIATE ★★★★☆

The Google Cloud provider enables Terraform to manage GCP infrastructure. GCP's project-based organization, strong Kubernetes integration (GKE), and data analytics services (BigQuery) make it a compelling multi-cloud option. This chapter covers provider setup, core resources, GKE, Cloud Functions, BigQuery, and IAM.

Learning Objectives

  • Configure the Google provider with service account authentication
  • Deploy Compute Engine instances, VPC networks, and Cloud Storage
  • Provision GKE clusters with node pools and auto-scaling
  • Create Cloud Functions and Cloud SQL instances
  • Build BigQuery datasets and tables for analytics
  • Manage IAM bindings and service accounts

Chapter Contents

49.1 Google Provider Setup 49.2 Core GCP Resources 49.3 Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) 49.4 Cloud Functions 49.5 BigQuery 49.6 IAM Configuration

49.1 Google Provider Setup

CONFIGURATION
📚 Official Documentation: Google Provider

The Google provider requires a GCP project, region, and authentication credentials. Unlike AWS (account-based) and Azure (subscription-based), GCP organizes resources in projects within an organization hierarchy.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    GCP RESOURCE HIERARCHY                              │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  Organization (example.com)                                           │
│  └── Folder: "Engineering"                                            │
│      ├── Project: "prod-web-app" (project-id)                         │
│      │   ├── google_compute_instance.web                              │
│      │   ├── google_sql_database_instance.db                          │
│      │   └── google_container_cluster.gke                             │
│      ├── Project: "prod-data" (project-id)                            │
│      │   ├── google_bigquery_dataset.analytics                        │
│      │   └── google_storage_bucket.datalake                           │
│      └── Project: "shared-services" (project-id)                      │
│          └── google_compute_network.shared_vpc                        │
│                                                                       │
│  Key Difference from AWS:                                             │
│  AWS  → 1 Account = 1 boundary (use Organizations for multi)         │
│  GCP  → Multiple Projects in 1 Organization (natural multi-tenancy)  │
│  Azure → Subscriptions under a Tenant                                │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
49.1.1 google Provider Configuration

Basic provider setup requires project ID and region. The google-beta provider gives access to preview features.

# versions.tf
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"
  
  required_providers {
    google = {
      source  = "hashicorp/google"
      version = "~> 5.20"
    }
    google-beta = {
      source  = "hashicorp/google-beta"
      version = "~> 5.20"
    }
  }
  
  backend "gcs" {
    bucket = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    prefix = "production"
  }
}

# Standard provider
provider "google" {
  project = var.project_id
  region  = var.region       # e.g., "us-central1"
  zone    = var.zone         # e.g., "us-central1-a"
}

# Beta provider for preview features
provider "google-beta" {
  project = var.project_id
  region  = var.region
}
49.1.2 Authentication (Service Account)

GCP authentication methods, from local development to CI/CD.

Method Use Case How
User CredentialsLocal devgcloud auth application-default login
Service Account KeyCI/CDJSON key file → GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS
Workload Identity (OIDC)GitHub ActionsFederated identity (no keys)
Attached Service AccountGCE/GKE runnersAuto-detected on GCP VMs
 
# Local development
gcloud auth application-default login

# Service Account key (CI/CD)
export GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/service-account-key.json"
# OR
export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="/path/to/key.json"

# In provider block (not recommended — use env vars)
provider "google" {
  credentials = file("service-account.json")
  project     = "my-project-id"
  region      = "us-central1"
}
49.1.3 Project and Region

GCP resources are scoped to projects and deployed to regions/zones. Terraform can enable required APIs automatically.

# Enable required GCP APIs
resource "google_project_service" "apis" {
  for_each = toset([
    "compute.googleapis.com",
    "container.googleapis.com",
    "sqladmin.googleapis.com",
    "bigquery.googleapis.com",
    "cloudfunctions.googleapis.com",
    "iam.googleapis.com",
  ])
  
  project = var.project_id
  service = each.value

  disable_dependent_services = false
  disable_on_destroy         = false
}

# Multi-region pattern with provider aliases
provider "google" {
  alias   = "us"
  project = var.project_id
  region  = "us-central1"
}

provider "google" {
  alias   = "eu"
  project = var.project_id
  region  = "europe-west1"
}
💡 GCP API Enablement: Unlike AWS (all services available by default), GCP requires APIs to be enabled per-project before resources can be created. Use google_project_service to enable them via Terraform, or gcloud services enable compute.googleapis.com manually.

49.2 Core GCP Resources

INFRASTRUCTURE
📚 Official Documentation: Google Resources

Core GCP resources include Compute Engine (VMs), VPC Networks, Cloud Storage (GCS), and Cloud SQL — the foundational building blocks of any GCP deployment.

49.2.1 Compute Engine

Compute Engine instances are GCP's virtual machines — equivalent to AWS EC2 and Azure VMs.

# Compute Engine Instance
resource "google_compute_instance" "web" {
  name         = "${var.project_name}-web"
  machine_type = "e2-medium"      # 2 vCPU, 4GB RAM
  zone         = "us-central1-a"
  
  boot_disk {
    initialize_params {
      image = "ubuntu-os-cloud/ubuntu-2204-lts"
      size  = 20    # GB
      type  = "pd-ssd"
    }
  }

  network_interface {
    network    = google_compute_network.main.id
    subnetwork = google_compute_subnetwork.app.id
    
    access_config {
      # Ephemeral public IP (omit for private-only)
    }
  }

  metadata = {
    ssh-keys = "ubuntu:${file("~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub")}"
  }

  metadata_startup_script = <<-EOF
    #!/bin/bash
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y python3-pip nginx
  EOF

  service_account {
    email  = google_service_account.web.email
    scopes = ["cloud-platform"]
  }

  tags = ["http-server", "https-server"]

  labels = {
    environment = var.environment
    managed_by  = "terraform"
  }
}
49.2.2 VPC Networks

GCP VPCs are global (not regional like AWS). Subnets, however, are regional. GCP also supports Shared VPC for multi-project networking.

# VPC Network (global in GCP)
resource "google_compute_network" "main" {
  name                    = "${var.project_name}-vpc"
  auto_create_subnetworks = false   # Custom mode (recommended)
  routing_mode            = "GLOBAL"
}

# Subnets (regional)
resource "google_compute_subnetwork" "app" {
  name          = "${var.project_name}-app-subnet"
  ip_cidr_range = "10.0.1.0/24"
  region        = var.region
  network       = google_compute_network.main.id

  secondary_ip_range {
    range_name    = "pods"
    ip_cidr_range = "10.1.0.0/16"
  }

  secondary_ip_range {
    range_name    = "services"
    ip_cidr_range = "10.2.0.0/20"
  }

  private_ip_google_access = true
}

# Firewall Rules (GCP equivalent of Security Groups)
resource "google_compute_firewall" "allow_http" {
  name    = "${var.project_name}-allow-http"
  network = google_compute_network.main.name

  allow {
    protocol = "tcp"
    ports    = ["80", "443"]
  }

  source_ranges = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  target_tags   = ["http-server", "https-server"]
}

# Cloud NAT (for private instances to reach internet)
resource "google_compute_router" "nat" {
  name    = "${var.project_name}-router"
  region  = var.region
  network = google_compute_network.main.id
}

resource "google_compute_router_nat" "nat" {
  name                               = "${var.project_name}-nat"
  router                             = google_compute_router.nat.name
  region                             = var.region
  nat_ip_allocate_option             = "AUTO_ONLY"
  source_subnetwork_ip_ranges_to_nat = "ALL_SUBNETWORKS_ALL_IP_RANGES"
}
49.2.3 Cloud Storage

Cloud Storage (GCS) is GCP's object storage — equivalent to AWS S3.

# Cloud Storage Bucket
resource "google_storage_bucket" "data" {
  name          = "${var.project_id}-app-data"    # Globally unique
  location      = "US"
  force_destroy = false
  
  storage_class = "STANDARD"
  
  uniform_bucket_level_access = true

  versioning {
    enabled = true
  }

  lifecycle_rule {
    condition {
      age = 90     # days
    }
    action {
      type          = "SetStorageClass"
      storage_class = "NEARLINE"
    }
  }

  lifecycle_rule {
    condition {
      age = 365
    }
    action {
      type = "Delete"
    }
  }

  labels = {
    environment = var.environment
  }
}
49.2.4 Cloud SQL

Cloud SQL provides managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, and SQL Server — equivalent to AWS RDS.

# Cloud SQL PostgreSQL (for Django/FastAPI)
resource "google_sql_database_instance" "main" {
  name             = "${var.project_name}-postgres"
  database_version = "POSTGRES_16"
  region           = var.region

  settings {
    tier              = "db-custom-2-4096"  # 2 vCPU, 4GB RAM
    availability_type = "REGIONAL"          # HA with standby
    disk_size         = 50
    disk_type         = "PD_SSD"
    disk_autoresize   = true

    backup_configuration {
      enabled                        = true
      point_in_time_recovery_enabled = true
      start_time                     = "03:00"
      transaction_log_retention_days = 7
    }

    ip_configuration {
      ipv4_enabled                                  = false
      private_network                               = google_compute_network.main.id
      enable_private_path_for_google_cloud_services = true
    }

    database_flags {
      name  = "max_connections"
      value = "200"
    }
  }

  deletion_protection = true
}

resource "google_sql_database" "app" {
  name     = "appdb"
  instance = google_sql_database_instance.main.name
}

resource "google_sql_user" "app" {
  name     = "app_user"
  instance = google_sql_database_instance.main.name
  password = var.db_password
}

49.3 Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)

CONTAINERS
📚 Official Documentation: GKE Cluster

GKE is Google's managed Kubernetes service — widely considered the most mature managed K8s offering. GKE Autopilot manages node pools automatically, while Standard mode gives full control.

# GKE Cluster (Standard mode)
resource "google_container_cluster" "primary" {
  name     = "${var.project_name}-gke"
  location = var.region           # Regional cluster (HA)

  # Use separately managed node pool
  remove_default_node_pool = true
  initial_node_count       = 1

  network    = google_compute_network.main.name
  subnetwork = google_compute_subnetwork.app.name

  ip_allocation_policy {
    cluster_secondary_range_name  = "pods"
    services_secondary_range_name = "services"
  }

  private_cluster_config {
    enable_private_nodes    = true
    enable_private_endpoint = false
    master_ipv4_cidr_block  = "172.16.0.0/28"
  }

  master_auth {
    client_certificate_config {
      issue_client_certificate = false
    }
  }

  release_channel {
    channel = "REGULAR"
  }

  workload_identity_config {
    workload_pool = "${var.project_id}.svc.id.goog"
  }
}

# Separately managed node pool
resource "google_container_node_pool" "app" {
  name       = "app-pool"
  location   = var.region
  cluster    = google_container_cluster.primary.name
  node_count = 2

  autoscaling {
    min_node_count = 2
    max_node_count = 10
  }

  node_config {
    preemptible  = false
    machine_type = "e2-standard-4"   # 4 vCPU, 16GB
    disk_size_gb = 50
    disk_type    = "pd-ssd"

    oauth_scopes = [
      "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/cloud-platform"
    ]

    labels = {
      environment = var.environment
      pool        = "application"
    }
  }

  management {
    auto_repair  = true
    auto_upgrade = true
  }
}

49.4 Cloud Functions

SERVERLESS
📚 Official Documentation: Cloud Functions v2

Cloud Functions is GCP's serverless compute — equivalent to AWS Lambda and Azure Functions. Gen2 functions are built on Cloud Run and support longer execution times.

# Cloud Function v2 (Gen2 — built on Cloud Run)
resource "google_cloudfunctions2_function" "processor" {
  name     = "${var.project_name}-processor"
  location = var.region

  build_config {
    runtime     = "python311"
    entry_point = "handle_event"
    
    source {
      storage_source {
        bucket = google_storage_bucket.functions.name
        object = google_storage_bucket_object.function_zip.name
      }
    }
  }

  service_config {
    max_instance_count = 10
    min_instance_count = 0
    available_memory   = "256M"
    timeout_seconds    = 60
    
    environment_variables = {
      DATABASE_URL = "postgresql://${google_sql_user.app.name}@/${google_sql_database.app.name}"
      PROJECT_ID   = var.project_id
    }
    
    service_account_email = google_service_account.functions.email
  }

  event_trigger {
    trigger_region = var.region
    event_type     = "google.cloud.storage.object.v1.finalized"
    
    event_filters {
      attribute = "bucket"
      value     = google_storage_bucket.data.name
    }
  }
}

# Upload function source code
resource "google_storage_bucket_object" "function_zip" {
  name   = "functions/processor-${filemd5("../functions/processor.zip")}.zip"
  bucket = google_storage_bucket.functions.name
  source = "../functions/processor.zip"
}

49.5 BigQuery

ANALYTICS
📚 Official Documentation: BigQuery

BigQuery is GCP's serverless data warehouse — no equivalent in AWS (closest: Redshift/Athena). It's a key differentiator for analytics-heavy workloads.

# BigQuery Dataset
resource "google_bigquery_dataset" "analytics" {
  dataset_id  = "app_analytics"
  location    = "US"
  description = "Application analytics and event data"
  
  default_table_expiration_ms     = 7776000000   # 90 days
  default_partition_expiration_ms = 15552000000   # 180 days

  labels = {
    environment = var.environment
  }
}

# BigQuery Table with schema
resource "google_bigquery_table" "events" {
  dataset_id = google_bigquery_dataset.analytics.dataset_id
  table_id   = "user_events"

  time_partitioning {
    type  = "DAY"
    field = "event_timestamp"
  }

  clustering = ["event_type", "user_id"]

  schema = jsonencode([
    { name = "event_id",       type = "STRING",    mode = "REQUIRED" },
    { name = "user_id",        type = "STRING",    mode = "REQUIRED" },
    { name = "event_type",     type = "STRING",    mode = "REQUIRED" },
    { name = "event_timestamp",type = "TIMESTAMP", mode = "REQUIRED" },
    { name = "properties",     type = "JSON",      mode = "NULLABLE" },
    { name = "session_id",     type = "STRING",    mode = "NULLABLE" },
  ])

  deletion_protection = true
}

# Grant access to the dataset
resource "google_bigquery_dataset_iam_member" "viewer" {
  dataset_id = google_bigquery_dataset.analytics.dataset_id
  role       = "roles/bigquery.dataViewer"
  member     = "serviceAccount:${google_service_account.analytics.email}"
}

49.6 IAM Configuration

SECURITY
📚 Official Documentation: GCP IAM

GCP IAM uses a binding model: bind a member (user, service account, group) to a role at a specific scope (organization, folder, project, or resource).

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              GCP IAM MODEL                                         │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  IAM Binding = Member + Role + Scope                              │
│                                                                   │
│  RESOURCE TYPES FOR IAM:                                          │
│  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────                │
│  google_project_iam_member        → Project-level                 │
│  google_project_iam_binding       → Project-level (authoritative) │
│  google_storage_bucket_iam_member → Bucket-level                  │
│  google_bigquery_dataset_iam_member → Dataset-level               │
│                                                                   │
│  ⚠️ _binding = authoritative (removes other members)              │
│  ✅ _member  = additive (safe to use alongside other bindings)    │
│                                                                   │
│  Best Practice: Use _member for Terraform-managed access          │
│                 Use _binding only for fully Terraform-managed roles│
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Service Account
resource "google_service_account" "app" {
  account_id   = "${var.project_name}-app-sa"
  display_name = "Application Service Account"
  description  = "Used by the application for GCP API access"
}

# Project-level IAM (additive — safe)
resource "google_project_iam_member" "app_roles" {
  for_each = toset([
    "roles/cloudsql.client",
    "roles/storage.objectViewer",
    "roles/logging.logWriter",
    "roles/monitoring.metricWriter",
  ])
  
  project = var.project_id
  role    = each.value
  member  = "serviceAccount:${google_service_account.app.email}"
}

# Workload Identity binding (GKE → GCP Service Account)
resource "google_service_account_iam_member" "workload_identity" {
  service_account_id = google_service_account.app.name
  role               = "roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser"
  member             = "serviceAccount:${var.project_id}.svc.id.goog[${var.k8s_namespace}/${var.k8s_service_account}]"
}
💡 IAM Best Practice: Always use google_*_iam_member (additive) instead of google_*_iam_binding (authoritative) unless you want Terraform to be the sole manager of that role. Bindings will remove any members not listed in Terraform, which can break manual access grants.
☸️ 🐳 📦 🔧
CHAPTER 50

Kubernetes Provider

kubernetes Namespaces Deployments Services RBAC StatefulSets DaemonSets CRDs HPA Storage
7Sections
25Subsections
50+Examples
☸️K8s
PART 14 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

The Kubernetes provider allows Terraform to manage Kubernetes resources declaratively — namespaces, deployments, services, RBAC, storage, stateful workloads, and custom resources. While kubectl and Helm are the traditional K8s tools, Terraform provides a unified workflow for provisioning both the cluster (EKS/AKS/GKE) and its workloads in a single plan. This chapter is your comprehensive reference for every major Kubernetes resource type in Terraform.

Learning Objectives

  • Configure the Kubernetes provider with multiple authentication methods and multi-cluster aliases
  • Create namespaces, deployments, services, config maps, secrets, and ingress
  • Implement RBAC with service accounts, roles, role bindings, and IRSA/Workload Identity
  • Manage persistent storage with PVCs, StorageClasses, and StatefulSets
  • Deploy advanced workloads: DaemonSets, Jobs, CronJobs, and HPAs
  • Use kubernetes_manifest for CRDs and operator-managed resources
  • Apply network policies for pod-to-pod security isolation
  • Understand when to use Terraform vs kubectl vs Helm and avoid common pitfalls

Chapter Contents

50.1 Kubernetes Provider Setup 50.2 Core Resources 50.3 RBAC & Service Accounts 50.4 Storage & Stateful Workloads 50.5 Advanced Workloads 50.6 kubernetes_manifest & CRDs 50.7 Best Practices & Patterns

50.1 Kubernetes Provider Setup

CONFIGURATION
📚 Official Documentation: Kubernetes Provider

The Kubernetes provider connects to a cluster's API server. Authentication varies by cluster type (EKS, AKS, GKE, or self-managed). The provider must be configured after the cluster is created.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TERRAFORM + KUBERNETES WORKFLOW                           │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  STEP 1: Provision Cluster (aws/azurerm/google provider)              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  aws_eks_cluster / azurerm_kubernetes_cluster / gke_cluster     │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ cluster endpoint + auth token           │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  STEP 2: Configure K8s Provider                                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  provider "kubernetes" {                                        │  │
│  │    host                   = cluster.endpoint                    │  │
│  │    cluster_ca_certificate = cluster.ca_cert                     │  │
│  │    token                  = auth.token                          │  │
│  │  }                                                              │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ K8s API calls                           │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  STEP 3: Deploy K8s Resources                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  kubernetes_namespace / kubernetes_deployment / kubernetes_service│ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
50.1.1 Authentication Methods

Configure the provider differently based on your cluster type.

# ─── EKS Authentication ────────────────────────
data "aws_eks_cluster" "main" {
  name = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
}

data "aws_eks_cluster_auth" "main" {
  name = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
}

provider "kubernetes" {
  host                   = data.aws_eks_cluster.main.endpoint
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(data.aws_eks_cluster.main.certificate_authority[0].data)
  token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
}

# ─── AKS Authentication ───────────────────────
provider "kubernetes" {
  host                   = azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].host
  client_certificate     = base64decode(azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].client_certificate)
  client_key             = base64decode(azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].client_key)
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].cluster_ca_certificate)
}

# ─── GKE Authentication ───────────────────────
data "google_client_config" "default" {}

provider "kubernetes" {
  host                   = "https://${google_container_cluster.primary.endpoint}"
  token                  = data.google_client_config.default.access_token
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(google_container_cluster.primary.master_auth[0].cluster_ca_certificate)
}
50.1.2 Kubeconfig

For local development or self-managed clusters, the provider can read from a kubeconfig file.

# Kubeconfig-based authentication
provider "kubernetes" {
  config_path    = "~/.kube/config"
  config_context = "my-cluster-context"
}

# Or use specific kubeconfig file
provider "kubernetes" {
  config_path = "${path.module}/kubeconfig.yaml"
}
50.1.3 In-Cluster Configuration

When Terraform runs inside a Kubernetes pod (e.g., Atlantis, CI runner on K8s), it can auto-detect cluster credentials.

# In-cluster configuration (auto-detect from pod's service account)
provider "kubernetes" {
  # No configuration needed — reads from:
  # /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
  # /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt
  # KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST / KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT
}
💡 Two-Stage Pattern: When creating a cluster AND deploying K8s resources in the same Terraform config, use depends_on to ensure the cluster is fully ready before the K8s provider tries to connect. Better yet, separate cluster provisioning and workload deployment into different Terraform workspaces/states.
50.1.4 Provider Aliases (Multi-Cluster)

Manage resources across multiple Kubernetes clusters in a single Terraform configuration using provider aliases.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MULTI-CLUSTER PROVIDER ALIASES                        │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  provider "kubernetes" {        ← default (production cluster)    │
│    alias = "prod"                                                 │
│    host  = module.eks_prod.endpoint                               │
│  }                                                                │
│                                                                   │
│  provider "kubernetes" {        ← staging cluster                 │
│    alias = "staging"                                              │
│    host  = module.eks_staging.endpoint                            │
│  }                                                                │
│                                                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────────┐            │
│  │  PROD Cluster (EKS)  │      │  STAGING Cluster     │           │
│  │                      │      │                      │           │
│  │  ns: app-prod        │      │  ns: app-staging     │           │
│  │  deploy: api (3)     │      │  deploy: api (1)     │           │
│  │  svc: api-lb         │      │  svc: api-clusterip  │           │
│  └─────────────────────┘      └─────────────────────┘            │
│                                                                   │
│  resource "kubernetes_namespace" "prod" {                         │
│    provider = kubernetes.prod                                     │
│  }                                                                │
│  resource "kubernetes_namespace" "staging" {                      │
│    provider = kubernetes.staging                                  │
│  }                                                                │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ─── Multi-Cluster Provider Configuration ─────

# Production cluster
provider "kubernetes" {
  alias = "prod"

  host                   = module.eks_prod.cluster_endpoint
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(module.eks_prod.cluster_ca_data)
  token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.prod.token
}

# Staging cluster
provider "kubernetes" {
  alias = "staging"

  host                   = module.eks_staging.cluster_endpoint
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(module.eks_staging.cluster_ca_data)
  token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.staging.token
}

# Deploy to production cluster
resource "kubernetes_namespace" "prod_app" {
  provider = kubernetes.prod

  metadata {
    name = "my-app"
    labels = { environment = "production" }
  }
}

# Deploy to staging cluster
resource "kubernetes_namespace" "staging_app" {
  provider = kubernetes.staging

  metadata {
    name = "my-app"
    labels = { environment = "staging" }
  }
}

# ─── Module with provider pass-through ─────
module "app_prod" {
  source    = "./modules/k8s-app"
  providers = { kubernetes = kubernetes.prod }

  app_name    = "my-app"
  environment = "production"
  replicas    = 3
}

module "app_staging" {
  source    = "./modules/k8s-app"
  providers = { kubernetes = kubernetes.staging }

  app_name    = "my-app"
  environment = "staging"
  replicas    = 1
}
Auth Method Best For Token Lifetime Security
EKS TokenAWS clusters15 min (auto-refresh)★★★★★
AKS Client CertAzure clustersCert expiry★★★★☆
GKE OAuth TokenGCP clusters1 hour (auto-refresh)★★★★★
KubeconfigLocal / self-managedVaries★★★☆☆
In-Cluster SACI runners on K8sAuto-rotated★★★★★
exec PluginCustom auth (Vault)Custom★★★★☆

50.2 Core Resources

WORKLOADS
📚 Official Documentation: K8s Resources

Terraform can manage all standard Kubernetes resources. Each resource maps directly to a K8s API object — the same objects you'd define in YAML manifests.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              KUBERNETES RESOURCE HIERARCHY IN TERRAFORM                 │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  CLUSTER-LEVEL (no namespace)                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  kubernetes_namespace          → Logical isolation              │  │
│  │  kubernetes_cluster_role       → Cluster-wide permissions       │  │
│  │  kubernetes_cluster_role_binding → Bind cluster roles           │  │
│  │  kubernetes_storage_class      → Dynamic volume provisioning    │  │
│  │  kubernetes_persistent_volume  → Cluster storage                │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
│  NAMESPACE-LEVEL (scoped to namespace)                                │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  WORKLOADS                    │  CONFIG                         │  │
│  │  ├── kubernetes_deployment    │  ├── kubernetes_config_map      │  │
│  │  ├── kubernetes_stateful_set  │  ├── kubernetes_secret          │  │
│  │  ├── kubernetes_daemon_set    │  └── kubernetes_resource_quota  │  │
│  │  ├── kubernetes_job           │                                 │  │
│  │  └── kubernetes_cron_job      │  NETWORKING                     │  │
│  │                               │  ├── kubernetes_service         │  │
│  │  RBAC                         │  ├── kubernetes_ingress_v1      │  │
│  │  ├── kubernetes_service_account│  └── kubernetes_network_policy │  │
│  │  ├── kubernetes_role          │                                 │  │
│  │  └── kubernetes_role_binding  │  STORAGE                        │  │
│  │                               │  └── kubernetes_persistent_     │  │
│  │                               │      volume_claim               │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
│  GENERIC (any K8s object)                                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  kubernetes_manifest → Apply any K8s YAML (CRDs, operators)    │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Resource Terraform Type K8s API Kind Scope
Namespacekubernetes_namespaceNamespaceCluster
Deploymentkubernetes_deploymentDeploymentNamespaced
StatefulSetkubernetes_stateful_setStatefulSetNamespaced
DaemonSetkubernetes_daemon_setDaemonSetNamespaced
Servicekubernetes_serviceServiceNamespaced
Ingresskubernetes_ingress_v1IngressNamespaced
ConfigMapkubernetes_config_mapConfigMapNamespaced
Secretkubernetes_secretSecretNamespaced
ServiceAccountkubernetes_service_accountServiceAccountNamespaced
Rolekubernetes_roleRoleNamespaced
PVCkubernetes_persistent_volume_claimPersistentVolumeClaimNamespaced
HPAkubernetes_horizontal_pod_autoscaler_v2HorizontalPodAutoscalerNamespaced
NetworkPolicykubernetes_network_policyNetworkPolicyNamespaced
Any Objectkubernetes_manifestAny KindVaries
50.2.1 Namespaces

Namespaces provide logical isolation within a cluster — separate environments, teams, or applications.

# Namespace with labels and annotations
resource "kubernetes_namespace" "app" {
  metadata {
    name = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"
    
    labels = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/managed-by" = "terraform"
      "environment"                  = var.environment
    }
    
    annotations = {
      "contact" = "platform-team@company.com"
    }
  }
}

# Resource Quota for the namespace
resource "kubernetes_resource_quota" "app" {
  metadata {
    name      = "compute-quota"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    hard = {
      "requests.cpu"    = "4"
      "requests.memory" = "8Gi"
      "limits.cpu"      = "8"
      "limits.memory"   = "16Gi"
      "pods"            = "20"
    }
  }
}
50.2.2 Deployments

Deployments manage replica sets and rolling updates for stateless applications.

# Deployment for a Django/FastAPI application
resource "kubernetes_deployment" "api" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-api"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
    
    labels = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/name"    = "${var.project}-api"
      "app.kubernetes.io/version" = var.app_version
    }
  }

  spec {
    replicas = var.replicas

    selector {
      match_labels = {
        "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-api"
      }
    }

    template {
      metadata {
        labels = {
          "app.kubernetes.io/name"    = "${var.project}-api"
          "app.kubernetes.io/version" = var.app_version
        }
      }

      spec {
        service_account_name = kubernetes_service_account.app.metadata[0].name
        
        container {
          name  = "api"
          image = "${var.ecr_repo}:${var.app_version}"

          port {
            container_port = 8000
            name           = "http"
          }

          env {
            name = "DATABASE_URL"
            value_from {
              secret_key_ref {
                name = kubernetes_secret.app.metadata[0].name
                key  = "database_url"
              }
            }
          }

          env_from {
            config_map_ref {
              name = kubernetes_config_map.app.metadata[0].name
            }
          }

          resources {
            requests = {
              cpu    = "250m"
              memory = "256Mi"
            }
            limits = {
              cpu    = "500m"
              memory = "512Mi"
            }
          }

          liveness_probe {
            http_get {
              path = "/health"
              port = 8000
            }
            initial_delay_seconds = 15
            period_seconds        = 10
          }

          readiness_probe {
            http_get {
              path = "/ready"
              port = 8000
            }
            initial_delay_seconds = 5
            period_seconds        = 5
          }
        }
      }
    }

    strategy {
      type = "RollingUpdate"
      rolling_update {
        max_surge       = "25%"
        max_unavailable = "0"
      }
    }
  }
}
50.2.3 Services

Services expose deployments to other pods (ClusterIP), the VPC (NodePort), or the internet (LoadBalancer).

# ClusterIP Service (internal)
resource "kubernetes_service" "api" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-api"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    selector = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-api"
    }

    port {
      port        = 80
      target_port = 8000
      protocol    = "TCP"
    }

    type = "ClusterIP"
  }
}

# LoadBalancer Service (external — creates cloud LB)
resource "kubernetes_service" "api_public" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-api-public"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
    
    annotations = {
      # AWS-specific annotations for NLB
      "service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type"   = "nlb"
      "service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-scheme" = "internet-facing"
    }
  }

  spec {
    selector = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-api"
    }

    port {
      port        = 443
      target_port = 8000
    }

    type = "LoadBalancer"
  }
}
50.2.4 ConfigMaps

ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs, injected into pods as environment variables or mounted files.

# ConfigMap for application settings
resource "kubernetes_config_map" "app" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-config"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  data = {
    DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE = "config.settings.production"
    ALLOWED_HOSTS          = var.allowed_hosts
    LOG_LEVEL              = var.environment == "prod" ? "WARNING" : "DEBUG"
    CACHE_TTL              = "300"
    WORKERS                = "4"
  }
}

# ConfigMap from file
resource "kubernetes_config_map" "nginx" {
  metadata {
    name      = "nginx-config"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  data = {
    "nginx.conf" = file("${path.module}/configs/nginx.conf")
  }
}
50.2.5 Secrets

Kubernetes Secrets store sensitive data. Note: they're base64-encoded (not encrypted) — use external secret managers for production.

# Secret for database credentials
resource "kubernetes_secret" "app" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-secrets"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  data = {
    database_url = var.database_url
    secret_key   = var.django_secret_key
    api_key      = var.external_api_key
  }

  type = "Opaque"
}

# Docker registry secret (for pulling from private registries)
resource "kubernetes_secret" "registry" {
  metadata {
    name      = "registry-credentials"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  type = "kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson"

  data = {
    ".dockerconfigjson" = jsonencode({
      auths = {
        "${var.registry_url}" = {
          auth = base64encode("${var.registry_user}:${var.registry_password}")
        }
      }
    })
  }
}
50.2.6 Ingress

Ingress exposes HTTP/HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to services inside. Requires an Ingress Controller (nginx, ALB, Traefik).

# Ingress with TLS
resource "kubernetes_ingress_v1" "api" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-ingress"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
    
    annotations = {
      "kubernetes.io/ingress.class"                = "nginx"
      "cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer"             = "letsencrypt-prod"
      "nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rate-limit"     = "100"
      "nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect"   = "true"
    }
  }

  spec {
    tls {
      hosts       = [var.domain_name]
      secret_name = "${var.project}-tls"
    }

    rule {
      host = var.domain_name

      http {
        path {
          path      = "/api"
          path_type = "Prefix"

          backend {
            service {
              name = kubernetes_service.api.metadata[0].name
              port {
                number = 80
              }
            }
          }
        }

        path {
          path      = "/"
          path_type = "Prefix"

          backend {
            service {
              name = kubernetes_service.frontend.metadata[0].name
              port {
                number = 80
              }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

50.3 RBAC & Service Accounts

SECURITY
📌 Category: Access Control & Identity

Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is the foundation of Kubernetes security. Terraform can declaratively manage service accounts, roles, and bindings to enforce least-privilege access across your cluster.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              KUBERNETES RBAC MODEL                                     │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│                WHO                 WHAT                WHERE           │
│           ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐    │
│           │   Subject   │     │     Rules      │  │   Scope      │    │
│           │             │     │                │  │              │    │
│           │ • User      │     │ • apiGroups    │  │ • Namespace  │    │
│           │ • Group     │────▶│ • resources    │──│   (Role)     │    │
│           │ • ServiceAcnt │   │ • verbs        │  │ • Cluster    │    │
│           └────────────┘     │   (get,list,   │  │   (ClusterRole│   │
│                  │            │    create,etc) │  └──────────────┘    │
│                  │            └────────────────┘                      │
│                  │                    │                               │
│                  └────────┬──────────┘                               │
│                           │                                          │
│                           ▼                                          │
