│               ┌──────────────────────┐                               │
│               │     Binding          │                               │
│               │                      │                               │
│               │  RoleBinding         │ → namespace-scoped            │
│               │  ClusterRoleBinding  │ → cluster-wide                │
│               └──────────────────────┘                               │
│                                                                       │
│  FORMULA: Subject + Role + Binding = Access Granted                   │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
50.3.1 Service Accounts

Service accounts provide an identity for pods. Every pod runs as a service account — if not specified, it uses the default account (which you should always replace).

# Service account for API workload
resource "kubernetes_service_account" "api" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-api"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name

    labels = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/name"       = "${var.project}-api"
      "app.kubernetes.io/managed-by" = "terraform"
    }

    # IRSA annotation for AWS (see 50.3.4)
    annotations = {
      "eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn" = aws_iam_role.api_pod.arn
    }
  }

  # Don't auto-mount the default token (security best practice)
  automount_service_account_token = false
}

# Service account for background workers
resource "kubernetes_service_account" "worker" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-worker"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name

    annotations = {
      "eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn" = aws_iam_role.worker_pod.arn
    }
  }

  automount_service_account_token = true  # Workers need API access
}

# Reference in deployment
resource "kubernetes_deployment" "api" {
  # ...
  spec {
    template {
      spec {
        service_account_name            = kubernetes_service_account.api.metadata[0].name
        automount_service_account_token = true

        container {
          # ...
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
50.3.2 Roles & ClusterRoles

Roles define what actions are allowed on which resources. Roles are namespace-scoped; ClusterRoles are cluster-wide.

# ─── Namespace-scoped Role ─────────────────────
resource "kubernetes_role" "app_reader" {
  metadata {
    name      = "app-reader"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  # Read pods and logs
  rule {
    api_groups = [""]
    resources  = ["pods", "pods/log", "services", "endpoints"]
    verbs      = ["get", "list", "watch"]
  }

  # Read deployments
  rule {
    api_groups = ["apps"]
    resources  = ["deployments", "replicasets"]
    verbs      = ["get", "list", "watch"]
  }
}

# Full access role for the namespace
resource "kubernetes_role" "app_admin" {
  metadata {
    name      = "app-admin"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  rule {
    api_groups = ["", "apps", "batch", "networking.k8s.io"]
    resources  = ["*"]
    verbs      = ["*"]
  }
}

# ─── Cluster-scoped ClusterRole ────────────────
resource "kubernetes_cluster_role" "namespace_viewer" {
  metadata {
    name = "namespace-viewer"
    labels = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/managed-by" = "terraform"
    }
  }

  # View all namespaces
  rule {
    api_groups = [""]
    resources  = ["namespaces"]
    verbs      = ["get", "list", "watch"]
  }

  # View all nodes (for dashboards)
  rule {
    api_groups = [""]
    resources  = ["nodes"]
    verbs      = ["get", "list", "watch"]
  }
}

# ClusterRole for monitoring tools (Prometheus)
resource "kubernetes_cluster_role" "prometheus" {
  metadata {
    name = "prometheus-scraper"
  }

  rule {
    api_groups = [""]
    resources  = ["nodes", "nodes/proxy", "services", "endpoints", "pods"]
    verbs      = ["get", "list", "watch"]
  }

  rule {
    api_groups = [""]
    resources  = ["configmaps"]
    verbs      = ["get"]
  }

  rule {
    non_resource_urls = ["/metrics"]
    verbs             = ["get"]
  }
}
Type Terraform Resource Scope Use Case
Rolekubernetes_roleSingle namespaceApp team access to their namespace
ClusterRolekubernetes_cluster_roleAll namespacesMonitoring, node access, CRDs
RoleBindingkubernetes_role_bindingSingle namespaceGrant Role to user/SA in namespace
ClusterRoleBindingkubernetes_cluster_role_bindingAll namespacesGrant ClusterRole cluster-wide
50.3.3 RoleBindings & ClusterRoleBindings

Bindings connect subjects (users, groups, service accounts) to roles. Without a binding, a role has no effect.

# ─── Bind Role to ServiceAccount (namespace-scoped) ────
resource "kubernetes_role_binding" "api_reader" {
  metadata {
    name      = "api-reader-binding"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  role_ref {
    api_group = "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    kind      = "Role"
    name      = kubernetes_role.app_reader.metadata[0].name
  }

  # Bind to a service account
  subject {
    kind      = "ServiceAccount"
    name      = kubernetes_service_account.api.metadata[0].name
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }
}

# ─── Bind ClusterRole to Group (cluster-wide) ────
resource "kubernetes_cluster_role_binding" "dev_team_viewers" {
  metadata {
    name = "dev-team-viewer-binding"
  }

  role_ref {
    api_group = "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    kind      = "ClusterRole"
    name      = kubernetes_cluster_role.namespace_viewer.metadata[0].name
  }

  # Bind to an IAM group (EKS aws-auth mapped)
  subject {
    kind      = "Group"
    name      = "dev-team"
    api_group = "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
  }
}

# ─── Dynamic RBAC per team using for_each ────
variable "teams" {
  default = {
    frontend = { namespace = "frontend", role = "admin" }
    backend  = { namespace = "backend",  role = "admin" }
    qa       = { namespace = "staging",  role = "reader" }
  }
}

resource "kubernetes_role_binding" "team_access" {
  for_each = var.teams

  metadata {
    name      = "${each.key}-access"
    namespace = each.value.namespace
  }

  role_ref {
    api_group = "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    kind      = "Role"
    name      = each.value.role == "admin" ? "app-admin" : "app-reader"
  }

  subject {
    kind      = "Group"
    name      = each.key
    api_group = "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
  }
}
50.3.4 IRSA & Workload Identity

IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA on AWS) and Workload Identity (GCP/Azure) let pods assume cloud IAM roles without storing credentials.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) — AWS              │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  ┌──────────────┐    OIDC     ┌───────────────────┐                  │
│  │  K8s Service  │───────────▶│   AWS IAM Role     │                  │
│  │  Account      │  Federation│                    │                  │
│  │              │             │  Trust Policy:     │                  │
│  │  annotation:  │             │  "Allow SA 'api'  │                  │
│  │  eks.amazonaws│             │   in namespace X   │                  │
│  │  .com/role-arn│             │   to assume me"   │                  │
│  └──────┬───────┘             └────────┬──────────┘                  │
│         │                              │                              │
│         ▼                              ▼                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐             ┌───────────────────┐                  │
│  │  Pod gets     │             │  IAM Policy        │                  │
│  │  AWS creds    │             │  s3:GetObject      │                  │
│  │  via projected│             │  sqs:SendMessage   │                  │
│  │  token volume │             │  secretsmanager:*  │                  │
│  └──────────────┘             └───────────────────┘                  │
│                                                                       │
│  NO ACCESS KEYS! Token auto-rotated. Least-privilege per pod.        │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ─── AWS IRSA: Complete Setup ─────────────────

# 1. OIDC Provider (usually set up once per cluster)
data "tls_certificate" "eks" {
  url = aws_eks_cluster.main.identity[0].oidc[0].issuer
}

resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "eks" {
  client_id_list  = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
  thumbprint_list = [data.tls_certificate.eks.certificates[0].sha1_fingerprint]
  url             = aws_eks_cluster.main.identity[0].oidc[0].issuer
}

# 2. IAM Role with OIDC trust policy
resource "aws_iam_role" "api_pod" {
  name = "${var.project}-api-pod-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Federated = aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.eks.arn
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "${replace(aws_eks_cluster.main.identity[0].oidc[0].issuer, "https://", "")}:sub" = "system:serviceaccount:${var.namespace}:${var.project}-api"
          "${replace(aws_eks_cluster.main.identity[0].oidc[0].issuer, "https://", "")}:aud" = "sts.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

# 3. Attach IAM policies (what the pod can do in AWS)
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "api_s3" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.api_pod.name
  policy_arn = aws_iam_policy.api_s3_access.arn
}

resource "aws_iam_policy" "api_s3_access" {
  name = "${var.project}-api-s3-access"
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect   = "Allow"
      Action   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
      Resource = [
        aws_s3_bucket.media.arn,
        "${aws_s3_bucket.media.arn}/*"
      ]
    }]
  })
}

# 4. K8s Service Account with IRSA annotation
resource "kubernetes_service_account" "api" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-api"
    namespace = var.namespace
    annotations = {
      "eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn" = aws_iam_role.api_pod.arn
    }
  }
}

# ─── GCP Workload Identity ─────────────────────
resource "google_service_account" "api" {
  account_id   = "${var.project}-api"
  display_name = "API Pod Service Account"
}

resource "google_service_account_iam_binding" "api_workload_identity" {
  service_account_id = google_service_account.api.name
  role               = "roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser"

  members = [
    "serviceAccount:${var.gcp_project}.svc.id.goog[${var.namespace}/${var.project}-api]"
  ]
}

resource "kubernetes_service_account" "api_gke" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-api"
    namespace = var.namespace
    annotations = {
      "iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account" = google_service_account.api.email
    }
  }
}
💡 IRSA Best Practice: Every pod that accesses AWS services should have its own IAM role via IRSA. Never use node-level IAM roles (all pods on the node share permissions) or hardcoded credentials. IRSA provides per-pod, least-privilege access with auto-rotating tokens — the gold standard for EKS security.

50.4 Storage & Stateful Workloads

STORAGE
📌 Category: Persistent Storage & StatefulSets

Stateful workloads (databases, message queues, caches) need persistent storage that survives pod restarts. Terraform manages StorageClasses, PersistentVolumeClaims, and StatefulSets to provide durable, cloud-backed storage.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              KUBERNETES STORAGE STACK                                   │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  StorageClass (cluster-level)                                         │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com / disk.csi.azure.com / pd.csi.. │  │
│  │  reclaimPolicy: Retain / Delete                                 │  │
│  │  volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer                        │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ provisions                              │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  PersistentVolumeClaim (namespace-scoped)                             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  storageClassName: "fast-ssd"                                   │  │
│  │  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]                                 │  │
│  │  resources.requests.storage: "50Gi"                             │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ binds to                                │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  PersistentVolume (auto-provisioned by CSI driver)                    │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  AWS: EBS gp3 volume  │  Azure: Premium SSD  │  GCP: PD SSD   │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ mounted into                            │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  Pod (via volumeMounts)                                               │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  mountPath: /data/db  ← persistent across pod restarts         │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
50.4.1 StorageClass

StorageClasses define the "tier" of storage available in the cluster. They're cluster-scoped and tell K8s which CSI driver and parameters to use when provisioning volumes.

# ─── AWS EBS StorageClasses ────────────────────
resource "kubernetes_storage_class" "fast_ssd" {
  metadata {
    name = "fast-ssd"
    annotations = {
      "storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class" = "false"
    }
  }

  storage_provisioner = "ebs.csi.aws.com"
  reclaim_policy      = "Retain"        # Keep volume after PVC deleted
  volume_binding_mode = "WaitForFirstConsumer"  # Provision in pod's AZ

  parameters = {
    type      = "gp3"
    iops      = "5000"
    throughput = "250"
    encrypted = "true"
    kmsKeyId  = var.ebs_kms_key_arn
  }

  allow_volume_expansion = true  # PVCs can grow without downtime
}

resource "kubernetes_storage_class" "standard" {
  metadata {
    name = "standard"
    annotations = {
      "storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class" = "true"
    }
  }

  storage_provisioner = "ebs.csi.aws.com"
  reclaim_policy      = "Delete"
  volume_binding_mode = "WaitForFirstConsumer"

  parameters = {
    type      = "gp3"
    encrypted = "true"
  }

  allow_volume_expansion = true
}

# ─── Azure Disk StorageClass ──────────────────
resource "kubernetes_storage_class" "azure_premium" {
  metadata {
    name = "premium-ssd"
  }

  storage_provisioner = "disk.csi.azure.com"
  reclaim_policy      = "Retain"
  volume_binding_mode = "WaitForFirstConsumer"

  parameters = {
    skuName = "Premium_LRS"
  }

  allow_volume_expansion = true
}
Parameter Values Impact
reclaimPolicyRetain / DeleteRetain keeps disk after PVC delete (data-safe); Delete cleans up
volumeBindingModeWaitForFirstConsumer / ImmediateWaitForFirst provisions in the pod's AZ; Immediate may cross-AZ
allowVolumeExpansiontrue / falseLets PVCs grow in-place without data migration
encryptedtrue + KMS keyAt-rest encryption for compliance (HIPAA, SOC2)
50.4.2 PersistentVolumeClaim

PVCs request storage from a StorageClass. Once bound, the volume persists across pod restarts and rescheduling.

# PVC for a database
resource "kubernetes_persistent_volume_claim" "postgres_data" {
  metadata {
    name      = "postgres-data"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
    labels = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/component" = "database"
    }
  }

  spec {
    access_modes       = ["ReadWriteOnce"]
    storage_class_name = kubernetes_storage_class.fast_ssd.metadata[0].name

    resources {
      requests = {
        storage = "100Gi"
      }
    }
  }

  # Prevent accidental deletion
  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }
}

# PVC for shared file uploads (EFS/Azure Files — ReadWriteMany)
resource "kubernetes_persistent_volume_claim" "media_uploads" {
  metadata {
    name      = "media-uploads"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    access_modes       = ["ReadWriteMany"]  # Multiple pods can write
    storage_class_name = "efs-sc"            # EFS or Azure Files

    resources {
      requests = {
        storage = "500Gi"
      }
    }
  }
}

# Mount PVC in a deployment
resource "kubernetes_deployment" "api" {
  # ...
  spec {
    template {
      spec {
        container {
          name  = "api"
          image = var.api_image

          volume_mount {
            name       = "media"
            mount_path = "/app/media"
          }
        }

        volume {
          name = "media"
          persistent_volume_claim {
            claim_name = kubernetes_persistent_volume_claim.media_uploads.metadata[0].name
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
50.4.3 StatefulSets

StatefulSets provide stable pod identities (pod-0, pod-1, pod-2) and persistent storage per replica. Essential for databases, message queues, and any workload requiring stable network identity.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              STATEFULSET vs DEPLOYMENT                              │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Deployment (stateless)         StatefulSet (stateful)            │
│  ┌──────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────┐              │
│  │ pod-a8f3x (random)│          │ redis-0 (stable)  │              │
│  │ pod-k2m9y (random)│          │ redis-1 (stable)  │              │
│  │ pod-p7d1z (random)│          │ redis-2 (stable)  │              │
│  └──────────────────┘          └──────────────────┘              │
│  • Random pod names             • Sequential, stable names        │
│  • Shared PVC (or none)         • Individual PVC per pod          │
│  • Any order start/stop         • Ordered start (0→1→2)           │
│  • No stable hostname           • redis-0.redis-headless.ns.svc   │
│  • Interchangeable pods         • Each pod has unique identity    │
│                                                                   │
│  USE DEPLOYMENT FOR:            USE STATEFULSET FOR:              │
│  • Web APIs (Django/FastAPI)    • Redis Cluster / Sentinel        │
│  • Workers / consumers          • PostgreSQL replicas             │
│  • Microservices                • Kafka brokers / ZooKeeper       │
│  • Stateless batch jobs         • Elasticsearch nodes             │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Redis StatefulSet with per-pod persistent storage
resource "kubernetes_stateful_set" "redis" {
  metadata {
    name      = "redis"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    service_name = "redis-headless"
    replicas     = 3

    selector {
      match_labels = {
        "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "redis"
      }
    }

    template {
      metadata {
        labels = {
          "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "redis"
        }
      }

      spec {
        container {
          name  = "redis"
          image = "redis:7.2-alpine"

          port {
            container_port = 6379
            name           = "redis"
          }

          command = ["redis-server", "/etc/redis/redis.conf"]

          volume_mount {
            name       = "data"
            mount_path = "/data"
          }

          volume_mount {
            name       = "config"
            mount_path = "/etc/redis"
          }

          resources {
            requests = {
              cpu    = "250m"
              memory = "512Mi"
            }
            limits = {
              cpu    = "500m"
              memory = "1Gi"
            }
          }

          liveness_probe {
            exec {
              command = ["redis-cli", "ping"]
            }
            initial_delay_seconds = 15
            period_seconds        = 10
          }
        }

        volume {
          name = "config"
          config_map {
            name = kubernetes_config_map.redis_config.metadata[0].name
          }
        }
      }
    }

    # Each replica gets its own PVC (redis-data-redis-0, redis-data-redis-1, ...)
    volume_claim_template {
      metadata {
        name = "data"
      }

      spec {
        access_modes       = ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        storage_class_name = kubernetes_storage_class.fast_ssd.metadata[0].name

        resources {
          requests = {
            storage = "20Gi"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# Headless service for StatefulSet DNS
resource "kubernetes_service" "redis_headless" {
  metadata {
    name      = "redis-headless"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    selector = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "redis"
    }

    port {
      port        = 6379
      target_port = 6379
    }

    cluster_ip = "None"  # Headless — returns pod IPs directly
  }
}

# DNS resolution with headless service:
# redis-0.redis-headless.my-app.svc.cluster.local
# redis-1.redis-headless.my-app.svc.cluster.local
# redis-2.redis-headless.my-app.svc.cluster.local
💡 StatefulSet + Terraform Caution: Terraform manages StatefulSet definitions, but the volume_claim_templates create PVCs that live outside Terraform state. Deleting a StatefulSet in Terraform does NOT delete the PVCs (by design — data preservation). You must delete orphaned PVCs manually or with a separate cleanup process.

50.5 Advanced Workloads

WORKLOADS
📌 Category: DaemonSets, Jobs, Autoscaling & Network Policies

Beyond Deployments and StatefulSets, Kubernetes offers specialized workload controllers for node-level agents, batch processing, autoscaling, and network segmentation — all manageable through Terraform.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              KUBERNETES WORKLOAD CONTROLLERS                           │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐  │
│  │   Deployment     │  │   StatefulSet    │  │   DaemonSet         │  │
│  │   ───────────    │  │   ────────────   │  │   ─────────         │  │
│  │   Stateless apps │  │   Databases,     │  │   One pod per node  │  │
│  │   N replicas     │  │   queues         │  │   Logging, monitoring│ │
│  │   Rolling update │  │   Ordered start  │  │   Node agents       │  │
│  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
│  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐  │
│  │   Job            │  │   CronJob        │  │   HPA               │  │
│  │   ───            │  │   ───────        │  │   ───               │  │
│  │   Run to         │  │   Scheduled jobs │  │   Auto-scale pods   │  │
│  │   completion     │  │   (cron syntax)  │  │   based on CPU/     │  │
│  │   Batch, migrate │  │   Backups, ETL   │  │   memory/custom     │  │
│  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
50.5.1 DaemonSets

DaemonSets ensure exactly one pod runs on every node (or a subset). Used for log collectors, monitoring agents, and node-level services.

# Fluentd log collector — runs on every node
resource "kubernetes_daemon_set" "fluentd" {
  metadata {
    name      = "fluentd"
    namespace = "logging"
    labels = {
      "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "fluentd"
    }
  }

  spec {
    selector {
      match_labels = {
        "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "fluentd"
      }
    }

    template {
      metadata {
        labels = {
          "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "fluentd"
        }
      }

      spec {
        service_account_name = kubernetes_service_account.fluentd.metadata[0].name

        # Tolerate all taints so it runs on ALL nodes
        toleration {
          operator = "Exists"
        }

        container {
          name  = "fluentd"
          image = "fluent/fluentd-kubernetes-daemonset:v1.16-debian-cloudwatch-1"

          env {
            name  = "AWS_REGION"
            value = var.aws_region
          }

          env {
            name  = "CLUSTER_NAME"
            value = var.cluster_name
          }

          volume_mount {
            name       = "varlog"
            mount_path = "/var/log"
            read_only  = true
          }

          volume_mount {
            name       = "containers"
            mount_path = "/var/lib/docker/containers"
            read_only  = true
          }

          resources {
            requests = {
              cpu    = "100m"
              memory = "200Mi"
            }
            limits = {
              cpu    = "200m"
              memory = "400Mi"
            }
          }
        }

        # Mount host paths for log access
        volume {
          name = "varlog"
          host_path {
            path = "/var/log"
          }
        }

        volume {
          name = "containers"
          host_path {
            path = "/var/lib/docker/containers"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# Node Exporter — Prometheus metrics from every node
resource "kubernetes_daemon_set" "node_exporter" {
  metadata {
    name      = "node-exporter"
    namespace = "monitoring"
  }

  spec {
    selector {
      match_labels = {
        "app" = "node-exporter"
      }
    }

    template {
      metadata {
        labels = {
          "app" = "node-exporter"
        }
        annotations = {
          "prometheus.io/scrape" = "true"
          "prometheus.io/port"   = "9100"
        }
      }

      spec {
        host_network = true   # Access node-level metrics
        host_pid     = true

        container {
          name  = "node-exporter"
          image = "prom/node-exporter:v1.7.0"

          port {
            container_port = 9100
            host_port      = 9100
          }

          resources {
            requests = { cpu = "50m", memory = "64Mi" }
            limits   = { cpu = "100m", memory = "128Mi" }
          }
        }

        toleration {
          operator = "Exists"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
50.5.2 Jobs & CronJobs

Jobs run a task to completion (exit 0). CronJobs schedule Jobs on a recurring basis. Perfect for migrations, backups, ETL pipelines, and cleanup tasks.

# ─── One-time Job: Database migration ─────────
resource "kubernetes_job" "db_migrate" {
  metadata {
    name      = "db-migrate-${var.app_version}"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    # Don't retry on failure (migrations should be idempotent)
    backoff_limit = 1

    template {
      metadata {
        labels = {
          "app.kubernetes.io/component" = "migration"
        }
      }

      spec {
        service_account_name = kubernetes_service_account.api.metadata[0].name
        restart_policy       = "Never"

        container {
          name  = "migrate"
          image = "${var.ecr_repo}:${var.app_version}"

          command = ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--noinput"]

          env {
            name = "DATABASE_URL"
            value_from {
              secret_key_ref {
                name = kubernetes_secret.app.metadata[0].name
                key  = "database_url"
              }
            }
          }

          resources {
            requests = { cpu = "500m", memory = "512Mi" }
            limits   = { cpu = "1",    memory = "1Gi" }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }

  wait_for_completion = true
  timeouts {
    create = "10m"
  }
}

# ─── CronJob: Nightly database backup ─────────
resource "kubernetes_cron_job_v1" "db_backup" {
  metadata {
    name      = "db-backup"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    schedule                      = "0 2 * * *"  # 2 AM daily
    timezone                      = "UTC"
    concurrency_policy            = "Forbid"  # Don't overlap
    successful_jobs_history_limit = 3
    failed_jobs_history_limit     = 3

    job_template {
      metadata {
        labels = {
          "app.kubernetes.io/component" = "backup"
        }
      }

      spec {
        backoff_limit = 2

        template {
          metadata {}

          spec {
            service_account_name = kubernetes_service_account.worker.metadata[0].name
            restart_policy       = "OnFailure"

            container {
              name  = "backup"
              image = "postgres:16-alpine"

              command = ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
              args = [
                "pg_dump $DATABASE_URL | gzip | aws s3 cp - s3://${var.backup_bucket}/db/$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).sql.gz"
              ]

              env {
                name = "DATABASE_URL"
                value_from {
                  secret_key_ref {
                    name = kubernetes_secret.app.metadata[0].name
                    key  = "database_url"
                  }
                }
              }

              resources {
                requests = { cpu = "250m", memory = "256Mi" }
                limits   = { cpu = "500m", memory = "512Mi" }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# ─── CronJob: Cache cleanup every 6 hours ─────
resource "kubernetes_cron_job_v1" "cache_cleanup" {
  metadata {
    name      = "cache-cleanup"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    schedule           = "0 */6 * * *"
    concurrency_policy = "Replace"

    job_template {
      metadata {}
      spec {
        template {
          metadata {}
          spec {
            restart_policy = "OnFailure"

            container {
              name    = "cleanup"
              image   = "${var.ecr_repo}:${var.app_version}"
              command = ["python", "manage.py", "clearsessions"]
            }
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
50.5.3 Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)

HPAs automatically scale pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. Terraform defines the scaling policy; the HPA controller adjusts replicas in real-time.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              HPA SCALING FLOW                                      │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Metrics Server                                                   │
│  ┌──────────────────┐                                             │
│  │ CPU: 78%         │──┐                                          │
│  │ Memory: 65%      │  │     HPA Controller                      │
│  │ RPS: 1200/s      │  │     ┌───────────────────────────┐       │
│  └──────────────────┘  ├────▶│ IF cpu > 70% → scale UP    │       │
│                        │     │ IF cpu < 30% → scale DOWN  │       │
│  Custom Metrics        │     │ min: 2, max: 20 replicas  │       │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  │     └─────────────┬─────────────┘       │
│  │ queue_depth: 500  │──┘                   │                     │
│  │ http_requests: 1k │                      ▼                     │
│  └──────────────────┘       Deployment: 3 → 5 → 8 → 12 pods     │
│                                                                   │
│  SCALE UP:   Fast (30s default cooldown)                          │
│  SCALE DOWN: Slow (5min default cooldown) — prevents flapping     │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# HPA v2 with CPU + Memory targets
resource "kubernetes_horizontal_pod_autoscaler_v2" "api" {
  metadata {
    name      = "${var.project}-api-hpa"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    scale_target_ref {
      api_version = "apps/v1"
      kind        = "Deployment"
      name        = kubernetes_deployment.api.metadata[0].name
    }

    min_replicas = var.environment == "prod" ? 3 : 1
    max_replicas = var.environment == "prod" ? 20 : 5

    # Scale on CPU utilization
    metric {
      type = "Resource"
      resource {
        name = "cpu"
        target {
          type                = "Utilization"
          average_utilization = 70
        }
      }
    }

    # Scale on memory utilization
    metric {
      type = "Resource"
      resource {
        name = "memory"
        target {
          type                = "Utilization"
          average_utilization = 80
        }
      }
    }

    # Scaling behavior (fine-tune up/down speed)
    behavior {
      scale_up {
        stabilization_window_seconds = 30
        policy {
          type           = "Percent"
          value          = 100   # Double pods on scale-up
          period_seconds = 60
        }
      }

      scale_down {
        stabilization_window_seconds = 300  # 5 min cooldown
        policy {
          type           = "Pods"
          value          = 1     # Remove 1 pod at a time
          period_seconds = 60
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# IMPORTANT: Ignore replica drift from HPA in the Deployment
resource "kubernetes_deployment" "api" {
  # ... (deployment config)

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [
      spec[0].replicas,  # HPA controls this, not Terraform
    ]
  }
}
💡 HPA + Terraform Interaction: When using HPA, always add ignore_changes = [spec[0].replicas] to the Deployment's lifecycle block. Otherwise, every terraform plan will show a diff because HPA has changed the replica count from what Terraform originally set.
50.5.4 Network Policies

Network Policies control pod-to-pod traffic at the network layer. By default, all pods can communicate with all other pods — Network Policies add firewall rules within the cluster.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              NETWORK POLICY: DENY-ALL + ALLOW-LIST                 │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  Namespace: my-app                                                │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                             │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐     ✅ port 8000     ┌─────────┐              │  │
│  │  │ Frontend │─────────────────────▶│   API    │              │  │
│  │  └─────────┘                      └────┬────┘              │  │
│  │                                        │ ✅ port 5432       │  │
│  │  ┌─────────┐     ❌ BLOCKED           ▼                    │  │
│  │  │ Worker   │──────────╳────▶  ┌──────────┐                │  │
│  │  └─────────┘                   │ Postgres │                │  │
│  │                                └──────────┘                │  │
│  │                                     ▲                       │  │
│  │  ┌───────────────┐  ❌ BLOCKED      │                       │  │
│  │  │ Other Namespace│──────╳──────────┘                       │  │
│  │  └───────────────┘                                          │  │
│  │                                                             │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                   │
│  Strategy: Default deny all → explicitly allow required paths     │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ─── Default deny all ingress in namespace ─────
resource "kubernetes_network_policy" "deny_all" {
  metadata {
    name      = "default-deny-all"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    pod_selector {}  # Matches ALL pods in namespace

    policy_types = ["Ingress", "Egress"]

    # No ingress rules = deny all incoming
    # No egress rules = deny all outgoing

    # Allow DNS resolution (required!)
    egress {
      ports {
        port     = "53"
        protocol = "TCP"
      }
      ports {
        port     = "53"
        protocol = "UDP"
      }
      to {
        namespace_selector {
          match_labels = {
            "kubernetes.io/metadata.name" = "kube-system"
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# ─── Allow Frontend → API on port 8000 ────────
resource "kubernetes_network_policy" "api_ingress" {
  metadata {
    name      = "allow-frontend-to-api"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    pod_selector {
      match_labels = {
        "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-api"
      }
    }

    policy_types = ["Ingress"]

    ingress {
      from {
        pod_selector {
          match_labels = {
            "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-frontend"
          }
        }
      }

      # Also allow from ingress controller namespace
      from {
        namespace_selector {
          match_labels = {
            "kubernetes.io/metadata.name" = "ingress-system"
          }
        }
      }

      ports {
        port     = "8000"
        protocol = "TCP"
      }
    }
  }
}

# ─── Allow API → Database on port 5432 ─────────
resource "kubernetes_network_policy" "db_ingress" {
  metadata {
    name      = "allow-api-to-db"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    pod_selector {
      match_labels = {
        "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "postgres"
      }
    }

    policy_types = ["Ingress"]

    ingress {
      from {
        pod_selector {
          match_labels = {
            "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-api"
          }
        }
      }

      ports {
        port     = "5432"
        protocol = "TCP"
      }
    }
  }
}

# ─── Allow API egress to external services ─────
resource "kubernetes_network_policy" "api_egress" {
  metadata {
    name      = "api-egress"
    namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
  }

  spec {
    pod_selector {
      match_labels = {
        "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-api"
      }
    }

    policy_types = ["Egress"]

    # Allow to database
    egress {
      to {
        pod_selector {
          match_labels = {
            "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "postgres"
          }
        }
      }
      ports {
        port     = "5432"
        protocol = "TCP"
      }
    }

    # Allow to Redis
    egress {
      to {
        pod_selector {
          match_labels = {
            "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "redis"
          }
        }
      }
      ports {
        port     = "6379"
        protocol = "TCP"
      }
    }

    # Allow DNS
    egress {
      ports {
        port     = "53"
        protocol = "TCP"
      }
      ports {
        port     = "53"
        protocol = "UDP"
      }
    }

    # Allow HTTPS to external APIs
    egress {
      ports {
        port     = "443"
        protocol = "TCP"
      }
    }
  }
}
⚠️ Network Policy Requirements: Network Policies only work if your cluster has a CNI plugin that supports them: Calico, Cilium, Weave Net, or Azure NPM. AWS VPC CNI requires the Calico add-on. Without a compatible CNI, NetworkPolicy resources are silently ignored — creating a false sense of security.

50.6 kubernetes_manifest & CRDs

ADVANCED
📌 Category: Custom Resources & Operators

The kubernetes_manifest resource lets Terraform manage any Kubernetes object — including Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and operator-managed resources that don't have dedicated Terraform resource types.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          NATIVE RESOURCES vs kubernetes_manifest                       │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  NATIVE (type-safe, full plan)     │  kubernetes_manifest (generic)   │
│  ──────────────────────────────    │  ──────────────────────────────  │
│  kubernetes_deployment             │  Any K8s YAML object:            │
│  kubernetes_service                │  • CRDs (cert-manager, Istio)    │
│  kubernetes_config_map             │  • Operator resources            │
│  kubernetes_secret                 │  • Custom controllers            │
│  kubernetes_ingress_v1             │  • Argo Applications             │
│  kubernetes_role                   │  • Prometheus ServiceMonitors    │
│                                    │  • Karpenter NodePools           │
│  ✅ Full plan diff                 │  ⚠️ Limited plan visibility      │
│  ✅ State tracking                 │  ✅ State tracking                │
│  ✅ HCL-native syntax              │  ⚠️ YAML-in-HCL syntax           │
│  ❌ Only built-in K8s kinds        │  ✅ ANY K8s kind including CRDs   │
│                                    │                                  │
│  RULE: Use native resources when   │  Use kubernetes_manifest for     │
│  available. Fall back to manifest  │  CRDs and operator objects.      │
│  for everything else.              │                                  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
50.6.1 kubernetes_manifest Resource

Convert any Kubernetes YAML manifest into Terraform-managed infrastructure. The manifest field accepts the same structure as a YAML manifest.

# ─── cert-manager ClusterIssuer (CRD) ──────────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "letsencrypt_prod" {
  manifest = {
    apiVersion = "cert-manager.io/v1"
    kind       = "ClusterIssuer"

    metadata = {
      name = "letsencrypt-prod"
    }

    spec = {
      acme = {
        server = "https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory"
        email  = var.acme_email

        privateKeySecretRef = {
          name = "letsencrypt-prod-key"
        }

        solvers = [{
          http01 = {
            ingress = {
              class = "nginx"
            }
          }
        }]
      }
    }
  }

  depends_on = [helm_release.cert_manager]
}

# ─── cert-manager Certificate ──────────────────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "app_certificate" {
  manifest = {
    apiVersion = "cert-manager.io/v1"
    kind       = "Certificate"

    metadata = {
      name      = "${var.project}-tls"
      namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
    }

    spec = {
      secretName = "${var.project}-tls"

      issuerRef = {
        name = "letsencrypt-prod"
        kind = "ClusterIssuer"
      }

      dnsNames = [
        var.domain_name,
        "*.${var.domain_name}"
      ]
    }
  }

  depends_on = [kubernetes_manifest.letsencrypt_prod]
}

# ─── Prometheus ServiceMonitor (CRD) ───────────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "api_service_monitor" {
  manifest = {
    apiVersion = "monitoring.coreos.com/v1"
    kind       = "ServiceMonitor"

    metadata = {
      name      = "${var.project}-api"
      namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
      labels = {
        release = "prometheus"   # Must match Prometheus selector
      }
    }

    spec = {
      selector = {
        matchLabels = {
          "app.kubernetes.io/name" = "${var.project}-api"
        }
      }

      endpoints = [{
        port     = "http"
        path     = "/metrics"
        interval = "30s"
      }]
    }
  }
}
50.6.2 Working with CRDs

CRDs extend the Kubernetes API with custom object types. Install CRDs (typically via Helm) before creating instances.

# ─── Install CRDs via Helm, then use kubernetes_manifest ──

# Step 1: Install the operator/controller (includes CRDs)
resource "helm_release" "cert_manager" {
  name       = "cert-manager"
  repository = "https://charts.jetstack.io"
  chart      = "cert-manager"
  version    = "v1.14.0"
  namespace  = "cert-manager"

  create_namespace = true

  set {
    name  = "installCRDs"
    value = "true"     # Helm installs the CRDs
  }
}

# Step 2: Create CRD instances (must wait for CRDs to exist)
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "issuer" {
  manifest = {
    apiVersion = "cert-manager.io/v1"
    kind       = "ClusterIssuer"
    # ...
  }

  # CRITICAL: CRDs must exist before creating instances
  depends_on = [helm_release.cert_manager]
}

# ─── Karpenter NodePool (CRD) ──────────────────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "default_nodepool" {
  manifest = {
    apiVersion = "karpenter.sh/v1beta1"
    kind       = "NodePool"

    metadata = {
      name = "default"
    }

    spec = {
      template = {
        spec = {
          requirements = [
            {
              key      = "kubernetes.io/arch"
              operator = "In"
              values   = ["amd64"]
            },
            {
              key      = "karpenter.sh/capacity-type"
              operator = "In"
              values   = ["on-demand", "spot"]
            },
            {
              key      = "karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-category"
              operator = "In"
              values   = ["c", "m", "r"]
            }
          ]

          nodeClassRef = {
            name = "default"
          }
        }
      }

      limits = {
        cpu    = "100"
        memory = "400Gi"
      }

      disruption = {
        consolidationPolicy = "WhenUnderutilized"
        expireAfter         = "720h"
      }
    }
  }

  depends_on = [helm_release.karpenter]
}

# ─── Istio VirtualService (CRD) ────────────────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "api_virtualservice" {
  manifest = {
    apiVersion = "networking.istio.io/v1beta1"
    kind       = "VirtualService"

    metadata = {
      name      = "${var.project}-api"
      namespace = kubernetes_namespace.app.metadata[0].name
    }

    spec = {
      hosts    = [var.domain_name]
      gateways = ["istio-system/main-gateway"]

      http = [{
        match = [{
          uri = {
            prefix = "/api"
          }
        }]

        route = [{
          destination = {
            host = "${var.project}-api"
            port = {
              number = 8000
            }
          }
        }]

        retries = {
          attempts      = 3
          perTryTimeout = "5s"
        }
      }]
    }
  }
}
50.6.3 Raw YAML with kubernetes_manifest

For complex manifests, load YAML from files and decode them into the manifest field. This preserves readability while keeping Terraform state management.

# ─── Load YAML file and decode ─────────────────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "custom_resource" {
  manifest = yamldecode(file("${path.module}/manifests/custom-resource.yaml"))
}

# ─── Templated YAML ───────────────────────────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "templated" {
  manifest = yamldecode(templatefile("${path.module}/manifests/app.yaml.tpl", {
    namespace   = var.namespace
    app_name    = var.project
    environment = var.environment
    replicas    = var.replicas
  }))
}

# ─── Multiple CRDs from a directory ────────────
locals {
  manifest_files = fileset("${path.module}/manifests/crds", "*.yaml")
  manifests = {
    for f in local.manifest_files :
    f => yamldecode(file("${path.module}/manifests/crds/${f}"))
  }
}

resource "kubernetes_manifest" "crds" {
  for_each = local.manifests
  manifest = each.value
}

# ─── ArgoCD Application (managing app via GitOps) ────
resource "kubernetes_manifest" "argocd_app" {
  manifest = {
    apiVersion = "argoproj.io/v1alpha1"
    kind       = "Application"

    metadata = {
      name      = var.project
      namespace = "argocd"
    }

    spec = {
      project = "default"

      source = {
        repoURL        = var.git_repo_url
        targetRevision = var.environment == "prod" ? "main" : "develop"
        path           = "k8s/overlays/${var.environment}"
      }

      destination = {
        server    = "https://kubernetes.default.svc"
        namespace = var.namespace
      }

      syncPolicy = {
        automated = {
          prune    = true
          selfHeal = true
        }

        syncOptions = ["CreateNamespace=true"]
      }
    }
  }

  depends_on = [helm_release.argocd]
}
💡 kubernetes_manifest vs kubectl_manifest: The built-in kubernetes_manifest requires the CRD to exist at plan time (it validates the schema). If this causes chicken-and-egg issues, consider the kubectl_manifest resource from the gavinbunney/kubectl provider — it applies raw YAML without schema validation and handles CRD ordering more gracefully.

50.7 Best Practices & Patterns

BEST PRACTICE
📌 Category: Operational Guidance

Understanding when to use Terraform, kubectl, or Helm for Kubernetes management is critical for building maintainable workflows.

50.7.1 Terraform vs kubectl/Helm

Each tool has strengths for different aspects of Kubernetes management.

Aspect Terraform kubectl/YAML Helm
Cluster Provisioning✅ Best choice❌ Cannot❌ Cannot
Infrastructure + K8s✅ Unified state⚠️ Separate tool⚠️ Separate tool
App Deployments⚠️ Possible but rigid✅ Fast iteration✅ Templated
Helm Charts✅ Via helm provider❌ Not native✅ Native
Rollbacks⚠️ State-based✅ kubectl rollout✅ helm rollback
CRDs⚠️ Limited✅ Full support✅ Via charts
Learning CurveHCL + K8s conceptsYAML + kubectlCharts + Go tmpl
50.7.2 When to Use Terraform for K8s

Follow this decision framework to choose the right tool.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WHEN TO USE TERRAFORM FOR KUBERNETES                   │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  ✅ USE TERRAFORM FOR:                                            │
│  ────────────────────                                             │
│  • Cluster provisioning (EKS, AKS, GKE)                          │
│  • Namespaces, RBAC, quotas (cluster-level config)                │
│  • Infrastructure components (Ingress Controller, cert-manager)   │
│  • Secrets from external sources (Vault, AWS SM → K8s)            │
│  • When you need unified state (infra + K8s in one plan)          │
│                                                                   │
│  ⚠️ USE HELM FOR:                                                 │
│  ─────────────────                                                │
│  • Third-party applications (nginx, prometheus, grafana)          │
│  • Complex apps with many configurable values                     │
│  • Anything that ships as a Helm chart                            │
│                                                                   │
│  ⚠️ USE kubectl/ArgoCD FOR:                                       │
│  ────────────────────────                                         │
│  • Application code deployments (frequent releases)               │
│  • GitOps workflows (ArgoCD/Flux)                                 │
│  • Debugging and ad-hoc changes                                   │
│  • CRDs and operator-managed resources                            │
│                                                                   │
│  RECOMMENDED PATTERN:                                             │
│  Terraform → Cluster + Infra → Helm charts (via helm provider)    │
│  ArgoCD/Flux → Application deployments (GitOps)                   │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Production Pattern: Use Terraform for cluster provisioning + infrastructure components (ingress controller, monitoring stack via Helm provider). Use ArgoCD or Flux for application deployments with GitOps. This separation gives infrastructure stability with Terraform's state management and application agility with GitOps continuous delivery.
50.7.3 Production Deployment Architecture

A proven architecture for managing Kubernetes with Terraform in production environments.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          PRODUCTION K8s + TERRAFORM ARCHITECTURE                      │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                       │
│  LAYER 1: INFRASTRUCTURE (Terraform — terraform/infra/)               │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  VPC / Subnets / NAT Gateway / Security Groups                  │  │
│  │  EKS / AKS / GKE Cluster + Node Groups                         │  │
│  │  RDS / ElastiCache / S3 / Cloud SQL                             │  │
│  │  IAM Roles / Service Accounts (IRSA)                            │  │
│  │  State: s3://infra-state/prod.tfstate                           │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ cluster endpoint + credentials          │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  LAYER 2: PLATFORM (Terraform — terraform/platform/)                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Namespaces + RBAC + ResourceQuotas + LimitRanges               │  │
│  │  Ingress Controller (nginx/ALB) via Helm                        │  │
│  │  cert-manager + ClusterIssuers                                  │  │
│  │  external-dns + external-secrets                                │  │
│  │  Prometheus + Grafana monitoring stack                          │  │
│  │  Network Policies (baseline security)                           │  │
│  │  State: s3://platform-state/prod.tfstate                        │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │ platform ready                          │
│                             ▼                                         │
│  LAYER 3: APPLICATIONS (ArgoCD / Flux — GitOps)                       │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  Application Deployments (Helm charts or kustomize)             │  │
│  │  Auto-sync from Git → cluster                                  │  │
│  │  Canary / Blue-Green via Argo Rollouts                          │  │
│  │  Application-specific ConfigMaps / Secrets                      │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                       │
│  WHY 3 LAYERS?                                                        │
│  ├── Blast radius isolation (infra change ≠ app outage)              │
│  ├── Different change velocities (infra: weekly, apps: daily)        │
│  ├── Different teams (platform eng vs app dev)                       │
│  └── Independent state files and approval workflows                  │
│                                                                       │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ─── Layer 2: Platform Module Example ─────────
# terraform/platform/main.tf

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "company-tf-state"
    key    = "platform/prod.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Read cluster info from Layer 1
data "terraform_remote_state" "infra" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "company-tf-state"
    key    = "infra/prod.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

provider "kubernetes" {
  host                   = data.terraform_remote_state.infra.outputs.cluster_endpoint
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(data.terraform_remote_state.infra.outputs.cluster_ca)
  token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
}

provider "helm" {
  kubernetes {
    host                   = data.terraform_remote_state.infra.outputs.cluster_endpoint
    cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(data.terraform_remote_state.infra.outputs.cluster_ca)
    token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
  }
}

# Platform namespaces
module "namespaces" {
  source       = "../modules/k8s-namespace"
  for_each     = toset(["api", "worker", "monitoring", "ingress-system"])
  name         = each.key
  environment  = var.environment
}

# Ingress controller via Helm
resource "helm_release" "ingress_nginx" {
  name       = "ingress-nginx"
  repository = "https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx"
  chart      = "ingress-nginx"
  namespace  = "ingress-system"
  version    = "4.9.0"

  create_namespace = true
  wait             = true

  values = [
    file("${path.module}/values/ingress-${var.environment}.yaml")
  ]
}
50.7.4 Common Pitfalls & Troubleshooting

Avoid these common mistakes when using Terraform with Kubernetes.

Pitfall Symptom Solution
Provider before clusterconnection refused on planSeparate into two Terraform states (infra + platform)
Token expiry401 Unauthorized mid-applyUse exec plugin or short-lived token refresh
Drift from kubectlPlan shows unexpected changesNever mix Terraform + manual kubectl for same resources
CRD orderingCRD resource fails — kind unknownApply CRDs first with kubernetes_manifest + depends_on
Namespace deletionDestroy hangs (finalizers)Remove finalizers or delete child resources first
Large state filesSlow plan/applySplit into smaller states by concern (infra/platform/app)
Secret in stateSecrets visible in state fileEncrypt state backend + use external-secrets operator
Image tag changesNo-op on latest tagAlways use specific image tags, never latest
 
# ─── Fix: Provider connection before cluster exists ─────
# BAD: Provider tries to connect during plan phase
provider "kubernetes" {
  host  = aws_eks_cluster.main.endpoint  # ← fails if cluster doesn't exist yet
  token = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
}

# GOOD: Use separate state files
# State 1: Create cluster → outputs endpoint
# State 2: Read endpoint from remote state → configure provider
data "terraform_remote_state" "cluster" {
  backend = "s3"
  config  = { bucket = "tf-state", key = "cluster.tfstate" }
}

provider "kubernetes" {
  host  = data.terraform_remote_state.cluster.outputs.endpoint
  token = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
}

# ─── Fix: Prevent drift from manual changes ─────
resource "kubernetes_deployment" "api" {
  # ...

  lifecycle {
    # Ignore changes made outside Terraform (e.g., HPA scaling)
    ignore_changes = [
      spec[0].replicas,
    ]
  }
}
💡 Golden Rule: If Terraform manages a K8s resource, never modify it with kubectl edit or kubectl apply. This creates state drift that causes confusing plan outputs. Either Terraform owns the resource completely, or kubectl/ArgoCD does — never both.
⎈ 📦 🎯 🔄
CHAPTER 51

Helm Provider

helm_release Charts Values Repositories EKS/AKS/GKE
5Sections
15+Examples
8Charts
⎈Helm
PART 14 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

The Helm provider allows Terraform to install, upgrade, and manage Helm charts on Kubernetes clusters. This bridges infrastructure provisioning and application deployment — provision your EKS/AKS/GKE cluster with Terraform, then deploy nginx-ingress, cert-manager, Prometheus, and your applications using helm_release in the same workflow.

Learning Objectives

  • Configure the Helm provider for different cluster types
  • Deploy applications with helm_release resources
  • Manage Helm values and repository configuration
  • Integrate Helm deployments with EKS, AKS, and GKE clusters

Chapter Contents

51.1 Helm Provider Setup 51.2 helm_release Resource 51.3 Values Configuration 51.4 Repository Management 51.5 Helm with EKS/AKS/GKE

51.1 Helm Provider Setup

CONFIGURATION
📚 Official Documentation: Helm Provider

The Helm provider requires the same authentication as the Kubernetes provider — it connects to the same K8s API server. Declare both providers together.

# Required providers
terraform {
  required_providers {
    helm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/helm"
      version = "~> 2.12"
    }
    kubernetes = {
      source  = "hashicorp/kubernetes"
      version = "~> 2.25"
    }
  }
}

# ─── EKS: Helm + Kubernetes providers ─────────
data "aws_eks_cluster" "main" {
  name = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
}

data "aws_eks_cluster_auth" "main" {
  name = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
}

provider "helm" {
  kubernetes {
    host                   = data.aws_eks_cluster.main.endpoint
    cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(data.aws_eks_cluster.main.certificate_authority[0].data)
    token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
  }
}

# ─── AKS: Helm provider ──────────────────────
provider "helm" {
  alias = "aks"
  kubernetes {
    host                   = azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].host
    client_certificate     = base64decode(azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].client_certificate)
    client_key             = base64decode(azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].client_key)
    cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.main.kube_config[0].cluster_ca_certificate)
  }
}

# ─── GKE: Helm provider ──────────────────────
data "google_client_config" "default" {}

provider "helm" {
  alias = "gke"
  kubernetes {
    host                   = "https://${google_container_cluster.primary.endpoint}"
    token                  = data.google_client_config.default.access_token
    cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(google_container_cluster.primary.master_auth[0].cluster_ca_certificate)
  }
}

51.2 helm_release Resource

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: helm_release

The helm_release resource installs a Helm chart into a Kubernetes cluster. It's the core resource of the Helm provider.

# Install nginx-ingress controller
resource "helm_release" "nginx_ingress" {
  name       = "ingress-nginx"
  repository = "https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx"
  chart      = "ingress-nginx"
  version    = "4.9.0"
  namespace  = "ingress-system"

  create_namespace = true
  atomic           = true
  wait             = true
  timeout          = 600

  # Inline values
  set {
    name  = "controller.replicaCount"
    value = "2"
  }

  set {
    name  = "controller.service.type"
    value = "LoadBalancer"
  }

  set {
    name  = "controller.metrics.enabled"
    value = "true"
  }
}

# Install cert-manager
resource "helm_release" "cert_manager" {
  name       = "cert-manager"
  repository = "https://charts.jetstack.io"
  chart      = "cert-manager"
  version    = "1.14.0"
  namespace  = "cert-manager"

  create_namespace = true
  atomic           = true

  set {
    name  = "installCRDs"
    value = "true"
  }
}

# Install Prometheus monitoring stack
resource "helm_release" "prometheus" {
  name       = "kube-prometheus-stack"
  repository = "https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts"
  chart      = "kube-prometheus-stack"
  version    = "56.0.0"
  namespace  = "monitoring"

  create_namespace = true
  timeout          = 900

  values = [
    file("${path.module}/helm-values/prometheus.yaml")
  ]
}
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                helm_release LIFECYCLE                               │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  terraform plan                                                    │
│  ├── Compares desired chart version + values vs current state      │
│  └── Shows install / upgrade / no-change                          │
│                                                                   │
│  terraform apply                                                   │
│  ├── helm install (first time)                                    │
│  │   └── Renders templates → kubectl apply                        │
│  ├── helm upgrade (values or version changed)                     │
│  │   └── Diff → rolling update                                    │
│  └── helm uninstall (on destroy)                                  │
│                                                                   │
│  Key Arguments:                                                    │
│  ├── atomic = true    → Rolls back on failed install/upgrade      │
│  ├── wait = true      → Waits until all pods are ready            │
│  ├── timeout = 600    → Max seconds to wait                       │
│  ├── cleanup_on_fail  → Deletes new resources on failure          │
│  └── force_update     → Force resource updates (use with caution) │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

51.3 Values Configuration

CONFIGURATION
📌 Category: Helm Values Management

Helm charts accept values to customize deployments. Terraform provides three ways to pass values: inline set blocks, YAML files via values, and sensitive values via set_sensitive.

# Method 1: Inline set blocks (simple key-value)
resource "helm_release" "app" {
  name  = "my-app"
  chart = "./charts/my-app"

  set {
    name  = "image.repository"
    value = "${var.ecr_repo}/my-app"
  }

  set {
    name  = "image.tag"
    value = var.app_version
  }

  set {
    name  = "replicaCount"
    value = var.environment == "prod" ? "3" : "1"
  }
}

# Method 2: YAML values file (complex configurations)
resource "helm_release" "grafana" {
  name       = "grafana"
  repository = "https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts"
  chart      = "grafana"
  namespace  = "monitoring"

  values = [
    file("${path.module}/helm-values/grafana-base.yaml"),
    file("${path.module}/helm-values/grafana-${var.environment}.yaml")
  ]
}

# Method 3: templatefile for dynamic YAML
resource "helm_release" "app_dynamic" {
  name  = "my-app"
  chart = "./charts/my-app"

  values = [
    templatefile("${path.module}/helm-values/app.yaml.tpl", {
      environment   = var.environment
      replicas      = var.replicas
      domain        = var.domain_name
      db_host       = aws_db_instance.main.address
    })
  ]
}

# Method 4: Sensitive values (not shown in plan output)
resource "helm_release" "app_with_secrets" {
  name  = "my-app"
  chart = "./charts/my-app"

  set_sensitive {
    name  = "secrets.databaseUrl"
    value = var.database_url
  }

  set_sensitive {
    name  = "secrets.apiKey"
    value = var.api_key
  }
}
💡 Values Precedence: When using both values (YAML files) and set blocks, the set blocks always take precedence and override values from YAML files. Use values for base configuration and set for environment-specific overrides or dynamic values from Terraform resources.

51.4 Repository Management

CONFIGURATION
📌 Category: Chart Repositories

Helm charts can come from public repositories, OCI registries, or local directories. Terraform supports all sources.

# Public repository (URL-based)
resource "helm_release" "nginx" {
  name       = "ingress-nginx"
  repository = "https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx"
  chart      = "ingress-nginx"
  version    = "4.9.0"
}

# OCI registry (Helm 3.8+)
resource "helm_release" "app" {
  name  = "my-app"
  chart = "oci://registry.example.com/charts/my-app"
  version = "1.2.0"
}

# Local chart directory
resource "helm_release" "local_app" {
  name  = "my-app"
  chart = "${path.module}/charts/my-app"
}

# Private repository with authentication
resource "helm_release" "private_chart" {
  name       = "enterprise-app"
  repository = "https://charts.company.com"
  chart      = "enterprise-app"
  version    = "2.1.0"

  repository_username = var.helm_repo_user
  repository_password = var.helm_repo_password
}
Chart Source Syntax Use Case
Public Reporepository + chartnginx, prometheus, grafana
OCI Registrychart = "oci://..."ECR, ACR, Artifact Registry
Local Pathchart = "./charts/..."Custom in-repo charts
Private Reporepository + authEnterprise charts

51.5 Helm with EKS/AKS/GKE

INTEGRATION
📌 Category: Cloud Provider Integration

The most powerful Terraform + Helm pattern: provision a managed cluster, then deploy infrastructure charts (ingress, monitoring, service mesh) in the same Terraform config.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        TERRAFORM + HELM: FULL CLUSTER BOOTSTRAP                    │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  MODULE: cluster/                                                  │
│  ├── aws_eks_cluster (or azurerm/google equivalent)               │
│  ├── aws_eks_node_group                                           │
│  └── Outputs: endpoint, ca_cert, token                            │
│                                                                   │
│  MODULE: platform/                                                 │
│  ├── helm_release "ingress_nginx"    → Ingress Controller          │
│  ├── helm_release "cert_manager"     → TLS Certificates            │
│  ├── helm_release "prometheus"       → Monitoring                  │
│  ├── helm_release "grafana"          → Dashboards                  │
│  ├── helm_release "external_dns"     → DNS Management              │
│  └── helm_release "external_secrets" → Secrets Sync                │
│                                                                   │
│  MODULE: apps/                                                     │
│  ├── helm_release "api"              → Your API service            │
│  ├── helm_release "worker"           → Background workers          │
│  └── helm_release "frontend"         → Web frontend                │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ─── Complete EKS + Helm Bootstrap Example ─────

# 1. EKS Cluster
module "eks" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws"
  version = "~> 20.0"

  cluster_name    = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"
  cluster_version = "1.29"

  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnets

  eks_managed_node_groups = {
    default = {
      instance_types = ["t3.large"]
      min_size       = 2
      max_size       = 10
      desired_size   = 3
    }
  }
}

# 2. Configure Helm + K8s providers from EKS outputs
provider "helm" {
  kubernetes {
    host                   = module.eks.cluster_endpoint
    cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(module.eks.cluster_certificate_authority_data)
    token                  = data.aws_eks_cluster_auth.main.token
  }
}

# 3. Deploy ingress controller
resource "helm_release" "ingress_nginx" {
  name       = "ingress-nginx"
  repository = "https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx"
  chart      = "ingress-nginx"
  version    = "4.9.0"
  namespace  = "ingress-system"

  create_namespace = true
  wait             = true

  set {
    name  = "controller.service.annotations.service\\.beta\\.kubernetes\\.io/aws-load-balancer-type"
    value = "nlb"
  }

  depends_on = [module.eks]
}

# 4. Deploy monitoring stack
resource "helm_release" "prometheus_stack" {
  name       = "monitoring"
  repository = "https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts"
  chart      = "kube-prometheus-stack"
  version    = "56.0.0"
  namespace  = "monitoring"

  create_namespace = true
  timeout          = 900

  values = [
    file("${path.module}/helm-values/prometheus-${var.environment}.yaml")
  ]

  depends_on = [module.eks]
}

# 5. Deploy application
resource "helm_release" "api" {
  name      = "${var.project}-api"
  chart     = "${path.module}/charts/api"
  namespace = var.project

  create_namespace = true

  set {
    name  = "image.repository"
    value = "${var.ecr_repo}/${var.project}-api"
  }

  set {
    name  = "image.tag"
    value = var.app_version
  }

  set_sensitive {
    name  = "env.DATABASE_URL"
    value = "postgresql://${var.db_user}:${var.db_password}@${aws_db_instance.main.address}:5432/${var.db_name}"
  }

  depends_on = [
    helm_release.ingress_nginx,
    helm_release.cert_manager
  ]
}
💡 depends_on is Critical: Helm releases that need infrastructure charts (ingress controller, cert-manager) must use depends_on to ensure proper ordering. Without it, Terraform may try to create an Ingress resource before the Ingress Controller is deployed, causing failures.
🌐 ☁️ 🔀 🏗️
CHAPTER 52

Multi-Cloud Patterns

Multi-Cloud Abstraction Cross-Cloud CI/CD State
4Sections
5Subsections
15+Examples
🌐Multi-Cloud
PART 14 EXPERT ★★★★★

Multi-cloud architecture uses multiple cloud providers (AWS + Azure, AWS + GCP, or all three) for redundancy, compliance, best-of-breed services, or vendor independence. Terraform's provider model makes it the natural choice for orchestrating multi-cloud infrastructure — but it introduces significant complexity in state management, abstraction, networking, and CI/CD.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand multi-cloud architecture use cases and challenges
  • Design provider abstraction layers with wrapper modules
  • Configure cross-cloud networking (VPN/peering)
  • Build multi-cloud CI/CD pipelines

Chapter Contents

52.1 Multi-Cloud Architecture 52.2 Provider Abstraction 52.3 Cross-Cloud Networking 52.4 Multi-Cloud CI/CD

52.1 Multi-Cloud Architecture

ARCHITECTURE
📌 Category: Design Patterns

Multi-cloud is not "use everything everywhere" — it's strategic placement of workloads across providers based on specific requirements.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MULTI-CLOUD ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW                     │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  │
│  │    AWS           │  │   AZURE          │  │   GCP            │ │
│  │                  │  │                  │  │                  │  │
│  │  • EKS Cluster   │  │  • AKS Cluster   │  │  • BigQuery      │ │
│  │  • RDS Postgres   │  │  • Azure AD      │  │  • GKE Cluster   │ │
│  │  • S3 Storage    │  │  • Blob Storage  │  │  • ML Platform   │  │
│  │  • Lambda        │  │  • Functions     │  │  • Pub/Sub       │  │
│  └────────┬─────────┘  └────────┬─────────┘  └────────┬─────────┘│
│           │                     │                      │          │
│           └─────────────────────┼──────────────────────┘          │
│                                 │                                 │
│                    ┌────────────▼────────────┐                    │
│                    │   TERRAFORM STATE        │                    │
│                    │   (per cloud or shared)  │                    │
│                    └─────────────────────────┘                    │
│                                                                   │
│                    ┌─────────────────────────┐                    │
│                    │   CI/CD PIPELINE          │                    │
│                    │   (unified or per-cloud) │                    │
│                    └─────────────────────────┘                    │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
52.1.1 Use Cases

Real-world scenarios that justify multi-cloud complexity.

Use Case Example Clouds
Disaster RecoveryPrimary on AWS, failover on AzureAWS + Azure
Data SovereigntyEU data on Azure (EU regions), US data on AWSAWS + Azure
Best-of-BreedAWS for compute, GCP for ML/BigQueryAWS + GCP
Vendor Lock-in AvoidanceK8s workloads portable across EKS/AKS/GKEAll three
M&A IntegrationCompany A on AWS, acquired B on AzureAWS + Azure
ComplianceGovernment workloads on Azure Gov, analytics on GCPAzure + GCP
52.1.2 Challenges

Multi-cloud introduces complexity at every level. Understand these trade-offs before committing.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MULTI-CLOUD CHALLENGES                                │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  COMPLEXITY MULTIPLIER                                             │
│  ──────────────────────                                           │
│  Single Cloud:  1x complexity   │ 1 IAM model, 1 network model   │
│  Two Clouds:    3-4x complexity │ 2 IAM, 2 networks, 1 bridge    │
│  Three Clouds:  8-10x complexity│ 3 IAM, 3 networks, 3 bridges   │
│                                                                   │
│  KEY CHALLENGES:                                                   │
│  ├── 🔐 Identity    → Each cloud has different IAM models          │
│  ├── 🌐 Networking  → Cross-cloud VPN/peering is complex          │
│  ├── 💰 Cost        → Data transfer between clouds is expensive   │
│  ├── 📊 Monitoring  → Unified observability across providers      │
│  ├── 🔄 State       → Terraform state per cloud vs shared         │
│  ├── 🧑‍💻 Skills     → Team must know multiple cloud platforms     │
│  └── 🏗️ Abstraction → Lowest common denominator problem           │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
52.1.3 State Management

Multi-cloud state strategies — each has trade-offs between isolation and cross-reference capability.

# ─── Strategy 1: Separate state per cloud (RECOMMENDED) ────
# aws/main.tf
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "company-terraform-state"
    key    = "aws/production/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# azure/main.tf
terraform {
  backend "azurerm" {
    resource_group_name  = "terraform-state"
    storage_account_name = "companyterraform"
    container_name       = "tfstate"
    key                  = "azure/production/terraform.tfstate"
  }
}

# ─── Cross-reference via remote state data sources ────
# In Azure config, read AWS outputs:
data "terraform_remote_state" "aws" {
  backend = "s3"
  config = {
    bucket = "company-terraform-state"
    key    = "aws/production/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Use AWS VPC CIDR to configure Azure peering
resource "azurerm_virtual_network" "main" {
  name                = "main-vnet"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  address_space       = ["10.1.0.0/16"]  # Non-overlapping with AWS VPC
}

# ─── Strategy 2: Single state (for tightly coupled multi-cloud) ────
# main.tf — Both providers in one config
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "company-terraform-state"
    key    = "multi-cloud/production/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

provider "azurerm" {
  features {}
}
💡 State Strategy: Separate state files per cloud is the recommended approach. It provides blast radius isolation (a bad Azure change won't affect AWS), independent deployments, and clear ownership. Use terraform_remote_state data sources for cross-cloud references like VPC CIDRs or endpoint addresses.

52.2 Provider Abstraction

PATTERN
📌 Category: Module Design
