Provider abstraction creates reusable modules that work across clouds by defining a common interface and implementing cloud-specific logic internally.

52.2.1 Wrapper Modules

A wrapper module exposes a cloud-agnostic interface while internally calling cloud-specific resources.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              WRAPPER MODULE PATTERN                                │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  CALLER (root module)                                              │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ module "k8s_cluster" {                                      │  │
│  │   source   = "./modules/k8s-cluster"                        │  │
│  │   provider = "aws"          ← cloud-agnostic interface      │  │
│  │   name     = "my-cluster"                                   │  │
│  │   version  = "1.29"                                         │  │
│  │   nodes    = 3                                              │  │
│  │ }                                                           │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                             │                                     │
│                             ▼                                     │
│  WRAPPER MODULE (./modules/k8s-cluster)                           │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ if var.provider == "aws"   → aws_eks_cluster                │  │
│  │ if var.provider == "azure" → azurerm_kubernetes_cluster      │  │
│  │ if var.provider == "gcp"   → google_container_cluster        │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                                   │
│  OUTPUT: Standardized { endpoint, ca_cert, token }                │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# modules/k8s-cluster/variables.tf
variable "cloud_provider" {
  description = "Cloud provider: aws, azure, or gcp"
  type        = string
  validation {
    condition     = contains(["aws", "azure", "gcp"], var.cloud_provider)
    error_message = "Must be aws, azure, or gcp."
  }
}

variable "cluster_name" {
  type = string
}

variable "cluster_version" {
  type    = string
  default = "1.29"
}

variable "node_count" {
  type    = number
  default = 3
}

variable "node_instance_type" {
  type    = string
  default = "medium"  # Abstracted — mapped internally
}

# modules/k8s-cluster/instance_map.tf
locals {
  instance_type_map = {
    aws = {
      small  = "t3.medium"
      medium = "t3.large"
      large  = "t3.xlarge"
    }
    azure = {
      small  = "Standard_D2s_v3"
      medium = "Standard_D4s_v3"
      large  = "Standard_D8s_v3"
    }
    gcp = {
      small  = "e2-medium"
      medium = "e2-standard-4"
      large  = "e2-standard-8"
    }
  }

  actual_instance_type = local.instance_type_map[var.cloud_provider][var.node_instance_type]
}

# modules/k8s-cluster/outputs.tf
output "cluster_endpoint" {
  value = (
    var.cloud_provider == "aws"   ? aws_eks_cluster.this[0].endpoint :
    var.cloud_provider == "azure" ? azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.this[0].kube_config[0].host :
    "https://${google_container_cluster.this[0].endpoint}"
  )
}

output "cluster_ca_certificate" {
  value = (
    var.cloud_provider == "aws"   ? base64decode(aws_eks_cluster.this[0].certificate_authority[0].data) :
    var.cloud_provider == "azure" ? base64decode(azurerm_kubernetes_cluster.this[0].kube_config[0].cluster_ca_certificate) :
    base64decode(google_container_cluster.this[0].master_auth[0].cluster_ca_certificate)
  )
  sensitive = true
}
52.2.2 Interface Standardization

Define consistent interfaces across cloud modules so callers don't need to know cloud specifics.

# Standard module interface — every cloud module implements these outputs
# modules/*/outputs.tf

# Compute
output "compute_id" {
  description = "Cloud-specific instance/VM ID"
  value       = local.compute_id
}

output "compute_public_ip" {
  description = "Public IP address"
  value       = local.public_ip
}

# Database
output "database_endpoint" {
  description = "Connection endpoint (host:port)"
  value       = local.db_endpoint
}

output "database_connection_string" {
  description = "Full connection string"
  value       = local.connection_string
  sensitive   = true
}

# Storage
output "storage_bucket_name" {
  description = "Bucket/container name"
  value       = local.bucket_name
}

output "storage_bucket_arn" {
  description = "Bucket ARN/ID for IAM policies"
  value       = local.bucket_id
}
# ROOT MODULE: Consume abstracted modules identically
module "primary_db" {
  source         = "./modules/database"
  cloud_provider = "aws"
  engine         = "postgresql"
  engine_version = "15"
  instance_size  = "medium"
  db_name        = "myapp"
}

module "dr_db" {
  source         = "./modules/database"
  cloud_provider = "azure"
  engine         = "postgresql"
  engine_version = "15"
  instance_size  = "medium"
  db_name        = "myapp"
}

# Both expose the same outputs:
# module.primary_db.database_endpoint → "mydb.xxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:5432"
# module.dr_db.database_endpoint      → "mydb.postgres.database.azure.com:5432"

52.3 Cross-Cloud Networking

NETWORKING
📌 Category: Multi-Cloud Connectivity

Connecting networks across clouds requires VPN tunnels or dedicated interconnects. IP address planning is critical — CIDRs must not overlap.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CROSS-CLOUD NETWORK TOPOLOGY                          │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  AWS VPC (10.0.0.0/16)           Azure VNet (10.1.0.0/16)        │
│  ┌──────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────┐            │
│  │  EKS Cluster     │            │  AKS Cluster     │            │
│  │  10.0.1.0/24     │            │  10.1.1.0/24     │            │
│  │                  │            │                  │            │
│  │  RDS Database    │            │  Azure SQL       │            │
│  │  10.0.2.0/24     │            │  10.1.2.0/24     │            │
│  └────────┬─────────┘            └────────┬─────────┘            │
│           │                               │                      │
│           │    ┌─────────────────────┐     │                      │
│           └────┤  VPN / Interconnect ├────┘                      │
│                │  (IPSec tunnels)    │                            │
│                └─────────────────────┘                            │
│                                                                   │
│  IP PLANNING:                                                      │
│  AWS:   10.0.0.0/16  (65,536 IPs)                                │
│  Azure: 10.1.0.0/16  (65,536 IPs)                                │
│  GCP:   10.2.0.0/16  (65,536 IPs)                                │
│  ← No CIDR overlap between any cloud →                              │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ─── AWS ↔ Azure VPN Connection ────────────────

# AWS Side: Virtual Private Gateway
resource "aws_vpn_gateway" "main" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-vpn-gw"
  }
}

resource "aws_customer_gateway" "azure" {
  bgp_asn    = 65000
  ip_address = azurerm_public_ip.vpn.ip_address
  type       = "ipsec.1"

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-azure-cgw"
  }
}

resource "aws_vpn_connection" "to_azure" {
  vpn_gateway_id      = aws_vpn_gateway.main.id
  customer_gateway_id = aws_customer_gateway.azure.id
  type                = "ipsec.1"
  static_routes_only  = true
}

resource "aws_vpn_connection_route" "azure_vnet" {
  vpn_connection_id      = aws_vpn_connection.to_azure.id
  destination_cidr_block = "10.1.0.0/16"  # Azure VNet CIDR
}

# Azure Side: VPN Gateway
resource "azurerm_virtual_network_gateway" "main" {
  name                = "${var.project}-vpn-gw"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  type                = "Vpn"
  vpn_type            = "RouteBased"
  sku                 = "VpnGw1"

  ip_configuration {
    public_ip_address_id          = azurerm_public_ip.vpn.id
    private_ip_address_allocation = "Dynamic"
    subnet_id                     = azurerm_subnet.gateway.id
  }
}

resource "azurerm_local_network_gateway" "aws" {
  name                = "aws-lng"
  location            = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  gateway_address     = aws_vpn_connection.to_azure.tunnel1_address
  address_space       = ["10.0.0.0/16"]  # AWS VPC CIDR
}

resource "azurerm_virtual_network_gateway_connection" "to_aws" {
  name                       = "aws-connection"
  location                   = azurerm_resource_group.main.location
  resource_group_name        = azurerm_resource_group.main.name
  type                       = "IPsec"
  virtual_network_gateway_id = azurerm_virtual_network_gateway.main.id
  local_network_gateway_id   = azurerm_local_network_gateway.aws.id
  shared_key                 = var.vpn_shared_key
}
💡 Cross-Cloud Costs: Data transfer between clouds is the #1 hidden cost in multi-cloud architectures. AWS charges $0.02-0.09/GB for outbound data, Azure charges $0.05-0.08/GB. A busy API transferring 10TB/month between clouds costs $200-900/month in transfer fees alone. Minimize cross-cloud traffic by keeping tightly coupled services on the same cloud.

52.4 Multi-Cloud CI/CD

AUTOMATION
📌 Category: Pipeline Design

Multi-cloud CI/CD pipelines must authenticate to multiple providers, manage separate state files, and coordinate deployments across clouds.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              MULTI-CLOUD CI/CD PIPELINE                            │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                   │
│  ┌──────────┐   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  Git Push │──▶│          CI/CD Platform                    │    │
│  └──────────┘   │     (GitHub Actions / GitLab CI)           │    │
│                 └────────┬───────────────┬───────────────┬───┘    │
│                          │               │               │        │
│                          ▼               ▼               ▼        │
│              ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌────────────┐    │
│              │  AWS Stage    │ │ Azure Stage   │ │ GCP Stage  │    │
│              │              │ │              │ │            │    │
│              │ OIDC Auth    │ │ OIDC Auth    │ │ WIF Auth   │    │
│              │ terraform    │ │ terraform    │ │ terraform  │    │
│              │   plan/apply │ │   plan/apply │ │  plan/apply│    │
│              │ S3 state     │ │ Blob state   │ │ GCS state  │    │
│              └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └────────────┘    │
│                                                                   │
│  KEY DESIGN DECISIONS:                                             │
│  ├── Per-cloud stages (parallel) vs sequential                    │
│  ├── Shared approval gate vs independent                          │
│  ├── Rollback: all-or-nothing vs independent                     │
│  └── Credentials: OIDC federation (no secrets stored)             │
│                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# .github/workflows/multi-cloud-deploy.yml
name: Multi-Cloud Terraform Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

jobs:
  # ─── AWS Deployment ─────────────────────────
  deploy-aws:
    name: Deploy AWS Infrastructure
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: ./terraform/aws

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Configure AWS Credentials (OIDC)
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::role/github-terraform
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - run: terraform init
      - run: terraform plan -out=tfplan
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'

  # ─── Azure Deployment ──────────────────────
  deploy-azure:
    name: Deploy Azure Infrastructure
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: ./terraform/azure

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - run: terraform init
      - run: terraform plan -out=tfplan
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'

  # ─── Cross-Cloud Validation ────────────────
  validate-connectivity:
    name: Validate Cross-Cloud Connectivity
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [deploy-aws, deploy-azure]
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Test VPN Connectivity
        run: |
          # Verify cross-cloud connectivity
          echo "Testing AWS → Azure connectivity..."
          echo "Testing Azure → AWS connectivity..."
# Terraform directory structure for multi-cloud
# terraform/
# ├── aws/
# │   ├── main.tf          # AWS resources
# │   ├── providers.tf     # AWS provider config
# │   ├── backend.tf       # S3 backend
# │   ├── variables.tf
# │   └── outputs.tf
# ├── azure/
# │   ├── main.tf          # Azure resources
# │   ├── providers.tf     # AzureRM provider config
# │   ├── backend.tf       # Azure Blob backend
# │   ├── variables.tf
# │   └── outputs.tf
# ├── cross-cloud/
# │   ├── main.tf          # VPN connections, DNS
# │   ├── providers.tf     # Both AWS + Azure providers
# │   ├── backend.tf       # S3 or Blob backend
# │   └── data.tf          # Remote state data sources
# └── modules/
#     ├── k8s-cluster/      # Cloud-agnostic K8s module
#     ├── database/          # Cloud-agnostic DB module
#     └── networking/        # Cloud-agnostic network module
💡 OIDC Federation: Never store cloud credentials as CI/CD secrets. Use OIDC federation (AWS IAM Identity Provider, Azure Federated Credentials, GCP Workload Identity Federation) so your CI/CD platform exchanges a short-lived token for cloud access — no long-lived keys to rotate or leak.
🔧 ⚙️ 🧪 📦
CHAPTER 53

Custom Provider Development

Go Lang Plugin Framework Testing Registry SDK
4Sections
11Subsections
20+Examples
🔧Advanced
PART 15 EXPERT ★★★★★

When no existing provider covers your internal platform, proprietary API, or custom workflow, you can build your own Terraform provider. Custom providers are written in Go and follow HashiCorp's Plugin Framework — giving your internal tools the same declarative, plan-and-apply workflow as AWS, Azure, and GCP resources. This chapter walks you from "When should I build one?" through implementation, testing, and publishing to the Terraform Registry.

Learning Objectives

  • Determine when a custom provider is the right solution vs. alternatives
  • Understand the Terraform Plugin Framework architecture and Go project structure
  • Implement provider schemas, resources, and data sources in Go
  • Write acceptance and unit tests with HashiCorp's testing framework
  • Publish providers to the Terraform Registry with proper documentation and versioning

Chapter Contents

53.1 Provider Development Overview 53.2 Terraform Plugin Framework 53.3 Provider Testing 53.4 Publishing Providers

53.1 Provider Development Overview

CONCEPTS
� Official Documentation: Plugin Framework Docs

Before writing a single line of Go code, you need to understand when a custom provider is the right tool — and when simpler alternatives exist.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're evaluating whether building a custom provider is justified. Creating a provider is a significant engineering investment (hundreds of lines of Go, testing infrastructure, ongoing maintenance). You should only build one when no existing provider covers your needs and simpler alternatives (like the http provider, external data source, or provisioners) won't work.

  • Know the decision criteria for building vs. not building a custom provider
  • Understand the two SDK options: Plugin Framework (recommended) vs. SDKv2 (legacy)
53.1.1 When to Create Custom Providers

The decision to build a custom provider should be deliberate. The diagram below shows a decision tree that maps common scenarios to the right solution. The key question is: "Do I need full CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) lifecycle management for an API resource?" If yes, a provider is the right answer. If you just need to read data or run a one-off script, simpler tools exist. 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              SHOULD YOU BUILD A CUSTOM PROVIDER?                         │
│                     Decision Tree                                        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  Does a provider already exist on the registry?                          │
│  ├── YES → Use the existing provider                                     │
│  └── NO ↓                                                                │
│                                                                          │
│  Do you need full CRUD lifecycle? (create, read, update, delete)         │
│  ├── NO → Use simpler alternatives:                                      │
│  │        • external data source (read from scripts)                     │
│  │        • http provider (read from REST APIs)                          │
│  │        • null_resource + provisioner (one-off scripts)                │
│  │        • shell provider (script-based CRUD)                           │
│  └── YES ↓                                                               │
│                                                                          │
│  Is this for internal/private use only?                                  │
│  ├── YES → Build private provider (internal registry or filesystem)      │
│  └── NO ↓                                                                │
│                                                                          │
│  Will multiple teams/orgs use it?                                        │
│  ├── YES → Build + publish to Terraform Registry                         │
│  └── NO → Build private, evaluate publishing later                       │
│                                                                          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│           COMMON USE CASES FOR CUSTOM PROVIDERS                          │
│                                                                          │
│  ✅ Internal platform APIs (deployment systems, service catalogs)        │
│  ✅ Proprietary SaaS products with REST/gRPC APIs                       │
│  ✅ Legacy systems with custom APIs (mainframes, on-prem appliances)    │
│  ✅ Domain-specific workflows (compliance, ticketing, approvals)         │
│  ❌ One-time provisioning scripts (use provisioner instead)              │
│  ❌ Simple HTTP data reads (use http or external data source)            │
│  ❌ Wrapping existing providers (use modules instead)                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Real-World Example: Imagine your company has an internal "Platform API" that creates Kubernetes namespaces, assigns resource quotas, sets up CI/CD pipelines, and configures SSO. No public Terraform provider exists. A custom provider would let teams write resource "platform_namespace" "api" in their Terraform code, getting plan/apply/destroy lifecycle management, drift detection, and state tracking — all the benefits Terraform provides for AWS resources, but for your internal platform.
53.1.2 Provider SDK Options

HashiCorp offers two SDKs for building providers. The Plugin Framework is the modern, recommended option. SDKv2 is the legacy SDK — still functional and used by many existing providers, but no longer receiving new features. The table below compares them so you can make an informed choice.

📌

SDK Comparison: Plugin Framework vs SDKv2

Feature Plugin Framework (Recommended) SDKv2 (Legacy)
Status ✅ Active development ⚠️ Maintenance mode
Go Module terraform-plugin-framework terraform-plugin-sdk/v2
Schema Model Strongly typed (Go structs) Map-based (schema.Schema)
Diagnostics Fine-grained per-attribute errors Resource-level errors only
Validation Built-in validators + custom ValidateFunc per attribute
Plan Modification Attribute-level plan modifiers CustomizeDiff (resource-level)
New Providers ✅ Use this ❌ Only for existing providers

Can they coexist? Yes. HashiCorp provides terraform-plugin-mux, which lets you combine Framework resources and SDKv2 resources in the same provider. This is how large providers like azurerm gradually migrate from SDKv2 to the Framework without rewriting everything at once.

53.2 Terraform Plugin Framework

IMPLEMENTATION ADVANCED
� Official Documentation: Getting Started Guide

The Plugin Framework is HashiCorp's modern Go library for building Terraform providers. It uses strongly-typed Go structs, attribute-level validators, and a clear separation between schema definition and CRUD operations.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning to build a provider from scratch using Go and the Plugin Framework. This section walks through the four major components: understanding Framework vs SDKv2 architecture, defining provider schemas, implementing resource CRUD operations, and building data sources. Each subsection builds on the previous one, creating a complete working provider by the end.

  • Set up a Go project structure for a Terraform provider
  • Define provider configuration schemas with typed attributes
  • Implement Create, Read, Update, Delete operations for resources
  • Build read-only data sources for querying external APIs
53.2.1 Framework vs SDK v2

The Plugin Framework and SDKv2 solve the same problem — bridging Terraform's gRPC protocol with your Go code — but they do so with fundamentally different architectures. The Framework uses Go structs as schema models (type-safe, compile-time checks), while SDKv2 uses maps of schema.Schema (looser, runtime checks). The diagram below illustrates the architecture. 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           TERRAFORM PROVIDER ARCHITECTURE                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  ┌──────────────┐     gRPC        ┌─────────────────────┐               │
│  │   Terraform  │ ◄─────────────► │   Provider Binary    │              │
│  │    CLI        │   (Protocol)    │   (Your Go Code)     │              │
│  └──────────────┘                  └──────────┬──────────┘               │
│                                               │                          │
│                          ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐     │
│                          │                    │                    │     │
│                 ┌────────▼────────┐ ┌─────────▼────────┐ ┌────────▼──┐  │
│                 │    Provider      │ │    Resources      │ │   Data    │  │
│                 │   Configure()    │ │   CRUD Methods    │ │  Sources  │  │
│                 │   (API client)   │ │   Create/Read/    │ │  Read()   │  │
│                 │                  │ │   Update/Delete   │ │           │  │
│                 └────────┬────────┘ └─────────┬────────┘ └────────┬──┘  │
│                          │                    │                    │     │
│                          └────────────────────┼────────────────────┘     │
│                                               │                          │
│                                    ┌──────────▼──────────┐               │
│                                    │   Your API Client    │              │
│                                    │   (HTTP/gRPC calls   │              │
│                                    │    to your service)   │              │
│                                    └─────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                          │
│  ─── Plugin Framework Flow ──────────────────────────────────────────    │
│  1. Terraform CLI sends gRPC request (e.g., "Create resource")           │
│  2. Framework deserializes request into your Go struct                    │
│  3. Your Create() method calls your API                                  │
│  4. You populate the response struct with the created resource's data    │
│  5. Framework serializes response back to Terraform via gRPC             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Setting up a provider project requires a specific Go module structure. The directory layout below is the standard convention that HashiCorp recommends and the Terraform Registry expects. 

# ============================================================================
# Standard Provider Project Structure
# ============================================================================
# terraform-provider-myplatform/
# ├── main.go                          # Entry point, starts the provider server
# ├── go.mod                           # Go module definition
# ├── go.sum                           # Dependency checksums
# ├── internal/
# │   └── provider/
# │       ├── provider.go              # Provider definition + Configure()
# │       ├── provider_test.go         # Provider-level tests
# │       ├── resource_namespace.go    # Resource: myplatform_namespace
# │       ├── resource_namespace_test.go
# │       ├── data_source_service.go   # Data source: myplatform_service
# │       └── data_source_service_test.go
# ├── docs/                            # Generated documentation for registry
# │   ├── index.md
# │   ├── resources/
# │   │   └── namespace.md
# │   └── data-sources/
# │       └── service.md
# ├── examples/                        # Example .tf files for docs
# │   ├── provider/
# │   │   └── provider.tf
# │   ├── resources/
# │   │   └── namespace/
# │   │       └── resource.tf
# │   └── data-sources/
# │       └── service/
# │           └── data-source.tf
# ├── GNUmakefile                      # Build, test, install targets
# └── .goreleaser.yml                  # Release automation for Registry
53.2.2 Provider Schema

The provider schema defines the configuration block that users write in their .tf files to set up the provider (API endpoint, credentials, etc.). In the Plugin Framework, you define a Go struct that maps to the HCL attributes, then implement the Schema() and Configure() methods. The Configure() method is where you create the API client that resources and data sources will use. 

// ============================================================================
// provider.go — Provider Definition
// ============================================================================
package provider

import (
    "context"
    "os"

    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/datasource"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/provider"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/provider/schema"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/resource"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/types"

    "github.com/myorg/terraform-provider-myplatform/internal/client"
)

// Ensure the implementation satisfies the provider interface
var _ provider.Provider = &MyPlatformProvider{}

// MyPlatformProvider — the provider struct
type MyPlatformProvider struct {
    version string  // Set during build via ldflags
}

// Provider-level configuration model (maps to HCL attributes)
type MyPlatformProviderModel struct {
    Endpoint types.String `tfsdk:"endpoint"`
    ApiKey   types.String `tfsdk:"api_key"`
    TeamID   types.String `tfsdk:"team_id"`
}

// Metadata — returns the provider type name
func (p *MyPlatformProvider) Metadata(_ context.Context,
    _ provider.MetadataRequest, resp *provider.MetadataResponse) {
    resp.TypeName = "myplatform"  // Used as prefix: myplatform_namespace
    resp.Version = p.version
}

// Schema — defines the provider configuration block
func (p *MyPlatformProvider) Schema(_ context.Context,
    _ provider.SchemaRequest, resp *provider.SchemaResponse) {
    resp.Schema = schema.Schema{
        Description: "Manage MyPlatform resources (namespaces, services, quotas).",
        Attributes: map[string]schema.Attribute{
            "endpoint": schema.StringAttribute{
                Description: "MyPlatform API endpoint URL.",
                Optional:    true,
                // Defaults to env var MYPLATFORM_ENDPOINT if not set
            },
            "api_key": schema.StringAttribute{
                Description: "API key for authentication.",
                Optional:    true,
                Sensitive:   true,  // ← Masked in plan output
            },
            "team_id": schema.StringAttribute{
                Description: "Default team ID for resources.",
                Optional:    true,
            },
        },
    }
}

// Configure — creates the API client shared by all resources
func (p *MyPlatformProvider) Configure(ctx context.Context,
    req provider.ConfigureRequest, resp *provider.ConfigureResponse) {

    var config MyPlatformProviderModel
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(req.Config.Get(ctx, &config)...)
    if resp.Diagnostics.HasError() {
        return
    }

    // Allow environment variable overrides
    endpoint := os.Getenv("MYPLATFORM_ENDPOINT")
    if !config.Endpoint.IsNull() {
        endpoint = config.Endpoint.ValueString()
    }

    apiKey := os.Getenv("MYPLATFORM_API_KEY")
    if !config.ApiKey.IsNull() {
        apiKey = config.ApiKey.ValueString()
    }

    if endpoint == "" {
        resp.Diagnostics.AddError(
            "Missing API Endpoint",
            "Set the 'endpoint' attribute or MYPLATFORM_ENDPOINT env var.",
        )
        return
    }

    // Create the API client
    apiClient := client.NewClient(endpoint, apiKey)

    // Share client with resources and data sources
    resp.DataSourceData = apiClient
    resp.ResourceData = apiClient
}

// Resources — registers all managed resources
func (p *MyPlatformProvider) Resources(_ context.Context) []func() resource.Resource {
    return []func() resource.Resource{
        NewNamespaceResource,
        // Add more resources here as you build them
    }
}

// DataSources — registers all data sources
func (p *MyPlatformProvider) DataSources(_ context.Context) []func() datasource.DataSource {
    return []func() datasource.DataSource{
        NewServiceDataSource,
    }
}

The corresponding HCL that users would write to configure this provider: 

# How users configure our custom provider
terraform {
  required_providers {
    myplatform = {
      source  = "myorg/myplatform"
      version = "~> 1.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "myplatform" {
  endpoint = "https://api.myplatform.internal"
  api_key  = var.myplatform_api_key   # Or use MYPLATFORM_API_KEY env var
  team_id  = "team-engineering"
}
53.2.3 Resource Implementation

A resource is the core building block of a provider. Each resource implements four CRUD operations: Create (called when a new resource appears in config), Read (called during plan to detect drift), Update (called when attributes change), and Delete (called when the resource is removed from config or terraform destroy runs).

The code below implements a complete myplatform_namespace resource. Pay attention to the pattern: each CRUD method (1) reads the plan/state into a typed Go struct, (2) calls the API client, (3) maps the API response back into the struct, and (4) saves it to Terraform state. 

// ============================================================================
// resource_namespace.go — Full CRUD Resource
// ============================================================================
package provider

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"

    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/resource"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/resource/schema"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/resource/schema/planmodifier"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/resource/schema/stringplanmodifier"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/types"

    "github.com/myorg/terraform-provider-myplatform/internal/client"
)

var _ resource.Resource = &NamespaceResource{}
var _ resource.ResourceWithImportState = &NamespaceResource{}

type NamespaceResource struct {
    client *client.Client  // API client from provider Configure()
}

// The data model — maps to HCL attributes
type NamespaceResourceModel struct {
    ID          types.String `tfsdk:"id"`
    Name        types.String `tfsdk:"name"`
    Description types.String `tfsdk:"description"`
    TeamID      types.String `tfsdk:"team_id"`
    CpuQuota    types.Int64  `tfsdk:"cpu_quota"`
    MemoryQuota types.Int64  `tfsdk:"memory_quota"`
    Status      types.String `tfsdk:"status"`      // Computed
    CreatedAt   types.String `tfsdk:"created_at"`   // Computed
}

func NewNamespaceResource() resource.Resource {
    return &NamespaceResource{}
}

func (r *NamespaceResource) Metadata(_ context.Context,
    req resource.MetadataRequest, resp *resource.MetadataResponse) {
    resp.TypeName = req.ProviderTypeName + "_namespace"
    // → "myplatform_namespace"
}

func (r *NamespaceResource) Schema(_ context.Context,
    _ resource.SchemaRequest, resp *resource.SchemaResponse) {
    resp.Schema = schema.Schema{
        Description: "Manages a platform namespace with resource quotas.",
        Attributes: map[string]schema.Attribute{
            "id": schema.StringAttribute{
                Computed: true,
                PlanModifiers: []planmodifier.String{
                    stringplanmodifier.UseStateForUnknown(),
                },
            },
            "name": schema.StringAttribute{
                Required:    true,
                Description: "Unique namespace name (3-63 chars, lowercase).",
                PlanModifiers: []planmodifier.String{
                    stringplanmodifier.RequiresReplace(),
                    // ^ Changing name forces resource recreation
                },
            },
            "description": schema.StringAttribute{
                Optional:    true,
                Description: "Human-readable description.",
            },
            "team_id": schema.StringAttribute{
                Required:    true,
                Description: "Team that owns this namespace.",
            },
            "cpu_quota": schema.Int64Attribute{
                Optional:    true,
                Description: "CPU quota in millicores (default: 4000).",
            },
            "memory_quota": schema.Int64Attribute{
                Optional:    true,
                Description: "Memory quota in MiB (default: 8192).",
            },
            "status": schema.StringAttribute{
                Computed:    true,
                Description: "Current status (active, provisioning, error).",
            },
            "created_at": schema.StringAttribute{
                Computed:    true,
                Description: "ISO 8601 creation timestamp.",
            },
        },
    }
}

// Configure — receive the API client from the provider
func (r *NamespaceResource) Configure(_ context.Context,
    req resource.ConfigureRequest, resp *resource.ConfigureResponse) {
    if req.ProviderData == nil {
        return
    }
    r.client = req.ProviderData.(*client.Client)
}

// ─── CREATE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
func (r *NamespaceResource) Create(ctx context.Context,
    req resource.CreateRequest, resp *resource.CreateResponse) {

    // 1. Read planned values into our model struct
    var plan NamespaceResourceModel
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(req.Plan.Get(ctx, &plan)...)
    if resp.Diagnostics.HasError() {
        return
    }

    // 2. Call our API
    ns, err := r.client.CreateNamespace(client.CreateNamespaceInput{
        Name:        plan.Name.ValueString(),
        Description: plan.Description.ValueString(),
        TeamID:      plan.TeamID.ValueString(),
        CpuQuota:    plan.CpuQuota.ValueInt64(),
        MemoryQuota: plan.MemoryQuota.ValueInt64(),
    })
    if err != nil {
        resp.Diagnostics.AddError("Create Failed",
            fmt.Sprintf("Unable to create namespace: %s", err))
        return
    }

    // 3. Map API response back to the model
    plan.ID = types.StringValue(ns.ID)
    plan.Status = types.StringValue(ns.Status)
    plan.CreatedAt = types.StringValue(ns.CreatedAt)

    // 4. Save to Terraform state
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(resp.State.Set(ctx, &plan)...)
}

// ─── READ ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
func (r *NamespaceResource) Read(ctx context.Context,
    req resource.ReadRequest, resp *resource.ReadResponse) {

    var state NamespaceResourceModel
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(req.State.Get(ctx, &state)...)
    if resp.Diagnostics.HasError() {
        return
    }

    // Call API to get current state
    ns, err := r.client.GetNamespace(state.ID.ValueString())
    if err != nil {
        resp.Diagnostics.AddError("Read Failed",
            fmt.Sprintf("Unable to read namespace %s: %s", state.ID.ValueString(), err))
        return
    }

    // Update state with latest API values (drift detection)
    state.Name = types.StringValue(ns.Name)
    state.Description = types.StringValue(ns.Description)
    state.TeamID = types.StringValue(ns.TeamID)
    state.CpuQuota = types.Int64Value(ns.CpuQuota)
    state.MemoryQuota = types.Int64Value(ns.MemoryQuota)
    state.Status = types.StringValue(ns.Status)

    resp.Diagnostics.Append(resp.State.Set(ctx, &state)...)
}

// ─── UPDATE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
func (r *NamespaceResource) Update(ctx context.Context,
    req resource.UpdateRequest, resp *resource.UpdateResponse) {

    var plan NamespaceResourceModel
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(req.Plan.Get(ctx, &plan)...)
    if resp.Diagnostics.HasError() {
        return
    }

    ns, err := r.client.UpdateNamespace(plan.ID.ValueString(),
        client.UpdateNamespaceInput{
            Description: plan.Description.ValueString(),
            CpuQuota:    plan.CpuQuota.ValueInt64(),
            MemoryQuota: plan.MemoryQuota.ValueInt64(),
        })
    if err != nil {
        resp.Diagnostics.AddError("Update Failed",
            fmt.Sprintf("Unable to update namespace: %s", err))
        return
    }

    plan.Status = types.StringValue(ns.Status)
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(resp.State.Set(ctx, &plan)...)
}

// ─── DELETE ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
func (r *NamespaceResource) Delete(ctx context.Context,
    req resource.DeleteRequest, resp *resource.DeleteResponse) {

    var state NamespaceResourceModel
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(req.State.Get(ctx, &state)...)
    if resp.Diagnostics.HasError() {
        return
    }

    err := r.client.DeleteNamespace(state.ID.ValueString())
    if err != nil {
        resp.Diagnostics.AddError("Delete Failed",
            fmt.Sprintf("Unable to delete namespace: %s", err))
    }
    // State is automatically removed on successful delete
}

// ─── IMPORT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
func (r *NamespaceResource) ImportState(ctx context.Context,
    req resource.ImportStateRequest, resp *resource.ImportStateResponse) {
    // terraform import myplatform_namespace.example ns-abc123
    resource.ImportStatePassthroughID(ctx, path.Root("id"), req, resp)
}
💡 Pattern Summary: Every CRUD method follows the same 4-step pattern: (1) Deserialize plan/state → Go struct, (2) Call your API, (3) Map API response → Go struct, (4) Serialize Go struct → Terraform state. Once you internalize this pattern, implementing new resources becomes straightforward.
53.2.4 Data Source Implementation

Data sources are simpler than resources — they only implement Read (no Create, Update, or Delete). Users use them to query existing objects from your API without managing their lifecycle. The implementation pattern is the same as a resource's Read method. 

// ============================================================================
// data_source_service.go — Read-Only Data Source
// ============================================================================
package provider

import (
    "context"
    "fmt"

    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/datasource"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/datasource/schema"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/types"

    "github.com/myorg/terraform-provider-myplatform/internal/client"
)

var _ datasource.DataSource = &ServiceDataSource{}

type ServiceDataSource struct {
    client *client.Client
}

type ServiceDataSourceModel struct {
    Name        types.String `tfsdk:"name"`
    ID          types.String `tfsdk:"id"`
    Namespace   types.String `tfsdk:"namespace"`
    Endpoint    types.String `tfsdk:"endpoint"`
    Replicas    types.Int64  `tfsdk:"replicas"`
    HealthCheck types.String `tfsdk:"health_check_url"`
}

func NewServiceDataSource() datasource.DataSource {
    return &ServiceDataSource{}
}

func (d *ServiceDataSource) Metadata(_ context.Context,
    req datasource.MetadataRequest, resp *datasource.MetadataResponse) {
    resp.TypeName = req.ProviderTypeName + "_service"
}

func (d *ServiceDataSource) Schema(_ context.Context,
    _ datasource.SchemaRequest, resp *datasource.SchemaResponse) {
    resp.Schema = schema.Schema{
        Description: "Look up an existing service by name and namespace.",
        Attributes: map[string]schema.Attribute{
            "name": schema.StringAttribute{
                Required:    true,
                Description: "Service name to look up.",
            },
            "namespace": schema.StringAttribute{
                Required:    true,
                Description: "Namespace containing the service.",
            },
            "id": schema.StringAttribute{
                Computed: true,
            },
            "endpoint": schema.StringAttribute{
                Computed:    true,
                Description: "Service endpoint URL.",
            },
            "replicas": schema.Int64Attribute{
                Computed: true,
            },
            "health_check_url": schema.StringAttribute{
                Computed: true,
            },
        },
    }
}

func (d *ServiceDataSource) Configure(_ context.Context,
    req datasource.ConfigureRequest, resp *datasource.ConfigureResponse) {
    if req.ProviderData == nil {
        return
    }
    d.client = req.ProviderData.(*client.Client)
}

func (d *ServiceDataSource) Read(ctx context.Context,
    req datasource.ReadRequest, resp *datasource.ReadResponse) {

    var config ServiceDataSourceModel
    resp.Diagnostics.Append(req.Config.Get(ctx, &config)...)
    if resp.Diagnostics.HasError() {
        return
    }

    svc, err := d.client.GetService(
        config.Namespace.ValueString(),
        config.Name.ValueString(),
    )
    if err != nil {
        resp.Diagnostics.AddError("Read Failed",
            fmt.Sprintf("Unable to read service: %s", err))
        return
    }

    config.ID = types.StringValue(svc.ID)
    config.Endpoint = types.StringValue(svc.Endpoint)
    config.Replicas = types.Int64Value(svc.Replicas)
    config.HealthCheck = types.StringValue(svc.HealthCheckURL)

    resp.Diagnostics.Append(resp.State.Set(ctx, &config)...)
}

Users would consume this data source in their HCL like this: 

# Look up an existing service from our platform API
data "myplatform_service" "api" {
  name      = "user-service"
  namespace = "production"
}

# Use the data source outputs
output "api_endpoint" {
  value = data.myplatform_service.api.endpoint
}

output "api_replicas" {
  value = data.myplatform_service.api.replicas
}

53.3 Provider Testing

TESTING ESSENTIAL
� Official Documentation: Testing Guide

Terraform providers need two types of tests: acceptance tests that exercise the full Terraform lifecycle against a real (or sandbox) API, and unit tests that verify pure logic functions without network calls.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're learning the testing patterns that HashiCorp requires for providers published to the Terraform Registry. Acceptance tests are the gold standard — they run real terraform plan and apply operations, verify resource attributes, and run destroy at the end. Unit tests complement them by testing validation logic, helper functions, and data transformations without touching any API.

  • Write acceptance tests using HashiCorp's testing framework
  • Verify resource creation, attribute values, and state cleanup
  • Write fast, isolated unit tests for helper logic
53.3.1 Acceptance Tests

Acceptance tests spin up a real Terraform instance, apply a configuration, check that the resource was created with the correct attributes, and then destroy it. They are gated by the TF_ACC environment variable — if it's not set, the tests are skipped (this prevents accidentally running expensive API calls during go test). 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              ACCEPTANCE TEST LIFECYCLE                                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  TF_ACC=1 go test ./internal/provider/ -run TestAccNamespace             │
│  │                                                                       │
│  ├──  Step 1: Apply Config                                               │
│  │    • Runs terraform plan + apply with the HCL you provide             │
│  │    • Creates the actual resource via your Create() method             │
│  │                                                                       │
│  ├──  Step 2: Check Functions                                            │
│  │    • resource.TestCheckResourceAttr("myplatform_namespace.test",      │
│  │      "name", "test-ns")                                               │
│  │    • Verifies attributes in the Terraform state match expected values │
│  │                                                                       │
│  ├──  Step 3: (Optional) Update Step                                     │
│  │    • Apply a modified config → exercises Update() method              │
│  │                                                                       │
│  ├──  Step 4: ImportState                                                │
│  │    • Tests terraform import with just the resource ID                 │
│  │                                                                       │
│  └──  Cleanup: Destroy                                                   │
│       • Automatically runs terraform destroy at the end                  │
│       • CheckDestroy verifies the resource no longer exists via API      │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

// ============================================================================
// resource_namespace_test.go — Acceptance Tests
// ============================================================================
package provider

import (
    "fmt"
    "testing"

    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-testing/helper/resource"
    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-testing/terraform"
)

func TestAccNamespace_basic(t *testing.T) {
    resource.Test(t, resource.TestCase{
        PreCheck:                 func() { testAccPreCheck(t) },
        ProtoV6ProviderFactories: testAccProtoV6ProviderFactories,
        CheckDestroy:             testAccCheckNamespaceDestroy,
        Steps: []resource.TestStep{
            // Step 1: Create and verify
            {
                Config: testAccNamespaceConfig("test-ns", "Test namespace"),
                Check: resource.ComposeAggregateTestCheckFunc(
                    resource.TestCheckResourceAttr(
                        "myplatform_namespace.test", "name", "test-ns"),
                    resource.TestCheckResourceAttr(
                        "myplatform_namespace.test", "description", "Test namespace"),
                    resource.TestCheckResourceAttr(
                        "myplatform_namespace.test", "status", "active"),
                    resource.TestCheckResourceAttrSet(
                        "myplatform_namespace.test", "id"),
                    resource.TestCheckResourceAttrSet(
                        "myplatform_namespace.test", "created_at"),
                ),
            },
            // Step 2: ImportState verification
            {
                ResourceName:      "myplatform_namespace.test",
                ImportState:       true,
                ImportStateVerify: true,
            },
            // Step 3: Update description
            {
                Config: testAccNamespaceConfig("test-ns", "Updated description"),
                Check: resource.ComposeAggregateTestCheckFunc(
                    resource.TestCheckResourceAttr(
                        "myplatform_namespace.test", "description", "Updated description"),
                ),
            },
        },
    })
}

// Template for test configurations
func testAccNamespaceConfig(name, description string) string {
    return fmt.Sprintf(`
resource "myplatform_namespace" "test" {
  name        = %[1]q
  description = %[2]q
  team_id     = "team-testing"
  cpu_quota   = 2000
  memory_quota = 4096
}
`, name, description)
}

// Verify the resource was actually destroyed
func testAccCheckNamespaceDestroy(s *terraform.State) error {
    for _, rs := range s.RootModule().Resources {
        if rs.Type != "myplatform_namespace" {
            continue
        }
        // Try to read the namespace — should return 404
        _, err := testAccClient.GetNamespace(rs.Primary.ID)
        if err == nil {
            return fmt.Errorf("namespace %s still exists after destroy", rs.Primary.ID)
        }
    }
    return nil
}
💡 Running Tests: Always run acceptance tests with TF_ACC=1 go test ./internal/provider/ -v -timeout 30m. The -timeout flag is critical because Terraform operations can be slow. Without it, Go's default 10-minute timeout may kill long-running tests.
53.3.2 Unit Tests

Unit tests are fast (no API calls) and test pure functions: validation logic, data transformation helpers, configuration parsing, and error handling. They complement acceptance tests by catching logic bugs before expensive API integration tests run. 

// ============================================================================
// validation_test.go — Unit Tests (No API calls, fast)
// ============================================================================
package provider

import (
    "testing"
)

func TestValidateNamespaceName(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        name    string
        input   string
        wantErr bool
    }{
        {"valid lowercase", "my-namespace", false},
        {"valid with numbers", "ns-123", false},
        {"too short", "ab", true},                // Min 3 chars
        {"too long", string(make([]byte, 64)), true},  // Max 63 chars
        {"uppercase", "My-Namespace", true},           // Must be lowercase
        {"spaces", "my namespace", true},              // No spaces
        {"starts with hyphen", "-invalid", true},      // Must start with letter
        {"empty", "", true},
    }

    for _, tt := range tests {
        t.Run(tt.name, func(t *testing.T) {
            err := validateNamespaceName(tt.input)
            if (err != nil) != tt.wantErr {
                t.Errorf("validateNamespaceName(%q) error = %v, wantErr %v",
                    tt.input, err, tt.wantErr)
            }
        })
    }
}

func TestBuildEndpointURL(t *testing.T) {
    tests := []struct {
        base     string
        path     string
        expected string
    }{
        {"https://api.example.com", "/v1/namespaces", "https://api.example.com/v1/namespaces"},
        {"https://api.example.com/", "/v1/namespaces", "https://api.example.com/v1/namespaces"},
        {"https://api.example.com", "v1/namespaces", "https://api.example.com/v1/namespaces"},
    }

    for _, tt := range tests {
        result := buildEndpointURL(tt.base, tt.path)
        if result != tt.expected {
            t.Errorf("buildEndpointURL(%q, %q) = %q, want %q",
                tt.base, tt.path, result, tt.expected)
        }
    }
}
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              TESTING STRATEGY PYRAMID                                    │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│                        ╱╲                                                │
│                       ╱  ╲         Acceptance Tests                      │
│                      ╱ E2E╲        • Full Terraform lifecycle            │
│                     ╱      ╲       • Slow, expensive, comprehensive      │
│                    ╱────────╲      • Run in CI/CD (gated by TF_ACC)      │
│                   ╱          ╲                                            │
│                  ╱ Integration╲    Integration Tests                      │
│                 ╱              ╲   • API client against sandbox           │
│                ╱────────────────╲  • Medium speed, focused on API calls   │
│               ╱                  ╲                                        │
│              ╱    Unit Tests      ╲  Unit Tests                           │
│             ╱                      ╲ • Fast, no network, pure logic       │
│            ╱________________________╲• Validation, helpers, transforms     │
│                                                                          │
│  Goal: Many fast unit tests, moderate integration, few full acceptance   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

53.4 Publishing Providers

PUBLISHING REGISTRY
� Official Documentation: Publishing Guide

Once your provider is tested, you need to sign it with GPG, build release binaries for multiple platforms, generate documentation, and publish it to the Terraform Registry.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're preparing your provider for public or private distribution through the Terraform Registry. This involves meeting specific naming conventions, setting up GoReleaser for cross-platform builds, generating documentation from your schema, and following semantic versioning for upgrades.

  • Configure GoReleaser to produce signed, multi-platform binaries
  • Generate documentation automatically from provider schemas
  • Follow semantic versioning and maintain a changelog
53.4.1 Registry Requirements

The Terraform Registry enforces strict naming and signing rules. Your GitHub repository must be named terraform-provider-NAME, and every release must include a GPG-signed SHA256SUMS file. GoReleaser automates the build and signing process.

Requirement Details Example
Repository Name Must follow terraform-provider-NAME terraform-provider-myplatform
Go Module Must match GitHub path github.com/myorg/terraform-provider-myplatform
GPG Key ASCII-armored public key uploaded to Registry 4096-bit RSA key recommended
Release Assets Per-OS/arch ZIP + SHA256SUMS + .sig terraform-provider-myplatform_1.0.0_linux_amd64.zip
Supported Platforms Minimum: linux_amd64, darwin_amd64, windows_amd64 Also: linux_arm64, darwin_arm64
Manifest terraform-registry-manifest.json in repo root Protocol version 6 
# .goreleaser.yml — Cross-platform builds with GPG signing
# ============================================================================
# Triggered by: git tag v1.0.0 && git push origin v1.0.0
# ============================================================================

before:
  hooks:
    - go mod tidy
    - go generate ./...

builds:
  - env:
      - CGO_ENABLED=0
    mod_timestamp: "{{ .CommitTimestamp }}"
    flags:
      - -trimpath
    ldflags:
      - "-s -w -X main.version={{.Version}} -X main.commit={{.Commit}}"
    goos:
      - linux
      - darwin
      - windows
    goarch:
      - amd64
      - arm64
    binary: "{{ .ProjectName }}_v{{ .Version }}"

archives:
  - format: zip
    name_template: "{{ .ProjectName }}_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}"

checksum:
  name_template: "{{ .ProjectName }}_{{ .Version }}_SHA256SUMS"
  algorithm: sha256

signs:
  - artifacts: checksum
    args:
      - "--batch"
      - "--local-user"
      - "{{ .Env.GPG_FINGERPRINT }}"
      - "--output"
      - "${signature}"
      - "--detach-sign"
      - "${artifact}"

release:
  draft: false

changelog:
  sort: asc
  filters:
    exclude:
      - "^docs:"
      - "^test:"
      - "^ci:"
53.4.2 Documentation

The Terraform Registry renders documentation directly from a docs/ folder in your repository. HashiCorp provides the tfplugindocs tool that auto-generates Markdown files from your provider's schema definitions — keeping docs always in sync with code. 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PROVIDER DOCUMENTATION STRUCTURE                             │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│  docs/                                                                   │
│  ├── index.md                   ← Provider overview & configuration      │
│  ├── resources/                                                          │
│  │   ├── namespace.md           ← myplatform_namespace resource          │
│  │   ├── service.md             ← myplatform_service resource            │
│  │   └── deployment.md          ← myplatform_deployment resource         │
│  ├── data-sources/                                                       │
│  │   ├── namespace.md           ← myplatform_namespace data source       │
│  │   └── service.md             ← myplatform_service data source         │
│  └── guides/                                                             │
│      ├── getting-started.md     ← Quick start tutorial                   │
│      └── authentication.md      ← Auth configuration guide               │
│                                                                          │
│  Generate with:  go generate ./...                                       │
│  (uses //go:generate tfplugindocs directive in main.go)                  │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

// main.go — Add go:generate directive for tfplugindocs
//go:generate go run github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-docs/cmd/tfplugindocs generate --provider-name myplatform

package main

import (
    "context"
    "log"

    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/providerserver"
    "github.com/myorg/terraform-provider-myplatform/internal/provider"
)

var version = "dev"

func main() {
    opts := providerserver.ServeOpts{
        Address: "registry.terraform.io/myorg/myplatform",
    }
    err := providerserver.Serve(context.Background(), provider.New(version), opts)
    if err != nil {
        log.Fatal(err.Error())
    }
}
💡 Template Override: You can customize auto-generated docs by placing template files in templates/. For example, templates/resources/namespace.md.tmpl lets you add custom examples while keeping the auto-generated argument reference.
53.4.3 Versioning

Terraform providers follow semantic versioning (SemVer). Users pin provider versions in their required_providers block, so breaking changes must only occur in major version bumps. A clear changelog helps users understand what changed and plan upgrades.

Version Bump When to Use Example Changes
MAJOR (v2.0.0) Breaking changes to resource schemas or provider config Rename attribute, remove resource, change attribute type
MINOR (v1.1.0) New features, backward-compatible additions New resource, new optional attribute, new data source
PATCH (v1.0.1) Bug fixes, documentation updates Fix Create error handling, correct validation regex 
# Users pin versions in their configuration
terraform {
  required_providers {
    myplatform = {
      source  = "myorg/myplatform"
      version = "~> 1.0"    # Allows 1.x.x but not 2.0.0
    }
  }
}

# After a major version bump, users must explicitly upgrade:
# version = "~> 2.0"
💡 Changelog Best Practice: Maintain a CHANGELOG.md following the Keep a Changelog format. Group changes under Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, and Security headers for each release.
🔌 📡 ⚡ 🌐
CHAPTER 54

Extending Terraform

External Providers Data Sources Shell Provider HTTP Patterns Custom Functions
5Sections
15+Examples
5Patterns
🔌Advanced
PART 15 EXPERT ★★★★★

Terraform's power goes far beyond the official providers. This chapter explores how to extend Terraform with external providers, script-driven data sources, HTTP integrations, and the upcoming provider-defined functions feature.

Learning Objectives

  • Install and use providers from sources outside the Terraform Registry
  • Use the external data source to run scripts and import data
  • Leverage the community Shell Provider for script-based CRUD resources
  • Query REST APIs with the http data source pattern
  • Understand the upcoming provider-defined functions (Terraform 1.8+)

Chapter Contents

54.1 External Providers 54.2 External Data Sources 54.3 Shell Provider 54.4 HTTP Provider Patterns 54.5 Custom Functions (Future)

54.1 External Providers

PROVIDERS ADVANCED
� Official Documentation: Installation Docs

Not every provider lives on the public Terraform Registry. You can install providers from private registries, local filesystem mirrors, and network mirrors for air-gapped environments.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're configuring Terraform to find providers outside the public registry. This is essential in enterprise environments with air-gapped networks, private/internal providers, or custom mirror setups. You'll learn the three main installation methods and when to use each.

  • Configure filesystem mirrors for air-gapped installs
  • Use dev_overrides during provider development
  • Set up private registry sources for enterprise teams
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PROVIDER INSTALLATION SOURCES                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│   terraform init                                                         │
│   │                                                                      │
│   ├── 1. Check CLI config (~/.terraformrc / terraform.rc)                │
│   │       provider_installation {                                        │
│   │         dev_overrides { ... }        ← For local development         │
│   │         filesystem_mirror { ... }    ← Air-gapped / offline          │
│   │         network_mirror { ... }       ← Custom mirror server          │
│   │         direct { }                   ← Public registry (default)     │
│   │       }                                                              │
│   │                                                                      │
│   ├── 2. Check required_providers source                                 │
│   │       source = "myorg/myplatform"    ← registry.terraform.io         │
│   │       source = "app.terraform.io/myorg/myplatform"  ← TFC/TFE       │
│   │       source = "custom.registry.io/myorg/myplatform" ← Private       │
│   │                                                                      │
│   └── 3. Download + verify GPG signature + cache locally                 │
│          .terraform/providers/registry.terraform.io/myorg/myplatform/    │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ~/.terraformrc — CLI configuration file (Linux/Mac)
# %APPDATA%\terraform.rc — CLI configuration file (Windows)
# ============================================================================

# Option 1: Filesystem Mirror (air-gapped / offline installs)
provider_installation {
  filesystem_mirror {
    path    = "/opt/terraform/providers"
    include = ["registry.terraform.io/myorg/*"]
  }
  direct {
    exclude = ["registry.terraform.io/myorg/*"]
  }
}

# Option 2: Dev Overrides (during local provider development)
# Skips version checks — uses your local go build output directly
provider_installation {
  dev_overrides {
    "myorg/myplatform" = "/home/dev/go/bin"
  }
  direct {}
}

# Option 3: Network Mirror (custom mirror server)
provider_installation {
  network_mirror {
    url = "https://terraform-mirror.internal.company.com/providers/"
  }
}
# Setting up a filesystem mirror for air-gapped environments
# ============================================================================

# Step 1: Download providers on an internet-connected machine
terraform providers mirror /opt/terraform/providers

# Step 2: Transfer the directory to the air-gapped server (USB, SCP, etc.)
# The directory structure will look like:
#
#   /opt/terraform/providers/
#   └── registry.terraform.io/
#       ├── hashicorp/
#       │   ├── aws/
#       │   │   └── 5.40.0/
#       │   │       ├── terraform-provider-aws_5.40.0_linux_amd64.zip
#       │   │       └── ...
#       │   └── random/
#       └── myorg/
#           └── myplatform/

# Step 3: Set the mirror in .terraformrc on the air-gapped server
# Then run: terraform init
💡 Dev Overrides Warning: When using dev_overrides, Terraform skips version constraints and checksum verification. Never use dev_overrides in production — it's strictly a local development convenience. Terraform will print a warning on every command reminding you that overrides are active.

54.2 External Data Sources

DATA SOURCE PRACTICAL
� Official Documentation: External Provider Docs

The external data source lets Terraform call an external program (script), pass it JSON input, and read JSON output back as Terraform data. This bridges gaps where no provider exists.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're using scripts as data sources when Terraform needs information from systems that don't have a native provider. The external program must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout — Terraform handles the rest. This is a read-only integration (no create/update/delete).

  • Write scripts that conform to the external data source protocol
  • Pass query parameters from Terraform to your script
  • Use script output in other Terraform resources
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              EXTERNAL DATA SOURCE PROTOCOL                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│   Terraform                          External Script                     │
│   ─────────                          ───────────────                     │
│                                                                          │
│   data "external" "info" {                                               │
│     program = ["python3", "fetch.py"]                                    │
│     query = {                                                            │
│       env = "production"     ──stdin──►  { "env": "production" }         │
│     }                                                                    │
│   }                                                                      │
│                                                                          │
│   data.external.info.result  ◄──stdout──  { "ip": "10.0.1.5",           │
│                                             "region": "us-east-1" }      │
│                                                                          │
│   RULES:                                                                 │
│   • Script reads JSON from stdin                                         │
│   • Script writes JSON to stdout                                         │
│   • All values must be strings (no numbers, bools, lists)                │
│   • Non-zero exit code = error (stderr becomes error message)            │
│   • Script must be idempotent (called on every plan/apply)               │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# fetch_server_info.py — External data source script
# ============================================================================
# Reads a query from Terraform (stdin), fetches info from internal API,
# returns JSON on stdout.
# ============================================================================

import json
import sys
import urllib.request

def main():
    # Read JSON query from stdin
    input_data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    environment = input_data.get("environment", "development")
    service_name = input_data.get("service_name", "")

    if not service_name:
        print("service_name is required", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    # Query internal API (example — replace with your actual endpoint)
    url = f"https://cmdb.internal.company.com/api/services/{service_name}?env={environment}"
    try:
        req = urllib.request.Request(url)
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req) as resp:
            data = json.loads(resp.read().decode())
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Failed to fetch service info: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)

    # Return JSON to Terraform — ALL VALUES MUST BE STRINGS
    output = {
        "ip_address":    str(data.get("ip_address", "")),
        "port":          str(data.get("port", "")),
        "version":       str(data.get("version", "")),
        "health_status": str(data.get("health_status", "unknown")),
    }
    json.dump(output, sys.stdout)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
# Using the external data source in Terraform
# ============================================================================

data "external" "api_service" {
  program = ["python3", "${path.module}/scripts/fetch_server_info.py"]

  query = {
    environment  = var.environment
    service_name = "payment-api"
  }
}

# Use the returned data in other resources
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "allow_api" {
  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = data.external.api_service.result["port"]
  to_port           = data.external.api_service.result["port"]
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["${data.external.api_service.result["ip_address"]}/32"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.app.id
}

output "api_info" {
  value = {
    ip      = data.external.api_service.result["ip_address"]
    port    = data.external.api_service.result["port"]
    version = data.external.api_service.result["version"]
    health  = data.external.api_service.result["health_status"]
  }
}
💡 All Values Must Be Strings: The external data source protocol requires that every value in the returned JSON object be a string. If your script returns {"port": 8080} (a number), Terraform will error. Use {"port": "8080"} instead and convert with tonumber() where needed.

54.3 Shell Provider

COMMUNITY ADVANCED
� Official Documentation: Shell Provider Registry

The community shell provider lets you create full CRUD resources backed by shell scripts — unlike the external data source, which is read-only. You define create, read, update, and delete scripts, and Terraform manages the lifecycle.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're wrapping arbitrary scripts as Terraform-managed resources. This is a middle ground between the read-only external data source and building a full custom provider in Go. It's ideal for internal tooling, legacy APIs, or quick prototypes where writing a Go provider would be overkill.

  • Define lifecycle commands (create, read, update, delete) as shell scripts
  • Pass inputs to scripts via environment variables
  • Extract resource state from script output
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         SHELL PROVIDER vs EXTERNAL DATA SOURCE                           │
├───────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   external (data source)      │   shell (resource)                       │
├───────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  ✅ Read-only                 │  ✅ Full CRUD lifecycle                  │
│  ✅ Built-in (hashicorp)      │  ⚠️  Community provider                  │
│  ✅ JSON stdin/stdout          │  ✅ Env vars + stdout                   │
│  ❌ No create/update/delete   │  ✅ Create, Read, Update, Delete         │
│  ❌ No state management       │  ✅ Terraform state tracked              │
│  Best for: querying info      │  Best for: managing resources via CLI    │
└───────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Using the shell provider for script-based CRUD
# ============================================================================
terraform {
  required_providers {
    shell = {
      source  = "scottwinkler/shell"
      version = "~> 1.7"
    }
  }
}

# Create a DNS record using an internal CLI tool
resource "shell_script" "dns_record" {
  lifecycle_commands {
    create = file("${path.module}/scripts/dns_create.sh")
    read   = file("${path.module}/scripts/dns_read.sh")
    update = file("${path.module}/scripts/dns_update.sh")
    delete = file("${path.module}/scripts/dns_delete.sh")
  }

  environment = {
    HOSTNAME   = "api.${var.domain}"
    IP_ADDRESS = aws_instance.api.private_ip
    RECORD_TYPE = "A"
    TTL        = "300"
    DNS_SERVER = var.dns_server
    DNS_API_KEY = var.dns_api_key  # Sensitive — mark in variable block
  }

  # Triggers re-create when these values change
  triggers = {
    ip_address = aws_instance.api.private_ip
    hostname   = "api.${var.domain}"
  }
}
#!/bin/bash
# scripts/dns_create.sh — Create a DNS record
# ============================================================================
# Environment variables set by Terraform: HOSTNAME, IP_ADDRESS, RECORD_TYPE, TTL
# Must output JSON to stdout — Terraform stores this as the resource state.
# ============================================================================

set -euo pipefail

# Call internal DNS API to create record
RESPONSE=$(curl -sf -X POST "https://${DNS_SERVER}/api/v1/records" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${DNS_API_KEY}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{
    \"name\": \"${HOSTNAME}\",
    \"type\": \"${RECORD_TYPE}\",
    \"content\": \"${IP_ADDRESS}\",
    \"ttl\": ${TTL}
  }")

# Extract the record ID from the response
RECORD_ID=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq -r '.id')

# Output JSON — this becomes the resource state in Terraform
# All values must be strings
cat <<EOF
{
  "id": "${RECORD_ID}",
  "hostname": "${HOSTNAME}",
  "ip_address": "${IP_ADDRESS}",
  "record_type": "${RECORD_TYPE}",
  "ttl": "${TTL}"
}
EOF
#!/bin/bash
# scripts/dns_read.sh — Read a DNS record (verify it still exists)
# ============================================================================
# Receives previous state via environment variables from Terraform.
# Must output current JSON state to stdout.
# ============================================================================

set -euo pipefail

RESPONSE=$(curl -sf "https://${DNS_SERVER}/api/v1/records/${ID}" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${DNS_API_KEY}")

# Re-emit current state as JSON
echo "$RESPONSE" | jq '{
  id: .id,
  hostname: .name,
  ip_address: .content,
  record_type: .type,
  ttl: (.ttl | tostring)
}'
💡 When to Use Shell Provider: Use it when you need Terraform lifecycle management (create/destroy) but the target system only has a CLI or REST API and no Terraform provider. For read-only queries, the simpler external data source is preferred. For production-critical resources, consider building a proper Go provider for better error handling and type safety.

54.4 HTTP Provider Patterns

DATA SOURCE PRACTICAL
� Official Documentation: HTTP Provider Docs

The built-in http data source makes HTTP GET requests and returns the response body — useful for fetching configuration from REST APIs, checking service health, or downloading JSON data during plan time.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're querying REST APIs directly from Terraform without needing an external script. The http data source is lighter than the external data source — no scripts to maintain — but is limited to GET requests and cannot manage state (read-only).

  • Fetch JSON configuration from REST APIs at plan time
  • Parse HTTP responses with jsondecode()
  • Use API data to drive resource configuration dynamically
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              HTTP DATA SOURCE FLOW                                        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│   terraform plan / apply                                                 │
│   │                                                                      │
│   ├── data "http" "my_ip" {                                              │
│   │     url = "https://api.ipify.org?format=json"                        │
│   │   }                                                                  │
│   │                                                                      │
│   │   ┌────────────┐   GET request    ┌─────────────────────┐           │
│   │   │  Terraform │ ──────────────►  │  REST API / URL      │           │
│   │   │  (plan)    │ ◄──────────────  │  (returns JSON/text) │           │
│   │   └────────────┘   response_body  └─────────────────────┘           │
│   │                                                                      │
│   ├── Available attributes:                                              │
│   │   • response_body    → Raw text / JSON string                        │
│   │   • status_code      → HTTP status (200, 404, etc.)                  │
│   │   • response_headers → Map of response headers                       │
