│   │                                                                      │
│   └── jsondecode(data.http.my_ip.response_body)                          │
│        └── Returns HCL object you can reference in resources             │
│                                                                          │
│   ⚠️  Runs on EVERY plan/apply — avoid slow endpoints                    │
│   ⚠️  GET only — no POST/PUT/DELETE                                      │
│   ⚠️  No state — purely read-time evaluation                             │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ============================================================================
# Pattern 1: Fetch your public IP (common for security group rules)
# ============================================================================

data "http" "my_ip" {
  url = "https://api.ipify.org?format=json"

  request_headers = {
    Accept = "application/json"
  }
}

locals {
  my_public_ip = jsondecode(data.http.my_ip.response_body).ip
}

resource "aws_security_group_rule" "ssh_from_my_ip" {
  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = 22
  to_port           = 22
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["${local.my_public_ip}/32"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.bastion.id
  description       = "SSH from my current IP"
}


# ============================================================================
# Pattern 2: Fetch configuration from an internal API
# ============================================================================

data "http" "app_config" {
  url = "https://config-service.internal.company.com/api/v1/config/${var.environment}"

  request_headers = {
    Accept        = "application/json"
    Authorization = "Bearer ${var.config_api_token}"
  }

  # Fail if API returns non-200
  retry {
    attempts     = 3
    min_delay_ms = 1000
  }
}

locals {
  app_config = jsondecode(data.http.app_config.response_body)
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = local.app_config.ami_id
  instance_type = local.app_config.instance_type
  subnet_id     = local.app_config.subnet_id

  tags = {
    Name    = "${var.environment}-app"
    Version = local.app_config.app_version
  }
}


# ============================================================================
# Pattern 3: Validate service health before deployment
# ============================================================================

data "http" "dependency_health" {
  url = "https://auth-service.internal.company.com/health"

  request_headers = {
    Accept = "application/json"
  }
}

# Fail the plan if the dependency is unhealthy
resource "terraform_data" "validate_dependency" {
  lifecycle {
    precondition {
      condition     = data.http.dependency_health.status_code == 200
      error_message = "Auth service is unhealthy (status: ${data.http.dependency_health.status_code}). Cannot proceed with deployment."
    }
  }
}
Pattern Use Case Key Function
IP Discovery Fetch current public IP for security group rules jsondecode(data.http.*.response_body)
Config Fetch Pull dynamic settings from config API request_headers for auth tokens
Health Check Validate dependencies before deploy status_code + precondition
Version Lookup Get latest artifact version from API Parse JSON body for version strings
Certificate Fetch Download CA certificates for trust stores Use response_body directly (PEM text)
💡 Limitations: The http data source only supports GET requests. For POST/PUT/DELETE operations, use the external data source with a script, the shell provider, or a dedicated Terraform provider. Also, avoid calling slow APIs — the HTTP call runs on every plan and apply, which can slow down workflows.

54.5 Custom Functions (Future)

FUNCTIONS TERRAFORM 1.8+
� Official Documentation: Functions Guide

Starting with Terraform 1.8, providers can define custom functions that users call directly in HCL expressions. This extends Terraform's built-in function library with domain-specific logic — without requiring data sources or external scripts.

🎯

What You're Doing Here

You're exploring Terraform's newest extensibility feature — provider-defined functions. Instead of encoding logic in data sources, local values, or external scripts, you define functions directly in the provider. Users call them with the provider::name::function() syntax.

  • Understand the provider-defined function architecture
  • See how to implement functions in the Plugin Framework
  • Compare functions to traditional data source approaches
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              PROVIDER-DEFINED FUNCTIONS (Terraform 1.8+)                  │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                          │
│   BEFORE (data source approach):                                         │
│   ─────────────────────────────                                          │
│   data "aws_arn" "parsed" {         ← Requires plan-time evaluation      │
│     arn = "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket"  ← Creates state entry                │
│   }                                 ← Awkward for simple transformations  │
│   local.account = data.aws_arn.parsed.account                            │
│                                                                          │
│   AFTER (provider function):                                             │
│   ──────────────────────────                                             │
│   provider::aws::arn_parse("arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket").account             │
│   │                                                                      │
│   ├── ✅ Pure function — no state, no side effects                       │
│   ├── ✅ Works in count, for_each, variable defaults                     │
│   ├── ✅ Evaluated during planning — no apply needed                     │
│   └── ✅ Cleaner, more expressive HCL                                    │
│                                                                          │
│   SYNTAX:   provider::::(args...)           │
│   EXAMPLE:  provider::aws::arn_parse("arn:aws:iam::123:role/Admin")      │
│             provider::http::decode_json(response.body)                   │
│                                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

// ============================================================================
// function_cidr_contains.go — Example provider-defined function
// ============================================================================
// This function checks if a given IP address falls within a CIDR block.
// Users call it as: provider::myplatform::cidr_contains("10.0.0.0/8", "10.1.2.3")
// ============================================================================

package provider

import (
    "context"
    "net"

    "github.com/hashicorp/terraform-plugin-framework/function"
)

var _ function.Function = &CIDRContainsFunction{}

type CIDRContainsFunction struct{}

func (f *CIDRContainsFunction) Metadata(
    ctx context.Context,
    req function.MetadataRequest,
    resp *function.MetadataResponse,
) {
    resp.Name = "cidr_contains"
}

func (f *CIDRContainsFunction) Definition(
    ctx context.Context,
    req function.DefinitionRequest,
    resp *function.DefinitionResponse,
) {
    resp.Definition = function.Definition{
        Summary:     "Check if an IP address is within a CIDR block",
        Description: "Returns true if the given IP is contained within the CIDR range.",
        Parameters: []function.Parameter{
            function.StringParameter{
                Name:        "cidr",
                Description: "The CIDR block (e.g., '10.0.0.0/8')",
            },
            function.StringParameter{
                Name:        "ip",
                Description: "The IP address to check (e.g., '10.1.2.3')",
            },
        },
        Return: function.BoolReturn{},
    }
}

func (f *CIDRContainsFunction) Run(
    ctx context.Context,
    req function.RunRequest,
    resp *function.RunResponse,
) {
    var cidr, ip string
    resp.Error = function.ConcatFuncErrors(
        req.Arguments.Get(ctx, &cidr, &ip),
    )
    if resp.Error != nil {
        return
    }

    _, network, err := net.ParseCIDR(cidr)
    if err != nil {
        resp.Error = function.NewFuncError("Invalid CIDR: " + err.Error())
        return
    }

    parsedIP := net.ParseIP(ip)
    if parsedIP == nil {
        resp.Error = function.NewFuncError("Invalid IP address: " + ip)
        return
    }

    result := network.Contains(parsedIP)
    resp.Error = resp.Result.Set(ctx, result)
}
# Using provider-defined functions in Terraform 1.8+
# ============================================================================

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.8.0"
  required_providers {
    myplatform = {
      source  = "myorg/myplatform"
      version = ">= 2.0.0"
    }
  }
}

# Use custom function — no data source needed!
locals {
  allowed_ips = [
    "10.1.0.5",
    "192.168.1.100",
    "172.16.0.50",
    "10.2.3.4",
    "8.8.8.8",
  ]

  # Filter IPs that belong to the internal network
  internal_ips = [
    for ip in local.allowed_ips :
    ip if provider::myplatform::cidr_contains("10.0.0.0/8", ip)
  ]
  # Result: ["10.1.0.5", "10.2.3.4"]
}

# Functions work in count, for_each, and variable defaults
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "internal" {
  count = length(local.internal_ips)

  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = 443
  to_port           = 443
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["${local.internal_ips[count.index]}/32"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.app.id
}
Approach State Side Effects Use in count/for_each Best For
Provider Function None None ✅ Yes Data transformation, validation, parsing
Data Source Stored API calls ⚠️ Limited Fetching external data, lookups
External Script None Script execution ⚠️ Limited Complex logic, legacy integration
Built-in Functions None None ✅ Yes General-purpose (string, math, collection)
💡 Adoption Note: Provider-defined functions are available in Terraform 1.8+ and OpenTofu 1.7+. They require the Terraform Plugin Framework (not SDKv2). Major providers like AWS, Azure, and Google are gradually adding functions — check your provider's changelog for updates.
🌐 ☁️ 🔒 🚀
CHAPTER 55

Project 1 - Static Website on S3 + CloudFront

S3 Hosting CloudFront CDN Route 53 ACM SSL CI/CD HTTPS OAC
7Sections
15Subsections
20+Code Examples
🌐Project
PART 16 INTERMEDIATE ★★★☆☆

Build a production-ready static website infrastructure using S3 for hosting, CloudFront for global CDN distribution, Route 53 for DNS management, ACM for SSL certificates, and GitHub Actions for automated deployments — all managed with Terraform.

Learning Objectives

  • Configure S3 buckets for static website hosting with proper policies
  • Set up CloudFront distributions with Origin Access Control (OAC)
  • Manage DNS records with Route 53 hosted zones and alias records
  • Provision and validate ACM SSL certificates via DNS
  • Implement a complete CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions
  • Apply security best practices including HTTPS-only and bucket restrictions
  • Deploy a fully automated, globally distributed static website

Chapter Contents

55.1 Project Requirements 55.2 Architecture Diagram 55.3 S3 Bucket Configuration 55.4 CloudFront Distribution 55.5 Route 53 DNS 55.6 ACM SSL Certificate 55.7 CI/CD Pipeline

55.1 Project Requirements

PROJECT
📚 Official Documentation: S3 Static Website Hosting | CloudFront Overview

This project deploys a production-grade static website on AWS using S3 as the origin, CloudFront as the CDN, Route 53 for DNS, and ACM for HTTPS — fully automated with Terraform and CI/CD.

📋

Project Goals

  • Host a static website (HTML/CSS/JS) on S3
  • Serve content globally via CloudFront CDN
  • Custom domain with HTTPS (ACM certificate)
  • DNS management via Route 53
  • Automated deployment pipeline
  • Infrastructure fully managed by Terraform
🛠️

AWS Services Used

  • S3 — Object storage for website files
  • CloudFront — Global CDN distribution
  • Route 53 — DNS management
  • ACM — SSL/TLS certificate
  • IAM — Access policies
  • GitHub Actions — CI/CD pipeline
💰

Estimated Cost

  • S3: ~$0.023/GB/month storage
  • CloudFront: ~$0.085/GB transfer
  • Route 53: $0.50/hosted zone/month
  • ACM: Free (public certificates)
  • Total: ~$1-5/month for small sites
  • Free tier eligible for 12 months

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Project File Structure
static-website-project/
├── main.tf              # Root module - orchestrates all resources
├── variables.tf         # Input variables (domain, region, tags)
├── outputs.tf           # Output values (CloudFront URL, S3 bucket)
├── providers.tf         # AWS provider + us-east-1 alias for ACM
├── terraform.tfvars     # Variable values
├── s3.tf                # S3 bucket and policies
├── cloudfront.tf        # CloudFront distribution
├── route53.tf           # DNS records
├── acm.tf               # SSL certificate
├── iam.tf               # IAM policies for OAC
└── website/             # Static website content
    ├── index.html
    ├── error.html
    ├── css/
    │   └── style.css
    └── js/
        └── app.js
Example: Provider Configuration with ACM Alias

ACM certificates for CloudFront must be in us-east-1, so we use a provider alias.

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.7.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }

  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state-bucket"
    key            = "static-website/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}

# Primary provider
provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "static-website"
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      Environment = var.environment
    }
  }
}

# ACM certificates for CloudFront MUST be in us-east-1
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "us_east_1"
  region = "us-east-1"

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = "static-website"
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      Environment = var.environment
    }
  }
}
Example: Variables Definition
variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region for main resources"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "domain_name" {
  description = "Primary domain name for the website"
  type        = string
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Environment name (dev, staging, prod)"
  type        = string
  default     = "prod"
}

variable "bucket_name" {
  description = "S3 bucket name for website content"
  type        = string
}

variable "price_class" {
  description = "CloudFront price class"
  type        = string
  default     = "PriceClass_100"
  validation {
    condition = contains([
      "PriceClass_100", "PriceClass_200", "PriceClass_All"
    ], var.price_class)
    error_message = "Invalid CloudFront price class."
  }
}

55.2 Architecture Diagram

ARCHITECTURE
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Web Hosting Architecture

The architecture leverages CloudFront as the entry point, with S3 as a private origin accessed via Origin Access Control (OAC). Route 53 provides DNS resolution, and ACM handles SSL termination at the edge.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Complete Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STATIC WEBSITE ARCHITECTURE                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│   User Request: https://www.example.com                                     │
│          │                                                                  │
│          ▼                                                                  │
│   ┌──────────────┐     DNS Resolution      ┌────────────────────────┐      │
│   │  Route 53    │ ◄──────────────────────► │  ACM Certificate       │      │
│   │  Hosted Zone │     (ALIAS Record)       │  (us-east-1)           │      │
│   │              │                          │  *.example.com         │      │
│   └──────┬───────┘                          └────────────────────────┘      │
│          │                                                                  │
│          ▼                                                                  │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│   │              CLOUDFRONT DISTRIBUTION                      │             │
│   │                                                          │             │
│   │   ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐        │             │
│   │   │ Edge       │  │ Edge       │  │ Edge       │        │             │
│   │   │ Location 1 │  │ Location 2 │  │ Location N │        │             │
│   │   │ (US-East)  │  │ (EU-West)  │  │ (AP-South) │        │             │
│   │   └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘        │             │
│   │                                                          │             │
│   │   Features:                                              │             │
│   │   • HTTPS termination (TLS 1.2+)                         │             │
│   │   • Gzip/Brotli compression                              │             │
│   │   • Custom error pages (403 → index.html for SPAs)       │             │
│   │   • Cache-Control headers                                │             │
│   │   • Geographic restrictions (optional)                   │             │
│   └─────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                         │  Origin Access Control (OAC)                      │
│                         ▼                                                   │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│   │                    S3 BUCKET (Private)                     │             │
│   │                                                          │             │
│   │   ├── index.html      (Default root object)              │             │
│   │   ├── error.html      (Custom error page)                │             │
│   │   ├── css/style.css   (Stylesheets)                      │             │
│   │   ├── js/app.js       (JavaScript)                       │             │
│   │   └── images/         (Static assets)                    │             │
│   │                                                          │             │
│   │   • Block all public access                              │             │
│   │   • Bucket policy allows CloudFront OAC only             │             │
│   │   • Versioning enabled                                   │             │
│   │   • Server-side encryption (AES-256)                     │             │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                                             │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│   │                    CI/CD PIPELINE                          │             │
│   │   GitHub Actions → Build → Sync to S3 → Invalidate CF    │             │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Component Service Purpose Key Config
DNS Route 53 Domain resolution ALIAS to CloudFront
CDN CloudFront Global content delivery OAC, custom domain, HTTPS
Storage S3 Website file storage Private, versioned, encrypted
SSL ACM HTTPS certificate DNS validation, us-east-1
Deploy GitHub Actions Automated deployment S3 sync + CF invalidation

55.3 S3 Bucket Configuration

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_s3_bucket | aws_s3_bucket_website_configuration

The S3 bucket serves as the origin for our static website. We configure it as a private bucket (no public access) with CloudFront OAC as the only access method, following AWS security best practices.

💻 Practical Examples

55.3.1 Static Website Hosting

Configure the S3 bucket with website hosting enabled, versioning for rollback capability, and server-side encryption for data protection.

Example: S3 Bucket with Website Configuration
# ─── S3 Bucket ───────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "website" {
  bucket = var.bucket_name

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.domain_name}-website"
  }
}

# ─── Versioning ──────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "website" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.website.id

  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

# ─── Server-Side Encryption ─────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "website" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.website.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm = "AES256"
    }
    bucket_key_enabled = true
  }
}

# ─── Website Configuration ──────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_website_configuration" "website" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.website.id

  index_document {
    suffix = "index.html"
  }

  error_document {
    key = "error.html"
  }

  # SPA routing — redirect all 404s to index.html
  routing_rule {
    condition {
      http_error_code_returned_equals = "404"
    }
    redirect {
      replace_key_with = "index.html"
    }
  }
}

# ─── Block All Public Access ─────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "website" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.website.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}
55.3.2 Bucket Policy

The bucket policy grants read access exclusively to CloudFront via Origin Access Control (OAC), ensuring no direct public access to the S3 bucket.

Example: OAC-Based Bucket Policy
# ─── Bucket Policy — CloudFront OAC Access Only ─────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_policy" "website" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.website.id
  policy = data.aws_iam_policy_document.s3_cloudfront.json

  depends_on = [
    aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block.website
  ]
}

data "aws_iam_policy_document" "s3_cloudfront" {
  statement {
    sid    = "AllowCloudFrontOAC"
    effect = "Allow"

    principals {
      type        = "Service"
      identifiers = ["cloudfront.amazonaws.com"]
    }

    actions   = ["s3:GetObject"]
    resources = ["${aws_s3_bucket.website.arn}/*"]

    condition {
      test     = "StringEquals"
      variable = "AWS:SourceArn"
      values   = [aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.arn]
    }
  }
}
55.3.3 CORS Configuration

CORS configuration allows web fonts, API calls, and other cross-origin requests from your domain.

Example: CORS Rules for Web Fonts and APIs
resource "aws_s3_bucket_cors_configuration" "website" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.website.id

  cors_rule {
    allowed_headers = ["*"]
    allowed_methods = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    allowed_origins = [
      "https://${var.domain_name}",
      "https://www.${var.domain_name}"
    ]
    expose_headers  = ["ETag"]
    max_age_seconds = 3600
  }
}

55.4 CloudFront Distribution

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_cloudfront_distribution | aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control

CloudFront serves as the global CDN, caching content at edge locations worldwide. Using OAC (Origin Access Control) instead of the legacy OAI provides enhanced security and supports all S3 features.

💻 Practical Examples

55.4.1 Origin Configuration

The Origin Access Control (OAC) replaces the deprecated Origin Access Identity (OAI), providing a secure connection between CloudFront and S3.

Example: CloudFront OAC Setup
# ─── Origin Access Control ───────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control" "website" {
  name                              = "${var.domain_name}-oac"
  description                       = "OAC for ${var.domain_name}"
  origin_access_control_origin_type = "s3"
  signing_behavior                  = "always"
  signing_protocol                  = "sigv4"
}
55.4.2 Cache Behaviors

Cache behaviors control how CloudFront caches and serves content. We configure caching policies, compression, and viewer protocol settings.

Example: Complete CloudFront Distribution
# ─── CloudFront Distribution ─────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudfront_distribution" "website" {
  enabled             = true
  is_ipv6_enabled     = true
  default_root_object = "index.html"
  comment             = "CDN for ${var.domain_name}"
  price_class         = var.price_class
  aliases             = [var.domain_name, "www.${var.domain_name}"]
  wait_for_deployment = false

  # ── S3 Origin ──
  origin {
    domain_name              = aws_s3_bucket.website.bucket_regional_domain_name
    origin_access_control_id = aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control.website.id
    origin_id                = "S3-${var.bucket_name}"
  }

  # ── Default Cache Behavior ──
  default_cache_behavior {
    allowed_methods        = ["GET", "HEAD", "OPTIONS"]
    cached_methods         = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    target_origin_id       = "S3-${var.bucket_name}"
    viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"
    compress               = true
    min_ttl                = 0
    default_ttl            = 3600
    max_ttl                = 86400

    forwarded_values {
      query_string = false
      cookies {
        forward = "none"
      }
    }
  }

  # ── Cache Behavior for Static Assets ──
  ordered_cache_behavior {
    path_pattern           = "/css/*"
    allowed_methods        = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    cached_methods         = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    target_origin_id       = "S3-${var.bucket_name}"
    viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"
    compress               = true
    min_ttl                = 86400
    default_ttl            = 604800
    max_ttl                = 31536000

    forwarded_values {
      query_string = false
      cookies {
        forward = "none"
      }
    }
  }

  ordered_cache_behavior {
    path_pattern           = "/js/*"
    allowed_methods        = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    cached_methods         = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    target_origin_id       = "S3-${var.bucket_name}"
    viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"
    compress               = true
    min_ttl                = 86400
    default_ttl            = 604800
    max_ttl                = 31536000

    forwarded_values {
      query_string = false
      cookies {
        forward = "none"
      }
    }
  }

  # ── Custom Error Responses (SPA Support) ──
  custom_error_response {
    error_code            = 403
    response_code         = 200
    response_page_path    = "/index.html"
    error_caching_min_ttl = 10
  }

  custom_error_response {
    error_code            = 404
    response_code         = 200
    response_page_path    = "/index.html"
    error_caching_min_ttl = 10
  }

  # ── SSL Certificate ──
  viewer_certificate {
    acm_certificate_arn      = aws_acm_certificate_validation.website.certificate_arn
    ssl_support_method       = "sni-only"
    minimum_protocol_version = "TLSv1.2_2021"
  }

  # ── Restrictions ──
  restrictions {
    geo_restriction {
      restriction_type = "none"
    }
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.domain_name}-cdn"
  }
}
55.4.3 SSL Certificate

The viewer certificate block links the CloudFront distribution to the ACM certificate. SNI-only mode avoids the dedicated IP cost ($600/month).

Example: CloudFront Security Headers (Response Policy)
# ─── Security Headers Policy ─────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudfront_response_headers_policy" "security" {
  name    = "${var.domain_name}-security-headers"
  comment = "Security headers for ${var.domain_name}"

  security_headers_config {
    content_type_options {
      override = true
    }
    frame_options {
      frame_option = "DENY"
      override     = true
    }
    strict_transport_security {
      access_control_max_age_sec = 31536000
      include_subdomains         = true
      preload                    = true
      override                   = true
    }
    xss_protection {
      mode_block = true
      protection = true
      override   = true
    }
    referrer_policy {
      referrer_policy = "strict-origin-when-cross-origin"
      override        = true
    }
    content_security_policy {
      content_security_policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';"
      override                = true
    }
  }
}
⚠️ OAC vs OAI

Origin Access Control (OAC) is the recommended approach (2023+). Origin Access Identity (OAI) is legacy and doesn't support SSE-KMS, S3 Object Lambda, or new S3 features. Always use OAC for new deployments.

55.5 Route 53 DNS

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_route53_zone | aws_route53_record

Route 53 manages DNS records that point the custom domain to the CloudFront distribution. ALIAS records provide native integration with AWS services without additional latency.

💻 Practical Examples

55.5.1 Hosted Zone

The hosted zone is the container for all DNS records for your domain. If the domain is registered externally, update the registrar's nameservers to point to Route 53.

Example: Route 53 Hosted Zone
# ─── Option A: Reference existing hosted zone ────────────
data "aws_route53_zone" "main" {
  name         = var.domain_name
  private_zone = false
}

# ─── Option B: Create new hosted zone ────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_zone" "main" {
  name    = var.domain_name
  comment = "Managed by Terraform - ${var.domain_name}"

  tags = {
    Name = var.domain_name
  }
}
55.5.2 Alias Records

ALIAS records point the apex domain and www subdomain to the CloudFront distribution. Unlike CNAME records, ALIAS records work at the zone apex and are free of charge.

Example: DNS Records for CloudFront
# ─── Apex Domain → CloudFront ────────────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_record" "apex" {
  zone_id = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = var.domain_name
  type    = "A"

  alias {
    name                   = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.domain_name
    zone_id                = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.hosted_zone_id
    evaluate_target_health = false
  }
}

# ─── IPv6 Apex Domain → CloudFront ──────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_record" "apex_ipv6" {
  zone_id = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = var.domain_name
  type    = "AAAA"

  alias {
    name                   = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.domain_name
    zone_id                = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.hosted_zone_id
    evaluate_target_health = false
  }
}

# ─── WWW Subdomain → CloudFront ─────────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_record" "www" {
  zone_id = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = "www.${var.domain_name}"
  type    = "A"

  alias {
    name                   = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.domain_name
    zone_id                = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.hosted_zone_id
    evaluate_target_health = false
  }
}

55.6 ACM SSL Certificate

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_acm_certificate | aws_acm_certificate_validation

ACM provides free SSL/TLS certificates. For CloudFront distributions, certificates must be provisioned in us-east-1. DNS validation is preferred over email validation for automation.

💻 Practical Examples

55.6.1 Certificate Request

Request a wildcard certificate that covers both the apex domain and all subdomains. Using the us-east-1 provider alias is mandatory for CloudFront.

Example: ACM Certificate with SAN
# ─── ACM Certificate (MUST be in us-east-1 for CloudFront) ──
resource "aws_acm_certificate" "website" {
  provider = aws.us_east_1

  domain_name               = var.domain_name
  subject_alternative_names = ["*.${var.domain_name}"]
  validation_method         = "DNS"

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.domain_name}-certificate"
  }
}
55.6.2 DNS Validation

DNS validation creates CNAME records in Route 53 to prove domain ownership. Terraform automates the entire process — certificate request, DNS record creation, and validation waiting.

Example: Automated DNS Validation
# ─── DNS Validation Records ──────────────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_record" "cert_validation" {
  for_each = {
    for dvo in aws_acm_certificate.website.domain_validation_options : dvo.domain_name => {
      name   = dvo.resource_record_name
      record = dvo.resource_record_value
      type   = dvo.resource_record_type
    }
  }

  allow_overwrite = true
  name            = each.value.name
  records         = [each.value.record]
  ttl             = 60
  type            = each.value.type
  zone_id         = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
}

# ─── Wait for Validation to Complete ─────────────────────
resource "aws_acm_certificate_validation" "website" {
  provider = aws.us_east_1

  certificate_arn         = aws_acm_certificate.website.arn
  validation_record_fqdns = [for record in aws_route53_record.cert_validation : record.fqdn]
}
💡 Certificate Validation Time

DNS validation typically completes within 5-30 minutes. The aws_acm_certificate_validation resource blocks terraform apply until validation succeeds. If validation takes too long, check that your Route 53 hosted zone nameservers match your domain registrar's NS records.

55.7 CI/CD Pipeline

DEVOPS
📚 Official Documentation: GitHub Actions | AWS CLI S3 Sync

Automate website deployments with GitHub Actions. On every push to main, the pipeline syncs files to S3 and invalidates the CloudFront cache for instant propagation.

💻 Practical Examples

55.7.1 GitHub Actions Workflow

This workflow uses OIDC federation for secure, keyless authentication to AWS — no long-lived access keys needed.

Example: GitHub Actions Deployment Workflow
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy Static Website

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'website/**'
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  id-token: write   # Required for OIDC
  contents: read

env:
  AWS_REGION: us-east-1
  S3_BUCKET: my-website-bucket
  CLOUDFRONT_DIST_ID: E1234567890ABC

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Configure AWS credentials (OIDC)
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/github-actions-deploy
          aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}

      - name: Sync to S3
        run: |
          aws s3 sync website/ s3://${{ env.S3_BUCKET }}/ \
            --delete \
            --cache-control "public, max-age=31536000, immutable" \
            --exclude "*.html" \
            --exclude "*.json"

          # HTML files — short cache, always revalidate
          aws s3 sync website/ s3://${{ env.S3_BUCKET }}/ \
            --exclude "*" \
            --include "*.html" \
            --cache-control "public, max-age=0, must-revalidate"

      - name: Invalidate CloudFront cache
        run: |
          aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
            --distribution-id ${{ env.CLOUDFRONT_DIST_ID }} \
            --paths "/*"

      - name: Deployment summary
        run: |
          echo "## ✅ Deployment Complete" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "- **Bucket:** ${{ env.S3_BUCKET }}" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "- **Distribution:** ${{ env.CLOUDFRONT_DIST_ID }}" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "- **Time:** $(date -u)" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
55.7.2 Content Deployment

The IAM role for GitHub Actions OIDC federation, granting minimal permissions for S3 sync and CloudFront invalidation.

Example: IAM Role for GitHub Actions OIDC
# ─── OIDC Provider for GitHub ────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github" {
  url             = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
  client_id_list  = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
  thumbprint_list = ["6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1"]
}

# ─── IAM Role for GitHub Actions ─────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "github_actions" {
  name = "github-actions-deploy"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect = "Allow"
        Principal = {
          Federated = aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github.arn
        }
        Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
        Condition = {
          StringEquals = {
            "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:aud" = "sts.amazonaws.com"
          }
          StringLike = {
            "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub" = "repo:my-org/my-repo:ref:refs/heads/main"
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  })
}

# ─── Deploy Policy ───────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "deploy" {
  name = "website-deploy-policy"
  role = aws_iam_role.github_actions.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Sid    = "S3Access"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "s3:PutObject",
          "s3:GetObject",
          "s3:DeleteObject",
          "s3:ListBucket"
        ]
        Resource = [
          aws_s3_bucket.website.arn,
          "${aws_s3_bucket.website.arn}/*"
        ]
      },
      {
        Sid    = "CloudFrontInvalidation"
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "cloudfront:CreateInvalidation",
          "cloudfront:GetInvalidation"
        ]
        Resource = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.arn
      }
    ]
  })
}
Example: Outputs for CI/CD Integration
# ─── Outputs ─────────────────────────────────────────────
output "website_url" {
  description = "Website URL"
  value       = "https://${var.domain_name}"
}

output "cloudfront_distribution_id" {
  description = "CloudFront distribution ID (for cache invalidation)"
  value       = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.id
}

output "s3_bucket_name" {
  description = "S3 bucket name (for content sync)"
  value       = aws_s3_bucket.website.id
}

output "cloudfront_domain_name" {
  description = "CloudFront domain name"
  value       = aws_cloudfront_distribution.website.domain_name
}

output "acm_certificate_arn" {
  description = "ACM certificate ARN"
  value       = aws_acm_certificate.website.arn
}

output "github_actions_role_arn" {
  description = "IAM role ARN for GitHub Actions"
  value       = aws_iam_role.github_actions.arn
}
💡 Deployment Workflow: After running terraform apply to create the infrastructure, use the output values to configure your GitHub Actions secrets: S3_BUCKET, CLOUDFRONT_DIST_ID, and the IAM role ARN for OIDC. The first git push to main will trigger an automatic deployment.
🏗️ ⚖️ 🛡️ 📊
CHAPTER 56

Project 2 - Three-Tier Web Application

VPC ALB EC2 Auto Scaling RDS Multi-AZ Security Groups IAM CloudWatch
7Sections
19Subsections
25+Code Examples
🏗️Project
PART 16 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

Build a highly available three-tier web application on AWS with a multi-AZ VPC, Application Load Balancer, EC2 Auto Scaling Groups, RDS Multi-AZ database, comprehensive security groups, IAM roles, and CloudWatch monitoring — all orchestrated with Terraform.

Learning Objectives

  • Design a multi-AZ VPC with public, private, and database subnets
  • Configure an Application Load Balancer with HTTPS and health checks
  • Set up EC2 Auto Scaling with launch templates and scaling policies
  • Deploy RDS Multi-AZ for high availability databases
  • Implement layered security groups following least-privilege
  • Create IAM roles and instance profiles for EC2
  • Build CloudWatch alarms with SNS notifications

Chapter Contents

56.1 Architecture Overview 56.2 VPC and Networking 56.3 Application Load Balancer 56.4 EC2 Auto Scaling 56.5 RDS Database 56.6 Security Implementation 56.7 Monitoring and Alerts

56.1 Architecture Overview

ARCHITECTURE
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Web Hosting Best Practices

The three-tier architecture separates the presentation (ALB), application (EC2), and data (RDS) layers across multiple availability zones for high availability and fault tolerance.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    THREE-TIER WEB APPLICATION                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│   Internet                                                                  │
│      │                                                                      │
│      ▼                                                                      │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│   │                APPLICATION LOAD BALANCER (ALB)                │          │
│   │   • HTTPS termination (ACM certificate)                      │          │
│   │   • Health checks (/health endpoint)                         │          │
│   │   • Spans: AZ-a, AZ-b, AZ-c                                 │          │
│   └─────────────┬────────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘          │
│                 │                        │                                   │
│   ┌─────────────▼────────────┐  ┌───────▼──────────────────┐               │
│   │   PRIVATE SUBNET (AZ-a)  │  │   PRIVATE SUBNET (AZ-b)  │               │
│   │                          │  │                          │               │
│   │   ┌──────────────────┐   │  │   ┌──────────────────┐   │               │
│   │   │   EC2 Instance   │   │  │   │   EC2 Instance   │   │               │
│   │   │   (App Server)   │   │  │   │   (App Server)   │   │               │
│   │   │   t3.medium      │   │  │   │   t3.medium      │   │               │
│   │   └──────────────────┘   │  │   └──────────────────┘   │               │
│   │                          │  │                          │               │
│   │   Auto Scaling Group     │  │   Auto Scaling Group     │               │
│   │   Min: 2 | Max: 6       │  │   (same ASG spans AZs)  │               │
│   └─────────────┬────────────┘  └───────┬──────────────────┘               │
│                 │                        │                                   │
│   ┌─────────────▼────────────┐  ┌───────▼──────────────────┐               │
│   │   DB SUBNET (AZ-a)      │  │   DB SUBNET (AZ-b)       │               │
│   │                          │  │                          │               │
│   │   ┌──────────────────┐   │  │   ┌──────────────────┐   │               │
│   │   │   RDS Primary    │   │  │   │   RDS Standby    │   │               │
│   │   │   PostgreSQL     │───┼──┼──▶│   (Multi-AZ)     │   │               │
│   │   │   db.r6g.large   │   │  │   │   Auto failover  │   │               │
│   │   └──────────────────┘   │  │   └──────────────────┘   │               │
│   └──────────────────────────┘  └──────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                                             │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│   │   MONITORING: CloudWatch Alarms → SNS → Email/Slack          │          │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💻 Practical Examples

56.1.1 High Availability Design

The architecture achieves high availability through multi-AZ deployment of all components. If one AZ fails, traffic automatically routes to healthy instances in the remaining AZ.

Tier Component HA Strategy Failover Time
Presentation ALB Multi-AZ by default Automatic, ~seconds
Application EC2 ASG ASG spans 2+ AZs New instance: 2-5 min
Database RDS Multi-AZ Synchronous replication Automatic: 60-120s
56.1.2 Security Layers

Security is implemented at every layer: ALB accepts only HTTPS, app servers accept traffic only from the ALB, and the database accepts connections only from app servers.

Example: Project Variables
variable "project_name" {
  description = "Project name used for resource naming"
  type        = string
  default     = "three-tier-app"
}

variable "vpc_cidr" {
  description = "VPC CIDR block"
  type        = string
  default     = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

variable "availability_zones" {
  description = "List of availability zones"
  type        = list(string)
  default     = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]
}

variable "db_name" {
  description = "Database name"
  type        = string
  default     = "appdb"
}

variable "db_username" {
  description = "Database master username"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true
}

variable "db_password" {
  description = "Database master password"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true
}

variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
  default     = "t3.medium"
}

variable "min_size" {
  description = "Minimum ASG size"
  type        = number
  default     = 2
}

variable "max_size" {
  description = "Maximum ASG size"
  type        = number
  default     = 6
}

56.2 VPC and Networking

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_vpc | aws_subnet

The VPC provides network isolation with three subnet tiers: public (ALB), private (application), and database subnets, each spanning multiple availability zones.

💻 Practical Examples

56.2.1 Multi-AZ VPC

The VPC uses a /16 CIDR block, providing 65,536 IP addresses distributed across six subnets (2 per tier).

Example: VPC with DNS Support
# ─── VPC ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_cidr
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-vpc"
  }
}

# ─── Internet Gateway ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_internet_gateway" "main" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-igw"
  }
}
56.2.2 Public/Private Subnets

Public subnets host the ALB, private subnets host application servers, and database subnets host RDS instances. Each tier has its own route table.

Example: Three-Tier Subnet Layout
# ─── Public Subnets (ALB) ────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count                   = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block              = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index)
  availability_zone       = var.availability_zones[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-public-${var.availability_zones[count.index]}"
    Tier = "public"
  }
}

# ─── Private Subnets (Application) ──────────────────────
resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count             = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index + 10)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-private-${var.availability_zones[count.index]}"
    Tier = "private"
  }
}

# ─── Database Subnets ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_subnet" "database" {
  count             = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index + 20)
  availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index]

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-db-${var.availability_zones[count.index]}"
    Tier = "database"
  }
}

# ─── Public Route Table ─────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_route_table" "public" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  route {
    cidr_block = "0.0.0.0/0"
    gateway_id = aws_internet_gateway.main.id
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-public-rt"
  }
}

resource "aws_route_table_association" "public" {
  count          = length(var.availability_zones)
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.public.id
}
56.2.3 NAT Gateways

NAT Gateways allow private subnet instances to reach the internet (for updates, API calls) while remaining inaccessible from outside.

Example: NAT Gateway with Private Route Table
# ─── Elastic IP for NAT ──────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_eip" "nat" {
  count  = length(var.availability_zones)
  domain = "vpc"

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-nat-eip-${count.index}"
  }
}

# ─── NAT Gateway (one per AZ for HA) ────────────────────
resource "aws_nat_gateway" "main" {
  count         = length(var.availability_zones)
  allocation_id = aws_eip.nat[count.index].id
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[count.index].id

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-nat-${var.availability_zones[count.index]}"
  }

  depends_on = [aws_internet_gateway.main]
}

# ─── Private Route Tables ───────────────────────────────
resource "aws_route_table" "private" {
  count  = length(var.availability_zones)
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  route {
    cidr_block     = "0.0.0.0/0"
    nat_gateway_id = aws_nat_gateway.main[count.index].id
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-private-rt-${count.index}"
  }
}

resource "aws_route_table_association" "private" {
  count          = length(var.availability_zones)
  subnet_id      = aws_subnet.private[count.index].id
  route_table_id = aws_route_table.private[count.index].id
}

56.3 Application Load Balancer

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_lb | aws_lb_target_group

The Application Load Balancer distributes incoming traffic across EC2 instances in multiple AZs, performs health checks, and terminates HTTPS connections.

💻 Practical Examples

56.3.1 Target Groups

Target groups define how the ALB routes requests to backend instances and monitor their health.

Example: ALB with Target Group
# ─── Application Load Balancer ───────────────────────────
resource "aws_lb" "app" {
  name               = "${var.project_name}-alb"
  internal           = false
  load_balancer_type = "application"
  security_groups    = [aws_security_group.alb.id]
  subnets            = aws_subnet.public[*].id

  enable_deletion_protection = true
  drop_invalid_header_fields = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-alb"
  }
}

# ─── Target Group ────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_lb_target_group" "app" {
  name     = "${var.project_name}-tg"
  port     = 80
  protocol = "HTTP"
  vpc_id   = aws_vpc.main.id

  health_check {
    enabled             = true
    healthy_threshold   = 3
    unhealthy_threshold = 3
    timeout             = 5
    interval            = 30
    path                = "/health"
    port                = "traffic-port"
    protocol            = "HTTP"
    matcher             = "200"
  }

  stickiness {
    type            = "lb_cookie"
    cookie_duration = 86400
    enabled         = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-tg"
  }
}
56.3.2 HTTPS Listener

The HTTPS listener terminates SSL connections at the ALB level, forwarding decrypted traffic to instances on port 80.

Example: HTTPS Listener with HTTP Redirect
# ─── HTTPS Listener ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_lb_listener" "https" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.app.arn
  port              = 443
  protocol          = "HTTPS"
  ssl_policy        = "ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06"
  certificate_arn   = aws_acm_certificate_validation.app.certificate_arn

  default_action {
    type             = "forward"
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.app.arn
  }
}

# ─── HTTP → HTTPS Redirect ──────────────────────────────
resource "aws_lb_listener" "http_redirect" {
  load_balancer_arn = aws_lb.app.arn
  port              = 80
  protocol          = "HTTP"

  default_action {
    type = "redirect"
    redirect {
      port        = "443"
      protocol    = "HTTPS"
      status_code = "HTTP_301"
    }
  }
}
56.3.3 Health Checks

Health checks ensure only healthy instances receive traffic. Unhealthy instances are automatically deregistered and replaced by the Auto Scaling Group.

Parameter Value Description
Path/healthEndpoint that returns 200 OK
Interval30 secondsTime between health checks
Timeout5 secondsWait time for response
Healthy Threshold3Consecutive passes to mark healthy
Unhealthy Threshold3Consecutive fails to mark unhealthy
Matcher200Expected HTTP response code

56.4 EC2 Auto Scaling

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_launch_template | aws_autoscaling_group

Auto Scaling automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances based on demand. Launch templates define the instance configuration, while scaling policies control when to scale.

💻 Practical Examples

56.4.1 Launch Template

Launch templates define the AMI, instance type, security groups, IAM role, and user data script for Auto Scaling instances.

Example: Launch Template with User Data
# ─── Latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI ────────────────────────
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["al2023-ami-*-x86_64"]
  }

  filter {
    name   = "virtualization-type"
    values = ["hvm"]
  }
}

# ─── Launch Template ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_launch_template" "app" {
  name_prefix   = "${var.project_name}-lt-"
  image_id      = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.app.id]

  iam_instance_profile {
    name = aws_iam_instance_profile.app.name
  }

  user_data = base64encode(templatefile("${path.module}/user_data.sh", {
    db_host     = aws_db_instance.main.address
    db_name     = var.db_name
    db_username = var.db_username
    db_password = var.db_password
    app_port    = 80
  }))

  monitoring {
    enabled = true
  }

  metadata_options {
    http_endpoint               = "enabled"
    http_tokens                 = "required"   # IMDSv2 only
    http_put_response_hop_limit = 1
  }

  tag_specifications {
    resource_type = "instance"
    tags = {
      Name = "${var.project_name}-app"
    }
  }

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}
56.4.2 Auto Scaling Group

The ASG maintains the desired number of instances across AZs, replaces unhealthy instances, and integrates with the ALB target group.

Example: Auto Scaling Group Configuration
# ─── Auto Scaling Group ──────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "app" {
  name                = "${var.project_name}-asg"
  vpc_zone_identifier = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  target_group_arns   = [aws_lb_target_group.app.arn]
  health_check_type   = "ELB"

  min_size         = var.min_size
  max_size         = var.max_size
  desired_capacity = var.min_size

  launch_template {
    id      = aws_launch_template.app.id
    version = "$Latest"
  }

  instance_refresh {
    strategy = "Rolling"
    preferences {
      min_healthy_percentage = 50
    }
  }

  tag {
    key                 = "Name"
    value               = "${var.project_name}-app"
    propagate_at_launch = true
  }
}
56.4.3 Scaling Policies

Target tracking scaling policies automatically adjust capacity to maintain a target metric value (e.g., 70% CPU utilization).

Example: Target Tracking Scaling Policy
# ─── CPU-Based Scaling ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_autoscaling_policy" "cpu" {
  name                   = "${var.project_name}-cpu-scaling"
  autoscaling_group_name = aws_autoscaling_group.app.name
  policy_type            = "TargetTrackingScaling"

  target_tracking_configuration {
    predefined_metric_specification {
      predefined_metric_type = "ASGAverageCPUUtilization"
    }
    target_value = 70.0
  }
}

# ─── Request Count Scaling ───────────────────────────────
resource "aws_autoscaling_policy" "requests" {
  name                   = "${var.project_name}-request-scaling"
  autoscaling_group_name = aws_autoscaling_group.app.name
  policy_type            = "TargetTrackingScaling"

  target_tracking_configuration {
    predefined_metric_specification {
      predefined_metric_type = "ALBRequestCountPerTarget"
      resource_label         = "${aws_lb.app.arn_suffix}/${aws_lb_target_group.app.arn_suffix}"
    }
    target_value = 1000.0
  }
}

56.5 RDS Database

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_db_instance | aws_db_subnet_group

RDS Multi-AZ provides a production-grade relational database with automatic failover, encrypted storage, automated backups, and point-in-time recovery.

💻 Practical Examples

56.5.1 Multi-AZ RDS

Multi-AZ deployments maintain a synchronous standby replica in a different AZ. AWS automatically fails over to the standby within 60-120 seconds.

Example: RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ
# ─── DB Subnet Group ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_subnet_group" "main" {
  name       = "${var.project_name}-db-subnet"
  subnet_ids = aws_subnet.database[*].id

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-db-subnet-group"
  }
}

# ─── RDS Instance ────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  identifier     = "${var.project_name}-db"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "16.3"
  instance_class = "db.r6g.large"

  allocated_storage     = 100
  max_allocated_storage = 500
  storage_type          = "gp3"
  storage_encrypted     = true

  db_name  = var.db_name
  username = var.db_username
  password = var.db_password

  multi_az               = true
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.db.id]

  backup_retention_period = 7
  backup_window           = "03:00-04:00"
  maintenance_window      = "sun:04:00-sun:05:00"

  skip_final_snapshot       = false
  final_snapshot_identifier = "${var.project_name}-final-snapshot"
  deletion_protection       = true

  performance_insights_enabled = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-db"
  }
}
56.5.2 Parameter Groups

Custom parameter groups let you tune database settings without modifying the default group shared by other instances.

Example: Custom Parameter Group
resource "aws_db_parameter_group" "main" {
  name   = "${var.project_name}-pg16"
  family = "postgres16"

  parameter {
    name  = "log_connections"
    value = "1"
  }

  parameter {
    name  = "log_disconnections"
    value = "1"
  }

  parameter {
    name  = "log_min_duration_statement"
    value = "1000"  # Log queries > 1 second
  }

  parameter {
    name  = "shared_preload_libraries"
    value = "pg_stat_statements"
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-parameter-group"
  }
}
56.5.3 Security Groups

The database security group only allows inbound connections from the application security group on the PostgreSQL port (5432).

Example: Database Security Group
resource "aws_security_group" "db" {
  name_prefix = "${var.project_name}-db-"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  ingress {
    description     = "PostgreSQL from app servers"
    from_port       = 5432
    to_port         = 5432
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.app.id]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-db-sg"
  }

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

56.6 Security Implementation

SECURITY
📚 Official Documentation: aws_security_group | aws_iam_role

Security is implemented at every tier with security groups acting as virtual firewalls and IAM roles providing least-privilege access to AWS services.

💻 Practical Examples

56.6.1 Security Group Design

Security groups form a chain: internet → ALB → App → DB. Each tier only accepts traffic from the tier above it.

Example: Layered Security Groups
# ─── ALB Security Group ──────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_security_group" "alb" {
  name_prefix = "${var.project_name}-alb-"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  ingress {
    description = "HTTPS from internet"
    from_port   = 443
    to_port     = 443
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  ingress {
    description = "HTTP (redirect to HTTPS)"
    from_port   = 80
    to_port     = 80
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-alb-sg"
  }
}

# ─── App Security Group ─────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
  name_prefix = "${var.project_name}-app-"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id

  ingress {
    description     = "HTTP from ALB only"
    from_port       = 80
    to_port         = 80
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.alb.id]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-app-sg"
  }
}
56.6.2 IAM Roles

EC2 instances use IAM instance profiles instead of embedded credentials. The role grants least-privilege access to required services.

Example: EC2 Instance Profile with SSM Access
# ─── IAM Role for EC2 ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "app" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-app-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com"
      }
    }]
  })
}

# ─── SSM Access (no SSH needed) ──────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "ssm" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.app.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore"
}

# ─── CloudWatch Agent ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cloudwatch" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.app.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy"
}

# ─── Instance Profile ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_instance_profile" "app" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-app-profile"
  role = aws_iam_role.app.name
}

56.7 Monitoring and Alerts

MONITORING
📚 Official Documentation: aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm | aws_sns_topic

CloudWatch alarms monitor key metrics and trigger SNS notifications when thresholds are breached, enabling proactive incident response.

💻 Practical Examples

56.7.1 CloudWatch Alarms

Set up alarms for critical metrics: ALB 5xx errors, high CPU, RDS connections, and response latency.

Example: Multi-Tier Monitoring Alarms
# ─── SNS Topic for Alerts ────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_sns_topic" "alerts" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-alerts"
}

resource "aws_sns_topic_subscription" "email" {
  topic_arn = aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn
  protocol  = "email"
  endpoint  = "ops-team@example.com"
}

# ─── ALB 5xx Errors ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "alb_5xx" {
  alarm_name          = "${var.project_name}-alb-5xx"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  metric_name         = "HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 10
  alarm_description   = "ALB 5xx errors exceeded threshold"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]

  dimensions = {
    LoadBalancer = aws_lb.app.arn_suffix
  }
}

# ─── RDS CPU Utilization ─────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "rds_cpu" {
  alarm_name          = "${var.project_name}-rds-cpu"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 3
  metric_name         = "CPUUtilization"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Average"
  threshold           = 80
  alarm_description   = "RDS CPU utilization above 80%"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]

  dimensions = {
    DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id
  }
}

# ─── RDS Free Storage Space ──────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "rds_storage" {
  alarm_name          = "${var.project_name}-rds-storage"
  comparison_operator = "LessThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "FreeStorageSpace"
  namespace           = "AWS/RDS"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Average"
  threshold           = 10737418240  # 10 GB in bytes
  alarm_description   = "RDS free storage below 10GB"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]

  dimensions = {
    DBInstanceIdentifier = aws_db_instance.main.id
  }
}
56.7.2 SNS Notifications

Combine all outputs for easy reference and operational use.

Example: Project Outputs
# ─── Outputs ─────────────────────────────────────────────
output "alb_dns_name" {
  description = "ALB DNS name"
  value       = aws_lb.app.dns_name
}

output "db_endpoint" {
  description = "RDS endpoint"
  value       = aws_db_instance.main.endpoint
  sensitive   = true
}

output "vpc_id" {
  description = "VPC ID"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  description = "Private subnet IDs"
  value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}

output "asg_name" {
  description = "Auto Scaling Group name"
  value       = aws_autoscaling_group.app.name
}
💡 Production Checklist: Before going live, ensure: ✅ deletion_protection enabled on ALB and RDS, ✅ Multi-AZ enabled for RDS, ✅ IMDSv2 required on EC2, ✅ encrypted storage on all volumes, ✅ CloudWatch alarms for all tiers, ✅ backup_retention_period ≥ 7 days, ✅ SSL/TLS policy set to TLS 1.2+.
⚡ 🐍 🌐 📦
CHAPTER 57

Project 3 - Serverless API with Lambda (Python/FastAPI)

API Gateway Lambda FastAPI DynamoDB Mangum Python
6Sections
15Subsections
20+Code Examples
⚡Serverless
PART 16 ADVANCED ★★★★☆

Deploy a production-ready serverless REST API using AWS Lambda with Python/FastAPI, API Gateway HTTP API, DynamoDB for data storage, Lambda Layers for dependencies, and automated deployment packaging — all managed with Terraform.

Learning Objectives

  • Design an event-driven serverless API architecture
  • Configure API Gateway HTTP API with routes and stages
  • Deploy Lambda functions with Python runtime and Mangum adapter
  • Create DynamoDB tables with GSIs for flexible querying
  • Implement least-privilege IAM for Lambda execution
  • Package Python dependencies with Lambda Layers

Chapter Contents

57.1 Architecture Overview 57.2 API Gateway Setup 57.3 Lambda Functions 57.4 DynamoDB Tables 57.5 IAM Roles and Policies 57.6 Deploying Python Code

57.1 Architecture Overview

ARCHITECTURE
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Lambda Developer Guide | Mangum (FastAPI → Lambda)

A fully serverless API architecture where API Gateway receives HTTP requests, Lambda executes Python/FastAPI code, and DynamoDB stores data — with zero servers to manage.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SERVERLESS API ARCHITECTURE                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│   Client (Browser / Mobile / CLI)                                           │
│      │                                                                      │
│      ▼                                                                      │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│   │           AWS API GATEWAY (HTTP API v2)                      │          │
│   │                                                              │          │
│   │   Routes:                                                    │          │
│   │     GET    /api/v1/items        → Lambda (list_items)        │          │
│   │     POST   /api/v1/items        → Lambda (create_item)       │          │
│   │     GET    /api/v1/items/{id}   → Lambda (get_item)          │          │
│   │     PUT    /api/v1/items/{id}   → Lambda (update_item)       │          │
│   │     DELETE /api/v1/items/{id}   → Lambda (delete_item)       │          │
│   │                                                              │          │
│   │   Stage: $default (auto-deploy)                              │          │
│   └─────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                         │                                                   │
│                         ▼                                                   │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│   │              AWS LAMBDA FUNCTION                             │          │
│   │                                                              │          │
│   │   Runtime: Python 3.12                                       │          │
│   │   Handler: mangum_handler.handler                            │          │
│   │   Memory:  512 MB  │  Timeout: 30s                           │          │
│   │                                                              │          │
│   │   ┌─────────────────┐   ┌───────────────────────────┐       │          │
│   │   │  FastAPI App    │   │  Lambda Layer             │       │          │
│   │   │  (main.py)      │   │  (fastapi, mangum,        │       │          │
│   │   │                 │   │   boto3, pydantic)        │       │          │
│   │   └─────────────────┘   └───────────────────────────┘       │          │
│   └─────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                         │                                                   │
│                         ▼                                                   │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│   │              AMAZON DYNAMODB                                 │          │
│   │                                                              │          │
│   │   Table: items                                               │          │
│   │   PK: item_id (S)  │  SK: created_at (S)                    │          │
│   │   GSI: status-index (status → created_at)                    │          │
│   │   Billing: PAY_PER_REQUEST                                   │          │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                             │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
│   │   CloudWatch Logs  │  X-Ray Tracing  │  Dead Letter Queue    │          │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💻 Practical Examples

57.1.1 API Gateway + Lambda

API Gateway HTTP API (v2) provides a low-latency, cost-effective entry point. It natively supports JWT authorization, CORS, and auto-deployment stages.

Feature HTTP API (v2) REST API (v1)
Cost$1.00/million$3.50/million
Latency~10ms overhead~30ms overhead
JWT AuthBuilt-inCustom authorizer
CORSBuilt-inManual setup
Best ForModern APIsLegacy/complex APIs
57.1.2 Event-Driven Design

Lambda functions are invoked on-demand by API Gateway events. Each request creates a new execution environment (or reuses a warm one), scaling automatically from 0 to thousands of concurrent requests.

Example: Project Variables and Provider
variable "project_name" {
  description = "Project name for resource naming"
  type        = string
  default     = "serverless-api"
}

variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment"
  type        = string
  default     = "production"
}

variable "lambda_memory" {
  description = "Lambda memory in MB"
  type        = number
  default     = 512
}

variable "lambda_timeout" {
  description = "Lambda timeout in seconds"
  type        = number
  default     = 30
}

# ─── Provider ────────────────────────────────────────────
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
    archive = {
      source  = "hashicorp/archive"
      version = "~> 2.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = var.project_name
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    }
  }
}

57.2 API Gateway Setup

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_apigatewayv2_api | aws_apigatewayv2_stage

API Gateway HTTP API provides the entry point for all API requests, routing them to the appropriate Lambda integration based on method and path.

💻 Practical Examples

57.2.1 HTTP API

The HTTP API resource creates the gateway with CORS configuration. All traffic is routed to a single Lambda function running FastAPI with a catch-all route.

Example: HTTP API with CORS
# ─── API Gateway HTTP API ────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_apigatewayv2_api" "main" {
  name          = "${var.project_name}-api"
  protocol_type = "HTTP"

  cors_configuration {
    allow_headers = ["Content-Type", "Authorization", "X-Amz-Date"]
    allow_methods = ["GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS"]
    allow_origins = ["https://myapp.example.com"]
    max_age       = 3600
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-api"
  }
}
57.2.2 Routes

A catch-all route forwards all requests to the Lambda function, where FastAPI handles routing internally via Mangum.

Example: Lambda Integration and Route
# ─── Lambda Integration ──────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_apigatewayv2_integration" "lambda" {
  api_id                 = aws_apigatewayv2_api.main.id
  integration_type       = "AWS_PROXY"
  integration_uri        = aws_lambda_function.api.invoke_arn
  payload_format_version = "2.0"
}

# ─── Catch-All Route ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_apigatewayv2_route" "catch_all" {
  api_id    = aws_apigatewayv2_api.main.id
  route_key = "$default"
  target    = "integrations/${aws_apigatewayv2_integration.lambda.id}"
}

# ─── API Gateway → Lambda Permission ────────────────────
resource "aws_lambda_permission" "apigw" {
  statement_id  = "AllowAPIGatewayInvoke"
  action        = "lambda:InvokeFunction"
  function_name = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name
  principal     = "apigateway.amazonaws.com"
  source_arn    = "${aws_apigatewayv2_api.main.execution_arn}/*/*"
}
57.2.3 Stages

The $default stage enables auto-deployment, so any route or integration changes deploy automatically without manual intervention.

Example: Auto-Deploy Stage with Logging
# ─── Default Stage (auto-deploy) ─────────────────────────
resource "aws_apigatewayv2_stage" "default" {
  api_id      = aws_apigatewayv2_api.main.id
  name        = "$default"
  auto_deploy = true

  access_log_settings {
    destination_arn = aws_cloudwatch_log_group.apigw.arn
    format = jsonencode({
      requestId      = "$context.requestId"
      ip             = "$context.identity.sourceIp"
      requestTime    = "$context.requestTime"
      httpMethod     = "$context.httpMethod"
      routeKey       = "$context.routeKey"
      status         = "$context.status"
      protocol       = "$context.protocol"
      responseLength = "$context.responseLength"
      integrationError = "$context.integrationErrorMessage"
    })
  }
}

# ─── CloudWatch Log Group for API Gateway ────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "apigw" {
  name              = "/aws/apigateway/${var.project_name}"
  retention_in_days = 30
}

57.3 Lambda Functions

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_lambda_function | aws_lambda_layer_version

Lambda functions execute Python code in response to API Gateway events. FastAPI handles request routing, and Mangum bridges ASGI to Lambda's event format.

💻 Practical Examples

57.3.1 Python Runtime

The Lambda function uses Python 3.12 runtime with X-Ray tracing, environment variables for DynamoDB table name, and a Lambda Layer for dependencies.

Example: Lambda Function Configuration
# ─── Lambda Function ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_lambda_function" "api" {
  function_name = "${var.project_name}-handler"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
  handler       = "mangum_handler.handler"
  runtime       = "python3.12"
  timeout       = var.lambda_timeout
  memory_size   = var.lambda_memory

  filename         = data.archive_file.lambda_code.output_path
  source_code_hash = data.archive_file.lambda_code.output_base64sha256

  layers = [aws_lambda_layer_version.dependencies.arn]

  environment {
    variables = {
      TABLE_NAME  = aws_dynamodb_table.items.name
      ENVIRONMENT = var.environment
      LOG_LEVEL   = "INFO"
    }
  }

  tracing_config {
    mode = "Active"
  }

  dead_letter_config {
    target_arn = aws_sqs_queue.dlq.arn
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-handler"
  }
}

# ─── CloudWatch Log Group ────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "lambda" {
  name              = "/aws/lambda/${aws_lambda_function.api.function_name}"
  retention_in_days = 14
}

# ─── Dead Letter Queue ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_sqs_queue" "dlq" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-dlq"
}
57.3.2 Mangum for FastAPI

Mangum is an ASGI adapter that converts API Gateway events into ASGI requests, allowing FastAPI to run natively inside Lambda.

Example: FastAPI App with Mangum Handler
# src/main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
import boto3
import os
import uuid
from datetime import datetime

app = FastAPI(title="Serverless Items API", version="1.0.0")
dynamodb = boto3.resource("dynamodb")
table = dynamodb.Table(os.environ["TABLE_NAME"])


class ItemCreate(BaseModel):
    name: str
    description: str
    status: str = "active"


class Item(ItemCreate):
    item_id: str
    created_at: str


@app.get("/health")
def health_check():
    return {"status": "healthy"}


@app.get("/api/v1/items")
def list_items():
    response = table.scan()
    return {"items": response["Items"], "count": response["Count"]}


@app.post("/api/v1/items", status_code=201)
def create_item(item: ItemCreate):
    db_item = {
        "item_id": str(uuid.uuid4()),
        "created_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        **item.model_dump(),
    }
    table.put_item(Item=db_item)
    return db_item


@app.get("/api/v1/items/{item_id}")
def get_item(item_id: str):
    response = table.get_item(Key={"item_id": item_id})
    if "Item" not in response:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Item not found")
    return response["Item"]
# src/mangum_handler.py
from mangum import Mangum
from main import app

handler = Mangum(app, lifespan="off")
57.3.3 Layers for Dependencies

Lambda Layers package Python dependencies separately from application code, reducing deployment size and enabling shared libraries across functions.

Example: Lambda Layer for Python Dependencies
# ─── Build Layer (via null_resource) ─────────────────────
resource "null_resource" "pip_install" {
  triggers = {
    requirements = filesha256("${path.module}/requirements.txt")
  }

  provisioner "local-exec" {
    command = <<-EOT
      pip install -r requirements.txt \
        -t ${path.module}/layer/python \
        --platform manylinux2014_x86_64 \
