        --only-binary=:all: \
        --upgrade
    EOT
  }
}

data "archive_file" "layer" {
  type        = "zip"
  source_dir  = "${path.module}/layer"
  output_path = "${path.module}/builds/layer.zip"
  depends_on  = [null_resource.pip_install]
}

# ─── Lambda Layer ────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_lambda_layer_version" "dependencies" {
  layer_name          = "${var.project_name}-deps"
  filename            = data.archive_file.layer.output_path
  source_code_hash    = data.archive_file.layer.output_base64sha256
  compatible_runtimes = ["python3.12"]
}

57.4 DynamoDB Tables

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_dynamodb_table

DynamoDB provides a fully managed NoSQL database with single-digit millisecond latency and automatic scaling — ideal for serverless workloads.

💻 Practical Examples

57.4.1 Table Design

The items table uses item_id as the partition key with on-demand billing (PAY_PER_REQUEST) for cost-effective scaling from 0 to peak loads.

Example: DynamoDB Table with Encryption
# ─── DynamoDB Table ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "items" {
  name         = "${var.project_name}-items"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "item_id"

  attribute {
    name = "item_id"
    type = "S"
  }

  attribute {
    name = "status"
    type = "S"
  }

  attribute {
    name = "created_at"
    type = "S"
  }

  # GSI for querying by status
  global_secondary_index {
    name            = "status-index"
    hash_key        = "status"
    range_key       = "created_at"
    projection_type = "ALL"
  }

  point_in_time_recovery {
    enabled = true
  }

  server_side_encryption {
    enabled = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-items"
  }
}
57.4.2 GSI Configuration

Global Secondary Indexes (GSIs) enable efficient queries on non-key attributes. The status-index allows querying items by status sorted by creation date.

Access Pattern Index Key Condition
Get item by IDPrimary (item_id)item_id = :id
List active itemsstatus-indexstatus = "active"
Active items by datestatus-indexstatus = "active" AND created_at > :date

57.5 IAM Roles and Policies

SECURITY
📚 Official Documentation: aws_iam_role | aws_iam_policy

Lambda functions require an execution role with least-privilege permissions — only the specific DynamoDB actions and CloudWatch Logs access needed.

💻 Practical Examples

57.5.1 Lambda Execution Role

The execution role allows Lambda to assume the role and write to CloudWatch Logs for monitoring.

Example: Lambda Execution Role
# ─── Lambda Execution Role ───────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "lambda" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-lambda-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "lambda.amazonaws.com"
      }
    }]
  })
}

# ─── CloudWatch Logs Policy ─────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "lambda_logs" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.lambda.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole"
}

# ─── X-Ray Tracing Policy ───────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "xray" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.lambda.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AWSXRayDaemonWriteAccess"
}
57.5.2 DynamoDB Access

A custom inline policy grants only the specific DynamoDB actions needed, scoped to the exact table and its indexes.

Example: Least-Privilege DynamoDB Policy
# ─── DynamoDB Access Policy ──────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "dynamodb" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-dynamodb-access"
  role = aws_iam_role.lambda.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "dynamodb:GetItem",
          "dynamodb:PutItem",
          "dynamodb:UpdateItem",
          "dynamodb:DeleteItem",
          "dynamodb:Scan",
          "dynamodb:Query"
        ]
        Resource = [
          aws_dynamodb_table.items.arn,
          "${aws_dynamodb_table.items.arn}/index/*"
        ]
      }
    ]
  })
}

# ─── SQS DLQ Access ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "dlq" {
  name = "${var.project_name}-dlq-access"
  role = aws_iam_role.lambda.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect   = "Allow"
      Action   = ["sqs:SendMessage"]
      Resource = [aws_sqs_queue.dlq.arn]
    }]
  })
}

57.6 Deploying Python Code

DEPLOYMENT
📚 Official Documentation: archive_file

Package the FastAPI application code into a ZIP archive for Lambda deployment using the Terraform archive provider.

💻 Practical Examples

57.6.1 Package Structure

The project follows a clean structure separating application code, dependencies layer, Terraform configuration, and build artifacts.

Example: Project Directory Layout
serverless-api/
├── main.tf                  # Provider, variables
├── api_gateway.tf           # API Gateway resources
├── lambda.tf                # Lambda function & layer
├── dynamodb.tf              # DynamoDB table
├── iam.tf                   # IAM roles & policies
├── outputs.tf               # Output values
├── variables.tf             # Input variables
├── requirements.txt         # Python dependencies
├── src/
│   ├── main.py              # FastAPI application
│   └── mangum_handler.py    # Lambda entry point
├── layer/                   # (generated) Layer dependencies
│   └── python/
│       ├── fastapi/
│       ├── mangum/
│       └── pydantic/
└── builds/                  # (generated) ZIP artifacts
    ├── lambda.zip
    └── layer.zip
57.6.2 Deployment Method

The archive provider creates ZIP files from source directories, and the source_code_hash ensures Lambda updates only when code changes.

Example: Package and Deploy
# ─── Package Application Code ────────────────────────────
data "archive_file" "lambda_code" {
  type        = "zip"
  source_dir  = "${path.module}/src"
  output_path = "${path.module}/builds/lambda.zip"
}

# ─── Outputs ─────────────────────────────────────────────
output "api_endpoint" {
  description = "API Gateway endpoint URL"
  value       = aws_apigatewayv2_stage.default.invoke_url
}

output "function_name" {
  description = "Lambda function name"
  value       = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name
}

output "dynamodb_table" {
  description = "DynamoDB table name"
  value       = aws_dynamodb_table.items.name
}
💡 Cold Start Tips: To minimize Lambda cold starts: ✅ Use Python 3.12 (fastest startup), ✅ Keep deployment package small (use layers), ✅ Set memory to 512MB+ (more memory = more CPU = faster init), ✅ Use provisioned concurrency for latency-sensitive endpoints, ✅ Avoid importing unused libraries in handler.
☸️ 🐳 🔧 📦
CHAPTER 58

Project 4 - EKS Kubernetes Cluster

EKS Kubernetes Node Groups IRSA Helm Add-ons
7Sections
20Subsections
25+Code Examples
☸️Kubernetes
PART 16 EXPERT ★★★★★

Deploy a production-ready Amazon EKS cluster with managed node groups, IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts), essential add-ons (CoreDNS, kube-proxy, vpc-cni, EBS CSI), kubectl configuration, and Helm chart deployments for ALB Ingress Controller, External DNS, and Cluster Autoscaler.

Learning Objectives

  • Create an EKS cluster with proper versioning and add-ons
  • Configure managed node groups with scaling and instance types
  • Design an EKS-optimized VPC with proper subnet tagging
  • Implement IRSA for secure pod-level AWS access
  • Deploy essential add-ons for networking and storage
  • Generate kubeconfig and manage aws-auth ConfigMap
  • Install Helm charts for ingress, DNS, and autoscaling

Chapter Contents

58.1 EKS Cluster Setup 58.2 Node Groups 58.3 VPC and Networking 58.4 IAM Roles for Service Accounts 58.5 Add-ons Configuration 58.6 kubectl Configuration 58.7 Helm Charts Deployment

58.1 EKS Cluster Setup

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_eks_cluster

Amazon EKS manages the Kubernetes control plane across multiple AZs with automatic upgrades, patching, and high availability.

💻 Practical Examples

58.1.1 Cluster Version

Always pin to a specific Kubernetes version to control upgrades. EKS supports N-3 minor versions with end-of-support dates.

Example: EKS Cluster Resource
variable "cluster_name" {
  description = "EKS cluster name"
  type        = string
  default     = "production-eks"
}

variable "cluster_version" {
  description = "Kubernetes version"
  type        = string
  default     = "1.30"
}

# ─── EKS Cluster ─────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_eks_cluster" "main" {
  name     = var.cluster_name
  version  = var.cluster_version
  role_arn = aws_iam_role.cluster.arn

  vpc_config {
    subnet_ids              = concat(aws_subnet.private[*].id, aws_subnet.public[*].id)
    endpoint_private_access = true
    endpoint_public_access  = true
    public_access_cidrs     = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
    security_group_ids      = [aws_security_group.cluster.id]
  }

  encryption_config {
    provider {
      key_arn = aws_kms_key.eks.arn
    }
    resources = ["secrets"]
  }

  enabled_cluster_log_types = [
    "api", "audit", "authenticator",
    "controllerManager", "scheduler"
  ]

  tags = {
    Name = var.cluster_name
  }

  depends_on = [
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_policy,
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cluster_vpc,
  ]
}

# ─── KMS Key for Secrets Encryption ─────────────────────
resource "aws_kms_key" "eks" {
  description = "EKS Secret Encryption Key"
  enable_key_rotation = true
}
58.1.2 Cluster Add-ons

EKS manages core Kubernetes components as add-ons that can be independently versioned and updated.

Example: Cluster IAM Role
# ─── Cluster IAM Role ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "cluster" {
  name = "${var.cluster_name}-cluster-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "eks.amazonaws.com"
      }
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_policy" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.cluster.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSClusterPolicy"
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cluster_vpc" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.cluster.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSVPCResourceController"
}

58.2 Node Groups

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_eks_node_group

Managed node groups automate the provisioning and lifecycle management of EC2 instances that serve as Kubernetes worker nodes.

💻 Practical Examples

58.2.1 Managed Node Groups

Managed node groups handle AMI selection, OS updates, draining, and node replacement automatically, reducing operational overhead.

Example: General Purpose Node Group
# ─── General Purpose Node Group ──────────────────────────
resource "aws_eks_node_group" "general" {
  cluster_name    = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  node_group_name = "${var.cluster_name}-general"
  node_role_arn   = aws_iam_role.node.arn
  subnet_ids      = aws_subnet.private[*].id

  scaling_config {
    desired_size = 3
    min_size     = 2
    max_size     = 10
  }

  update_config {
    max_unavailable = 1
  }

  instance_types = ["t3.large"]
  capacity_type  = "ON_DEMAND"

  labels = {
    role     = "general"
    workload = "default"
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.cluster_name}-general-node"
  }

  depends_on = [
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.node_policy,
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.cni_policy,
    aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.ecr_policy,
  ]
}
58.2.2 Instance Types

Choose instance types based on workload requirements. Use multiple instance types for better availability with Spot instances.

Node Group Instance Type vCPU Memory Use Case
Generalt3.large28 GBWeb apps, microservices
Computec6i.xlarge48 GBCPU-intensive tasks
Memoryr6i.xlarge432 GBCaching, in-memory DBs
SpotMixedVariesVariesBatch processing, CI/CD
58.2.3 Scaling Configuration

Combine Managed Node Group scaling with Cluster Autoscaler for automatic capacity adjustment based on pending pod demand.

Example: Spot Instance Node Group
# ─── Spot Instance Node Group ────────────────────────────
resource "aws_eks_node_group" "spot" {
  cluster_name    = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  node_group_name = "${var.cluster_name}-spot"
  node_role_arn   = aws_iam_role.node.arn
  subnet_ids      = aws_subnet.private[*].id

  scaling_config {
    desired_size = 2
    min_size     = 0
    max_size     = 20
  }

  instance_types = ["t3.large", "t3a.large", "m5.large", "m5a.large"]
  capacity_type  = "SPOT"

  labels = {
    role     = "spot"
    workload = "batch"
  }

  taint {
    key    = "spot"
    value  = "true"
    effect = "NO_SCHEDULE"
  }

  tags = {
    Name                                              = "${var.cluster_name}-spot-node"
    "k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/${var.cluster_name}"   = "owned"
    "k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled"               = "true"
  }
}

# ─── Node IAM Role ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "node" {
  name = "${var.cluster_name}-node-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "ec2.amazonaws.com"
      }
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "node_policy" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.node.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKSWorkerNodePolicy"
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "cni_policy" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.node.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEKS_CNI_Policy"
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "ecr_policy" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.node.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryReadOnly"
}

58.3 VPC and Networking

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: EKS VPC Requirements

EKS requires specific VPC configuration including subnet tagging for automatic load balancer discovery and sufficient IP addresses for pods.

💻 Practical Examples

58.3.1 EKS-Optimized VPC

EKS subnets require specific tags for the ALB Ingress Controller and internal load balancer discovery.

Example: EKS VPC with Tagged Subnets
# ─── VPC ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_vpc" "eks" {
  cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.cluster_name}-vpc"
  }
}

# ─── Public Subnets (for ALB) ────────────────────────────
resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count                   = 3
  vpc_id                  = aws_vpc.eks.id
  cidr_block              = cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, count.index)
  availability_zone       = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true

  tags = {
    Name                                        = "${var.cluster_name}-public-${count.index}"
    "kubernetes.io/cluster/${var.cluster_name}"  = "shared"
    "kubernetes.io/role/elb"                     = "1"
  }
}

# ─── Private Subnets (for Pods) ──────────────────────────
resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count             = 3
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.eks.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, count.index + 10)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]

  tags = {
    Name                                        = "${var.cluster_name}-private-${count.index}"
    "kubernetes.io/cluster/${var.cluster_name}"  = "shared"
    "kubernetes.io/role/internal-elb"            = "1"
  }
}

data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}
58.3.2 Security Groups

EKS creates a managed security group automatically, but you can add a custom cluster security group for additional rules.

Example: Cluster Security Group
# ─── Cluster Security Group ──────────────────────────────
resource "aws_security_group" "cluster" {
  name_prefix = "${var.cluster_name}-cluster-"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.eks.id

  ingress {
    description = "HTTPS API server"
    from_port   = 443
    to_port     = 443
    protocol    = "tcp"
    cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/16"]
  }

  egress {
    from_port   = 0
    to_port     = 0
    protocol    = "-1"
    cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.cluster_name}-cluster-sg"
  }
}

58.4 IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA)

SECURITY 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: IRSA Guide

IRSA enables pod-level AWS IAM permissions by associating Kubernetes service accounts with IAM roles via OIDC federation — eliminating the need for node-level permissions.

💻 Practical Examples

58.4.1 OIDC Provider

The OIDC provider establishes trust between the EKS cluster and IAM, enabling Kubernetes service accounts to assume IAM roles.

Example: EKS OIDC Provider
# ─── OIDC Provider ───────────────────────────────────────
data "tls_certificate" "eks" {
  url = aws_eks_cluster.main.identity[0].oidc[0].issuer
}

resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "eks" {
  client_id_list  = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
  thumbprint_list = [data.tls_certificate.eks.certificates[0].sha1_fingerprint]
  url             = aws_eks_cluster.main.identity[0].oidc[0].issuer
}

locals {
  oidc_provider     = replace(aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.eks.url, "https://", "")
  oidc_provider_arn = aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.eks.arn
}
58.4.2 IAM Role Configuration

Create IAM roles that trust specific Kubernetes service accounts using OIDC conditions, granting fine-grained AWS access per pod.

Example: IRSA Role for Application Pod
# ─── IRSA Role with Trust Policy ─────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "app_irsa" {
  name = "${var.cluster_name}-app-irsa"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Federated = local.oidc_provider_arn
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "${local.oidc_provider}:aud" = "sts.amazonaws.com"
          "${local.oidc_provider}:sub" = "system:serviceaccount:default:app-sa"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

# ─── Attach Application-Specific Policy ─────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "app_s3" {
  name = "app-s3-access"
  role = aws_iam_role.app_irsa.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect   = "Allow"
      Action   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:PutObject"]
      Resource = ["arn:aws:s3:::my-app-bucket/*"]
    }]
  })
}

58.5 Add-ons Configuration

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_eks_addon

EKS add-ons are curated software packages for cluster networking, DNS, and storage that AWS manages for version compatibility and security updates.

💻 Practical Examples

58.5.1 CoreDNS

CoreDNS provides DNS-based service discovery within the cluster, resolving Kubernetes service names to ClusterIP addresses.

58.5.2 kube-proxy

kube-proxy maintains network rules for pod-to-service communication using iptables or IPVS.

58.5.3 vpc-cni

The VPC CNI plugin assigns VPC IP addresses directly to pods, enabling native VPC networking without overlay networks.

58.5.4 aws-ebs-csi-driver

The EBS CSI driver enables Kubernetes to provision and attach EBS volumes as PersistentVolumes.

Example: All Four EKS Add-ons
# ─── CoreDNS ─────────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_eks_addon" "coredns" {
  cluster_name                = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  addon_name                  = "coredns"
  resolve_conflicts_on_update = "OVERWRITE"
}

# ─── kube-proxy ──────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_eks_addon" "kube_proxy" {
  cluster_name                = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  addon_name                  = "kube-proxy"
  resolve_conflicts_on_update = "OVERWRITE"
}

# ─── VPC CNI ─────────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_eks_addon" "vpc_cni" {
  cluster_name                = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  addon_name                  = "vpc-cni"
  resolve_conflicts_on_update = "OVERWRITE"
  service_account_role_arn    = aws_iam_role.vpc_cni.arn

  configuration_values = jsonencode({
    env = {
      ENABLE_PREFIX_DELEGATION = "true"
      WARM_PREFIX_TARGET       = "1"
    }
  })
}

# ─── EBS CSI Driver ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_eks_addon" "ebs_csi" {
  cluster_name                = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  addon_name                  = "aws-ebs-csi-driver"
  service_account_role_arn    = aws_iam_role.ebs_csi.arn
  resolve_conflicts_on_update = "OVERWRITE"
}

# ─── EBS CSI IRSA Role ──────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "ebs_csi" {
  name = "${var.cluster_name}-ebs-csi-role"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Federated = local.oidc_provider_arn
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "${local.oidc_provider}:sub" = "system:serviceaccount:kube-system:ebs-csi-controller-sa"
          "${local.oidc_provider}:aud" = "sts.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "ebs_csi" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.ebs_csi.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AmazonEBSCSIDriverPolicy"
}

58.6 kubectl Configuration

CONFIG
📚 Official Documentation: Kubeconfig for EKS

Generate kubeconfig files and manage the aws-auth ConfigMap to control who can access the EKS cluster.

💻 Practical Examples

58.6.1 kubeconfig Generation

Terraform outputs enable automatic kubeconfig generation for kubectl access to the cluster.

Example: Outputs for kubeconfig
# ─── Outputs ─────────────────────────────────────────────
output "cluster_endpoint" {
  description = "EKS cluster endpoint"
  value       = aws_eks_cluster.main.endpoint
}

output "cluster_ca_certificate" {
  description = "Cluster CA certificate (base64)"
  value       = aws_eks_cluster.main.certificate_authority[0].data
  sensitive   = true
}

output "cluster_name" {
  description = "EKS cluster name"
  value       = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
}

# ─── Update kubeconfig ──────────────────────────────────
# Run after apply:
# aws eks update-kubeconfig --name production-eks --region us-east-1
58.6.2 aws-auth ConfigMap

The aws-auth ConfigMap maps IAM roles/users to Kubernetes RBAC groups for cluster access control.

Example: aws-auth ConfigMap via Kubernetes Provider
# ─── Kubernetes Provider ─────────────────────────────────
provider "kubernetes" {
  host                   = aws_eks_cluster.main.endpoint
  cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(aws_eks_cluster.main.certificate_authority[0].data)

  exec {
    api_version = "client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1"
    command     = "aws"
    args        = ["eks", "get-token", "--cluster-name", aws_eks_cluster.main.name]
  }
}

# ─── aws-auth ConfigMap ─────────────────────────────────
resource "kubernetes_config_map_v1" "aws_auth" {
  metadata {
    name      = "aws-auth"
    namespace = "kube-system"
  }

  data = {
    mapRoles = yamlencode([
      {
        rolearn  = aws_iam_role.node.arn
        username = "system:node:{{EC2PrivateDNSName}}"
        groups   = ["system:bootstrappers", "system:nodes"]
      },
      {
        rolearn  = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminRole"
        username = "admin"
        groups   = ["system:masters"]
      }
    ])
  }
}

58.7 Helm Charts Deployment

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: helm_release

Deploy Kubernetes applications using Helm charts via the Terraform Helm provider for consistent, repeatable installations.

💻 Practical Examples

58.7.1 ALB Ingress Controller

The AWS Load Balancer Controller creates ALBs/NLBs from Kubernetes Ingress resources, enabling external access to services.

Example: AWS Load Balancer Controller
# ─── Helm Provider ───────────────────────────────────────
provider "helm" {
  kubernetes {
    host                   = aws_eks_cluster.main.endpoint
    cluster_ca_certificate = base64decode(aws_eks_cluster.main.certificate_authority[0].data)
    exec {
      api_version = "client.authentication.k8s.io/v1beta1"
      command     = "aws"
      args        = ["eks", "get-token", "--cluster-name", aws_eks_cluster.main.name]
    }
  }
}

# ─── AWS Load Balancer Controller ────────────────────────
resource "helm_release" "lb_controller" {
  name       = "aws-load-balancer-controller"
  repository = "https://aws.github.io/eks-charts"
  chart      = "aws-load-balancer-controller"
  namespace  = "kube-system"
  version    = "1.7.1"

  set {
    name  = "clusterName"
    value = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  }

  set {
    name  = "serviceAccount.create"
    value = "true"
  }

  set {
    name  = "serviceAccount.annotations.eks\\.amazonaws\\.com/role-arn"
    value = aws_iam_role.lb_controller.arn
  }
}
58.7.2 External DNS

External DNS automatically creates Route 53 records from Kubernetes Ingress and Service annotations.

Example: External DNS Helm Release
# ─── External DNS ────────────────────────────────────────
resource "helm_release" "external_dns" {
  name       = "external-dns"
  repository = "https://kubernetes-sigs.github.io/external-dns/"
  chart      = "external-dns"
  namespace  = "kube-system"
  version    = "1.14.3"

  set {
    name  = "provider"
    value = "aws"
  }

  set {
    name  = "aws.zoneType"
    value = "public"
  }

  set {
    name  = "serviceAccount.annotations.eks\\.amazonaws\\.com/role-arn"
    value = aws_iam_role.external_dns.arn
  }

  set {
    name  = "policy"
    value = "sync"
  }
}
58.7.3 Cluster Autoscaler

The Cluster Autoscaler adjusts the number of nodes when pods are unschedulable due to insufficient resources or when nodes are underutilized.

Example: Cluster Autoscaler Helm Release
# ─── Cluster Autoscaler ──────────────────────────────────
resource "helm_release" "cluster_autoscaler" {
  name       = "cluster-autoscaler"
  repository = "https://kubernetes.github.io/autoscaler"
  chart      = "cluster-autoscaler"
  namespace  = "kube-system"
  version    = "9.35.0"

  set {
    name  = "autoDiscovery.clusterName"
    value = aws_eks_cluster.main.name
  }

  set {
    name  = "awsRegion"
    value = var.aws_region
  }

  set {
    name  = "rbac.serviceAccount.annotations.eks\\.amazonaws\\.com/role-arn"
    value = aws_iam_role.cluster_autoscaler.arn
  }

  set {
    name  = "extraArgs.balance-similar-node-groups"
    value = "true"
  }

  set {
    name  = "extraArgs.skip-nodes-with-system-pods"
    value = "false"
  }
}
💡 EKS Best Practices: ✅ Use IRSA instead of node-level IAM policies, ✅ Enable envelope encryption for Secrets, ✅ Use managed node groups for automated patching, ✅ Tag subnets for ALB auto-discovery, ✅ Enable control plane logging for audit trails, ✅ Use Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter for dynamic scaling.
🏢 🔐 🌐 📋
CHAPTER 59

Project 5 - Multi-Account AWS Organization

Organizations SCPs Account Factory Transit Gateway CloudTrail GuardDuty
6Sections
14Subsections
20+Code Examples
🏢Enterprise
PART 16 EXPERT ★★★★★

Build an enterprise-grade multi-account AWS Organization with organizational units, service control policies, an account factory, cross-account IAM roles, shared services VPC with Transit Gateway, centralized logging via CloudTrail, and security baselines with GuardDuty and Security Hub.

Learning Objectives

  • Structure an AWS Organization with OUs and SCPs
  • Automate account provisioning with an account factory
  • Set up cross-account IAM roles with trust policies
  • Create a shared services VPC with Transit Gateway
  • Centralize logging with CloudTrail organization trail
  • Deploy security baselines with GuardDuty and Security Hub

Chapter Contents

59.1 AWS Organizations 59.2 Account Factory 59.3 Cross-Account IAM Roles 59.4 Shared Services VPC 59.5 Centralized Logging 59.6 Security Baseline

59.1 AWS Organizations

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_organizations_organization

AWS Organizations enables centralized management of multiple AWS accounts with consolidated billing, policy-based governance, and hierarchical account organization.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    AWS ORGANIZATION STRUCTURE                                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│   ROOT (Management Account)                                                 │
│   ├── OU: Security                                                          │
│   │   ├── Log Archive Account      (centralized logging)                    │
│   │   └── Security Tooling Account  (GuardDuty, Security Hub)               │
│   │                                                                         │
│   ├── OU: Infrastructure                                                    │
│   │   ├── Shared Services Account   (Transit GW, DNS, VPN)                  │
│   │   └── Network Account           (Direct Connect, VPC)                   │
│   │                                                                         │
│   ├── OU: Workloads                                                         │
│   │   ├── OU: Production                                                    │
│   │   │   ├── Prod-App1 Account                                             │
│   │   │   └── Prod-App2 Account                                             │
│   │   ├── OU: Staging                                                       │
│   │   │   └── Staging Account                                               │
│   │   └── OU: Development                                                   │
│   │       └── Dev Account                                                   │
│   │                                                                         │
│   └── OU: Sandbox                                                           │
│       └── Sandbox Account           (experimentation)                       │
│                                                                             │
│   SCPs Applied:                                                             │
│   • Root     → Deny leaving org, Deny disabling CloudTrail                  │
│   • Prod     → Deny region outside us-east-1/us-west-2                      │
│   • Sandbox  → Deny expensive services, spending limit                      │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💻 Practical Examples

59.1.1 Organization Structure

Create the organization with all features enabled (consolidated billing + SCPs) from the management account.

Example: Organization Resource
# ─── AWS Organization ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_organizations_organization" "org" {
  feature_set = "ALL"

  aws_service_access_principals = [
    "cloudtrail.amazonaws.com",
    "config.amazonaws.com",
    "guardduty.amazonaws.com",
    "securityhub.amazonaws.com",
    "sso.amazonaws.com",
  ]

  enabled_policy_types = [
    "SERVICE_CONTROL_POLICY",
    "TAG_POLICY",
  ]
}
59.1.2 Organizational Units

OUs group accounts by function or environment, enabling targeted policy application.

Example: OU Hierarchy
# ─── Top-Level OUs ───────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "security" {
  name      = "Security"
  parent_id = aws_organizations_organization.org.roots[0].id
}

resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "infrastructure" {
  name      = "Infrastructure"
  parent_id = aws_organizations_organization.org.roots[0].id
}

resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "workloads" {
  name      = "Workloads"
  parent_id = aws_organizations_organization.org.roots[0].id
}

resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "sandbox" {
  name      = "Sandbox"
  parent_id = aws_organizations_organization.org.roots[0].id
}

# ─── Nested OUs under Workloads ─────────────────────────
resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "production" {
  name      = "Production"
  parent_id = aws_organizations_organizational_unit.workloads.id
}

resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "staging" {
  name      = "Staging"
  parent_id = aws_organizations_organizational_unit.workloads.id
}

resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "development" {
  name      = "Development"
  parent_id = aws_organizations_organizational_unit.workloads.id
}
59.1.3 Service Control Policies

SCPs set permission guardrails across accounts. They don't grant permissions — they restrict which actions can be performed even if IAM allows them.

Example: Deny-Leave-Org and Region-Restriction SCPs
# ─── Deny Leaving Organization ───────────────────────────
resource "aws_organizations_policy" "deny_leave_org" {
  name = "deny-leave-organization"
  type = "SERVICE_CONTROL_POLICY"

  content = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Sid       = "DenyLeaveOrg"
      Effect    = "Deny"
      Action    = ["organizations:LeaveOrganization"]
      Resource  = "*"
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_organizations_policy_attachment" "deny_leave_root" {
  policy_id = aws_organizations_policy.deny_leave_org.id
  target_id = aws_organizations_organization.org.roots[0].id
}

# ─── Region Restriction (Production) ────────────────────
resource "aws_organizations_policy" "region_restrict" {
  name = "restrict-regions"
  type = "SERVICE_CONTROL_POLICY"

  content = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Sid       = "DenyNonApprovedRegions"
      Effect    = "Deny"
      NotAction = [
        "iam:*", "sts:*", "organizations:*",
        "support:*", "budgets:*"
      ]
      Resource = "*"
      Condition = {
        StringNotEquals = {
          "aws:RequestedRegion" = ["us-east-1", "us-west-2"]
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_organizations_policy_attachment" "region_prod" {
  policy_id = aws_organizations_policy.region_restrict.id
  target_id = aws_organizations_organizational_unit.production.id
}

59.2 Account Factory

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_organizations_account

Automate account creation with consistent baselines — each new account gets VPC, IAM roles, Config rules, and logging configured automatically.

💻 Practical Examples

59.2.1 Account Creation

Use for_each to create accounts from a map variable, enabling batch provisioning from configuration.

Example: Account Factory with for_each
variable "accounts" {
  description = "Map of accounts to create"
  type = map(object({
    email     = string
    ou_id     = string
    role_name = string
  }))
  default = {
    "prod-app1" = {
      email     = "aws+prod-app1@example.com"
      ou_id     = "ou-xxxx-production"
      role_name = "OrganizationAccountAccessRole"
    }
    "staging" = {
      email     = "aws+staging@example.com"
      ou_id     = "ou-xxxx-staging"
      role_name = "OrganizationAccountAccessRole"
    }
    "log-archive" = {
      email     = "aws+logs@example.com"
      ou_id     = "ou-xxxx-security"
      role_name = "OrganizationAccountAccessRole"
    }
  }
}

# ─── Create Accounts ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_organizations_account" "accounts" {
  for_each = var.accounts

  name      = each.key
  email     = each.value.email
  parent_id = each.value.ou_id
  role_name = each.value.role_name

  close_on_deletion = true

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [role_name]
  }
}
59.2.2 Baseline Configuration

Apply baseline configuration to each new account using a Terraform module that assumes the cross-account role.

Example: Account Baseline Module
# ─── Baseline Module per Account ─────────────────────────
module "account_baseline" {
  source   = "./modules/account-baseline"
  for_each = aws_organizations_account.accounts

  providers = {
    aws = aws.member
  }

  account_id   = each.value.id
  account_name = each.key
  vpc_cidr     = cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/8", 8, index(keys(var.accounts), each.key))
}

# ─── Provider for Member Account ────────────────────────
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "member"
  region = "us-east-1"

  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::${each.value.id}:role/OrganizationAccountAccessRole"
  }
}

59.3 Cross-Account IAM Roles

SECURITY 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Cross-Account IAM Roles

Cross-account roles enable secure access between accounts without sharing credentials — users in the management account assume roles in member accounts.

💻 Practical Examples

59.3.1 Role Assumption

Terraform providers in multi-account setups use assume_role blocks to operate in different accounts from a single configuration.

Example: Multi-Provider Cross-Account Setup
# ─── Management Account Provider ─────────────────────────
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  alias  = "management"
}

# ─── Shared Services Account ────────────────────────────
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  alias  = "shared"

  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "terraform-shared"
  }
}

# ─── Production Account ─────────────────────────────────
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  alias  = "production"

  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "terraform-production"
  }
}
59.3.2 Trust Policies

Trust policies define which principals (accounts, roles, users) can assume a role using sts:AssumeRole.

Example: Cross-Account Admin and ReadOnly Roles
# ─── Terraform Execution Role (in member account) ───────
resource "aws_iam_role" "terraform" {
  provider = aws.member
  name     = "TerraformRole"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        AWS = "arn:aws:iam::${var.management_account_id}:root"
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRole"
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "aws:PrincipalOrgID" = aws_organizations_organization.org.id
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "terraform_admin" {
  provider   = aws.member
  role       = aws_iam_role.terraform.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess"
}

59.4 Shared Services VPC

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_ec2_transit_gateway

A centralized shared services VPC connected via Transit Gateway enables cross-account connectivity for shared resources like DNS, monitoring, and CI/CD.

💻 Practical Examples

59.4.1 Centralized Networking

The shared services VPC hosts centralized resources accessible from all accounts through Transit Gateway peering.

Example: Shared Services VPC
# ─── Shared Services VPC (in shared account) ────────────
resource "aws_vpc" "shared" {
  provider             = aws.shared
  cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  enable_dns_support   = true

  tags = {
    Name = "shared-services-vpc"
  }
}

# ─── Private Hosted Zone for Internal DNS ────────────────
resource "aws_route53_zone" "internal" {
  provider = aws.shared
  name     = "internal.example.com"

  vpc {
    vpc_id = aws_vpc.shared.id
  }
}
59.4.2 Transit Gateway

Transit Gateway acts as a hub that connects multiple VPCs across accounts with a single gateway.

Example: Transit Gateway Hub-and-Spoke
# ─── Transit Gateway (in shared account) ─────────────────
resource "aws_ec2_transit_gateway" "main" {
  provider    = aws.shared
  description = "Organization Transit Gateway"

  default_route_table_association = "enable"
  default_route_table_propagation = "enable"
  dns_support                     = "enable"

  tags = {
    Name = "org-transit-gateway"
  }
}

# ─── Share via RAM ───────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_ram_resource_share" "tgw" {
  provider                  = aws.shared
  name                      = "transit-gateway-share"
  allow_external_principals = false
}

resource "aws_ram_resource_association" "tgw" {
  provider           = aws.shared
  resource_arn       = aws_ec2_transit_gateway.main.arn
  resource_share_arn = aws_ram_resource_share.tgw.arn
}

resource "aws_ram_principal_association" "org" {
  provider           = aws.shared
  principal          = aws_organizations_organization.org.arn
  resource_share_arn = aws_ram_resource_share.tgw.arn
}

# ─── TGW Attachment (in member account) ─────────────────
resource "aws_ec2_transit_gateway_vpc_attachment" "production" {
  provider           = aws.production
  transit_gateway_id = aws_ec2_transit_gateway.main.id
  vpc_id             = aws_vpc.production.id
  subnet_ids         = aws_subnet.production_private[*].id

  tags = {
    Name = "production-tgw-attachment"
  }
}

59.5 Centralized Logging

LOGGING
📚 Official Documentation: aws_cloudtrail

Centralize all API call logs from every account into a dedicated log archive account for compliance, auditing, and security investigations.

💻 Practical Examples

59.5.1 CloudTrail Organization Trail

An organization trail automatically logs API calls from all accounts in the organization to a single S3 bucket.

Example: Organization-Wide CloudTrail
# ─── Organization Trail ──────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudtrail" "org" {
  name                       = "organization-trail"
  s3_bucket_name             = aws_s3_bucket.cloudtrail.id
  is_organization_trail      = true
  is_multi_region_trail      = true
  enable_log_file_validation = true
  kms_key_id                 = aws_kms_key.cloudtrail.arn

  event_selector {
    read_write_type                 = "All"
    include_management_events       = true
  }

  cloud_watch_logs_group_arn = "${aws_cloudwatch_log_group.cloudtrail.arn}:*"
  cloud_watch_logs_role_arn  = aws_iam_role.cloudtrail_cw.arn

  depends_on = [aws_s3_bucket_policy.cloudtrail]
}
59.5.2 Log Archive Account

The log archive S3 bucket uses object lock, versioning, and restrictive policies to ensure tamper-proof log storage.

Example: Secure Log Archive Bucket
# ─── Log Archive Bucket (in log-archive account) ────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "cloudtrail" {
  provider = aws.log_archive
  bucket   = "org-cloudtrail-logs-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "cloudtrail" {
  provider = aws.log_archive
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.cloudtrail.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "cloudtrail" {
  provider = aws.log_archive
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.cloudtrail.id
  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.cloudtrail.arn
    }
    bucket_key_enabled = true
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "cloudtrail" {
  provider                = aws.log_archive
  bucket                  = aws_s3_bucket.cloudtrail.id
  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

59.6 Security Baseline

SECURITY
📚 Official Documentation: aws_guardduty_detector | aws_securityhub_account

Deploy GuardDuty for threat detection and Security Hub for compliance posture management across all organization accounts.

💻 Practical Examples

59.6.1 GuardDuty

GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs to detect threats like compromised credentials, cryptocurrency mining, and unauthorized access.

Example: Organization-Wide GuardDuty
# ─── GuardDuty Detector (admin account) ──────────────────
resource "aws_guardduty_detector" "main" {
  provider = aws.security
  enable   = true

  datasources {
    s3_logs {
      enable = true
    }
    kubernetes {
      audit_logs {
        enable = true
      }
    }
    malware_protection {
      scan_ec2_instance_with_findings {
        ebs_volumes {
          enable = true
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

# ─── Organization Admin Delegation ──────────────────────
resource "aws_guardduty_organization_admin_account" "security" {
  admin_account_id = var.security_account_id
}

# ─── Auto-Enable for New Accounts ───────────────────────
resource "aws_guardduty_organization_configuration" "main" {
  provider    = aws.security
  detector_id = aws_guardduty_detector.main.id
  auto_enable_organization_members = "ALL"
}
59.6.2 Security Hub

Security Hub aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and AWS Config, providing a unified security posture dashboard.

Example: Security Hub with Standards
# ─── Security Hub ────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_securityhub_account" "main" {
  provider = aws.security
}

# ─── Enable CIS Benchmark ───────────────────────────────
resource "aws_securityhub_standards_subscription" "cis" {
  provider      = aws.security
  standards_arn = "arn:aws:securityhub:::ruleset/cis-aws-foundations-benchmark/v/1.4.0"
  depends_on    = [aws_securityhub_account.main]
}

# ─── Enable AWS Foundational Best Practices ──────────────
resource "aws_securityhub_standards_subscription" "aws_best" {
  provider      = aws.security
  standards_arn = "arn:aws:securityhub:us-east-1::standards/aws-foundational-security-best-practices/v/1.0.0"
  depends_on    = [aws_securityhub_account.main]
}

# ─── Organization Admin ─────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_securityhub_organization_admin_account" "security" {
  admin_account_id = var.security_account_id
}
💡 Multi-Account Best Practices: ✅ Never run workloads in the management account, ✅ Use SCPs as guardrails (not permissions), ✅ Centralize logging in a dedicated account, ✅ Use Transit Gateway instead of VPC peering at scale, ✅ Delegate security services to a dedicated security account, ✅ Apply baseline configuration to every new account automatically.
🐍 🐳 🗄️ 🚀
CHAPTER 60

Project 6 - Django Application Deployment

ECS Fargate RDS PostgreSQL S3 Static Files CloudFront GitHub Actions ECR
6Sections
11Subsections
18+Code Examples
🐍Django
PART 16 ADVANCED ★★★★★

Deploy a production-ready Django application on AWS using ECS Fargate containers, RDS PostgreSQL, S3 for static/media files, CloudFront CDN, and a complete CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions for Docker build, ECR push, and ECS deployment.

Learning Objectives

  • Choose the right compute platform for Django (ECS vs EC2 vs Lambda)
  • Deploy Django on ECS Fargate with task definitions and services
  • Provision RDS PostgreSQL with secrets management
  • Configure S3 for Django static and media file storage
  • Set up CloudFront CDN for static asset delivery
  • Build a full CI/CD pipeline with GitHub Actions

Chapter Contents

60.1 Architecture for Django 60.2 ECS Fargate Deployment 60.3 RDS PostgreSQL 60.4 S3 for Static Files 60.5 CloudFront CDN 60.6 CI/CD with GitHub Actions

60.1 Architecture for Django

ARCHITECTURE
📚 Official Documentation: ECS Best Practices Guide

Choosing the right compute platform for Django depends on your traffic patterns, team expertise, and operational requirements.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  DJANGO ON AWS — ARCHITECTURE                               │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                             │
│   Users ──→ CloudFront ──→ ALB ──→ ECS Fargate                              │
│              (static)         (gunicorn + Django)                            │
│                                    │         │                              │
│                            ┌───────┘         └───────┐                      │
│                            ▼                         ▼                      │
│                    RDS PostgreSQL              S3 (media)                    │
│                    (Multi-AZ)                                                │
│                            │                                                │
│                     Secrets Manager                                          │
│                     (DB credentials)                                         │
│                                                                             │
│   CI/CD: GitHub Actions → Docker Build → ECR → ECS Deploy                   │
│                                                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💻 Practical Examples

60.1.1 ECS vs EC2 vs Lambda

Compare compute platforms for Django deployments:

Feature ECS Fargate EC2 (ASG) Lambda (Mangum)
Server Management None (serverless) Full (patching, AMIs) None (serverless)
Scaling Task-level auto scaling Instance-level ASG Automatic per-request
Cold Starts Minimal (~seconds) None Significant (~5-10s)
Cost Model Per vCPU/memory/second Per instance hour Per request + duration
Best For Most Django apps ✅ Heavy compute, GPU Low-traffic APIs
60.1.2 Database Options

RDS PostgreSQL is the recommended database for Django due to native Django support, rich feature set, and managed operations.

Example: Infrastructure Variables
# ─── Project Variables ───────────────────────────────────
variable "project" {
  description = "Project name"
  type        = string
  default     = "django-app"
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment"
  type        = string
  default     = "production"
}

variable "domain_name" {
  description = "Primary domain name"
  type        = string
  default     = "myapp.example.com"
}

variable "db_instance_class" {
  description = "RDS instance class"
  type        = string
  default     = "db.t3.medium"
}

variable "container_port" {
  description = "Container port for Gunicorn"
  type        = number
  default     = 8000
}

60.2 ECS Fargate Deployment

RESOURCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: aws_ecs_service | aws_ecs_task_definition

ECS Fargate runs Django in containers without managing servers — define the task (CPU, memory, container image) and the service (desired count, load balancer, health checks).

💻 Practical Examples

60.2.1 Task Definition

The task definition specifies the Docker image, resource limits, environment variables, and log configuration for the Django container.

Example: Django ECS Task Definition
# ─── ECS Cluster ─────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_ecs_cluster" "main" {
  name = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"

  setting {
    name  = "containerInsights"
    value = "enabled"
  }
}

# ─── Task Definition ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_ecs_task_definition" "django" {
  family                   = "${var.project}-django"
  network_mode             = "awsvpc"
  requires_compatibilities = ["FARGATE"]
  cpu                      = 512
  memory                   = 1024
  execution_role_arn       = aws_iam_role.ecs_execution.arn
  task_role_arn            = aws_iam_role.ecs_task.arn

  container_definitions = jsonencode([{
    name  = "django"
    image = "${aws_ecr_repository.django.repository_url}:latest"

    portMappings = [{
      containerPort = var.container_port
      protocol      = "tcp"
    }]

    environment = [
      { name = "DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE", value = "config.settings.production" },
      { name = "ALLOWED_HOSTS", value = var.domain_name },
      { name = "AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME", value = aws_s3_bucket.static.id },
    ]

    secrets = [
      { name = "DATABASE_URL", valueFrom = aws_secretsmanager_secret.db_url.arn },
      { name = "SECRET_KEY", valueFrom = aws_secretsmanager_secret.django_secret.arn },
    ]

    logConfiguration = {
      logDriver = "awslogs"
      options = {
        "awslogs-group"         = aws_cloudwatch_log_group.django.name
        "awslogs-region"        = data.aws_region.current.name
        "awslogs-stream-prefix" = "django"
      }
    }

    healthCheck = {
      command     = ["CMD-SHELL", "python manage.py check --deploy || exit 1"]
      interval    = 30
      timeout     = 5
      retries     = 3
      startPeriod = 60
    }
  }])
}
60.2.2 Container Configuration

The Dockerfile runs Django with Gunicorn as the production WSGI server.

Example: Production Dockerfile
# ─── Multi-Stage Django Dockerfile ───────────────────────
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY . .
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "config.wsgi:application", \
     "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", \
     "--workers", "4", \
     "--threads", "2", \
     "--timeout", "120"]
60.2.3 Service Definition

The ECS service manages task lifecycle, load balancer integration, and rolling deployments.

Example: ECS Service with ALB
# ─── ECS Service ─────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_ecs_service" "django" {
  name            = "${var.project}-django"
  cluster         = aws_ecs_cluster.main.id
  task_definition = aws_ecs_task_definition.django.arn
  desired_count   = 2
  launch_type     = "FARGATE"

  network_configuration {
    subnets          = aws_subnet.private[*].id
    security_groups  = [aws_security_group.ecs.id]
    assign_public_ip = false
  }

  load_balancer {
    target_group_arn = aws_lb_target_group.django.arn
    container_name   = "django"
    container_port   = var.container_port
  }

  deployment_configuration {
    maximum_percent         = 200
    minimum_healthy_percent = 100
  }

  deployment_circuit_breaker {
    enable   = true
    rollback = true
  }

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [task_definition]
  }
}

# ─── Auto Scaling ────────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_appautoscaling_target" "django" {
  max_capacity       = 10
  min_capacity       = 2
  resource_id        = "service/${aws_ecs_cluster.main.name}/${aws_ecs_service.django.name}"
  scalable_dimension = "ecs:service:DesiredCount"
  service_namespace  = "ecs"
}

resource "aws_appautoscaling_policy" "django_cpu" {
  name               = "${var.project}-cpu-scaling"
  policy_type        = "TargetTrackingScaling"
  resource_id        = aws_appautoscaling_target.django.resource_id
  scalable_dimension = aws_appautoscaling_target.django.scalable_dimension
  service_namespace  = aws_appautoscaling_target.django.service_namespace

  target_tracking_scaling_policy_configuration {
    predefined_metric_specification {
      predefined_metric_type = "ECSServiceAverageCPUUtilization"
    }
    target_value = 60.0
  }
}

60.3 RDS PostgreSQL

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_db_instance | aws_secretsmanager_secret

RDS PostgreSQL provides a managed database optimized for Django's ORM, with automated backups, Multi-AZ failover, and integrated secrets management.

💻 Practical Examples

60.3.1 Database Setup

Configure RDS with a private subnet group, encryption, and automated backups.

Example: RDS PostgreSQL for Django
# ─── RDS Subnet Group ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_subnet_group" "main" {
  name       = "${var.project}-db"
  subnet_ids = aws_subnet.database[*].id
}

# ─── RDS PostgreSQL ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_instance" "postgres" {
  identifier     = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "16.3"
  instance_class = var.db_instance_class

  allocated_storage     = 50
  max_allocated_storage = 200
  storage_type          = "gp3"
  storage_encrypted     = true

  db_name  = "django"
  username = "django_admin"
  password = random_password.db.result

  multi_az               = true
  db_subnet_group_name   = aws_db_subnet_group.main.name
  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.rds.id]

  backup_retention_period = 14
  backup_window           = "03:00-04:00"
  maintenance_window      = "sun:04:00-sun:05:00"

  deletion_protection       = true
  skip_final_snapshot       = false
  final_snapshot_identifier = "${var.project}-final-${formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD", timestamp())}"

  performance_insights_enabled = true
}

resource "random_password" "db" {
  length  = 32
  special = false
}
60.3.2 Secrets Management

Store database credentials in Secrets Manager and inject them into ECS tasks as environment variables.

Example: Database URL Secret
# ─── Database URL Secret ─────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret" "db_url" {
  name = "${var.project}/${var.environment}/database-url"
}

resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_url" {
  secret_id = aws_secretsmanager_secret.db_url.id
  secret_string = join("", [
    "postgres://",
    aws_db_instance.postgres.username, ":",
    random_password.db.result, "@",
    aws_db_instance.postgres.endpoint, "/",
    aws_db_instance.postgres.db_name
  ])
}

# ─── Django Secret Key ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret" "django_secret" {
  name = "${var.project}/${var.environment}/django-secret-key"
}

resource "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "django_secret" {
  secret_id     = aws_secretsmanager_secret.django_secret.id
  secret_string = random_password.django_key.result
}

resource "random_password" "django_key" {
  length  = 50
  special = true
}

60.4 S3 for Static Files

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_s3_bucket

Django's collectstatic command copies static assets (CSS, JS, images) to S3 via django-storages, while media uploads go to a separate prefix.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: S3 Buckets for Static and Media
# ─── Static/Media Bucket ─────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "static" {
  bucket = "${var.project}-${var.environment}-assets"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_cors_configuration" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id

  cors_rule {
    allowed_headers = ["*"]
    allowed_methods = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    allowed_origins = ["https://${var.domain_name}"]
    max_age_seconds = 86400
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "static" {
  bucket                  = aws_s3_bucket.static.id
  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

# ─── OAC Policy for CloudFront ──────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_policy" "static" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.static.id
  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Service = "cloudfront.amazonaws.com"
      }
      Action   = "s3:GetObject"
      Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.static.arn}/*"
      Condition = {
        StringEquals = {
          "AWS:SourceArn" = aws_cloudfront_distribution.static.arn
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}
Example: Django Settings (django-storages)
# settings/production.py
# ─── django-storages S3 Backend ──────────────────────────
STORAGES = {
    "default": {
        "BACKEND": "storages.backends.s3boto3.S3Boto3Storage",
        "OPTIONS": {
            "bucket_name": os.environ["AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME"],
            "location": "media",
        },
    },
    "staticfiles": {
        "BACKEND": "storages.backends.s3boto3.S3StaticStorage",
        "OPTIONS": {
            "bucket_name": os.environ["AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME"],
            "location": "static",
        },
    },
}

STATIC_URL = f"https://{os.environ.get('CDN_DOMAIN', '')}/static/"
MEDIA_URL  = f"https://{os.environ.get('CDN_DOMAIN', '')}/media/"

60.5 CloudFront CDN

RESOURCE
📚 Official Documentation: aws_cloudfront_distribution

CloudFront serves Django static assets from edge locations worldwide, reducing latency and offloading bandwidth from the application servers.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: CloudFront for Django Static Assets
# ─── CloudFront OAC ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control" "static" {
  name                              = "${var.project}-static-oac"
  origin_access_control_origin_type = "s3"
  signing_behavior                  = "always"
  signing_protocol                  = "sigv4"
}

# ─── CloudFront Distribution ────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudfront_distribution" "static" {
  enabled         = true
  is_ipv6_enabled = true
  comment         = "${var.project} static assets"
  aliases         = ["cdn.${var.domain_name}"]

  origin {
    domain_name              = aws_s3_bucket.static.bucket_regional_domain_name
    origin_id                = "s3-static"
    origin_access_control_id = aws_cloudfront_origin_access_control.static.id
  }

  default_cache_behavior {
    allowed_methods        = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    cached_methods         = ["GET", "HEAD"]
    target_origin_id       = "s3-static"
    viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"
    compress               = true

    cache_policy_id = "658327ea-f89d-4fab-a63d-7e88639e58f6"  # CachingOptimized
  }

  restrictions {
    geo_restriction {
      restriction_type = "none"
    }
  }

  viewer_certificate {
    acm_certificate_arn      = aws_acm_certificate.cdn.arn
    ssl_support_method       = "sni-only"
    minimum_protocol_version = "TLSv1.2_2021"
  }
}

60.6 CI/CD with GitHub Actions

CI/CD
📚 Official Documentation: GitHub Actions | aws_ecr_repository

Automate the full deploy cycle: test Django, build Docker image, push to ECR, and deploy to ECS with zero-downtime rolling updates.

💻 Practical Examples

60.6.1 Docker Build

Define the ECR repository in Terraform, which GitHub Actions pushes images to.

Example: ECR Repository
# ─── ECR Repository ──────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_ecr_repository" "django" {
  name                 = "${var.project}-django"
  image_tag_mutability = "IMMUTABLE"

  image_scanning_configuration {
    scan_on_push = true
  }
}

resource "aws_ecr_lifecycle_policy" "django" {
  repository = aws_ecr_repository.django.name

  policy = jsonencode({
    rules = [{
      rulePriority = 1
      description  = "Keep last 10 images"
      selection = {
        tagStatus   = "any"
        countType   = "imageCountMoreThan"
        countNumber = 10
      }
      action = {
        type = "expire"
      }
    }]
  })
}
60.6.2 ECR Push

Authenticate to ECR, build the Docker image, tag with the Git SHA, and push.

60.6.3 ECS Deployment

The full GitHub Actions workflow handles build, push, and deploy in a single pipeline.

Example: GitHub Actions Deploy Workflow
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy Django

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

env:
  AWS_REGION: us-east-1
  ECR_REPOSITORY: django-app-django

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: python manage.py test

  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}

      - uses: aws-actions/amazon-ecr-login@v2
        id: ecr

      - name: Build, tag, and push
        env:
          REGISTRY: ${{ steps.ecr.outputs.registry }}
          IMAGE_TAG: ${{ github.sha }}
        run: |
          docker build -t $REGISTRY/$ECR_REPOSITORY:$IMAGE_TAG .
          docker push $REGISTRY/$ECR_REPOSITORY:$IMAGE_TAG

      - name: Deploy to ECS
        run: |
          TASK_DEF=$(aws ecs describe-task-definition \
            --task-definition django-app-django \
            --query taskDefinition)

          NEW_TASK_DEF=$(echo $TASK_DEF | jq \
            --arg IMG "$REGISTRY/$ECR_REPOSITORY:$IMAGE_TAG" \
            '.containerDefinitions[0].image = $IMG |
             del(.taskDefinitionArn, .revision, .status,
                 .requiresAttributes, .compatibilities,
                 .registeredAt, .registeredBy)')

          aws ecs register-task-definition \
            --cli-input-json "$NEW_TASK_DEF"

          aws ecs update-service \
            --cluster django-app-production \
            --service django-app-django \
            --force-new-deployment
💡 Django Deployment Checklist: ✅ Run manage.py check --deploy in health checks, ✅ Set DEBUG=False and configure ALLOWED_HOSTS, ✅ Use django-storages for S3 backends, ✅ Store secrets in Secrets Manager (never in env files), ✅ Enable container insights for ECS monitoring, ✅ Use immutable ECR image tags for rollback capability, ✅ Run migrations as a separate ECS task (not at container startup).
⚙️ 🔄 🏗️ 📊
CHAPTER 61

Project 7 - Complete CI/CD Infrastructure

GitHub Actions Multi-Environment State Management Secrets Monitoring Cost Controls
7Sections
3Environments
15+Code Examples
🔄Pipeline
PART 16 EXPERT ★★★★★

Build a complete, production-grade CI/CD infrastructure with Terraform: Git repository structure, GitHub Actions workflows, multi-environment promotion (dev → staging → production), remote state management, secrets handling, monitoring dashboards, and cost control guardrails.

Learning Objectives

  • Structure Terraform repositories for team collaboration
  • Build GitHub Actions workflows for plan, apply, and destroy
  • Implement multi-environment promotion pipelines
  • Manage remote state with locking and isolation
  • Handle secrets across environments securely
  • Set up monitoring and alerting for infrastructure
  • Implement cost controls and budget alerts

Chapter Contents

61.1 Source Control Structure 61.2 GitHub Actions Workflows 61.3 Multiple Environments 61.4 State Management Strategy 61.5 Secrets Management 61.6 Monitoring and Alerting 61.7 Cost Controls

61.1 Source Control Structure

BEST PRACTICE
📚 Official Documentation: Standard Module Structure

A well-organized repository structure separates environments, modules, and configuration to enable safe, reviewable infrastructure changes.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Production Repository Layout
# ─── Recommended Repository Structure ────────────────────
terraform-infrastructure/
├── .github/
│   └── workflows/
│       ├── terraform-plan.yml      # PR plan workflow
│       ├── terraform-apply.yml     # Merge apply workflow
│       └── terraform-destroy.yml   # Manual destroy workflow
├── modules/
│   ├── networking/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── outputs.tf
│   │   └── README.md
│   ├── compute/
│   ├── database/
│   └── monitoring/
├── environments/
│   ├── dev/
│   │   ├── main.tf               # Module calls
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars      # Dev values
│   │   ├── backend.tf            # Dev state config
│   │   └── providers.tf
│   ├── staging/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars
│   │   └── backend.tf
│   └── production/
│       ├── main.tf
│       ├── terraform.tfvars
│       └── backend.tf
├── scripts/
│   ├── bootstrap.sh              # Initial setup
│   └── cleanup.sh
├── .gitignore
├── .terraform-version
└── README.md
Example: Environment Main Configuration
# environments/production/main.tf
# ─── Root Module for Production ──────────────────────────

module "networking" {
  source = "../../modules/networking"

  environment = "production"
  vpc_cidr    = "10.0.0.0/16"
  azs         = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
}

module "compute" {
  source = "../../modules/compute"

  environment   = "production"
  vpc_id        = module.networking.vpc_id
  subnet_ids    = module.networking.private_subnet_ids
  instance_type = "t3.large"
  min_size      = 3
  max_size      = 10
}

module "database" {
  source = "../../modules/database"

  environment    = "production"
  vpc_id         = module.networking.vpc_id
  subnet_ids     = module.networking.database_subnet_ids
  instance_class = "db.r6g.xlarge"
  multi_az       = true
}

module "monitoring" {
  source = "../../modules/monitoring"

  environment   = "production"
  cluster_name  = module.compute.cluster_name
  db_identifier = module.database.db_identifier
  alert_email   = "ops-team@example.com"
}

61.2 GitHub Actions Workflows

CI/CD
📚 Official Documentation: hashicorp/setup-terraform Action

GitHub Actions workflows automate the Terraform lifecycle: plan on PRs for review, apply on merge to main, and destroy via manual dispatch.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Terraform Plan Workflow (PR)
# .github/workflows/terraform-plan.yml
name: Terraform Plan

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["environments/**", "modules/**"]

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  plan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        environment: [dev, staging, production]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "1.9.0"

      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Terraform Init
        working-directory: environments/${{ matrix.environment }}
        run: terraform init

      - name: Terraform Validate
        working-directory: environments/${{ matrix.environment }}
        run: terraform validate

      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        working-directory: environments/${{ matrix.environment }}
        run: terraform plan -no-color -out=tfplan
        continue-on-error: true

      - name: Comment Plan on PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const output = `#### 🏗️ ${{ matrix.environment }}
            \`\`\`
            ${{ steps.plan.outputs.stdout }}
            \`\`\`
            `;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: output
            })
Example: Terraform Apply Workflow (Merge)
# .github/workflows/terraform-apply.yml
name: Terraform Apply

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ["environments/**", "modules/**"]

concurrency:
  group: terraform-apply
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  apply-dev:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: development
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - run: terraform init
        working-directory: environments/dev
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve
        working-directory: environments/dev

  apply-staging:
    needs: apply-dev
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - run: terraform init
        working-directory: environments/staging
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve
        working-directory: environments/staging

  apply-production:
    needs: apply-staging
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production    # Requires manual approval
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1
      - run: terraform init
        working-directory: environments/production
      - run: terraform apply -auto-approve
        working-directory: environments/production

61.3 Multiple Environments

PATTERN 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Workspaces

Each environment uses the same modules with different variable values, ensuring consistency while allowing environment-specific sizing and configuration.

💻 Practical Examples

61.3.1 Development

Minimal resources, single-AZ, smallest instance types to minimize cost during active development.

Example: Development tfvars
# environments/dev/terraform.tfvars
# ─── Development Environment ────────────────────────────
environment    = "dev"
vpc_cidr       = "10.10.0.0/16"
instance_type  = "t3.small"
min_size       = 1
max_size       = 2

# Database
db_instance_class = "db.t3.micro"
db_multi_az       = false
db_backup_days    = 1

# Monitoring
enable_detailed_monitoring = false
alarm_actions_enabled      = false
61.3.2 Staging

Mirrors production architecture at reduced scale for pre-production validation and testing.

Example: Staging tfvars
# environments/staging/terraform.tfvars
# ─── Staging Environment ─────────────────────────────────
environment    = "staging"
vpc_cidr       = "10.20.0.0/16"
instance_type  = "t3.medium"
min_size       = 2
max_size       = 4

# Database
db_instance_class = "db.t3.medium"
db_multi_az       = true
db_backup_days    = 7

# Monitoring
enable_detailed_monitoring = true
alarm_actions_enabled      = true
61.3.3 Production

Full-scale resources, multi-AZ, enhanced monitoring, and all security measures enabled.

Example: Production tfvars
# environments/production/terraform.tfvars
# ─── Production Environment ─────────────────────────────
environment    = "production"
vpc_cidr       = "10.0.0.0/16"
instance_type  = "t3.large"
min_size       = 3
max_size       = 10

# Database
db_instance_class = "db.r6g.xlarge"
db_multi_az       = true
db_backup_days    = 35

# Monitoring
enable_detailed_monitoring = true
alarm_actions_enabled      = true

61.4 State Management Strategy

BACKEND
📚 Official Documentation: S3 Backend

Isolate state per environment with separate S3 keys, DynamoDB locking, and encryption to prevent conflicts and data loss.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Per-Environment Backend Configuration
# environments/production/backend.tf
# ─── Remote State Configuration ──────────────────────────
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "mycompany-terraform-state"
    key            = "production/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    encrypt        = true
    kms_key_id     = "alias/terraform-state"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
  }
}

# ─── State Bucket (bootstrap once) ──────────────────────
# Run this separately to create the state infrastructure
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "state" {
  bucket = "mycompany-terraform-state"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.state.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "locks" {
  name         = "terraform-locks"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "LockID"

  attribute {
    name = "LockID"
    type = "S"
  }
}

61.5 Secrets Management

SECURITY
📚 Official Documentation: Sensitive Variables

Manage secrets across environments without exposing them in code, state, or logs using a combination of GitHub secrets, AWS Secrets Manager, and Terraform sensitive variables.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Secrets Strategy
# ─── Sensitive Variables ─────────────────────────────────
variable "db_password" {
  description = "Database password"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true
}

# ─── Read Existing Secret from AWS ───────────────────────
data "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db" {
  secret_id = "${var.project}/${var.environment}/database"
}

locals {
  db_credentials = jsondecode(data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db.secret_string)
}

# ─── Use in Resources ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  username = local.db_credentials.username
  password = local.db_credentials.password
  # ... other config
}

# ─── OIDC for GitHub Actions (no static keys) ───────────
resource "aws_iam_openid_connect_provider" "github" {
  url             = "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com"
  client_id_list  = ["sts.amazonaws.com"]
  thumbprint_list = ["6938fd4d98bab03faadb97b34396831e3780aea1"]
}

resource "aws_iam_role" "github_actions" {
  name = "github-actions-terraform"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect = "Allow"
      Principal = {
        Federated = aws_iam_openid_connect_provider.github.arn
      }
      Action = "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity"
      Condition = {
        StringLike = {
          "token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub" = "repo:myorg/terraform-infra:*"
        }
      }
    }]
  })
}

61.6 Monitoring and Alerting

MONITORING
📚 Official Documentation: aws_cloudwatch_dashboard

Deploy CloudWatch dashboards and alarms as code to monitor infrastructure health, performance metrics, and operational events across all environments.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Infrastructure Dashboard
# ─── CloudWatch Dashboard ────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_dashboard" "infrastructure" {
  dashboard_name = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"

  dashboard_body = jsonencode({
    widgets = [
      {
        type = "metric"
        properties = {
          title  = "ECS CPU Utilization"
          region = data.aws_region.current.name
          metrics = [
            ["AWS/ECS", "CPUUtilization",
             "ClusterName", aws_ecs_cluster.main.name,
             { stat = "Average" }]
          ]
          period = 300
          view   = "timeSeries"
        }
        x = 0, y = 0, width = 12, height = 6
      },
      {
        type = "metric"
        properties = {
          title  = "RDS Connections"
          region = data.aws_region.current.name
          metrics = [
            ["AWS/RDS", "DatabaseConnections",
             "DBInstanceIdentifier", module.database.db_identifier,
             { stat = "Average" }]
          ]
          period = 300
          view   = "timeSeries"
        }
        x = 12, y = 0, width = 12, height = 6
      },
      {
        type = "metric"
        properties = {
          title  = "ALB Request Count"
          region = data.aws_region.current.name
          metrics = [
            ["AWS/ApplicationELB", "RequestCount",
             "LoadBalancer", module.compute.alb_arn_suffix,
             { stat = "Sum" }]
          ]
          period = 60
          view   = "timeSeries"
        }
        x = 0, y = 6, width = 24, height = 6
      }
    ]
  })
}

# ─── Critical Alerts ─────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "high_error_rate" {
  alarm_name          = "${var.project}-${var.environment}-high-5xx"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 2
  metric_name         = "HTTPCode_Target_5XX_Count"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApplicationELB"
  period              = 300
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 50
  alarm_description   = "High 5xx error rate detected"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]

  dimensions = {
    LoadBalancer = module.compute.alb_arn_suffix
  }
}

61.7 Cost Controls

FINOPS
📚 Official Documentation: aws_budgets_budget

Implement budget alerts, tagging policies, and auto-shutdown schedules to keep infrastructure costs predictable and prevent billing surprises.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Budget Alerts and Tagging
# ─── Monthly Budget Alert ────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_budgets_budget" "monthly" {
  name         = "${var.project}-${var.environment}-monthly"
  budget_type  = "COST"
  limit_amount = var.monthly_budget
  limit_unit   = "USD"
  time_unit    = "MONTHLY"

  cost_filter {
    name   = "TagKeyValue"
