    values = ["user:Project$${var.project}"]
  }

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 80
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "ACTUAL"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.alert_email]
  }

  notification {
    comparison_operator        = "GREATER_THAN"
    threshold                  = 100
    threshold_type             = "PERCENTAGE"
    notification_type          = "FORECASTED"
    subscriber_email_addresses = [var.alert_email]
  }
}

# ─── Default Tags for Cost Allocation ────────────────────
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = var.project
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      CostCenter  = var.cost_center
    }
  }
}

# ─── Auto-Shutdown for Dev (Lambda + EventBridge) ────────
resource "aws_scheduler_schedule" "dev_shutdown" {
  count = var.environment == "dev" ? 1 : 0

  name       = "${var.project}-dev-shutdown"
  group_name = "default"

  flexible_time_window {
    mode = "OFF"
  }

  schedule_expression = "cron(0 19 ? * MON-FRI *)"

  target {
    arn      = aws_lambda_function.shutdown[0].arn
    role_arn = aws_iam_role.scheduler[0].arn
  }
}
💡 CI/CD Infrastructure Checklist: ✅ Use OIDC federation (no static AWS keys in GitHub), ✅ Require PR review before merge to main, ✅ Run terraform plan on every PR, ✅ Use GitHub Environment protection rules for production, ✅ Isolate state files per environment, ✅ Enable state file versioning for rollback, ✅ Tag all resources for cost allocation, ✅ Set budget alerts at 80% and 100%.
🛡️ 🌎 🔄 ⚡
CHAPTER 62

Project 8 - Disaster Recovery Setup

Pilot Light Warm Standby Active-Active Cross-Region Route 53 Failover State DR
4Sections
8Subsections
12+Code Examples
🛡️Resilience
PART 16 EXPERT ★★★★★

Design and implement multi-region disaster recovery infrastructure with Terraform: choose between Pilot Light, Warm Standby, and Active-Active architectures, configure cross-region replication for S3, RDS, and DynamoDB, automate DNS failover with Route 53 health checks, and protect Terraform state itself against regional outages.

Learning Objectives

  • Compare Pilot Light, Warm Standby, and Active-Active DR strategies
  • Calculate RTO and RPO for each DR architecture
  • Configure cross-region S3 replication with encryption
  • Deploy RDS read replicas and promote on failover
  • Set up DynamoDB Global Tables for multi-region writes
  • Implement Route 53 health checks and failover routing
  • Protect Terraform state with cross-region replication

Chapter Contents

62.1 DR Architecture 62.2 Cross-Region Replication 62.3 Route 53 Failover 62.4 Terraform State for DR

62.1 DR Architecture

ARCHITECTURE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: AWS DR Whitepaper

AWS defines four DR strategies with increasing cost, complexity, and recovery speed. Each can be fully automated with Terraform to ensure consistent, repeatable disaster recovery infrastructure.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              DISASTER RECOVERY STRATEGIES COMPARISON                      │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                           │
│  Cost ▲                                                                   │
│       │                                              ┌───────────────┐    │
│       │                                              │ ACTIVE-ACTIVE │    │
│       │                                              │  RTO: ~0      │    │
│       │                                              │  RPO: ~0      │    │
│       │                         ┌───────────────┐    └───────────────┘    │
│       │                         │ WARM STANDBY  │                         │
│       │                         │  RTO: Minutes │                         │
│       │                         │  RPO: Seconds │                         │
│       │    ┌───────────────┐    └───────────────┘                         │
│       │    │  PILOT LIGHT  │                                              │
│       │    │  RTO: 10 min  │                                              │
│       │    │  RPO: Minutes │                                              │
│  ┌────┤    └───────────────┘                                              │
│  │BACK│                                                                   │
│  │ UP │ RTO: Hours                                                        │
│  └────┘ RPO: Hours                                                        │
│  ─────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────────► Recovery Speed  │
│       │                                                                   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💻 Practical Examples

62.1.1 Pilot Light

Pilot Light keeps a minimal version of your environment always running in the DR region — typically just the database with continuous replication. Compute and other resources are provisioned only during failover.

Example: Pilot Light Architecture
# ─── Pilot Light DR Configuration ────────────────────────
# Primary region provider
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  alias  = "primary"
}

# DR region provider
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-west-2"
  alias  = "dr"
}

# ─── Primary Database ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_instance" "primary" {
  provider = aws.primary

  identifier     = "${var.project}-primary"
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "15.4"
  instance_class = "db.r6g.xlarge"
  multi_az       = true

  backup_retention_period = 35
  backup_window           = "03:00-04:00"
  
  # Enable automated backups for cross-region replication
  storage_encrypted = true
  kms_key_id        = aws_kms_key.primary_db.arn

  tags = { Environment = "production", Role = "primary" }
}

# ─── DR Read Replica (always running = Pilot Light) ─────
resource "aws_db_instance" "dr_replica" {
  provider = aws.dr

  identifier          = "${var.project}-dr-replica"
  replicate_source_db = aws_db_instance.primary.arn
  instance_class      = "db.r6g.large"    # Smaller in DR
  
  kms_key_id        = aws_kms_key.dr_db.arn
  storage_encrypted = true

  tags = { Environment = "dr", Role = "replica" }
}

# ─── DR Compute (only created during failover) ──────────
resource "aws_launch_template" "dr_app" {
  provider = aws.dr
  count    = var.activate_dr ? 1 : 0

  name_prefix   = "${var.project}-dr-"
  image_id      = data.aws_ami.app_dr.id
  instance_type = "t3.large"

  tag_specifications {
    resource_type = "instance"
    tags          = { Name = "${var.project}-dr-app" }
  }
}

resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "dr_app" {
  provider = aws.dr
  count    = var.activate_dr ? 1 : 0

  desired_capacity = 2
  min_size         = 2
  max_size         = 6
  
  launch_template {
    id      = aws_launch_template.dr_app[0].id
    version = "$Latest"
  }

  vpc_zone_identifier = module.dr_networking[0].private_subnet_ids
}

# ─── DR Toggle Variable ─────────────────────────────────
variable "activate_dr" {
  description = "Set to true to activate DR compute resources"
  type        = bool
  default     = false
}
62.1.2 Warm Standby

Warm Standby runs a scaled-down but fully functional copy of the production environment in the DR region. On failover, you scale up the DR resources to handle production traffic.

Example: Warm Standby Configuration
# ─── Warm Standby: Reduced-Scale DR Environment ─────────
module "dr_networking" {
  source = "../../modules/networking"
  providers = { aws = aws.dr }

  environment = "dr"
  vpc_cidr    = "10.100.0.0/16"
  azs         = ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b"]
}

module "dr_compute" {
  source = "../../modules/compute"
  providers = { aws = aws.dr }

  environment   = "dr"
  vpc_id        = module.dr_networking.vpc_id
  subnet_ids    = module.dr_networking.private_subnet_ids
  instance_type = "t3.medium"        # Smaller than prod
  min_size      = 1                  # Minimal capacity
  max_size      = var.activate_dr ? 10 : 2
  desired       = var.activate_dr ? 3  : 1
}

# ─── Scale-Up Policy (triggered during failover) ────────
resource "aws_autoscaling_policy" "dr_scale_up" {
  provider               = aws.dr
  name                   = "${var.project}-dr-scale-up"
  autoscaling_group_name = module.dr_compute.asg_name
  policy_type            = "StepScaling"

  step_adjustment {
    scaling_adjustment          = 4
    metric_interval_lower_bound = 0
  }
}

# ─── Warm Standby Comparison Table ───────────────────────
# Primary:   3x t3.large  + db.r6g.xlarge (Multi-AZ)
# DR Warm:   1x t3.medium + db.r6g.large  (Read Replica)
# Failover:  Scale DR ASG to 3+, promote RDS replica
62.1.3 Multi-Site Active-Active

Active-Active serves traffic from both regions simultaneously. Both databases accept writes (using DynamoDB Global Tables or Aurora Global Database) and Route 53 distributes traffic with latency-based routing.

Example: Active-Active with Aurora Global
# ─── Aurora Global Database (Active-Active) ─────────────
resource "aws_rds_global_cluster" "main" {
  global_cluster_identifier = "${var.project}-global"
  engine                    = "aurora-postgresql"
  engine_version            = "15.4"
  database_name             = var.db_name
  storage_encrypted         = true
}

# Primary cluster (us-east-1)
resource "aws_rds_cluster" "primary" {
  provider = aws.primary

  cluster_identifier        = "${var.project}-primary"
  global_cluster_identifier = aws_rds_global_cluster.main.id
  engine                    = aws_rds_global_cluster.main.engine
  engine_version            = aws_rds_global_cluster.main.engine_version
  master_username           = var.db_username
  master_password           = var.db_password
  db_subnet_group_name      = aws_db_subnet_group.primary.name
  vpc_security_group_ids    = [aws_security_group.primary_db.id]
}

resource "aws_rds_cluster_instance" "primary" {
  provider = aws.primary
  count    = 2

  identifier         = "${var.project}-primary-${count.index}"
  cluster_identifier = aws_rds_cluster.primary.id
  instance_class     = "db.r6g.xlarge"
  engine             = aws_rds_cluster.primary.engine
}

# Secondary cluster (us-west-2) — auto-replicates from primary
resource "aws_rds_cluster" "secondary" {
  provider   = aws.dr
  depends_on = [aws_rds_cluster_instance.primary]

  cluster_identifier        = "${var.project}-secondary"
  global_cluster_identifier = aws_rds_global_cluster.main.id
  engine                    = aws_rds_global_cluster.main.engine
  engine_version            = aws_rds_global_cluster.main.engine_version
  db_subnet_group_name      = aws_db_subnet_group.dr.name
  vpc_security_group_ids    = [aws_security_group.dr_db.id]
}

resource "aws_rds_cluster_instance" "secondary" {
  provider = aws.dr
  count    = 2

  identifier         = "${var.project}-secondary-${count.index}"
  cluster_identifier = aws_rds_cluster.secondary.id
  instance_class     = "db.r6g.xlarge"
  engine             = aws_rds_cluster.secondary.engine
}
Strategy RTO RPO Cost (% of prod) Complexity Best For
Backup & Restore Hours Hours ~5% Low Non-critical workloads
Pilot Light 10-30 min Minutes ~10-15% Medium Core business apps
Warm Standby Minutes Seconds ~30-50% High Business-critical apps
Active-Active ~0 ~0 ~100% Very High Mission-critical / SLA 99.99%+

62.2 Cross-Region Replication

DATA
📚 Official Documentation: S3 Replication | RDS Read Replicas | DynamoDB Global Tables

Cross-region replication ensures data durability and availability across AWS regions. Each AWS service has its own replication mechanism that Terraform can configure declaratively.

💻 Practical Examples

62.2.1 S3 Replication

S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) automatically copies objects from a source bucket to a destination bucket in another region, providing data redundancy with configurable replication rules.

Example: S3 Cross-Region Replication
# ─── Source Bucket (Primary Region) ──────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "primary" {
  provider = aws.primary
  bucket   = "${var.project}-primary-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "primary" {
  provider = aws.primary
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.primary.id
  versioning_configuration { status = "Enabled" }
}

# ─── Destination Bucket (DR Region) ─────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "dr" {
  provider = aws.dr
  bucket   = "${var.project}-dr-${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "dr" {
  provider = aws.dr
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.dr.id
  versioning_configuration { status = "Enabled" }
}

# ─── Replication IAM Role ────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_iam_role" "replication" {
  name = "${var.project}-s3-replication"

  assume_role_policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [{
      Effect    = "Allow"
      Principal = { Service = "s3.amazonaws.com" }
      Action    = "sts:AssumeRole"
    }]
  })
}

resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "replication" {
  name = "${var.project}-s3-replication"
  role = aws_iam_role.replication.id

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "s3:GetReplicationConfiguration",
          "s3:ListBucket"
        ]
        Resource = aws_s3_bucket.primary.arn
      },
      {
        Effect   = "Allow"
        Action   = ["s3:GetObjectVersionForReplication", "s3:GetObjectVersionAcl"]
        Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.primary.arn}/*"
      },
      {
        Effect   = "Allow"
        Action   = ["s3:ReplicateObject", "s3:ReplicateDelete"]
        Resource = "${aws_s3_bucket.dr.arn}/*"
      }
    ]
  })
}

# ─── Replication Configuration ───────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_replication_configuration" "primary_to_dr" {
  provider   = aws.primary
  depends_on = [aws_s3_bucket_versioning.primary]

  role   = aws_iam_role.replication.arn
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.primary.id

  rule {
    id     = "replicate-all"
    status = "Enabled"

    destination {
      bucket        = aws_s3_bucket.dr.arn
      storage_class = "STANDARD_IA"

      encryption_configuration {
        replica_kms_key_id = aws_kms_key.dr_s3.arn
      }
    }

    source_selection_criteria {
      sse_kms_encrypted_objects {
        status = "Enabled"
      }
    }
  }
}
62.2.2 RDS Read Replicas

Cross-region RDS read replicas provide asynchronous replication. During a disaster, the replica can be promoted to a standalone primary database.

Example: Cross-Region RDS Read Replica
# ─── Cross-Region Read Replica ───────────────────────────
resource "aws_db_instance" "cross_region_replica" {
  provider = aws.dr

  identifier          = "${var.project}-dr-replica"
  replicate_source_db = aws_db_instance.primary.arn

  instance_class    = "db.r6g.large"
  storage_encrypted = true
  kms_key_id        = aws_kms_key.dr_db.arn

  # DR-specific settings
  auto_minor_version_upgrade = true
  publicly_accessible        = false
  db_subnet_group_name       = aws_db_subnet_group.dr.name
  vpc_security_group_ids     = [aws_security_group.dr_db.id]

  # Monitoring
  monitoring_interval = 60
  monitoring_role_arn = aws_iam_role.rds_monitoring_dr.arn

  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project}-dr-replica"
    Environment = "dr"
    Role        = "read-replica"
  }
}

# ─── Promotion Script (run during failover) ──────────────
# aws rds promote-read-replica \
#   --db-instance-identifier "${var.project}-dr-replica" \
#   --region us-west-2
#
# Or use Terraform:
# 1. Remove replicate_source_db from config
# 2. Run terraform apply (promotes automatically)
# 3. Update application connection strings
62.2.3 DynamoDB Global Tables

DynamoDB Global Tables provide fully managed, multi-region, multi-active replication with sub-second replication latency and automatic conflict resolution.

Example: DynamoDB Global Table
# ─── DynamoDB Global Table (Active-Active) ───────────────
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "global" {
  provider = aws.primary

  name         = "${var.project}-sessions"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "SessionId"
  range_key    = "CreatedAt"

  attribute {
    name = "SessionId"
    type = "S"
  }

  attribute {
    name = "CreatedAt"
    type = "N"
  }

  attribute {
    name = "UserId"
    type = "S"
  }

  global_secondary_index {
    name            = "UserIndex"
    hash_key        = "UserId"
    range_key       = "CreatedAt"
    projection_type = "ALL"
  }

  # ─── Enable Global Table Replication ───────────────────
  replica {
    region_name = "us-west-2"
    kms_key_arn = aws_kms_key.dr_dynamodb.arn

    point_in_time_recovery {
      enabled = true
    }
  }

  # Primary region settings
  server_side_encryption {
    enabled     = true
    kms_key_arn = aws_kms_key.primary_dynamodb.arn
  }

  point_in_time_recovery {
    enabled = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name        = "${var.project}-sessions"
    Replication = "global-table-v2"
  }
}

62.3 Route 53 Failover

DNS
📚 Official Documentation: Route 53 Health Checks | Route 53 Records

Route 53 failover routing automatically directs traffic from a failed primary endpoint to a healthy DR endpoint using health checks, providing automated DNS-level disaster recovery with no manual intervention required.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ROUTE 53 FAILOVER ARCHITECTURE                         │
├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                           │
│                        ┌──────────────┐                                   │
│                        │   Route 53   │                                   │
│            ┌───────────┤  Failover    ├───────────┐                       │
│            │           │  Policy      │           │                       │
│            │           └──────┬───────┘           │                       │
│            │                  │                   │                       │
│    ┌───────▼────────┐  ┌─────▼──────┐  ┌────────▼────────┐              │
│    │  Health Check  │  │  PRIMARY   │  │   SECONDARY     │              │
│    │  (us-east-1)   │  │  Record    │  │   Record        │              │
│    │  /health → 200 │  │  (active)  │  │   (standby)     │              │
│    └───────┬────────┘  └─────┬──────┘  └────────┬────────┘              │
│            │                 │                   │                       │
│    ┌───────▼─────────────────▼──────┐  ┌────────▼────────┐              │
│    │          PRIMARY REGION         │  │    DR REGION    │              │
│    │         us-east-1               │  │   us-west-2    │              │
│    │  ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌──────────┐  │  │  ┌─────┐      │              │
│    │  │ ALB │ │ EC2 │ │ RDS (P)  │  │  │  │ ALB │      │              │
│    │  └─────┘ └─────┘ └──────────┘  │  │  │(DR) │      │              │
│    └────────────────────────────────┘  │  └─────┘      │              │
│                                        └───────────────┘              │
│                                                                           │
│    Normal:    Route 53 → Primary ALB (health check passes)               │
│    Failover:  Route 53 → DR ALB (health check fails on primary)          │
│                                                                           │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💻 Practical Examples

Example: Complete Failover Configuration
# ─── Hosted Zone ─────────────────────────────────────────
data "aws_route53_zone" "main" {
  name         = var.domain_name
  private_zone = false
}

# ─── Health Check for Primary Region ─────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_health_check" "primary" {
  fqdn              = aws_lb.primary.dns_name
  port              = 443
  type              = "HTTPS"
  resource_path     = "/health"
  failure_threshold = 3
  request_interval  = 10

  regions = ["us-east-1", "us-west-2", "eu-west-1"]

  tags = {
    Name = "${var.project}-primary-health"
  }
}

# ─── CloudWatch Alarm for Health Check ───────────────────
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "primary_health" {
  alarm_name          = "${var.project}-primary-unhealthy"
  comparison_operator = "LessThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "HealthCheckStatus"
  namespace           = "AWS/Route53"
  period              = 60
  statistic           = "Minimum"
  threshold           = 1
  alarm_description   = "Primary region is unhealthy"
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.dr_alerts.arn]

  dimensions = {
    HealthCheckId = aws_route53_health_check.primary.id
  }
}

# ─── Primary Failover Record ────────────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_record" "primary" {
  zone_id = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = "app.${var.domain_name}"
  type    = "A"

  failover_routing_policy {
    type = "PRIMARY"
  }

  set_identifier  = "primary"
  health_check_id = aws_route53_health_check.primary.id

  alias {
    name                   = aws_lb.primary.dns_name
    zone_id                = aws_lb.primary.zone_id
    evaluate_target_health = true
  }
}

# ─── Secondary (DR) Failover Record ─────────────────────
resource "aws_route53_record" "secondary" {
  zone_id = data.aws_route53_zone.main.zone_id
  name    = "app.${var.domain_name}"
  type    = "A"

  failover_routing_policy {
    type = "SECONDARY"
  }

  set_identifier = "secondary"

  alias {
    name                   = aws_lb.dr.dns_name
    zone_id                = aws_lb.dr.zone_id
    evaluate_target_health = true
  }
}

# ─── SNS Alert Topic ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_sns_topic" "dr_alerts" {
  name = "${var.project}-dr-alerts"
}

resource "aws_sns_topic_subscription" "dr_email" {
  topic_arn = aws_sns_topic.dr_alerts.arn
  protocol  = "email"
  endpoint  = var.dr_alert_email
}

62.4 Terraform State for DR

STATE
📚 Official Documentation: S3 Backend Configuration

Terraform state files are themselves critical infrastructure. If you lose your state, you lose the ability to manage existing resources. Protecting state with cross-region replication, versioning, and encryption is essential for any DR strategy.

💻 Practical Examples

Example: DR-Protected State Infrastructure
# ─── State Bucket with Cross-Region Replication ─────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "state_primary" {
  provider = aws.primary
  bucket   = "${var.project}-terraform-state-primary"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "state_primary" {
  provider = aws.primary
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.state_primary.id
  versioning_configuration { status = "Enabled" }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "state_primary" {
  provider = aws.primary
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.state_primary.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.state_primary.arn
    }
    bucket_key_enabled = true
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "state_primary" {
  provider = aws.primary
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.state_primary.id

  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}

# ─── DR State Bucket ────────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "state_dr" {
  provider = aws.dr
  bucket   = "${var.project}-terraform-state-dr"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "state_dr" {
  provider = aws.dr
  bucket   = aws_s3_bucket.state_dr.id
  versioning_configuration { status = "Enabled" }
}

# ─── State Replication ───────────────────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_replication_configuration" "state_replication" {
  provider   = aws.primary
  depends_on = [aws_s3_bucket_versioning.state_primary]

  role   = aws_iam_role.state_replication.arn
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.state_primary.id

  rule {
    id     = "replicate-state"
    status = "Enabled"

    destination {
      bucket        = aws_s3_bucket.state_dr.arn
      storage_class = "STANDARD"

      encryption_configuration {
        replica_kms_key_id = aws_kms_key.state_dr.arn
      }
    }

    source_selection_criteria {
      sse_kms_encrypted_objects {
        status = "Enabled"
      }
    }
  }
}

# ─── DynamoDB Lock Table (both regions) ──────────────────
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "locks_primary" {
  provider     = aws.primary
  name         = "${var.project}-terraform-locks"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "LockID"

  attribute {
    name = "LockID"
    type = "S"
  }

  # Replicate lock table to DR region
  replica {
    region_name = "us-west-2"
  }

  tags = { Name = "terraform-locks" }
}
Example: DR Failover Runbook (as Code Comments)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DISASTER RECOVERY FAILOVER RUNBOOK
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#
# STEP 1: Assess the outage
#   - Check AWS Health Dashboard
#   - Verify Route 53 health checks
#   - Confirm primary region is unreachable
#
# STEP 2: Activate DR compute (Pilot Light / Warm Standby)
#   cd environments/dr
#   terraform apply -var="activate_dr=true"
#
# STEP 3: Promote RDS read replica
#   aws rds promote-read-replica \
#     --db-instance-identifier "${PROJECT}-dr-replica" \
#     --region us-west-2
#   # Wait for status=available (~5-10 minutes)
#
# STEP 4: Verify DR application
#   curl -f https://dr-alb.us-west-2.elb.amazonaws.com/health
#
# STEP 5: DNS failover (automatic if health check-based)
#   # Manual override if needed:
#   aws route53 change-resource-record-sets \
#     --hosted-zone-id $ZONE_ID \
#     --change-batch file://failover-records.json
#
# STEP 6: Notify stakeholders
#   # Automated via SNS + CloudWatch alarm
#
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FAILBACK PROCEDURE (after primary recovers)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#
# STEP 1: Verify primary region is healthy
# STEP 2: Re-sync data from DR to primary
# STEP 3: Create new read replica from promoted DR DB
# STEP 4: Switch DNS back to primary
# STEP 5: Scale down DR resources
#   terraform apply -var="activate_dr=false"
# STEP 6: Post-incident review
💡 DR Best Practices: ✅ Test DR failover quarterly with "game days", ✅ Automate everything — manual steps fail under pressure, ✅ Use Route 53 health checks for automatic DNS failover, ✅ Replicate Terraform state to the DR region, ✅ Document RTO/RPO requirements and validate with tests, ✅ Use activate_dr variable pattern for cost-effective Pilot Light, ✅ Monitor replication lag for RDS and DynamoDB, ✅ Keep DR Terraform code in the same repository as production.
🐛 🔧 ❌ ✅
CHAPTER 63

Common Errors and Solutions

State Locks Auth Errors Cycles Drift Import Timeouts Permissions
12Sections
30Subsections
40+Error Fixes
🐛Debug
PART 17 ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

A comprehensive troubleshooting guide covering every common Terraform error — from state locks and authentication failures to dependency cycles, drift detection, and backend issues. Each error includes the exact error message, root cause analysis, and step-by-step solutions.

Learning Objectives

  • Diagnose and resolve state lock errors with force-unlock
  • Fix provider authentication issues (NoCredentialProviders, ExpiredToken, AccessDenied)
  • Handle "Resource Already Exists" errors via import or recreate strategies
  • Identify and break dependency cycles using terraform graph
  • Detect, resolve, and prevent infrastructure state drift
  • Troubleshoot import failures, timeout errors, and permission issues
  • Resolve provider version conflicts and backend initialization failures

Chapter Contents

63.1 State Lock Errors 63.2 Provider Authentication Errors 63.3 Resource Already Exists 63.4 Dependency Cycle Errors 63.5 Invalid Resource Arguments 63.6 State Drift 63.7 Import Errors 63.8 Module Version Conflicts 63.9 Timeout Errors 63.10 Permission Denied Errors 63.11 Provider Version Errors 63.12 Backend Errors

63.1 State Lock Errors

ERROR 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: State Locking | force-unlock Command

State locking prevents concurrent operations from corrupting your state file. When a lock is not properly released — due to crashes, network issues, or aborted runs — subsequent operations will fail with a lock error.

💻 Practical Examples

63.1.1 Error: Error acquiring the state lock

This error occurs when another Terraform process holds the lock, or when a previous process exited without releasing it.

Example: The Error Message
╷
│ Error: Error acquiring the state lock
│
│ Error message: ConditionalCheckFailedException: The conditional request failed
│ Lock Info:
│   ID:        a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890
│   Path:      my-project/terraform.tfstate
│   Operation: OperationTypeApply
│   Who:       user@hostname
│   Version:   1.7.0
│   Created:   2024-01-15 10:30:00.000000 +0000 UTC
│   Info:
│
│ Terraform acquires a state lock to protect the state from being written
│ by multiple users at the same time. Please resolve the issue above and try
│ again. For most commands, you can disable locking with the "-lock=false"
│ flag, but this is not recommended.
╵
63.1.2 Causes and Solutions

Identify the root cause before attempting to force-unlock, as a legitimate operation may still be in progress.

Cause Symptoms Solution
Concurrent run Another user/CI job is running Wait for the other operation to finish
Crashed process Ctrl+C during apply, OOM kill Verify no process running, then force-unlock
Network failure Lost connection during operation Check DynamoDB/backend, then force-unlock
CI/CD timeout Pipeline killed mid-operation Check pipeline status, then force-unlock
Stale lock Lock older than expected duration Verify with "Who" field, then force-unlock
63.1.3 terraform force-unlock

Use force-unlock only after confirming no legitimate Terraform process holds the lock. Unlocking while another operation is running can corrupt the state.

Example: Force Unlock Procedure
# STEP 1: Verify no Terraform process is running
ps aux | grep terraform
# On Windows: tasklist | findstr terraform

# STEP 2: Check who holds the lock (from error message)
# Look at "Who:" field — is it you? Another team member? CI/CD?

# STEP 3: If confirmed stale, force-unlock with the Lock ID
terraform force-unlock a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890

# STEP 4: If auto-confirm needed (CI/CD scripts)
terraform force-unlock -force a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890

# STEP 5: Verify state is accessible
terraform plan
⚠️ Never Use -lock=false

While -lock=false bypasses locking entirely, it's extremely dangerous in team environments. Two concurrent terraform apply operations without locking can corrupt your state file beyond repair. Always use force-unlock instead.

63.2 Provider Authentication Errors

ERROR 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Provider Authentication | Provider Configuration

Authentication errors are among the most common Terraform issues. They occur when the provider cannot find valid credentials, tokens have expired, or IAM permissions are insufficient.

💻 Practical Examples

63.2.1 NoCredentialProviders

This error means Terraform cannot find any AWS credentials in the standard credential chain.

Example: NoCredentialProviders Error and Fix
# ── THE ERROR ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: error configuring Terraform AWS Provider:
#   no valid credential sources for Terraform AWS Provider found.
#
# Please see https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/aws
# for more information about providing credentials.

# ── AWS Credential Chain (checked in order) ──────────────
# 1. Environment variables (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY)
# 2. Shared credentials file (~/.aws/credentials)
# 3. Shared config file (~/.aws/config)
# 4. EC2 Instance Metadata (IAM Role)
# 5. ECS Task Role

# ── FIX: Set environment variables ──────────────────────
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE"
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY"
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION="us-east-1"

# ── FIX: Use AWS CLI profile ────────────────────────────
export AWS_PROFILE="my-project-profile"

# ── FIX: Configure in provider block (NOT recommended for secrets) ──
provider "aws" {
  region                   = "us-east-1"
  shared_credentials_files = ["~/.aws/credentials"]
  profile                  = "my-project-profile"
}
63.2.2 ExpiredToken

Temporary credentials (STS tokens, SSO sessions) expire after a set duration, causing this error mid-operation.

Example: ExpiredToken Error and Fix
# ── THE ERROR ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: error reading S3 Bucket: ExpiredToken:
#   The security token included in the request is expired
#   status code: 403

# ── FIX: Refresh SSO session ────────────────────────────
aws sso login --profile my-profile

# ── FIX: Re-assume role ─────────────────────────────────
aws sts assume-role \
  --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole \
  --role-session-name terraform-session \
  --duration-seconds 3600

# ── FIX: For long-running applies, increase session duration ──
provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole"
    session_name = "terraform-long-session"
    duration     = "2h"  # Up to 12 hours if role allows
  }
}
63.2.3 AccessDenied

AccessDenied means credentials are valid but lack the required IAM permissions for the specific API action.

Example: AccessDenied Error and Fix
# ── THE ERROR ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: error creating S3 Bucket: AccessDenied:
#   Access Denied
#   status code: 403

# ── DIAGNOSE: Check your identity ────────────────────────
aws sts get-caller-identity
# {
#   "UserId": "AROA3XFRBF23RJEXAMPLE",
#   "Account": "123456789012",
#   "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/terraform-user"
# }

# ── DIAGNOSE: Simulate the IAM policy ──────────────────
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
  --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/terraform-user \
  --action-names s3:CreateBucket \
  --output table

# ── FIX: Add missing permissions to IAM policy ─────────
resource "aws_iam_user_policy" "terraform" {
  name = "terraform-permissions"
  user = "terraform-user"

  policy = jsonencode({
    Version = "2012-10-17"
    Statement = [
      {
        Effect = "Allow"
        Action = [
          "s3:CreateBucket",
          "s3:PutBucketPolicy",
          "s3:PutBucketVersioning",
          "s3:PutEncryptionConfiguration"
        ]
        Resource = "*"
      }
    ]
  })
}
63.2.4 Debugging Steps

A systematic approach to diagnosing authentication issues.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              AUTHENTICATION DEBUGGING FLOWCHART                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│   terraform plan fails                                              │
│          │                                                          │
│          ▼                                                          │
│   ┌──────────────────┐     NO     ┌────────────────────────┐       │
│   │ aws sts           │ ────────► │ Fix credential chain:  │       │
│   │ get-caller-identity│           │ env vars, profile,     │       │
│   │ works?            │           │ SSO login, IAM role    │       │
│   └────────┬─────────┘           └────────────────────────┘       │
│            │ YES                                                    │
│            ▼                                                        │
│   ┌──────────────────┐     NO     ┌────────────────────────┐       │
│   │ Correct account   │ ────────► │ Switch profile/role:   │       │
│   │ and role?         │           │ AWS_PROFILE or         │       │
│   │                   │           │ assume_role block      │       │
│   └────────┬─────────┘           └────────────────────────┘       │
│            │ YES                                                    │
│            ▼                                                        │
│   ┌──────────────────┐     NO     ┌────────────────────────┐       │
│   │ Has required      │ ────────► │ Add IAM permissions:   │       │
│   │ permissions?      │           │ simulate-principal-    │       │
│   │ (simulate-        │           │ policy to find missing │       │
│   │  principal-policy)│           │ actions                │       │
│   └────────┬─────────┘           └────────────────────────┘       │
│            │ YES                                                    │
│            ▼                                                        │
│   ┌──────────────────┐                                              │
│   │ Check SCP, VPC    │                                              │
│   │ endpoints,        │                                              │
│   │ resource policies │                                              │
│   └──────────────────┘                                              │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

63.3 Resource Already Exists

ERROR
📚 Official Documentation: terraform import | Import Block (1.5+)

This error occurs when Terraform tries to create a resource that already exists in the cloud provider but isn't tracked in Terraform's state. Common when migrating from manual/ClickOps management to Terraform.

💻 Practical Examples

63.3.1 Error: Resource Already Exists

The exact error message varies by resource type, but always indicates a conflict between desired and actual state.

Example: Common "Already Exists" Errors
# ── S3 Bucket ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: creating S3 Bucket (my-bucket):
#   BucketAlreadyOwnedByYou: Your previous request to create
#   the named bucket succeeded and you already own it.

# ── Security Group ───────────────────────────────────────
# Error: creating Security Group (my-sg):
#   InvalidGroup.Duplicate: A security group with the name
#   'my-sg' already exists for VPC 'vpc-0abc123'

# ── IAM Role ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: creating IAM Role (my-role):
#   EntityAlreadyExists: Role with name my-role already exists

# ── Route 53 Record ─────────────────────────────────────
# Error: creating Route53 Record:
#   InvalidChangeBatch: Tried to create resource record set
#   [name='example.com.', type='A'] but it already exists
63.3.2 Import vs Recreate

You have two options: import the existing resource into state, or delete the existing resource and let Terraform recreate it.

📥

Option A: Import

  • Use when: Resource is in production
  • Risk: Low — no downtime
  • Brings existing resource under Terraform management
  • Must write matching HCL configuration first
♻️

Option B: Recreate

  • Use when: Resource is dev/test only
  • Risk: High — causes downtime
  • Delete manually, then terraform apply
  • Clean state, no import quirks
Example: Import Workflow (CLI and Import Block)
# ── CLI Import (traditional) ─────────────────────────────
# terraform import aws_s3_bucket.website my-bucket
# terraform import aws_security_group.web sg-0abc123def456
# terraform import aws_iam_role.app my-role

# ── Import Block (Terraform 1.5+) — preferred ───────────
import {
  to = aws_s3_bucket.website
  id = "my-bucket"
}

import {
  to = aws_security_group.web
  id = "sg-0abc123def456"
}

# After import, run terraform plan to verify the config matches:
# terraform plan
# If there are diffs, adjust your HCL to match the existing resource.
# Goal: terraform plan shows "No changes."

63.4 Dependency Cycle Errors

ERROR 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Resource Dependencies | terraform graph

A dependency cycle occurs when resource A depends on resource B, which in turn depends on resource A (directly or indirectly). Terraform cannot determine the creation order and fails.

💻 Practical Examples

63.4.1 Error: Cycle Detected

The error lists the resources involved in the circular dependency.

Example: Cycle Error and Common Patterns
# ── THE ERROR ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: Cycle: aws_security_group.web, aws_security_group.app

# ── COMMON CYCLE PATTERN: Mutual Security Group References ──
# ❌ CAUSES A CYCLE — each SG references the other
resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name = "web-sg"
  ingress {
    from_port       = 80
    to_port         = 80
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.app.id]  # References app
  }
}

resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
  name = "app-sg"
  ingress {
    from_port       = 8080
    to_port         = 8080
    protocol        = "tcp"
    security_groups = [aws_security_group.web.id]  # References web → CYCLE!
  }
}
63.4.2 Finding Cycles

Use terraform graph to visualize the dependency graph and identify the circular reference.

Example: Visualizing the Dependency Graph
# Generate the dependency graph
terraform graph | dot -Tpng -o graph.png

# For a text-based view of the cycle
terraform graph 2>&1 | grep -i cycle

# Validate configuration (also reports cycles)
terraform validate
63.4.3 Breaking Cycles

The solution is to use separate aws_security_group_rule resources instead of inline rules, breaking the circular dependency.

Example: Breaking the Cycle with Separate Rules
# ✅ CORRECT — Create SGs without inline rules, then add rules separately
resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name        = "web-sg"
  description = "Web tier security group"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id
}

resource "aws_security_group" "app" {
  name        = "app-sg"
  description = "App tier security group"
  vpc_id      = aws_vpc.main.id
}

# Rules are separate resources — no cycle!
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "web_from_app" {
  type                     = "ingress"
  from_port                = 80
  to_port                  = 80
  protocol                 = "tcp"
  security_group_id        = aws_security_group.web.id
  source_security_group_id = aws_security_group.app.id
}

resource "aws_security_group_rule" "app_from_web" {
  type                     = "ingress"
  from_port                = 8080
  to_port                  = 8080
  protocol                 = "tcp"
  security_group_id        = aws_security_group.app.id
  source_security_group_id = aws_security_group.web.id
}
💡 General Rule for Breaking Cycles

When two resources reference each other through inline blocks, extract the references into separate resources. This applies to security group rules, IAM policy attachments, route table associations, and any resource pair with mutual references.

63.5 Invalid Resource Arguments

ERROR
📚 Official Documentation: Type Constraints | terraform validate

Invalid argument errors occur when HCL configuration uses wrong attribute names, incorrect types, missing required fields, or deprecated arguments.

💻 Practical Examples

63.5.1 Common Argument Errors

These errors are caught during terraform validate or terraform plan and show exactly which argument is problematic.

Example: Argument Errors and Fixes
# ── ERROR: Unsupported argument ──────────────────────────
# Error: Unsupported argument
#   on main.tf line 5, in resource "aws_instance" "web":
#    5:   ami_id = "ami-0abcdef1234567890"
#
# An argument named "ami_id" is not expected here.
# Did you mean "ami"?

# ❌ Wrong
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami_id        = "ami-0abcdef1234567890"  # Wrong name!
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# ✅ Correct
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0abcdef1234567890"  # Correct name
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
}

# ── ERROR: Missing required argument ────────────────────
# Error: Missing required argument
#   on main.tf line 1, in resource "aws_instance" "web":
#   The argument "ami" is required, but no definition was found.

# ── ERROR: Invalid value for argument ───────────────────
# Error: Invalid value for variable
#   var.instance_type is "t3.superxlarge"
#   Allowed values: t3.micro, t3.small, t3.medium, ...

# ── ERROR: Incorrect attribute value type ───────────────
# Expected type: list of string
# Got: string
# Fix: Wrap single values in brackets
#   ❌ security_groups = aws_security_group.web.id
#   ✅ security_groups = [aws_security_group.web.id]
63.5.2 Reference Documentation

Always check the Terraform Registry documentation for correct argument names and types.

Example: Checking Documentation
# ── Quick reference for any resource ─────────────────────
# https://registry.terraform.io/providers/hashicorp/aws/latest/docs/resources/instance

# ── Use terraform validate for syntax checking ──────────
terraform validate

# ── Use terraform console to test expressions ────────────
terraform console
> type(var.my_list)      # Check variable types
> length(var.my_list)    # Verify list contents

# ── IDE Support (VS Code with Terraform extension) ──────
# Install HashiCorp Terraform extension for:
# • Auto-completion of argument names
# • Type checking in real-time
# • Documentation hover tooltips
# • Format-on-save with terraform fmt

63.6 State Drift

CONCEPT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: State Inspection | terraform refresh

State drift occurs when the actual infrastructure diverges from what Terraform's state file records — typically caused by manual changes (ClickOps), other automation tools, or AWS service auto-scaling.

💻 Practical Examples

63.6.1 Detecting Drift

Terraform detects drift by comparing the state file with the actual infrastructure during terraform plan.

Example: Detecting and Reading Drift Output
# ── Run plan to detect drift ─────────────────────────────
terraform plan

# ── Drift output looks like this: ────────────────────────
# Note: Objects have changed outside of Terraform
#
# Terraform detected the following changes made outside of Terraform
# since the last "terraform apply":
#
#   # aws_security_group.web has been changed
#   ~ resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
#         id          = "sg-0abc123def456"
#         name        = "web-sg"
#       ~ description = "Web server SG" -> "Modified manually"
#       + ingress {
#           + cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
#           + from_port   = 22
#           + to_port     = 22
#           + protocol    = "tcp"
#         }
#     }

# ── Refresh-only plan (detect without changing) ──────────
terraform plan -refresh-only
63.6.2 Resolving Drift

You have three options: accept the drift, revert to Terraform's config, or update Terraform to match reality.

Strategy Command Effect Use When
Accept drift terraform apply -refresh-only Updates state to match reality Manual change was intentional
Revert drift terraform apply Overwrites reality to match config Manual change was unauthorized
Update config Edit HCL, then terraform apply Config matches reality, clean apply Change is desired, make it official
63.6.3 Preventing Drift

Implement guardrails to prevent manual changes and detect drift early.

Example: Drift Prevention Strategies
# ── 1. Use lifecycle ignore_changes for expected drift ───
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "app" {
  desired_capacity = 2
  min_size         = 2
  max_size         = 10

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [desired_capacity]  # ASG auto-scales this
  }
}

# ── 2. Scheduled drift detection in CI/CD ────────────────
# .github/workflows/drift-detection.yml
# schedule:
#   - cron: '0 */6 * * *'  # Every 6 hours
# steps:
#   - run: terraform plan -refresh-only -detailed-exitcode
#     # Exit code 2 = drift detected → notify team

# ── 3. AWS Config Rules for compliance ───────────────────
resource "aws_config_config_rule" "required_tags" {
  name = "required-tags"
  source {
    owner             = "AWS"
    source_identifier = "REQUIRED_TAGS"
  }
  input_parameters = jsonencode({
    tag1Key = "ManagedBy"
    tag1Value = "terraform"
  })
}

# ── 4. SCPs to prevent console changes ──────────────────
# Service Control Policies can restrict specific actions
# to only the Terraform IAM role, preventing ClickOps
⚠️ Never Use terraform refresh Alone

The standalone terraform refresh command silently updates state without showing what changed. Always use terraform plan -refresh-only or terraform apply -refresh-only instead, which show the detected changes before applying them.

63.7 Import Errors

ERROR
📚 Official Documentation: terraform import CLI | Import Block

Import errors occur when trying to bring existing infrastructure under Terraform management. Common causes include wrong resource IDs, missing configuration blocks, and unsupported resource types.

💻 Practical Examples

63.7.1 Common Import Failures

Each resource type expects a specific import ID format. Using the wrong format is the most frequent cause of import failures.

Example: Import ID Format by Resource Type
# ── THE ERROR ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: Cannot import non-existent remote object
#
# While attempting to import an existing object to
# "aws_instance.web", the provider detected that no object
# exists with the given id.

# ── CORRECT IMPORT ID FORMATS ────────────────────────────
terraform import aws_instance.web              i-0abc123def456
terraform import aws_s3_bucket.data            my-bucket-name
terraform import aws_security_group.web        sg-0abc123def456
terraform import aws_iam_role.app              my-role-name
terraform import aws_vpc.main                  vpc-0abc123def456
terraform import aws_subnet.public             subnet-0abc123def456
terraform import aws_db_instance.main          my-rds-identifier
terraform import aws_lambda_function.handler   my-function-name
terraform import aws_route53_record.www        ZONEID_example.com_A
terraform import aws_lb.main                   arn:aws:elasticloadbalancing:...

# ── TIP: Find resource IDs with AWS CLI ──────────────────
aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[].InstanceId'
aws s3api list-buckets --query 'Buckets[].Name'
aws iam list-roles --query 'Roles[].RoleName'
63.7.2 Post-Import Drift

After importing, terraform plan often shows differences because your HCL doesn't perfectly match the imported resource's configuration.

Example: Import and Align Workflow
# STEP 1: Write a minimal resource block
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "imported" {
  bucket = "my-existing-bucket"
}

# STEP 2: Import the resource
# terraform import aws_s3_bucket.imported my-existing-bucket

# STEP 3: Show the full state to see all attributes
# terraform state show aws_s3_bucket.imported

# STEP 4: Update HCL to match all attributes from state
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "imported" {
  bucket = "my-existing-bucket"

  tags = {
    Name        = "My Existing Bucket"
    Environment = "production"
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

# STEP 5: Verify — plan should show "No changes"
# terraform plan
💡 Generate Config for Imports (Terraform 1.5+)

Use terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf with import blocks to auto-generate HCL that matches the imported resource. This eliminates manual config alignment.

63.8 Module Version Conflicts

ERROR
📚 Official Documentation: Module Sources | Version Constraints

Version conflict errors arise when multiple module calls require different versions of the same provider, or when a module version constraint cannot be satisfied by the available versions.

💻 Practical Examples

63.8.1 Version Constraint Failures

These errors occur during terraform init when the lock file or registry doesn't have a version matching all constraints.

Example: Version Conflict Errors and Fixes
# ── THE ERROR ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: Failed to query available provider packages
#
# Could not retrieve the list of available versions for provider
# hashicorp/aws: no available releases match the given constraints
# >= 4.0.0, < 4.50.0, >= 5.0.0

# ── ANOTHER COMMON ERROR ────────────────────────────────
# Error: Module version requirements have changed
#
# The module at module.vpc requires version "~> 5.0",
# which does not match the previously-selected version 4.2.0.
# ── FIX: Align version constraints ──────────────────────
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"  # All modules must agree
    }
  }
}

# ── FIX: Update the lock file ───────────────────────────
# terraform init -upgrade

# ── FIX: Pin modules to compatible versions ─────────────
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"  # Compatible with AWS provider 5.x
}

module "ec2" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/ec2-instance/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"  # Also compatible with AWS provider 5.x
}
63.8.2 Lock File Issues

The .terraform.lock.hcl file pins the exact provider versions. Conflicts can arise when the lock file is out of date.

Scenario Command Effect
Update all providers to latest within constraints terraform init -upgrade Updates lock file + re-downloads providers
Lock file missing or corrupted Delete .terraform.lock.hcl, re-run terraform init Generates fresh lock file
Lock file conflicts across platforms terraform providers lock -platform=linux_amd64 -platform=darwin_amd64 Adds hashes for multiple platforms
Cache stale providers Delete .terraform/ directory, re-run terraform init Fresh download of all providers and modules

63.9 Timeout Errors

ERROR
📚 Official Documentation: Operation Timeouts | Lifecycle Meta-Arguments

Timeout errors occur when a resource takes longer to create, update, or delete than the provider's default timeout allows. Common with RDS instances, EKS clusters, CloudFront distributions, and other long-running operations.

💻 Practical Examples

63.9.1 Error: Timeout waiting for resource

The error indicates which operation (create, update, or delete) exceeded its timeout.

Example: Timeout Errors and Custom Timeouts
# ── THE ERROR ────────────────────────────────────────────
# Error: error waiting for RDS Cluster (my-cluster) to become available:
# timeout while waiting for state to become 'available'
# (last state: 'creating', timeout: 30m0s)

# Error: error waiting for EKS Cluster (my-cluster) delete:
# timeout while waiting for state to become 'DELETED'
# (last state: 'DELETING', timeout: 15m0s)
# ── FIX: Increase timeouts in resource block ────────────
resource "aws_rds_cluster" "main" {
  cluster_identifier = "my-cluster"
  engine             = "aurora-mysql"
  engine_version     = "8.0.mysql_aurora.3.04.0"
  master_username    = "admin"
  master_password    = var.db_password

  timeouts {
    create = "60m"   # Default: 30m
    update = "90m"   # Default: 30m
    delete = "60m"   # Default: 30m
  }
}

resource "aws_eks_cluster" "main" {
  name     = "my-cluster"
  role_arn = aws_iam_role.eks.arn

  vpc_config {
    subnet_ids = var.subnet_ids
  }

  timeouts {
    create = "45m"   # Default: 30m
    update = "60m"   # Default: 60m
    delete = "30m"   # Default: 15m
  }
}
63.9.2 Resources with Long Default Timeouts

Know which resources commonly need extended timeouts to avoid surprises in CI/CD pipelines.

Resource Operation Default Timeout Recommended
aws_rds_cluster Create 120m 120m (usually sufficient)
aws_db_instance Create 40m 60m for large instances
aws_eks_cluster Create 30m 45m
aws_cloudfront_distribution Create 70m 90m
aws_elasticsearch_domain Create 60m 90m
aws_redshift_cluster Create 75m 90m for multi-node

63.10 Permission Denied Errors

ERROR
📚 Official Documentation: Troubleshoot Access Denied | IAM Policies

Permission denied errors go beyond basic authentication — they involve IAM policy misconfigurations, Service Control Policies (SCPs), resource policies, VPC endpoint policies, and permission boundaries that block specific API actions.

💻 Practical Examples

63.10.1 IAM Permission Layers

AWS evaluates multiple policy layers. A "Deny" at any layer overrides all "Allow" statements.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               AWS PERMISSION EVALUATION ORDER                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                     │
│   API Request                                                       │
│       │                                                             │
│       ▼                                                             │
│   ┌────────────────────┐                                            │
│   │ 1. SCPs (Org level)│ ──── Explicit Deny? → DENIED              │
│   └────────┬───────────┘                                            │
│            ▼                                                        │
│   ┌────────────────────┐                                            │
│   │ 2. Resource Policy │ ──── Explicit Deny? → DENIED              │
│   │    (S3, KMS, etc.) │                                            │
│   └────────┬───────────┘                                            │
│            ▼                                                        │
│   ┌────────────────────┐                                            │
│   │ 3. Permission      │ ──── Not allowed? → DENIED                │
│   │    Boundaries      │                                            │
│   └────────┬───────────┘                                            │
│            ▼                                                        │
│   ┌────────────────────┐                                            │
│   │ 4. Session Policy  │ ──── Not allowed? → DENIED                │
│   │    (AssumeRole)    │                                            │
│   └────────┬───────────┘                                            │
│            ▼                                                        │
│   ┌────────────────────┐                                            │
│   │ 5. Identity Policy │ ──── Explicit Allow? → ALLOWED            │
│   │    (IAM user/role) │ ──── No match? → DENIED (implicit)        │
│   └────────────────────┘                                            │
│                                                                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
63.10.2 Diagnosing and Fixing Permissions

Use IAM policy simulator, CloudTrail, and Access Analyzer to pinpoint the exact permission gap.

Example: Permission Debugging Workflow
# ── STEP 1: Check your current identity ──────────────────
aws sts get-caller-identity

# ── STEP 2: Simulate the failing action ─────────────────
aws iam simulate-principal-policy \
  --policy-source-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/TerraformRole \
  --action-names ec2:CreateVpc ec2:CreateSubnet \
  --output table

# ── STEP 3: Check CloudTrail for the exact error ────────
aws cloudtrail lookup-events \
  --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=CreateVpc \
  --max-results 5

# ── STEP 4: Check for SCP restrictions ──────────────────
aws organizations list-policies-for-target \
  --target-id ou-abc123 \
  --filter SERVICE_CONTROL_POLICY

# ── STEP 5: Review IAM Access Analyzer findings ─────────
aws accessanalyzer list-findings --analyzer-name my-analyzer
💡 Minimum IAM Policy for Terraform

A common mistake is granting AdministratorAccess. Instead, use least privilege: start with read-only permissions, run terraform plan, and iteratively add only the specific actions that are denied. The simulate-principal-policy API is your best friend for this.

63.11 Provider Version Errors

ERROR
📚 Official Documentation: Provider Requirements | Version Constraints Syntax

Provider version errors occur during terraform init when version constraints conflict, the required version isn't published, or the lock file is incompatible with the current platform.

💻 Practical Examples

63.11.1 Version Constraint Syntax

Understanding version constraint operators is key to avoiding conflicts.

Operator Example Meaning Matches
= = 5.30.0 Exact version only 5.30.0
!= != 5.30.0 Exclude specific version Any except 5.30.0
>= >= 5.0 Minimum version 5.0, 5.1, 6.0, ...
~> ~> 5.30 Pessimistic (rightmost increments) 5.30.0 – 5.99.x
~> ~> 5.30.0 Patch-level only 5.30.0 – 5.30.x
compound >= 5.0, < 6.0 Range constraint 5.0 – 5.x.x
63.11.2 Fixing Provider Version Errors

Step-by-step resolution for the most common provider version issues.

Example: Resolving Provider Version Conflicts
# ── ERROR: Incompatible provider version ─────────────────
# Error: Incompatible provider version
#   Provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws v4.67.0
#   does not match the constraints >= 5.0.0

# ── FIX 1: Update constraint to allow current version ───
# In terraform block:
#   version = ">= 4.0"  (instead of >= 5.0)

# ── FIX 2: Upgrade provider to match constraint ─────────
terraform init -upgrade

# ── ERROR: Lock file mismatch ───────────────────────────
# Error: Failed to install provider
#   The local lock file indicates that provider
#   registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws 5.30.0 is signed
#   with a different set of checksums.

# ── FIX: Regenerate lock file for all platforms ──────────
rm .terraform.lock.hcl
terraform init
# Or for multi-platform:
terraform providers lock \
  -platform=linux_amd64 \
  -platform=darwin_amd64 \
  -platform=windows_amd64

# ── ERROR: Provider not found ───────────────────────────
# Error: Failed to query available provider packages
#   Could not retrieve the list of available versions for
#   provider hashicorp/awss: provider not found

# ── FIX: Correct the source in required_providers ───────
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"   # NOT "hashicorp/awss"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

63.12 Backend Errors

ERROR 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Backend Configuration | S3 Backend

Backend errors prevent Terraform from reading or writing the state file. Common causes include missing S3 buckets, DynamoDB table access issues, and backend reconfiguration problems.

💻 Practical Examples

63.12.1 Common Backend Errors

Backend errors always occur during terraform init because that's when Terraform connects to the backend.

Example: Backend Errors and Fixes
# ── ERROR: S3 bucket not found ───────────────────────────
# Error: Failed to get existing workspaces:
#   S3 bucket does not exist.
#
# The referenced S3 bucket must have been previously created.

# FIX: Create the bucket first (bootstrap)
aws s3api create-bucket \
  --bucket my-terraform-state \
  --region us-east-1

aws s3api put-bucket-versioning \
  --bucket my-terraform-state \
  --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled

# ── ERROR: DynamoDB table not found ─────────────────────
# Error: Failed to get state lock:
#   ResourceNotFoundException: Requested resource not found

# FIX: Create the lock table
aws dynamodb create-table \
  --table-name terraform-locks \
  --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \
  --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST

# ── ERROR: Backend configuration changed ────────────────
# Error: Backend configuration changed
#   A change in the backend configuration has been detected,
#   which may require migrating existing state.

# FIX: Re-init with migration
terraform init -migrate-state

# Or to start fresh (careful — loses state!):
terraform init -reconfigure
63.12.2 Backend Migration Strategies

When changing backends (e.g., local → S3, or S3 → Terraform Cloud), use the correct migration workflow to avoid data loss.

Example: Safe Backend Migration Workflow
# ── STEP 1: Current backend (local) ─────────────────────
terraform {
  # backend "local" {}  # Implicit default
}

# ── STEP 2: Add new backend configuration ───────────────
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}

# ── STEP 3: Initialize with migration ───────────────────
# terraform init -migrate-state
#
# Terraform will ask:
# "Do you want to copy existing state to the new backend?"
# Answer: yes
#
# ── STEP 4: Verify migration ────────────────────────────
# terraform state list
# (Should show all your resources)
#
# ── STEP 5: Remove local state file (optional) ──────────
# rm terraform.tfstate
# rm terraform.tfstate.backup
⚠️ -reconfigure vs -migrate-state

terraform init -reconfigure does NOT copy state — it abandons the old backend. Use -migrate-state to safely transfer state between backends. Using -reconfigure when switching backends means starting with an empty state, which can lead to Terraform trying to recreate all existing infrastructure.

🔍 🪲 📋 🔬
CHAPTER 64

Debugging Techniques

TF_LOG terraform console Graph Visualization State Inspection
4Sections
10Subsections
5Log Levels
🔍Debug
PART 17 INTERMEDIATE ★★★★☆

Master Terraform's debugging toolkit — from verbose logging with TF_LOG to interactive expression testing with terraform console, dependency graph visualization, and deep state inspection techniques.

Learning Objectives

  • Use TF_LOG with TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, and ERROR levels
  • Redirect debug logs to files for analysis
  • Interactively test expressions with terraform console
  • Visualize and analyze resource dependency graphs
  • Inspect and manipulate state for troubleshooting

Chapter Contents

64.1 TF_LOG and Log Levels 64.2 Using terraform console 64.3 Dependency Graph Visualization 64.4 State Inspection Commands

64.1 TF_LOG and Log Levels

DEBUG 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Debugging Terraform | Environment Variables

Terraform uses the TF_LOG environment variable to control logging verbosity. This is the primary tool for diagnosing provider communication issues, state operations, and internal Terraform behavior.

💻 Practical Examples

64.1.1 Log Levels Overview

Terraform provides five log levels, from most verbose to least.

Level Verbosity Content Use When
TRACE 🔊🔊🔊🔊🔊 Everything — HTTP requests, responses, JSON bodies Provider bugs, API issues, filing bug reports
DEBUG 🔊🔊🔊🔊 Internal decisions, graph walks, state operations State corruption, dependency issues
INFO 🔊🔊🔊 General operation flow, provider init Understanding execution order
WARN 🔊🔊 Deprecations, potential issues Finding deprecated features
ERROR 🔊 Only errors and failures Quick error triage
64.1.2 Setting TF_LOG

Set TF_LOG before running any Terraform command. The logs appear on stderr.

Example: Using TF_LOG on Different OS
# ── Linux/macOS ──────────────────────────────────────────
export TF_LOG=DEBUG
terraform plan

# ── Windows PowerShell ───────────────────────────────────
$env:TF_LOG = "DEBUG"
terraform plan

# ── Windows CMD ──────────────────────────────────────────
set TF_LOG=DEBUG
terraform plan

# ── One-liner (Linux/macOS) ──────────────────────────────
TF_LOG=TRACE terraform plan

# ── Provider-specific logging ────────────────────────────
export TF_LOG_CORE=WARN       # Core Terraform: minimal output
export TF_LOG_PROVIDER=TRACE  # Provider only: full detail

# ── Disable logging ─────────────────────────────────────
unset TF_LOG
# Or on Windows:
# Remove-Item Env:\TF_LOG
64.1.3 Logging to Files

Use TF_LOG_PATH to write debug logs to a file for easier analysis and sharing.

Example: File-Based Logging and Analysis
# ── Write logs to a file ─────────────────────────────────
export TF_LOG=TRACE
export TF_LOG_PATH="./terraform-debug.log"
terraform plan

# ── Analyze the log file ────────────────────────────────
# Search for HTTP errors
grep -i "error\|failed\|status: 4\|status: 5" terraform-debug.log

# Find specific API calls
grep "POST\|PUT\|DELETE\|GET" terraform-debug.log

# Find provider responses
grep "Response body" terraform-debug.log | head -20

# ── Create a crash log for bug reports ───────────────────
export TF_LOG=TRACE
export TF_LOG_PATH="./crash.log"
terraform apply 2>&1

# ── File rotation (prevent huge logs in CI/CD) ───────────
export TF_LOG=DEBUG
export TF_LOG_PATH="./terraform-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).log"
terraform apply
⚠️ Sensitive Data in Logs

TF_LOG=TRACE may expose sensitive values (API keys, passwords, tokens) in HTTP request/response bodies. Never commit log files to version control and add *.log to your .gitignore. Redact sensitive data before sharing logs in bug reports.

64.2 Using terraform console

DEBUG 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: terraform console Command | Built-in Functions

The terraform console command opens an interactive REPL where you can evaluate expressions, test functions, inspect variables, and explore state data — all without running plan or apply.

💻 Practical Examples

64.2.1 Interactive Expression Testing

Launch the console and test expressions in real-time against your current configuration and state.

Example: terraform console Sessions
# ── Launch the console ───────────────────────────────────
terraform console

# ── Test string functions ────────────────────────────────
> upper("hello terraform")
"HELLO TERRAFORM"

> format("arn:aws:s3:::%s/*", "my-bucket")
"arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"

> join("-", ["app", "prod", "us-east-1"])
"app-prod-us-east-1"

> regex("^([a-z]+)-([a-z]+)$", "web-prod")
["web", "prod"]

# ── Test numeric expressions ────────────────────────────
> max(5, 12, 3)
12

> ceil(4.3)
5

> parseint("FF", 16)
255

# ── Test collection functions ────────────────────────────
> flatten([["a", "b"], ["c", "d"]])
["a", "b", "c", "d"]

> zipmap(["name", "env"], ["web", "prod"])
{ "env" = "prod", "name" = "web" }

> { for s in ["a", "b", "c"] : s => upper(s) }
{ "a" = "A", "b" = "B", "c" = "C" }

# ── Inspect variables and locals ────────────────────────
> var.environment
"production"

> var.instance_types
{ "dev" = "t3.micro", "prod" = "t3.large" }

> var.instance_types["prod"]
"t3.large"

# ── Inspect state (existing resources) ──────────────────
> aws_vpc.main.id
"vpc-0abc123def456"

> aws_subnet.public[*].id
["subnet-0abc123", "subnet-0def456", "subnet-0ghi789"]

# ── Exit the console ────────────────────────────────────
> exit
64.2.2 Non-Interactive Mode

Pipe expressions into the console for scripting and CI/CD use.

Example: Console in Scripts
# ── Evaluate a single expression ─────────────────────────
echo 'var.environment' | terraform console

# ── Use in shell scripts ────────────────────────────────
VPC_ID=$(echo 'aws_vpc.main.id' | terraform console | tr -d '"')
echo "VPC ID: $VPC_ID"

# ── Test complex expressions before using in config ─────
echo 'cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, 1)' | terraform console
# "10.0.1.0/24"

echo 'cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, 2)' | terraform console
# "10.0.2.0/24"

# ── Evaluate with a specific plan ───────────────────────
terraform console -plan  # Uses the saved plan file context

64.3 Dependency Graph Visualization

DEBUG
📚 Official Documentation: terraform graph | Resource Graph Internals

The terraform graph command outputs a DOT-format representation of the resource dependency graph. Visualize it to understand creation order, diagnose cycles, and optimize parallelism.

💻 Practical Examples

64.3.1 Generating Graph Output

Generate graphs for different operation types and visualize them with Graphviz or web tools.

Example: Graph Generation and Visualization
# ── Basic graph output (DOT format) ──────────────────────
terraform graph

# ── Save to file ─────────────────────────────────────────
terraform graph > graph.dot

# ── Convert to PNG image (requires Graphviz) ─────────────
terraform graph | dot -Tpng -o graph.png

# ── Convert to SVG (scalable) ────────────────────────────
terraform graph | dot -Tsvg -o graph.svg

# ── Graph types ──────────────────────────────────────────
terraform graph -type=plan           # Plan graph (default)
terraform graph -type=plan-refresh-only  # Refresh-only plan
terraform graph -type=plan-destroy   # Destroy plan
terraform graph -type=apply          # Apply graph

# ── Draw options for cleaner output ──────────────────────
terraform graph -draw-cycles    # Highlights cycles in red

# ── Online visualization (no Graphviz needed) ────────────
# Paste DOT output at: https://dreampuf.github.io/GraphvizOnline/
# Or: https://edotor.net/
64.3.2 Reading the Graph

Understanding the DOT output helps you trace dependency chains and identify optimization opportunities.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                TERRAFORM DEPENDENCY GRAPH                          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                    │
│   [root] root                                                      │
│      │                                                             │
│      ├──► [root] aws_vpc.main                                      │
│      │       │                                                     │
│      │       ├──► [root] aws_subnet.public[0]                      │
│      │       │       │                                             │
│      │       │       └──► [root] aws_instance.web                  │
│      │       │               │                                     │
│      │       │               └──► [root] aws_eip.web               │
│      │       │                                                     │
│      │       ├──► [root] aws_subnet.public[1]                      │
│      │       │                                                     │
│      │       └──► [root] aws_security_group.web                    │
│      │               │                                             │
│      │               └──► [root] aws_instance.web                  │
│      │                                                             │
│      └──► [root] aws_iam_role.app                                  │
│              │                                                     │
│              └──► [root] aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.app         │
│                                                                    │
│   Legend:                                                          │
│     ──►  "depends on" (arrow points to dependency)                 │
│     Resources at same depth level can be created in parallel       │
│                                                                    │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

64.4 State Inspection Commands

DEBUG 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: State Commands | terraform show

State inspection commands let you query, view, and manipulate the Terraform state without running plan or apply. Essential for troubleshooting state-related issues.

💻 Practical Examples

64.4.1 Read-Only State Commands

These commands only read the state — they never modify it and are safe to run at any time.

Example: State Inspection Commands
# ── List all resources in state ──────────────────────────
terraform state list
# aws_vpc.main
# aws_subnet.public[0]
# aws_subnet.public[1]
# aws_security_group.web
# aws_instance.web
# module.rds.aws_db_instance.main

# ── Filter by resource type ─────────────────────────────
terraform state list | grep aws_subnet
# aws_subnet.public[0]
# aws_subnet.public[1]

# ── Show full details of a specific resource ─────────────
terraform state show aws_instance.web
# resource "aws_instance" "web" {
#     ami                    = "ami-0abcdef1234567890"
#     instance_type          = "t3.micro"
#     id                     = "i-0abc123def456"
#     private_ip             = "10.0.1.50"
#     public_ip              = "54.123.45.67"
#     subnet_id              = "subnet-0abc123"
#     vpc_security_group_ids = ["sg-0abc123def456"]
#     tags                   = { Name = "web-server" }
# }

# ── Show module resources ───────────────────────────────
terraform state list module.rds

# ── Show entire state as JSON ────────────────────────────
terraform show -json | jq '.values.root_module.resources[] | {type, name, values: .values | keys}'

# ── Pull state to local file (for inspection) ───────────
terraform state pull > state-backup.json
cat state-backup.json | jq '.resources | length'

# ── Show plan output as JSON ────────────────────────────
terraform plan -out=plan.bin
terraform show -json plan.bin > plan.json
jq '.resource_changes[] | {address, change: .change.actions}' plan.json
64.4.2 State Manipulation Commands

These commands modify the state. Always back up state before using them.

Example: State Modification Commands
# ⚠️ ALWAYS BACKUP STATE FIRST
terraform state pull > state-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).json

# ── Remove a resource from state (don't destroy it) ─────
terraform state rm aws_instance.legacy
# Removed aws_instance.legacy
# Successfully removed 1 resource instance(s).
# The resource still exists in AWS — just not tracked by Terraform

# ── Move/rename a resource in state ─────────────────────
terraform state mv aws_instance.web aws_instance.app
# Move "aws_instance.web" to "aws_instance.app"
# (Update your HCL to match the new name!)

# ── Move into a module ──────────────────────────────────
terraform state mv aws_instance.web module.compute.aws_instance.web

# ── Replace a provider in state ──────────────────────────
terraform state replace-provider \
  registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws \
  registry.terraform.io/-/aws

# ── Taint a resource (force recreation on next apply) ────
# Deprecated in 1.5+ — use -replace flag instead
terraform apply -replace="aws_instance.web"

# ── Push local state to remote backend ───────────────────
# terraform state push state-backup.json
# ⚠️ DANGEROUS — can overwrite remote state!
💡 Debugging Checklist
  1. terraform validate — Catch syntax errors first
  2. terraform plan — See what Terraform wants to do
  3. TF_LOG=DEBUG terraform plan — Get detailed operation logs
  4. terraform console — Test expressions interactively
  5. terraform state list/show — Inspect current state
  6. terraform graph — Visualize dependencies
✅ 📋 🏆 🛡️
CHAPTER 65

Best Practices Checklist

Code Organization State Management Security Modules CI/CD Versioning
10Sections
24Subsections
60+Best Practices
✅Checklist
PART 17 ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

A comprehensive checklist of Terraform best practices covering code organization, state management, security, modules, variables, CI/CD pipelines, versioning, documentation, testing, and cost optimization.

Learning Objectives

  • Organize Terraform code with clear file and directory structure
  • Implement remote state with proper locking and access controls
  • Secure sensitive data using encryption, Vault, and least privilege
  • Design reusable, versioned modules following composition patterns
  • Build robust CI/CD pipelines with automated plan, apply, and testing

Chapter Contents

65.1 Code Organization 65.2 Naming Conventions 65.3 Version Pinning 65.4 State Management Best Practices 65.5 Secret Handling 65.6 Module Design Principles 65.7 Documentation 65.8 Testing Strategy 65.9 CI/CD Best Practices 65.10 Team Collaboration

65.1 Code Organization

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Standard Module Structure | File and Directory Layout

Well-organized Terraform code is easier to read, maintain, review, and debug. Follow standard conventions for file naming, directory structure, and code grouping.

💻 Practical Examples

65.1.1 File Structure

Follow the HashiCorp standard file naming convention. Keep each file focused on a single concern — this makes code reviews cleaner and reduces merge conflicts.

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  STANDARD TERRAFORM FILE STRUCTURE                   │
