├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   project/                                                           │
│   ├── main.tf              # Primary resources and data sources      │
│   ├── variables.tf         # All input variable declarations         │
│   ├── outputs.tf           # All output value declarations           │
│   ├── versions.tf          # terraform {} block, required_providers  │
│   ├── providers.tf         # Provider configuration blocks           │
│   ├── locals.tf            # Local values and computed expressions    │
│   ├── data.tf              # Data sources (optional, can be in main) │
│   ├── backend.tf           # Backend configuration                   │
│   ├── terraform.tfvars     # Variable values (don't commit secrets!) │
│   ├── .terraform.lock.hcl  # Provider lock file (commit this!)       │
│   ├── .gitignore           # Ignore .terraform/, *.tfstate, *.tfvars │
│   └── README.md            # Project documentation                   │
│                                                                      │
│   ✅ DO: Keep files focused — one concern per file                   │
│   ✅ DO: Commit .terraform.lock.hcl to version control               │
│   ❌ DON'T: Put everything in a single main.tf                       │
│   ❌ DON'T: Commit terraform.tfstate or .terraform/ directory        │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
65.1.2 Naming Conventions

Consistent naming across files, resources, and variables makes codebases navigable. Use snake_case everywhere and adopt meaningful, descriptive names.

Example: File and Resource Naming Convention
# ── File Naming ──────────────────────────────────────────
# ✅ Good: descriptive, lowercase, .tf extension
#    main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, networking.tf
# ❌ Bad:  MyResources.tf, MAIN.TF, resources-v2.tf

# ── Resource Naming ──────────────────────────────────────
# ✅ Good: snake_case, descriptive, no resource type prefix
resource "aws_instance" "web_server" { }
resource "aws_security_group" "allow_https" { }
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "application_logs" { }

# ❌ Bad: camelCase, vague, redundant type in name
resource "aws_instance" "webServer" { }         # camelCase
resource "aws_security_group" "sg1" { }          # not descriptive
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "aws_s3_logs" { }       # redundant prefix

# ── Variable Naming ──────────────────────────────────────
# ✅ Good: descriptive, with type hint where helpful
variable "instance_count" { }
variable "enable_monitoring" { }
variable "database_subnet_ids" { }

# ❌ Bad
variable "cnt" { }        # too short
variable "EnableMon" { }  # wrong case
65.1.3 Module Organization

Structure multi-environment projects using directories and shared modules. Keep the blast radius small by splitting infrastructure into logical components.

Example: Multi-Environment Module Organization
# ── RECOMMENDED: Directory-per-environment ───────────────
infrastructure/
├── modules/                  # Shared reusable modules
│   ├── vpc/
│   │   ├── main.tf
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   └── outputs.tf
│   ├── compute/
│   └── database/
├── environments/
│   ├── dev/
│   │   ├── main.tf          # Calls modules with dev values
│   │   ├── variables.tf
│   │   ├── terraform.tfvars # Dev-specific values
│   │   └── backend.tf       # Dev state bucket/key
│   ├── staging/
│   │   └── ...              # Same structure, staging values
│   └── prod/
│       └── ...              # Same structure, prod values
└── global/                   # Shared resources (IAM, DNS)
    ├── main.tf
    └── backend.tf
✅ Practice Why
☐ Separate files per concern (main, variables, outputs) Readability and code review clarity
☐ Use consistent naming: snake_case for all identifiers HCL convention, avoids confusion
☐ Extract reusable patterns into shared modules DRY principle, single source of truth
☐ Use directory-per-environment structure Clear isolation, independent state
☐ Keep blast radius small (split large stacks) Minimize risk per apply

65.2 Naming Conventions

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Configuration Syntax | Resource Blocks

Consistent naming conventions make Terraform code predictable and navigable. Every identifier — resources, variables, outputs, and tags — should follow a clear, team-wide standard.

💻 Practical Examples

65.2.1 Resource Naming

Use snake_case for all Terraform resource names. Choose descriptive names that convey purpose — never repeat the resource type in the name.

Example: Resource Naming Patterns
# ── ✅ GOOD: Descriptive snake_case names ─────────────────
resource "aws_instance" "web_server" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
}

resource "aws_security_group" "allow_https" {
  name = "allow-https-inbound"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "application_logs" {
  bucket = "${var.project}-${var.environment}-app-logs"
}

# ── ❌ BAD: Common anti-patterns ──────────────────────────
resource "aws_instance" "instance1" { }       # Non-descriptive
resource "aws_instance" "myWebServer" { }     # camelCase
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "aws_s3_bucket" { }  # Repeats type
resource "aws_instance" "i" { }               # Single letter
65.2.2 Variable Naming

Variable names should describe the value, include type hints for collections, and use boolean prefixes like enable_ or is_.

Example: Variable Naming Standards
# ── ✅ GOOD: Clear, descriptive variable names ───────────
variable "instance_type" {
  type        = string
  description = "EC2 instance type for the web servers"
  default     = "t3.micro"
}

variable "enable_monitoring" {       # Boolean: enable_ prefix
  type        = bool
  description = "Whether to enable detailed monitoring"
  default     = true
}

variable "database_subnet_ids" {     # Collection: plural + _ids
  type        = list(string)
  description = "List of subnet IDs for the database tier"
}

variable "allowed_cidr_blocks" {     # Collection: plural
  type        = list(string)
  description = "CIDR blocks allowed to access the service"
}

# ── ❌ BAD: Vague or inconsistent names ──────────────────
variable "type" { }        # Too generic
variable "cnt" { }         # Abbreviation
variable "EnableMon" { }   # Wrong case
65.2.3 Output Naming

Output names should describe what they return. Always include a description so consumers understand the value.

Example: Output Naming Standards
# ── ✅ GOOD: Descriptive output names ─────────────────────
output "vpc_id" {
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
  description = "The ID of the VPC"
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  description = "List of private subnet IDs"
}

output "load_balancer_dns_name" {
  value       = aws_lb.main.dns_name
  description = "DNS name of the application load balancer"
}

output "database_connection_string" {
  value       = aws_db_instance.main.endpoint
  description = "RDS instance connection endpoint"
  sensitive   = true
}

# ── ❌ BAD: Vague or missing descriptions ────────────────
output "id" { value = aws_vpc.main.id }    # Which ID?
output "out1" { value = aws_lb.main.arn }  # Meaningless
65.2.4 Tag Naming

Tags enable cost tracking, access control, and operational automation. Use a consistent tagging standard across all resources.

Example: Tag Naming Strategy
# ── Standard tag map using locals ─────────────────────────
locals {
  common_tags = {
    Project     = var.project_name      # Business project
    Environment = var.environment        # dev, staging, prod
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"            # IaC tool
    Owner       = var.team_email         # Contact point
    CostCenter  = var.cost_center        # Billing allocation
    Repository  = var.repo_url           # Source code link
  }
}

# ── Apply tags to every resource ──────────────────────────
resource "aws_instance" "web_server" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  tags = merge(local.common_tags, {
    Name = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-web"
    Role = "web-server"
  })
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "logs" {
  bucket = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}-logs"
  tags   = local.common_tags
}
Identifier Type Convention Example
Resources snake_case, descriptive, no type prefix web_server, allow_https
Variables snake_case, bool prefix enable_/is_ instance_type, enable_logging
Outputs snake_case, always add description vpc_id, private_subnet_ids
Tags PascalCase keys, centralized via locals Project, Environment, CostCenter
Locals snake_case, computed value hint common_tags, full_name

65.3 Version Pinning

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Version Constraints | Dependency Lock File

Pinning versions ensures reproducible builds and protects against breaking changes. Pin Terraform itself, all providers, and all modules to prevent unexpected upgrades.

💻 Practical Examples

65.3.1 Terraform Version

Always constrain the Terraform CLI version in required_version. This prevents team members from accidentally running an incompatible version.

Example: Terraform Version Constraints
# ── versions.tf ──────────────────────────────────────────
terraform {
  # ✅ Pessimistic constraint: allows 1.5.x patches only
  required_version = "~> 1.5.0"

  # ✅ Range constraint: any 1.x version ≥ 1.5
  # required_version = ">= 1.5.0, < 2.0.0"

  # ❌ BAD: No constraint — any version can run
  # required_version = ""  # DON'T DO THIS!

  # ❌ BAD: Too loose — allows major version jumps
  # required_version = ">= 1.0"
}

# ── Common version constraint operators ──────────────────
# =  1.5.3    → Exact version only
# != 1.5.3    → Anything except this version
# >  1.5.0    → Greater than
# >= 1.5.0    → Greater than or equal
# <  2.0.0    → Less than
# ~> 1.5.0    → Pessimistic (>= 1.5.0, < 1.6.0)
# ~> 1.5      → Pessimistic (>= 1.5.0, < 2.0.0)
65.3.2 Provider Versions

Pin every provider to a pessimistic constraint. Commit .terraform.lock.hcl to version control so all team members use identical provider builds.

Example: Provider Version Pinning
# ── versions.tf ──────────────────────────────────────────
terraform {
  required_version = "~> 1.5.0"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.31"   # Allows 5.31.x patches
    }
    random = {
      source  = "hashicorp/random"
      version = "~> 3.6"    # Allows 3.6.x patches
    }
    tls = {
      source  = "hashicorp/tls"
      version = ">= 4.0, < 5.0"  # Range constraint
    }
  }
}

# ── Lock file management ────────────────────────────────
# .terraform.lock.hcl is auto-generated on `terraform init`
# ✅ DO: Commit .terraform.lock.hcl to version control
# ✅ DO: Run `terraform init -upgrade` to update providers
# ❌ DON'T: Delete .terraform.lock.hcl
# ❌ DON'T: Add .terraform.lock.hcl to .gitignore
65.3.3 Module Versions

Pin module versions from the Terraform Registry and Git sources. Never use an unversioned module in production.

Example: Module Version Pinning
# ── Registry modules: use version constraint ─────────────
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.5"   # ✅ Pinned to 5.5.x patches
}

# ── Git modules: use ref for specific tag/commit ─────────
module "custom_module" {
  source = "git::https://github.com/company/terraform-modules.git//vpc?ref=v2.1.0"
  #                                                              ✅ Pinned to tag
}

# ── Local modules: no version needed (same repo) ─────────
module "networking" {
  source = "../../modules/networking"   # Versioned with repo
}

# ❌ BAD: Unversioned registry module
module "vpc_bad" {
  source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  # No version = latest = BREAKING CHANGES RISK!
}

# ❌ BAD: Unversioned git source
module "risky" {
  source = "git::https://github.com/company/modules.git//vpc"
  # No ref = default branch HEAD = unpredictable!
}
Component Where to Pin Recommended Constraint
Terraform CLI required_version ~> 1.5.0
Providers required_providers ~> 5.31
Registry Modules version argument ~> 5.5
Git Modules ?ref= parameter ref=v2.1.0 (tag)
Lock File .terraform.lock.hcl Always commit to VCS

65.4 State Management Best Practices

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: State | Remote State

State is the heart of Terraform. Proper state management prevents data loss, enables team collaboration, and ensures infrastructure consistency. Never use local state in production.

💻 Practical Examples

65.4.1 Remote State

Always use a remote backend for team environments. Remote state enables collaboration, prevents conflicts, and provides durability.

Example: S3 Backend with All Best Practices
# ── versions.tf ──────────────────────────────────────────
terraform {
  required_version = "~> 1.5.0"

  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "company-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/networking/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"   # State locking
    encrypt        = true                 # Encryption at rest
    kms_key_id     = "alias/terraform-state"
  }
}

# ── Alternative: Terraform Cloud backend ─────────────────
# terraform {
#   cloud {
#     organization = "my-company"
#     workspaces { name = "prod-networking" }
#   }
# }
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    STATE ISOLATION PATTERN                        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   S3 Bucket: company-terraform-state                             │
│   ├── dev/                                                       │
│   │   ├── networking/terraform.tfstate    ← Dev network          │
│   │   ├── compute/terraform.tfstate       ← Dev compute          │
│   │   └── database/terraform.tfstate      ← Dev database         │
│   ├── staging/                                                   │
│   │   ├── networking/terraform.tfstate                           │
│   │   ├── compute/terraform.tfstate                              │
│   │   └── database/terraform.tfstate                             │
│   └── prod/                                                      │
│       ├── networking/terraform.tfstate                           │
│       ├── compute/terraform.tfstate                              │
│       └── database/terraform.tfstate                             │
│                                                                  │
│   Benefits:                                                      │
│   • Dev changes can't affect prod state                          │
│   • Smaller state = faster operations                            │
│   • Independent locking per component                            │
│   • Granular access control via IAM                              │
│                                                                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
65.4.2 State Locking

State locking prevents concurrent operations from corrupting state. DynamoDB provides locking for S3 backends; Terraform Cloud has built-in locking.

Example: DynamoDB Lock Table Setup
# ── Create the lock table (run once, manually or via separate state) ──
resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "terraform_locks" {
  name         = "terraform-locks"
  billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST"
  hash_key     = "LockID"

  attribute {
    name = "LockID"
    type = "S"
  }

  tags = {
    Name      = "Terraform State Lock Table"
    ManagedBy = "terraform"
  }
}

# ── If locked by a crashed process ───────────────────────
# terraform force-unlock LOCK_ID
# ⚠️ DANGER: Only use if you're SURE no other process is running!
65.4.3 State Encryption

State files contain sensitive data (passwords, keys, IPs). Always encrypt at rest and in transit.

Example: Encryption Configuration
# ── S3 backend with KMS encryption ───────────────────────
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket     = "company-terraform-state"
    key        = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region     = "us-east-1"
    encrypt    = true                          # SSE enabled
    kms_key_id = "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789012:key/abc-123"
  }
}

# ── S3 bucket policy: enforce encryption ─────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id

  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm     = "aws:kms"
      kms_master_key_id = aws_kms_key.terraform.arn
    }
    bucket_key_enabled = true
  }
}

# ── Block public access to state bucket ──────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "state" {
  bucket                  = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id
  block_public_acls       = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}
65.4.4 State Backup

Enable versioning on your state bucket so you can recover from accidental corruption or deletion.

Example: State Backup with S3 Versioning
# ── Enable versioning on state bucket ─────────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

# ── Lifecycle rule: keep 30 days of old versions ──────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration" "state" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.terraform_state.id

  rule {
    id     = "state-retention"
    status = "Enabled"

    noncurrent_version_expiration {
      noncurrent_days = 30
    }
  }
}
✅ Practice Priority
☐ Use remote backend (S3, GCS, Azure Blob, Terraform Cloud) 🔴 Critical
☐ Enable state locking (DynamoDB for S3 backend) 🔴 Critical
☐ Encrypt state at rest with KMS 🔴 Critical
☐ Enable S3 bucket versioning for state recovery 🟡 High
☐ Isolate state per environment and component 🟡 High
☐ Never store state in version control 🔴 Critical

65.5 Secret Handling

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Sensitive Variables | Vault Integration

Secrets must never appear in Terraform code, state, or version control. Use dedicated secret managers and mark variables as sensitive to prevent accidental exposure.

💻 Practical Examples

65.5.1 Never Commit Secrets

Secrets hardcoded in .tf files end up in state AND version control. Use .gitignore and pre-commit hooks to prevent accidental commits.

Example: Preventing Secret Leaks
# ── .gitignore: prevent secret files from being committed ─
*.tfvars           # Variable files may contain secrets
!*.auto.tfvars     # Exception: auto-loaded non-secret vars
*.tfstate          # State files contain ALL attribute values
*.tfstate.backup   # State backups too
.terraform/        # Provider binaries and cache

# ── Pre-commit hook to scan for secrets ──────────────────
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/Yelp/detect-secrets
    rev: v1.4.0
    hooks:
      - id: detect-secrets
        args: ['--baseline', '.secrets.baseline']
  - repo: https://github.com/trufflesecurity/trufflehog
    rev: v3.63.0
    hooks:
      - id: trufflehog
⚠️ Common Secret Leak Locations
  • terraform.tfstate — contains ALL resource attributes including passwords
  • plan files — contain intended changes including secret values
  • CI/CD logs — plan output may print secrets unless marked sensitive
  • PR comments — automated plan comments may expose values
65.5.2 Use Secret Managers

Retrieve secrets at apply-time from a dedicated secrets manager — never store them in Terraform configuration files.

Example: Secrets Manager Integration
# ── Option 1: AWS Secrets Manager ─────────────────────────
data "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_password" {
  secret_id = "prod/database/master-password"
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine               = "postgres"
  instance_class       = "db.t3.micro"
  username             = "admin"
  password             = data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db_password.secret_string
  skip_final_snapshot  = true
}

# ── Option 2: HashiCorp Vault ─────────────────────────────
data "vault_generic_secret" "db" {
  path = "secret/data/prod/database"
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  password = data.vault_generic_secret.db.data["password"]
}

# ── Option 3: Environment variables ──────────────────────
# Set: export TF_VAR_db_password="my-secret-password"
variable "db_password" {
  type      = string
  sensitive = true
}

resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  password = var.db_password  # Value comes from TF_VAR_
}
65.5.3 Sensitive Variables

Mark variables and outputs as sensitive = true to prevent them from appearing in CLI output and plan logs.

Example: Sensitive Data Handling
# ── Sensitive variable: redacted in CLI output ────────────
variable "api_key" {
  type        = string
  description = "API key for the monitoring service"
  sensitive   = true   # Redacted in plan/apply output
}

variable "db_password" {
  type        = string
  description = "Master password for the RDS instance"
  sensitive   = true
}

# ── Sensitive output: hidden in console ──────────────────
output "database_connection_string" {
  value       = "postgresql://${aws_db_instance.main.username}:${var.db_password}@${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}/mydb"
  description = "Full database connection string"
  sensitive   = true
}

# ── Using sensitive values in resources ──────────────────
resource "aws_ssm_parameter" "api_key" {
  name  = "/app/prod/api-key"
  type  = "SecureString"
  value = var.api_key   # Automatically treated as sensitive
}
✅ Secret Handling Practice Priority
☐ No secrets in .tf files committed to VCS 🔴 Critical
☐ Mark sensitive variables with sensitive = true 🔴 Critical
☐ Use Vault or Secrets Manager for production credentials 🟡 High
☐ Encrypt state at rest (S3 SSE / KMS) 🔴 Critical
☐ Use pre-commit hooks for secret scanning 🟡 High

65.6 Module Design Principles

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Module Development | Module Composition

Well-designed modules encapsulate infrastructure patterns, promote reuse, and enforce organizational standards. Follow these principles for modules that scale.

💻 Practical Examples

65.6.1 Single Responsibility

Each module should own one concern. Avoid "god modules" that create everything — compose smaller modules instead.

Example: Single Responsibility Modules
# ── ✅ GOOD: Focused modules, composed together ──────────
module "vpc" {
  source = "./modules/vpc"              # Only VPC resources
  cidr_block  = "10.0.0.0/16"
  environment = var.environment
}

module "security_groups" {
  source = "./modules/security-groups"   # Only SG resources
  vpc_id = module.vpc.vpc_id
}

module "compute" {
  source = "./modules/ec2-cluster"       # Only compute resources
  subnet_ids         = module.vpc.private_subnet_ids
  security_group_ids = [module.security_groups.app_sg_id]
}

# ── ❌ BAD: God module that does everything ──────────────
# module "infrastructure" {
#   source = "./modules/everything"   # VPC + SGs + EC2 + RDS + ALB
#   # 50+ variables, impossible to reuse partially
# }
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   MODULE COMPOSITION PATTERN                         │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Root Module (environments/prod/main.tf)                            │
│       │                                                              │
│       ├── module "vpc"           ─── modules/vpc/                    │
│       │   └── VPC, Subnets, Route Tables, NAT Gateway               │
│       │                                                              │
│       ├── module "security"      ─── modules/security-groups/        │
│       │   └── Security Groups, NACLs                                 │
│       │                                                              │
│       ├── module "compute"       ─── modules/ec2-cluster/            │
│       │   └── Launch Template, ASG, Instances                        │
│       │                                                              │
│       └── module "database"      ─── modules/rds/                    │
│           └── DB Instance, Subnet Group, Parameter Group             │
│                                                                      │
│   Each module:                                                       │
│   ✅ Has clear inputs (variables) and outputs                        │
│   ✅ Can be used independently                                       │
│   ✅ Has its own tests and documentation                             │
│   ✅ Follows semantic versioning                                     │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
65.6.2 Clear Interfaces

Module variables and outputs are its API. Every variable needs a type, description, and validation. Every output needs a description.

Example: Well-Defined Module Interface
# ── modules/vpc/variables.tf ─────────────────────────────
variable "cidr_block" {
  type        = string
  description = "CIDR block for the VPC (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16)"

  validation {
    condition     = can(cidrhost(var.cidr_block, 0))
    error_message = "Must be a valid CIDR block."
  }
}

variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Deployment environment: dev, staging, or prod"

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

variable "enable_nat_gateway" {
  type        = bool
  description = "Whether to create a NAT Gateway for private subnets"
  default     = true
}

# ── modules/vpc/outputs.tf ──────────────────────────────
output "vpc_id" {
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
  description = "The ID of the created VPC"
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  description = "List of private subnet IDs"
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
  description = "List of public subnet IDs"
}
65.6.3 Documentation

Every module must include a README, usage examples, and auto-generated variable/output docs via terraform-docs.

Example: Module Documentation with terraform-docs
# ── Standard module file structure ────────────────────────
modules/vpc/
├── main.tf           # Core resources
├── variables.tf      # Input variables with descriptions
├── outputs.tf        # Output values with descriptions
├── versions.tf       # Required providers and version
├── README.md         # Auto-generated + manual sections
├── examples/         # Example usage configurations
│   └── complete/
│       ├── main.tf
│       └── outputs.tf
└── tests/            # Terraform test files (1.6+)
    └── vpc_test.tftest.hcl

# ── Auto-generate docs ──────────────────────────────────
terraform-docs markdown table ./modules/vpc/ > ./modules/vpc/README.md

# ── Or inject into existing README between markers ──────
terraform-docs markdown table --output-file README.md ./modules/vpc/
# Injects between <!-- BEGIN_TF_DOCS --> and <!-- END_TF_DOCS -->
❌ Anti-Pattern Problem ✅ Better Approach
God module (does everything) Not reusable, hard to test Compose focused single-purpose modules
Provider blocks inside modules Blocks multi-region/account use Inherit provider from caller
Backend config in modules Conflicts with root backend Backend only in root modules
Deeply nested modules (>3 levels) Hard to debug and understand Flatten module hierarchy
No variable descriptions or types Users can't understand the API Type + description + validation

65.7 Documentation

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Module Documentation | terraform-docs

Good documentation reduces onboarding time, prevents misconfiguration, and makes modules self-service. Every variable, output, and module needs clear documentation.

💻 Practical Examples

65.7.1 README Files

Every module and root configuration must have a README.md with purpose, usage examples, requirements, and auto-generated inputs/outputs tables.

Example: Module README Template
# AWS VPC Module

## Purpose
Creates a production-ready VPC with public and private subnets,
NAT Gateways, and proper route table configuration.

## Usage
```hcl
module "vpc" {
  source  = "git::https://github.com/org/terraform-modules.git//vpc?ref=v2.1.0"

  cidr_block          = "10.0.0.0/16"
  availability_zones  = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
  environment         = "prod"
  enable_nat_gateway  = true
}
```

## Requirements
| Name      | Version  |
|-----------|----------|
| terraform | ~> 1.7   |
| aws       | ~> 5.30  |

<!-- BEGIN_TF_DOCS -->
(auto-generated by terraform-docs)
<!-- END_TF_DOCS -->

## Architecture
(include diagrams where helpful)
65.7.2 Variable Descriptions

Every variable must have a description that explains what it does, expected format, and any constraints — not just restates the name.

Example: Good vs Bad Variable Descriptions
# ── ❌ BAD: Description restates the name ────────────────
variable "instance_type" {
  type        = string
  description = "The instance type"   # Useless!
}

# ── ✅ GOOD: Description explains purpose and format ────
variable "instance_type" {
  type        = string
  description = "EC2 instance type for the application servers (e.g., t3.medium). Larger types increase cost but improve performance. See: https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/instance-types/"
  default     = "t3.medium"
}

# ── ✅ GOOD: Describes complex variable structure ───────
variable "subnet_config" {
  type = map(object({
    cidr_block        = string
    availability_zone = string
    public            = bool
  }))
  description = <<-EOT
    Map of subnet configurations keyed by subnet name.
    Each entry creates one subnet with the specified CIDR,
    AZ, and public/private designation.
    Example: { "web-1a" = { cidr_block = "10.0.1.0/24", availability_zone = "us-east-1a", public = true } }
  EOT
}
65.7.3 Output Descriptions

Outputs are the module's return values. Each must describe what it returns, its format, and when it might be empty or null.

Example: Well-Documented Outputs
# ── outputs.tf ──────────────────────────────────────────
output "vpc_id" {
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
  description = "The ID of the VPC (e.g., vpc-0abc123def456). Use this to associate other resources with this VPC."
}

output "private_subnet_ids" {
  value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
  description = "List of private subnet IDs, one per AZ. Empty list if no private subnets are configured. Order matches the availability_zones input variable."
}

output "nat_gateway_ip" {
  value       = var.enable_nat_gateway ? aws_eip.nat[0].public_ip : null
  description = "Public IP of the NAT Gateway. Null if enable_nat_gateway is false. Use this IP for allowlisting in external firewalls."
}
65.7.4 Inline Comments

Use comments to explain why, not what. Document workarounds, business rules, and non-obvious decisions.

Example: Effective Inline Comments
# ── ❌ BAD: Comment restates the code ────────────────────
# Create an S3 bucket
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "logs" {     # Obviously creating a bucket!
  bucket = "company-logs"
}

# ── ✅ GOOD: Comment explains WHY ───────────────────────
# Separate bucket for ALB access logs — required by AWS to be in
# the same region and owned by the ELB service account.
# See: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/load-balancer-access-logs.html
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "alb_logs" {
  bucket = "${var.project}-alb-logs-${var.environment}"
}

# WORKAROUND: AWS provider v5.30 has a bug where lifecycle rules
# aren't applied on first create. We use a separate resource
# instead of inline configuration until hashicorp/terraform-provider-aws#12345 is fixed.
resource "aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration" "logs" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.alb_logs.id

  rule {
    id     = "expire-old-logs"
    status = "Enabled"

    # Compliance requires 90 days retention; 180 days for audit logs
    expiration {
      days = var.environment == "prod" ? 180 : 90
    }
  }
}
Documentation Type Where What to Include
README.md Every module root Purpose, usage, requirements, examples
Variable descriptions variables.tf Purpose, format, constraints, examples
Output descriptions outputs.tf What it returns, format, null conditions
Inline comments *.tf files WHY decisions, workarounds, business rules
CHANGELOG.md Module root Version history, breaking changes

65.8 Testing Strategy

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Test Framework | terraform test Command

A layered testing strategy catches errors at each stage — from code formatting to real infrastructure validation. Automate as much as possible and fail fast.

💻 Practical Examples

65.8.1 Pre-Commit Hooks

Catch issues before they reach the repository. Use pre-commit to run formatters, validators, and linters locally on every commit.

Example: Pre-Commit Configuration for Terraform
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform
    rev: v1.86.0
    hooks:
      # ── Formatting ──────────────────────────────────────
      - id: terraform_fmt

      # ── Validation ──────────────────────────────────────
      - id: terraform_validate
        args:
          - --hook-config=--retry-once-with-cleanup=true

      # ── Linting ─────────────────────────────────────────
      - id: terraform_tflint
        args:
          - --args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.tflint.hcl

      # ── Security scanning ──────────────────────────────
      - id: terraform_tfsec

      # ── Auto-generate docs ─────────────────────────────
      - id: terraform_docs
        args:
          - --args=--config=.terraform-docs.yml

  - repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks
    rev: v4.5.0
    hooks:
      - id: trailing-whitespace
      - id: end-of-file-fixer
      - id: check-merge-conflict
Example: Install and Run Pre-Commit
# ── Install pre-commit ───────────────────────────────────
pip install pre-commit

# ── Install hooks in repo ───────────────────────────────
pre-commit install

# ── Run against all files (first time) ──────────────────
pre-commit run --all-files

# ── Now hooks run automatically on every git commit ─────
git commit -m "feat: add new VPC module"
# Automatically runs: fmt → validate → tflint → tfsec → docs
65.8.2 CI Testing

Run static analysis and plan-only tests in CI on every pull request. These tests are fast, free, and catch most issues.

Example: CI Test Pipeline
# .github/workflows/terraform-test.yml
name: "Terraform Tests"

on:
  pull_request:
    paths: ['modules/**', 'environments/**']

jobs:
  static-analysis:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Format Check
        run: terraform fmt -check -recursive

      - name: Validate All Modules
        run: |
          for dir in modules/*/; do
            echo "=== Validating $dir ==="
            terraform -chdir="$dir" init -backend=false
            terraform -chdir="$dir" validate
          done

      - name: TFLint
        uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@v4
        with:
          tflint_version: latest
      - run: tflint --recursive

      - name: Security Scan (tfsec)
        uses: aquasecurity/tfsec-action@v1.0.3

  unit-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Run Terraform Tests (plan-only)
        run: |
          for dir in modules/*/; do
            if [ -d "$dir/tests" ]; then
              echo "=== Testing $dir ==="
              terraform -chdir="$dir" init -backend=false
              terraform -chdir="$dir" test
            fi
          done
65.8.3 Integration Testing

Integration tests deploy real infrastructure in an isolated account, verify behavior, then tear it down. Run on merge or on a schedule.

Example: Native Terraform Integration Test
# tests/vpc_integration.tftest.hcl
# ── This test creates REAL infrastructure ────────────────

variables {
  environment = "test"
  cidr_block  = "10.99.0.0/16"
}

run "deploy_vpc" {
  command = apply   # Creates real resources

  assert {
    condition     = aws_vpc.main.state == "available"
    error_message = "VPC was not created successfully"
  }

  assert {
    condition     = length(aws_subnet.private) == 3
    error_message = "Expected 3 private subnets, got ${length(aws_subnet.private)}"
  }
}

run "verify_connectivity" {
  command = apply
  module {
    source = "./tests/connectivity-check"
  }

  variables {
    vpc_id     = run.deploy_vpc.aws_vpc.main.id
    subnet_ids = run.deploy_vpc.aws_subnet.private[*].id
  }

  assert {
    condition     = output.connectivity_test_passed == true
    error_message = "Subnets cannot reach NAT Gateway"
  }
}
# Resources are automatically destroyed after all runs complete
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TESTING PYRAMID FOR TERRAFORM                     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│                        ┌─────────┐                                   │
│                        │  E2E /  │  ← Smoke tests against           │
│                        │  Smoke  │    deployed infrastructure        │
│                       ╱└─────────┘╲   (slowest, most expensive)      │
│                      ╱─────────────╲                                 │
│                     │  Integration  │  ← terraform test (apply)      │
│                     │    Tests      │    Real infra, isolated acct    │
│                    ╱└───────────────┘╲                                │
│                   ╱───────────────────╲                               │
│                  │    Unit Tests       │  ← terraform test (plan)     │
│                  │   (Plan-Only)       │    No real infra              │
│                 ╱└─────────────────────┘╲                             │
│                ╱─────────────────────────╲                            │
│               │    Static Analysis        │  ← fmt, validate, tflint │
│               │   (Pre-Commit + CI)       │    tfsec, checkov         │
│               └───────────────────────────┘    (fastest, cheapest)    │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

65.9 CI/CD Best Practices

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Automation Tutorials | Plan Output Files

Automate Terraform workflows with CI/CD pipelines that enforce code review, run tests, and provide safe, repeatable, auditable deployments across environments.

💻 Practical Examples

65.9.1 Plan on PR

Run terraform plan on every pull request and post the output as a PR comment so reviewers can see exactly what will change.

Example: Plan on Pull Request
# .github/workflows/terraform-plan.yml
name: "Terraform Plan"

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['infrastructure/**']

permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write
  id-token: write

jobs:
  plan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: infrastructure/
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: 1.7.0

      - name: Configure AWS (OIDC — no long-lived keys)
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init

      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -no-color -out=plan.bin 2>&1 | tee plan.txt

      - name: Post Plan to PR
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const plan = fs.readFileSync('infrastructure/plan.txt', 'utf8');
            const truncated = plan.length > 60000
              ? plan.substring(0, 60000) + '\n... (truncated)'
              : plan;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: '### Terraform Plan\n```\n' + truncated + '\n```'
            });
65.9.2 Apply with Approval

Never auto-apply to production. Require manual approval, apply the exact saved plan, and use OIDC for authentication.

Example: Apply with Manual Approval Gate
# .github/workflows/terraform-apply.yml
name: "Terraform Apply"

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths: ['infrastructure/**']

jobs:
  plan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      has_changes: ${{ steps.plan.outputs.has_changes }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Plan
        id: plan
        run: |
          terraform init
          terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=plan.bin || echo "has_changes=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Upload Plan Artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: terraform-plan
          path: infrastructure/plan.bin

  apply:
    needs: plan
    if: needs.plan.outputs.has_changes == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production        # ← Requires manual approval!
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3

      - name: Download Plan
        uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: terraform-plan

      - name: Apply EXACT Reviewed Plan
        run: terraform apply plan.bin   # ← Same plan that was reviewed
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    CI/CD PIPELINE FLOW                                │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Pull Request                     Push to main                      │
│       │                                │                             │
│       ▼                                ▼                             │
│   ┌──────────┐                   ┌──────────┐                        │
│   │ fmt      │                   │ Plan     │                        │
│   │ validate │                   │ (saved)  │                        │
│   │ tflint   │                   └────┬─────┘                        │
│   │ tfsec    │                        ▼                              │
│   │ plan     │──── Comment ──►   ┌──────────┐                        │
│   └──────────┘     to PR         │ Approval │  ← Manual gate        │
│                                  │  (GATE)  │    for production      │
│                                  └────┬─────┘                        │
│                                       ▼                              │
│                                  ┌──────────┐                        │
│                                  │  Apply   │  ← Uses saved plan     │
│                                  │ plan.bin │                        │
│                                  └────┬─────┘                        │
│                                       ▼                              │
│                                  ┌──────────┐                        │
│                                  │  Verify  │  ← Smoke tests         │
│                                  └──────────┘                        │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
65.9.3 Environment Separation

Promote changes through environments (dev → staging → prod) with separate state files, workspaces, or directory structures.

Example: Environment-Separated Pipeline
# ── Directory-based environment separation ───────────────
# environments/
# ├── dev/
# │   ├── main.tf         → references shared modules
# │   ├── terraform.tfvars → dev-specific values
# │   └── backend.tf      → dev state bucket
# ├── staging/
# │   ├── main.tf
# │   ├── terraform.tfvars
# │   └── backend.tf      → staging state bucket
# └── prod/
#     ├── main.tf
#     ├── terraform.tfvars
#     └── backend.tf      → prod state bucket (separate account)

# ── Each environment has its own backend ─────────────────
# environments/dev/backend.tf
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "company-tfstate-dev"
    key            = "infrastructure/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks-dev"
  }
}

# environments/prod/backend.tf
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "company-tfstate-prod"
    key            = "infrastructure/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks-prod"
    # Prod state in a separate, locked-down AWS account
  }
}
Practice Why Priority
Save plan with -out=plan.bin Apply exactly what was reviewed 🔴 Critical
Use OIDC authentication No long-lived credentials in CI 🔴 Critical
Manual approval for production Human gate prevents accidents 🔴 Critical
Post plan output to PR Reviewers see infrastructure changes 🟡 High
Separate state per environment Blast radius isolation 🟡 High
Run cost estimation (Infracost) Catch cost surprises before merge 🟢 Moderate

65.10 Team Collaboration

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Cloud Workspaces | Remote State

Effective team collaboration requires clear ownership, structured reviews, and standardized processes. Define who owns what, how changes are reviewed, and what information PRs must include.

💻 Practical Examples

65.10.1 Code Review Process

Every Terraform change needs a code review. Reviewers should check the plan output, not just the code diff — the plan shows the real impact.

Example: Terraform Code Review Checklist
## Terraform Code Review Checklist

### Plan Review (MOST IMPORTANT)
- [ ] Plan output is posted on the PR
- [ ] No unexpected resource DESTROYS (check red lines!)
- [ ] No changes to resources outside the PR scope
- [ ] Resource count matches expectations

### Code Review
- [ ] Variables have type, description, and validation
- [ ] No hardcoded values (use variables or data sources)
- [ ] No secrets in code (check for keys, passwords, tokens)
- [ ] Provider and module versions are pinned
- [ ] Tags follow organizational standards
- [ ] Resources use consistent naming convention

### Best Practices
- [ ] State is not modified manually
- [ ] No `terraform import` without documentation
- [ ] Lifecycle rules used where appropriate (prevent_destroy)
- [ ] Count/for_each used correctly (prefer for_each)

### Documentation
- [ ] README updated if module interface changed
- [ ] CHANGELOG updated for module version bumps
- [ ] Inline comments explain non-obvious decisions
65.10.2 CODEOWNERS

Use GitHub CODEOWNERS to automatically assign reviewers based on file paths. Infrastructure changes should require approval from the platform team.

Example: CODEOWNERS for Terraform
# .github/CODEOWNERS

# ── Global infrastructure ownership ─────────────────────
# All Terraform files require platform team review
*.tf                          @org/platform-team
*.tfvars                      @org/platform-team

# ── Module-specific ownership ───────────────────────────
# Networking modules owned by network team
modules/vpc/                  @org/network-team
modules/security-groups/      @org/network-team
modules/transit-gateway/      @org/network-team

# Database modules owned by data team
modules/rds/                  @org/data-team
modules/dynamodb/             @org/data-team

# ── Environment-specific ownership ──────────────────────
# Production requires senior engineer approval
environments/prod/            @org/platform-leads
environments/staging/         @org/platform-team
environments/dev/             @org/developers

# ── CI/CD pipeline changes require DevOps review ────────
.github/workflows/terraform*  @org/devops-team

# ── IAM and security changes require security review ────
modules/iam/                  @org/security-team
**/iam_*.tf                   @org/security-team
65.10.3 PR Templates

Standardize pull request descriptions so every infrastructure change includes context, plan output, and a risk assessment.

Example: Terraform PR Template
<!-- .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/terraform.md -->

## Infrastructure Change

### Description
<!-- What does this change do and why? -->

### Type of Change
- [ ] New resource(s)
- [ ] Modify existing resource(s)
- [ ] Destroy resource(s)
- [ ] Module update/refactor
- [ ] Provider/Terraform version upgrade

### Environments Affected
- [ ] dev
- [ ] staging
- [ ] production

### Risk Assessment
- **Blast radius:** Low / Medium / High
- **Rollback plan:** <describe how to undo if needed>
- **Downtime expected:** None / Brief / Extended

### Plan Output
<!-- Paste or link to terraform plan output -->
```
<plan output here>
```

### Checklist
- [ ] Plan output reviewed and attached
- [ ] No secrets in code
- [ ] Version constraints updated (if applicable)
- [ ] Documentation updated
- [ ] Tested in dev/staging first
Collaboration Practice Tool / File Purpose
Code review checklist Team wiki / PR template Consistent, thorough reviews
CODEOWNERS .github/CODEOWNERS Auto-assign reviewers by file path
PR templates .github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE/ Standardize change descriptions
Branch protection GitHub settings Require approvals before merge
Remote state locking DynamoDB / Cloud backend Prevent concurrent modifications
📏 🎨 📐 ✍️
CHAPTER 66

Style Guide and Conventions

Formatting Naming Standards Code Review
7Sections
18Subsections
40+Rules
📏Standards
PART 17 ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

Consistent coding style makes Terraform configurations readable, maintainable, and reviewable. This chapter defines naming conventions, formatting rules, comment standards, and a comprehensive code review checklist for teams.

Learning Objectives

  • Organize Terraform files with standard naming and ordering
  • Apply consistent formatting with terraform fmt and alignment rules
  • Follow snake_case naming conventions for resources, variables, and outputs
  • Define variable and output standards for type safety and documentation
  • Implement a structured code review process for security, style, and logic

Chapter Contents

66.1File Organization 66.2Formatting 66.3Resource Naming 66.4Variable Standards 66.5Output Standards 66.6Comment Standards 66.7Code Review Checklist

66.1 File Organization

CONVENTION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Standard Module Structure | Override Files

Consistent file organization makes Terraform projects navigable at a glance. Follow the standard module structure and use predictable file names so every team member knows where to find configuration, variables, and outputs.

💻 Practical Examples

66.1.1 Standard File Names

Use these canonical file names in every module and root configuration.

File Name Purpose Required?
main.tf Primary resource definitions and data sources ✅ Yes
variables.tf All input variable declarations ✅ Yes
outputs.tf All output value declarations ✅ Yes
versions.tf terraform { } block with version constraints ✅ Yes
providers.tf Provider configurations (root modules only) 🔶 Recommended
backend.tf Backend configuration (root modules only) 🔶 Recommended
locals.tf Local value definitions ⚪ Optional
data.tf Data source queries (when many exist) ⚪ Optional
terraform.tfvars Default variable values (not committed for secrets) ⚪ Optional
66.1.2 File Ordering

Within each file, organize blocks in a consistent order for readability.

Example: Recommended Block Order in main.tf
# ── 1. Local values (computed from variables) ────────────
locals {
  common_tags = {
    Project     = var.project_name
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }
}

# ── 2. Data sources (read existing resources) ────────────
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

# ── 3. Resources (grouped by logical component) ─────────
# --- Networking ---
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr
  tags       = merge(local.common_tags, { Name = "${var.project_name}-vpc" })
}

resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count             = length(var.private_subnets)
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = var.private_subnets[count.index]
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
}

# --- Compute ---
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.private[0].id
  tags          = local.common_tags
}
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   STANDARD MODULE STRUCTURE                          │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   modules/                                                           │
│   └── vpc/                                                           │
│       ├── README.md           ← Module documentation                 │
│       ├── main.tf             ← Resource definitions                 │
│       ├── variables.tf        ← Input variables                      │
│       ├── outputs.tf          ← Output values                        │
│       ├── versions.tf         ← Required providers & version         │
│       ├── locals.tf           ← Computed local values                │
│       └── examples/                                                  │
│           └── simple/                                                │
│               ├── main.tf     ← Example usage                        │
│               └── outputs.tf  ← Example outputs                     │
│                                                                      │
│   environments/                                                      │
│   ├── dev/                                                           │
│   │   ├── main.tf             ← Module calls                         │
│   │   ├── variables.tf        ← Env-specific variables               │
│   │   ├── outputs.tf          ← Env-specific outputs                 │
│   │   ├── backend.tf          ← Remote state config                  │
│   │   ├── providers.tf        ← Provider config                      │
│   │   └── terraform.tfvars    ← Env-specific values                  │
│   ├── staging/                                                       │
│   └── prod/                                                          │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

66.2 Formatting

CONVENTION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: terraform fmt | Style Conventions

Consistent formatting eliminates bikeshedding in code reviews and makes diffs cleaner. Always run terraform fmt and follow HCL style conventions for indentation, alignment, and spacing.

💻 Practical Examples

66.2.1 terraform fmt

The canonical formatting tool — always run it before committing code.

Example: Using terraform fmt
# ── Format all files in current directory ────────────────
terraform fmt

# ── Format recursively (all subdirectories) ──────────────
terraform fmt -recursive

# ── Check formatting without changing files (CI) ────────
terraform fmt -check -recursive
# Exit code 0 = formatted, 3 = needs formatting

# ── Show diff of formatting changes ─────────────────────
terraform fmt -diff

# ── Pre-commit hook for auto-format ──────────────────────
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
# repos:
#   - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-tf
#     rev: v1.83.5
#     hooks:
#       - id: terraform_fmt
66.2.2 Indentation

HCL uses 2-space indentation. Never use tabs.

Example: Correct Indentation
# ✅ CORRECT — 2-space indentation
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  root_block_device {
    volume_size = 20
    volume_type = "gp3"
    encrypted   = true
  }

  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"
  }
}

# ❌ WRONG — 4-space or tab indentation
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
    ami           = var.ami_id        # 4 spaces — wrong
    instance_type = var.instance_type

    root_block_device {
        volume_size = 20              # 8 spaces — wrong
    }
}
66.2.3 Alignment

Align equals signs within a block for readability. terraform fmt does this automatically.

Example: Argument Alignment
# ✅ CORRECT — aligned equals signs within blocks
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "https" {
  type              = "ingress"
  from_port         = 443
  to_port           = 443
  protocol          = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks       = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.web.id
}

# ✅ CORRECT — maps and tags also aligned
locals {
  common_tags = {
    Project     = var.project_name
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    Team        = var.team_name
  }
}

# ❌ WRONG — inconsistent alignment
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "https" {
  type = "ingress"
  from_port = 443
  to_port = 443
  protocol = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
  security_group_id = aws_security_group.web.id
}

66.3 Resource Naming

CONVENTION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Resource Syntax | Style Conventions

Resource names are the internal labels you use in Terraform code (not the cloud resource names). Use snake_case, be descriptive, and follow consistent patterns across your entire codebase.

💻 Practical Examples

66.3.1 Snake_case Convention

All Terraform identifiers must use snake_case — lowercase letters and underscores only.

Example: Naming Do's and Don'ts
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# RESOURCE NAMES (Terraform internal labels)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ✅ CORRECT — snake_case, descriptive
resource "aws_instance" "web_server" { }
resource "aws_security_group" "alb_ingress" { }
resource "aws_db_instance" "primary_postgres" { }
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "application_logs" { }

# ❌ WRONG — camelCase
resource "aws_instance" "webServer" { }

# ❌ WRONG — PascalCase
resource "aws_instance" "WebServer" { }

# ❌ WRONG — kebab-case (hyphens not allowed)
resource "aws_instance" "web-server" { }

# ❌ WRONG — too generic
resource "aws_instance" "instance" { }
resource "aws_instance" "this" { }  # Only OK if single resource

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SPECIAL CASE: Single resource of its type → use "this"
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ✅ OK when there's only ONE of this resource type
resource "aws_vpc" "this" {
  cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr
}

# ✅ But use a descriptive name when there are multiples
resource "aws_subnet" "public" { }
resource "aws_subnet" "private" { }
66.3.2 Descriptive Names

Resource names should describe what the resource does, not repeat the resource type.

❌ Bad Name ✅ Good Name Reason
aws_instance.instance aws_instance.web_server Describes the role, not the type
aws_s3_bucket.bucket aws_s3_bucket.access_logs Explains the bucket's purpose
aws_security_group.sg aws_security_group.web_ingress Clarifies which traffic is allowed
aws_iam_role.role aws_iam_role.lambda_execution Identifies what assumes the role
aws_subnet.subnet1 aws_subnet.private Number-suffixes are meaningless
aws_db_instance.db aws_db_instance.orders_primary Names the data domain + role

66.4 Variable Standards

CONVENTION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Input Variables | Validation Rules

Variables are the public API of your module. Define them with clear types, helpful descriptions, sensible defaults, and validation rules to make your modules self-documenting and error-resistant.

💻 Practical Examples

66.4.1 Naming Pattern

Variable names should be clear, descriptive, and follow a consistent pattern.

Example: Variable Naming Conventions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# VARIABLE NAMING PATTERNS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ✅ Pattern: {resource}_{attribute}
variable "vpc_cidr" { }
variable "instance_type" { }
variable "db_engine_version" { }
variable "alb_idle_timeout" { }

# ✅ Pattern: enable_{feature} for booleans
variable "enable_dns_hostnames" { }
variable "enable_encryption" { }
variable "enable_monitoring" { }

# ✅ Pattern: {resource}_{attribute}_list for lists
variable "private_subnet_cidrs" { }
variable "allowed_ip_ranges" { }

# ✅ Pattern: {noun} for simple, obvious values
variable "environment" { }
variable "project_name" { }
variable "region" { }

# ❌ WRONG — abbreviated or unclear
variable "vpc_c" { }        # What is "c"?
variable "inst_t" { }       # Unreadable
variable "flag1" { }        # Meaningless
variable "enableDNS" { }    # camelCase not allowed
66.4.2 Type Specification

Always specify explicit types — never leave variables untyped.

Example: Complete Variable Definitions
# ── String variable with validation ──────────────────────
variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment (dev, staging, prod)"
  type        = string

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Environment must be one of: dev, staging, prod."
  }
}

# ── Number variable with range validation ────────────────
variable "instance_count" {
  description = "Number of EC2 instances to create"
  type        = number
  default     = 2

  validation {
    condition     = var.instance_count >= 1 && var.instance_count <= 10
    error_message = "Instance count must be between 1 and 10."
  }
}

# ── Boolean variable ────────────────────────────────────
variable "enable_encryption" {
  description = "Enable encryption at rest for all storage resources"
  type        = bool
  default     = true
}

# ── Complex type (object) ───────────────────────────────
variable "database_config" {
  description = "Database configuration parameters"
  type = object({
    engine         = string
    engine_version = string
    instance_class = string
    storage_gb     = number
    multi_az       = bool
  })
  default = {
    engine         = "postgres"
    engine_version = "15.4"
    instance_class = "db.t3.medium"
    storage_gb     = 100
    multi_az       = false
  }
}

# ── Map variable ────────────────────────────────────────
variable "extra_tags" {
  description = "Additional tags to apply to all resources"
  type        = map(string)
  default     = {}
}
66.4.3 Default Values

Guidelines for when to use defaults and when to require explicit values.

Scenario Default? Rationale
Environment name ❌ No default Must be explicitly set per deployment
Region ❌ No default Highly deployment-specific
Instance type ✅ Default (t3.micro) Safe starting point, overridden per env
Enable encryption ✅ Default (true) Secure by default
Extra tags ✅ Default ({}) Optional enrichment, empty is safe
Database password ❌ No default Secrets must never have defaults
CIDR blocks ❌ No default Network ranges are deployment-specific

66.5 Output Standards

CONVENTION
📚 Official Documentation: Output Values | Sensitive Outputs

Outputs are the public interface of your module — they define what information is exposed to callers. Follow consistent naming patterns, always include descriptions, and mark sensitive values appropriately.

💻 Practical Examples

66.5.1 Naming Pattern

Output names should follow {resource}_{attribute} pattern, matching what the consumer expects.

Example: Output Naming Conventions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# OUTPUT NAMING — modules/vpc/outputs.tf
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ✅ Pattern: {resource}_{attribute}
output "vpc_id" {
  description = "The ID of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.id
}

output "vpc_cidr_block" {
  description = "The CIDR block of the VPC"
  value       = aws_vpc.main.cidr_block
}

# ✅ Pattern: {resource}_{attribute}_ids (for lists)
output "private_subnet_ids" {
  description = "List of private subnet IDs"
  value       = aws_subnet.private[*].id
}

output "public_subnet_ids" {
  description = "List of public subnet IDs"
  value       = aws_subnet.public[*].id
}

# ✅ Pattern: {resource}_{attribute} with sensitive flag
output "db_connection_string" {
  description = "PostgreSQL connection string"
  value       = "postgresql://${aws_db_instance.main.endpoint}/${aws_db_instance.main.db_name}"
  sensitive   = true
}

# ❌ WRONG — no description
output "id" {
  value = aws_vpc.main.id
}

# ❌ WRONG — too generic
output "result" {
  value = aws_instance.web.public_ip
}
66.5.2 Descriptions

Every output MUST have a description. This is used by terraform-docs and helps consumers understand what they're getting.

✅

Good Descriptions

  • "The ID of the VPC"
  • "List of private subnet IDs for use in ECS task placement"
  • "ARN of the KMS key used for S3 encryption"
  • "DNS name of the ALB (use for CNAME records)"
❌

Bad Descriptions

  • "The id" — obvious repetition
  • "Output" — says nothing
  • "" — empty string (worst)
  • "See docs" — unhelpful

66.6 Comment Standards

CONVENTION
📚 Official Documentation: HCL Comments | Style Conventions

Comments should explain why, not what. Terraform code should be self-documenting through descriptive names and types; comments add context that code alone cannot convey.

💻 Practical Examples

66.6.1 Block Comments

Use block comments to separate logical sections of a file and explain design decisions.

Example: Section Separators and Design Comments
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# NETWORKING — VPC, Subnets, Route Tables
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Create a VPC with DNS support enabled.
# DNS hostnames are required for EKS cluster endpoint resolution.
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = var.vpc_cidr
  enable_dns_support   = true
  enable_dns_hostnames = true  # Required by EKS
}

# ── Private Subnets ──────────────────────────────────────
# Spread across 3 AZs for high availability.
# These subnets have no direct internet access — outbound
# traffic goes through the NAT Gateway.
resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count             = 3
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, count.index)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
}

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# SECURITY — Security Groups, NACLs
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
66.6.2 Inline Comments

Use inline comments sparingly — only for non-obvious arguments or workarounds.

Example: When to Use Inline Comments
# ✅ GOOD — explains a non-obvious value
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "web" {
  min_size         = 2
  max_size         = 10
  desired_capacity = 3  # Keep odd number for quorum-based health checks

  health_check_grace_period = 300  # 5 min — app needs ~3 min to boot
  health_check_type         = "ELB"
}

# ✅ GOOD — documents a workaround
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "extra" {
  role       = aws_iam_role.lambda.name
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess"
  # WORKAROUND: Lambda needs S3 access for config files
  # until we migrate config to Parameter Store (JIRA-1234)
}

# ❌ BAD — states the obvious
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id       # Set the AMI
  instance_type = "t3.micro"      # Use t3.micro instance type
  tags = {
    Name = "web-server"           # Tag with name web-server
  }
}
66.6.3 TODO Comments

Use TODO, FIXME, and HACK tags for actionable items. Include a ticket reference when possible.

Example: Actionable Comment Tags
# TODO(JIRA-5678): Replace hardcoded CIDR with data source
#   lookup once the shared-infra module exposes it as output.
resource "aws_security_group_rule" "vpn" {
  type        = "ingress"
  from_port   = 443
  to_port     = 443
  protocol    = "tcp"
  cidr_blocks = ["10.0.0.0/8"]  # Corporate VPN range
}

# FIXME: This IAM policy is too broad — restrict to specific
#   S3 buckets after migration is complete (JIRA-9012).
resource "aws_iam_role_policy" "broad_access" {
  role   = aws_iam_role.app.id
  policy = data.aws_iam_policy_document.s3_full.json
}

# HACK: AWS provider bug — must create and then import
#   the default security group. Remove after provider v6.0.
resource "aws_default_security_group" "default" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
}
Tag Meaning Example
TODO Planned improvement, not urgent # TODO(JIRA-123): Add monitoring
FIXME Known issue, needs fix # FIXME: Policy too broad
HACK Workaround for external issue # HACK: Provider bug workaround
NOTE Important context for readers # NOTE: Order matters here

66.7 Code Review Checklist

BEST PRACTICE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Module Development | Well-Architected Framework

A structured code review checklist ensures every Terraform pull request is evaluated for security vulnerabilities, style consistency, and logical correctness before it reaches production.

💻 Practical Examples

66.7.1 Security Review

The most critical part of any Terraform code review — check for exposed secrets, overly permissive access, and missing encryption.

🔒 Security Review Checklist
  • ☐ No hardcoded secrets — passwords, API keys, tokens must come from Vault/SSM/environment
  • ☐ Least privilege IAM — no * actions or * resources unless justified
  • ☐ Encryption at rest — S3, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB all encrypted
  • ☐ Encryption in transit — TLS/SSL enforced, no HTTP listeners
  • ☐ No public access — S3 buckets ACL disabled, security groups restricted
  • ☐ Sensitive outputs — marked with sensitive = true
  • ☐ No 0.0.0.0/0 ingress rules — unless behind ALB/CloudFront
  • ☐ Logging enabled — CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, access logs
66.7.2 Style Review

Ensure code follows the team's style guide for consistency and readability.

🎨 Style Review Checklist
  • ☐ terraform fmt — code passes terraform fmt -check
  • ☐ snake_case — all resources, variables, outputs, locals
  • ☐ Descriptive names — no generic labels like "this", "main", "default"
  • ☐ Variable descriptions — every variable has a meaningful description
  • ☐ Output descriptions — every output has a description
  • ☐ Explicit types — every variable has a type constraint
  • ☐ File organization — follows standard module structure
  • ☐ Comment quality — explains "why" not "what"
66.7.3 Logic Review

Verify the Terraform plan is correct, changes are intentional, and infrastructure will behave as expected.

🧠 Logic Review Checklist
  • ☐ Plan reviewed — terraform plan output checked for unexpected changes
  • ☐ No destroy/recreate surprises — understand why resources are replaced
  • ☐ Count/for_each correctness — iteration produces the right resources
  • ☐ Dependencies are correct — implicit and explicit refs are accurate
  • ☐ Lifecycle rules — prevent_destroy on databases, create_before_destroy where needed
  • ☐ Conditional logic — ternaries and dynamic blocks behave correctly
  • ☐ Version constraints — Terraform, providers, and modules are pinned
  • ☐ State impact — no state moves or imports required without documentation
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 TERRAFORM CODE REVIEW FLOW                           │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│   Developer opens PR                                                 │
│       │                                                              │
│       ▼                                                              │
│   ┌──────────────────────┐     Automated                             │
│   │  CI PIPELINE CHECKS  │     (no human needed)                     │
│   │  ☐ terraform fmt     │                                           │
│   │  ☐ terraform validate│                                           │
│   │  ☐ tflint            │                                           │
│   │  ☐ tfsec / checkov   │                                           │
│   │  ☐ infracost         │                                           │
│   │  ☐ terraform plan    │                                           │
│   └──────────┬───────────┘                                           │
│              ▼                                                       │
│   ┌──────────────────────┐     Human                                 │
│   │  SECURITY REVIEW     │     (by security team or senior eng)      │
│   │  ☐ No secrets        │                                           │
│   │  ☐ Least privilege   │                                           │
│   │  ☐ Encryption        │                                           │
│   │  ☐ Network access    │                                           │
│   └──────────┬───────────┘                                           │
│              ▼                                                       │
│   ┌──────────────────────┐     Human                                 │
│   │  STYLE + LOGIC       │     (by peer reviewer)                    │
│   │  ☐ Naming conventions│                                           │
│   │  ☐ Plan correctness  │                                           │
│   │  ☐ Dependencies      │                                           │
│   │  ☐ State impact      │                                           │
│   └──────────┬───────────┘                                           │
│              ▼                                                       │
│   ┌──────────────────────┐                                           │
│   │  APPROVED ✅          │ ──► Merge to main ──► CI Apply           │
│   └──────────────────────┘                                           │
│                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📚 🏗️ 🔗 ✨
CHAPTER 67

Terraform Stacks (HCP Terraform)

Stacks HCP Terraform Orchestration Multi-Deploy
4Sections
9Subsections
15+Examples
✨Advanced
PART 18 ADVANCED ★★★★★

Terraform Stacks is an HCP Terraform feature that lets you orchestrate multiple Terraform configurations as a single deployable unit. Stacks introduce components and deployments to manage complex multi-environment, multi-region infrastructure from one definition.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand the Stacks concept and how it differs from workspaces
  • Define stack components and deployments for multi-environment infrastructure
  • Write tfstack.hcl and tfdeploy.hcl configuration files
  • Orchestrate deployment order with dependencies and rollback strategies

Chapter Contents

67.1What are Stacks? 67.2Stack Components 67.3Stack Configuration 67.4Stack Orchestration

67.1 What are Stacks?

CONCEPT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Stacks Overview | Create a Stack

Stacks are an HCP Terraform feature that groups multiple Terraform configurations (components) into a single, coordinated deployment unit. Instead of managing many workspaces independently, a Stack orchestrates them together — like a conductor leading an orchestra.

💻 Practical Examples

67.1.1 Stack Concept

A Terraform Stack groups related infrastructure components and deploys them across multiple environments or regions from a single definition.

📘 Stacks vs Workspaces

Workspaces manage a single Terraform configuration with isolated state. Stacks orchestrate multiple configurations as one unit, handling cross-component dependencies and multi-environment rollouts automatically.


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      TERRAFORM STACK                             │
│                                                                  │
│   ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐   ┌─────────────┐          │
│   │  Component   │   │  Component   │   │  Component   │          │
│   │  Networking  │──▶│  Compute     │──▶│  Database    │          │
│   │  (VPC, SGs)  │   │  (EC2, ECS)  │   │  (RDS)      │          │
│   └─────────────┘   └─────────────┘   └─────────────┘          │
│          │                 │                 │                    │
│   ┌──────▼─────────────────▼─────────────────▼──────┐           │
│   │              DEPLOYMENTS                         │           │
│   │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐         │           │
│   │  │   dev   │  │ staging │  │  prod   │          │           │
│   │  │ us-east │  │ us-east │  │ us-east │          │           │
│   │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘         │           │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘           │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Feature Workspaces Stacks
Scope Single configuration Multiple configurations
State One state per workspace State per component per deployment
Dependencies Manual via remote_state Automatic via component references
Multi-env Variable sets / workspaces Built-in deployment targets
Orchestration External (CI/CD) Built-in dependency ordering
Rollback Manual Built-in rollback support
67.1.2 Use Cases

Stacks shine in scenarios where you need coordinated, multi-component deployments.

✅ When to Use Stacks
  • Multi-region deployments — Deploy identical infrastructure to us-east-1, eu-west-1, and ap-southeast-1 from one definition
  • Environment promotion — Promote changes from dev → staging → production with coordinated plans
  • Microservice platforms — Deploy networking, compute, databases, and monitoring as a single unit
  • Disaster recovery — Spin up a full replica of your infrastructure in a DR region
  • Team platforms — Provide golden paths where teams fill in variables and get complete environments
Example: Multi-Region E-Commerce Stack
# ── A stack that deploys a full e-commerce platform ──────
# across three regions with coordinated dependencies
#
# Stack Structure:
#   components/
#     networking/   → VPC, subnets, security groups
#     compute/      → ECS cluster, services, ALB
#     database/     → RDS Aurora Global Database
#     cdn/          → CloudFront distribution
#   deployments/
#     us-east-1.tfdeployment.hcl
#     eu-west-1.tfdeployment.hcl
#     ap-southeast-1.tfdeployment.hcl

67.2 Stack Components

CORE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Stack Components | Stack Deployments

Components are the building blocks of a Stack. Each component references a Terraform module and wires it into the Stack with inputs, providers, and dependency declarations.

💻 Practical Examples

67.2.1 Components Definition

A component block in tfstack.hcl connects a Terraform module into the Stack.

Example: Defining Stack Components
# ── tfstack.hcl ──────────────────────────────────────────

# Component: Networking Layer
component "networking" {
  source = "./components/networking"

  inputs = {
    vpc_cidr     = var.vpc_cidr
    environment  = var.environment
    project_name = var.project_name
  }

  providers = {
    aws = provider.aws.this
  }
}

# Component: Compute Layer (depends on networking)
component "compute" {
  source = "./components/compute"

  inputs = {
    vpc_id             = component.networking.vpc_id
