    private_subnet_ids = component.networking.private_subnet_ids
    instance_type      = var.instance_type
    environment        = var.environment
  }

  providers = {
    aws = provider.aws.this
  }
}

# Component: Database Layer (depends on networking)
component "database" {
  source = "./components/database"

  inputs = {
    vpc_id             = component.networking.vpc_id
    database_subnet_ids = component.networking.database_subnet_ids
    db_instance_class  = var.db_instance_class
    environment        = var.environment
  }

  providers = {
    aws = provider.aws.this
  }
}
Component Attribute Description Required
source Path to the Terraform module (local or registry) ✅ Yes
inputs Map of variable values to pass to the module ✅ Yes
providers Provider configuration mapping ✅ Yes
for_each Create multiple instances of the component ❌ Optional
67.2.2 Deployments

Deployments define where and how a stack is instantiated — each deployment represents a target environment or region.

Example: Deployment Definitions
# ── tfdeploy.hcl ─────────────────────────────────────────

# Development Environment
deployment "development" {
  inputs = {
    environment    = "dev"
    vpc_cidr       = "10.0.0.0/16"
    instance_type  = "t3.micro"
    db_instance_class = "db.t3.micro"
    project_name   = "ecommerce"
  }
}

# Staging Environment
deployment "staging" {
  inputs = {
    environment    = "staging"
    vpc_cidr       = "10.1.0.0/16"
    instance_type  = "t3.small"
    db_instance_class = "db.t3.small"
    project_name   = "ecommerce"
  }
}

# Production — requires approval
deployment "production" {
  inputs = {
    environment    = "prod"
    vpc_cidr       = "10.2.0.0/16"
    instance_type  = "m5.large"
    db_instance_class = "db.r5.large"
    project_name   = "ecommerce"
  }
}

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   DEPLOYMENT TARGETS                             │
│                                                                  │
│   Stack Definition ──────────────────────────────▶              │
│   (tfstack.hcl)                                                  │
│        │                                                         │
│        ├──▶ deployment "development"                             │
│        │      vpc_cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"                          │
│        │      instance_type = "t3.micro"                        │
│        │                                                         │
│        ├──▶ deployment "staging"                                 │
│        │      vpc_cidr = "10.1.0.0/16"                          │
│        │      instance_type = "t3.small"                        │
│        │                                                         │
│        └──▶ deployment "production"                              │
│               vpc_cidr = "10.2.0.0/16"                          │
│               instance_type = "m5.large"                        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

67.3 Stack Configuration

CONFIGURATION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Stack Configuration Files | Component Blocks

Stacks use two main configuration files: tfstack.hcl for defining the stack structure (variables, providers, components) and tfdeploy.hcl for defining deployment targets and orchestration rules.

💻 Practical Examples

67.3.1 tfstack.hcl

The main stack definition file declares variables, required providers, and components.

Example: Complete tfstack.hcl
# ── tfstack.hcl — Full Stack Definition ──────────────────

# Required Providers
required_providers {
  aws = {
    source  = "hashicorp/aws"
    version = "~> 5.0"
  }
}

# Stack-Level Variables
variable "project_name" {
  type        = string
  description = "Name of the project"
}

variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Deployment environment (dev, staging, prod)"
}

variable "aws_region" {
  type        = string
  description = "AWS region for this deployment"
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "vpc_cidr" {
  type        = string
  description = "CIDR block for the VPC"
}

# Provider Configuration
provider "aws" "this" {
  config {
    region = var.aws_region

    default_tags {
      tags = {
        Project     = var.project_name
        Environment = var.environment
        ManagedBy   = "terraform-stacks"
      }
    }
  }
}

# ── Components ──────────────────────────────────────────
component "networking" {
  source = "./components/networking"

  inputs = {
    vpc_cidr     = var.vpc_cidr
    environment  = var.environment
    project_name = var.project_name
  }

  providers = {
    aws = provider.aws.this
  }
}

component "compute" {
  source = "./components/compute"

  inputs = {
    vpc_id     = component.networking.vpc_id
    subnet_ids = component.networking.private_subnet_ids
  }

  providers = {
    aws = provider.aws.this
  }
}
67.3.2 Component Blocks

Component blocks define the relationship between stack-level variables and module inputs, as well as cross-component dependencies.

⚠️ Implicit Dependencies

When a component references another via component.<name>.<output>, Terraform automatically creates a dependency. The referenced component will always be planned and applied first.

Example: Component with for_each
# ── Deploy a component per availability zone ────────────
component "subnet" {
  source   = "./components/subnet"
  for_each = var.availability_zones

  inputs = {
    vpc_id            = component.networking.vpc_id
    availability_zone = each.value
    cidr_block        = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, each.key)
    environment       = var.environment
  }

  providers = {
    aws = provider.aws.this
  }
}
67.3.3 Deployment Configuration

The tfdeploy.hcl file defines deployment targets with their specific variable values and orchestration rules.

Example: tfdeploy.hcl with Orchestration Rules
# ── tfdeploy.hcl — Deployment Configuration ─────────────

identity_token "aws" {
  audience = ["terraform-stacks-private-preview"]
}

deployment "development" {
  inputs = {
    aws_region     = "us-east-1"
    environment    = "dev"
    project_name   = "myapp"
    vpc_cidr       = "10.0.0.0/16"
  }
}

deployment "production" {
  inputs = {
    aws_region     = "us-east-1"
    environment    = "prod"
    project_name   = "myapp"
    vpc_cidr       = "10.2.0.0/16"
  }
}

# ── Orchestration Rules ─────────────────────────────────
orchestrate "auto_approve" "safe_plans" {
  # Auto-approve plans that only update existing resources
  check {
    condition = context.plan.changes.remove == 0
    reason    = "Plan includes resource deletions — manual approval required."
  }
}
File Purpose Key Blocks
tfstack.hcl Stack structure definition variable, provider, component, required_providers
tfdeploy.hcl Deployment targets & orchestration deployment, orchestrate, identity_token
components/*/ Standard Terraform modules resource, variable, output (regular .tf files)

67.4 Stack Orchestration

OPERATIONS 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Deploying Stacks | Orchestration Rules

Stack orchestration controls the order in which components are deployed, how approvals work, and what happens when a deployment fails. HCP Terraform resolves the dependency graph automatically and supports rollback strategies.

💻 Practical Examples

67.4.1 Deployment Order

HCP Terraform automatically determines the deployment order based on component references. You can visualize and control this ordering.


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 DEPLOYMENT ORDER (AUTO-RESOLVED)                 │
│                                                                  │
│   Phase 1                Phase 2               Phase 3           │
│   ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐  │
│   │  networking    │────▶│  compute      │────▶│  monitoring  │  │
│   │  (VPC, SGs)   │     │  (ECS, ALB)   │     │  (CloudWatch)│  │
│   └───────────────┘     └───────────────┘     └──────────────┘  │
│          │                                                       │
│          │              ┌───────────────┐                        │
│          └─────────────▶│  database     │                        │
│                         │  (RDS Aurora) │                        │
│                         └───────────────┘                        │
│                                                                  │
│   🔄 Parallel: compute + database (no mutual dependency)        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Example: Controlling Deployment Orchestration
# ── tfdeploy.hcl — Orchestration Rules ───────────────────

# Auto-approve if no resources are being destroyed
orchestrate "auto_approve" "safe_changes" {
  check {
    condition = context.plan.changes.remove == 0
    reason    = "Destroying resources requires manual approval."
  }
  check {
    condition = context.plan.deployment == "development"
    reason    = "Only dev deployments can be auto-approved."
  }
}

# Require sequential deployment: dev → staging → prod
orchestrate "replan" "after_dev" {
  check {
    condition = context.plan.deployment == "staging"
    reason    = "Staging plans must be regenerated after dev succeeds."
  }
}
67.4.2 Rollback Handling

When a deployment fails, Stacks provide built-in rollback capabilities. Understanding the failure modes helps you design resilient stack configurations.

Failure Scenario Behavior Recovery Action
Component plan fails Entire deployment halted Fix configuration, re-trigger plan
Component apply fails Dependent components skipped Fix issue, re-apply (partial state preserved)
Provider auth failure Component marked as errored Update credentials, re-trigger
Cross-deployment failure Other deployments continue independently Fix and re-trigger the failed deployment
⚠️ Rollback Limitations

Stacks do not automatically "undo" a partial apply. If component A succeeds but component B fails, component A's changes remain in the infrastructure. Design components with lifecycle { create_before_destroy = true } and health checks to reduce the blast radius of partial failures.

Example: Resilient Component Design
# ── components/compute/main.tf ───────────────────────────
# Design for rollback safety

resource "aws_ecs_service" "app" {
  name            = "${var.project_name}-${var.environment}"
  cluster         = aws_ecs_cluster.main.id
  task_definition = aws_ecs_task_definition.app.arn
  desired_count   = var.desired_count

  deployment_circuit_breaker {
    enable   = true
    rollback = true
  }

  deployment_configuration {
    maximum_percent         = 200
    minimum_healthy_percent = 100
  }

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}
⏱️ 🔑 💨 🛡️
CHAPTER 68

Ephemeral Resources (Terraform 1.10+)

Ephemeral Secrets Short-Lived v1.10+
4Sections
9Subsections
12+Examples
🔑Security
PART 18 ADVANCED ★★★★★

Ephemeral resources are a Terraform 1.10+ feature that produce values which are never stored in state. They are ideal for short-lived credentials, temporary tokens, and sensitive data that should only exist during a plan or apply operation.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand what ephemeral resources are and how they differ from standard resources
  • Identify use cases for short-lived credentials and temporary secrets
  • Declare ephemeral resources, variables, and outputs in configuration
  • Apply security best practices when working with ephemeral values

Chapter Contents

68.1Ephemeral Resource Concept 68.2Use Cases 68.3Configuration 68.4Best Practices

68.1 Ephemeral Resource Concept

CONCEPT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Ephemeral Resources | Ephemeral Variables

Ephemeral resources produce values that exist only during a Terraform operation. Unlike normal resources, their attributes are never written to state, making them safe for handling secrets, short-lived tokens, and temporary credentials.

💻 Practical Examples

68.1.1 What Makes a Resource Ephemeral?

An ephemeral resource is declared with the ephemeral keyword. Its values are computed every time Terraform runs and are discarded after the operation completes.

📘 Key Principle

The core guarantee of ephemeral resources: nothing is persisted to state. This means no sensitive data leaks into state files, remote backends, or plan files — the values exist only in memory during execution.


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│             STANDARD vs EPHEMERAL RESOURCE LIFECYCLE             │
│                                                                  │
│   Standard Resource:                                             │
│   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌──────────────┐  │
│   │  Plan   │──▶│  Apply  │──▶│  State  │──▶│  Persisted   │   │
│   │         │   │ (create)│   │ (write) │   │  (forever)   │   │
│   └─────────┘   └─────────┘   └─────────┘   └──────────────┘  │
│                                                                  │
│   Ephemeral Resource:                                            │
│   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌──────────────┐  │
│   │  Open   │──▶│  Use    │──▶│  Close  │──▶│  Discarded   │   │
│   │ (fetch) │   │ (in-mem)│   │(cleanup)│   │  (nothing    │   │
│   └─────────┘   └─────────┘   └─────────┘   │   stored)    │   │
│                                               └──────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Example: Basic Ephemeral Resource
# ── Ephemeral resource — value never stored in state ────
ephemeral "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_password" {
  secret_id = "prod/database/password"
}

# Use the ephemeral value in another resource
resource "aws_db_instance" "main" {
  engine         = "postgres"
  engine_version = "15.4"
  instance_class = "db.r5.large"

  # This password is fetched at apply time, never stored in state
  password = ephemeral.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db_password.secret_string
}
68.1.2 Lifecycle Differences

Ephemeral resources have a fundamentally different lifecycle compared to managed resources.

Aspect Managed Resource Data Source Ephemeral Resource
Keyword resource data ephemeral
Stored in state? ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ❌ No
Creates infra? ✅ Yes ❌ No (read-only) ❌ No (read-only)
Refreshed on plan? ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Yes (re-opened)
Has close/cleanup? ❌ via destroy ❌ No ✅ Yes (auto close)
Safe for secrets? ⚠️ Risky (in state) ⚠️ Risky (in state) ✅ Yes (never stored)

68.2 Use Cases

PATTERNS 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Ephemeral Resources | Managing Ephemeral Resources

Ephemeral resources solve long-standing security pain points in Terraform, especially around credential handling and secret management.

💻 Practical Examples

68.2.1 Short-Lived Credentials

The most compelling use case: fetching short-lived credentials that should never be stored.

Example: AWS STS Temporary Credentials
# ── Fetch temporary STS credentials ──────────────────────
# These credentials expire and are NEVER stored in state

ephemeral "aws_sts_session_token" "deploy" {
  duration_seconds = 900  # 15 minutes
}

# Use the temporary credentials for a provider
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "deploy"
  region = "us-east-1"

  access_key = ephemeral.aws_sts_session_token.deploy.access_key
  secret_key = ephemeral.aws_sts_session_token.deploy.secret_key
  token      = ephemeral.aws_sts_session_token.deploy.session_token
}
Example: HashiCorp Vault Dynamic Secrets
# ── Generate a dynamic database credential from Vault ────
ephemeral "vault_database_secret" "postgres" {
  backend = "database"
  role    = "app-readonly"
}

# The credential is auto-revoked when Terraform closes
# the ephemeral resource after apply completes
resource "kubernetes_secret" "db_creds" {
  metadata {
    name      = "db-credentials"
    namespace = "app"
  }

  data = {
    username = ephemeral.vault_database_secret.postgres.username
    password = ephemeral.vault_database_secret.postgres.password
  }
}
68.2.2 Temporary Secrets

Fetch secrets from any secret store without leaking them into the Terraform state file.


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              THE SECRET LEAK PROBLEM (BEFORE v1.10)              │
│                                                                  │
│   ┌──────────┐    data source     ┌──────────┐                  │
│   │  Vault   │──────────────────▶│ Terraform │                  │
│   │  Secret  │   (read secret)   │   State   │                  │
│   └──────────┘                    └─────┬────┘                   │
│                                         │                        │
│                          ╔══════════════▼══════════════╗         │
│                          ║  ⚠️ SECRET IN STATE FILE   ║         │
│                          ║  "password": "s3cret123"   ║         │
│                          ║  Visible in S3 / Consul    ║         │
│                          ╚═════════════════════════════╝         │
│                                                                  │
│              THE SOLUTION (v1.10+ EPHEMERAL)                     │
│                                                                  │
│   ┌──────────┐   ephemeral res    ┌──────────┐                  │
│   │  Vault   │──────────────────▶│ Terraform │                  │
│   │  Secret  │   (open → use)    │   State   │                  │
│   └──────────┘                    └─────┬────┘                   │
│                                         │                        │
│                          ╔══════════════▼══════════════╗         │
│                          ║  ✅ NO SECRET IN STATE     ║         │
│                          ║  Value used in-memory only ║         │
│                          ║  Auto-closed after apply   ║         │
│                          ╚═════════════════════════════╝         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Example: AWS Secrets Manager Without State Leaks
# ── BEFORE (v1.9 and earlier) — secret stored in state ──
data "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "api_key" {
  secret_id = "prod/api-key"
}
# ⚠️ data.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.api_key.secret_string
#    is stored in terraform.tfstate!

# ── AFTER (v1.10+) — secret NEVER in state ─────────────
ephemeral "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "api_key" {
  secret_id = "prod/api-key"
}
# ✅ ephemeral.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.api_key.secret_string
#    exists only in memory during apply

68.3 Configuration

SYNTAX 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Ephemeral Resource Syntax | Ephemeral Variables

Ephemeral resources, variables, and outputs each have specific syntax rules. Understanding these ensures you use the feature correctly and avoid configuration errors.

💻 Practical Examples

68.3.1 Declaring Ephemeral Resources

Ephemeral resources use the same provider resource types but with the ephemeral keyword instead of resource or data.

Example: Ephemeral Resource Syntax
# ── Syntax ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# ephemeral "<provider>_<type>" "<name>" {
#   <arguments>
# }

# AWS Secrets Manager
ephemeral "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "app_config" {
  secret_id = aws_secretsmanager_secret.app_config.id
}

# Azure Key Vault
ephemeral "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "db_password" {
  name         = "database-password"
  key_vault_id = azurerm_key_vault.main.id
}

# Google Cloud Secret Manager
ephemeral "google_secret_manager_secret_version" "api_key" {
  secret = "projects/my-project/secrets/api-key"
}
Provider Ephemeral Resource Type Purpose
AWS aws_secretsmanager_secret_version Retrieve secret values
AWS aws_sts_session_token Temporary STS credentials
Azure azurerm_key_vault_secret Key Vault secret retrieval
GCP google_secret_manager_secret_version Secret Manager access
Vault vault_database_secret Dynamic database credentials
Vault vault_aws_secret Dynamic AWS credentials
68.3.2 Ephemeral Variables and Outputs

Variables and outputs can also be marked as ephemeral, ensuring sensitive values flow through Terraform without touching state at any point.

Example: Ephemeral Variables
# ── Ephemeral variable — never stored in state ──────────
variable "deploy_token" {
  type      = string
  ephemeral = true   # ← marks this variable as ephemeral
  sensitive = true
}

# ── Ephemeral output — never stored in state ────────────
output "temporary_password" {
  value     = ephemeral.aws_secretsmanager_secret_version.db.secret_string
  ephemeral = true   # ← marks this output as ephemeral
}
⚠️ Propagation Rule

Ephemeral values can only be assigned to other ephemeral contexts. You cannot assign an ephemeral value to a non-ephemeral variable, output, or local. Terraform will raise an error: Cannot use ephemeral value in non-ephemeral context.

68.3.3 Provider Integration

Ephemeral resources integrate with providers the same way as normal resources. The provider must implement the ephemeral resource type for it to be available.

Example: Full Workflow — Vault to AWS
# ── Step 1: Get dynamic AWS creds from Vault ─────────────
ephemeral "vault_aws_secret" "deploy_creds" {
  backend = "aws"
  role    = "deploy-role"
  type    = "sts"
  ttl     = "15m"
}

# ── Step 2: Configure AWS provider with ephemeral creds ──
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "ephemeral"
  region = "us-east-1"

  access_key = ephemeral.vault_aws_secret.deploy_creds.access_key
  secret_key = ephemeral.vault_aws_secret.deploy_creds.secret_key
  token      = ephemeral.vault_aws_secret.deploy_creds.security_token
}

# ── Step 3: Use the ephemeral provider ───────────────────
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "deploy_artifacts" {
  provider = aws.ephemeral
  bucket   = "myapp-deploy-artifacts-${var.environment}"
}

# When Terraform finishes:
# - The Vault lease is revoked (close lifecycle)
# - The STS token expires
# - No credentials are stored in state

68.4 Best Practices

GUIDELINES 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Ephemeral Resources | Ephemeral Values

Follow these guidelines to maximize the security benefits of ephemeral resources and avoid common pitfalls.

💻 Practical Examples

68.4.1 Security Guidelines

Ephemeral resources are a security feature. Use them consistently for all sensitive data.

Practice Do Don't
Database passwords Use ephemeral to fetch from secret store Don't use data source (leaks to state)
API keys Use ephemeral + sensitive = true Don't pass as plain variable
Cloud credentials Use ephemeral STS/dynamic creds Don't hardcode or store long-lived keys
TLS certificates Use ephemeral for private keys Don't store private keys in state
Output values Mark as ephemeral = true Don't expose secrets in non-ephemeral outputs
✅ Migration Checklist
  • Audit existing data sources that fetch secrets → replace with ephemeral
  • Check state files for leaked sensitive values → remove after migrating to ephemeral
  • Update CI/CD pipelines to use Terraform 1.10+ if switching to ephemeral resources
  • Test with terraform plan — ephemeral values show as (ephemeral) in plan output
  • Verify provider support — not all providers support ephemeral resource types yet
68.4.2 When Not to Use Ephemeral

Ephemeral resources are not appropriate for every situation. Use them specifically for sensitive, temporary data.

⚠️ Don't Use Ephemeral For
  • Infrastructure you manage — Use resource blocks for things Terraform creates and tracks
  • Reference data you need in plans — Use data sources for AMI IDs, subnet lookups, etc.
  • Values needed across runs — Ephemeral values don't persist; if you need a value in future runs, it must be re-fetched
  • Non-sensitive configuration — No security benefit; adds unnecessary complexity
Example: Decision Guide
# ── ✅ USE EPHEMERAL: database password ──────────────────
ephemeral "aws_secretsmanager_secret_version" "db_pass" {
  secret_id = "prod/db-password"
}

# ── ❌ DON'T USE EPHEMERAL: AMI lookup ──────────────────
# This is non-sensitive reference data — use a data source
data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["099720109477"]
  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-*-amd64-server-*"]
  }
}

# ── ❌ DON'T USE EPHEMERAL: infrastructure ──────────────
# This creates real infrastructure — use a resource block
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "logs" {
  bucket = "my-logs-bucket"
}
🍴 🌐 📜 🤝
CHAPTER 69

OpenTofu Considerations

OpenTofu Open Source Migration Community
4Sections
9Subsections
10+Comparisons
🍴Fork
PART 18 ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

OpenTofu is an open-source fork of Terraform, created after HashiCorp changed Terraform's license from MPL 2.0 to BSL 1.1. This chapter covers the differences, feature parity, migration path, and community ecosystem to help you make an informed decision.

Learning Objectives

  • Understand why OpenTofu was created and how it relates to Terraform
  • Compare feature parity between Terraform and OpenTofu
  • Plan and execute a migration from Terraform to OpenTofu
  • Evaluate community support, governance, and ecosystem differences

Chapter Contents

69.1What is OpenTofu? 69.2Feature Parity 69.3Migration Considerations 69.4Community and Support

69.1 What is OpenTofu?

CONCEPT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: OpenTofu Documentation | OpenTofu Manifesto

OpenTofu is a community-driven, open-source fork of Terraform maintained by the Linux Foundation. It was created in response to HashiCorp's August 2023 license change and aims to keep the Terraform ecosystem truly open source.

💻 Practical Examples

69.1.1 Fork Background

Understanding why OpenTofu exists helps you evaluate its role in the IaC ecosystem.


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    TERRAFORM → OPENTOFU TIMELINE                 │
│                                                                  │
│   2014 ───── Terraform released (MPL 2.0 — open source)        │
│      │                                                           │
│   2023 ───── Aug: HashiCorp changes license to BSL 1.1          │
│      │       (Business Source License — NOT open source)        │
│      │                                                           │
│   2023 ───── Sep: OpenTofu manifesto published                  │
│      │       Community fork announced                           │
│      │       Linux Foundation accepts the project               │
│      │                                                           │
│   2024 ───── Jan: OpenTofu 1.6.0 (first stable release)        │
│      │       Drop-in replacement for Terraform 1.6.x            │
│      │                                                           │
│   2024 ───── Mar: OpenTofu 1.7.0 (state encryption,            │
│      │              client-side encryption)                      │
│      │                                                           │
│   2024+ ──── Continued divergence: both projects add            │
│              features independently                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📘 What Does the License Change Mean?

BSL 1.1 (Terraform's current license) permits most usage but restricts offering Terraform as a competing hosted service. MPL 2.0 (OpenTofu's license) has no such restriction — it's a true open-source license approved by the OSI. For most end users deploying their own infrastructure, the practical difference is minimal. The license matters primarily for companies building competing IaC platforms.

69.1.2 Licensing Differences

A side-by-side comparison of the two licensing models.

Aspect Terraform (BSL 1.1) OpenTofu (MPL 2.0)
OSI Approved? ❌ No ✅ Yes
Free to use? ✅ Yes (most uses) ✅ Yes (all uses)
Competing service? ❌ Restricted ✅ Allowed
Modify & distribute? ✅ With conditions ✅ Freely
Embed in products? ⚠️ Review required ✅ Allowed
Governance HashiCorp (single vendor) Linux Foundation (community)

69.2 Feature Parity

COMPARISON 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: OpenTofu Migration Guide | OpenTofu Language

OpenTofu started as a 1:1 fork of Terraform 1.5.x and maintains backward compatibility. However, both projects have since diverged with unique features.

💻 Practical Examples

69.2.1 Shared Features

The vast majority of Terraform features work identically in OpenTofu.

Feature Terraform OpenTofu Notes
HCL Configuration Language ✅ ✅ Identical syntax
Provider ecosystem ✅ ✅ Same providers work in both
State management ✅ ✅ Compatible state format
Modules (local & registry) ✅ ✅ OpenTofu has its own registry
Workspaces ✅ ✅ Same workspace support
import blocks ✅ ✅ Config-driven import
check blocks ✅ ✅ Continuous validation
for_each / count ✅ ✅ Identical iteration
69.2.2 OpenTofu-Only Features

Features that OpenTofu has added independently — not available in Terraform.

✅ OpenTofu Exclusive Features
  • Client-side state encryption — Encrypt state files at rest with AES-GCM, AWS KMS, GCP KMS, or Azure Key Vault. No more worrying about sensitive data in state files.
  • Early variable/local evaluation — Use variables and locals in module source and backend configuration blocks (a highly requested Terraform feature).
  • for_each on providers — Dynamically instantiate providers with for_each, useful for multi-region or multi-account configurations.
  • OpenTofu Registry — Independent provider/module registry at registry.opentofu.org mirroring community providers.
Example: State Encryption (OpenTofu Only)
# ── tofu configuration — client-side state encryption ────
# This feature does NOT exist in Terraform

terraform {
  encryption {
    method "aes_gcm" "default" {
      keys = key_provider.aws_kms.main
    }

    state {
      method   = method.aes_gcm.default
      enforced = true
    }

    plan {
      method   = method.aes_gcm.default
      enforced = true
    }
  }
}

key_provider "aws_kms" "main" {
  kms_key_id = "arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789:key/abcd-1234"
  region     = "us-east-1"
}
Example: Early Variable Evaluation (OpenTofu Only)
# ── Use variables in backend config — OpenTofu only ──────
# In Terraform, backend blocks cannot use variables!

variable "state_bucket" {
  type    = string
  default = "my-terraform-state"
}

variable "state_key" {
  type    = string
  default = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
}

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    # ✅ Works in OpenTofu — variables allowed here
    bucket = var.state_bucket
    key    = var.state_key
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}
69.2.3 Terraform-Only Features

Features exclusive to Terraform, not available in OpenTofu.

Feature Description Requires
Terraform Stacks Multi-component orchestration (Ch 67) HCP Terraform
Ephemeral Resources State-free secret handling (Ch 68) Terraform 1.10+
HCP Terraform Managed SaaS platform (runs, policies, VCS) HashiCorp account
Sentinel Policies Policy-as-code framework for governance HCP Terraform / Enterprise
Run Tasks External integrations in HCP workflow HCP Terraform

69.3 Migration Considerations

MIGRATION 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Migrating to OpenTofu | OpenTofu CLI Reference

Migrating from Terraform to OpenTofu is straightforward for most configurations, since OpenTofu maintains backward compatibility with Terraform 1.5.x state and configuration formats.

💻 Practical Examples

69.3.1 Migration Steps

Follow these steps for a safe migration from Terraform to OpenTofu.


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                TERRAFORM → OPENTOFU MIGRATION PATH               │
│                                                                  │
│   Step 1: Backup                                                 │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│   │  $ cp terraform.tfstate terraform.tfstate.bak               │
│   │  $ cp -r .terraform .terraform.bak      │                   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘                   │
│         │                                                        │
│   Step 2: Install OpenTofu                                       │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│   │  $ brew install opentofu       (macOS)  │                   │
│   │  $ choco install opentofu     (Windows) │                   │
│   │  $ snap install opentofu       (Linux)  │                   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘                   │
│         │                                                        │
│   Step 3: Replace CLI                                            │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│   │  terraform → tofu                       │                   │
│   │  $ tofu init     (re-initialize)        │                   │
│   │  $ tofu plan     (verify no changes)    │                   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘                   │
│         │                                                        │
│   Step 4: Validate                                               │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│   │  $ tofu plan   → "No changes"           │                   │
│   │  ✅ Migration complete!                  │                   │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘                   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Example: Complete Migration Script
#!/bin/bash
# ── Terraform → OpenTofu Migration Script ───────────────

set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Step 1: Backup existing state ==="
cp terraform.tfstate terraform.tfstate.backup
cp -r .terraform .terraform.backup

echo "=== Step 2: Verify OpenTofu is installed ==="
tofu version

echo "=== Step 3: Re-initialize with OpenTofu ==="
tofu init

echo "=== Step 4: Validate — should show 'No changes' ==="
tofu plan -detailed-exitcode

if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "✅ Migration successful! No infrastructure changes detected."
else
  echo "⚠️ Plan shows changes — review before proceeding."
  echo "   Restore backup: cp terraform.tfstate.backup terraform.tfstate"
fi

echo "=== Step 5: Update CI/CD pipelines ==="
echo "   Replace 'terraform' commands with 'tofu' in:"
echo "   - GitHub Actions workflows"
echo "   - Jenkinsfiles"
echo "   - GitLab CI pipelines"
echo "   - Makefiles"
69.3.2 Compatibility Notes

Important compatibility details to review before and after migration.

Area Compatibility Notes
State file format ✅ Compatible OpenTofu reads Terraform state files directly
HCL syntax ✅ Compatible All standard HCL constructs work identically
Provider plugins ✅ Compatible Same provider binaries work in both
Module registry ⚠️ Partial Public modules work; private registries may need updates
Remote backends ✅ Compatible S3, Azure, GCS, Consul, etc. all work
HCP Terraform backend ❌ Incompatible HCP Terraform (formerly TFC) is Terraform-only
Sentinel policies ❌ Incompatible Sentinel is HashiCorp-proprietary; use OPA instead
CLI commands ✅ Compatible tofu replaces terraform; same subcommands
⚠️ Before You Migrate
  • Check version alignment — OpenTofu 1.6.x ≈ Terraform 1.6.x. Ensure your Terraform version has an equivalent OpenTofu release.
  • Audit HCP features — If you use HCP Terraform workspaces, Sentinel, or run tasks, you'll need replacements (e.g., Spacelift, Env0, or self-hosted runners).
  • Test in non-production — Always migrate a dev/staging environment first.
  • Lock file — Run tofu init to regenerate .terraform.lock.hcl with OpenTofu-compatible hashes.

69.4 Community and Support

ECOSYSTEM 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: OpenTofu Community | OpenTofu GitHub

Both Terraform and OpenTofu have active communities, but with fundamentally different governance models. Understanding the ecosystem helps you choose the right tool for your organization.

💻 Practical Examples

69.4.1 Governance Model

The governance model determines who controls the project's direction, roadmap, and release schedule.

Aspect Terraform OpenTofu
Governance Single vendor (HashiCorp/IBM) Linux Foundation + Technical Steering Committee
Roadmap HashiCorp decides Community-driven RFC process
Contributions CLA required DCO (Developer Certificate of Origin)
Funding HashiCorp/IBM revenue Corporate sponsors + Linux Foundation
Release cadence Regular (HashiCorp schedule) Community-driven milestones 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    DECISION FRAMEWORK                            │
│                                                                  │
│   Choose Terraform if:              Choose OpenTofu if:          │
│   ├── You use HCP Terraform         ├── License compliance is    │
│   │   or Terraform Enterprise        │   critical (OSI approved)  │
│   ├── You need Sentinel policies    ├── You want state           │
│   │   and run tasks                  │   encryption built-in      │
│   ├── You rely on HashiCorp         ├── You need variables in    │
│   │   commercial support             │   backend config           │
│   └── You need Terraform Stacks    ├── You want community       │
│                                      │   governance               │
│                                      └── You're building a       │
│                                          competing IaC platform   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
69.4.2 Ecosystem and Tooling

The broader ecosystem of tools and integrations available for each project.

Tool / Integration Terraform OpenTofu
Terragrunt ✅ Supported ✅ Supported
Atlantis ✅ Supported ✅ Supported
Spacelift ✅ Supported ✅ Supported
Env0 ✅ Supported ✅ Supported
tflint ✅ Supported ✅ Supported
Checkov / tfsec ✅ Supported ✅ Supported
Infracost ✅ Supported ✅ Supported
HCP Terraform ✅ Native ❌ Not available
Sentinel ✅ Native ❌ Use OPA/Rego instead
📘 Bottom Line

For most teams, both tools work well. Your existing Terraform skills transfer directly to OpenTofu — the HCL language, provider ecosystem, and CLI commands are nearly identical. The choice comes down to licensing requirements, governance preferences, and whether you need HCP Terraform-specific features like Stacks, Sentinel, or managed runs.

⌨️ 📋 🔧 📖
APPENDIX A

Terraform CLI Complete Reference

CLI Commands State Management Environment Variables Quick Reference
7Sections
50+Commands
20+Env Vars
📋Reference
APPENDIX ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

A comprehensive, quick-reference guide to every Terraform CLI command, flag, environment variable, and exit code. Keep this appendix bookmarked for daily use — it covers everything from core workflow commands to advanced state manipulation and debugging utilities.

What You'll Find Here

  • Complete reference for all core workflow commands (init, plan, apply, destroy)
  • State inspection and manipulation commands with flags and examples
  • Workspace and import/migration command reference
  • All Terraform environment variables and their effects

Appendix Contents

A.1Core Commands A.2State Commands A.3Workspace Commands A.4Import and Migration A.5Utility Commands A.6Environment Variables A.7Exit Codes

A.1 Core Commands

ESSENTIAL 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform CLI Commands | terraform init

These are the primary commands you use in the daily Terraform workflow: initializing projects, planning changes, applying infrastructure, and tearing it down. Mastering these commands and their flags is essential for both daily work and certification exams.

💻 Core Workflow Commands


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 TERRAFORM CORE WORKFLOW                          │
│                                                                  │
│    Write          Plan           Apply          Destroy          │
│   ┌──────┐     ┌──────┐      ┌──────┐       ┌──────┐           │
│   │ .tf  │────▶│ plan │─────▶│apply │──···──▶│destroy│          │
│   │files │     │      │      │      │        │       │          │
│   └──────┘     └──────┘      └──────┘       └──────┘           │
│      │            │              │               │               │
│      ▼            ▼              ▼               ▼               │
│   terraform    terraform     terraform      terraform            │
│     init         plan          apply          destroy            │
│                                                                  │
│   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐            │
│   │  validate ──▶ fmt ──▶ plan ──▶ apply           │            │
│   │  (check)    (style)  (preview) (execute)       │            │
│   └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘            │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
terraform init

Initializes a working directory, downloads providers, configures backends, and installs modules.

Flag Description Example
-backend=false Skip backend configuration terraform init -backend=false
-backend-config=FILE Partial backend config from file terraform init -backend-config=prod.hcl
-backend-config="KEY=VALUE" Partial backend config inline terraform init -backend-config="bucket=my-state"
-reconfigure Reconfigure backend, discard old state terraform init -reconfigure
-migrate-state Reconfigure backend, migrate state terraform init -migrate-state
-upgrade Upgrade providers/modules to latest terraform init -upgrade
-get=false Skip module installation terraform init -get=false
-plugin-dir=DIR Use local plugin directory terraform init -plugin-dir=./plugins
-lockfile=MODE Lock file mode: readonly terraform init -lockfile=readonly 
# ── Common terraform init patterns ──────────────────────

# Basic initialization
terraform init

# Initialize with a specific backend config
terraform init -backend-config="backends/production.hcl"

# Re-initialize and migrate state to new backend
terraform init -migrate-state

# Initialize in CI/CD (no interactive prompts)
terraform init -input=false -backend-config="key=prod/terraform.tfstate"

# Upgrade all providers to latest allowed versions
terraform init -upgrade

# Air-gapped / offline initialization
terraform init -plugin-dir=/opt/terraform/plugins
terraform plan

Creates an execution plan showing what Terraform will do without making any changes.

Flag Description Example
-out=FILE Save plan to binary file terraform plan -out=tfplan
-var 'KEY=VALUE' Set a variable value terraform plan -var 'env=prod'
-var-file=FILE Load variables from file terraform plan -var-file=prod.tfvars
-target=RESOURCE Plan only specific resource terraform plan -target=aws_instance.web
-replace=RESOURCE Force replacement of resource terraform plan -replace=aws_instance.web
-refresh-only Only update state, no changes terraform plan -refresh-only
-refresh=false Skip refreshing state terraform plan -refresh=false
-destroy Plan a destroy operation terraform plan -destroy
-parallelism=N Limit concurrent operations terraform plan -parallelism=5
-compact-warnings Show condensed warnings terraform plan -compact-warnings
-generate-config-out=FILE Generate config for imported resources terraform plan -generate-config-out=gen.tf 
# ── Common terraform plan patterns ──────────────────────

# Basic plan
terraform plan

# Save plan for later apply (CI/CD best practice)
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Plan with variables
terraform plan -var-file="environments/production.tfvars"

# Plan targeting specific resources
terraform plan -target=module.networking

# Plan to replace a tainted/broken resource
terraform plan -replace=aws_instance.web[0]

# Refresh-only plan (detect drift)
terraform plan -refresh-only

# Plan with JSON output for automation
terraform plan -out=tfplan && terraform show -json tfplan
terraform apply

Applies the changes required to reach the desired state of the configuration.

Flag Description Example
-auto-approve Skip interactive approval terraform apply -auto-approve
[PLAN_FILE] Apply a saved plan file terraform apply tfplan
-var 'KEY=VALUE' Set variable (not with plan file) terraform apply -var 'env=prod'
-var-file=FILE Load variables from file terraform apply -var-file=prod.tfvars
-target=RESOURCE Apply only specific resource terraform apply -target=aws_s3_bucket.logs
-replace=RESOURCE Force resource recreation terraform apply -replace=aws_instance.web
-parallelism=N Limit concurrent operations terraform apply -parallelism=2
-refresh-only Only refresh state terraform apply -refresh-only
-input=false Disable interactive prompts terraform apply -input=false tfplan
terraform destroy

Destroys all resources managed by the current configuration.

Flag Description Example
-auto-approve Skip interactive confirmation terraform destroy -auto-approve
-target=RESOURCE Destroy specific resource only terraform destroy -target=aws_instance.web
-var-file=FILE Load variables from file terraform destroy -var-file=prod.tfvars
-parallelism=N Limit concurrent operations terraform destroy -parallelism=3
⚠️ Dangerous Commands

terraform destroy and terraform apply -auto-approve make irreversible changes. Always run terraform plan first and review the output carefully. In production, use saved plan files: terraform plan -out=tfplan && terraform apply tfplan.

terraform validate & terraform fmt

Validation and formatting commands to ensure configuration correctness and consistent style.

Command Flag Description
terraform validate -json Output validation result as JSON
terraform validate -no-color Disable color output
terraform fmt -check Check if files are formatted (exit 0/3)
terraform fmt -diff Show formatting differences
terraform fmt -recursive Format files in subdirectories too
terraform fmt -write=false Don't overwrite files (preview) 
# ── Validate and format workflow ────────────────────────

# Validate configuration syntax and logic
terraform validate

# Validate with JSON output (for CI/CD)
terraform validate -json

# Auto-format all .tf files
terraform fmt

# Format recursively (includes modules/)
terraform fmt -recursive

# Check formatting without changing files (CI/CD gate)
terraform fmt -check -recursive

# Show what fmt would change
terraform fmt -diff
📘 CI/CD Pipeline Pattern

A robust CI/CD pipeline runs these commands in order: terraform fmt -check → terraform init → terraform validate → terraform plan -out=tfplan → (manual approval) → terraform apply tfplan. This ensures code quality, correctness, and safe deployment.

A.2 State Commands

STATE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: State Commands | terraform show

State commands let you inspect, modify, and manage Terraform's state file. These are critical for advanced operations like refactoring resources, recovering from errors, and debugging state drift.

💻 State Management Commands


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  TERRAFORM STATE OPERATIONS                      │
│                                                                  │
│   READ OPERATIONS          WRITE OPERATIONS                      │
│   ┌──────────────┐        ┌──────────────────┐                  │
│   │ state list   │        │ state mv         │                  │
│   │ state show   │        │ state rm         │                  │
│   │ show         │        │ state replace-   │                  │
│   │ output       │        │   provider       │                  │
│   │ graph        │        │ state push       │                  │
│   │ providers    │        │ state pull       │                  │
│   └──────────────┘        └──────────────────┘                  │
│                                                                  │
│   ⚠️  Write operations modify state and can be dangerous!        │
│       Always backup state first: terraform state pull > bak.json │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
terraform state list & terraform state show

Inspect what resources are tracked in state.

Command Description Example
state list List all resources in state terraform state list
state list [FILTER] Filter resources by address terraform state list module.vpc
state show ADDR Show attributes of one resource terraform state show aws_instance.web
show Show full state or plan in readable format terraform show
show -json Show state/plan as JSON (for automation) terraform show -json tfplan
output Show all output values terraform output
output NAME Show specific output terraform output -raw db_endpoint
output -json Show all outputs as JSON terraform output -json 
# ── Inspecting state ────────────────────────────────────

# List everything in state
terraform state list

# List only resources in a specific module
terraform state list module.networking

# Show full details of a specific resource
terraform state show 'aws_instance.web[0]'

# Show the full state in human-readable format
terraform show

# Export plan to JSON for policy tools (OPA, Sentinel)
terraform show -json tfplan > plan.json

# Get a specific output value (raw, no quotes)
terraform output -raw api_endpoint

# Get all outputs as JSON
terraform output -json | jq '.vpc_id.value'
terraform state mv & terraform state rm

Move or remove resources from state — essential for refactoring.

Command Description Example
state mv SRC DST Rename/move a resource in state terraform state mv aws_instance.old aws_instance.new
state mv -state-out=FILE Move resource to a different state file terraform state mv -state-out=other.tfstate aws_s3_bucket.logs
state rm ADDR Remove resource from state (not cloud) terraform state rm aws_instance.legacy
state rm -dry-run Preview what would be removed terraform state rm -dry-run module.old
⚠️ state mv vs moved Block

Since Terraform 1.1, prefer the moved {} block in your .tf files over terraform state mv. The moved block is declarative, version-controlled, and works across teams — while state mv is imperative and only affects the person running it.

# ── State modification operations ────────────────────────

# Rename a resource (refactoring)
terraform state mv aws_instance.web aws_instance.app_server

# Move resource into a module
terraform state mv aws_vpc.main module.networking.aws_vpc.main

# Move indexed resource
terraform state mv 'aws_instance.web[0]' 'aws_instance.web["primary"]'

# Remove from state (resource stays in cloud)
terraform state rm aws_instance.legacy

# Backup state before dangerous operations
terraform state pull > backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).json
terraform state pull, push & replace-provider

Advanced state operations for backend migrations and provider changes.

Command Description Use Case
state pull Download remote state as JSON to stdout Backup, inspection, debugging
state push FILE Upload local state to remote backend Disaster recovery, migration
state replace-provider OLD NEW Replace provider in state Provider namespace changes
graph Generate dependency graph (DOT format) Visualization, debugging
graph -type=plan Graph of planned changes Understanding apply order
providers List providers used in configuration Audit, compliance
providers lock Update lock file for specified platforms Cross-platform CI/CD 
# ── Advanced state operations ────────────────────────────

# Download state as JSON
terraform state pull > state-backup.json

# Push state (dangerous — use for disaster recovery only)
terraform state push corrected-state.json

# Replace provider after namespace change
terraform state replace-provider \
  "registry.terraform.io/-/aws" \
  "registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws"

# Generate and visualize dependency graph
terraform graph | dot -Tpng > graph.png

# Lock providers for multiple platforms (CI/CD)
terraform providers lock \
  -platform=linux_amd64 \
  -platform=darwin_arm64 \
  -platform=windows_amd64
📘 State Safety Checklist

Before any state modification: 1) Run terraform state pull > backup.json, 2) Verify you're targeting the correct workspace, 3) Use -dry-run when available, 4) Plan after changes to verify no unexpected diffs. State corruption can cause resources to be recreated or orphaned.

A.3 Workspace Commands

WORKSPACES 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Workspace Commands | Workspaces

Workspace commands manage isolated state environments within a single configuration. Each workspace has its own state file, enabling you to deploy the same infrastructure for different environments (dev, staging, prod) without duplicating code.

💻 Workspace Management


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  WORKSPACE ARCHITECTURE                          │
│                                                                  │
│   Configuration (same .tf files)                                 │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐                   │
│   │  main.tf  │  variables.tf  │  outputs.tf│                   │
│   └─────────────────┬───────────────────────┘                   │
│                     │                                            │
│         ┌───────────┼───────────┐                                │
│         ▼           ▼           ▼                                │
│   ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐                       │
│   │   dev    │ │ staging  │ │   prod   │   ◀── Workspaces       │
│   │  state   │ │  state   │ │  state   │                        │
│   └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘                        │
│                                                                  │
│   terraform.workspace = "dev" | "staging" | "prod"               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Command Description Example
workspace list List all workspaces (current marked with *) terraform workspace list
workspace show Show current workspace name terraform workspace show
workspace new NAME Create and switch to new workspace terraform workspace new staging
workspace select NAME Switch to existing workspace terraform workspace select prod
workspace delete NAME Delete an empty workspace terraform workspace delete old-env
workspace delete -force NAME Delete workspace even with resources terraform workspace delete -force old-env 
# ── Workspace workflow ──────────────────────────────────

# List all workspaces
terraform workspace list
#   default
# * dev
#   staging
#   prod

# Create a new workspace for a feature branch
terraform workspace new feature-auth

# Switch to production workspace
terraform workspace select prod

# Show which workspace you're in
terraform workspace show
# prod

# Delete a workspace (must have no resources)
terraform workspace delete feature-auth
Using Workspaces in Configuration

Reference the current workspace name in your configuration to vary behavior per environment.

# ── Using terraform.workspace in config ──────────────────

locals {
  env_config = {
    dev = {
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      min_size      = 1
      max_size      = 2
    }
    staging = {
      instance_type = "t3.small"
      min_size      = 2
      max_size      = 4
    }
    prod = {
      instance_type = "t3.large"
      min_size      = 3
      max_size      = 10
    }
  }

  config = local.env_config[terraform.workspace]
}

resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  instance_type = local.config.instance_type

  tags = {
    Environment = terraform.workspace
    Name        = "app-${terraform.workspace}"
  }
}
⚠️ Workspace Limitations

CLI workspaces are not the same as HCP Terraform workspaces. CLI workspaces share the same backend and configuration — they only separate state. For true environment isolation (separate backends, separate access controls, separate configurations), use separate root modules or HCP Terraform workspaces instead.

A.4 Import and Migration

IMPORT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: terraform import | Import Blocks

Import commands bring existing cloud resources under Terraform management. Terraform offers both the legacy CLI terraform import command and the modern declarative import {} block (Terraform 1.5+). The moved {} block handles resource renaming and refactoring.

💻 Import & Migration Commands


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               IMPORT METHODS COMPARISON                          │
│                                                                  │
│   Legacy CLI Import (all versions):                              │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│   │  terraform import aws_instance.web i-12345    │             │
│   │  • Imperative (one resource at a time)        │             │
│   │  • Not version-controlled                     │             │
│   │  • Must write config manually                 │             │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
│                                                                  │
│   Import Block (Terraform 1.5+):                                 │
│   ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐             │
│   │  import {                                     │             │
│   │    to = aws_instance.web                      │             │
│   │    id = "i-12345"                             │             │
│   │  }                                            │             │
│   │  • Declarative (in .tf files)                 │             │
│   │  • Version-controlled                         │             │
│   │  • Can auto-generate config (1.5+)            │             │
│   │  • Supports for_each (1.7+)                   │             │
│   └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
terraform import (CLI Command)

Import existing resources into state using the imperative CLI command.

Flag Description Example
ADDR ID Import resource by address and cloud ID terraform import aws_instance.web i-1234567890abcdef0
-var 'KEY=VALUE' Set variable for import terraform import -var 'region=us-east-1' aws_vpc.main vpc-abc123
-var-file=FILE Load variables from file terraform import -var-file=prod.tfvars aws_s3_bucket.logs my-logs
-allow-missing-config Import even without matching config terraform import -allow-missing-config aws_instance.temp i-abc
-input=false Disable interactive prompts terraform import -input=false aws_rds_instance.db mydb 
# ── CLI import examples ─────────────────────────────────

# Import an EC2 instance
terraform import aws_instance.web i-1234567890abcdef0

# Import an S3 bucket
terraform import aws_s3_bucket.data my-data-bucket

# Import a resource into a module
terraform import module.vpc.aws_vpc.main vpc-abc123

# Import an indexed resource
terraform import 'aws_subnet.private[0]' subnet-abc123

# Import a resource with a string key
terraform import 'aws_route53_record.www["www"]' Z1234_www.example.com_A
import {} Block (Terraform 1.5+)

The modern, declarative approach to importing resources.

# ── Import block syntax ──────────────────────────────────

# Basic import block
import {
  to = aws_instance.web
  id = "i-1234567890abcdef0"
}

# Import into a module
import {
  to = module.networking.aws_vpc.main
  id = "vpc-abc123"
}

# Import with for_each (Terraform 1.7+)
import {
  for_each = var.existing_buckets
  to       = aws_s3_bucket.imported[each.key]
  id       = each.value
}

# Auto-generate configuration for imported resources
# Run: terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf
moved {} Block (Terraform 1.1+)

Declarative resource renaming and refactoring.

Use Case moved Block Example
Rename a resource moved { from = aws_instance.old to = aws_instance.new }
Move into a module moved { from = aws_vpc.main to = module.net.aws_vpc.main }
Change index key type moved { from = aws_instance.app[0] to = aws_instance.app["primary"] }
Rename a module call moved { from = module.old_name to = module.new_name } 
# ── moved block examples ─────────────────────────────────

# Rename a resource (prevents destroy + recreate)
moved {
  from = aws_instance.web_server
  to   = aws_instance.application
}

# Move a resource into a module
moved {
  from = aws_security_group.web
  to   = module.security.aws_security_group.web
}

# Refactor count to for_each
moved {
  from = aws_subnet.private[0]
  to   = aws_subnet.private["us-east-1a"]
}

moved {
  from = aws_subnet.private[1]
  to   = aws_subnet.private["us-east-1b"]
}
📘 Import Best Practice

For new imports, always prefer the import {} block over terraform import CLI. Combined with -generate-config-out, you can import resources and auto-generate their configuration in one step. After a successful apply, remove the import blocks — they're no longer needed.

removed {} Block (Terraform 1.7+)

Declaratively remove resources from state without destroying them.

# ── removed block (Terraform 1.7+) ───────────────────────

# Stop managing a resource without destroying it
removed {
  from = aws_instance.legacy_server

  lifecycle {
    destroy = false
  }
}

# Remove an entire module from management
removed {
  from = module.deprecated_service

  lifecycle {
    destroy = false
  }
}

A.5 Utility Commands

UTILITIES 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: terraform console | terraform force-unlock

Utility commands help with debugging, testing expressions, managing locks, and generating documentation. These commands don't modify infrastructure but are invaluable for troubleshooting and development workflows.

💻 Utility & Debugging Commands

terraform console

Interactive REPL for evaluating Terraform expressions against current state and configuration.

Command / Flag Description Example
console Start interactive console terraform console
console -var 'K=V' Console with variable set terraform console -var 'env=dev'
console -plan Evaluate against planned state terraform console -plan
Pipe expression Evaluate without interactive mode echo 'var.name' | terraform console 
# ── terraform console examples ──────────────────────────

# Start interactive console
terraform console
# > var.region
# "us-east-1"
# > length(var.subnets)
# 3
# > cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, 1)
# "10.0.1.0/24"
# > [for s in var.subnets : upper(s)]
# ["US-EAST-1A", "US-EAST-1B", "US-EAST-1C"]
# > exit

# One-liner evaluation (useful in scripts)
echo 'cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, 5)' | terraform console
# 10.0.5.0/24

# Test complex expressions
echo 'formatdate("YYYY-MM-DD", timestamp())' | terraform console
terraform taint, untaint & force-unlock

Resource lifecycle and lock management utilities.

Command Description Notes
taint ADDR Mark resource for recreation on next apply ⚠️ Deprecated in 1.5+ — use -replace flag instead
untaint ADDR Remove taint from a resource Undo an accidental taint
force-unlock LOCK_ID Release a stuck state lock ⚠️ Use only when lock is orphaned
force-unlock -force Force unlock without confirmation For automation only 
# ── Resource lifecycle commands ──────────────────────────

# Legacy taint (deprecated — use -replace instead)
terraform taint aws_instance.web
# Preferred alternative:
terraform apply -replace=aws_instance.web

# Remove taint
terraform untaint aws_instance.web

# Force unlock a stuck state lock
# (get LOCK_ID from the error message)
terraform force-unlock 9a5b7c3d-1234-5678-abcd-ef0123456789
⚠️ force-unlock Safety

Only use terraform force-unlock when you are certain the lock is orphaned (e.g., a CI/CD runner crashed mid-apply). If another process is genuinely running, force-unlocking can cause state corruption. Always check for running processes first.

Other Utility Commands

Authentication, version management, and testing utilities.

Command Description Example
login Authenticate to HCP Terraform / Enterprise terraform login
login HOSTNAME Login to a specific TFE instance terraform login tfe.company.com
logout Remove stored credentials terraform logout
version Show Terraform and provider versions terraform version
version -json Version info as JSON terraform version -json
test Run integration tests (1.6+) terraform test
test -filter=NAME Run specific test file terraform test -filter=tests/vpc.tftest.hcl
test -verbose Show detailed test output terraform test -verbose
metadata functions List all built-in functions (1.4+) terraform metadata functions -json 
# ── Utility command examples ────────────────────────────

# Check Terraform version
terraform version
# Terraform v1.9.5
# on linux_amd64
# + provider registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws v5.60.0

# Login to HCP Terraform
terraform login
# Opens browser for OAuth authentication

# Run tests
terraform test
# tests/network.tftest.hcl... pass
# tests/compute.tftest.hcl... pass

# Run specific test with verbose output
terraform test -filter=tests/network.tftest.hcl -verbose

# List all available functions
terraform metadata functions -json | jq '.function_signatures | keys'
📘 Quick Command Cheat Sheet

The most common workflow: terraform init → terraform fmt → terraform validate → terraform plan -out=tfplan → terraform apply tfplan. For debugging: terraform console (test expressions), terraform state list (check state), terraform show (inspect state/plan).

A.6 Environment Variables

CONFIG 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Environment Variables | TF_VAR_ Variables

Terraform reads several environment variables to control its behavior — from logging verbosity to provider credentials to input variable values. Understanding these is essential for CI/CD pipelines and debugging.

💻 Environment Variables Reference

Core Terraform Environment Variables

These environment variables control Terraform's global behavior.

Variable Description Example Value
TF_VAR_name Set input variable name via environment TF_VAR_region=us-east-1
TF_LOG Enable logging (TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR) TF_LOG=DEBUG
TF_LOG_PATH Write logs to a file TF_LOG_PATH=./terraform.log
TF_LOG_CORE Log level for Terraform core only TF_LOG_CORE=WARN
TF_LOG_PROVIDER Log level for providers only TF_LOG_PROVIDER=DEBUG
TF_INPUT Disable interactive prompts (0 or false) TF_INPUT=0
TF_IN_AUTOMATION Adjust output for CI/CD (non-empty = true) TF_IN_AUTOMATION=1
TF_DATA_DIR Override location of .terraform directory TF_DATA_DIR=/tmp/tf-data
TF_WORKSPACE Override the current workspace TF_WORKSPACE=staging
TF_CLI_ARGS Set default arguments for all commands TF_CLI_ARGS="-no-color"
TF_CLI_ARGS_plan Set default arguments for plan command TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-compact-warnings"
TF_CLI_ARGS_apply Set default arguments for apply command TF_CLI_ARGS_apply="-auto-approve"
TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR Directory for caching provider plugins TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR=$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache
TF_REGISTRY_DISCOVERY_RETRY Max retries for registry discovery TF_REGISTRY_DISCOVERY_RETRY=3
TF_REGISTRY_CLIENT_TIMEOUT Timeout for registry requests (seconds) TF_REGISTRY_CLIENT_TIMEOUT=30 
# ── Setting Terraform environment variables ──────────────

# Set input variables via environment
export TF_VAR_region="us-east-1"
export TF_VAR_instance_type="t3.large"
export TF_VAR_environment="production"

# Enable debug logging to a file
export TF_LOG=DEBUG
export TF_LOG_PATH="./terraform-debug.log"

# Debug only provider (not core) — less noise
export TF_LOG_CORE=WARN
export TF_LOG_PROVIDER=DEBUG

# CI/CD pipeline settings
export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=1
export TF_INPUT=0
export TF_CLI_ARGS_plan="-compact-warnings"
export TF_CLI_ARGS_apply="-auto-approve"

# Plugin caching (saves bandwidth and time)
export TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR="$HOME/.terraform.d/plugin-cache"
mkdir -p "$TF_PLUGIN_CACHE_DIR"
Common Provider Environment Variables

Major providers read credentials from environment variables — avoiding hardcoded secrets.

Provider Variable Description
AWS AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID AWS access key
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY AWS secret key
AWS_DEFAULT_REGION Default AWS region
Azure ARM_CLIENT_ID Azure service principal ID
ARM_CLIENT_SECRET Azure service principal secret
ARM_SUBSCRIPTION_ID Azure subscription
GCP GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS Path to or contents of service account JSON
GOOGLE_PROJECT Default GCP project
HCP Terraform TF_TOKEN_app_terraform_io API token for app.terraform.io
TF_CLOUD_ORGANIZATION Default organization
📘 Variable Precedence

Terraform variable values are resolved in this order (later overrides earlier): 1) Default value in variable {} block → 2) terraform.tfvars / *.auto.tfvars → 3) -var-file flag → 4) TF_VAR_ environment variable → 5) -var CLI flag → 6) Interactive prompt. Environment variables (TF_VAR_) rank high, making them ideal for CI/CD secrets.

A.7 Exit Codes

REFERENCE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: CLI Commands Overview | Plan Exit Codes

Terraform CLI commands return specific exit codes that indicate success, failure, or special conditions. Understanding these is critical for CI/CD pipeline logic, scripting, and automated decision-making.

💻 Exit Code Reference

General Exit Codes (Most Commands)

These exit codes apply to the majority of Terraform commands.

Exit Code Meaning When
0 Success Command completed without errors
1 Error Command failed (config error, API error, etc.)
terraform plan Exit Codes (with -detailed-exitcode)

When using -detailed-exitcode, terraform plan provides more granular exit codes for CI/CD automation.

Exit Code Meaning CI/CD Action
0 Success, no changes needed Skip apply — infrastructure is up to date
1 Error occurred Fail the pipeline, alert team
2 Success, changes pending Proceed to approval / apply step 
# ── Using -detailed-exitcode in CI/CD ────────────────────

# Plan with detailed exit code
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=tfplan
EXIT_CODE=$?

case $EXIT_CODE in
  0)
    echo "✅ No changes needed — infrastructure is up to date"
    ;;
  1)
    echo "❌ Error during planning — check configuration"
    exit 1
    ;;
  2)
    echo "📋 Changes detected — proceeding to apply"
    terraform apply tfplan
    ;;
esac
terraform fmt & terraform test Exit Codes

Special exit codes for formatting checks and test execution.

Command Exit Code Meaning
fmt -check 0 All files are properly formatted
fmt -check 3 Some files need formatting
validate 0 Configuration is valid
validate 1 Configuration has errors
test 0 All tests passed
test 1 One or more tests failed 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CI/CD PIPELINE EXIT CODE DECISION TREE              │
│                                                                  │
│   terraform fmt -check -recursive                                │
│   ├── Exit 0 ──▶ ✅ Continue                                    │
│   └── Exit 3 ──▶ ❌ Fail: "Run terraform fmt"                   │
│                                                                  │
│   terraform validate                                             │
│   ├── Exit 0 ──▶ ✅ Continue                                    │
│   └── Exit 1 ──▶ ❌ Fail: "Fix config errors"                   │
│                                                                  │
│   terraform plan -detailed-exitcode -out=tfplan                  │
│   ├── Exit 0 ──▶ ✅ Skip apply (no changes)                     │
│   ├── Exit 1 ──▶ ❌ Fail: "Plan error"                          │
│   └── Exit 2 ──▶ 📋 Changes detected                            │
│                  └──▶ Manual approval gate                       │
│                       └──▶ terraform apply tfplan                │
│                            ├── Exit 0 ──▶ ✅ Success             │
│                            └── Exit 1 ──▶ ❌ Fail: "Apply error" │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📘 CI/CD Best Practice

Always use -detailed-exitcode with terraform plan in CI/CD pipelines. Without it, exit code 0 means both "no changes" and "changes planned" — making it impossible to distinguish between a no-op and a deployment. The three-code system (0/1/2) gives your pipeline the logic it needs to make smart decisions.

Complete Exit Code Quick Reference Card
Command Exit 0 Exit 1 Exit 2 Exit 3
init Success Error — —
validate Valid Invalid — —
plan Success Error — —
plan -detailed-exitcode No changes Error Changes pending —
apply Success Error — —
destroy Success Error — —
fmt -check Formatted Error — Unformatted files
test All pass Failures — —
☁️ 🏗️ 📦 🔌
APPENDIX B

AWS Provider Resource Quick Reference

AWS Resources Data Sources Provider Config Quick Reference
4Sections
50+Resources
30+Data Sources
☁️AWS
APPENDIX ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

A comprehensive quick-reference of the most frequently used AWS provider resources, data sources, common arguments, and provider configuration options. Bookmark this for daily Terraform + AWS work.

What You'll Find Here

  • Most used AWS resources with their key arguments and attributes
  • Common data sources for querying existing AWS infrastructure
  • Quick reference for resource argument patterns across services
  • Complete AWS provider configuration options and authentication methods

Appendix Contents

B.1Most Used Resources B.2Most Used Data Sources B.3Resource Arguments Quick Reference B.4Provider Configuration Reference

B.1 Most Used Resources

RESOURCES 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Provider Docs | AWS Getting Started

The most commonly used AWS resources in Terraform projects, organized by service category. Each entry shows the resource type, key required/optional arguments, and important exported attributes.

💻 AWS Resources by Service

Compute Resources
Resource Key Arguments Key Attributes
aws_instance ami, instance_type, subnet_id, vpc_security_group_ids, key_name, user_data id, public_ip, private_ip, arn
aws_launch_template image_id, instance_type, network_interfaces, block_device_mappings id, latest_version, arn
aws_autoscaling_group min_size, max_size, desired_capacity, launch_template, vpc_zone_identifier id, arn, name
aws_lambda_function function_name, role, handler, runtime, filename/s3_bucket arn, invoke_arn, qualified_arn
aws_ecs_service name, cluster, task_definition, desired_count, launch_type id, iam_role, cluster
Networking Resources
Resource Key Arguments Key Attributes
aws_vpc cidr_block, enable_dns_support, enable_dns_hostnames id, arn, default_route_table_id
aws_subnet vpc_id, cidr_block, availability_zone, map_public_ip_on_launch id, arn, ipv6_cidr_block
aws_security_group vpc_id, name, description, ingress, egress id, arn, owner_id
aws_internet_gateway vpc_id id, arn, owner_id
aws_nat_gateway allocation_id, subnet_id id, public_ip, private_ip
aws_route_table vpc_id, route id, arn, owner_id
aws_lb name, internal, load_balancer_type, subnets, security_groups arn, dns_name, zone_id
Storage & Database Resources
Resource Key Arguments Key Attributes
aws_s3_bucket bucket id, arn, bucket_domain_name, region
aws_s3_object bucket, key, source/content id, etag, version_id
aws_db_instance engine, instance_class, allocated_storage, username, password endpoint, arn, address, port
aws_dynamodb_table name, billing_mode, hash_key, attribute arn, id, stream_arn
aws_elasticache_cluster cluster_id, engine, node_type, num_cache_nodes cache_nodes, configuration_endpoint
IAM & Security Resources
Resource Key Arguments Key Attributes
aws_iam_role name, assume_role_policy arn, id, name, unique_id
aws_iam_policy name, policy (JSON) arn, id, policy_id
aws_iam_role_policy_attachment role, policy_arn id
aws_iam_instance_profile name, role arn, id, unique_id
aws_kms_key description, key_usage, policy arn, key_id, id
📘 Resource Naming Convention

All AWS resources follow the pattern aws_<service>_<resource>. For example: aws_s3_bucket, aws_iam_role, aws_lambda_function. Data sources follow the same pattern: data.aws_ami.example.

B.2 Most Used Data Sources

DATA SOURCES 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Provider Data Sources | Data Sources Concepts

Data sources let you query existing AWS resources without managing them. They're essential for referencing shared infrastructure, looking up AMIs, and reading configuration from your AWS account.

💻 Common AWS Data Sources

Data Source Purpose Key Filters / Arguments Key Attributes
aws_ami Look up AMI by filters filter, owners, most_recent id, name, image_id
aws_vpc Look up existing VPC id, tags, default cidr_block, id, arn
aws_subnets List subnets matching filters filter, tags ids (list)
aws_availability_zones List available AZs state, filter names, zone_ids
aws_caller_identity Current AWS account info (none) account_id, arn, user_id
aws_region Current region info name name, endpoint
aws_iam_policy_document Build IAM JSON policies in HCL statement blocks json
aws_s3_bucket Look up existing bucket bucket arn, region, hosted_zone_id
aws_route53_zone Look up hosted zone name, private_zone zone_id, name_servers
aws_secretsmanager_secret_version Retrieve secret value secret_id secret_string, secret_binary 
# ── Common data source patterns ─────────────────────────

# Look up the latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI
data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]

  filter {
    name   = "name"
    values = ["al2023-ami-*-x86_64"]
  }
}

# Get current account and region info
data "aws_caller_identity" "current" {}
data "aws_region" "current" {}

# Look up existing VPC by tag
data "aws_vpc" "main" {
  filter {
    name   = "tag:Name"
    values = ["production-vpc"]
  }
}

# Get all available AZs in the region
data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}

# Build IAM policy with HCL (type-safe, no raw JSON)
data "aws_iam_policy_document" "s3_read" {
  statement {
    actions   = ["s3:GetObject", "s3:ListBucket"]
    resources = ["arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket", "arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*"]
  }
}

B.3 Resource Arguments Quick Reference

ARGUMENTS 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Resource Syntax | Resource Behavior

Common argument patterns that appear across many AWS resources. Learning these patterns means you can work with new resources faster — most AWS resources share the same tagging, encryption, and networking argument structures.

💻 Common Argument Patterns

Pattern Argument Used In Example
Tagging tags Almost all resources tags = { Name = "web" }
tags_all Computed (includes defaults) Read-only attribute
Encryption kms_key_id S3, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB kms_key_id = aws_kms_key.db.arn
encrypted EBS, EFS encrypted = true
Networking vpc_id SG, Subnet, IGW, NAT vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
subnet_id Instance, NAT, ENI subnet_id = aws_subnet.pub.id
Dependencies depends_on All resources depends_on = [aws_iam_role.r]
Lifecycle lifecycle {} All resources prevent_destroy = true
Provisioners provisioner All resources (last resort) provisioner "local-exec" {} 
# ── Common argument patterns in practice ──────────────────

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  subnet_id     = aws_subnet.public[0].id

  vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web.id]

  root_block_device {
    volume_size = 20
    encrypted   = true
    kms_key_id  = aws_kms_key.ebs.arn
  }

  tags = {
    Name        = "web-server"
    Environment = var.environment
    ManagedBy   = "terraform"
  }

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
    ignore_changes        = [ami]
  }
}
📘 Default Tags

Instead of repeating tags on every resource, configure default_tags in the provider block. These apply automatically to all resources that support tags: provider "aws" { default_tags { tags = { ManagedBy = "terraform" } } }

B.4 Provider Configuration Reference

PROVIDER 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Provider Configuration | Authentication

Complete reference for configuring the AWS provider — authentication methods, assume role, default tags, and multi-region setups.

💻 Provider Configuration

Argument Description Example
region AWS region (required) region = "us-east-1"
profile Named AWS CLI profile profile = "production"
shared_credentials_files Custom credentials file path ["/custom/.aws/credentials"]
assume_role {} Cross-account role assumption See example below
default_tags {} Tags applied to all resources See example below
alias Named provider configuration alias = "west"
allowed_account_ids Restrict to specific accounts ["123456789012"]
