# ── Complete AWS provider configuration ──────────────────

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"

  # Safety: restrict to known account
  allowed_account_ids = ["123456789012"]

  # Cross-account access
  assume_role {
    role_arn     = "arn:aws:iam::654321098765:role/TerraformDeploy"
    session_name = "terraform-deploy"
    external_id  = "unique-external-id"
  }

  # Default tags for ALL resources
  default_tags {
    tags = {
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
      Project     = "platform"
      Environment = var.environment
    }
  }
}

# Multi-region setup with alias
provider "aws" {
  alias  = "west"
  region = "us-west-2"
}

provider "aws" {
  alias  = "eu"
  region = "eu-west-1"
}

# Use aliased provider in resources
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "dr_backup" {
  provider = aws.west
  bucket   = "dr-backup-west"
}

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           AWS PROVIDER AUTHENTICATION CHAIN                      │
│                                                                  │
│   Terraform checks these in order (first match wins):            │
│                                                                  │
│   1. Provider block ──▶ access_key / secret_key (avoid!)        │
│   2. Environment    ──▶ AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY│
│   3. Shared Config  ──▶ ~/.aws/credentials + ~/.aws/config      │
│   4. IAM Role       ──▶ EC2 Instance Profile / ECS Task Role    │
│   5. Web Identity   ──▶ OIDC (GitHub Actions, K8s)              │
│                                                                  │
│   ✅ Best practice: Use IAM roles or OIDC — never hardcode keys │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
⚠️ Never Hardcode Credentials

Never put access_key and secret_key directly in provider blocks — they end up in version control. Use environment variables, IAM roles, or OIDC federation instead. For CI/CD (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI), use OIDC with assume_role_with_web_identity.

📝 🔤 🧩 ⚙️
APPENDIX C

HCL Language Quick Reference

HCL Syntax Variable Types Functions Expressions
6Sections
40+Functions
8Types
📝HCL
APPENDIX ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

A complete quick-reference for the HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL) used by Terraform — block syntax, variable types, meta-arguments, lifecycle rules, built-in functions, and expression patterns.

What You'll Find Here

  • All HCL block types and their syntax structure
  • Variable and output type system reference
  • Meta-arguments and lifecycle rules quick lookup
  • Most-used built-in functions with examples

Appendix Contents

C.1Block Syntax C.2Variable Types C.3Meta-Arguments C.4Lifecycle Arguments C.5Common Functions C.6Expression Patterns

C.1 Block Syntax

SYNTAX 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Configuration Syntax | Terraform Language

Every Terraform configuration is built from blocks. Each block type serves a specific purpose — from declaring infrastructure to configuring behavior.

💻 All Block Types

Block Type Purpose Labels Syntax
terraform {} Settings, backend, required providers None terraform { ... }
provider Configure a provider Provider name provider "aws" { ... }
resource Declare managed infrastructure Type + Name resource "aws_instance" "web" { ... }
data Query existing infrastructure Type + Name data "aws_ami" "latest" { ... }
variable Input parameters Variable name variable "region" { ... }
output Export values Output name output "ip" { ... }
locals Local computed values None locals { ... }
module Call a reusable module Module name module "vpc" { ... }
moved Refactor without destroy None moved { from = ... to = ... }
import Import existing resources (v1.5+) None import { to = ... id = ... } 
# ── Block syntax overview ────────────────────────────────

# Terraform settings block (no labels)
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5"
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "my-tf-state"
    key    = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Provider block (1 label: provider name)
provider "aws" {
  region = var.region
}

# Resource block (2 labels: type + name)
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.latest.id
  instance_type = var.instance_type
}

# Data source block (2 labels: type + name)
data "aws_ami" "latest" {
  most_recent = true
  owners      = ["amazon"]
}

# Variable block (1 label: variable name)
variable "region" {
  type    = string
  default = "us-east-1"
}

# Output block (1 label: output name)
output "instance_ip" {
  value = aws_instance.web.public_ip
}

# Locals block (no labels)
locals {
  env_prefix = "${var.project}-${var.environment}"
}

# Module block (1 label: module name)
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.1.0"
}

C.2 Variable Types

TYPES 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Input Variables | Type Constraints

Terraform supports primitive types, collection types, and structural types. Understanding the type system is critical for writing type-safe modules and passing complex data structures.

💻 Type System Reference

Category Type Description Example Value
Primitive string Text value "hello"
number Numeric value (int or float) 42, 3.14
bool Boolean true/false true, false
Collection list(type) Ordered sequence (same type) ["a", "b", "c"]
set(type) Unordered unique values toset(["a", "b"])
map(type) Key-value pairs (same value type) { dev = "t3.micro" }
Structural object({ ... }) Named attributes (mixed types) { name = "x", port = 80 }
tuple([...]) Fixed-length, mixed-type sequence ["hello", 42, true]
Special any Accepts any type (avoid in modules) Anything 
# ── Variable type examples ───────────────────────────────

# Primitive types
variable "environment" {
  type        = string
  description = "Deployment environment"
  default     = "dev"

  validation {
    condition     = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment)
    error_message = "Must be dev, staging, or prod."
  }
}

variable "instance_count" {
  type    = number
  default = 2
}

variable "enable_monitoring" {
  type    = bool
  default = true
}

# Collection types
variable "availability_zones" {
  type    = list(string)
  default = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]
}

variable "instance_types" {
  type = map(string)
  default = {
    dev  = "t3.micro"
    prod = "t3.large"
  }
}

# Structural type (object)
variable "database_config" {
  type = object({
    engine         = string
    instance_class = string
    storage_gb     = number
    multi_az       = bool
    tags           = map(string)
  })
  default = {
    engine         = "postgres"
    instance_class = "db.t3.micro"
    storage_gb     = 20
    multi_az       = false
    tags           = {}
  }
}

# Complex nested type
variable "services" {
  type = list(object({
    name     = string
    port     = number
    protocol = optional(string, "HTTP")  # optional with default (v1.3+)
    health_check = optional(object({
      path     = string
      interval = number
    }))
  }))
}
📘 Optional Attributes (v1.3+)

Use optional(type, default) inside object types to make attributes optional. This is especially useful in module interfaces: optional(string, "HTTP") means the caller can omit the attribute and it defaults to "HTTP".

C.3 Meta-Arguments

META 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: count | for_each | depends_on

Meta-arguments are special arguments available on ALL resource and module blocks. They control how Terraform creates, updates, and manages resources.

💻 Meta-Arguments Reference

Meta-Argument Purpose Works With Key Behaviors
count Create N copies of a resource Resources, Modules Index-based (count.index), creates list
for_each Create one copy per map/set item Resources, Modules Key-based (each.key, each.value), creates map
depends_on Explicit dependency ordering Resources, Modules Override auto-detection (use sparingly)
provider Select non-default provider Resources, Modules provider = aws.west
lifecycle Control create/update/destroy Resources See Section C.4 
# ── count vs for_each comparison ──────────────────────────

# COUNT — good for simple numeric scaling
resource "aws_instance" "worker" {
  count         = var.worker_count
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  tags = { Name = "worker-${count.index}" }
}
# Reference: aws_instance.worker[0], aws_instance.worker[1]

# FOR_EACH with set — good for named resources
resource "aws_iam_user" "users" {
  for_each = toset(["alice", "bob", "carol"])
  name     = each.value
}
# Reference: aws_iam_user.users["alice"]

# FOR_EACH with map — good for config-driven resources
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "buckets" {
  for_each = {
    logs   = "my-logs-bucket"
    assets = "my-assets-bucket"
    backup = "my-backup-bucket"
  }
  bucket = each.value
  tags   = { Purpose = each.key }
}
# Reference: aws_s3_bucket.buckets["logs"]

# DEPENDS_ON — explicit dependency (last resort)
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  depends_on = [aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.app]
}

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              count vs for_each Decision Guide                    │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────────╗                    │
│  │ Resources are  │────▶║  Use for_each     ║                    │
│  │ uniquely named │     ║  (map or set)     ║                    │
│  └───────────────┘     ╚═══════════════════╝                    │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────────╗                    │
│  │ Resources are  │────▶║  Use count        ║                    │
│  │ identical/     │     ║  (integer)        ║                    │
│  │ interchangeable│     ╚═══════════════════╝                    │
│  └───────────────┘                                               │
│                                                                  │
│  ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────────╗                    │
│  │ Toggle on/off  │────▶║  Use count        ║                    │
│  │ conditionally  │     ║  count = flag?1:0 ║                    │
│  └───────────────┘     ╚═══════════════════╝                    │
│                                                                  │
│  ⚠ count: removing item 0 shifts all → for_each is safer       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

C.4 Lifecycle Arguments

LIFECYCLE 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Lifecycle Meta-Argument | Resource Behavior

The lifecycle block controls how Terraform handles resource creation, updates, and destruction. These are critical for production stability and zero-downtime deployments.

💻 Lifecycle Arguments

Argument Type Purpose Use Case
create_before_destroy bool Create replacement before destroying old Zero-downtime updates (ASG, LB targets)
prevent_destroy bool Block terraform destroy for this resource Protect databases, important state
ignore_changes list Ignore external changes to listed attributes Auto-scaled groups, externally modified tags
replace_triggered_by list Force replace when referenced values change Restart instance when user_data changes
precondition block Validate before apply Check AMI region matches provider region
postcondition block Validate after apply Confirm public IP was assigned 
# ── Lifecycle arguments in practice ──────────────────────

# Protect critical resources from destruction
resource "aws_db_instance" "production" {
  engine            = "postgres"
  instance_class    = "db.r5.large"
  allocated_storage = 100

  lifecycle {
    prevent_destroy = true
  }
}

# Zero-downtime replacement
resource "aws_launch_template" "app" {
  image_id      = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.medium"

  lifecycle {
    create_before_destroy = true
  }
}

# Ignore externally-managed attributes
resource "aws_autoscaling_group" "web" {
  min_size         = 2
  max_size         = 10
  desired_capacity = 2

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [desired_capacity, target_group_arns]
  }
}

# Force replacement on dependency change
resource "aws_instance" "app" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"
  user_data     = file("init.sh")

  lifecycle {
    replace_triggered_by = [null_resource.config_version]
  }
}

# Pre/post conditions (v1.2+)
resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = var.ami_id
  instance_type = "t3.micro"

  lifecycle {
    precondition {
      condition     = var.environment != "prod" || var.instance_type != "t3.micro"
      error_message = "Production must use larger than t3.micro."
    }
    postcondition {
      condition     = self.public_ip != ""
      error_message = "Instance must have a public IP."
    }
  }
}

C.5 Common Functions

FUNCTIONS 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Built-in Functions | templatefile

Terraform provides dozens of built-in functions for string manipulation, collection transformations, encoding, file operations, and more. Here are the most commonly used ones.

💻 Functions by Category

String Functions
Function Description Example Result
format() Format string (printf-style) format("Hello, %s!", "world") "Hello, world!"
join() Join list to string join(", ", ["a","b","c"]) "a, b, c"
split() Split string to list split(",", "a,b,c") ["a","b","c"]
lower() / upper() Change case lower("HELLO") "hello"
replace() String replacement replace("hello", "l", "L") "heLLo"
trimspace() Remove leading/trailing whitespace trimspace(" hi ") "hi"
regex() Regex match regex("^[a-z]+", "hello123") "hello"
Collection Functions
Function Description Example Result
length() Length of list/map/string length(["a","b","c"]) 3
lookup() Map lookup with default lookup({a=1}, "b", 0) 0
merge() Merge maps merge({a=1}, {b=2}) {a=1, b=2}
concat() Concatenate lists concat(["a"], ["b"]) ["a","b"]
flatten() Flatten nested lists flatten([["a"],["b"]]) ["a","b"]
toset() Convert to set (unique values) toset(["a","a","b"]) set("a","b")
keys() / values() Extract map keys/values keys({a=1, b=2}) ["a","b"]
contains() Check if list contains value contains(["a","b"], "a") true
element() List element by index (wraps) element(["a","b"], 3) "b" (wraps)
Encoding, File & Type Functions
Function Description Common Use
jsonencode() / jsondecode() JSON encode/decode IAM policies, Lambda env vars
yamldecode() Parse YAML file content Config files, Kubernetes manifests
base64encode() Base64 encode User data scripts
file() Read file contents as string SSH keys, scripts, certificates
templatefile() Render template with variables User data, config file generation
try() Return first non-error expression Safe attribute access
can() Test if expression is valid Validation conditions
coalesce() First non-null/empty value Default fallback chains

C.6 Expression Patterns

EXPRESSIONS 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Expressions | for Expressions

HCL expressions let you compute values dynamically — conditional logic, loops, string interpolation, splat expressions, and dynamic blocks. Master these patterns for advanced Terraform configurations.

💻 Expression Patterns

Pattern Syntax Purpose
String Interpolation "${var.name}-${var.env}" Embed expressions in strings
Conditional condition ? true_val : false_val Ternary if/else
For (list) [for s in var.list : upper(s)] Transform list → list
For (map) {for s in var.list : s.name => s.value} Transform list → map
For (filter) [for s in var.list : s if s.enabled] Filter with condition
Splat aws_instance.web[*].id Extract attribute from all instances
Dynamic Block dynamic "ingress" { ... } Generate repeated nested blocks 
# ── Expression patterns in practice ──────────────────────

# Conditional expression — toggle resources
resource "aws_eip" "nat" {
  count  = var.enable_nat ? 1 : 0
  domain = "vpc"
}

# For expression — transform a list
locals {
  upper_names = [for name in var.users : upper(name)]

  # List → Map
  user_map = {
    for user in var.users :
    user.name => user.email
  }

  # Filter with condition
  admins = [
    for user in var.users :
    user.name
    if user.role == "admin"
  ]
}

# Splat expression — shorthand for for
output "instance_ids" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].id
  # Equivalent: [for i in aws_instance.web : i.id]
}

# Dynamic blocks — generate repeated nested blocks
resource "aws_security_group" "web" {
  name   = "web-sg"
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id

  dynamic "ingress" {
    for_each = var.ingress_rules
    content {
      from_port   = ingress.value.port
      to_port     = ingress.value.port
      protocol    = ingress.value.protocol
      cidr_blocks = ingress.value.cidr_blocks
    }
  }
}

# Nested for expressions
locals {
  subnet_pairs = flatten([
    for az in var.availability_zones : [
      for tier in ["public", "private"] : {
        az   = az
        tier = tier
        cidr = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, index(var.availability_zones, az) * 2 + (tier == "private" ? 1 : 0))
      }
    ]
  ])
}
⚠️ Dynamic Block Warning

Use dynamic blocks only when the number of nested blocks is truly variable. For a fixed set of blocks, write them explicitly — it's clearer and easier to review. Over-using dynamic blocks makes configurations hard to read and debug.

🔄 📦 🚀 🔀
APPENDIX D

Migration Guides

CloudFormation Import Resources Version Upgrades Provider Migration
4Sections
3Migration Paths
🔄Migrations
✅Tested
APPENDIX ADVANCED ★★★★★

Step-by-step guides for migrating to Terraform from other tools, importing existing infrastructure, and upgrading between Terraform and provider versions safely.

What You'll Find Here

  • CloudFormation to Terraform migration strategy and tooling
  • Importing existing AWS resources into Terraform state
  • Terraform version upgrade procedures and breaking changes
  • AWS provider major version migration steps

Appendix Contents

D.1CloudFormation to Terraform D.2Manual AWS Resources to Terraform D.3Terraform Version Upgrades D.4Provider Version Migrations

D.1 CloudFormation to Terraform

MIGRATION ADVANCED
📚 Official Documentation: Import | terraform import CLI

Migrating from AWS CloudFormation to Terraform requires understanding the conceptual mapping between the two tools and a systematic approach to re-creating your stacks as Terraform configurations.

💻 Migration Strategy


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           CLOUDFORMATION → TERRAFORM MIGRATION WORKFLOW          │
│                                                                  │
│   Phase 1: Inventory                                             │
│   ├── List all CFN stacks (aws cloudformation list-stacks)      │
│   ├── Export stack resources and their Physical IDs              │
│   └── Map CFN resource types → Terraform resource types         │
│                                                                  │
│   Phase 2: Write Terraform Config                                │
│   ├── Create provider, backend, variables                       │
│   ├── Write resource blocks matching CFN resources               │
│   └── Use terraform plan to validate structure                   │
│                                                                  │
│   Phase 3: Import State                                          │
│   ├── terraform import <resource> <physical_id>                │
│   ├── Run terraform plan — fix any drift                        │
│   └── Repeat until plan shows "No changes"                      │
│                                                                  │
│   Phase 4: Cutover                                               │
│   ├── Delete CFN stack with DeletionPolicy: Retain              │
│   ├── Verify resources still exist — owned by Terraform now     │
│   └── Run terraform plan — confirm clean state                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Resource Type Mapping
CloudFormation Type Terraform Resource Import ID
AWS::EC2::Instance aws_instance Instance ID (i-1234abcd)
AWS::EC2::VPC aws_vpc VPC ID (vpc-1234abcd)
AWS::EC2::Subnet aws_subnet Subnet ID (subnet-1234abcd)
AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup aws_security_group SG ID (sg-1234abcd)
AWS::S3::Bucket aws_s3_bucket Bucket name (my-bucket)
AWS::RDS::DBInstance aws_db_instance DB Identifier (mydb)
AWS::IAM::Role aws_iam_role Role name (MyRole)
AWS::Lambda::Function aws_lambda_function Function name (my-func)
⚠️ Critical: DeletionPolicy

Before deleting CloudFormation stacks, set DeletionPolicy: Retain on ALL resources. If you delete a CFN stack without this, AWS will delete the actual underlying resources — even though Terraform has already imported them.

D.2 Manual AWS Resources to Terraform

IMPORT 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Import Block (v1.5+) | Config Generation

Import manually created (ClickOps) AWS resources into Terraform state. Terraform 1.5+ supports declarative import blocks and can auto-generate configuration.

💻 Import Methods

Method Terraform Version Pros Cons
terraform import CLI All versions Works everywhere, well documented Must write config first, one resource at a time
import {} block v1.5+ Declarative, plan-previewable, bulk imports Requires v1.5+
-generate-config-out v1.5+ Auto-generates HCL from imported resource Generated code needs cleanup 
# ── Method 1: CLI import (all versions) ──────────────────
# Step 1: Write the resource block first (empty or partial)
resource "aws_instance" "imported_server" {
  # Will be populated after import
}

# Step 2: Run import command
# $ terraform import aws_instance.imported_server i-0abc123def456

# Step 3: Run terraform plan, fill in missing arguments
# Step 4: Iterate until plan shows "No changes"


# ── Method 2: Import block (v1.5+) ──────────────────────
import {
  to = aws_instance.imported_server
  id = "i-0abc123def456"
}

import {
  to = aws_s3_bucket.legacy_logs
  id = "my-legacy-logs-bucket"
}

import {
  to = aws_security_group.legacy_sg
  id = "sg-0abc123def456"
}

# Run: terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf
# This creates generated.tf with auto-generated config!
# Then review, clean up, and run terraform apply

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          IMPORT WORKFLOW (v1.5+ Recommended)                     │
│                                                                  │
│   1. Write import blocks ──────────────────────────────┐        │
│      import { to = aws_vpc.main  id = "vpc-123" }      │        │
│      import { to = aws_subnet.a  id = "subnet-456" }   │        │
│                                                         │        │
│   2. Generate config ◀──────────────────────────────────┘        │
│      $ terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf          │
│                                                                  │
│   3. Review & clean up generated.tf                          │
│      • Remove computed-only attributes                           │
│      • Replace hardcoded values with variables                   │
│      • Add lifecycle rules if needed                             │
│                                                                  │
│   4. Apply to import into state                                  │
│      $ terraform apply                                           │
│                                                                  │
│   5. Verify — plan should show "No changes"                     │
│      $ terraform plan                                            │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📘 Find Resource IDs for Import

Each resource type has its own import ID format (documented on its registry page). Common patterns: EC2 uses instance ID (i-xxx), S3 uses bucket name, IAM uses name, VPC resources use their AWS ID (vpc-xxx, subnet-xxx).

D.3 Terraform Version Upgrades

UPGRADES ADVANCED
📚 Official Documentation: Upgrade Guides | Version Constraints

Safely upgrading between Terraform versions requires understanding breaking changes, state format upgrades, and the recommended step-by-step upgrade process.

💻 Version History & Key Changes

Version Key Changes Breaking? State Format
0.12 HCL2, first-class expressions, for/for_each ✅ Major v3 → v4
0.13 required_providers block, count/for_each on modules ⚠️ Minor v4
0.14 Sensitive variables, concise plan output No v4
0.15 moved blocks, structured output ⚠️ Minor v4
1.0 Stability promise, no new breaking changes in 1.x No v4
1.1 moved blocks in config No v4
1.2 Preconditions/postconditions No v4
1.3 optional() object attributes No v4
1.5 import blocks, check blocks, config generation No v4
1.6+ terraform test framework No v4 
# ── Safe upgrade procedure ───────────────────────────────

# Step 1: Lock current version
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.4, < 1.6"  # Current constraint
}

# Step 2: Read the upgrade guide for target version
# Step 3: Back up state
# $ terraform state pull > terraform.tfstate.backup

# Step 4: Install new version (use tfenv or manual)
# $ tfenv install 1.6.0
# $ tfenv use 1.6.0

# Step 5: Run init to upgrade providers and modules
# $ terraform init -upgrade

# Step 6: Run plan — check for unexpected changes
# $ terraform plan

# Step 7: Update version constraint
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.5, < 2.0"  # Updated
}
⚠️ Never Skip Major Versions

Always upgrade one minor version at a time (0.12 → 0.13 → 0.14 → ...) for 0.x releases. For 1.x releases, you can jump between minor versions safely. Always backup state before upgrading, and never downgrade state formats — they are forward-only.

D.4 Provider Version Migrations

PROVIDERS ADVANCED
📚 Official Documentation: AWS Provider v5 Upgrade | AWS Provider v4 Upgrade

AWS provider major version upgrades introduce breaking changes — renamed resources, removed arguments, and changed defaults. Follow these steps for smooth migrations.

💻 AWS Provider Major Version Changes

Migration Key Changes Impact
v3 → v4 S3 bucket split into separate resources (aws_s3_bucket_versioning, etc.); many arguments extracted High — S3 configs need major refactoring
v4 → v5 Removed deprecated resources/arguments; default tags behavior changes; EC2 changes Medium — mostly removing deprecations 
# ── AWS Provider v3 → v4: S3 Bucket Migration ───────────

# BEFORE (v3) — everything in one resource
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "logs" {
  bucket = "my-logs"
  acl    = "private"          # ← Removed in v4

  versioning {                 # ← Moved to separate resource
    enabled = true
  }

  server_side_encryption_configuration {  # ← Separate resource
    rule {
      apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
        sse_algorithm = "AES256"
      }
    }
  }

  lifecycle_rule {             # ← Separate resource
    enabled = true
    transition {
      days          = 30
      storage_class = "STANDARD_IA"
    }
  }
}

# AFTER (v4+) — split into individual resources
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "logs" {
  bucket = "my-logs"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "logs" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.logs.id
  versioning_configuration {
    status = "Enabled"
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration" "logs" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.logs.id
  rule {
    apply_server_side_encryption_by_default {
      sse_algorithm = "AES256"
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration" "logs" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.logs.id
  rule {
    id     = "transition-to-ia"
    status = "Enabled"
    transition {
      days          = 30
      storage_class = "STANDARD_IA"
    }
  }
}

# After splitting, import the new resources:
# $ terraform import aws_s3_bucket_versioning.logs my-logs
# $ terraform import aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration.logs my-logs
# $ terraform import aws_s3_bucket_lifecycle_configuration.logs my-logs
# ── Provider upgrade procedure ───────────────────────────

# Step 1: Pin current version
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 4.0"  # Current
    }
  }
}

# Step 2: Read the upgrade guide (linked in docs)
# Step 3: Update constraint
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"  # Target
    }
  }
}

# Step 4: Run init -upgrade
# $ terraform init -upgrade

# Step 5: Run plan and fix all errors
# $ terraform plan

# Step 6: Address deprecation warnings
# Step 7: Apply when plan is clean
📘 Use moved Blocks for Renames

When a provider renames a resource type (e.g., aws_s3_bucket_object → aws_s3_object), use moved { from = aws_s3_bucket_object.x to = aws_s3_object.x } to update state without destroying and recreating the resource.

🎓 📜 ✅ 🏆

Certification Guide

Associate 003 Exam Topics Study Plan Tips
3Sections
9Exam Domains
57Questions
🎓Cert
APPENDIX INTERMEDIATE ★★★★★

Everything you need to prepare for the HashiCorp Terraform Associate (003) certification — exam structure, domain breakdown, key topics, and exam-day strategies.

What You'll Find Here

  • Complete exam structure and domain weight breakdown
  • Key topics and concepts tested in each domain
  • Common question patterns and tricky concepts
  • Exam day tips and study strategies

Appendix Contents

E.1Terraform Associate (003) Exam E.2Common Exam Questions Topics E.3Exam Day Tips

E.1 Terraform Associate (003) Exam

EXAM 📝 CERTIFICATION
📚 Official Documentation: Terraform Associate Certification | Study Guide

The Terraform Associate (003) certification validates your knowledge of Terraform concepts, workflows, and HCL syntax. Here's the complete exam breakdown.

💻 Exam Overview

Detail Specification
Exam CodeTerraform Associate 003
Duration60 minutes
Questions~57 questions
FormatMultiple choice, multiple answer, fill-in-the-blank
Passing Score~70% (not officially published)
DeliveryOnline proctored (PSI)
Validity2 years
Cost$70.50 USD
PrerequisitesNone (recommended: 6+ months hands-on experience)
Exam Domains & Weights
# Domain Weight Key Topics
1 Understand IaC Concepts ~8% IaC benefits, day 0/day 1/day 2 operations
2 Terraform Purpose (vs others) ~8% Terraform vs Ansible/CloudFormation/Pulumi, cloud-agnostic
3 Terraform Basics ~12% Providers, init, .terraform, plugin cache
4 Terraform CLI ~12% init, plan, apply, destroy, fmt, validate, taint
5 Modules ~10% Module sources, inputs/outputs, versioning, registry
6 Terraform Workflow ~12% Write → Plan → Apply, core workflow, collaboration
7 State ~14% Purpose, storage, locking, backends, sensitive data in state
8 Read/Generate/Modify Config ~16% Variables, outputs, data sources, resource addressing, expressions
9 HCP Terraform (formerly TFC) ~8% Workspaces, runs, governance, Sentinel, private registry
⚠️ Exam Version Alert

The exam was updated from 002 to 003 in 2023. Key changes: deprecated commands like terraform taint are still testable but questions now favor terraform apply -replace=. HCP Terraform (formerly Terraform Cloud) questions have increased. Always study from 003-specific materials.

# ── Sample Exam-Style Question ──────────────────────────
# Q: "What does this configuration do?"

variable "instance_count" {
  type    = number
  default = 2
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  count         = var.instance_count
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"

  tags = {
    Name = "web-${count.index}"
  }
}

output "instance_ids" {
  value = aws_instance.web[*].id
}

# Answer: Creates 2 EC2 instances named "web-0" and "web-1"
# using count meta-argument. The splat expression [*]
# collects all instance IDs into a list output.
# Key concepts tested: count, count.index, splat [*], outputs

E.2 Common Exam Question Topics

TOPICS 📝 HIGH-YIELD
📚 Official Documentation: Review Guide 003 | Sample Questions

These are the most frequently tested concepts and common "trick question" areas. Focus your study here for maximum exam impact.

💻 High-Yield Exam Topics

Topic What to Know Common Tricks
State File JSON format, stores resource mapping, sensitive data in plaintext State is NOT encrypted by default; must be stored securely
Backend Types local (default), S3, Azure Blob, GCS, Consul, HCP Terraform Backend config can't use variables or expressions
State Locking Prevents concurrent modifications; DynamoDB for S3 backend Not all backends support locking (local does not by default)
Variable Precedence CLI -var > *.auto.tfvars > terraform.tfvars > ENV > default Highest precedence wins; -var overrides everything
count vs for_each count = integer, for_each = set/map; can't use both together count.index vs each.key/each.value; for_each is more stable
Provisioners local-exec, remote-exec, file; last resort, use user_data instead Provisioners run only at creation (unless destroy provisioner)
terraform taint Marks resource for recreation; replaced by -replace flag taint is deprecated in favor of terraform apply -replace=
Data Sources Read-only, query external data, refreshed on every plan Data sources are read during plan, not apply
Workspaces CLI workspaces = separate state files; terraform.workspace CLI workspaces ≠ HCP Terraform workspaces (different concepts)
Sentinel Policy as code — HCP Terraform/Enterprise only Sentinel is NOT open source; requires paid tier 

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              EXAM CONCEPT MAP — Key Relationships                │
│                                                                  │
│   terraform init                                                 │
│   ├── Downloads providers to .terraform/                        │
│   ├── Initializes backend (state storage)                       │
│   └── Downloads modules to .terraform/modules/                  │
│                                                                  │
│   terraform plan                                                 │
│   ├── Reads current state                                       │
│   ├── Reads data sources                                        │
│   ├── Compares desired config vs actual state                   │
│   └── Shows execution plan (+ create, ~ update, - destroy)     │
│                                                                  │
│   terraform apply                                                │
│   ├── Executes plan (creates/updates/destroys)                  │
│   ├── Updates state file                                        │
│   └── Runs provisioners (if any)                                │
│                                                                  │
│   State File (terraform.tfstate)                                 │
│   ├── Maps config resources → real infrastructure              │
│   ├── Contains sensitive data in PLAIN TEXT                     │
│   ├── Must be locked during operations                          │
│   └── Should be stored remotely (S3, GCS, HCP Terraform)       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# ── Common Exam Trap: Backend Configuration ─────────────
# Q: "Is this valid backend configuration?"

variable "bucket_name" {
  default = "my-state-bucket"
}

terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = var.bucket_name   # ❌ INVALID!
    key    = "terraform.tfstate"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

# Answer: NO — backend blocks cannot use variables,
# local values, or expressions. Only literal values
# and -backend-config CLI flags are allowed.
# This is one of the MOST COMMON exam tricks!
⚠️ Top 5 Exam Traps

1. Backend config cannot use variables or expressions
2. terraform.workspace is CLI workspaces, NOT HCP Terraform workspaces
3. State file contains sensitive data in plaintext — always encrypt at rest
4. terraform taint is deprecated → use terraform apply -replace=ADDR
5. for_each only accepts map or set(string) — NOT a list

E.3 Exam Day Tips

TIPS 🎯 STRATEGY
📚 Official Documentation: Certification Page | Practice Exams

Practical tips for exam preparation and test-taking strategies to maximize your score on the Terraform Associate exam.

💻 Preparation Strategy

Phase Duration Activities
Phase 1: Learn 2-3 weeks Read official docs, follow tutorials, build real projects
Phase 2: Practice 1-2 weeks Hands-on labs, build VPC + EC2 + S3 from scratch, use modules
Phase 3: Review 3-5 days Practice exams, review weak domains, re-read exam review guide
Phase 4: Test 1 day Take practice test under timed conditions, review mistakes
Test-Taking Strategies
Strategy Details
Time Management ~1 minute per question. Flag difficult questions and return to them. Don't spend more than 2 minutes on any single question.
Elimination Eliminate obviously wrong answers first. Usually 2 of 4 options can be quickly eliminated.
Read Carefully Watch for words like "ONLY", "ALWAYS", "NEVER", "BEST" — they change the correct answer significantly.
Code Questions Read HCL snippets line-by-line. Check for syntax errors, missing arguments, and wrong block types.
Fill-in-the-blank These test exact command syntax. Practice writing commands from memory: terraform init, terraform plan -out=plan.tfplan.
📘 Essential Study Resources

Must-study: (1) Official HashiCorp Learn tutorials for certification, (2) Terraform documentation — especially the language reference, (3) Practice with real AWS/cloud resources (free tier is fine), (4) Take the official sample questions at least twice. Focus 80% of study time on Domains 7 and 8 (State + Read/Generate/Modify Config) — they make up 30% of the exam.

💼 🎤 💡 🧠
APPENDIX F

Interview Questions

Junior Mid-Level Senior Scenario-Based
4Levels
40+Questions
💼Interviews
🧠Scenarios
APPENDIX ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

A curated collection of Terraform interview questions organized by seniority level — from foundational concepts to advanced architecture scenarios. Each question includes a concise model answer.

What You'll Find Here

  • Basic questions testing Terraform fundamentals (Junior level)
  • Intermediate questions on modules, state, and workflows (Mid level)
  • Advanced questions on architecture and design patterns (Senior level)
  • Scenario-based questions simulating real-world problems

Appendix Contents

F.1Basic Questions (Junior Level) F.2Intermediate Questions (Mid Level) F.3Advanced Questions (Senior Level) F.4Scenario-Based Questions

F.1 Basic Questions (Junior Level)

JUNIOR 🟢 BEGINNER
📚 Focus Areas: Core concepts, basic commands, simple configurations

These questions test fundamental Terraform knowledge. If you're preparing for your first Terraform-related role, master every one of these.

💻 Junior-Level Questions & Answers

# Question Model Answer
1 What is Terraform? An open-source Infrastructure as Code tool by HashiCorp that lets you define, provision, and manage infrastructure using declarative HCL configuration files. It supports multiple cloud providers through a plugin-based architecture.
2 What is the Terraform workflow? Write → Plan → Apply. Write HCL config, run terraform plan to preview changes, run terraform apply to execute. terraform init must run first to install providers.
3 What is a Terraform provider? A plugin that allows Terraform to interact with APIs of cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), SaaS services, or other platforms. Providers are downloaded during terraform init.
4 What is the state file? A JSON file (terraform.tfstate) that maps your Terraform configuration to real-world resources. It tracks what Terraform manages and detects drift between config and reality.
5 What's the difference between terraform plan and terraform apply? plan is a dry-run that shows what changes will be made without modifying anything. apply actually executes those changes against real infrastructure.
6 How do you pass variables to Terraform? Via -var flag, -var-file, terraform.tfvars files, *.auto.tfvars files, or TF_VAR_name environment variables.
7 What's the difference between a resource and a data source? A resource creates and manages infrastructure. A data source reads existing infrastructure without managing it — it's read-only.
8 What does terraform init do? Initializes the working directory: downloads providers, downloads modules, configures the backend (state storage), and creates the .terraform directory.
9 What are outputs in Terraform? Values exported after apply for display or consumption by other configurations/modules. Defined with output blocks. Useful for passing data between modules or to CI/CD pipelines.
10 What is terraform destroy? Destroys all resources managed by the current configuration. It reads state to know what to delete. Use -target to destroy specific resources only. 
# ── Junior Interview: "Show me a basic Terraform config" ──

terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
}

variable "instance_type" {
  description = "EC2 instance type"
  type        = string
  default     = "t2.micro"
}

resource "aws_instance" "web" {
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = var.instance_type

  tags = {
    Name = "HelloTerraform"
  }
}

output "public_ip" {
  value = aws_instance.web.public_ip
}

# Key points to explain:
# - terraform block: version constraints
# - provider block: configures AWS
# - variable: parameterizes the config
# - resource: declares infrastructure
# - output: exports useful values

F.2 Intermediate Questions (Mid Level)

MID-LEVEL 🟡 INTERMEDIATE
📚 Focus Areas: State management, modules, backends, workspaces, provisioners

These questions test deeper understanding of Terraform operations, state management, and modularity. Expected for 1-3 years of experience.

💻 Mid-Level Questions & Answers

# Question Model Answer
1 Why should state be stored remotely? Remote state enables team collaboration (shared access), state locking (prevent concurrent modifications), state encryption (security), and automatic backups. Local state doesn't support locking or team access.
2 What is state locking and why is it important? State locking prevents two users/CI jobs from modifying state simultaneously, which could corrupt it. S3 backend uses DynamoDB for locking. HCP Terraform provides automatic locking.
3 Explain count vs for_each. count creates N identical resources indexed by number (0,1,2...). for_each creates resources keyed by map/set keys. for_each is more stable — removing an item doesn't shift other resources.
4 What are Terraform modules? Reusable, encapsulated groups of Terraform resources. Modules accept inputs (variables), produce outputs, and can be sourced from local paths, Git repos, or the Terraform Registry. Every config is technically a "root module".
5 How do you handle secrets in Terraform? Never hardcode secrets in config. Use: (1) Environment variables, (2) sensitive = true on variables, (3) External secret stores (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) via data sources, (4) Encrypted remote state backends. Note: state file always contains sensitive values in plaintext.
6 What is the terraform.workspace value? A built-in variable holding the current workspace name. CLI workspaces create separate state files, useful for managing environments (dev/staging/prod) with the same config.
7 What are provisioners and when should you use them? Provisioners (local-exec, remote-exec, file) run scripts on resources after creation. They're a last resort — prefer cloud-init, user_data, or configuration management tools (Ansible, Chef) instead.
8 How do you import existing infrastructure? Use terraform import <resource> <id> (CLI) or import {} blocks (v1.5+). Write the resource config first, import into state, then iterate with plan until "No changes".
9 What is terraform refresh and when does it run? Reconciles state with real infrastructure. It runs automatically at the start of plan and apply. The standalone command is deprecated — use terraform apply -refresh-only instead.
10 Explain the lifecycle block. Controls resource behavior: create_before_destroy (zero-downtime), prevent_destroy (safety), ignore_changes (external modifications), replace_triggered_by (force replacement). 
# ── Mid-Level Interview: "Show me count vs for_each" ────

# Using count — indexed by number
resource "aws_instance" "counted" {
  count         = 3
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = "t2.micro"
  tags = { Name = "server-${count.index}" }
}
# Creates: server-0, server-1, server-2
# Risk: removing server-1 shifts server-2 to index 1!

# Using for_each — keyed by string (more stable)
variable "instances" {
  default = {
    web  = "t2.micro"
    api  = "t2.small"
    db   = "t2.medium"
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "mapped" {
  for_each      = var.instances
  ami           = "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0"
  instance_type = each.value
  tags = { Name = each.key }
}
# Creates: web, api, db — each independently addressable
# Removing "api" does NOT affect "web" or "db"
📘 Interview Signal

Interviewers at mid-level look for understanding of why not just what. Don't just say "for_each uses a map" — explain that it creates stable resource keys, so removing one item doesn't cascade changes to others like count does.

F.3 Advanced Questions (Senior Level)

SENIOR 🔴 ADVANCED
📚 Focus Areas: Architecture, scaling, CI/CD, multi-account, custom providers

These questions test architectural thinking, large-scale Terraform design, and deep operational knowledge. Expected for 3+ years of experience.

💻 Senior-Level Questions & Answers

# Question Model Answer
1 How do you structure Terraform for a large organization? Split by blast radius: separate state per environment/service. Use a module monorepo or registry for shared modules. Implement CI/CD with plan on PR, apply on merge. Use remote state data sources for cross-stack references.
2 How do you manage Terraform across multiple AWS accounts? Use provider aliases with assume_role for cross-account access. One state per account. Use an automation account with permission to assume roles in target accounts. Consider HCP Terraform for governance.
3 What strategies exist for zero-downtime deployments with Terraform? create_before_destroy lifecycle, blue/green with ASG + ALB target group swaps, canary with weighted Route53 routing. Terraform manages infra, not deployment strategy — combine with deployment tooling.
4 How do you handle state file corruption or conflicts? Pull backup from versioned S3 bucket. Use terraform state pull/push carefully. Force-unlock with terraform force-unlock <LOCK_ID>. For corruption, restore from S3 version history or DynamoDB backup.
5 How do you test Terraform configurations? terraform validate (syntax), terraform plan (preview), terraform test (v1.6+ native testing), Terratest (Go integration tests), checkov/tfsec (security scanning), Sentinel (policy).
6 Explain the Terraform dependency graph. Terraform builds a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of all resources showing dependencies. It determines creation/destruction order automatically from references. depends_on adds explicit edges. terraform graph outputs DOT format for visualization.
7 How do you implement DR (Disaster Recovery) with Terraform? Store config in Git (version controlled). Use remote state with versioning/replication. Define DR region infrastructure with provider aliases. Use terraform plan to validate DR config stays in sync. Automate failover with CI/CD.
8 What are moved blocks and when do you use them? moved blocks tell Terraform that a resource was renamed or relocated in config, so it updates state instead of destroying and recreating. Essential for safe refactoring of modules and resource names. 
# ── Senior Interview: "Multi-account provider pattern" ──

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  alias  = "production"
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::111111111111:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = "us-east-1"
  alias  = "staging"
  assume_role {
    role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::222222222222:role/TerraformRole"
  }
}

module "vpc_prod" {
  source    = "./modules/vpc"
  providers = { aws = aws.production }
  cidr      = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

module "vpc_staging" {
  source    = "./modules/vpc"
  providers = { aws = aws.staging }
  cidr      = "10.1.0.0/16"
}

# Key points to explain:
# - One automation account assumes roles into targets
# - Provider aliases separate configurations
# - Modules receive specific providers via providers map
# - Each environment gets its own state (blast radius)

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          SENIOR-LEVEL INTERVIEW TOPIC MAP                        │
│                                                                  │
│   Architecture                                                   │
│   ├── State splitting by blast radius                           │
│   ├── Module monorepo vs polyrepo                               │
│   ├── Cross-stack references (remote_state)                     │
│   └── Provider aliases for multi-account                        │
│                                                                  │
│   Operations                                                     │
│   ├── CI/CD pipeline design (plan on PR, apply on merge)        │
│   ├── Drift detection strategies                                │
│   ├── State recovery and backup                                 │
│   └── Performance optimization (-parallelism, -refresh=false)   │
│                                                                  │
│   Security                                                       │
│   ├── Least privilege IAM for Terraform                         │
│   ├── Secrets management (Vault, SSM, Secrets Manager)          │
│   ├── Policy as Code (Sentinel, OPA)                            │
│   └── State encryption and access control                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

F.4 Scenario-Based Questions

SCENARIOS 🧠 PROBLEM SOLVING
📚 Focus Areas: Real-world problem solving, debugging, design decisions

Scenario questions test how you think through real-world problems. Interviewers want to see your troubleshooting process and architectural reasoning.

💻 Scenario Questions & Model Responses

# Scenario How to Approach
1 "Someone manually changed a security group in AWS Console. terraform plan now shows differences. What do you do?" Assess if the manual change was intentional. If yes: update config to match → plan shows no changes. If no: run terraform apply to revert to desired state. For persistent external changes, add ignore_changes in lifecycle. Discuss drift detection strategy.
2 "You need to rename a resource from aws_instance.web to aws_instance.frontend. How do you avoid destroying it?" Use moved { from = aws_instance.web to = aws_instance.frontend } (Terraform 1.1+). Alternatively, use terraform state mv aws_instance.web aws_instance.frontend (older approach).
3 "Your terraform apply failed halfway through. Some resources were created, others weren't. What now?" Terraform state is already updated for created resources. Run terraform plan to see remaining work. Run terraform apply again — it's idempotent and will only create missing resources. Check for root cause of the failure (permissions, quotas, API errors).
4 "Your team has 200+ resources in one state file and plans take 10+ minutes. How do you improve this?" Split into smaller state files by service/domain (microservice pattern). Use terraform_remote_state data source for cross-references. Consider -target for focused plans (temporary). Use -refresh=false when you know state is current.
5 "You need to deploy the same infrastructure to 3 AWS regions. How?" Create provider aliases for each region. Use a module encapsulating all region-specific resources. Call the module 3 times with different providers. Or use for_each with a map of regions on the module block (Terraform 0.13+).
6 "State is locked and the CI job that locked it crashed. How do you unlock?" Use terraform force-unlock <LOCK_ID>. Get the lock ID from the error message. Verify no other operations are running first. For S3+DynamoDB, you can also manually delete the lock item from the DynamoDB table.
📘 Interview Pro Tip

For scenario questions, always structure your answer: (1) Clarify the situation, (2) Explain your diagnosis/investigation steps, (3) Give the solution, (4) Mention prevention — what would you do to prevent this in the future? This shows both technical depth and engineering maturity.

# ── Scenario: "Rename without destroy" ──────────────────

# Before: resource "aws_instance" "web" { ... }
# After:  resource "aws_instance" "frontend" { ... }

# Solution 1: moved block (Terraform 1.1+, preferred)
moved {
  from = aws_instance.web
  to   = aws_instance.frontend
}

# Solution 2: CLI state mv (older approach)
# terraform state mv aws_instance.web aws_instance.frontend

# Both approaches update state WITHOUT destroying the
# real EC2 instance. Plan will show "no changes" after.

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          INTERVIEW DIFFICULTY PROGRESSION                        │
│                                                                  │
│   Junior (0-1 yr)                                                │
│   ├── "What is Terraform?"                                      │
│   ├── "Explain init → plan → apply"                             │
│   └── "What is a provider?"                                     │
│                                                                  │
│   Mid-Level (1-3 yr)                                             │
│   ├── "Why remote state?"                                       │
│   ├── "count vs for_each?"                                      │
│   └── "How do you handle secrets?"                              │
│                                                                  │
│   Senior (3+ yr)                                                 │
│   ├── "Design multi-account architecture"                       │
│   ├── "CI/CD pipeline for Terraform"                            │
│   └── "How do you test Terraform?"                              │
│                                                                  │
│   Scenario (All levels)                                          │
│   ├── "State is locked — what do you do?"                       │
│   ├── "Plan takes 10 minutes — how to fix?"                     │
│   └── "Manual drift detected — now what?"                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📋 ⚡ 🔖 📌
APPENDIX G

Quick Reference Cheat Sheet

Commands Variable Precedence Meta-Arguments Patterns
5Sections
30+Commands
📋Cheat Sheet
⚡Quick Ref
APPENDIX ALL LEVELS ★★★★★

A condensed, print-friendly cheat sheet covering the most frequently needed Terraform information — commands, variable precedence, meta-arguments, common patterns, and version constraint syntax.

What You'll Find Here

  • Common CLI commands with flags at a glance
  • Variable precedence order (exam favorite)
  • Meta-arguments quick reference table
  • Common patterns and version constraint syntax

Appendix Contents

G.1Common Commands G.2Variable Precedence G.3Meta-Arguments G.4Common Patterns G.5Version Constraints

G.1 Common Commands

COMMANDS ⚡ QUICK REF
📚 Official Documentation: CLI Commands

The essential Terraform commands you'll use daily, organized by workflow phase.

💻 Command Quick Reference

Phase Command Purpose Key Flags
Init terraform init Initialize working directory -upgrade, -reconfigure, -migrate-state
terraform get Download modules only -update
Write terraform fmt Format HCL files -check, -diff, -recursive
terraform validate Validate config syntax -json
terraform console Interactive expression eval —
Plan/Apply terraform plan Preview changes -out=FILE, -target=RES, -var="k=v", -destroy
terraform apply Apply changes -auto-approve, -replace=RES, -parallelism=N
terraform destroy Destroy all resources -auto-approve, -target=RES
State terraform state list List resources in state —
terraform state show RES Show resource detail —
terraform state mv Rename/move in state —
terraform state rm Remove from state (not infra) —
Inspect terraform output Read output values -json, -raw, NAME
terraform show Show state or plan file -json
Workspace terraform workspace list List workspaces —
terraform workspace select Switch workspace NAME

G.2 Variable Precedence

VARIABLES 📝 EXAM FAVORITE
📚 Official Documentation: Variable Definition Precedence

When the same variable is set in multiple places, Terraform uses this precedence order. This is one of the most frequently tested concepts on the certification exam.

💻 Precedence Order (Highest to Lowest)


┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           TERRAFORM VARIABLE PRECEDENCE                          │
│           (Highest priority wins)                                │
│                                                                  │
│   🥇 1. Command line: -var and -var-file flags                  │
│         $ terraform apply -var="region=us-west-2"               │
│                                                                  │
│   🥈 2. *.auto.tfvars / *.auto.tfvars.json (alphabetical)      │
│         Files loaded automatically by lexical order              │
│                                                                  │
│   🥉 3. terraform.tfvars / terraform.tfvars.json                │
│         Auto-loaded if present in working directory              │
│                                                                  │
│   4️⃣  4. Environment variables: TF_VAR_name                     │
│         $ export TF_VAR_region="us-east-1"                      │
│                                                                  │
│   5️⃣  5. Variable default value                                  │
│         variable "region" { default = "us-east-1" }             │
│                                                                  │
│   ❌ 6. No value → Terraform prompts interactively              │
│         (only in CLI, fails in CI/CD automation)                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Priority Source Auto-loaded? Best For
1 (highest) -var / -var-file No (explicit) CI/CD overrides, one-off changes
2 *.auto.tfvars Yes (alphabetical) Environment-specific defaults
3 terraform.tfvars Yes Default variable values for project
4 TF_VAR_* Yes (if set) Secrets in CI/CD, per-user config
5 (lowest) default in variable block Yes Sensible defaults for modules

G.3 Meta-Arguments

META ⚡ QUICK REF
📚 Official Documentation: Meta-Arguments

Condensed meta-argument reference — what each does, where it works, and key syntax.

💻 Meta-Arguments at a Glance

Meta-Argument Works On Syntax Remember
count resource, module count = 3 count.index — integer based, creates list
for_each resource, module for_each = toset([...]) each.key / each.value — creates map
depends_on resource, module depends_on = [res.name] Last resort — prefer implicit deps via references
provider resource, module provider = aws.west Select aliased provider config
lifecycle resource only lifecycle { ... } create_before_destroy, prevent_destroy, ignore_changes
📘 Can't Use Both

count and for_each are mutually exclusive — you cannot use both on the same resource or module block. Pick one based on whether your resources are indexed by number (count) or by name/key (for_each).

G.4 Common Patterns

PATTERNS ⚡ QUICK REF
📚 Official Documentation: Expressions

Copy-paste-ready Terraform patterns you'll use constantly — conditional resources, dynamic blocks, lookups, and more.

💻 Pattern Quick Reference

Pattern Code When to Use
Conditional resource count = var.create ? 1 : 0 Feature flag, optional resources
Conditional value var.env == "prod" ? "t3.large" : "t3.micro" Environment-specific settings
Map lookup var.sizes[var.env] or lookup(var.sizes, var.env, "default") Config-driven values
Splat to list aws_instance.web[*].id Collect attributes from count/for_each
For to filter [for s in var.list : s if s.active] Conditional filtering
For to transform {for k, v in var.map : k => upper(v)} Value transformation
Null for optional key_name = var.key != "" ? var.key : null Omit argument if not needed
Try for safe access try(var.config.nested.value, "default") Access potentially missing attrs
Flatten nested loops flatten([for a in var.x : [for b in a.y : { ... }]]) Multiple dimensions → flat list
Merge tags tags = merge(local.common_tags, { Name = "x" }) Combine default + resource tags

G.5 Version Constraints

VERSIONS 📝 EXAM
📚 Official Documentation: Version Constraints

Version constraint syntax used in required_version, required_providers, and module version arguments. Understanding these is critical for the certification exam.

💻 Version Constraint Operators

Operator Syntax Meaning Example Matches
= (or none) "1.5.0" Exact version only 1.5.0
!= "!= 1.5.0" Exclude specific version Anything except 1.5.0
>, >=, <, <= ">= 1.5.0" Comparison operators 1.5.0, 1.6.0, 2.0.0, ...
~> (pessimistic) "~> 1.5" Allow only rightmost increment ≥ 1.5, < 2.0
~> (patch) "~> 1.5.0" Allow only patch increments ≥ 1.5.0, < 1.6.0
compound ">= 1.5, < 2.0" Multiple constraints (AND) 1.5.x, 1.6.x, ..., 1.9.x 
# ── Version constraint examples ──────────────────────────

terraform {
  # Terraform CLI version
  required_version = "~> 1.5"  # >= 1.5.0, < 2.0.0

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"      # >= 5.0.0, < 6.0.0
    }
    random = {
      source  = "hashicorp/random"
      version = ">= 3.0, < 4.0"  # Compound constraint
    }
  }
}

# Module version constraint
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.1.0"  # >= 5.1.0, < 5.2.0 (patch only)
}
⚠️ Exam Trap: ~> Operator

The ~> (pessimistic) operator behaves differently based on precision: ~> 1.5 allows 1.5 through 1.x (minor+patch), but ~> 1.5.0 allows only 1.5.0 through 1.5.x (patch only). This is a very common exam question!

📚 🎥 🌐 🔗

H.1 Official Documentation

DOCS 📚 OFFICIAL
📚 Start Here: Terraform Developer Portal

The official HashiCorp documentation is the single best resource for Terraform. It's comprehensive, well-organized, and regularly updated.

💻 Essential Official Resources

Resource URL Best For
Terraform Documentation developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/docs Complete language and CLI reference
Terraform Tutorials developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/tutorials Guided hands-on learning paths
Terraform Registry registry.terraform.io Provider docs, modules, policy libraries
AWS Provider Docs AWS Provider Registry All AWS resource and data source docs
Language Reference developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language HCL syntax, functions, expressions
CLI Documentation developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/cli All CLI commands and flags
Certification Prep Certification Study Guide Official exam prep and sample questions
GitHub Repository github.com/hashicorp/terraform Source code, issues, changelogs

H.2 Books

BOOKS 📖 READING
📚 Recommended Reading List

The best Terraform and IaC books for different experience levels — from getting started to production-grade architecture.

💻 Recommended Books

Book Author Level Focus
Terraform: Up & Running (3rd Ed.) Yevgeniy Brikman Beginner → Advanced Best overall Terraform book. Covers basics through production patterns, testing, and team workflows.
Infrastructure as Code (2nd Ed.) Kief Morris Intermediate Principles and patterns of IaC. Tool-agnostic design thinking applicable to Terraform.
Terraform in Action Scott Winkler Intermediate Hands-on projects with real-world examples. Good for learning by building.
The Terraform Book James Turnbull Beginner Gentle introduction, great for absolute beginners coming to IaC for the first time.
Terraform Cookbook Mikael Krief All Levels Recipe-style problem-solution format. Good reference for specific tasks.
📘 Top Pick

"Terraform: Up & Running" by Yevgeniy Brikman is widely considered the definitive Terraform book. The 3rd edition covers Terraform 1.x, testing with Terratest, module design, and production-grade infrastructure patterns. Start here if you buy only one book.

H.3 Online Courses

COURSES 🎓 LEARNING
📚 Top Platforms: HashiCorp Learn, Udemy, A Cloud Guru, Pluralsight

Curated online courses for structured learning paths — from free official tutorials to deep-dive paid courses and certification preparation.

💻 Recommended Courses

Course / Platform Level Cost Highlights
HashiCorp Learn — Terraform Beginner→Intermediate Free Official tutorials, hands-on labs, certification prep. The gold standard.
Udemy — HashiCorp Certified Terraform Associate (by Bryan Krausen) Beginner→Intermediate Paid Comprehensive exam prep, practice tests, regularly updated.
A Cloud Guru — HashiCorp Certified Terraform Associate Beginner→Intermediate Subscription Hands-on labs with cloud sandboxes, good for certification.
Pluralsight — Terraform Deep Dive Intermediate→Advanced Subscription In-depth modules on state, modules, and advanced patterns.
KodeKloud — Terraform for DevOps Beginner Paid Interactive labs, gamified learning, great for visual learners.
freeCodeCamp — Terraform Course Beginner Free Full-length free course on YouTube, great starting point.

H.4 Community Resources

COMMUNITY 🌐 SOCIAL
📚 Connect & Learn: Forums, Discord, Reddit, newsletters

Stay connected with the Terraform community for troubleshooting help, best practices sharing, and keeping up with the latest developments.

💻 Community Channels

Resource Type Best For
HashiCorp Discuss Forum Official Q&A forum, detailed problem discussions, HashiCorp team responses
r/Terraform (Reddit) Community Community discussions, tips, experience sharing, career questions
HashiCorp Community Slack Chat Real-time help, #terraform channel for quick questions
Stack Overflow — [terraform] Q&A Searchable archive of solved problems, good for error messages
GitHub Issues (hashicorp/terraform) Bug Tracker Report bugs, request features, track development
HashiCorp User Groups (HUGs) Meetups Local and virtual meetups, presentations, networking
HashiConf Conference Annual conference with talks, workshops, and product announcements
Terraform Weekly Newsletter Newsletter Weekly roundup of Terraform articles, releases, and community content

H.5 Practice Environments

HANDS-ON 🛠️ PRACTICE
📚 Learn by Doing: Sandboxes, free tiers, local environments

The best way to learn Terraform is hands-on practice. Here are environments where you can safely experiment without incurring costs or breaking production.

💻 Practice Environment Options

Environment Cost Setup Best For
AWS Free Tier Free (12 months) Create AWS account, set billing alerts Real AWS resources: EC2, S3, VPC, IAM. Set budget alerts!
LocalStack Free (Community) Docker: docker run localstack/localstack Local AWS API emulation. Practice without AWS account. Supports S3, Lambda, DynamoDB, and more.
HCP Terraform (Free Tier) Free (5 users) Sign up at app.terraform.io Remote state, runs, workspace management. Good for team workflow practice.
KillerCoda / Katacoda Free Browser-based Pre-configured interactive environments in the browser. No setup needed.
Terraform Console Free terraform console Test functions, expressions, and interpolations interactively. No cloud required.
Docker Provider Free Docker Desktop + Terraform Manage containers with Terraform locally. Great for learning resource lifecycle. 
# ── Practice with Docker provider (free, local) ─────────

terraform {
  required_providers {
    docker = {
      source  = "kreuzwerker/docker"
      version = "~> 3.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "docker" {}

resource "docker_image" "nginx" {
  name = "nginx:latest"
}

resource "docker_container" "web" {
  name  = "terraform-nginx"
  image = docker_image.nginx.image_id

  ports {
    internal = 80
    external = 8080
  }
}

# terraform init && terraform apply
# Visit http://localhost:8080 — managed by Terraform!
⚠️ AWS Free Tier Warning

Always set a billing alarm when using AWS Free Tier for practice. Some resources (NAT Gateways, large RDS instances) are NOT free-tier eligible. Run terraform destroy when done practicing to avoid surprise charges.

H.6 YouTube Channels

VIDEO 🎥 YOUTUBE
📚 Video Learning: Channels for visual learners and tutorial walkthroughs

YouTube channels with quality Terraform content — tutorials, deep-dives, project walkthroughs, and certification preparation videos.

💻 Recommended Channels

Channel Content Type Level Why Watch
HashiCorp Official talks, demos, HashiConf recordings All Levels Product announcements, deep-dive presentations, customer stories
freeCodeCamp Full-length courses Beginner Free complete Terraform courses (3-4 hours), great starting point
TechWorld with Nana DevOps tutorials Beginner→Intermediate Clear explanations, DevOps context, Terraform + CI/CD integration
CloudChamp Cloud & IaC tutorials Beginner→Intermediate Terraform with AWS hands-on projects, certification focused
Spacelift IaC & Terraform deep-dives Intermediate→Advanced Advanced patterns, state management, CI/CD for Terraform
DevOps Directive Project-based tutorials Intermediate Complete project builds with Terraform, real-world scenarios
Ned in the Cloud (Ned Bellavance) Deep-dives, opinion pieces Intermediate→Advanced Thoughtful analysis, Terraform best practices, certification tips
KodeKloud Structured learning Beginner Well-structured beginner courses with animations and diagrams
📘 Learning Path Recommendation

Ideal learning sequence: (1) Start with HashiCorp Learn tutorials (free, official), (2) Watch a full YouTube course (freeCodeCamp or TechWorld with Nana), (3) Read "Terraform: Up & Running" for depth, (4) Build real projects on AWS Free Tier, (5) Take practice exams and get certified. Total timeline: 4-8 weeks of focused study.
